Keturah was Abraham's concubine. A concubine was like a wife or companion of a man who already had one wife. A concubine did not have the same rights as a man's first wife. The sons of Keturah were also not the direct ancestors of the Israelites. In spite of this, the storyteller of Chronicles thought it was important to record the names of Keturah's sons in his book. This is the first time that the storyteller of Chronicles thinks that the identity of a mother is important enough to mention. We do not know for certain why the storyteller chooses to mention Keturah's name here. One possibility is that, although Keturah was Abraham's concubine, Yahweh did not include her children in the agreement that Yahweh had made with Abraham. Perhaps the storyteller wanted to emphasize that Keturah's children did not receive the promises about possessing the land of Canaan, or any of the other promises that were for Isaac only.

We know where some of the descendants of Abraham through Keturah lived.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location where some of Keturah's descendants lived.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:28–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller focuses on naming the descendants of Ishmael.

In the second scene: The storyteller focuses on naming the descendants of Abraham through Abraham's concubine Keturah.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Isaac, Ishmael, Nebaioth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah
  • And Keturah, Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah, Sheba, Dedan, Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated all of these names. If the book of Genesis is available in your language, it would be helpful to go back and listen to the beginning section of Genesis chapter 25, which lists the descendants of Abraham through Keturah and through Ishmael.

Stop here and listen to Genesis chapter 25, up until the descendants of Ishmael. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces this new section of the book by saying, "The sons of Abraham were Isaac and Ishmael." Isaac's mother was Sarah, Abraham's wife, while Ishmael's mother was Hagar, Sarah's servant. Although it was the Israelites' custom to list their children beginning with the oldest and continuing in order of age, the storyteller of Chronicles lists Abraham's younger son, Isaac, before Ishmael.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, what is the relationship like between older siblings and younger siblings? How much authority do older siblings have over younger siblings? Do older siblings or younger siblings have any special responsibilities or privileges? Pause this audio here.

There are several reasons why the storyteller of Chronicles might have put Isaac's name before Ishmael's name. First, Isaac was the ancestor of the Israelites, and therefore, Isaac was most important to the Israelites. So the storyteller of Chronicles just put Isaac's name first. It is possible that it could reflect the fact that Abraham was not married to Ishmael's mother, so even though Ishmael was older, Isaac had a higher status than Ishmael. It might also reflect the fact that Yahweh told Abraham that Yahweh would fulfill his promises to Abraham through Isaac and not through Ishmael.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, when you recite a list of family members, how do you decide the order in which you list your family members? What rules or customs determine how you list your family members? Do you always follow these rules? If not, under what circumstances might you choose not to follow the rules? Pause this audio here.

This introduction also does not mention the sons of Keturah. The storyteller waits to list these sons after he lists the descendants of Ishmael. The storyteller probably assumes that his audience knows that Abraham also had sons through Keturah, who became Abraham's concubine after Sarah died. A concubine is a woman who has a lower status than a wife, but is socially acceptable as a man's companion. People considered that the children of a concubine were legitimate children and had the same rights as the children of a wife. In the Ancient Near East, it was a very common practice for a man to have one or more concubines. In Genesis chapter 25, the storyteller calls Keturah Abraham's wife. The storyteller of Chronicles might call her Abraham's concubine here to show that Keturah did not have the same status as Sarah, the mother of Isaac, who inherited the promises from God.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What word or phrase could you use in your language to describe a concubine? Do you have a similar word or concept in your culture? If not, how do you feel about this category of wife? Pause this audio here.

You might find a small difference in the list of descendants of Keturah in Genesis compared to the list in 1 Chronicles. The passage in Genesis lists the descendants of Dedan, while the list in 1 Chronicles leaves those descendants out. Since we want our translation to accurately reflect what is in the original story, do not include the descendants of Dedan here.

The storyteller mentions that Nebaioth was Ishmael's firstborn son. In that time and place, the firstborn son had special privileges and special responsibilities. The firstborn son got twice as much inheritance as any other sons did. The firstborn often had more authority than other sons did.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how do you talk about the firstborn child, or the firstborn son? Pause this audio here.

You may notice that the storyteller once again includes a summary statement at the end of the list of Ishmael's descendants and at the end of the list of Abraham's descendants through Keturah. The storyteller says, "These were the sons of Ishmael," and later says, "These were the descendants of Keturah." You previously talked about whether or not to include this kind of summary statement in your translation.

Stop here and remind each other of the decision you made about including summary statements for each section in your translation. Are you happy with the decision you made, or do you want to change it? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:28–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Isaac, Ishmael, Nebaioth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah
  • And Keturah, Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah, Sheba, Dedan, Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, imagine you are an ancient Israelite listening to this list of descendants. You have heard the stories about Abraham, Isaac, and Ishmael many times. You know that Yahweh made great promises to Abraham. Yahweh also told Abraham that Yahweh would fulfill those promises through Isaac, Abraham's son with Sarah. You know that Abraham and Sarah had to wait a very long time before Sarah became pregnant with Isaac, and that Abraham and Sarah's trust in God was weak. That is how Hagar gave birth to Ishmael. You know that after the birth of Isaac, Abraham sent his son Ishmael away. You know that Sarah died before Abraham died, and that Abraham grieved for Sarah. Then Abraham married Keturah and had more children. But Isaac remained the son that Yahweh had miraculously provided for Abraham.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should read this passage a second time. At certain points in the passage, stop reading and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The sons of Abraham were Isaac and Ishmael. These are their family records: the firstborn son of Ishmael was Nabaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel excited that we are now talking about the descendants of Abraham; or
  • I do not like the descendants of Ishmael; or
  • It is important to remember the other descendants of our father Abraham.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Those Ishmaelites have been a problem for our people; or
  • That situation with Ishmael was sad. I feel bad for Ishmael and his mother; or
  • I am excited. I want to know more about these people.

Continue reciting the names in the passage.

The sons of Keturah, Abraham's concubine, were Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. The sons of Jokshan were Sheba and Dedan. The sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abidah, and Eldaah. All these were the descendants of Keturah.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is important to remember that these people are also descendants of Abraham; or
  • I wonder what happened to all these people over the years; or
  • I hope my audience is still listening to these long lists of names.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am relieved that I recognize some of these names; or
  • I feel happy for Abraham that he was blessed with so many children; or
  • It is important to remember who descended from which ancestors.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:28–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Remember that sometimes the word sons in the original language refers to the male children of a parent, and sometimes it refers to a group of descendants. Translate sons, or descendants, in the same way you have translated in the previous passages. In the previous passages, you may have chosen to use one word to mean both sons and descendants, or you may have two separate words for sons and descendants. For more information about sons and descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

To introduce the next section, the storyteller begins by saying, "The sons of Abraham were Isaac and Ishmael. These are their genealogies." Genealogy is a special word that means "a record of ancestors and descendants."

Stop here and discuss this question: Does your language have a special word or phrase for a list of ancestors and descendants? If so, can you use that word here? If not, how will you introduce this list of descendants? For more information on the word genealogy, see the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Be sure to translate the names Abraham, Isaac, and Ishmael in the same way that you have translated them in previous stories. Also see the Master Glossary for more information on Abraham.

Now we come to the lists of the descendants of Abraham. If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated these names. It would be helpful to listen again to the section of the book of Genesis where you find this list of names.

Stop here and listen to the first section of Genesis chapter 25, which lists the descendants of Abraham. Pay attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, according to where the people lived, or did you translate them to sound like the original name? Pause this audio here.

Next, the storyteller lists the descendants of Ishmael. Ishmael's descendants lived in modern-day Saudi Arabia, and possibly in parts of Jordan and in the eastern part of Egypt, but it is hard to know for sure exactly where each group lived. So it is probably best not to use modern place names for the people groups who descended from Ishmael. We do know what some of these names mean. The name Ishmael means "God hears." Nebaioth means "fruitfulness." Kedar means "dark or dusky." Adbeel means "disciplined of God." Mibsam means "smells good." We do not know the meanings of Mishma, Dumah, and Massa. Hadad means "fierce." We do not know the meaning of Tema. Jetur probably means "surrounded or closed in." Naphish means "refreshed." Kedemah means "to come first, to be first."

Stop here and listen again to Genesis chapter 25, specifically the part that lists Ishmael's descendants. Pay attention to how you translated the names Nebaioth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. Are you happy with how you translated these names in Genesis, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller lists the sons of Abraham through Keturah. In this story, the storyteller calls Keturah Abraham's concubine. A concubine is any woman who lives with a man and provides him with children, but who has an inferior status to that man's primary wife. People consider that the children of a concubine are legitimate children and those children can inherit wealth and property from their father. In the case of Abraham, Sarah, and Keturah, Sarah was the primary wife. Even though Sarah died before Abraham married Keturah, the storyteller of Chronicles still refers to Keturah as a concubine and not as a wife.

Stop here and discuss what word you will use concubine. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, be sure to use the same word here. Pause this audio here.

Bible scholars believe that some of these people groups that descended from Abraham and Keturah probably lived in Arabia. But with the exception of the descendants of Midian, we do not know exactly where each of these groups lived. Midian is a region in the far northwest part of Arabia. The Midianites lived on both sides of the Gulf of Aqaba and were probably nomadic. We hear about the country of Midian and the Midianite people many times in the Bible.

The name Keturah means "perfumed", as in "smelling good", or possibly "frankincense," which was a pleasant-smelling substance. Zimran means "musical." Jokshan means "tricky or sneaky." Medan means "arguing." Midian means "argument." Ishbak means "he will leave." Shuah means "to think." We are not certain what Sheba and Dedan mean. Ephah means "darkness." Epher means "gazelle, or deer-like animal." Hanoch means "introduced." Abidah means "father of knowledge." Eldaah means "God of knowledge."

Stop here and listen again to the very beginning of Genesis chapter 25, which lists the descendants of Abraham through Keturah. Pay attention to how you translated the names Keturah, Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah, Sheba, Dedan, Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abidah, and Eldaah. Are you happy with how you translated these names in Genesis, or do you want to change them?

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 1:34–37
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 1:34–37 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:34–37 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:34–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of Chronicles introduced us to the descendants of Abraham. The storyteller listed the descendants of Ishmael, Abraham's firstborn son, and the descendants of Abraham through his concubine Keturah. In this passage, the storyteller begins to list the descendants of Abraham through his son, Isaac. First, the storyteller lists Isaac's two sons, Esau and Israel, and then the storyteller lists some of Esau's descendants.

The storyteller follows the same pattern from previous passages by first listing the descendants of Esau, the son of Isaac, who was not the direct ancestor of the Israelites. Esau and Jacob were twins. Esau was born first, and Jacob was born second. Later, Yahweh gave Jacob the name Israel, so the name "Israel" in this passage refers to Jacob, Isaac's second son. The Israelites are descendants of Isaac through his son Jacob and received their name from "Israel," the name that Yahweh gave to Jacob. The storyteller of Chronicles often uses the name "Israel" when he talks about Jacob to remind the Israelites of this connection.

The descendants of Esau lived in a region called Edom. This area was south of the Dead Sea and southeast of Israel. Edom included territory in the far southeastern part of modern-day Israel, the south of modern-day Jordan, and the far northwest of modern-day Saudi Arabia.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Edom and Israel. Pause this audio here.

The descendants of Esau were even more closely related to the Israelites than any other group we have discussed up to this point. Because Esau's descendants lived in the region called Edom, people sometimes called Esau's descendants the Edomites. The Israelites often came in contact with the Edomites, and the two groups often acted hostilely toward each other. Yahweh even told the Israelites, "You are not to hate an Edomite, for he is your brother," emphasizing the close relationship between the two groups.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Name some people groups that your group considers to be related to you. What is the relationship like between your group and the other groups? What are some reasons why you might feel close to or friendly with the other groups? What are some reasons why you might have conflict? Pause this audio here.

The descendants of Esau were all part of the people group that we know as the Edomites. Just like the Israelites, the Edomites had smaller subgroups that people sometimes called tribes. People usually named these groups after an ancestor.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Do you have tribes in your people group? If so, how do you know which tribe a person belongs to? How important is membership in a tribe? What is the relationship like between the tribes?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:34–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller focuses on the family line from Abraham to Esau and Israel. Then the storyteller names the sons of Esau.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists some of the descendants of Esau's sons.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Abraham, Isaac, Esau, Israel, Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah
  • Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, Kenaz, Amalek, Timna
  • And Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller tells us about the family line from Abraham to Esau and Israel. Then the storyteller tells us the names of Esau's sons. If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated all of these names. It would probably be helpful to go back and listen to the part of Genesis that contains this part of the family line.

Stop here and listen to the first five verses of Genesis chapter 36, which list the sons that were born to Esau in the land of Canaan. Pause this audio here.

You probably noticed that the story in Genesis chapter 36 contains more details than the list of ancestors in 1 Chronicles chapter 1. For example, the storyteller of Chronicles leaves out the names of Esau's wives. The storyteller might have assumed that his audience already knew which of Esau's wives was the mother of which son. Or, the storyteller might have assumed that if his audience did not know, they could refer to Genesis chapter 36 and find out. Remember that the Israelites considered the family line of fathers and sons to be the most important detail of their family lines.

Because we can refer back to Genesis chapter 36, we know all of the names in this scene are names of individual men. The Bible has many stories about Abraham, Isaac, Esau, and Israel, that is, Jacob. We also know that Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah were individual men. Therefore, in this scene, whenever the storyteller uses the word "father," it means a literal "father" rather than "ancestor." And when the storyteller uses the word "son," it means a literal "son" rather than "descendant."

Jacob and Israel are two different names for the same person. Throughout the Bible, the storytellers frequently call the descendants of Jacob by the name Israel, and sometimes call them Israelites. This can be confusing for listeners. It might not always be obvious when the name "Israel" refers to Jacob the individual, or to his descendants, the Israelites. Here, the storyteller is talking about Jacob the person and not about Jacob's descendants.

In scene two, the storyteller tells us the names of some descendants of Esau's sons, Eliphaz and Reuel. The storyteller does not tell us any names of descendants of Esau's sons Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.

In 1 Chronicles 1, the storyteller includes Timna in the list of descendants of Eliphaz. But Genesis 36 says that Timna was actually Eliphaz's concubine and the mother of Amalek. This difference is easy to explain if the storyteller of Chronicles assumed that his audience already knew who Timna was. In the original language, Hebrew, the name Timna is a name for a woman, so the storyteller probably assumed that his audience knew she was the mother of the person whose name came after hers.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, are there names that people use for males only? For females only? Is it possible for a name to be both a name for a male and a name for a female? Give some examples. Pause this audio here.

Some translations of this passage include the information that Timna was not a descendant of Eliphaz, but rather that Timna was Eliphaz's concubine and was the mother of Amalek.

Stop here and discuss this question: Will you include the information that Timna was the mother of Amalek and not a descendant of Eliphaz? Why or why not? Pause this audio here.

The descendants of Esau were all part of one people group, the Edomites. Although the Edomites were closely related to the Israelites, they saw themselves as a separate, independent group. The Edomites had their own territory and their own leaders. Also, like the Israelites, the Edomites recognized that there were smaller groups within their own people group. These smaller groups were descended from the sons and grandsons of Esau. Some translations of the Bible refer to these smaller groups of people as tribes. The word "tribes" does not appear in the original language, but it is acceptable to use here if it helps to make the text more clear and natural. For example, some translations say something like, "Eliphaz became the ancestor of the following tribes."

Stop here and discuss these questions: What words does your language use to refer to smaller groups of people within the larger group? Which word might you use to translate the word tribe? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:34–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Abraham, Isaac, Esau, Israel, Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah
  • Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, Kenaz, Amalek, Timna
  • And Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, imagine you are an ancient Israelite listening to this list of descendants. You are familiar with the stories of Abraham, Isaac, Esau, and Jacob. You know that Esau sold his birthright to Jacob for a bowl of stew and was angry about it afterwards. You know that Esau was so angry that he planned to kill Jacob. You know that your ancestors had conflict with the descendants of Esau for a long time. You know that King David and King Solomon ruled over the Edomites for a time, but that later the Edomites rebelled and attacked Israel.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Abraham's son Isaac had two sons, Esau and Jacob. Esau's sons were Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored to be the one who tells my people about these important parts of our history; or
  • I feel sad that there has been so much conflict between our people and the descendants of Esau; or
  • It is important to remember the other descendants of our father Isaac.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The peoplemight answer things like:

  • I feel sad for Esau, who really suffered for his poor decisions; or
  • I feel angry at the Edomites because they treated our people badly so many times; or
  • I wish I knew more about the descendants of our brother Esau.

Continue reciting the list of names.

The sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, Kenaz, and by Timna, Amalek. The sons of Reuel were Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel angry when I remember the Amalekites, who have been our enemies for a long time; or
  • I hope our people do not continue to have conflict with the Edomites; or
  • I wonder what happened to the descendants of Esau's other three sons?

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I recognize some of these names, and I do not like them; or
  • I wonder why the storyteller mentioned Timna but not any other wives or mothers; or
  • I am afraid that the Edomites could still hurt our people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:34–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Remember that sometimes the word sons in the original language refers to the male children of a parent, and sometimes it refers to a group of descendants. In this passage, all the names in scene one are names of individual sons. The names in the second scene are both the name of an individual son and of a tribe. Translate these types of sons and descendants in the same way you have in previous passages. For more information about sons and descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

In the first scene, the storyteller recounts the family line through Abraham, Isaac, Esau, and Israel. Then he lists Esau's sons. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are called the patriarchs, or fathers, of Israel. Their names have important meaning. Abraham means "father of many." Isaac means "laughter," which reminds us that Isaac's mother, Sarah, laughed when she first heard that she would have a son. Esau means "rough or rugged" in the sense of the opposite of smooth and even. Israel means "wrestles with God." Be sure to translate Abraham, Isaac, Esau, and Israel the same way you have in previous passages. Look up Abraham and Israel in the Master Glossary for more information.

The storyteller begins this scene by using the special word begot, or "became the father of," to say that Abraham was the father of Isaac. Be sure to translate this word in the same way that you have done in previous passages.

The sons of Esau were Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. The name Eliphaz means "God of gold." The name Reuel means "friend of God." The name Jeush means "too fast." The name Jalam means "hidden." The name Korah means "ice."

Stop here and listen again to Genesis chapter thirty-six, the first five verses, if it is available in your language. Pay close attention to how you translated the names of Esau's sons. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated the names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

Eliphaz was the father of Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, and Kenaz. Through his concubine Timna, Eliphaz also fathered Amalek. The name Teman means "south." The name Omar means "talkative." The name Zephi means "observant." We do not know the meaning of the name Gatam. The name Kenaz means "hunter." The name Timna means "restraint." We do not know the meaning of the name Amalek.

Stop here and listen again to the middle part of Genesis chapter 36 if it is available in your language. Pay close attention to how you translated the names of the family of Eliphaz. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated the names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

Reuel became the father of Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. The name Nahath means "to go down." The name Zerah means "to appear." The name Shammah means "desolation." The name Mizzah means "to faint from fear."

Stop here and listen again to Genesis chapter 36 if it is available in your language. Pay close attention to how you translated the names of the sons of Reuel. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated the names, or do you want to change them?

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 1:38–42
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 1:38–42 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:38–42 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:38–42 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of Chronicles told us about some of the descendants of Esau, who was Isaac's oldest son. In this passage, the storyteller tells us the names of some people who lived in Seir, which is another name for Edom. These are probably the groups of people who lived in Edom around the time that Esau's descendants settled there.

The land of Edom is south of the land of Israel.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the locations of Edom and Israel. Pause this audio here.

There are many mountains and valleys in the land of Edom. The name of one of those mountains was Mount Seir. Bible scholars are not sure exactly where Mount Seir was located. But over time, people began to call the entire area "the hill country of Seir" or just "Seir." So "Edom" and "Seir" are two different names for the same place.

Stop here and discuss this question: Tell a story about a place in your country that people call by more than one name. How did this place get each of its names? Pause this audio here.

Before the descendants of Esau lived in Seir, a group called the Horites lived there. Esau's descendants had to fight the Horites and drive them out in order to make a place for themselves to live. In the book of Genesis, the same group of people is called "the sons of Seir the Horite." So it seems that "the Horites" and "the sons of Seir" are two different names for the same group of people. Seir is probably the name of both the area of land people called Seir and the person from whom the sons of Seir descended.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Tell a story about a group of people in your country that you call by more than one name. How did this group get its different names? Or, if others call your group by more than one name, tell the story of how your group got those names. Pause this audio here.

You may find it surprising that the storyteller listed the descendants of Seir here. These people are not descendants of Abraham, and so to include them here might seem out of place. In the previous passage, the storyteller told us that Esau's son Eliphaz had a concubine named Timna, who was the mother of Eliphaz's son Amalek. In this passage, the storyteller tells us that Lotan, a son of Seir, had a sister named Timna. Some Bible scholars think that this was the same Timna. Remember, we previously talked about how the Israelites think that the family line through the father is most important, and that the Biblical storytellers usually do not mention mothers unless there is something unusual or significant about the mother. So if Esau's son Eliphaz married a Horite woman named Timna, this might explain why the storyteller of Chronicles mentioned her in two passages. If Timna's family was an important family, or if this was an important marriage, then it also might explain why the storyteller listed the descendants of Seir here.

In the Bible, marriages were not always just for romance or childbearing. Sometimes, a leader or a father might arrange a marriage in order to show that two different groups were making a political partnership with each other, or that two families were making peace with each other. This was especially true for kings, but it could be true for other people as well.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How often do families arrange marriages in your culture? For what reasons, other than romance or having children, might two families arrange a marriage? Do people ever arrange marriages to form an alliance between two families or two larger groups? Pause this audio here.

Some people might also find this passage confusing because there are several sets of people with the same name. For example, Seir named one of his sons Anah, and Zibeon also named one of his sons Anah.. Seir named another son Dishon, and Anah also named one of his sons Dishon..

Stop here and remind yourselves of the discussion you had earlier about identifying different people with the same name. In your culture, how often do you encounter different people who have the same name? How often do parents give their child the same name as a relative? How can you make it clear to your audience that there are different people with the same name in this story?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:38–42 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the names of the sons of Seir.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists the names of the sons of Lotan, then mentions that Lotan's sister was Timna.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists the names of the sons of the remaining sons of Seir.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Seir, Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan
  • Hori, Homam, Timna
  • And Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, Onam, Aiah, Anah, Dishon, Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, Keran, Bilhan, Zaavan, Akan, Uz, and Aran

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated a story about the sons of Seir. This story is in the middle of Genesis chapter 36. If it is available in your language, it would be helpful and go back and listen to this part of Genesis to remind yourself of each of these names.

Stop here and listen to the middle part of Genesis chapter 36, which discusses the sons of Seir. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the storyteller of Chronicles lists the names of the sons of Seir. You probably noticed that in Genesis chapter 36, the storyteller tells us that Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan are chiefs of the Horites living in the land of Edom. The storyteller of Chronicles leaves out this detail and just tells us their names. The storyteller also leaves out Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah.

The storyteller of Chronicles has not mentioned Seir before in his book. The storyteller probably assumed that his audience knew who Seir was and why Seir's descendants were important. However, this will not be the case with many modern audiences. For this reason, some Bible translations choose to include a short statement to explain who the sons of Seir were. You may choose to do the same if you think it will help your audience understand this section. You may choose to say something like, "The original people of Edom were descended from the following sons of Seir."

Stop here and discuss whether or not you will include a short statement to explain who the descendants of Seir were. What are the advantages and disadvantages of doing so? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller tells us the names of the sons of Lotan. The storyteller also tells us that Lotan had a sister named Timna. Some Bible scholars think that this Timna is the same person who was the concubine of Eliphaz and the mother of Amalek. If this is true, then this is evidence that the descendants of Esau intermarried with the people who lived in the hill country of Seir.

In the third scene, the storyteller tells us the names of the rest of the grandsons of Seir.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:38–42 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Seir, Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan
  • Hori, Homam, Timna
  • And Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, Onam, Aiah, Anah, Dishon, Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, Keran, Bilhan, Zaavan, Akan, Uz, and Aran

This section lists the original inhabitants of Edom. Esau's descendants drove the original inhabitants out of their land. The ancient Israelites knew what that felt like. The Israelites' ancestors had experienced the same thing. On the other hand, Esau's descendants were their brothers. The ancient Israelites had a stronger connection to Esau's descendants than they did to the original inhabitants of Edom. In spite of this, the Edomites had fought the Israelites many times during their history. The ancient Israelites might have had a lot of conflicting feelings about this story!

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue to read or recite the passage.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The original people of Edom were the descendants of Seir: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel impatient to get through this section because these people are not descendants of Abraham; or
  • It is important to remember the people that our brothers, the Edomites, had to fight in order to get their land; or
  • This information is important but not very exciting.

Ask the person playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • These people are not descendants of Abraham, so why do we need to know this? or
  • I am glad that the descendants of Esau defeated these people; or
  • I feel sad when I remember all these people who Esau's descendants drove outof the land of Edom; or
  • I wonder where Seir came from?

Continue reciting the list of names.

The sons of Lotan were Hori and Homam. Lotan's sister was Timna.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Timna must have been from an important family; or
  • Lotan was honored that his sister Timna married Eliphaz; or
  • Maybe if these people had defeated the Edomites, we would not have such problems with the Edomites now.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wonder why Timna became the concubine of Eliphaz? or
  • I do not like Timna because she was the mother of the Amalekites; or
  • I do not care about these people.

Continue reciting the list of names.

The sons of Shobal were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam. The sons of Zibeon were Aiah and Anah. The son of Anah was Dishon. The sons of Dishon were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Keran. The sons of Ezer were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan. The sons of Dishan were Uz and Aran.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope my audience is still listening; or
  • I am glad that my people have recorded this important information; or
  • It is good to remember that God calls our people to be a blessing to these other people.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wish I knew more about these people; or
  • It is sad that these people lost their land and their homes; or
  • I wonder what happened to the descendants of these people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:38–42 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already decided how to translate these names.

Stop here and listen to the middle part of Genesis chapter 36 that lists the descendants of Seir, if it is available in your language. Pause this audio here.

Remember that sometimes, the word sons in the original language refers to the male children of a parent, and sometimes it refers to a group of descendants. In this passage, sons refers to individual male children of a parent. Translate "sons" in the same way you have translated it in the previous passage. For more information about sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

The sons of Seir were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. The name Seir means "rough," as in "not smooth or soft." The name Lotan means "covering." The name Shobal means "overflowing." The name Zibeon means "striped." The name Anah probably means "an answer." The name Dishon probably means "one who leaps" or "antelope." The name Ezer means "treasure." The name Dishan probably means the same thing as Dishon, "antelope."

Stop here and listen again to the part of Genesis chapter 36 that lists the sons of Seir. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

The sons of Lotan were Hori and Homam. Lotan's sister was Timna. The name Hori means "burning anger." The name Homam means "destroying." The name Timna means "restraint."

Stop here and listen again to the part of Genesis chapter 36 that lists the sons of Lotan and Lotan's sister Timna. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

The sons of Shobal were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam. The sons of Zibeon were Aiah and Anah. The son of Anah was Dishon. The name Alvan means "high or lofty." The name Manahath means "rest." The name Ebal means "bald or bare." The name Shepho means "a bare hill or plain." The name Onam means "strong." The name Aiah means "hawk or vulture." The name Anah means "an answer." The name Dishon probably means "one who leaps" or "antelope."

Stop here and listen again to the part of Genesis chapter 36 that lists the sons of Shobal, Zibeon, and Anah. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

The sons of Dishon were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Keran. The sons of Ezer were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan. The sons of Dishan were Uz and Aran. The name Hemdan means "red." The name Eshban means "energetic." The name Ithran means "excellent." We are not certain what the name Keran means. The name Bilhan means "timid." The name Zaavan means "restless." The name Akan means "twisting." The name Uz means "to get advice." The name Aran means "to shout."

Stop here and listen again to the part of Genesis chapter 36 that lists the sons of Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them?

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 1:43–54
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 1:43–54 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:43–54 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:43–54 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller told us the names of some of the descendants of Seir the Horite. Those descendants were the original people who lived in Edom before Esau's descendants settled there. In this passage, the storyteller tells us about the kings who ruled in Edom after Esau's descendants settled there and before Israel had any kings. The storyteller also tells us the names of some tribal chiefs of the Edomites.

The land of Edom was south of Israel. The storyteller mentions several cities in this story, but apart from Bozrah, Bible scholars are not sure where any of these cities were. The city of Bozrah was so strongly connected with the land of Edom that sometimes in the Bible, Bozrah represents all of Edom.

Stop here and look at a map of Edom that shows the location of Israel and the location of the city of Bozrah. Pause this audio here.

The kings of Edom, who the storyteller listed here, are probably descendants of Esau. The storyteller tells us the name of each king, the name of that king's father, and where that king is from. In a few cases, we get a little extra information about each king. The storyteller makes it clear that the position of King of Edom did not pass from father to son, which is rather unusual for this area of the world. We do not know what the relationship of each king was to the king before him or the king after him.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, how do you choose your leaders? Do positions of leadership ever pass from one family member to another family member? Is your way of choosing leaders now different from how it was in the past? If so, talk about how your people used to choose their leaders. Pause this audio here.

In the last part of this passage, the storyteller of Chronicles gives us a list of names of Edomites. Some Bible scholars think these are the names of individual people who were chiefs of Edom. A chief is a person who is a leader in the community, but not the leader of the entire people group. Normally, the leader of an entire people group is called a king. A chief might be the leader of an extended family, the leader of a tribe, or the leader of the people in a certain geographical area, like a city. The people might choose a chief, or the position of chief might pass from one family member to another.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Talk about the different kinds of leaders that exist in your people group. Why does your group have these different kinds of leaders? What kind of authority does each kind of leader have? Which of these leaders might be similar to a chief? Pause this audio here.

Some Bible scholars do not agree that these are the names of individual people. Rather, those scholars think these might be the names of clans or tribes of Edomites. We know that the Israelites had tribes, such as the tribe of Judah. The Edomites might have had a similar system. A clan or tribe is a group of people that is larger than a family, but where the people still see themselves as related in some way.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, do you organize families into larger groups like clans or tribes? If so, how does each person know which clan or tribe he or she belongs to? How do you determine who the leader of the clan or tribe is? What are the reasons why your group organizes people into clans or tribes? Does each clan or tribe have any special jobs or responsibilities?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:43–54 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us the names of the eight Edomite kings. The storyteller also tells us the names of the fathers of most of the kings, as well as the city or region that the king came from.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells us the names of 11 chiefs or clans of Edom who descended from Esau.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Bela, Beor, Jobab, Zerah, Husham, Hadad, Bedad, Samlah, Shaul, Baal-Hanan, Acbor, Hadad, Mehetabel, Matred, Me-Zahab
  • And Timna, Alvah, Jetheth, Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon, Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar, Magdiel, and Iram

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated this story about the kings and chiefs of Edom. It would probably be helpful to listen again to that story to remind you how you translated the names in this story.

Stop here and listen again to the final part of Genesis chapter 36, which contains this story about the kings and chiefs of Edom, if it is available in your language. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the storyteller tells us the names of eight kings of Edom. The storyteller makes it clear that the kings are not fathers and sons. After each king dies, another man becomes king and rules in his place. This means that the next man took on all the activities and the authority that comes with being the king. It does not necessarily mean that each king lived in the same city as the previous kings, the way that all of the Israelite kings ruled in Jerusalem. Bible scholars do not agree on whether the Edomites had one capital city throughout the reigns of these eight kings, or if different kings ruled from different cities.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What is the most natural way in your language to say that one person took over the position of another person? How will you say that each Edomite king took over the position of king from the man who came before him? Pause this audio here.

In this story, the fourth king of Edom is named Hadad, and the eighth king of Edom is also named Hadad.

Stop here and remind yourselves of the discussion you had before about different people with the same name. How will you make it clear to your audience that there are two different men named Hadad in this story? Pause this audio here.

Most of the Edomite kings came from a different city or different region in Edom. For example, the first king, Bela, was from a city called Dinhabah, while the third king, Husham, was from a part of Edom called "the land of the Temanites." The storyteller tells us where each king of Edom is from except for Baal-Hanan.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how do you describe where a person is from? Pause this audio here.

The fourth king of Edom was named Hadad. In this section, the storyteller tells us that Hadad defeated Midian in the country of Moab. Here, "Midian" probably refers to the army of the Midianite people, who were descendants of Abraham through his concubine Keturah. This may seem confusing because the territory of the Midianites was south of Edom, while the country of Moab was north of Edom. The people who lived in Moab were the descendants of Abraham's nephew Lot. So apparently around this time, the Edomites, Moabites, and Midianites were all fighting with each other in Moab's territory, and Hadad's army defeated the Midianites' army.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the nations of Moab, Edom, and Midian in relation to Israel. Pause this audio here.

The eighth king of Edom was also named Hadad. The storyteller of Chronicles gives us some information about Hadad's wife, Mehetabel. The storyteller tells us that Mehetabel is "the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-Zahab." It is not clear if this means that Matred and Me-Zahab are Mehetabel's mother and father, or if it means that Mehetabel's mother was Matred, and Me-Zahab was the mother or father of Matred. To put it another way, we know that Matred was one of Mehetabel's parents, but we do not know if Me-Zahab was Mehetabel's other parent or one of Mehetabel's grandparents. Bible scholars do not know if Matred is the name of a woman or a man, and we also do not know this for the name Me-Zahab.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Does your language have names that can be a name for both a male and a female? If so, give some examples. How could you translate the names Matred and Me-Zahab so that they could be the names of either a woman or a man? Pause this audio here.

It is unusual for a Biblical storyteller to describe someone the way they describe Mehetabel. Because it is so unusual, some Bible scholars think that perhaps Me-Zahab is not a person but rather a place, and that Mehetabel and/or Matred came from the place called Me-Zahab. This explanation would fit the pattern that the storyteller has used so far in this passage to name a king, that king's father, and the place he was from. Perhaps Mehetabel was from such an important family that the storyteller felt it was necessary to mention her name, her father's name and the place they came from.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your language, can the name of a place also be the name of a person? If so, give examples of places and people that share the same name. Pause this audio here.

Since we do not know if Matred and Me-Zahab are male or female, if Me-Zahab was Mehetabel's parent or grandparent, or if Me-Zahab was a person's name or a place name, you should keep these things uncertain in your translation. The best option would probably be to follow the original language as closely as possible to describe Mehetabel as "the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezehab."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the phrase "Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezehab." Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller shifts his focus to naming the chiefs or clans of the Edomites. As we mentioned before, Bible scholars do not agree on whether these are the names of individuals who were chiefs of Edom, or if they are the names of clans of Edomites.

If these are the names of individual chiefs, then there are some interesting things to note. A chief is a leader of a smaller group of people in the community, such as an extended family or possibly people from a certain area. In the list of Edomite chiefs, there is a chief named Timna and a chief named Oholibamah. You may remember that Timna was the name of the wife of Eliphaz, and Oholibamah was the name of one of Esau's wives. Bible scholars do not know if the Timna and Oholibamah in this passage are men who had the same names as those women, or if Edomite society allowed women to be chiefs. In Bible times, it was unusual for women to have a position like a chief, but it was also not completely impossible.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, what positions of leadership do women have? Are there any positions of leadership that women are not allowed to have? Pause this audio here.

Bible scholars who believe that these are the names of Edomite chiefs do not agree on whether the chiefs of Edom led their groups at the same time that the king of Edom ruled the entire country, or if the kings of Edom were the leaders for a time and then chiefs replaced them. It is possible that the chiefs existed at the same time as a king and cooperated with the king to lead the nation. But it is also possible that kings ruled Edom first and that chiefs later replaced those kings.

In this scene, the word for "chief" or "clan" appears with the name of each chief. In some languages, this amount of repetition may be unnatural. If this is the case, you may choose to introduce the list of chiefs and then list the names, without repeating the word "chief" before each name.

Stop here and discuss this question: Will you include the word for "chief" or "clan" with each name on this list, or will you just list the names? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller of Chronicles repeats at the end of the passage that these were the chiefs of Edom. You may keep this repetition if it is natural in your language.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:43–54 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Bela, Beor, Jobab, Zerah, Husham, Hadad, Bedad, Samlah, Shaul, Baal-Hanan, Acbor, Hadad, Mehetabal, Matred, Me-Zahab
  • And Timna, Alvah, Jetheth, Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon, Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar, Magdiel, and Iram

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, think about how the ancient Israelites might have felt while they listened to these lists of names. The kings and chiefs of Edom were these people's relatives, the descendants of Jacob's brother Esau. Because Esau sold his birthright to Jacob, there was bitterness and conflict between the two brothers and their descendants. Yet God blessed Esau with wealth, territory, and descendants—even descendants who became kings!

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the passage, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue reading or reciting the passage.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the passage and possible responses.

These are the kings who ruled in the land of Edom before any king ruled over the Israelites: Bela, the son of Beor, of the city of Dinhabah. When Bela died, Jobab, the son of Zerah, of Bozrah, ruled in his place. When Jobab died, Husham of the land of the Temanites ruled in his place. When Husham died, Hadad, the son of Bedad, who was from the city of Avith, ruled in his place. Hadad destroyed the Midianite army in the land of Moab.

Pause the reciting here. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wish we knew the story of how Hadad defeated the Midianites; or
  • Some of these kings of Edom treated our ancestors badly, so I do not like them; or
  • It is important to remember the descendants of our brother Esau.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that Esau's descendants had their own country and leaders; or
  • I wish our relationship with the Edomites was better; or
  • I do not like the Edomites.

Continue reciting the names.

After Hadad died, the rest of the Edomite kings were Samlah from Masrekah, Shaul from Rehoboth-on-the-river, Baal-Hanan the son of Acbor and Hadad from the city of Pai, whose wife's name was Mehetabel. She was the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-Zahab.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder how the Edomites decided who the next king would be; or
  • I want my people to remember this important history; or
  • No matter how we feel about the Edomites, we must remember that God told us to be a blessing to all people.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I really want to know more about these people; or
  • I am angry at the Edomites for the way they treated our people; or
  • I am sad when I think about how the Babylonians killed and overthrew the Edomites, just like they did to our ancestors.

Continue reciting the names.

Then Hadad died. The names of the clans of Edom were Timna, Alvah, Jetheth, Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon, Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar, Magdiel, and Iram.

Pause. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am curious about who these people were; or
  • It is important to remember these people because they are our brothers; or
  • I wish these people were not related to us.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel impatient to get past this and onto the information about our ancestors; or
  • I do not think this information is important to know; or
  • We should forgive the Edomites for the conflicts we have had with them.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 1:43–54 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated this story about the kings and chiefs of Edom.

Stop here and listen again to the final part of Genesis chapter 36, which contains this story about the kings and chiefs of Edom. Pause this audio here.

Throughout this passage, son means the individual male child of a person. Use the same word or phrase for son that you have used in previous passages, and see the Master Glossary for more information about son.

In this passage, the storyteller tells us about the kings who ruled Edom before there were any kings in Israel. A king is a ruler of a city or a territory.

Stop here and discuss what word you will use to translate the word "king." Look up king in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The first king of Edom was Bela son of Beor. Bela's city was called Dinhabah. A city is a large settlement that usually has a wall around it to protect the people.

Stop here and decide what word or phrase you will use for city. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah became king. When Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites became king. The name Bela means "destruction." The name Beor means "a light." We do not know what Dinhabah's name means. The name Jobab means "to yell." The name Zerah means "to appear." The name Bozrah means "sheep pasture." The name Husham means "hurried." The name Teman means "south."

Stop here and listen again to the first part of this story in Genesis chapter 36. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

When Husham died, Hadad son of Bedad from the city of Avith became king. Hadad was the one who destroyed the army of Midian in the land of Moab. We do not know what Hadad's name means. The name Bedad means "to divide." The name Avith means "ruin." Be sure to translate "Midian" and "Moab" the same way that you have translated them before.

Stop here and listen again to this part of the story in Genesis chapter 36. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

When Hadad died, Samlah from Masrekah became king. When Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth-on-the-river became king. When Shaul died, Baal-Hanan son of Acbor became king. The name Samlah means "covering." The name Masrekah means "vineyard." The name Shaul is the same as the name of King Saul of Israel, which means "to ask." Be sure to translate this name the same way you have translated it before. The name Rehoboth means "broad places," and the city was probably next to a major river in the area. The name Baal-Hanan means "one who has grace." The name Acbor means "attacking."

Stop here and listen again to this part of the story in Genesis chapter 36. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

When Baal-Hanan died, Hadad became king. Hadad's city was called Pau. Hadad's wife was named Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-Zahab. We do not know what Hadad's name means. We do not know what Pau's name means. The name Mehetabel probably means "God made it better." The name Matred means "moving forward." The name Mezahab probably means "gold waters."

Stop here and listen again to this part of the story in Genesis chapter 36. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

The chiefs of Edom were Timna, Alvah, Jetheth, Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon, Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar, Magdiel, and Iram. The name Timna means "restraint." The name Alvah means "immorality." We do not know what Jetheth's name means. The name Oholibamah means "a high tent." The name Elah means "a strong tree." The name Pinon means "confusion." The name Kenaz means "hunter." The name Teman means "south." The name Mibzar means "fortress." The name Magdiel means "preciousness of God." The name Iram means "related to a city."

Stop here and listen again to this part of the story in Genesis chapter 36. Pay close attention to how you translated the names. Did you translate the names according to the meaning, or according to how they sounded? Do you like the way you translated these names, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

You will have to decide if you want to say that these are the names of chiefs of the Edomites or the names of clans of the Edomites. Either choice is acceptable. A clan is a group of related families within a people group. A chief is a leader of a clan or tribe.

Stop here and discuss whether you want to say that these are the names of chiefs or clans.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:1–8
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 2:1–8 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:1–8 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller told us the names of some of the leaders of the Edomites, who were descendants of Isaac's son Esau. In this passage, the storyteller begins a new section of the book in which he tells us about the sons of Isaac's son Jacob. Yahweh gave Jacob the name Israel.

In this section, the storyteller introduces a new pattern of giving us information. After the storyteller tells us the names of the twelve sons of Israel, he immediately tells us about the family of Israel's fourth son, Judah. To the storyteller of Chronicles, Judah's line of descent is the most important because Judah's line led to King David. Yahweh promised David that one of David's descendants would always sit on the throne of Israel. So many people think that David's family line is the most important family line of all the Israelites. The storyteller of Chronicles will spend most of the rest of the book talking about the actions of King David and his descendants.

You might notice that when we see a list of the 12 sons of Jacob in the Bible, the list is not always in the same order. Sometimes the storyteller lists the sons in the order of their birth. Other times, the storyteller lists them in groups of brothers born to the same mother, with the oldest brothers always listed first.
