Remember that Jacob had two wives and two concubines, and all four of them gave birth to Jacob's children. In this list, the storyteller names the first four sons of Jacob, who were born to Leah, in order of their birth. After that, the storyteller switches between birth order and grouping by mother a few times. Bible scholars do not know why the storyteller of Chronicles chose to list the sons of Jacob in this way.

In this passage, the storyteller of Chronicles assumes that his audience already knows the details of the story of Judah's family, so the storyteller quickly summarizes the most important points. Judah had three sons with his wife, who is called Bathshua in this story. Judah and Bathshua's sons were called Er, Onan, and Shelah. Judah's family was not perfect. Judah's oldest son, Er, was an evil man, so Yahweh killed Er.

Stop here and tell a story in your language about someone who did something bad and how someone in authority punished that person for that bad thing. Did that person get what he or she deserved? Why or why not? How do you feel about that person's punishment? Pause this audio here.

Later, Judah had sexual relations with Er's widow, Tamar, and she became pregnant. Tamar gave birth to Perez and Zerah. So Judah had a total of five sons. While this story about Judah and Tamar might sound strange and even disturbing to modern hearers, Bible scholars believe it may have been relatively normal in the surrounding Canaanite culture. Bible scholars have discovered legal documents from the Hittite culture that say that if a widow's brother-in-law was not available to produce a male son to inherit the property, the father-in-law was required to do so.

Stop here and discuss this question: How do you take care of widows in your culture? Who is responsible for the well-being of a widow after her husband dies? What kind of things might happen to a widow if no one takes care of her properly? What responsibilities does the husband's family have to the widow? What responsibilities does the widow have to her husband's family? How are these practices different from the other people around you? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller does not tell us if Judah had any daughters. Remember that to the Israelites, the family line through the father was most important. So a list of family members does not usually contain the names of women unless there is something significant or unusual about the woman. In this story, the storyteller probably tells us the names of Bathshua and Tamar because the way that Tamar got pregnant with Judah's son Perez was unusual in Israelite culture. Importantly, the family line of Perez is the family line that led to King David.

In this passage, the storyteller also reminds us of the story of Achar, son of Carmi, who was a descendant of Judah's son Zerah. When the Israelites were fighting to conquer the Canaanites, Yahweh told them to kill everyone in Jericho except for Rahab and her family. Yahweh also told the Israelites to destroy everything they found except for valuable items that people made of gold, silver, bronze, and iron. Those valuable items were supposed to go into Yahweh's treasury. But Achar stole some goods from Jericho and hid them in his tent. This made Yahweh angry, and the Israelites lost a battle. When Joshua, the military commander, asked Yahweh why the Israelites lost the battle, Yahweh told Joshua that an Israelite had stolen goods from Jericho. Joshua found out that Achar was the one who stole the goods, and the Israelites killed Achar and his family. This is why Achar is called "a troubler of Israel," because Achar's selfish and greedy actions caused the Israelites to lose a battle.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who did something selfish, and how other people suffered because of that action. What did people think about the person who committed the selfish act? Did that person receive any punishment for what he or she did? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also tells us about a son of Ethan named Azariah. Bible scholars do not know why the storyteller of Chronicles tells us about Azariah here. Although there are many people in the Bible with the name Azariah, none of them is the son of Ethan. The storyteller of Chronicles might have assumed that his audience knew the story of Azariah son of Ethan from another source, but we do not find that story in the Bible.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us the names of the 12 sons of Jacob.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells us the names of Judah's five sons, as well as the names of their mothers.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells us the names of some of the descendants of Judah's sons Perez and Zerah.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Israel, Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, Asher
  • Bathshua, Er, Onan, Shelah, Tamar, Perez, Zerah
  • And Hezron, Hamul, Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, Darda, Achar, Carmi, and Azariah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller lists the sons of Israel. In this case, the word "sons" means male "children" and not descendants. We know that Jacob, Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and all the rest are specific individuals. If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated this list of the sons of Jacob. It would be helpful to go back and listen to that list to remind you of how you chose to translate all the names.

Stop here and listen to the final part of Genesis chapter 36, after the death of Rachel, which lists the sons of Jacob. Pay close attention to how you translated the sons' names. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller of Chronicles refers to Jacob by the name "Israel" in this part of the story. We have discussed before that although Israel is the name of an individual, it is also the name people use for all the descendants of Jacob, the Israelites. Sometimes this can confuse listeners. It might be more clear to refer to Jacob by the name his father gave him at birth when Jacob is a character in a story, like he is here.

Stop here and remind each other of your previous discussion about whether to call Jacob by the name Israel or the name Jacob. Pause this audio here.

In scene two, the storyteller focuses on telling us about the family of Judah, Jacob's fourth son. If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have translated a much longer version of this story. It would be helpful to go back and listen to that version to remind you of how you chose to translate all the names.

Stop here and listen to Genesis chapter 38, if it is available in your language. Pay close attention to how you translated the names of Judah's sons, Judah's wife, and Tamar. Pause this audio here.

The story of Judah's family in 1 Chronicles is shorter than it is in Genesis. We hear little more than the names of Judah's sons and their mothers. Actually, Bible scholars do not agree about whether Judah's wife's name was Bathshua. In the original language, Bathshua means "daughter of Shua," and so it was actually a way of identifying her father, Shua. It is possible that people may have called her by that name, but we do not know for sure.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your language, do you have a way to name a person according to the name of that person's father or mother? If so, how do you feel about using that system to name people in this case? Pause this audio here.

Judah had three sons with his wife. Judah named these sons Er, Onan, and Shelah.

Stop here and discuss this question: What is the most natural way in your language to say that a woman gave birth to a man's child? Pause this audio here.

Judah's firstborn son, Er, married Tamar. But Er was evil in the sight of Yahweh. This means that Er did evil things and Yahweh saw that those things were evil. Some translations may explain this by saying that Er displeased Yahweh, Er disobeyed Yahweh and did what was evil, or Er was a wicked man. We do not know what Er did, but we do know that the evil things that Er did were so bad that Yahweh killed Er before Er had a chance to have children.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that Er was evil in the sight of Yahweh, or that Yahweh saw the evil things that Er did. Pause this audio here.

From the Genesis story, we know that Er and Onan, Judah's two oldest sons, died before they had any children. Tamar was supposed to marry Shelah, Judah's youngest son, but when Shelah was old enough to marry, Judah did not give him to Tamar. Because of this, Tamar tricked Judah into having sex with her, and Tamar became pregnant. This is how Perez and Zerah were born.

In the third scene, the storyteller focuses on telling us about some of the descendants of Judah's sons Perez and Zerah. First, the storyteller names the sons of Perez and Zerah. Then the storyteller reminds us of the story of Zerah's descendant Achar. If you have already translated the book of Joshua, then you have already translated the long version of this story. If the story is available in your language, it would be helpful to go back and listen to it to remind you of the story and how you translated the names of Achar, Carmi, Zimri, and Zerah.

Stop here and listen to Joshua chapter seven if it is available in your language. Pay attention to how you translated the names of Achar, Carmi, Zimri, and Zerah. Pause this audio here.

You will notice that in the passage in Joshua, the storyteller calls Achar "Achan." You may use the name Achan in this passage if it will make more sense to your audience. In this passage, the storyteller of Chronicles calls Achar "the troubler of Israel." This means that Achar's actions brought trouble on everyone in Israel at that time. Because Achar disobeyed Yahweh, some Israelite soldiers died, the Israelite army lost the first battle of Ai, and all the people were terrified. The storyteller of Chronicles assumed that his audience knew the story of Achar and knew exactly how Achar caused trouble for Israel. But this might not be true for modern audiences who listen to the story. Instead of giving Achar a title like "The Troubler of Israel," some translations choose to describe the effect that Achar's actions had on the Israelites. Those translations might say something like "Achar, who brought disaster on Israel" or "Achar, who brought doom on the people of Israel." This provides a little more context and might help people understand the verse more clearly.

Stop here and discuss whether you will use a title like "The Troubler of Israel" or whether you will use a phrase to describe how Achar's actions affected the Israelites. Pause this audio here.

When Achar took the items from Jericho, he disobeyed Yahweh. But the word in the original language has an even stronger meaning than "to disobey." This word can mean "to act unfaithfully or treacherously," "to betray," or "to violate an agreement." The word definitely describes a person who chooses to do something that he has already agreed not to do. In some Bible passages, the storyteller uses this word to describe the situation when a married man or woman commits adultery. Just like a husband or wife who acts unfaithfully toward his or her marriage partner, Achar acted unfaithfully toward Yahweh. The idea is not just of a morally wrong action, but a personal betrayal.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who betrayed someone else and caused something bad to happen. Pay attention to the words you use to describe the betrayal. Which of these words might be best to use here? Pause this audio here.

In the original story in Joshua chapter seven, Yahweh told the Israelites to destroy everything in Jericho except for a list of items that the Israelites were supposed to save and put into Yahweh's treasury. This list included items that people had made from silver, gold, bronze, and iron. The people called these things "devoted things," "condemned things," or "plunder that had been set apart for God." The word for these items implies that the people could only use them to worship Yahweh. This is why they are called devoted things.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your language, how do you describe things that people put aside for one special use only? How do you describe things that people put aside for religious use only? Which of these words might be appropriate here? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Israel, Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, Asher
  • Bathshua, Er, Onan, Shelah, Tamar, Perez, Zerah
  • And Hezron, Hamul, Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, Darda, Achar, Carmi, and Azariah

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, think about how the ancient Israelites might have felt as they listened to these names and stories. It is likely that every single ancient Israelite knew the names of the sons of Jacob because they were also the names of the tribes of Israel. They were the names of the people who inherited the promises that Yahweh made to Abraham. Every single Israelite knew which tribe he or she belonged to and probably knew the names of all their ancestors, starting with the founder of the tribe. Furthermore, the ancestry of King David was very important because this family line fulfilled ancient prophecy. The descendants of David were not only the rulers of Israel but also the family line from which the Messiah would come. All the hope of Israel depended on this royal line of descent.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the passage, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue reading or reciting the passage.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the passage and possible responses.

The sons of Jacob were Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so glad we are finally talking about our own people; or
  • I feel proud to be a descendant of Jacob; or
  • I feel sad when I remember all the struggle and pain our ancestors went through; or
  • I wish our ancestors could have been completely unified. Then our fate might have turned out differently.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am happy that I know all of these names; or
  • I feel proud when I hear my tribe's name here; or
  • This reminds me of someone from another tribe, and I do not like that person.

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Judah had three sons with his wife Bathshua, a Canaanite woman. Those sons were named Er, Onan, and Shelah. Er displeased Yahweh, so Yahweh put him to death. Then Tamar, the widow of Er, Judah's daughter-in-law, gave birth to Judah's sons Perez and Zerah. So Judah had a total of five sons.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder what Er did that was so bad that Yahweh decided to kill him; or
  • It is important for our people to know the lineage of King David; or
  • I feel proud that I am teaching our people these important things about our history.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wonder why Judah's son Er was such a terrible man; or
  • I am disturbed by the fact that Judah got his daughter-in-law pregnant; or
  • I wonder why Yahweh picked the tribe of Judah for the king to come from. The people of Judah are not very good people.

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Perez had two sons named Hezron and Hamul. Perez's brother Zerah had five sons: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Darda. One of Zerah's descendants was Achar son of Carmi. Achar brought disaster on the people of Israel by keeping plunder that Israel had devoted to Yahweh. Ethan had one son, Azariah.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder why Achar thought he could get away with stealing the devoted things; or
  • I hope my audience will remember all these names; or
  • I am glad that my people have recorded these important aspects of our history.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wish Achar had not done what he did; or
  • I wonder who Ethan's son Azariah was and what he did; or
  • I feel excited that we are getting closer to talking about King David.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Israel had 12 sons: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. The name Israel means "struggles with God." The name Reuben means "a son." The name Simeon means "heard." The name Levi means "attached." The name Judah means "celebrate or praise." The name Issachar means "reward." The name Zebulun means "place to live." The name Dan means "to judge." The name Joseph means "may he add." The name Benjamin means "son of the right hand." The name Naphtali means "my wrestling." The name Gad means "luck." The name Asher means "happy."

Stop here and listen to the final part of Genesis chapter 36, after Rachel's death, which lists the sons of Jacob. Pay close attention to how you translated the sons' names. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated them, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

Judah's wife is called Bathshua. This name means "daughter of Shua." The name Shua means either "freedom" or "wealth." Bathshua was a Canaanite. Be sure to translate Canaanite the same way that you have in previous passages. See Canaan in the Master Glossary for more information on Canaanite.

Judah's sons with Bathshua were named Er, Onan, and Shelah. The name Er means "watchful." The name Onan means "strong." The name Shelah means "request."

Judah's oldest son, Er, was evil, so Yahweh killed Er. Be sure to translate evil the same way that you have in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on evil.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the word "evil." Pause this audio here.

Yahweh is God's personal name. Be sure to translate Yahweh the same way you have in previous books of the Bible.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the name "Yahweh." See the Master Glossary for more information on Yahweh, and translate this name in the same way you have in previous books of the Bible. Pause this audio here.

Judah had two more sons with his daughter-in-law, Tamar. The names of their sons were Perez and Zerah. The name Tamar means "palm tree." The name Perez means "to break through." The name Zerah means "brightness."

Stop here and listen to Genesis chapter 38. Pay close attention to how you translated the names of Judah's family members. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated them, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for daughter-in-law. A daughter-in-law is the wife of a person's son. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul. The name Hezron means "courtyard." The name Hamul means "to have compassion on someone."

Stop here and listen to the middle of Genesis chapter 46, which lists the sons of Perez. Pay close attention to how you translated the names of Hezron and Hamul. Did you translate the names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated them, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

The sons of Zerah were Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Darda. The name Zimri means "musical or melodic." The name Ethan means "permanent or enduring." The name Heman means "faithful." The name Calcol means "nourishment." We do not know what Darda's name means. Achar son of Carmi was a descendant of Zimri. The name Achar means "trouble." The name Carmi means "gardener."

Stop here and listen to the beginning of Joshua chapter seven if you have it in your language. Pay close attention to how you translated the names Achan, Carmi, Zimri and Zerah. Did you translate them according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated them, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

Achar brought disaster on the Israelites by transgressing in the matter of the devoted things.

When someone transgresses, they act unfaithfully to betray someone, to disobey an order, or to break an agreement. If you have already translated this word in a previous book of the Bible, be sure to use the same word here as you did previously.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the word transgress. Pause this audio here.

Devoted things are objects that people set aside for a specific purpose, usually for a religious purpose. In the story of Achar the "Troubler of Israel," people were supposed to put the devoted things into Yahweh's treasury.

Stop here and decide how you will translate devoted things. Pause this audio here.

Ethan's son was named Azariah. The name Azariah means "Yahweh has helped."

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:9–17
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, Yahweh saw all that King Solomon and the people of Israel were doing. When the priests placed the Ark of the Covenant in the Most Holy Place, Yahweh responded by filling the Temple with a thick cloud. In this passage, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh in a prayer and then addresses the people of Israel in response to Yahweh's presence in the Temple. Stop here and review the photo of King Solomon's Temple. Pause this audio here.

This passage is almost exactly the same as 1 Kings 8:12–21 that you may have translated previously.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If you have already translated 1 Kings 8:12–21, listen to your translation of that passage. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon begins his prayer by acknowledging words that Yahweh has spoken previously. In the book of Leviticus, Yahweh spoke to Moses and told Moses not to allow his brother Aaron to go into the Most Holy Place. Yahweh also said that Yahweh would dwell in a dark cloud in the Holy Place over the Ark of the Covenant. This was a sign that Yahweh was pleased and was present in the Tabernacle. When Yahweh shows up in a dark and thick cloud this time, Yahweh is doing the same thing as He did in the time of Moses. King Solomon acknowledges this, and King Solomon tells Yahweh that King Solomon has built a magnificent Temple for Yahweh to dwell in forever. While King Solomon says he built it, King Solomon represents all of the people who helped build the Temple, as King Solomon did not build it by himself. Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember that the people of Israel likely knew the story of Moses and Yahweh's presence in the Tabernacle. How do you think they felt when they saw this happen? How would you feel if you saw this with your own eyes instead of only hearing about it? Pause this audio here.

After King Solomon prays to and acknowledges Yahweh, King Solmon then turns away from the Temple and faces the people of Israel. The storyteller uses the word 'assembly' to describe the people. The use of assembly most likely means that King Solomon is standing before all of the tribes of people of Israel and not just the priests and Levites. King Solomon then blesses the people. Again, King Solomon does the same act that Moses did at the Tabernacle. King Solomon sees all that has happened, and King Solomon honors Yahweh and then speaks Yahweh's blessings over the people of Israel. Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the most important or famous person in your country or beyond. This could be a king or leader. How would you feel if this person acknowledged you and spoke kind words to you? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon begins his prayer and blessing by praising the Lord, the God of Israel. In this case, the Lord and God are the same person, and King Solomon is praising Yahweh. King Solomon gives a specific reason for offering these praises. King Solomon says that Yahweh has fulfilled Yahweh's promises through Yahweh's hands. The storyteller is not saying that Yahweh's actual hands built the Temple. But, the storyteller is likely saying that Yahweh made it possible through Yahweh's power and strength for King Solomon and the people to complete the Temple. The storyteller also says that the promise that Yahweh fulfilled through the Temple completion is exactly what Yahweh told King Solomon's father, King David, that Yahweh would do. King Solomon likely wants to remind the people of Yahweh's faithfulness and trustworthiness. Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you use imagery to describe something? For example, King Solomon talks about the 'hand of Yahweh' in this passage, but King Solomon doesn't mean Yahweh's actual hand. How do you use imagery in your culture? Pause this audio here.

In the next section, King Solomon speaks words that Yahweh has already spoken. These words are not King Solomon's interpretation of Yahweh's words. King Solomon is quoting Yahweh exactly by saying the following words:

"Since the day I brought my people Israel out of Egypt, I have not chosen a city in any tribe of Israel to have a Temple built so that my name might be there, nor have I chosen anyone to be ruler over my people Israel. But, now I have chosen Jerusalem for my name to be there, and I have chosen David to rule my people Israel." Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Refer back to the 1 Kings 8:12–21 translation. What is different about this section? In your culture, how often do people tell stories that still tell the same story with minor differences? Pause this audio here.

In this part of the passage, King Solomon likely shares what Yahweh said to King David, because these words help explain why King David did not build the Temple sooner. Yahweh wanted King David to rule over the people of Israel, but Yahweh did not give King David permission or instructions for building a Temple. Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Imagine that you are King David. How would you feel if you were king and you wanted to build a Temple, but Yahweh would not let you? Describe your feelings and thoughts. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then explains that his father, King David, 'had it in his heart' to build a Temple for Yahweh. In the Hebrew language, the heart usually means the same as the mind. King David wanted to build a Temple, but this was likely more of a thought than an emotional response. King David desired to build the Temple to honor Yahweh's name. Again, King Solomon says, "Lord, God of Israel", and King Solomon is using two names to describe Yahweh.

King Solomon returns to quoting Yahweh when he says Yahweh's words:

"You did well to have it in your heart to build a temple for my Name. Nevertheless, you are not the one to build the temple, but your son, your own flesh and blood—he is the one who will build the temple for my Name."

In this part of the passage, King Solomon reminds the people of Israel why King Solomon has built the Temple. Yahweh did not let King David build the Temple, even though Yahweh loved King David and King David wanted to build the Temple to honor Yahweh's name. However, Yahweh did have a plan. Yahweh told King David that King David's son, King Solomon would build the Temple. Yahweh makes sure that King David understands the word 'son' when he says that David's 'flesh and blood' would be the one to build the Temple. By sharing Yahweh's words, King Solomon reminds the people of Israel that Yahweh is faithful and is in control of all things, including time. Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Imagine a time where you thought someone would choose you for a special role. Imagine that the person chooses someone else. Share with the group how you would feel? Pause this audio here.

After King Solomon reminds the people of what Yahweh said, King Solomon then confirms that what Yahweh said has happened. Yahweh has kept all of Yahweh's promises, and King Solomon wants the people to see this. King Solomon then lists specific promises, including that King Solomon has become king after his father, King David, and King Solomon sits on the throne and rules over the people of Israel. King Solomon also lists that he has built the Temple for Yahweh. Again, King Solomon says that he has built the Temple for the "Lord, God of Israel". It is important to remember that King Solomon is not saying that he built the Temple by himself. This means that King Solomon commissioned people to build the Temple and watched over the process.

Stop here and look at a picture of a throne. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think King Solomon repeats Yahweh's promises? What are some reasons why it's helpful to repeat stories or phrases? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon finishes this prayer and blessing by explaining that King Solomon has built a place within the Temple for the Ark of the Covenant. It is important to remember that the Ark of the Covenant was where Yahweh rested. When Yahweh rested on the ark, Israel experienced victory in battles. It is also good to remember the previous passage where Yahweh's presence filled the Temple after the priests placed the Ark of the Covenant in the Temple. King Solomon also acknowledges the covenant in the Ark. The covenant refers to the stone tablets in the Ark of the Covenant that act as a reminder of Yahweh's covenant with the people of Israel after Yahweh led the people of Israel out of Egypt. King Solomon reminded the people of Israel of all the promises Yahweh made to their ancestors and showed the people of Israel that Yahweh is keeping these promises at this moment. Stop here and look at a picture of the Ark of the Covenant. Pause this audio here. Stop here and look at a picture of the stone tablets. Pause this audio here. Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you pass information down through generations so that people will know what happened during their ancestors' lives? How do you keep a record of all that has happened? Pause this audio here.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has 2 scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon faces the Temple and speaks to Yahweh.

In the second scene: King Solomon turns around and blesses the people of Israel.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel
  • King David
  • People of Israel's Ancestors

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this entire passage, King Solomon is the only one who speaks. King Solomon speaks first to Yahweh and then to the people of Israel.

In the first scene, King Solomon faces the Temple and speaks to Yahweh. King Solomon reminds Yahweh, and anyone listening, that Yahweh previously said that he would live in a dark cloud. King Solomon is referring to another point in time when Yahweh spoke to Moses. King Solomon then offers the new Temple to Yahweh so that Yahweh can have a place to dwell in forever. The storyteller shows that King Solomon acknowledges Yahweh's previous words and offers a new idea to Yahweh instead.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how will you translate this part so that people will understand that King Solomon's words are repeated words from earlier passages? Decide how you will make sure to keep words and stories consistent throughout different books. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, King Solomon turns his attention to the people of Israel. The storyteller says that the people are standing as an assembly or group, so King Solomon is likely talking to people who are actively listening to him. The storyteller says that King Solomon blessed the people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what type or types of blessings do people speak over people? Think of examples of how people might offer words to people for comfort or to remind them of how they are blessed. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon changes back and forth in this section between the present day of the passage and a previous time when Yahweh spoke. King Solomon starts by praising the "Lord, the God of Israel" for helping King Solomon fulfill what Yahweh told King David that King Solomon would do. It is important to notice the small difference here from the parallel passage in 1 Kings 8:12–21. King Solomon says here that Yahweh's hands fulfilled Yahweh's promises.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you tell a story in your language and culture, how do you make it clear when you are changing between present day and past experiences? What words would you use to help make a person aware of the time changes? Also, how will you translate hands versus hand? Does this make a difference in your language? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then references what Yahweh said to King David. After the people of Israel left Egypt, Yahweh did not give a specific place for anyone to build a Temple and Yahweh had not chosen anyone to rule. However, eventually Yahweh did choose Jerusalem as the place where people would honor His name. Yahweh also blessed King David and chose King David to rule over the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how can you explain the imagery here when Yahweh says He chose Jerusalem for His name? How can you show this? Pause this audio here.

This part of this passage also differs slightly from the passage in 1 Kings 8:12–21. King Solomon then tells the people that King Solomon's father, King David, wanted to build a Temple for Yahweh. Then, King Solomon goes back to Yahweh's words that Yahweh spoke to King David. Yahweh told King David that Yahweh was pleased that King David wanted to build a Temple for Yahweh. However, Yahweh tells King David that King David will not be the one that builds the Temple. Instead, Yahweh chose King Solomon, King David's own 'flesh and blood', to build the Temple for Yahweh. In this case, flesh and blood means King David's son, not King David's own skin and blood.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how can you translate phrases like 'flesh and blood' so that people can understand the meaning of the imagery without picturing the words literally? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon finishes this prayer of blessing by making it clear to the people that Yahweh has answered and fulfilled the promises that Yahweh made to King David. King Solomon now sits on Israel's throne and rules over the people of Israel. King Solomon has also built the Temple for Yahweh and has placed the Ark of the Covenant in the Temple. King Solomon also reminds the people that the covenant is in the Ark of the Covenant. The covenant refers to the tablets which represent the covenant that Yahweh made with Moses and the people of Israel when Yahweh freed the people of Israel from Egypt. Again, pay close attention to this part of the passage, as it is slightly different to the parallel passage in 1 Kings 8:12–21. Stop here and look at a picture of a throne. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of the Ark of the Covenant. Pause this audio here. Stop here and look at a picture of the stone tablets. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 2 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel
  • King David
  • People of Israel's Ancestors

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage. Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama. Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The storyteller should read the drama.

After King Solomon built a Temple for Yahweh, King Solomon faced the Temple and spoke to Yahweh. King Solomon said that in a previous time, Yahweh said he would dwell in a dark cloud. But, King Solomon then says that King Solomon has built a Temple for Yahweh to dwell in forever.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • "I hope Yahweh approves of this Temple!"; or
  • "Look at how much better this Temple is than a cloud!"; or
  • "I feel proud of what I have accomplished.".

After King Solomon spoke to Yahweh, King Solomon turned around and faced all of the people of Israel that were standing in front of him. King Solomon blessed the people.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel , "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • "I must be quiet and listen to King Solomon!"; or
  • "I'm excited to hear what King Solomon is going to say to us!"; or
  • "Look at this beautiful Temple!".

Continue the drama.

King Solomon praises Yahweh by saying, "Praise be to the Lord, the God of Israel!" King Solomon then tells the people why he is praising Yahweh. King Solomon praises Yahweh because Yahweh has fulfilled the promise that Yahweh made to King David.

Pause this drama. Ask the person playing the Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • "I remember exactly what King Solomon is saying!"; or
  • "I love when King Solomon honors me!"; or
  • "It makes me happy when King Solomon sees what I have done for him!".

King Solomon speaks about what Yahweh did by reminding the people of Israel of the time that Yahweh led the people of Israel out of Egypt. After the people left Egypt, Yahweh did not choose a place anywhere in Israel for anyone to build a Temple, and Yahweh did not choose anyone to rule over the people of Israel. However, Yahweh eventually chose Jerusalem as the place where people would honor His name, and Yahweh chose King Solomon's father, King David, to rule the people of Israel.

Pause this drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • "I want the people to see Yahweh's faithfulness!"; or
  • "I am in awe of all Yahweh has done!"; or
  • "I wonder why Yahweh chose me!".

King Solomon then tells the people of Israel that his father, King David, wanted to build a Temple for Yahweh. King Solomon then tells the people of Israel what Yahweh said to King David. Yahweh told King David that King David was right to have a desire to build the Temple for Yahweh. But, Yahweh then tells King David that King David cannot be the person to build the Temple. Instead, Yahweh says that King Solomon will build the Temple.

Pause this drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • "I wish I could build the Temple!"; or
  • "I am thankful that my son will get to build the Temple"; or
  • "I am sad that I will not get to see the Temple".

King Solomon then finishes his blessing by declaring that Yahweh has fulfilled the promises that Yahweh made to King David and by listing the promises. King Solomon now sits on the throne of Israel and has built the Temple for Yahweh. King Solomon placed the Ark of the Covenant in the Temple with the stone tablets in the Ark to represent the covenant that Yahweh made when Yahweh led the people of Israel out of Egypt.

Pause this drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • "Wow! Look at all Yahweh has done!"; or
  • "I feel like I can trust Yahweh when He speaks!"; or
  • "I am glad that Yahweh approves of our king!".

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

After Yahweh's presence fills the Temple, King Solomon faces the Temple and speaks to Yahweh. King Solomon says that Yahweh previously said that He would dwell in a dark cloud. King Solomon says that he has built a magnificent Temple for Yahweh where Yahweh can dwell forever. Use the same word or phrase for Temple and cloud as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for dwell. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

After King Solomon speaks to Yahweh, King Solomon turns around and faces the assembly of Israel. Assembly likely means the people of Israel all together. King Solomon praises the Lord, the God of Israel and tells the people of Israel that Yahweh has fulfilled with His hands what Yahweh told King Solomon's father, King David with His mouth. Use the same word or phrase for father and assembly as you used in previous passages.

King Solomon then tells the people of Israel what Yahweh previously said to King David. Yahweh told King David that Yahweh had not chosen any city in any tribe of Israel for anyone to build a Temple and no one to rule over Israel. However, Yahweh did eventually choose for King David to rule over the people of Israel and for Jerusalem to be the place where people would honor Yahweh's name .

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for tribe and chosen. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon tells the people of Israel that King David wanted to build the Temple, and Yahweh approved of King David's desire to build the Temple. However, Yahweh told King David that King David could not build the Temple. Instead, Yahweh said that King David's son, King David's own flesh and blood, would build the Temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for flesh and blood. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then assures the people of Israel that Yahweh has kept His promises! King Solomon lists the promises by saying that King Solomon is seated on the throne after he succeeded King David. King Solomon also confirms that he has built the Temple like Yahweh promised. Finally, King Solomon reminds the people of Israel that King Solomon placed the Ark of the Covenant in the Temple. The Ark of the Covenant contains the stone tablets inside the Ark representing the covenant Yahweh made with the people's ancestors when Yahweh led the people of Israel's ancestors out of Egypt. Use the same words or phrases for promises, Ark of the Covenant, stone tablets, covenant and ancestors as you used in previous passages.

Media Requests:

  1. King Solomon's Temple: photo or illustration of King Solomon's Temple. This should be a wide shot that shows all of the areas that were included in the Temple complex.

King Solomon's Temple Example

  1. Ark of the Covenant: photo or illustration of the Ark of the Covenant that the priests carried to the Temple. (We have this image in the works already)
  2. Tablets in the Ark of the Covenant: photo of what the stone tablets would have looked like that Moses put into the ark. (We have this image already)
  3. Throne: photo of a throne that a king would sit on as he ruled over a kingdom. (We have this image already)

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step. Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage. Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene. Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:18–24
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 2:18–24 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:18–24 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:18–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of Chronicles told us about the line of descent from Hezron to Jesse. Then the storyteller told us about Jesse's seven sons, two daughters, and some of Jesse's grandchildren. In this passage, the storyteller tells us about some other descendants of Hezron. Some of these descendants of Hezron might have lived and ruled in the land of Canaan while the rest of the Israelites were living in Egypt.

This passage has two statements that are very hard to understand in the original language. Bible scholars do not agree on how to translate these statements. The first statement in this passage is one of them. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you might have heard some different translations of this statement.

We know for sure that Hezron had a son named Caleb. He is not the same Caleb who went with Joshua to spy on the Promised Land. Most translations agree that Caleb's wife was named Azubah. Some Bible scholars think that Jerioth was a second wife or a concubine of Caleb. It is possible that Caleb's wife, Azubah, could not have children, and so Azubah gave her servant, Jerioth, to Caleb to have children for Azubah, just like Sarah gave her servant Hagar to Abraham to have children.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How common is it in your culture for a man to have more than one wife? If it is not common now, was it common in the past? What do most people think about a man having more than one wife? Pause this audio here.

Other Bible scholars think that perhaps Jerioth was just another name for Azubah. Most translations identify Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon as sons of Caleb and Azubah. A third possibility is that Jerioth was the daughter of Caleb and Azubah and that Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon were the sons of Jerioth.

After Azubah died, Caleb married a woman named Ephrath, who was the mother of Caleb's son Hur. Hur was the ancestor of Uri, and Uri was the ancestor of Bezalel. Bezalel was famous because Bezalel lived during the 40 years the Israelites wandered in the wilderness after they left Egypt. Bezalel was a skilled artist. Yahweh chose Bezalel and Oholiab to make the Tent of Meeting and all of the objects that would go inside it, including the covenant box.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Tell a story in your language about a person from your people group who was or is a good artist. What has this person done or made that your people especially admire? How do people remember or honor this person? Pause this audio here.

The tabernacle, or Tent of Meeting, and the ark of the covenant were very important to the Israelites. For hundreds of years, the Tent of Meeting was the central location where the Israelites were supposed to worship Yahweh. Yahweh himself was present in the Tent of Meeting. The Israelites also kept the ark of the covenant in the Tent of Meeting. The ark of the covenant contained the Ten Commandments and other important objects. The Israelites considered the ark of the covenant to be an earthly representation of Yahweh's throne, which symbolized Yahweh's power and authority. Bezalel, a descendant of Hezron, was the chief artist in charge of making these items, as well as many more items inside the Tent of Meeting. This was a position of great honor.

In the book of Genesis, Moses tells us that Hezron moved from Canaan to Egypt with his father Perez, his grandfather Judah, and his great-grandfather Jacob. The Israelites stayed in Egypt for 430 years, and the Egyptians eventually forced the Israelites to be slaves. But this passage tells us that some of Hezron's descendants went back to Canaan for a while. Hezron's descendant Jair ruled 23 towns in a land called Gilead.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Egypt, Canaan, Gilead, Geshur, and Aram, including the towns of Jair and Kenath. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller of Chronicles tells us that Hezron married a woman who was the daughter of Makir and the sister of a man named Gilead. Bible scholars are not sure if this means that Hezron left Egypt to marry the daughter of Makir and then raised his family in Gilead, or if Hezron married the daughter of Makir in Egypt and then their descendants went back to Gilead later. What is certain is that Hezron's descendant Jair became a leader of 23 cities in Gilead.

But the descendants of Hezron lost control of this territory in Canaan. The people of Geshur and the people of Aram came and took the territory away from Hezron's descendants. The land of Geshur was northeast of Israel, and the land of Aram was north of Israel.

The last statement of this passage is very hard to understand in the original language. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you might have heard some different translations of this sentence. Some Bible scholars think this sentence means that Caleb's wife Ephrath was originally married to Hezron, Caleb's father. When Hezron died, Ephrath did not have any children by him, so Caleb married her in order to give her children. In this understanding, Ephrath is the mother of Ashhur.

Another possible understanding is that Hezron's wife was named Abijah and that Hezron died while Abijah was pregnant with his child. Then Abijah gave birth to Ashhur, who was the ancestor of Tekoa. In this understanding, Ephrathah is the name of a place.

Tekoa is both the name of a person and the name of a town in Israel. The town is located about sixteen kilometers south of Jerusalem and about eight kilometers south of Bethlehem. Tekoa was the hometown of an Israelite prophet named Amos.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Tekoa.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:18–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller focuses on telling us about some descendants of Caleb, the son of Hezron.

In the second scene: The storyteller focuses on telling us about the family line of Hezron, Segub, and Jair.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells us about Hezron, Ashhur, and Tekoa.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Caleb, Hezron, Azubah, Jerioth, Jesher, Shobab, Ardon, Ephrath, Hur, Uri, and Bezalel
  • Makir, Gilead, the daughter of Makir, Segub, Jair, the people of Geshur, the people of Aram
  • And Abijah, Ashhur, and Tekoa

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller tells us about the family of Caleb, son of Hezron. Bible scholars are certain that Azubah was Caleb's wife, but they do not agree on who Jerioth was. Most translations of this sentence say that Caleb also had children with Jerioth, which means that she was probably a second wife or concubine. But some translations say that Jerioth was the daughter of Caleb and Azubah. We recommend that you translate this part of the passage to match the Bible translation that your local churches use.

After Azubah died, Caleb married another woman called Ephrath. Ephrath's son was named Hur. Hur was the ancestor of Uri, who was the ancestor of Bezalel. When the storyteller describes the relationship of Hur, Uri, and Bezalel, he uses the special word "begot" that can mean "to be the father of" or "to be the ancestor of." Your translation should reflect the fact that this word can mean "father of" or "ancestor of."

Stop here and remember the discussion you had previously about the different words you could use for "father" and "ancestor." Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, we find out about more descendants of Hezron. When Hezron was 60 years old, he married a woman who was the daughter of Makir. The storyteller says that Hezron "went in to" or "lay with" the daughter of Makir, which means that Hezron had sexual relations with her.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How would you say that a man had sexual relations with his wife, in a way that is appropriate to say in a group of both men and women? Pause this audio here.

Most Bible scholars think that Makir was the son of Manasseh, one of the sons of Joseph. Makir also had a son named Gilead. So Hezron married the sister of Gilead. The region people called Gilead was east of the Jordan River and of the Sea of Galilee. People may have named this region after Gilead, the son of Makir.

Stop here and look again at the map that shows the location of the land of Egypt, Canaan, Gilead, Geshur, and Aram. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller does not tell us the name of Hezron's wife. The important information is that she gave birth to Segub, and Segub was the ancestor of Jair, the leader of 23 towns in Gilead. We do not know how long Jair controlled this territory. We only know that the people of Geshur and the people of Aram, who lived north of the region of Gilead, took this territory away from Jair. We do not know what happened to Jair and his descendants after they lost their territory.

The storyteller of Chronicles finishes this scene by saying, "All of these were the descendants of Makir, the father of Gilead." This is unusual for Hebrew because these people were actually the descendants of Makir's daughter, and not of his son Gilead. The ancient Israelites considered descent through the father to be most important. But it is possible that, because Jair, the descendant of Makir's daughter, ruled 23 towns, the other descendants of Makir wanted to claim Jair as one of their own. In this case, it is best to use the more general term for "descendants" rather than "sons" to show the fact that this line of descent includes a woman.

Stop here and decide which word you will use for "descendants" to reflect the fact that this line of descent goes through a woman. Pause this audio here.

Scene three is the hardest of all the scenes in this passage to understand. Depending on how you translate from the original language, the characters and their relationships change. We recommend that you translate this section with the following meaning: "Soon after Hezron died in the town of Caleb Ephrathah, Hezron's wife Abijah gave birth to a son named Ashhur. And Ashhur became the father of Tekoa."

According to this understanding of the story, Hezron had another wife named Abijah. When Hezron died in a town called Caleb Ephrathah, Abijah was pregnant with Hezron's child. No one knows where the town of Caleb Ephrathah was located. After a little while, Abijah gave birth to a son called Ashhur.

Bible scholars are unsure if the final part of this verse means that Ashhur was the ancestor of a person called Tekoa, who founded a town named after himself, or if it means that Ashhur was the founder of the town of Tekoa. Remember that the word "father of" can mean a person's father, but it can also mean "founder of." In this case, it would mean that Ashhur was the first person to move his family to the place that became the town of Tekoa.

Stop here and remember the discussion you had before about words that can mean "father of" and "founder of." Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:18–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Caleb, Hezron, Azubah, Jerioth, Jesher, Shobab, Ardon, Ephrath, Hur, Uri, and Bezalel
  • Makir, Gilead, the daughter of Makir, Segub, Jair, the people of Geshur, the people of Aram
  • And Abijah, Ashhur, and Tekoa

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, think about how the ancient Israelites might have felt as they listened to these names. Bezalel had an important and honorable role in the religious history of Israel. Bezalel was the chief artist in charge of making the tabernacle, or Tent of Meeting, and all the objects that the people kept inside it. People used these objects to worship Yahweh. The objects included altars, gold lampstands, bowls, incense, and the clothes for the priests. People eventually moved these objects into the temple and used them for hundreds of years.

Hezron's descendant Jair ruled 23 towns in the land of Gilead for a period of time, but he eventually lost that territory. This might have reminded the ancient Israelites of how their ancestors eventually lost control of the land of Israel. That was a terrible time for the Israelite community. Many people died in battle, and the Israelites' enemies forced many of them to live in a foreign land. Although the ancient Israelites who listened to this story were living in the land of Israel, they still faced many difficulties. These Israelites were probably familiar with the town of Tekoa, which was near Jerusalem. Some of the ancient Israelites who listened to this passage might even have been in the town of Tekoa! It might have comforted and encouraged these people to know the ancestry of the Israelite who founded that town.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the reading, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the reading or reciting the passage.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the passage and possible responses.

Hezron's son Caleb had sons with his wife Azubah and with Jerioth. Her sons were called Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon. After Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath, and they had a son named Hur. Hur was the ancestor of Uri, who was the ancestor of Bezalel.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am impressed that the tribe of Judah produced so many important Israelites; or
  • I wish that we still had all the original items in the temple; or
  • I am amazed that Bezalel had enough skill to make all those items for the Tent of Meeting.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad that we no longer have the original items that Bezalel made for the Tent of Meeting; or
  • What an honor for Bezalel to be in charge of making all the items people used to worship Yahweh! or
  • I wish I knew more about these people. These people's lives must have been very interesting.

Continue reciting the passage.

When Hezron was 60 years old, he married Gilead's sister, the daughter of Makir. They had a son named Segub. Segub was the ancestor of Jair, who ruled 23 towns in the land of Gilead. But the Geshurites and the Arameans captured the towns of Jair and also took the town of Kenath and its 60 surrounding villages. All these were the descendants of Makir, the father of Gilead.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder why the descendants of Makir went back to Gilead and conquered that territory; or
  • I am sad that our people have had to fight so hard just to survive; or
  • I think it is unusual that the storyteller calls these folks the descendants of Makir.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not like the Geshurites and Arameans because they have been such a problem for our people; or
  • The tribe of Judah is strong. Many rulers came from that tribe; or
  • I feel sad that Jair lost control of his territory, and I wonder what happened to Jair and his descendants.

Continue reciting the passage.

After Hezron died in Caleb Ephrathah, Hezron's wife Abijah gave birth to a son named Ashhur. And Ashhur was the father of Tekoa.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud that someone from our people founded the town of Tekoa; or
  • The prophet Amos came from Tekoa, so Tekoa must be a good town; or
  • I feel sad for Abijah that her husband died before their son was born.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have been to Tekoa, and I am glad that I know who founded it; or
  • I feel sad for Ashhur that he never knew his father; or
  • I am happy for Abijah that she was able to have a son and a famous descendant.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:18–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The word sons in this passage refers to the male children of a parent. Translate sons when it means male children in the same way you have translated it in the previous passage. For more information about sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

Caleb had sons by his wife, Azubah, and also by a woman named Jerioth. The sons were called Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon. The name Caleb means "faithful." The name Azubah means "strength." The name Jerioth means "curtains." The name Jesher means "right, pleasant, or prosperous." The name Shobab means "a heathen." The name Ardon means "roaming."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these names. Pause this audio here.

After Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath. Caleb and Ephrath's son was named Hur. Hur was the ancestor of Uri, and Uri was the ancestor of Bezalel. The name Ephrath means "fruitful." The name Hur means "white." The name Uri means "fiery." The name Bezalel means "the shadow of God." If you have already translated the book of Exodus, then you have already translated these names.

Stop here and listen again to the first few verses of Exodus chapter 31. Pay close attention to how you translated the names Hur, Uri, and Bezalel. Did you translate these names according to their meaning, or according to how they sound? Do you like the way you translated them, or do you want to change them? Pause this audio here.

When Hezron was 60 years old, he married the daughter of Makir. Makir was the father of Gilead. Hezron's wife gave birth to Segub. Segub was the ancestor of Jair, who controlled 23 cities in the land of Gilead. But the people of Geshur and the people of Aram came and captured the territory of Jair as well as the territory of Kenath and all 60 of the towns that surrounded Kenath. These were the descendants of Makir, the father of Gilead. Use the same words for descendants as you have used in previous passages. Remember that descendants are sons and daughters who descended from one person. For more information about descendants, refer to the Master Glossary, and use the same word for descendants as you have used previously.

In the Bible, a city was a large settlement surrounded by a wall. The wall protected the people who lived inside the city. Often, there were smaller settlements called villages near a city. The villages might not have walls around them.

Stop here and remember previous conversations you have had about how to translate the words city and village. Be sure to translate these words the same way you did in previous books of the Bible. Pause this audio here.

The name Hezron means "courtyard." The name Makir means "salesman." The name Gilead means "rocky." The name Segub means "exalted." The name Jair means "someone who brings light." The name Geshur means "bridge." The name Aram means "highland." The name Kenath means "a possession."

Stop here and decide how you want to translate these names. Pause this audio here.

After Hezron died in the town of Caleb Ephrathah, Hezron's wife Abijah gave birth to his son Ashhur. Ashhur was the father of Tekoa. The name Caleb Ephrathah is a combination of the names Caleb and Ephrath, which mean "faithful" and "fruitful." The name Ashhur probably means "black." The name Tekoa means "place where you set up a tent."

Stop here and decide how you want to translate these names.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:25–33
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 2:25–33 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:25–33 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of Chronicles told us about some descendants of Hezron, the grandson of Judah. The storyteller also told us about the descendants of Caleb, who was one of Hezron's sons. In this passage, the storyteller tells us about the descendants of Jerahmeel, who was the firstborn son of Hezron.

Bible scholars know very little information about the people the storyteller mentions in this passage. The storyteller of Chronicles might have assumed that his audience already knew some information about the descendants of Jerahmeel. Jerahmeel was a member of the tribe of Judah, which was an important tribe of the Israelites. Jerahmeel's descendants made up a clan or smaller group of related individuals within the tribe of Judah. Members of a clan like the Jerahmeelites would see themselves as related to other clans in the tribe of Judah, but more closely related to other people in the clan of Jerahmeel.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your people group, how do you decide who is a close relative and who is a distant relative? What kind of groupings of related individuals do you use? Do you have groups like clans and tribes? How closely related does another person have to be in order for you to consider that person family? Pause this audio here.

When the Israelites left Egypt and went into the Promised Land, the descendants of Jerahmeel received land in the territory that Yahweh gave to the tribe of Judah. During the time of King David, the descendants of Jerahmeel lived in the southern part of Judah, south of a town people called Beersheba. People call this part of Israel the Negev wilderness. The Negev is a very dry area of Israel.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel that shows the location of the territory of the Jerahmeelites. Pause this audio here.

Many of the ancient Israelites who went into exile in Babylon and later returned to Israel were from the tribe of Judah. It is possible that the original audience who listened to the book of Chronicles understood the importance of the descendants of Jerahmeel. Some of those people were probably descendants of Jerahmeel themselves. The people would probably feel very honored that their ancestors were included in the Book of Chronicles.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Tell some stories about famous people from your people group that you think everyone already knows. Does everyone on your team actually know these stories? Does everyone know the same version of the stories, or are there different versions? Pause this audio here.

King David was a descendant of Judah through Ram, the brother of Jerahmeel. The ancient Israelites might have considered the Jerahmeelites to be close relatives of David's family. This is another possible reason why the storyteller of Chronicles takes the time to tell us about the descendants of Jerahmeel. Bible scholars do not know what the relationship was like between the descendants of Jerahmeel and other clans of the tribe of Judah.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How important are family relationships in your culture? Discuss the good things that can happen because of good family connections. Also, discuss the bad things that can happen because of bad family connections. Pause this audio here.

Jerahmeel, son of Hezron, had six sons. Five of those sons were from one wife, and one son was born to Jerahmeel's other wife, Atarah. The storyteller chooses to tell us about the descendants of Jerahmeel's son Ram, who was the firstborn, and about the descendants of Jerahmeel's son Onam. The storyteller does not tell us anything about the descendants of Jerahmeel's other four sons.

Although the storyteller of Chronicles tells us the names of Ram's sons, he does not give us any more information about those sons' descendants. The storyteller uses the rest of this passage to talk about the descendants of Onam. Specifically, the storyteller names six generations of descendants from Onam. This suggests that the storyteller thinks the line of descent through Onam is the most important information in this passage.

Ahlai is the descendant of Sheshan, son of Ishi. In the next passage, we will find out that Ahlai is Sheshan's daughter. Sheshan had no sons, so this is a valid reason for including his daughter Ahlai in this list of descendants.

In the next passage, the storyteller will tell us about the descendants of Sheshan through his daughter Ahlai.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the sons of Jerahmeel.

In the second scene: The storyteller names the grandsons of Jerahmeel through his firstborn son Ram and his son Onam.

In the third scene: The storyteller follows the family line of Jerahmeel's son Onam through Shammai.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller follows the family line of Onam through Jada, the brother of Shammai.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Hezron, Jerahmeel, Ram, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, Ahijah, Atarah, Onam
  • Maaz, Jamin, Eker
  • Shammai, Jada, Nadab, Abishur, Abihail, Ahban, Molid, Seled, Appaim, Ishi, Sheshan, Ahlai
  • And Jether, Jonathan, Peleth, and Zaza

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:
