In the first scene, the storyteller lists the sons of Jerahmeel. The storyteller reminds us that Jerahmeel was the firstborn son of Hezron. The storyteller also tells us that Ram was the firstborn son of Jerahmeel. This might confuse some audiences because Jerahmeel also had a brother named Ram.

Stop here and remember the discussion you had about multiple people in a family with the same name. If this is something unusual in your culture, how will you make it clear to your audience that Jerahmeel's brother Ram and Jerahmeel's son Ram are two different people? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller does not tell us the name of the mother of Jerahmeel's sons Ram, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah. The storyteller does tell us the name of Jerahmeel's other wife, Atarah, who was the mother of Onam. This might be because, for most of the rest of the passage, the storyteller will talk about the descendants of Onam. Apparently, it was important to the storyteller of Chronicles to make it clear that Onam had a different mother from the rest of Jerahmeel's sons.

In the second scene, the storyteller of Chronicles tells us the names of Jerahmeel's grandsons through Ram and Onam. The storyteller does not tell us the names of any descendants of Bunah, Oren, Ozem, or Ahijah.

In the third scene, the storyteller tells us more about the family line of Shammai, the grandson of Hezron, through Onam. First, the storyteller recites the list of ancestors from Shammai through his son Abishur to his grandsons Ahban and Molid. The storyteller also includes the name of Abishur's wife, Abihail.

Next, the storyteller traces a line of descent from Shammai through his son Nadab. This list of ancestors contains several generations. Through Nadab, his son Appaim, his grandson Ishi, and great-grandson Sheshan, we get to Ahlai, the daughter of Sheshan.

The storyteller tells us that two people in this list of ancestors, Seled and Jether, died without children. You might have heard two different ways to say this when you listened to different translations of the Bible. Some translations say that these men died without children, but other translations say that the men died without having sons. Because people in the Israelite culture considered descent through the father to be most important, children were usually born into their father's tribe. Therefore, a man had to father sons in order to continue his family line. A man's daughter's children belonged to the daughter's husband's family line. Therefore, in the Israelite culture, for a man to die with no sons was kind of the same as if the man died with no children at all. However, it is also important to remember that the original Hebrew word for "sons" can also be understood to mean "descendants," which would include both male descendants and female descendants. So it is possible that this means that both Seled and Jether died with no children at all, either male or female.

Stop here and discuss whether you will translate these sentences to say that each person died without sons or that each person died without children. Pause this audio here.

Ahlai is the daughter of Sheshan, son of Ishi. In the next passage, the storyteller will tell us that Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. In the original language, the storyteller of Chronicles uses the word that people usually translate as "sons of" or "descendants of" to describe the relationship between Sheshan and Ahlai. In many languages, it will be unnatural to say that Ahlai was the son of Sheshan when it soon becomes clear that Ahlai was Sheshan's daughter. It is acceptable in this scene to say that Ahlai was a descendant of Sheshan, or to say that Sheshan was the father of Ahlai.

Stop here and discuss how you will describe the relationship between Sheshan and Ahlai in your language. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller traces the descendants of Jada, the brother of Shammai. Once again, Bible scholars do not know why the storyteller mentions these members of the tribe of Judah. This scene ends with a summary statement that says, "These were the sons of Jerahmeel." In this case, the word "sons" clearly means "descendants," since the storyteller has talked about a total of eight generations.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate this summary statement. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Hezron, Jerahmeel, Ram, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, Ahijah, Atarah, Onam
  • Maaz, Jamin, Eker
  • Shammai, Jada, Nadab, Abishur, Abihail, Ahban, Molid, Seled, Appaim, Ishi, Sheshan, Ahlai
  • And Jether, Jonathan, Peleth, and Zaza

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite listening to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

As you do this, imagine you are an ancient Israelite who lives in Jerusalem and is listening to one of your leaders tell you the names of the descendants of Jerahmeel. Perhaps you are a descendant of Jerahmeel, and you feel honored to hear your leader read the names of your ancestors out loud. You might hope that your ancestors had faith that their family line would survive. You might think of the sadness your ancestors felt when enemies forcibly removed your ancestors from their homes and sent them to Babylon. Or you might think about the joy your ancestors felt when other people allowed your ancestors to return and rebuild Jerusalem. You might think of your family's ancient territory, the towns and farms left behind in the Negev wilderness in the south of Judah, and wish that you could take control of it again. Or, you might be from another Israelite tribe. Maybe your ancestors did not get along with the ancestors of the Jerahmeelites. Maybe you know some descendants of Jerahmeel, and you do not like those people. Or, maybe you see yourself and the descendants of Jerahmeel as brothers, who all work toward the same goal and serve the same God.

Pause this audio here and recite the passage.

The group should listen to this passage a second time. You might decide to listen to an audio recording of the passage if the many names are too difficult to remember at this point. At certain points in the passage, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the passage.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the passage and possible responses.

The sons of Jerahmeel, the firstborn son of Hezron, were named Ram, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah. Jerahmeel had another wife named Atarah, and she was the mother of Onam.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is important to remember all the ancestors of our people; or
  • God blessed Jerahmeel with six sons; or
  • The tribe of Judah had many important people.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am excited to hear more about our ancestors; or
  • It makes me proud to hear the names of my ancestors read aloud; or
  • I wonder what Jerahmeel's other wife was called?

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

The sons of Ram, the firstborn of Jerahmeel, were Maaz, Jamin, and Eker. The sons of Onam were Shammai and Jada.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder why Jerahmeel gave his firstborn son the same name as his brother; or
  • These people all probably lived in Egypt; or
  • I hope my people feel honored when they remember their ancestors.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wonder why the storyteller does not tell us anything about Jerahmeel's other sons; or
  • I know some descendants of Jerahmeel, and I do not like those people; or
  • Why do we have to talk so much about the tribe of Judah?

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Shammai had two sons named Nadab and Abishur. Abishur's wife was named Abihail, and she was the mother of Ahban and Molid. Nadab had two sons named Seled and Appaim. Seled died without any sons. Appaim had a son named Ishi, who was the father of Sheshan. And Sheshan was the father of Ahlai.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope my people will remember this important information about our ancestors; or
  • I wonder what life was like for these people who lived so long ago; or
  • I feel honored to remember these people.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wonder what happened to Seled, and why he died without any sons; or
  • I wonder why the storyteller tells us the name of Abishur's wife. Was Abishur's wife an important person at that time? or
  • It is important to remember the names of our ancestors. I hope I can remember all this!

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Shammai's brother Jada had two sons, Jether and Jonathan. Jether died without having any sons. Jonathan had two sons named Peleth and Zaza. All these people were the descendants of Jerahmeel.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wish we knew more about these Israelites; or
  • I feel honored to teach our people this important history; or
  • I am excited to share more information about the tribe of Judah!

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wonder why this branch of Jerahmeel's family was so important; or
  • I feel confused why the storyteller is telling us about so many random people of Judah; or
  • I wish I did not have to listen to all this information about the Jerahmeelites.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Remember that sometimes the word sons in the original language refers to the male children of a parent, and sometimes it refers to a group of descendants. Translate sons, or descendants, in the same way you have translated in the previous passage. For more information about sons and descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

The sons of Jerahmeel, the firstborn son of Hezron, were Ram, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah. Jerahmeel had another wife named Atarah. Atarah was the mother of Onam.

You have already translated the names Hezron, Jerahmeel, and Ram. Be sure to translate these names the same way that you did in previous passages in Chronicles. You have also already translated the term "firstborn," so be sure to translate it the same way you did in previous passages.

The name Bunah means "wisdom." The name Oren means "pine tree." The name Ozem means "strong." The name Ahijah means "brother of Yahweh." The name Atarah means "crown." The name Onam also means "strong."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of the sons of Jerahmeel. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Ram, who was Jerahmeel's firstborn son, were Maaz, Jamin, and Eker. The sons of Onam were called Shammai and Jada. The name Maaz could mean "vigor" or "anger." The name Jamin means "right hand" or "authority, power." The name Eker means "barren" or "weak." The name Shammai means "They have heard my name." The name Jada means "wise."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of the sons of Ram and Onam. Pause this audio here.

Shammai's sons were called Nadab and Abishur. Abishur's wife was called Abihail. Abihail was the mother of Abishur's sons Ahban and Molid. The name Nadab means "generous." The name Abishur means "my father is a wall" or "my father is a bull," both of which refer to strength and protection. The name Abihail means "my father is strong." The name Ahban means "intelligent." The name Molid means "birthplace" or "generation."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of these descendants of Shammai. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Nadab were called Seled and Appaim. Seled died without children. Appaim had a son named Ishi, who was the father of Sheshan. And Sheshan was the father of Ahlai. The name Seled means "rock." The name Appaim means "nostrils." The name Ishi means "salvation." The name Sheshan could mean "joy, rejoicing." The name Ahlai means "desire."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of these descendants of Nadab. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Jada, Shammai's brother, were Jether and Jonathan. Jether died without children. Jonathan's sons were named Peleth and Zaza. These were the descendants of Jerahmeel. The name Jether means "excellence." The name Jonathan means "Yahweh has given." The name Peleth means "speed." The name Zaza can mean "light."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of these descendants of Jada.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:34–41
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 2:34–41 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:34–41 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:34–41 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of Chronicles told us about the descendants of Jerahmeel, son of Hezron. In this passage, the storyteller continues to tell us about the list of ancestors from Jerahmeel's descendant Sheshan to a man named Elishama.

Although the passage does not say so, many Bible scholars assume that the Sheshan the storyteller mentions in this passage is the same Sheshan from the previous passage, Sheshan who was the son of Ishi and the father of Ahlai. Many Bible scholars also assume that Ahlai was the daughter that Sheshan gave in marriage to Jarha. That means that Sheshan and his descendants are members of the tribe of Judah and descendants of Jerahmeel.

At the very beginning of the passage, the storyteller tells us that Sheshan had no sons, but he did have daughters. In ancient Israelite culture, this was a difficult situation for Sheshan. Previously, we discussed how in Israelite culture, a child was born into his or her father's tribe. That means that normally, the children of Sheshan's daughters would not belong to Sheshan's tribe, but to their father's tribe. You have already seen how almost all the names in the genealogies of the Book of Chronicles are the names of men. Without male descendants to carry on the family line, Sheshan's family line would cease to exist.

But a man who only had daughters also faced other disadvantages. In the Israelite culture, a man would normally pass his property and wealth to his sons. If a man only had daughters, then there was no one to inherit that man's property. This was a huge problem for an ancient Israelite because Israelites often connected wealth to the land. It was very important to the Israelites to maintain their family and tribal possession of the land.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, what kind of inheritance rights do daughters have? Are there any rights or privileges that sons have but daughters do not have? If men and women in your culture have equal rights of inheritance now, was there ever a time in the past when this was not the case? If a husband and wife do not have children who are able to inherit their wealth or property, what happens to their wealth or property? Pause this audio here.

Sheshan had an Egyptian servant named Jarha. Bible scholars disagree on whether Jarha was a paid servant that Sheshan employed, or if Jarha was actually a slave, who was not free to come and go as he liked. When you listened to different translations of this passage, you may have heard some translations call Jarha a servant, and other translations call Jarha a slave.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, how common is it for a person to have servants? How common is it for a person to have slaves? If these things are not common now, was there a time in the past when these things were common? What do people in your culture think about slavery? Pause this audio here.

Whether Jarha was a servant or a slave, Sheshan gave one of his daughters in marriage to Jarha, and everyone considered their children to be Sheshan's descendants. This is similar to how Abraham took Sarah's Egyptian slave Hagar as a concubine in order to father children. People thought this was an acceptable solution to a serious problem. People considered Abraham and Hagar's son Ishmael a legitimate descendant of Abraham. In the case of Sheshan's daughter and Jarha, this solution probably only worked because Jarha was Egyptian and not a member of any tribe of Israel. It probably did not matter to Jarha which Israelite tribe his children belonged to. Whether Jarha was a servant or a slave, this arranged marriage would most likely give Jarha's children a better future because those children would inherit Sheshan's property.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, how common is it for parents to arrange the marriages of their children? If it is not common now, was it common in the past? Do parents do this for both sons and daughters? When parents arrange a marriage, what details should they consider when they choose a bride or groom? Pause this audio here.

The ancient Israelites probably considered this an emergency solution to a serious problem. This solution does not follow normal inheritance practices, but it was a way to make sure Sheshan's grandchildren could inherit his property.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Think of some circumstances where, in your culture, it is socially acceptable to make an exception to the rules. What is the normal rule in this situation? What is the exception to this rule? Under what circumstances is it okay to break the rule? Why is it acceptable to make an exception to this rule? Pause this audio here.

The rest of this passage focuses on the list of ancestors from Attai to Elishama. This is actually one of the longest genealogies in the Bible. Because of this, Bible scholars believe that Elishama must have been an important person. Unfortunately, nobody knows for sure who Elishama was or why he was important. It seems that once again, the storyteller of Chronicles assumed that his audience knew who Elishama was and why Elishama was important. But modern audiences do not know why Elishama was important.

Stop here and tell a story about someone from your people group whom everyone respected and honored. What did this person do to cause others to respect and admire him or her so much? When you talk about this person, what kind of words do you use to describe him or her? What kind of qualities did he or she have?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:34–41 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller explains how Sheshan arranged the marriage of his daughter to his Egyptian servant, Jarha, so that Sheshan's family line could continue.

In the second scene: The storyteller focuses on telling his audience the list of ancestors from Sheshan's grandson Attai to Elishama. There are 13 generations in this scene.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Sheshan, Jarha, Attai, and Sheshan's daughter
  • And Nathan, Zabad, Ephlal, Obed, Jehu, Azariah, Helez, Eleasah, Sismai, Shallum, Jekamiah, and Elishama

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller explains how Sheshan's family line continued through his daughter's marriage to Jarha, an Egyptian servant. Sheshan had no sons, and this meant that Sheshan's family line would end with Sheshan. This meant that Sheshan had to find an unusual way to make sure that Sheshan had descendants to inherit his property.

Most Bible translations say that Sheshan gave his daughter in marriage to Jarha. This simply means that Sheshan made the arrangements for his daughter to marry Jarha. In ancient Israelite culture, parents usually arranged marriages for their children. Parents might have considered their children's feelings in the matter, but the parents usually made the final decision about who their children married. We do not know how Sheshan's daughter and Jarha felt about this matter. Jarha and Sheshan's daughter might have been happy about the marriage, or they might have been unhappy about it. Jarha and Sheshan's daughter may have felt they were simply doing their duty.

Stop here and discuss this question: What is the most natural way in your language to say that Sheshan arranged for his daughter to marry Jarha? Pause this audio here.

Most Bible scholars assume that Ahlai was Sheshan's daughter who married Jarha. But since the storyteller of Chronicles does not include this information here, it is better to simply say, "Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha to be Jarha's wife" or "Sheshan gave one of his daughters in marriage to Jarha."

In the second scene, the storyteller uses a specific way to describe the list of ancestors from Attai to Elishama. The storyteller tells us that each man was the father of the man whose name comes after.

Stop here and remember the conversation you had previously about the most natural way to express this kind of list of ancestors in your language. Pause this audio here.

You might notice that some of the names in this section are common Israelite names. For example, we hear of other characters in the Bible with the names Nathan, Obed, Jehu, Shallum, and Azariah.

Stop here and discuss this question: If it is unusual or forbidden in your culture for two different people to have the same name, how will you make it clear to your audience that these Israelites with the same name are different people? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:34–41 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Sheshan, Jarha, Attai, and Sheshan's daughter
  • And Nathan, Zabad, Ephlal, Obed, Jehu, Azariah, Helez, Eleasah, Sismai, Shallum, Jekamiah, and Elishama

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite listening to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

As you do this, imagine you are an ancient Israelite who lives in Jerusalem. For the sake of this exercise, imagine that you are a member of the tribe of Judah and part of the clan of Jerahmeel. Imagine that Elishama was your ancestor, and that everyone respected and honored Elishama. You know that you are listening to Elishama's genealogy. You probably feel honored and proud that your ancestor has been included in the Book of Chronicles. The other people around you know that Elishama was an honorable and important man, and they know that you are Elishama's descendant.

Pause this audio here and recite the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the passage, ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue to read or recite the passage.

Pause this audio here and recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of possible responses.

Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. Sheshan also had an Egyptian servant named Jarha. Sheshan arranged for his daughter to marry Jarha, and they had a son named Attai.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel bad for Sheshan that he had no sons; or
  • Sheshan figured out a very clever solution to his problem; or
  • It is a good thing that Sheshan's family line was able to continue so that Elishama could be born.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I feel honored that these stories about my ancestors made it into this book! or
  • I am thankful that our ancestor Sheshan found a way to continue our family line; or
  • I wonder how Sheshan's daughter and Jarha felt about their arranged marriage?

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Attai became the father of Nathan. Nathan became the father of Zabad. Zabad became the father of Ephlal. Ephlal became the father of Obed. Obed became the father of Jehu. Jehu became the father of Azariah. Azariah became the father of Helez. Helez became the father of Eleasah. Eleasah became the father of Sismai. Sismai became the father of Shallum. Shallum became the father of Jekamiah. Jekamiah became the father of Elishama.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored to teach our people these important parts of our history; or
  • It is important to remember the family line of Elishama because he was an important man; or
  • I am impressed that so many good people came from the tribe of Judah.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • It is exciting to hear someone read about the family line of Elishama from this book; or
  • I feel proud to be a descendant of Elishama; or
  • I am grateful that the storyteller thought our family history was important enough to include in his book.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:34–41 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Remember that sometimes the word sons in the original language refers to the male children of a parent. In this passage, Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. "Sons" in this case refers to the male children of parents. For more information about sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

Sheshan did not have any sons, but he had daughters. Sheshan had an Egyptian servant named Jarha. A servant is a person who does work for someone else. Some translations of the Bible call Jarha a slave. A slave also does work for someone else, but is not free to leave and find another job. People consider a slave the property of the person he or she works for.

Stop here and discuss as a group whether you will call Jarha a servant or a slave, if you have different words for the different types of servants. Look up servant in the Master Glossary for more information. Once you decide which word to use, if you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used before. Pause this audio here.

Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha to be Jarha's wife, and she had a son named Attai. We do not know what Jarha's name means. The name Attai means "good timing."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names Jarha and Attai. Will you translate these names according to the meaning, or will you translate them to sound the same as the original names? Pause this audio here.

For the rest of the passage, the storyteller uses a special word that means "to become the father of" or "to become the ancestor of."

Stop here and remember the decision you made about how to translate this special word or phrase. Be sure to translate it the same way that you have before. Pause this audio here.

Attai was the father of Nathan, who was the father of Zabad. Zabad was the father of Ephlal, who was the father of Obed. Obed was the father of Jehu, who was the father of Azariah. The name Nathan means "gift." The name Zabad means "giver." The name Ephlal means "God has judged." The name Obed means "worshipper." The name Jehu means "Yahweh is God." The name Azariah means "Yahweh has helped."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these names. Pause this audio here.

Azariah was the father of Helez, who was the father of Eleasah. Eleasah was the father of Sismai, who was the father of Shallum. Shallum was the father of Jekamiah, who was the father of Elishama. The name Helez means "strong" or "freedom." The name Eleasah means "God has made." The name Sismai means "my protector." The name Shallum probably means "reward." The name Jekamiah means "Yahweh will rise" or "Yahweh will establish." The name Elishama means "God hears."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these names.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of Chronicles told us about many generations of descendants of Jerahmeel, the oldest son of Hezron of the tribe of Judah. In this passage, the storyteller returns to tell us about the descendants of Caleb, the brother of Jerahmeel.

Caleb and Jerahmeel were sons of Hezron. Hezron had five sons in total. Jerahmeel was the oldest. Hezron's other sons were Ram, Caleb, Segub, and Ashhur. Ram was the ancestor of King David. The storyteller of Chronicles previously told us about some of Caleb's descendants, but apparently, the storyteller did not finish telling us about all of Caleb's descendants. In the next two passages, the storyteller will finish his list of the descendants of Caleb. Bible scholars do not know why the storyteller split his account of Caleb's descendants into two parts.

Caleb had many wives and concubines. In previous passages, the storyteller told us that Caleb had a wife named Azubah and possibly a wife or concubine named Jerioth. After Azubah died, Caleb took a wife named Ephrathah. In this passage, the storyteller does not tell us the name of the woman who was the mother of Caleb's firstborn son. But the storyteller does tell us that Caleb had two more concubines named Ephah and Maacah.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, how common is it for a man to have more than one wife at the same time? If it is not common now, was it common in the past? What do people in your culture think about a man having more than one wife? If a man has more than one wife, do people consider some wives to have a different status than the others? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller suddenly introduces a person named Jahdai with no connection to any other person in this list. Bible scholars do not agree on who Jahdai was or how Jahdai is connected to the descendants of Caleb. Bible scholars do not even agree on whether Jahdai was a man or a woman. It is possible that Jahdai was another wife or concubine of Caleb, and that Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph were Caleb's sons with Jahdai. It is also possible that Jahdai was another name for one of the male descendants of Caleb. Unfortunately, it is impossible to know for sure.

In this passage, the storyteller repeats some names. We hear about two different people named Shaaph and two different people named Gazez. Caleb had a concubine named Ephah, but another male descendant of Caleb is also called Ephah.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Say some names in your language that can be a name for both a man and a woman. If your language does not have names that can be the name for both a man and a woman, how can you make it clear to your audience that names in the Bible, like "Ephah," can be the name of both a man and a woman? Pause this audio here.

We also hear several names in this passage that occur in other parts of the Bible for different people.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How confusing will it be for your listeners to hear names in this passage that are the same names for other famous people in the Bible? If necessary, discuss how you will make it clear to your audience that the people the storyteller mentions here are different from those other famous people in the Bible with the same names. Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller mentions "Shaaph, the father of Madmannah." Bible scholars know that Madmannah is the name of a town in the southern part of Judah. Because of this, some translations say that Shaaph was the founder of the town of Madmannah. Some Bible scholars also believe that Macbenah and Gibea are the names of towns. Since people sometimes name towns in the Bible after people, it is possible that these are the names for both a person and a town.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Talk about some cities, towns, or settlements in your area that someone has named after a person. Why did people name these places after that person? Talk about other locations in your area, such as mountains, rivers, forests, or valleys, that people have named after a person. Why have people named those locations after that person? Pause this audio here.

One purpose of these family lists in the Book of Chronicles was to remind the Israelites of how their family groups began. To the Israelites, their beginnings not only included families and tribes, but also land and towns. Each Israelite tribe received territory in Israel, and each clan also received a portion of land. The leaders of those clans divided up their territory and gave it to families. Those families worked hard to farm the land, build towns, and defend their land when enemies threatened it. Each tribe, clan, and family not only had family connections but also strong connections to their territory.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Have each person in the group say what town or region they come from. How long has your family lived in that area? Does your family own land in that area? How do your people determine who the land belongs to? Are there any cities, towns, or land features in the area that have the same name as one of your ancestors? How important is it to your people that you live in the same area where your ancestors lived? Why is it important?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us about Caleb's descendants through his firstborn son, Mesha.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells us about Caleb's descendants through a concubine named Ephah.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells us about the descendants of a person called Jahdai.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller tells us about Caleb's descendants through a concubine named Maacah.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Caleb, Mesha, Ziph, Mareshah, Hebron, Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, Shema, Raham, Jorkeam, Shammai, Maon, Beth Zur
  • Ephah, Haran, Moza, Gazez, and another man named Gazez
  • Jahdai, Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, Shaaph
  • And Maacah, Sheber, Tirhanah, Shaaph, Madmannah, Sheva, Macbenah, Gibea, and Acsah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, at the very beginning, the storyteller does not follow the usual pattern to describe relationships between people. This makes it very difficult to understand some of the relationships. Because of this, you might have heard a few different ways to translate this verse when you listened to three different versions of this story. What is clear is that Caleb's firstborn son was named Mesha, and that Mesha was the father of Ziph. It is also clear that Mareshah was the father of Hebron. What is not clear is the relationship between Caleb, Mesha, and Mareshah. The storyteller uses the word "son" for all of these relationships.

Stop here and gather five objects to represent Caleb, Mesha, Ziph, Mareshah, and Hebron. You will use these objects to visualize possible relationships between these five men. Pause this audio here.

Some Bible scholars think that Ziph was the father of Mareshah, so that this list of ancestors shows five generations of men.

Stop here and arrange the five objects in such a way that they show that Caleb was the father of Mesha, Mesha was the father of Ziph, Ziph was the father of Mareshah, and Mareshah was the father of Hebron. If anyone has a device that can take pictures, you might want to take a picture of this arrangement. Or you could draw the arrangement. Pause this audio here.

Other Bible scholars think that Mesha and Mareshah were brothers. In this understanding, Caleb was the father of Mesha and Mareshah. Mesha was the father of Ziph, and Mareshah was the father of Hebron, which means that Caleb was the grandfather of both Ziph and Hebron.

Stop here and rearrange the objects to show that Caleb was the father of Mesha and Mareshah, Mesha was the father of Ziph, and Mareshah was the father of Hebron. Make sure everyone understands the changes. If anyone has a device that can take pictures, you might want to take a picture of this arrangement. Or you could draw the arrangement. Pause this audio here.

Some Bible scholars think that Mesha and Mareshah are two names for the same person. So, according to this understanding, Caleb was the father of Mesha, and Ziph and Hebron were Mesha's sons.

Stop here and rearrange the objects so that Mesha and Mareshah are the same person, and that Caleb is the father of Mesha and Mesha is the father of Ziph and Hebron. Make sure everyone understands the changes. Take a picture or draw this arrangement. Pause this audio here.

Some Bible translations intentionally leave the relationship between Mareshah and the other men unclear. For example, these translations might say something like, "The descendants of Caleb, the brother of Jerahmeel, included Mesha, the firstborn son, who became the father of Ziph. Caleb's descendants also included the sons of Mareshah, the father of Hebron." This option makes it clear that all these men were descendants of Caleb, but does not try to explain the relationship between Mareshah and Caleb.

Stop here and rearrange the objects so that Caleb is the father of Mesha and the grandfather of Ziph, and so that Mareshah is the father of Hebron, but the relationship between Mareshah and the others is undefined. Make sure everyone understands the changes. Take a photo or draw this arrangement. Pause this audio here.

It is good practice that if the meaning of a Biblical passage is unclear in the original language, it should be equally unclear in your translation. For this reason, we recommend that you choose to leave the relationship between Mareshah and the other men in this section unclear. However, if you wish to choose one of the other options, that is acceptable.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate this part of the passage. Pause this audio here.

Some of the names in this scene are also the names of towns. Hebron is a town located about 32 kilometers southwest of Jerusalem, and Ziph is a town near Hebron. Tappuah is the name of a town in the western foothills of Judah. Jorkeam may be the name of a village. Beth Zur is a town in the southern part of Judah. People established Hebron before the time of Abraham. It is possible that people named the other towns after these descendants of Caleb, but Bible scholars do not know this for certain. For this reason, we recommend that you do not use the word "founder" in this scene.

In the second scene, the storyteller tells us about the descendants of Caleb through his concubine named Ephah. In this scene, the storyteller describes the relationships between people in yet another way. The storyteller tells us that Ephah gave birth to, or became the mother of, Caleb's sons.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your language, what are some common ways to say that a woman gave birth to a child, or became a mother to her child? Pause this audio here.

This scene contains a statement that might make people feel very uncomfortable. The statement has to do with sex between very close relatives.

Stop here and discuss this topic: For a possibly uncomfortable discussion about sex between close relatives, would it be better to split men and women into two groups and have separate discussions? Pause this audio here.

In this scene, the storyteller tells us, "Caleb's concubine Ephah gave birth to Haran, Moza, and Gazez. Haran was the father of Gazez." There are two ways to interpret this statement. One is that Caleb was the father of Haran, Moza, and Gazez, and that Haran later had a son named Gazez. In this understanding, Haran gave his son the same name as his brother. In the Israelite culture, it was acceptable for a person to give a child the same name as a living relative, so this is a very good possible explanation.

Another interpretation could be that Haran had a sexual relationship with his own mother, Ephah, so that he was both the father and the brother of Gazez. This kind of relationship between a mother and son was strictly forbidden in Israel. Therefore, Bible scholars do not believe that Haran had this kind of relationship with his mother. Some translations say something like, "Haran also had a son named Gazez," in order to be very clear that there are two different people named Gazez in this family.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How likely do you think it is that your audience might misunderstand this verse to mean that Haran had a son with his own mother? Do you feel it is necessary to make it clear that there are two different people named Gazez in this family? If so, how will you make it clear that Haran had a brother named Gazez and also a son named Gazez? Pause this audio here.

We are now done discussing this topic. If you split into groups of men and women for this discussion, you may return to a large group now.

Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller tells us about the sons of Jahdai. Bible scholars do not know if Jahdai was a male descendant of Caleb, or if Jahdai was a woman and the mother of these six sons of Caleb. Since the storyteller lists these sons of Jahdai between the sons of Caleb's concubine Ephah and Caleb's concubine Maacah, it is possible that Jahdai was another concubine. But since the original language does not say this, you should leave the relationship of Jahdai to Caleb unclear.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller tells us about Caleb's descendants through a concubine named Maacah. Maacah had four sons named Sheber, Tirhanah, Shaaph, and Sheva. Madmannah, Macbenah, and Gibea may have been the names of people, but they also might have been the names of towns. In the Book of Chronicles, the storyteller uses the word "father" to not only mean "male parent" or "ancestor" but also "founder." A founder is a person who starts something new, such as the first person to move his family to a new location and start a town. A founder could also be the first king in a dynasty of kings, the original owner of a company, the first person to start a new religious movement, or a person to start a new school.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how do you describe someone who was the founder of a city or settlement? What other special words might you use for the first person to do something? Pause this audio here.

Some translations say that Shaaph was the founder of Madmannah, and Sheva was the founder of Macbenah and Gibea. But it is also acceptable to say that Shaaph was the father of Madmannah, and Sheva was the father of Macbenah and Gibea.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate this: Will you say "father" or "founder"? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us here that Caleb had a daughter named Acsah. The storyteller does not tell us the name of Acsah's mother. We do not know any more information about Acsah or why the storyteller mentioned Acsah here.

At the end of this passage, the storyteller tells us that all these people were descendants of Caleb. This sounds like a summary statement. However, in the next passage, the storyteller will continue to talk about more descendants of Caleb. For this reason, some Bible scholars think that this sentence is supposed to go with the next passage in 1 Chronicles chapter 2. Instead of translating this sentence as a summary statement, such as "These were all descendants of Caleb," these scholars translate the sentence as an introduction to the next passage and say something like, "The following people were also descendants of Caleb." You are free to make whatever choice is the most clear and natural in your language.

Stop here and discuss whether you will translate this sentence as a summary of this passage or as an introduction to the next passage. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Caleb, Mesha, Ziph, Mareshah, Hebron, Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, Shema, Raham, Jorkeam, Shammai, Maon, Beth Zur
  • Ephah, Haran, Moza, Gazez, and another man named Gazez
  • Jahdai, Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, Shaaph
  • And Maacah, Sheber, Tirhanah, Shaaph, Madmannah, Sheva, Macbenah, Gibea, and Acsah

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite listening to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

As you do this, imagine you are an ancient Israelite who lives in Jerusalem. Imagine that you are a descendant of Caleb, and that you feel honored to hear someone read the names of your ancestors aloud. You might feel grateful that the storyteller of the Book of Chronicles wrote this information down and included your ancestors in it. You might also feel grateful that the storyteller listed the names of your ancestors who founded cities in the land of Judah. These words remind you that your family has been very attached to the land for hundreds of years. Your ancestors worked hard to start cities, farm the land, raise children, and fight battles for survival all over the land of Judah. You might really want to go and see towns like Tappuah, Madmannah, and Beth Zur for yourself. Perhaps one of your own ancestors was the founder of a town. When you think about how many generations of your ancestors were born, lived, and died in this land, you realize even more how important the land is to your people.

Pause this audio here and recite the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the passage, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue reading or reciting the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The descendants of Caleb, the brother of Jerahmeel, included Mesha, his firstborn, who became the father of Ziph. Caleb's descendants also included the sons of Mareshah, the father of Hebron. The sons of Hebron were Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema. Shema was the father of Raham, who was the father of Jorkeam. Rekem was the father of Shammai, who was the father of Maon. And Maon was the father of Beth Zur.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is important for our people to know about these members of the tribe of Judah; or
  • We should all keep in mind who founded our cities and towns; or
  • I feel proud that the tribe of Judah is so important in Israel.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • I feel humbled to think about how hard my ancestors worked to settle this land; or
  • I am glad that I know the origins of the people who founded the towns of Judah; or
  • When I hear this, it makes me feel proud to be a member of the tribe of Judah!

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Caleb's concubine Ephah gave birth to Haran, Moza, and Gazez. And Haran also had a son named Gazez. The sons of Jahdai were Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Caleb was a very blessed man who had a lot of children! or
  • I hope that when people hear this, it helps our people realize that we are all one people.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • Caleb had a lot of concubines and a lot of children. I wonder how Caleb provided for all of these people; or
  • I wonder why Haran gave his son the same name as his brother; or
  • I feel honored that the names of my ancestors are in this book; or

Continue reading or reciting the passage.

Another one of Caleb's concubines, Maacah, gave birth to Sheber and Tirhanah. Maacah also gave birth to Shaaph, the founder of Madmannah, and Sheva, the founder of Macbenah and Gibea. Caleb also had a daughter named Acsah. These were all descendants of Caleb.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • No wonder the descendants of Caleb founded so many towns. There were so many descendants! or
  • The descendants of Caleb must have been very capable to have founded so many towns; or
  • It is important for our people to know who founded the towns of Judah.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • It is very important for our people to remember who settled the land; or
  • I wonder what it was like for all of Caleb's children to have so many brothers and sisters! or
  • I want to go visit the towns that my ancestors founded.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:42–50a in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller talks about the sons, or descendants, of Caleb. Use the same word or phrase for sons as you used in previous passages when it means descendants, or all the sons and grandsons and the continuing children of someone. Sometimes the storyteller talks about individual sons of a father, and at that time, you can use the word you have chosen for a male child of a father. For more information, refer to "sons" and "descendants" in the Master Glossary.

Caleb was a brother of Jerahmeel. Mesha, Caleb's firstborn son, was the father of Ziph, and Caleb's son Mareshah was the father of Hebron. The sons of Hebron were Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema. Be sure to translate the names Caleb and Jerahmeel the same way that you did in previous passages. The name Mesha means "safety." The name Ziph means "flowing." The name Mareshah means "place at the top." The name Hebron means "friendship." The name Korah means "smooth." The name Tappuah means "apple tree." The name Rekem means "multi-colored." The name Shema means "a sound."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of these descendants of Caleb. Will you translate the names according to their meaning, or will you translate them to sound the same as the original names? Pause this audio here.

Shema was the father of Raham, who was the father of Jorkeam. Rekem was the father of Shammai, who was the father of Maon. Maon was the father of Beth Zur. The name Raham means "to have mercy." The name Jorkeam probably means "the people are green" in the sense that "the people are growing or spreading." The name Shammai means "my name has been heard." The name Maon means "place to live." The name Beth Zur means "house of rock."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the names of these descendants of Caleb. Pause this audio here.

Ephah, Caleb's concubine, was the mother of Haran, Moza, and Gazez. Haran also had a son named Gazez. Be sure to translate concubine the same way that you have in previous passages. The name Ephah means "dark." The name Haran means "dry." The name Moza could mean "to go out." The name Gazez means "one who shears sheep."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these names. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Jahdai were called Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph. The name Jahdai probably means "Yahweh leads." The name Regem means "pile of stones." The name Jotham means "Yahweh is perfect." The name Geshan means "strong." The name Pelet means "escape." The name Ephah means "darkness." The name Shaaph might mean "to desire."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these names. Pause this audio here.

Maacah, Caleb's concubine, was the mother of Sheber and Tirhanah. Maacah was also the mother of Shaaph, the father of Madmannah, and of Sheba, the father of Macbenah and Gibea. The name Maacah means "to press down." The name Sheber means "hope." We are not certain what the name Tirhanah means, but it might mean "kindness." The name Shaaph might mean "to desire." The name Madmannah means "manure pit." The name Sheba means "false." The name Macbenah means "a belt or a cloak." The name Gibea means "hill."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these names. Pause this audio here.

Caleb also had a daughter named Acsah. The name Acsah means "anklet," which is a piece of jewelry that people wore on their ankle.

Stop here and look at a picture of an anklet. Discuss these questions: In your culture, what do you call a chain or piece of jewelry that someone wears around the ankle? Could you use this word as a name for Caleb's daughter? If not, how will you translate the name of Caleb's daughter?

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 2:50b–55
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 2:50b-55 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:50b-55 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:50b-55 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller of the Book of Chronicles continued to tell us about the descendants of Caleb. In this passage, the storyteller focuses on telling us about the descendants of Caleb's son Hur. Hur was the oldest son of Caleb's wife Ephrathah. Caleb married Ephrathah after his wife Azubah died, so Hur was not Caleb's firstborn son.

In this passage, the storyteller uses the word "father" not only to describe relationships between people but also to describe people who founded cities. Because of this, when you listened to different translations of this passage, you might have heard the speaker say "Shobal was the father of Kiriath Jearim" or "Shobal was the founder of Kiriath Jearim." Both of these ways of saying it mean the same thing: Shobal was the Israelite who started living in Kiriath Jearim.

Several of Hur's descendants founded important towns in the territory of the tribe of Judah. Hur's son Shobal founded Kiriath Jearim, his son Salma founded Bethlehem, and his son Hareph founded the town of Beth Gader. Bible scholars know where Kiriath Jearim and Bethlehem were located, but they do not know where Beth Gader was located.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel. Look for the towns of Kiriath Jearim and Bethlehem. You might want to continue to look at this map during the discussion. Pause this audio here.

Kiriath Jearim is about 13 kilometers northwest of Jerusalem. This town once belonged to the Gibeonites, one of the Canaanite tribes. However, the Israelites eventually went and lived in this town. Bethlehem is located about 10 kilometers south of Jerusalem. Bethlehem was the hometown of King David. Bible scholars do not know anything about the town of Beth Gader. No one mentions this town anywhere else in the Bible. The storyteller of Chronicles might have assumed that his audience knew why Beth Gader was important, but modern audiences do not.

The descendants of Shobal lived in and around Kiriath Jearim. Shobal's descendants divided themselves into clans, or smaller groups of families. The storyteller of Chronicles tells us that these clans were known as the Ithrites, the Puthites, the Shumathites, and the Mishraites.

Apparently, the descendants of Shobal grew to large enough numbers that they needed to start new towns. Some of the descendants of Shobal lived in two towns that people called Zorah and Eshtaol. These two towns were right next to each other, just a few kilometers west of Kiriath Jearim.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Kiriath Jearim, Zorah, and Eshtaol. Pause this audio here.

In this passage, we hear some information that is difficult to understand in the original language. Whenever this is the case, you might hear different explanations when you listen to different translations of the Bible. You might even hear some different versions of names. All this means is that Bible scholars are not sure what the storyteller meant to say in the original language.

For example, Bible scholars are not sure if the name "Haroeh" refers to a person, a clan, or a town. It is the same for the name "Manahath." The storyteller of Chronicles probably assumed that his audience knew what these names refer to, but Bible scholars now do not know for sure.

Salma was the founder of Bethlehem. Salma's descendants lived in that town and the surrounding area. Some of Salma's descendants lived in a town people called Netophah, which is located eight kilometers southeast of Bethlehem.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Bethlehem and Netophah. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller of Chronicles tells us that the descendants of Salma include "Atroth Beth Joab" and half the Manahathites. Bible scholars are not sure if the words "Atroth Beth Joab" refer to a person, a clan, or a place. Bible scholars also do not know if the Manahathites were a clan or the people who lived in a town.

In this passage, the storyteller tells us that some of the descendants of Salma were scribes. Scribes were important and respected people in Israelite culture. People hired a scribe because the scribe was able to read and write. A scribe's most important job was to make perfect copies of the Old Testament books. But Israelite scribes probably did other jobs too. Scribes might have copied other written documents, taught the Old Testament to the Israelites, worked in the temple or the house of the king, and even served as elders, judges, or advisers. In this passage, the storyteller of Chronicles tells us that there were entire families who had members working as scribes.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, how do you describe a person who is able to read and write? How common is it in your culture that a person is able to read and write? What jobs require someone to be able to read and write? What jobs can you think of in which a person does things similar to what a scribe does? Pause this audio here.

The families of scribes lived in a town called Jabez. Bible scholars do not agree on where the town of Jabez was located. Some scholars think Jabez was probably close to Bethlehem.

The last section of this passage is very difficult to understand in the original language. Bible scholars do not agree on what the word "Kenites" means here, who Hammath was, or what "house of Rechab" means. You might have heard different translations of this verse when you listened to different translations of the Bible.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:50b-55 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the sons of Hur and the names of the towns the sons founded.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists the names of the clans descended from Shobal, as well as the names of some towns where the clans lived.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists names of the clans descended from Salma.

The storyteller does not list any descendants of Hareph.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Hur, Caleb, Ephrathah, Shobal, Salma, Hareph
  • The people of Haroeh and Manahath; the Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, Mishraites, Zorathites, Eshtaolites
  • And the people of Netophath, Atroth Beth Joab, Manahath, the Zorites, Tirathites, Shimeathites, Sucathites, Kenites, Recabites, and Hammath

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller lists three sons of Hur named Shobal, Salma, and Hareph. Each son founded a town. Shobal was the founder of Kiriath Jearim, Salma was the founder of Bethlehem, and Hareph was the founder of Beth Gader.

Stop here and look again at the map that shows the locations of Kiriath Jearim and Bethlehem. Keep looking at the map during this discussion. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and decide how you will translate that each of these sons was the father of a town or the founder of the town. Pause this audio here.

If you have translated the book of Joshua, you might remember that Kiriath Jearim belonged to the Gibeonites, who were a Canaanite tribe. The Gibeonites tricked the Israelites so that the Israelites agreed not to kill the Gibeonites. As part of this agreement, the Gibeonites became the slaves of the Israelites. This means that other people had already built the city of Kiriath Jearim before the Israelites moved there. When the storyteller of Chronicles says that Shobal was the founder of Kiriath Jearim, this probably means that Shobal was the leader of the first group of Israelites to start living in that town. The Israelites might have lived alongside the Gibeonites, or it is possible that the Israelites lived in their own section of the town. But the storyteller gives Shobal the credit for being the first person to start an Israelite settlement in Kiriath Jearim. Kiriath Jearim became an important town in Judah. Before King David built the temple, people kept the ark of the covenant in Kiriath Jearim for about 20 years.

Bethlehem was a small but very important town in Israel. Rachel, one of the four wives of Jacob, was buried in Bethlehem. Bethlehem was also the hometown of King David. It is likely that the original audience of the Book of Chronicles would have heard the prophecy about Bethlehem that Yahweh gave to the prophet Micah. The words of the prophecy are, "Yahweh says, 'Bethlehem Ephrathah, you are one of the smallest towns of Judah, but out of you I will bring a ruler for Israel, whose family line goes back to ancient times.'" Micah probably lived in the eighth century B.C., about 250 years after King David was alive, so this prophecy does not refer to David or to Solomon. It refers to a ruler who had not yet been born when the storyteller of Chronicles wrote his book.

In the second scene, some names refer to clans, while other terms refer to people who came from a town.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how do you refer to someone who comes from a particular town? Pause this audio here.

The first statement in this scene is difficult to understand in the original language. It clearly says that Shobal had descendants, but the words that follow are not typical names. The word "Haroeh" means "the prophet." Bible scholars do not know if this is a person's name, a title for a person, or the name of a place. Most translations of the Bible translate this word in a way so that it sounds like the original word, but some translations say "the people of Haroeh."

The next group of people in this list of descendants is "half the Manahathites." Bible scholars do not agree on what "half the Manahathites" means. Scholars do not even agree on what the correct pronunciation of the word "Manahathites" should be. Some Bible scholars think Manahath was a town, and for this reason, you might have heard some Bible translations say "half of the people who lived in Manahath."

Fortunately, the rest of this scene is easier to understand. Most Bible scholars think that the words Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, and Mishraites refer to people who were descended from ancestors named Jether, Puth, Shuma, and Mishra. These four clans, or family groups, probably lived in Kiriath Jearim. In contrast, the words Zorathites and Eshtaolites refer to people who lived in the towns called Zorah and Eshtaol.

Stop here and remember what you previously discussed. How did you decide to refer to a group of people who are descended from a particular ancestor? What is the best way in your language to refer to the descendants of Jether, Puth, Shuma, and Mishra? How did you decide to refer to a group of people who live in a specific town? What is the best way in your language to refer to the people who lived in the towns of Zorah and Eshtaol? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller tells us about the descendants of Salma. Many of these people lived in Bethlehem or the area surrounding Bethlehem. Netophah was a town eight kilometers from Bethlehem.

The words "Atroth Beth Joab" mean "Crown of the House of Joab." Bible scholars do not know if this is the name of a person, a title for a person, or the name of a town. For this reason, most Bible translations translate this phrase to sound like it does in the original language.

Stop here and discuss the best way to say "Atroth Beth Joab" in your language. Pause this audio here.

The next group of people in this list of descendants of Salma is "half of the Manahathites." Bible scholars do not agree on what this means. Some scholars think that Manahath was a town. These scholars interpret this entire passage to mean that half of the people who lived in Manahath were descendants of Shobal and that the other half were descendants of Salma. For this reason, some translations of this verse say "the other half of the Manahathites" or that these descendants of Salma were one of two clans that lived in the town of Manahath.

The Zorites are the next group in this list of descendants. Bible scholars are not sure if this refers to a clan of descendants of Salma, to the name of a town, or both.

Fortunately, it is clear that the next group of descendants, the scribes who are descended from Salma, consist of three clans: the Tirathites, Shimeathites and Sucathites. A scribe was a person who could read and write. In Israel, a scribe's most important job was to make perfect copies of the Old Testament, especially the first five books, which they sometimes called the Books of Moses or the Book of the Law. Because the scribes spent so much of their time reading and writing the Old Testament, they understood it very well, and so scribes were also religious teachers.

Stop here and discuss what the best way is to refer to the Tirathites, Shimeathites, and Sucathites in your language. Pause this audio here.

The last section of this passage is very hard to understand in the original language. We suggest you use this statement as a model for translation: "These were the Kenites who were descendants of Hammath, the founder of the city of Beth Recab."

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 2:50b-55 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Hur, Caleb, Ephrathah, Shobal, Salma, Hareph
  • The people of Haroeh and Manahath; the Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, Mishraites, Zorathites, Eshtaolites
  • And the people of Netophath, Atroth Beth Joab, Manahath, the Zorites, Tirathites, Shimeathites, Sucathites, Kenites, Recabites, and Hammath

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite listening to the passage.

First, the person playing the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you could also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, think about how the ancient Israelites might have felt as they listened to these lists of names. Imagine that you are an ancient Israelite and a member of the tribe of Judah. You live in a village close to Jerusalem. Your family has worked very hard to make a living in your village. Your people live in some of the towns the storyteller mentions in this passage, but foreigners control some of these towns, and your people cannot live in them. This section of the Book of Chronicles reminds you of your people's important ancestors. It reminds you that your ancestors did important things in this land. When you hear about the town of Bethlehem, you remember King David and his son, Solomon, who became Israel's two greatest kings. You know about Micah's prophecy of a future ruler who will come from Bethlehem, and you wonder if that promised ruler will become the ruler during your lifetime, or maybe during the lifetime of your children. When you hear about the scribes who were descendants of Salma, you feel grateful for them because they worked hard to write down Micah's prophecy and teach it to all of your people. Because of this, your people can have hope that a better future is coming and that all your hard work to survive is worth it.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should read or recite this passage a second time. At certain points, pause and ask each person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Each person should answer what they would be feeling or thinking. Then continue reading or reciting the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the passage and possible responses.

The descendants of Hur, the firstborn of Caleb's wife Ephrathah, were Shobal, the founder of Kiriath Jearim; Salma, the founder of Bethlehem; and Hareph, the founder of Beth Gader.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am impressed that Hur was the ancestor of so many important Israelites; or
  • I feel proud that our people established these cities; or
  • I feel ashamed that our people have not regained all of our territory.

Ask the people playing the ancient Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • It is important to remember who founded the town of Bethlehem, the hometown of King David; or
  • I wish our people were as strong now as they were back then; or
  • I am so glad that we are learning this important history about our people.

Continue reading or reciting the passage.
