Jabez prayed to the God of Israel, and God answered his prayers. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you have previously, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you have used for the general name for God in previous books of the Bible. For more information about God, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Jabez asks God to have his "hand" on him. When the storyteller of Chronicles talks about someone's hand, he is talking about their power. Jabez is asking God for God's power or God's presence to be with him.

Stop here and, as a group, discuss how you will talk about God's hand or God's presence being with Jabez.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

acai.preferred_label: Bring Honor and Glory on Oneself

title: 1 Chronicles 4:11–20
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 4:11–20 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:11–20 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:11–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, you heard a list of more sons, or descendants, of Judah. Some of those descendants settled towns in the land of Judah. The list ended with Jabez, who prayed to the God of Israel, and God answered Jabez's prayers.

In this passage, you will hear more lists of the family line of Judah. This list begins with Kelub. We do not know exactly who Kelub's father was, although some people think Kelub is Caleb, the son of Hezron, who appeared in an earlier list of Judah's descendants. People may also call Kelub by the names Caleb or Chelubai. Kelub was Shuhah's brother, and Kelub was the father of a son named Mehir. Mehir was the father of Eshton. The Bible does not tell us anything else about Shuhah or Mehir.

Eshton was the father of three sons named Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah. Tehinnah was the father or founder of the city of Ir Nahash, which means that Tehinnah settled the city of Nahash. The descendants of these men lived in the city of Recah. We are not sure where the cities of Nahash and Recah may have been located, but they were probably in the territory of Judah.

Kenaz had sons, or descendants, named Othniel and Seraiah. The descendants of Kenaz, known as the Kenizzites, became a tribe in southern Israel that eventually joined the southern kingdom of Judah. Othniel was probably a brother or nephew of Caleb, the son of Jephunneh. This is probably a different man from the Kelub or Caleb mentioned earlier in the passage. You will remember from a previous passage that Caleb, the son of Jephunneh, was a former Israelite spy, and he helped Joshua and the Israelites conquer the land God promised to give them.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story about two different people who had the same name but who did very different things. Pause this audio here.

Othniel conquered the city of Debir when the Israelites took the land God had promised them. As a reward, Caleb allowed Othniel to marry Caleb's daughter, Acsah. Othniel became Israel's first judge in the time before kings ruled over Israel. Othniel had sons named Hathath and Meonothai, and Meonothai was the father of a son named Ophrah. Kenaz's son Seraiah was the father of Joab.

Stop here and look at a map as a group that contains Israel, Judah, the territory of the Kenizzites, and the city of Debir. Pause this audio here.

Joab was the father or founder of a place called Ge Harashim, which means "valley of the craftsmen." That place was called the valley of the craftsmen because the people who lived there were skilled in making things. This valley was probably northwest of Jerusalem, but we are not sure where it was located.

Caleb was the son of Jephunneh, and Caleb had sons named Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah had a son named Kenaz. We are not sure if this Kenaz is the same as the Kenaz the storyteller mentioned before, who had sons named Othniel and Seraiah.

In the rest of the list, the Bible does not tell us anything else about these people, and we are not sure how each person may be related to the rest of the family line. We only know that these descendants came from the family line of Judah, one of Israel's sons.

Jehallelel had sons named Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel. Ziph may have been a grandson of Caleb. The Bible does not tell us how Jehallelel and his sons may be related to anyone else in this list of descendants.

Ezrah had sons named Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon. Mered married a woman named Bithiah from Egypt. Bithiah was a daughter of the Pharaoh, or the king of Egypt. Mered and Bithiah had children named Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah. Ishbah was the father of Eshtemoa. We are not sure if this means Ishbah was the father of a person named Eshtemoa or if Ishbah was the founder of a town called Eshtemoa, which was south of Hebron. Mered also married an Israelite woman from the tribe of Judah, and they had three sons. Their son Jered was the father or founder of Gedor, their son Heber was the father of the town of Soco, and their son Jekuthiel was the father of the town of Zanoah. Gedor, Soco, and Zanoah were towns located in Judah, but we are not sure exactly where they were.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about some of the towns or places in your area. How do people name those places? Pause this audio here.

Hodiah married Naham's sister. Their descendants were the fathers of Keilah and Eshtemoa. Keilah's descendants were a clan or family group called the Garmites, and Eshtemoa's descendants were a clan or family group called the Maacathites. The storyteller is not very clear about these clans and the towns in which they lived.

Shimon had sons named Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-Hanan, and Tilon. Ishi had sons named Zoheth and Ben-Zoheth. The Bible does not give us any more information on any of these people.

Stop here and discuss as a group: We do not know much about many of the people the storyteller listed in this passage. How do you talk about people in your culture when you do not know many details about them?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:11–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has 2 scenes.

In the first scene: This list contains more members of the family of Judah. The list begins with Shuhah's brother Kelub and provides several generations of names down to Kenaz.

In the second scene: The list continues with Jehallelel and provides the names of more descendants and towns those men founded. This passage ends with Zoheth and Ben-Zoheth.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Shuhah
  • Kelub
  • Mehir
  • Eshton
  • Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah
  • Kenaz
  • Othniel and Seraiah
  • Hathath and Meonothai
  • Ophrah
  • Joab
  • Jephunneh
  • Caleb
  • Iru, Elah, and Naam
  • Kenaz
  • Jehallelel
  • Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel
  • Ezrah
  • Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon
  • Bithiah
  • Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah
  • A woman from the tribe of Judah
  • Jered, Heber, and Jekuthiel
  • Hodiah
  • Naham's sister
  • Keilah and Eshtemoa
  • Shimon
  • Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-Hanan, and Tilon
  • Ishi
  • And Zoheth and Ben-Zoheth

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In scene one, Shuhah's brother Kelub was the father of Mehir. Mehir was the father of Eshton. Eshton was the father of Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah. Tehinnah was the father or founder of the city of Nahash. The descendants of these men lived in a place called Recah. Although Nahash and Recah are probably cities, the storyteller does not say that they are. You may need to state that these are names of cities.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In the previous passage, you heard that sometimes the word "father" describes a relationship between a man and his son. In other places, "father" means the man who founded or settled a particular city. Remind each other how you translated the two different meanings of this word in your language so that you can use the same words or phrases again to be consistent. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will show that Nahash and Recah are not the names of people, but the names of cities. Pause this audio here.

Kenaz had sons named Othniel and Seraiah. Othniel's sons were Hathath and Meonothai, who was the father of Ophrah. Seraiah was the father of Joab, who was the father or founder of Ge Harashim, the valley of craftsmen. People called this place the "valley of the craftsmen" because the people who lived there were good at making things.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In the previous script, you discussed how people name places in your area. What will you call this valley of craftsmen? Pause this audio here.

Caleb was the son of Jephunneh, and Caleb's sons were Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah's son was Kenaz.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In some places in this passage, the storyteller emphasizes who the father of someone is. In other places, the storyteller emphasizes who the sons of someone are. In your culture, is it more important to talk about fathers or sons? Is there a difference in the way you will talk about someone's father or someone's sons? Pause this audio here.

In scene two, Jehallelel had sons named Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel.

Ezrah had sons named Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon. Mered had two wives. One of Mered's wives was named Bithiah, and she was the daughter of the Pharaoh or king of Egypt. Bithiah and Mered had children named Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah, who was the father of a person called Eshtemoa or the founder of a city called Eshtemoa. Mered's other wife was an Israelite from the tribe of Judah. She and Mered had sons named Jered, Heber, and Jekuthiel. Jered was the father or founder of the city of Gedor. Heber was the father or founder of the city of Soco, and Jekuthiel was the father or founder of the city of Zanoah.

Hodiah's wife was Naham's sister. Hodiah and his wife had two sons. One son was the father of Keilah, who came from the family group of the Garmites. The other son was the father of Eshtemoa, who came from the family group of the Maacathites.

Shimon had sons named Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-Hanan, and Tilon.

Ishi had sons or descendants named Zoheth and Ben-Zoheth.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:11–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Shuhah
  • Kelub
  • Mehir
  • Eshton
  • Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah
  • Kenaz
  • Othniel and Seraiah
  • Hathath and Meonothai
  • Ophrah
  • Joab
  • Jephunneh
  • Caleb
  • Iru, Elah, and Naam
  • Kenaz
  • Jehallelel
  • Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel
  • Ezrah
  • Jether, Mered, Epher and Jalon
  • Bithiah
  • Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah
  • A woman from the tribe of Judah
  • Jered, Heber, and Jekuthiel
  • Hodiah
  • Naham's sister
  • Keilah and Eshtemoa
  • Shimon
  • Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-Hanan, and Tilon
  • Ishi
  • And Zoheth and Ben-Zoheth

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person who plays the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If this section is too difficult to remember, you may listen to an audio recording again.

While you do this, think about the Israelites. When they heard this list of names, the Israelites would remember God's faithfulness to keep God's promises. The Israelites may have thought about how God kept different groups of Israelite people safe through several generations. The Israelites also may have thought about how God abundantly blessed them with many children.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask one of the characters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Then the person playing that character should answer what that character would be feeling or thinking. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Kelub, Shuhah's brother, was the father of Mehir, who was the father of Eshton, who was the father of Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah. Tehinnah was the father, or founder, of the city of Ir Nahash. These men's descendants lived in the city of Recah.

Kenaz had sons named Othniel and Seraiah. Othniel's sons were Hathath and Meonothai. Meonothai was the father of Ophrah. Seraiah was the father or founder of the place called Ge Harashim, which means valley of craftsmen. People called this place the valley of craftsmen because the people who lived there were skilled in making things.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the storyteller of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud to know more of the descendants of the tribe of Judah; or
  • I celebrate what the Lord has done through the generations.

Continue to recite the passage.

Caleb was the son of Jephunneh, and Caleb had sons named Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah had a son named Kenaz. Jehallelel had sons named Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel. Ezrah had sons named Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon.

Mered had two wives. One wife was named Bithiah, and she was the daughter of the pharaoh or king of Egypt. Mered and his wife Bithiah had children named Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah, who was either the father of a man called Eshtemoa or the founder of the town of Eshtemoa. Mered and his other wife, an Israelite from Judah, had sons named Jered, the father or founder of the town of Gedor; Heber, the father or founder of the town of Soco; and Jekuthiel, the father or founder of the town of Zanoah.

Naham's sister was Hodiah's wife, and they had sons named Keilah, who was from the Garmite family group, and Eshtemoa, who was from the family group of the Maacathites.

Shimon's sons were Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-Hanan, and Tilon. Ishi's descendants were Zoheth and Ben-Zoheth.

Pause the reciting. Ask the people playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are amazed at how God has worked to provide for us and to keep us safe from the time we entered the land; or
  • We are grateful that God has blessed us with so many descendants; or
  • We look forward to what God will do for us while we wait for the ruler God promised to give us.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:11–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Kelub, Shuhah's brother, was the father of Mehir, who was the father of Eshton, who was the father of Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah. Tehinnah was the father, or founder, of the city of Ir Nahash. Use the same word for father or founder as you used in previous passages.

The descendants of these men lived in the city of Recah. A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. In some places in this passage, the word sons can also mean descendants. Use the same word or phrase for descendants as you used in previous passages. For more information on descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

Kenaz had sons named Othniel and Seraiah. Othniel's sons were Hathath and Meonothai. The word son can mean the male child of his parents, or it can mean all of a person's descendants over generations. In this part of the passage, the word means the male child of his parents. Use the same word or phrase for sons as you used in previous passages. For more information on sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

Meonothai was the father of Ophrah. Seraiah was the father or founder of the place called Ge Harashim, which means "valley of the craftsmen." People called this place the valley of the craftsmen because the people who lived there were skilled in making things.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you will use for craftsmen. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the word or phrase that you used there. Pause this audio here.

Caleb was the son of Jephunneh, and Caleb had sons named Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah had a son named Kenaz. Jehallelel had sons named Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel. Ezrah had sons named Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon.

Remember that Ir Nahash, Recah, Eshtemoa, Gedor, Soco, and Zanoah are cities, and Ge Harashim is the "valley of the craftsmen." You may need to state that these are cities and a valley so that your audience will not think that these are people's names. If you use the word for city, use the same word that you have used in previous passages for city.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will show that these are place names. Pause this audio here.

Mered had two wives. One wife was named Bithiah, and she was the daughter of the pharaoh. Pharaoh is the title for the king in the country of Egypt.

Stop here and discuss what word or phrase you will use for pharaoh. Use the same word or phrase for pharaoh as you used in previous passages. For more information on pharaoh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 4:21–23
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 4:21–23 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:21–23 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:21–23 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller gave a list of more descendants of Judah, who was one of Israel's sons. The Bible talks about many of those descendants only in that passage.

In this passage, the storyteller gives a list of the descendants of Shelah, who was one of Judah's sons. Shelah's mother was a Canaanite woman, the daughter of Shua. Shelah named one of his sons Er. Shelah may have chosen this name to honor Er, one of Judah's other sons. Shelah's son Er was the father or founder of the town of Lecah. Another of Shelah's sons was named Laadah, and he founded, or was the father of, the town of Mareshah. We are not sure of the exact locations of any of the towns the storyteller mentioned in this passage, except for the town of Mareshah. We do know that these towns were probably located in the southern part of Israel, in the territory that became the southern kingdom of Judah.

Stop here and look at a map as a group. Look at the territory of the southern kingdom of Judah. Find the location of the town of Mareshah. Pause this audio here.

Shelah had more descendants, who included the families or clans who worked with linen. Linen is a white cloth material that people weave from the fibers of the flax plant. Linen is a strong material that helps people stay cool when they wear it. We are not certain if Ashbea is a place or a person. It could be that the linen workers lived in a town called Beth Ashbea, or it may be that these people came from the house or family of a person named Ashbea.

Stop here and look at a picture of linen or fine white cloth as a group. Pause this audio here.

Shelah had more descendants who included Jokim, the men of the town of Cozeba, and Joash and Saraph. These men probably lived before the Babylonian exile. These men probably went to Moab to take wives. It is unclear what these men did next. Most scholars believe that these men then returned to Bethlehem, but this could also mean that these men went to rule over Moab before they returned to Bethlehem. Moab was a country east of Judah, on the other side of the Dead Sea. The people who lived in Moab were not part of God's people. Bethlehem was a small city in Israel.

Stop here and look at a map as a group. Find the country of Moab and the city of Bethlehem. Pause this audio here.

The people in these lists come from ancient times, probably before the Babylonian people took the people of Judah as prisoners to the city of Babylon. People probably thought these lists were old before the storyteller of Chronicles wrote the book.

These people were potters, or people who made pots and jars out of clay. We are not sure if all of Shelah's descendants made pots or if only some of them became potters. These potters lived at Netaim and Gederah, which means "plants and hedges." However, these are two cities in southern Judah. These people probably lived on land that one of the kings provided. The king probably gave the potters the materials they needed to make jars and pots for the king, so these people worked for the king.

Stop here and look at a picture of a pot as a group. Why do you think making pots for the king might be an important job?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:21–23 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has one scene.

In the first scene: One of Judah's sons is named Shelah, and Shelah had several descendants. These descendants probably lived before the Babylonian exile.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Judah
  • Shelah
  • Er
  • Laadah
  • Families of linen workers at Beth Ashbea
  • Jokim
  • Men of Cozeba
  • Joash
  • Saraph
  • And the king

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Shelah was one of Judah's sons. Shelah had a son named Er, who founded or was the father of the city of Lecah. Another of Shelah's sons was Laadah, who founded or was the father of the city of Mareshah. Shelah also had descendants who formed the clans or family groups of the people who wove linen cloth at Beth Ashbea. Beth Ashbea may be the name of a place or the name of a person's household.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Some of these descendants formed clans or family groups, and they all had similar jobs. How do you talk about family groups who people know for the kind of work that they do in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Shelah had more descendants: Jokim, the men who lived in the city of Cozeba, and Joash and Saraph. These descendants went to the country of Moab to get wives before they returned to Bethlehem. The storyteller now gives us the extra information that the records of these descendants are very old. People probably created these records before the people of Babylon took the Israelites as prisoners.

Stop here and discuss as a group: The storyteller of Chronicles gives some extra information when he tells us about these "ancient" records. What is the best natural way for you to include this extra information from the storyteller in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Do you keep very old records of people in your culture? How do you normally communicate lists or stories of your ancestors? Pause this audio here.

Some of these people worked as potters where they lived in the towns of Netaim and Gederah. These potters created pots and jars for a king. We do not know exactly when these people lived in these towns or which king they served.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:21–23 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has one scene.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Judah
  • Shelah
  • Er
  • Laadah
  • Families of linen workers at Beth Ashbea
  • Jokim
  • Men of Cozeba
  • Joash
  • Saraph
  • And the king

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the author of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person who plays the author should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If this section is too difficult to remember, you may listen to an audio recording again.

While you do this, think about the Israelites. When the Israelites heard this list of names, they would remember God's faithfulness to keep God's promises. The Israelites may have thought about how God kept different groups of Israelite people safe through several generations.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should recite this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask one of the characters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" Then the person playing that character should answer what that character would be feeling or thinking. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Shelah was one of Judah's sons. Shelah had sons named Er, who founded Lecah, and Laadah, who founded Mareshah. Shelah also had descendants in the clans or family groups who wove linen cloth at Beth Ashbea.

Pause the reciting. Ask the people playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We celebrate our clans who were successful in weaving linen; or
  • We feel sad that some of the important places where our descendants lived no longer exist.

Continue to recite the passage.

More of Shelah's descendants are Jokim, the men from the town of Cozeba, and Joash and Saraph. These men went to Moab to find wives to marry, and then they returned to the city of Bethlehem. These records are very old.

Pause the reciting. Ask the person playing the author of Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am surprised that some of the people looked for wives in Moab instead of in Israel; or
  • I am happy to know that God continues to use the Israelites through the generations.

Continue to recite the passage.

These descendants were potters who made pots and jars. These descendants lived in the towns of Netaim and Gederah, and they worked for the king.

Pause the reciting. Ask the people playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are happy to know that our ancestors did good work weaving linen cloth and making pots; or
  • We feel surprise and comfort when we hear the lists of people that God preserved for us to remember.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:21–23 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Shelah was the son of Judah. A son is the male child of his parents, and Shelah was the male child of his father, Judah. Sometimes in this passage, "sons" means the same thing as descendants. A person's descendants include their children, grandchildren, and anyone else in the direct family line. Use the same word for son and descendants as you used in previous passages. For more information on son and descendant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Shelah had sons named Er, who founded the city of Lecah, and Laadah, who founded the city of Mareshah. Shelah also had sons or descendants in the clans or family groups who wove linen cloth in the city of Beth Ashbea. Linen is a fine, white cloth that people weave from the flax plant. You may need to state that Lecah, Laadah, Mareshah, and Beth Ashbea are cities. Use the same word for clans or family groups, and cities, as you have used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for linen. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Other sons of Shelah include Jokim, the men of the city of Cozeba, and Joash and Saraph. These men went to the country of Moab to find wives before they returned to the city of Bethlehem. Moab was a country to the east of Judah. The people who lived in Moab were not part of God's people, the Israelites. Bethlehem was a small village in Israel. Use the same words or phrases for Moab and Bethlehem as you used in previous passages. For more information on Bethlehem, refer to the Master Glossary.

These records, or lists of descendants, are from ancient or very old times. Some of these descendants were potters who lived in the cities of Netaim and Gederah and worked for the king. A potter was a person who made clay pots and jars. A king is a ruler of a city or country. Use the same word for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss what word or phrase you will use for potter. Look at a picture of a pot as a group. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 4:24–43
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 4:24–43 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:24–43 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:24–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The previous passage talks about the descendants of Judah's son Shelah.

In this story, the storyteller now tells the history of the tribe of Simeon. The storyteller talks about the family history of some of Simeon's descendants, where they lived, and the lands their leaders conquered. You will remember that the storyteller of Chronicles tells the story to the Israelites who recently came from the place where the conquering nation had taken them as captives. These Israelites, from the southern kingdom of Judah, were in that foreign land for 70 years. Some Israelites return home to their own land. Simeon's land no longer exists separately because it becomes part of Judah's land. The storyteller likely reminds the descendants of Simeon of the special agreement that Yahweh made with the people of Israel. Yahweh gave the Israelites land and promised to bless them if they obeyed him. The storyteller probably wants to give the people of Israel hope that Yahweh still remembers his promises to his people.

Simeon is Jacob's second son and Abraham's great-grandson. The storyteller says Simeon has five sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul. Three other Bible passages also list Simeon's descendants, but those passages differ in the number and names of Simeon's sons. This account does not mention Simeon's third son, Ohad. We do not know why, but maybe Ohad's descendants died out and could not form a family clan.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you decide what really happened if there are different versions of the same story? Talk about why there might be different versions of a family history in your culture. Pause this audio here.

Simeon's sons started families, or clans, that became part of the tribe of Simeon. We do not know much about Simeon's sons, except for Shaul. You might remember that Shaul's mother was a Canaanite woman, which made Shaul different from his brothers. Now, the storyteller focuses on Shaul's descendants. In the book of Chronicles, when the storyteller lists several brothers, the lists often follow the family line of the youngest brother on the list. Shaul was the father of Shallum. Shallum was the father of Mibsam. Mibsam was the father of Mishma. This family list shows three generations.

Mishma's descendants were Hammuel, Zaccur, and Shimei. This likely means that Mishma was the father of Hammuel, Hammuel was the father of Zaccur, and Zaccur was the father of Shimei. This family list shows three generations. The Bible does not tell us much about these men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What rules does your culture have for how to share family history? How do you choose which descendants to include and which ones to leave out? What are the rules about including women in the family history? Pause this audio here.

This story ends this part of Simeon's family history with Shimei, who had 16 sons and 6 daughters. Shimei's relatives did not have many children, so the tribe of Simeon did not grow as much as the tribe of Judah. Yahweh gave the tribe of Simeon a small area of land inside Judah's land. By the time the storyteller tells this story, the tribe of Simeon is no longer separate. It has become part of the tribe of Judah.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the territory of the tribe of Simeon. Pause this audio here.

In the second part of the story, the storyteller lists the cities where the descendants of Simeon lived. These cities are in the southern part of Judah's territory because that is where the tribe of Simeon's land was. The first of the cities of the tribe of Simeon was Beersheba, which people knew as the most southern city of Israel. All the cities and villages that the story mentions from now on are near Beersheba. The other cities are: Moladah, Hazar Shual, Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad, Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag, Beth Marcaboth, Hazar Susim, Beth Biri, and Shaaraim. The families from the tribe of Simeon owned these cities before David became king of Israel. The following settlements surrounded these cities: Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Token, and Ashan, five towns or villages in all. There were other settlements around the same cities, and they went as far as the city of Baalath. These settlements belonged to the clans of Simeon. The people from the tribe of Simeon wrote down the names of their ancestors. Even though they were a small tribe and lived among the tribe of Judah, the Simeonites kept their own genealogy, or family list.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the cities and settlements in the territory of the tribe of Simeon. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller tells us about another part of the history of the tribe of Simeon, when some leaders of clans or family groups conquer more land. These people may have been officers in the tribal military units. These people's names were Meshobab, Jamlech, and Joshah, whose father was Amaziah. Other leaders were Joel and Jehu, whose father was Joshibiah. Joshibiah's father was Seraiah. Seraiah's father was Asiel. These leaders also included Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, and Benaiah. The leaders also included Ziza, whose father was Shiphi. Shiphi's father was Allon. Allon was the son of Jedaiah. Jedaiah was the son of Shimri. Shimri was the son of Shemaiah. We do not know much about these leaders. The fact that Ziza's family history goes back five generations means that Ziza may have been from an important family. The clans or families of these 13 leaders grew very large.

These leaders went to the region of the city of Gedor, in the eastern part of the valley, where the city was. We do not know exactly where Gedor was. The city might have been called Gerar. The leaders went here to find land for their flocks to eat grass. Flocks include groups of smaller animals like sheep and goats. The leaders found good land with plenty of grass in a wide, quiet, and peaceful open area. Before the leaders of the families of the tribe of Simeon got there, some of Noah's son Ham's descendants lived in the land. These people might have been Egyptians, Cushites, or Canaanites. These people were peaceful shepherds who lived in tents.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story from the history of your people group about a time when the leaders wanted more land. What did the leaders want the land for, and what did they do to get the land? Pause this audio here.

The Simeonite leaders whom we have just heard about came to Gedor when Hezekiah was king of Judah. Hezekiah was king of Judah about 700 years before Jesus' birth. This means these leaders lived many years after the first generations of Simeon's descendants we heard about earlier. The leaders attacked the descendants of Ham and destroyed the tents where they lived. The leaders also attacked the Meunites, a people group who lived there too. The leaders of Simeon's clans completely destroyed both the Hamites and the Meunites, and these people are completely gone now. Yahweh had told the Israelites to destroy the original people who lived in the land he gave the Israelites to live in. Afterward, the leaders from the tribe of Simeon took over the land and settled there because it had good pasture for their flocks.

Some of the descendants of Simeon went on a second battle to take land. Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel, the sons of Ishi who were military leaders, took 500 fighting men and went east to attack the Edomite people who lived near Mount Seir in Edom. Mount Seir was a mountainous area in Edom next to the southeastern border with the tribe of Judah. The Simeonites killed the Amalekites who had stayed in Edom since earlier battles. The Amalekites were the descendants of Amalek, a grandson of Esau. The Amalekites had lived with the Edomites since Saul first chased them there, and David also chased them there later. The Simeonites took the land and still lived there until the storyteller wrote this story.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the territory of Mount Seir in Edom.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:24–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us about the family history of some of Simeon's descendants.

  • In the first part of scene one: The storyteller lists Simeon's sons.

  • In the second part of scene one: The storyteller lists the descendants of Shaul, Simeon's youngest son.

  • In the third part of scene one: The storyteller lists the descendants of Mishma, Shaul's youngest descendant.

  • In the fourth part of scene one: The storyteller lists the descendants of Shimei, Mishma's youngest descendant.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists the cities and settlements where the descendants of Simeon lived.

In the third scene: The storyteller talks about the lands that the leaders of the tribe of Simeon conquered.

  • In the first part of scene three: The storyteller lists the names of leaders of some other descendants of Simeon. These people live in the time of King Hezekiah, several hundred years after the other descendants of Simeon.

  • In the second part of scene three: The leaders travel to the region of the city of Gedor to look for land for their sheep to eat grass. The leaders find good land there and destroy the people groups who live there—the descendants of Noah's son Ham and the Meunites. The leaders take over the land and settle there.

  • In the third part of scene three: Some of Simeon's descendants, four military leaders who are sons of Ishi, take 500 fighting men and go on a second battle to take land. They go to the region of Mount Seir in Edom and defeat the remaining Amalekites who live there. The Simeonites settle in the land.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Simeon
  • Simeon's sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul
  • Shaul's descendants: Shallum, Mibsam, and Mishma
  • Mishma's descendants: Hammuel, Zaccur, and Shimei
  • Shimei's 16 sons and 6 daughters
  • Shimei's brothers
  • Shimei's brothers' children
  • Thirteen leaders of family groups from the tribe of Simeon:
  • Meshobab
  • Jamlech
  • Joshah, son of Amaziah
  • Joel
  • Jehu, son of Joshibiah, whose father was Seraiah, whose father was Asiel
  • Elioenai
  • Jaakobah
  • Jeshohaiah
  • Asaiah
  • Adiel
  • Jesimiel
  • Benaiah
  • Ziza, son of Shiphi, whose father was Allon, whose father was Jedaiah, whose father was Shimri, whose father was Shemaiah
  • The families of the 13 princes or important leaders from the tribe of Simeon
  • King Hezekiah
  • Descendants of Noah's son Ham who live in the region of Gedor
  • The Meunites who live in the region of Gedor
  • Other leaders from the tribe of Simeon who were Ishi's sons:
  • Pelatiah
  • Neariah
  • Rephaiah
  • Uzziel
  • Five hundred fighting men from the tribe of Simeon
  • And the Amalekites who live in the region of Mount Seir

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This family history begins about 1,900 years before Jesus came to Earth and covers about 1,000 years.

Scene one tells the family history of some of Simeon's descendants.

Jacob's second son, Simeon, has five sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul. Three other Bible passages—Genesis 46:10, Exodus 6:15, and Numbers 26:12–13—also list Simeon's descendants, but they differ in the number and names of Simeon's sons.

Stop here. If you have already translated Simeon's family history in other books of the Bible, listen to the stories together as a team. Pay close attention to the names. If a person's name is a little different in the stories, you can either change it to match the other stories or keep the name from this story. Talk together about what you will do and why. Pause this audio here.

Now, the story focuses on Shaul's descendants. In the book of Chronicles, when the story lists several brothers, it then often follows the family line of the youngest brother on the list. Shaul was the father of Shallum. Shallum was the father of Mibsam. Mibsam was the father of Mishma. This family list shows three generations.

Mishma's descendants were Hammuel, Zaccur, and Shimei. This likely means that Mishma was the father of Hammuel, Hammuel was the father of Zaccur, and Zaccur was the father of Shimei. This family list shows three generations. The Bible does not tell us much about these men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the way you will translate this family history when the exact relationship between a person and their descendants is not clear. Pause this audio here.

This story ends this part of Simeon's family history with Shimei, who had 16 sons and 6 daughters. However, Shimei's brothers did not have many children. Therefore, the number of people in the tribe of Simeon did not grow larger like the number of people in the tribe of Judah did. The word "brothers" refers here to Shimei's other relatives rather than Shimei's brothers from the same father. The phrase "men of Judah" refers to the whole tribe of Judah and not just to its male members.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the territory of the tribe of Simeon. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words do you use in your language to refer to relatives in general? What words do you use to talk about the people who are part of your family? Pause this audio here.

Scene two tells about the places where Simeon's descendants lived.

The story lists the cities where the descendants of Simeon lived. These cities are in the southern part of Judah's territory because that is where the tribe of Simeon's land was. Joshua 19:2–8 also lists the cities that the tribe of Simeon received.

Stop here. If you have already translated Joshua 19:2–8, listen to the story together as a team. Pay close attention to the city names and how you translated them in the story in Joshua. If you see a small difference in a city's name between the stories, you can either change the name to match the other name or keep the name from this story. Talk together about what you will do and why. Pause this audio here.

The first of the cities of the tribe of Simeon was Beersheba, which people knew as the most southern city of Israel. All the cities and villages that the story mentions from now on were near Beersheba.

The other cities are: Moladah, Hazar Shual, Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad, Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag, Beth Marcaboth, Hazar Susim, Beth Biri, and Shaaraim. The families from the tribe of Simeon owned these cities before David became king of Israel. The following settlements surrounded these cities: Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Token, and Ashan, five towns or villages in all. There were other settlements around the same cities, and they went as far as the city of Baalath. These settlements belonged to the clans of Simeon. The people from the tribe of Simeon wrote down the names of their ancestors in their own genealogy, or family list.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the cities, villages, and settlements in the territory of the tribe of Simeon. Pause this audio here.

Scene three tells about the lands that the leaders of the tribe of Simeon conquered.

Now the storyteller tells us another part of the history of the tribe of Simeon and lists the names of the princes, or leaders, who are part of it: Meshobab, Jamlech, and Joshah, whose father was Amaziah. Other leaders were Joel and Jehu, whose father was Joshibiah. Joshibiah's father was Seraiah. Seraiah's father was Asiel. These leaders also included Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, and Benaiah. The leaders also included Ziza, whose father was Shiphi. Shiphi's father was Allon. Allon was the son of Jedaiah. Jedaiah was the son of Shimri. Shimri was the son of Shemaiah. The storyteller says that "the princes' fathers' houses increased greatly." This means that the clans or families of these 13 leaders grew very large, so that there were many people in the families.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you talk about the way a family grows large, like the princes' fathers' house that "grew large?" Pause this audio here.

The leaders traveled to the entrance of the city of Gedor, in the eastern part of the valley, where the city was. We do not know exactly where Gedor was, but it was in the eastern part of the valley. You may remember that "east" is the direction from which the sun comes up. The city might have been called Gerar. The word which people translated from the original language as "entrance" means that the leaders went in the direction of Gedor, and very close to it. The leaders went here to find land for their flocks to eat grass. The leaders found good land with plenty of grass in a wide, quiet, and peaceful open area where the descendants of Noah's son Ham lived in tents.

The men whose names the storyteller just listed came to Gedor when Hezekiah was king of Judah. Hezekiah was king of Judah about 700 years before Jesus' birth. This means these leaders lived many years after the first generations of Simeon's descendants we heard about earlier. The leaders attacked the descendants of Ham and destroyed their tents or dwellings. The leaders also attacked the Meunites, a people group who lived there too. The leaders of Simeon's clans completely destroyed both the Hamites and the Meunites, and there is no trace of these people today. The phrase "completely destroy" in the original language means to set people apart for Yahweh. The Israelites did this by killing the Hamites and the Meunites to show that they honoured Yahweh. Afterward, the leaders from the tribe of Simeon took over the land and settled there because it had good pasture for their flocks.

Some of the descendants of Simeon went on a second battle to take land. Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel were the sons of Ishi. These men were military leaders who took 500 fighting men and went east to attack the Edomite people who lived near Mount Seir in Edom. Mount Seir was a mountainous area in Edom next to the southeastern border with the tribe of Judah. The Simeonites killed the Amalekites who had stayed in Edom since earlier battles. The Simeonites took the land and still live there to this day. "This day" refers to the time when the storyteller was telling the story.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the territory of Mount Seir in Edom. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:24–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Simeon
  • Simeon's sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul
  • Shaul's descendants: Shallum, Mibsam, and Mishma
  • Mishma's descendants: Hammuel, Zaccur, and Shimei
  • Shimei's 16 sons and 6 daughters
  • Shimei's brothers
  • Shimei's brothers' children
  • Thirteen princes or leaders of family groups from the tribe of Simeon:
  • Meshobaab
  • Jamlech
  • Joshah, son of Amaziah
  • Joel
  • Jehu, son of Joshibiah, whose father was Seraiah, whose father was Asiel
  • Elioenai
  • Jaakobah
  • Jeshohaiah
  • Asaiah
  • Adiel
  • Jesimiel
  • Benaiah
  • Ziza, son of Shiphi, whose father was Allon, whose father was Jedaiah, whose father was Shimri, whose father was Shemaiah
  • The families of the 13 princes or important leaders from the tribe of Simeon
  • King Hezekiah
  • Descendants of Noah's son Ham who live in the region of Gedor
  • The Meunites who live in the region of Gedor
  • Other leaders from the tribe of Simeon who were Ishi's sons:
  • Pelatiah
  • Neariah
  • Rephaiah
  • Uzziel
  • Five hundred fighting men from the tribe of Simeon
  • And the Amalekites who live in the region of Mount Seir

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Simeon, the son of Jacob, has five sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul.

Shaul was the father of Shallum. Shallum was the father of Mibsam. Mibsam was the father of Mishma.

Mishma's descendants were Hammuel, Zaccur, and Shimei.

Shimei had 16 sons and 6 daughters. However, Shimei's relatives did not have many children. Therefore, the tribe of Simeon did not multiply like the men of Judah.

The descendants of Simeon lived in the following cities: Beersheba, Moladah, Hazar Shual, Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad, Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag, Beth Marcaboth, Hazar Susim, Beth Biri, and Shaaraim. The families from the tribe of Simeon owned these cities before David became king of Israel. The following villages surrounded these cities: Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Token, and Ashan, five towns or villages in all. There were other settlements around the same cities, and they went as far as the city of Baalath. These settlements belonged to the clans of Simeon. The people from the tribe of Simeon wrote down the names of their ancestors in their own family list.

Now the storyteller tells us about 13 princes or leaders from the tribe of Simeon: Meshobab, Jamlech, and Joshah, whose father was Amaziah. Other leaders were Joel and Jehu, whose father was Joshibiah. Joshibiah's father was Seraiah. Seraiah's father was Asiel. These leaders also included Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, and Benaiah. The leaders also included Ziza, whose father was Shiphi. Shiphi's father was Allon. Allon was the son of Jedaiah. Jedaiah was the son of Shimri. Shimri was the son of Shemaiah. The families of these 13 leaders grew very large.

The leaders traveled to the entrance of the city of Gedor, in the eastern part of the valley, where the city was. The leaders went here to find land for their flocks to eat grass. The leaders found good land with plenty of grass in a wide, quiet, and peaceful open area where the descendants of Noah's son Ham lived in tents.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Simeonite leaders, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are grateful to Yahweh that our clans have grown; or
  • Because we have more flocks now, we need to look for more land to feed our sheep; or
  • We are happy that Yahweh has led us to the land near Gedor. This land has plenty of grass and is peaceful.

Continue the drama.

The princes, or leaders, came to Gedor when Hezekiah was king of Judah. The leaders attacked the descendants of Ham and destroyed their tents. The leaders also attacked the Meunites, a people group who lived there too. The leaders of Simeon's clans completely destroyed both the Hamites and the Meunites, and there is no trace of them today. Afterward, the leaders from the tribe of Simeon took over the land and settled there because it had good pasture for their flocks.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Simeonite leaders, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We have to completely destroy the people who live in this land; or
  • We want to obey Yahweh, who said that we cannot spare anyone in the land we are to possess;
  • We trust Yahweh. We will not live in the same land as the descendants of Ham or the Meunites, or we will become like them and start to worship idols.

Continue the drama.

Some of the descendants of Simeon went on a second battle to take land. Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel, the sons of Ishi and military leaders, took 500 fighting men and went east to attack the Edomite people who lived near Mount Seir in Edom. The Simeonites killed the Amalekites who had stayed in Edom since earlier battles. The Simeonites took the land and still live there to this day.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Amalekites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are so scared. The Israelites have attacked us! The Israelites want our land! or
  • The Israelite God is really powerful. What will happen to us now? or
  • We must run away, or the Israelites will kill us!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 4:24–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Simeon has five sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul. The word son can mean either a biological male child of his parents or a descendant, which is any person from all future generations from the same family line. Here, "the sons of Simeon" refers to the biological male children of Simeon. However, the "sons of Mishma" include grandsons and great-grandsons, so you may want to say that these are the "descendants of Mishma" if you are using a different word for descendants. The number of people from the tribe of Simeon did not become as large as the number of people from the tribe of Judah. Use the same words or phrases for Judah, son, and descendants as you used in previous passages. For more information on Judah, son, or descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller talks about cities and their surrounding villages. Cities usually had walls around them for protection. Smaller villages surrounded the cities and probably did not have walls around them. Use the same words or phrases for cities and villages as you used in previous passages. The storyteller summarizes by saying that "these were their settlements." A settlement is a more general term for a place where many homes are together.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for cities, villages, and settlements in this passage. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people from the tribe of Simeon wrote down the names of their ancestors in their own genealogy, or family list. Use the same word or phrase for genealogy as you used in previous passages. For more information on genealogy, refer to the Master Glossary.

Now the storyteller tells us about another part of the history of the tribe of Simeon, when some princes or leaders of clans, or family groups, conquer more land. These leaders' names were Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah, Joel, and Jehu. These leaders also included Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah, and Ziza. The clans or families of these 13 leaders grew very large. Use the same word or phrase for prince or leader as you used in previous passages. Use the same word or phrase for clan or family group as you used in previous passages.

The men whose names the storyteller just listed came to Gedor, in the east part of the valley, when Hezekiah was king of Judah. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for east. Pause this audio here.

The Simeonite leaders attacked the descendants of Ham and destroyed their homes. Here, the homes were tents. People make tents from heavy cloth or animal skins. Tents are easy to pack up and move quickly.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for home or tent. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 5:1–10
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 5:1–10 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:1–10 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The previous story told us about the tribe of Simeon. That story described the family history of some of Simeon's descendants, the places where they lived, and the lands their leaders conquered.

This story is the first of three stories about the tribes that once lived east of the Jordan River: the tribe of Reuben, the tribe of Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. This story focuses on the tribe of Reuben and tells us about some of Reuben's descendants and where those people lived. You will remember that the storyteller tells the story to Israelites who recently came from the place where the conquering nation had taken them as captives. The Israelites were in that place for 70 years. Some Israelites returned home to their land, but the descendants of the tribe of Reuben did not return to their own land. Instead, they settled west of the Jordan River. The storyteller likely reminds Reuben's descendants of the special agreement that Yahweh made with the people of Israel. Yahweh gave the Israelites land and promised to bless them if they obeyed Yahweh. The storyteller probably wants to give the people of Israel hope that Yahweh still remembers his promises to his people.

The storyteller is about to list the sons of Reuben, but pauses to explain Reuben's situation. Reuben was Israel's firstborn son. Israel is the name that Yahweh gave to Jacob, Abraham's grandson. Usually, the firstborn son gets special rights and privileges that other sons do not have. In some cultures, the firstborn even inherits everything and leads the family. These rights and privileges are called the birthright. But Reuben slept with Bilhah, one of Israel's concubines. Because of this, Reuben lost his special birthright and his right to lead the family. Israel took away Reuben's birthright and gave it to the two sons of Israel's son Joseph: Manasseh and Ephraim. You will remember that Israel gave a blessing, or prophecy, to Joseph and his sons just before Israel died.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about special rights which the firstborn son or daughter receives in your culture. Tell a story about how a firstborn or special child in your culture lost their special rights. Pause this audio here.

Even though Israel gave the birthright to Joseph's sons, people did not list Joseph as the firstborn in the family records. This happened because Joseph was not born first. The storyteller also explains that even though Judah was powerful among his brothers, Judah did not have the rights of the firstborn, but Joseph did. You will remember that Jacob had also blessed Judah's descendants before he died, and God made that promise come true in many ways. Under Moses, the tribe of Judah grew strong, and God made Judah the first tribe to take the land of Canaan after Joshua's death. Here, the storyteller tells us that God chose a leader, or prince, from Judah's family to rule Israel. In the original language, the word for "leader" here means a ruler whom God chose. We know this leader was King David. Even though Judah became the strongest tribe, they did not have the birthright. Joseph's sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, kept the special right of the firstborn.

The sons of Reuben, the firstborn son of Israel, or Jacob, were: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. These sons became leaders of major family groups within the tribe of Reuben. You may remember that the descendants of Pallu—Eliab and his sons Dathan and Abiram—rebelled against Moses and Aaron. The Bible does not give us any information about the others.

The storyteller tells us about Joel's family history. Joel came from one of Reuben's sons, but we do not know which son or when. Joel had a son named Shemaiah. Shemaiah had a son named Gog. Gog had a son named Shimei. Shimei had a son named Micah. Micah had a son named Reaiah. Reaiah had a son named Baal. The scriptures do not tell us anything more about these men. The name Baal means "lord" or "master" and was also a title for a Canaanite false god. This shows that the culture of the nations that lived near the Reubenites influenced what the Reubenites named their children. Baal had a son named Beerah. Then Tiglath-Pileser, king of a big kingdom called Assyria, captured Beerah and took him into exile, or to live in Assyria. Beerah was a leader or a prince of a family in the tribe of Reuben. Tiglath-Pileser ruled Assyria for 18 years, around 700 years before Jesus.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate Israel and Assyria. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time in your people's history when enemies captured and took someone away. Talk about what happened. Pause this audio here.

When people wrote down their genealogies, or family histories, and listed each clan, or family group, they recorded that Beerah's relatives included Jeiel, who was their leader.

People also listed Zechariah and Bela. Bela's father was Azaz, Azaz's father was Shema, and Shema's father was Joel. This Joel might be the same one the storyteller mentioned before. All of Beerah's relatives in the list came from either Joel or his brothers. This family group lived east of the Jordan River in an area that stretched from the strong city of Aroer in the south up to Nebo and the city of Baal Meon in the north of the land of the tribe of Reuben. The name Nebo refers to either a mountain called Mount Nebo or a city called Nebo located near the mountain.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate Aroer city, Mount Nebo, Nebo city, and Baal Meon in the land of the tribe of Reuben. Pause this audio here.

While the Reubenites lived in Gilead, their livestock grew in number, so they had very many animals. Livestock refers to the animals the Reubenites kept: cows, sheep, horses, camels, and donkeys. Gilead was a small area on the eastern side of the Jordan River, about 40 kilometers wide. Since the Reubenites had many animals to feed, they needed more land. So, the Reubenites settled farther east of Gilead, all the way to the edge of the wilderness. The wilderness stretched from there to the Euphrates River.

Stop here and, as a group, look at pictures of livestock: cows, sheep, horses, camels, and donkeys. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate the area to the east of Gilead where the Reubenites settled. Pause this audio here.

Back during King Saul's reign, about 300 years before King Tiglath-Pileser ruled Assyria, the tribe of Reuben attacked the Hagrite people who lived in tents east of the Jordan River. The Reubenites won the battle and defeated the Hagrites. Afterward, the Reubenites took over the Hagrites' land and lived there, which was east of Gilead.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller explains that Reuben, Israel's firstborn son, lost his birthright because he slept with Israel's concubine. Israel then gave the birthright to Joseph's sons. However, people did not list Joseph as the firstborn in the family records because Joseph was not born first. Although Judah was powerful among his brothers, Judah did not have the rights of the firstborn, but Joseph did.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists the sons of Reuben.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells a family history of Joel, who was a descendant of one of the sons of Reuben. We learn that Tiglath-Pileser, king of Assyria, captured Beerah, one of Joel's descendants and a leader in the tribe of Reuben, and took him to live in Assyria.

In the fourth scene: We learn about the family history of Beerah, which people wrote down in a record. This family history records some family members all the way back to Beerah's ancestor, Joel. This family group lived east of the Jordan River in an area that stretched from the strong city of Aroer in the south up to Nebo and the city of Baal Meon in the north of the land of the tribe of Reuben.

In the fifth scene: While the people from the tribe of Reuben lived in Gilead, their livestock increased in number, so they had very many animals. So, they settled farther east of Gilead, all the way to the edge of the wilderness. The wilderness stretched from there to the Euphrates River.

In the sixth scene: The storyteller takes us back to Saul's reign when the tribe of Reuben attacked the Hagrite people who lived in tents east of the Jordan River. The Reubenites won the battle and defeated the Hagrites. Afterward, the Reubenites took over the Hagrites' land, which was east of Gilead.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Reuben
  • Israel, or Jacob
  • Joseph
  • Joseph's sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, but the storyteller does not mention their names
  • Judah
  • Reuben's sons: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi
  • Joel's descendants: Shemaiah, Gog, Shimei, Micah, Reaiah, Baal, and Beerah
  • Tiglath-Pileser
  • The tribe of Reuben, or the Reubenites
  • Beerah's ancestors, including Jeiel, Zechariah, Bela, Azaz, Shema, and Joel
  • King Saul
  • And the Hagrites

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story talks about the family history of the tribe of Reuben, some of Reuben's descendants, and where those people lived. The story begins about 1,900 years before Jesus comes to earth and covers over 1,000 years.

The storyteller is about to list the sons of Reuben, but pauses to explain Reuben's situation. Reuben was Israel's firstborn son. Usually, the firstborn son gets special rights and privileges that other sons do not have. People call these rights and privileges the "birthright." But Reuben slept with Bilhah, one of Israel's concubines. The storyteller uses special language to describe this act. The storyteller says that Reuben dishonoured or defiled his father's marriage bed. When Reuben slept with Bilhah, Reuben made the bed unclean. Because of this, Reuben lost his special birthright. Israel then took away Reuben's birthright and gave it to the two sons of Israel's son Joseph: Manasseh and Ephraim.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do people in your language call it when someone does something very shameful and sleeps with another person's husband or wife? How do you talk about the way that person dishonours the husband and wife's marriage or marriage bed? Pause this audio here.

Even though Jacob gave the birthright to Joseph's sons, people did not list Joseph as the firstborn in the family records. This happened because even though Joseph had the birthright, Joseph was not born first. The storyteller also explains that Judah did not have the birthright, even though the tribe of Judah became the most powerful and took the lead among the tribes of Israel. Here, the storyteller tells us that a leader from Judah's family became whom God chose to rule Israel. In the original language, the word for "leader" here means a ruler whom God chose. Even though Judah became the strongest tribe, Joseph's sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, kept the special right of the firstborn.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about different words that you use for a "leader" in your language. How do the words differ in meaning? How will you say that God chose this leader? Pause this audio here.

The sons of Reuben, the firstborn son of Israel, were: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron and Carmi. These sons became heads of major family groups within the tribe of Reuben. Three other Bible passages—Genesis 46:9, Exodus 6:14, and Numbers 26:5–6—also list Reuben's sons.

Stop here. If you have already translated Reuben's family history in other Bible books, listen to the stories together as a team. Pay close attention to the names and how you translated them. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us about Joel's family history. Joel came from one of Reuben's sons, but we do not know which son or when. Joel had a son named Shemaiah. Shemaiah had a son named Gog. Gog had a son named Shimei. Shimei had a son named Micah. Micah had a son named Reaiah. Reaiah had a son named Baal. Baal had a son named Beerah. Then Tiglath-Pileser, king of Assyria, captured Beerah and took him into exile, or to live in Assyria. Beerah was a leader, or a prince of a family, in the tribe of Reuben. Tiglath-Pileser ruled Assyria for 18 years, around 700 years before Jesus.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate Israel and Assyria at the time of Tiglath-Pileser. Pause this audio here.

When people wrote down their genealogies, or family histories, and listed each clan, or family group, they recorded that Beerah's relatives included Jeiel, who was their leader.

People also listed Zechariah and Bela. Bela's father was Azaz, Azaz's father was Shema, and Shema's father was Joel. This Joel might be the same one the storyteller mentioned before. All Beerah's relatives in the list came from either the first Joel or his brothers. This family group lived east of the Jordan River in an area that stretched from the strong city of Aroer in the south up to Nebo and the city of Baal Meon in the north of the land of the tribe of Reuben. The name Nebo refers to either a mountain called Mount Nebo or a city called Nebo located near the mountain.
