Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate Aroer city, Mount Nebo, Nebo city, and Baal Meon in the land of the tribe of Reuben. Pause this audio here.

While the Reubenites lived in Gilead, their livestock grew in number very much. Gilead was a small area on the eastern side of the Jordan River, about 40 kilometers wide. Since the Reubenites needed more land because they had so much livestock, they settled farther east of Gilead, all the way to the edge of the wilderness. The wilderness stretched from there to the Euphrates River.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate the area to the east of Gilead where the Reubenites settled. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller takes us back to the time of the reign of King Saul. Saul was king over Israel for about 40 years and likely reigned 900 years after Reuben's sons were born and 300 years before Tiglath-Pileser took Beerah into captivity. During Saul's reign, the tribe of Reuben attacked the Hagrite people who lived in tents east of the Jordan River. The Hagrites fell into the Reubenites' hand, which means that the Reubenites won the battle and defeated the Hagrites. Afterward, the Reubenites took over the Hagrites' land and lived there, which was east of Gilead.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you tell a story when the events happened a long time ago, but you need to share those events out of order? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Reuben
  • Israel, or Jacob
  • Joseph
  • Joseph's sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, but the storyteller does not mention their names
  • Judah
  • Reuben's sons: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi
  • Joel's descendants: Shemaiah, Gog, Shimei, Micah, Reaiah, Baal, and Beerah
  • Tiglath-Pileser
  • The tribe of Reuben, or the Reubenites
  • Beerah's ancestors, including Jeiel, Zechariah, Bela, Azaz, Shema, and Joel
  • King Saul
  • And the Hagrites

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Reuben was Israel's firstborn son. Usually, the firstborn son gets special rights and privileges that other sons do not have, which people call the birthright. But Reuben slept with Bilhah, one of Israel's concubines. Because of this, Reuben lost his special birthright and his right to lead the family. Israel took away Reuben's birthright and gave it to the two sons of Israel's son Joseph: Manasseh and Ephraim.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Reuben, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel upset that I foolishly slept with Bilhah and lost my birthright; or
  • I feel ashamed that I defiled my father's bed and embarrassed my father in this way; or
  • I feel angry with myself because I lost the special position in the family that Yahweh wanted me to have.

Continue the drama.

Even though Israel gave the birthright to Joseph's sons, people did not list Joseph as the firstborn in the family records. This happened because Joseph was not born first. The tribe of Judah became the most powerful and took the lead among the tribes of Israel. God chose a leader from Judah's family to rule Israel. Even though Judah became the strongest tribe, they did not have the birthright. Joseph's sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, kept the special right of the firstborn.

The sons of Reuben, the firstborn son of Israel, or Jacob, were: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

The storyteller tells us about the family history of Joel, one of Reuben's descendants. Joel had a son named Shemaiah. Shemaiah had a son named Gog. Gog had a son named Shimei. Shimei had a son named Micah. Micah had a son named Reaiah. Reaiah had a son named Baal. Baal had a son named Beerah. Then Tiglath-Pileser, king of Assyria, captured Beerah and took him to live in Assyria. Beerah was a leader or a prince of a family in the tribe of Reuben.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Reubenites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are happy that Yahweh blessed our tribe with many relatives; or
  • We are very sad that Tiglath-Pileser captured our leader Beerah; or
  • We are heartbroken because Yahweh has sent the Assyrian king to judge us for our sins.

Continue the drama.

When people wrote down their family stories one by one, they recorded Beerah's relatives. People included Jeiel, who served as their leader. People also listed Zechariah and Bela. Bela's father was Azaz, Azaz's father was Shema, and Shema's father was Joel. This family group lived east of the Jordan River in an area that stretched from the strong city of Aroer in the south up to Mount Nebo or the city of Nebo and the city of Baal Meon in the north of the land of the tribe of Reuben.

While the Reubenites lived in Gilead, their livestock grew in number, so they had very many animals. So, the Reubenites settled farther east of Gilead, all the way to the edge of the wilderness. The wilderness stretched from there to the Euphrates River.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Reubenites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel happy because Yahweh has blessed us with a lot of livestock; or
  • We are grateful for the land east of Gilead, as we can take it and move there so our animals have grass to eat; or
  • We want to praise Yahweh because he is faithful. Yahweh provided us with the land when we needed it.

Continue the drama.

Back during King Saul's reign, the tribe of Reuben attacked the Hagrite people who lived in tents east of the Jordan River. The Reubenites won the battle and defeated the Hagrites. Afterward, the Reubenites took over the Hagrites' land and lived there, which was east of Gilead.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Reuben was Israel's firstborn son. But Reuben had sex with one of his father's concubines, and this dishonoured Reuben's father. So Reuben's father, Israel, gave Reuben's birthright to his other son, Joseph, and to Joseph's sons. Use the same word for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Birthright refers to the special privileges and inheritance that the father gives to his firstborn son in a family. In some cultures, the firstborn son even inherits everything and leads the family.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for birthright. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Judah was powerful among his brothers, and God chose a leader from Judah's family to rule Israel. However, Judah did not have the rights of the firstborn, but Joseph did. In the original language, the word for "leader" here means a ruler whom God chose. For more information about Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for leader here. Many translations use the same word for leader here as they do in other places. However, you may have a word that means a leader whom someone has especially chosen to be in a high position. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Reuben, the firstborn son of Israel, or Jacob, were: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Karmi. Here, sons refers to the biological male children of Reuben. However, the word son can mean descendant when it is at the beginning of a list of sons. Use the same word or phrase for son or descendant as you used in previous passages. For more information on son or descendant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Then Tiglath-Pileser, king of a big kingdom called Assyria, captured Beerah and took him into exile, or to live in Assyria. Use the same word for king as you have previously, and refer to king in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for when a ruler captures people and takes them away to a different place, into exile. If you have already translated this idea in another book of the Bible, use the same words or phrases that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Beerah was a leader, or a prince of a family, in the tribe of Reuben. The word leader here means a clan leader or chief. Use the same word or phrase for clan leader or chief as you used in previous passages.

When people wrote down their genealogies, or family histories, and listed each clan, they recorded that Beerah's kinsmen, or relatives, included Jeiel, who was their leader. Use the same word or phrase for clan, or family group, as you used in previous passages. Use the same word or phrase for genealogy as you have previously, and for more information on genealogy, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jeiel was a leader also. Here, the word leader means chief, or head of a group of men. Use the same word or phrase for leader of a group of men as you used in previous passages.

While the Reubenites lived in Gilead, their livestock grew in number until they had very many animals. Livestock refers to the animals the Reubenites kept: cows, sheep, horses, camels, and donkeys.

Stop here and, as a group, look at pictures of livestock: cows, sheep, horses, camels, and donkeys. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for livestock. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Since the Reubenites needed more land because they had so much livestock, the Reubenites settled farther east of Gilead, all the way to the edge of the wilderness. The wilderness stretched from there to the Euphrates River.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for wilderness. The wilderness is an area of land where very few people live and where not much grows. Use the same word or phrase for wilderness as you used in previous books of the Bible. For more information on wilderness, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The Reubenites defeated the Hagrites with their "hand." You will remember that "hand" symbolizes someone's power. The Reubenites were powerful and defeated the Hagrites.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about the Reubenites defeating the Hagrites with their power.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

resource.label: Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold
Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold

title: 1 Chronicles 5:11–17
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 5:11–17 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:11–17 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:11–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller talked about the family history of the tribe of Reuben, who some of Reuben's descendants were, and where those people lived.

This story tells us the family history of the tribe of Gad, who lived next to the tribe of Reuben. The storyteller tells us who some of the descendants were and where those people lived.

Before enemies conquered the people of Israel, the descendants of Gad lived in Bashan, which was north of the tribe of Reuben. Bashan was a fertile plain east of the Sea of Galilee. Mount Hermon marked Bashan's northern border, and the Yarmuk River marked its southern border. King Og ruled Bashan before, and he controlled 60 walled cities. Moses and the Israelites conquered Bashan, and Yahweh gave it to the half-tribe of Manasseh. At first, the tribe of Gad lived south of Bashan, mostly in Gilead, but their land stretched close to Bashan and may have included parts of it. The storyteller is likely talking about a later time when Gad's descendants spread into Bashan, but we do not know how far north. The tribe of Gad's land reached all the way to the town of Salecah in the southeast.

Stop here and locate the region of Bashan on the map. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller talks about some of Gad's descendants. These descendants are not Gad's sons because other stories that list Gad's sons do not mention these men. These men must have descended from Gad's sons, but we do not know their full family histories, and no other Bible stories mention them. Joel was the leader of the main clan, or family group, and Shapham led the second clan. Janai and Shaphat were the leaders of other clans in the Bashan region. It is also possible that Joel led the tribe of Gad, Shapham was second in command, and Janai and Shaphat followed them. These men's exact roles and relationships are unclear.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about some family descendants whose names you know, but you do not know how exactly those people are related. Pause this audio here.

The four men were the relatives of leaders of seven other fathers' houses, or clans of the Reubenites: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jacan, Zia, and Eber. These seven men were all descendants of Abihail, son of Huri. Huri was the son of Jaroah, and Jaroah was the son of Gilead. Gilead was the son of Michael. Michael was the son of Jeshishai. Jeshishai was the son of Jahdo, and Jahdo was the son of Buz.

Ahi was another descendant of Gad. Ahi was the son of Abdiel, and Abdiel was the son of Guni. Ahi was the leader of the Gadite family clans that the storyteller mentioned before: the clans of Joel, Shapham, Janai, Shaphat, and their seven relatives, the other clan leaders. The Gadite people lived in the Gilead region, the Bashan region, and the nearby villages. The Gilead region was south of Bashan and stretched between the Yarmuk River in the north and the Arnon River in the south. The Gadites also lived in a place called Sharon, which was near Gilead and Bashan, but we do not know exactly where. Sharon had many grassy fields where cattle, sheep, and goats could feed. The land belonged to the Gadites all the way to the borders of Sharon.

Stop here and locate the region of Gilead and Bashan on the map. Pause this audio here.

The descendants of Gad wrote down the names of all the people the storyteller mentioned in a family record. The people did this during the reign of Jotham, king of Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam, king of Israel. King Jotham ruled Judah, and King Jeroboam ruled Israel, around 750 years before Jesus. Since these two kings did not rule at exactly the same time, people likely wrote two separate family records for the tribe of Gad during their reigns. You will remember that Judah here refers to the southern kingdom and Israel to the northern kingdom. The kingdom of Israel split into Israel and Judah after King Solomon, son of King David, died. This event happened around 930 B.C. This event was part of God's judgment because Solomon and the people had turned away from God. The split was permanent, and the two kingdoms stayed separate until foreign nations conquered both.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:11–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us that the descendants of Gad lived in the Bashan region. The descendants' land reached as far as the town of Salecah in the southeast.

In the second scene: The storyteller talks about four of Gad's descendants: Joel probably led the main clan, and Shaphan probably led the second clan. Janai and Shaphat were probably the leaders of other clans in the Bashan region.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells us about seven clan leaders who were relatives of the four leaders he has just mentioned. These leaders are Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jacan, Zia, and Eber. We also learn about these leaders' family history.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller introduces Ahi and some of his family history. Ahi was the leader of all the Gadite family clans that the storyteller told us about in this story. The Gadite people lived in the Gilead region, the Bashan region, and the nearby villages. The Gadite people also lived in a place called Sharon.

In the fifth scene: The descendants of Gad wrote down the names of all the people the storyteller mentioned in this story in a family record. The descendants did this during the reign of Jotham, king of Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam, king of Israel.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Descendants of Gad
  • Joel
  • Shapham
  • Janai
  • Shaphat
  • Michael
  • Meshullam
  • Sheba
  • Jorai
  • Jacan
  • Zia
  • Eber
  • Abihail
  • Huri
  • Jaroah
  • Gilead
  • Michael
  • Jeshishai
  • Jahdo
  • Buz
  • Ahi
  • Abdiel
  • And Guni

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The descendants of Gad lived in the region of Bashan, north of the tribe of Reuben. Even though the storyteller says the "sons" of Gad, we know that he is referring to a wider group of Gad's relatives.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What do you say in your language when you talk about different types of descendants? What words do you use for sons and daughters, and what words do you use for more distant relatives? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller shows that the tribes of Gad and Reuben had a close relationship when he mentions that they lived next to each other. The tribe of Gad lived in the Bashan region. The Bashan region was east of the Sea of Galilee. Mount Hermon marked its northern border, and the Yarmuk River marked its southern border. At first, the tribe of Gad lived south of Bashan, mostly in Gilead, but their land stretched close to Bashan and may have included parts of it. The storyteller is probably talking about a later time when Gad's descendants moved into Bashan, but we do not know how far north they went. The tribe of Gad's land reached all the way to the town of Salecah in the southeast.

Stop here and locate the region of Bashan on the map. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you say in your language to show that two people groups that live near each other have a close relationship? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller talks about some of Gad's descendants. These descendants are not Gad's sons, but they must have descended from Gad's sons. Joel was the leader of the main clan, and Shapham led the second clan. Janai and Shaphat were the leaders of other clans in the Bashan region. However, Joel and Shapham might have been the two most important leaders of the same clan. It is also possible that Joel led the tribe of Gad, Shapham was second in command, and Janai and Shaphat followed them. These men's exact roles and relationships are unclear.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you talk about Joel, Shapham, Janai, and Shaphat since their roles and relationships in this story are not very clear? Pause this audio here.

The four men were the relatives of leaders of seven other fathers' houses, or clans of the Reubenites: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jacan, Zia, and Eber. These seven men were all descendants of Abihail, son of Huri. Huri was the son of Jaroah, and Jaroah was the son of Gilead. Gilead was the son of Michael. Michael was the son of Jeshishai. Jeshishai was the son of Jahdo, and Jahdo was the son of Buz.

The storyteller now introduces Ahi, who was the son of Abdiel, and Abdiel was the son of Guni. Ahi was the leader of the Gadite family clans that the storyteller mentioned before: the clans of Joel, Shapham, Janai, Shaphat, and their seven relatives, the other clan leaders. The Gadite people lived in the Gilead region, the Bashan region, and the nearby villages. The Gilead region was south of Bashan and stretched between the Yarmuk River in the north and the Arnon River in the south. The Gadites also lived in a place called Sharon. Sharon had many grassy fields where cattle, sheep, and goats could feed. The land belonged to the Gadites all the way to the borders of Sharon.

Stop here and locate the region of Gilead and Bashan on the map. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, do the following: Find objects like sticks or pebbles that will represent all the descendants of Gad the storyteller spoke about in this story. Arrange the objects in groups to show the relationships that the various people had with each other. Talk about how you will show distant relatives, fathers and sons, and leaders. Pause this audio here.

The descendants of Gad wrote down the names of all the people the storyteller mentioned in a family record. The people did this during the reign of Jotham, king of Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam, king of Israel. King Jotham ruled Judah, and King Jeroboam ruled Israel, around 750 years before Jesus. Since these two kings did not rule at exactly the same time, people likely wrote two separate family records for the tribe of Gad during their reigns.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:11–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Descendants of Gad
  • Joel
  • Shapham
  • Janai
  • Shaphat
  • Michael
  • Meshullam
  • Sheba
  • Jorai
  • Jacan
  • Zia
  • Eber
  • Abihail
  • Huri
  • Jaroah
  • Gilead
  • Michael
  • Jeshishai
  • Jahdo
  • Buz
  • Ahi
  • Abdiel
  • And Guni

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The descendants of Gad lived in the region of Bashan, north of the tribe of Reuben. The tribe of Gad's land reached all the way to the town of Salecah in the southeast.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing descendants of Gad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel blessed that Yahweh has given us land next to the tribe of Reuben. We have a good relationship with them; or
  • We are grateful that Yahweh gave us more land as our tribe and our herds and flocks increased. We used to be mostly in Gilead, but now we are also in Bashan; or
  • We praise Yahweh for his faithfulness to us.

Continue the drama.

Gad's descendant Joel led the main clan, and Shapham led the second clan. Janai and Shaphat were the leaders of other clans in the Bashan region.

The four men were the relatives of leaders of seven other clans of the Reubenites: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jakan, Zia, and Eber. These seven men were all descendants of Abihail, son of Huri. Huri was the son of Jaroah, and Jaroah was the son of Gilead. Gilead was the son of Michael. Michael was the son of Jeshishai. Jeshishai was the son of Jahdo, and Jahdo was the son of Buz.

Ahi was another descendant of Gad. Ahi was the son of Abdiel, and Abdiel was the son of Guni. Ahi was the leader of the Gadite family clans of Joel, Shapham, Janai, Shaphat, and their seven relatives, the other clan leaders. The Gadite people lived in the Gilead region, the Bashan region, and the nearby villages. The Gadites also lived in a place called Sharon. The land belonged to the Gadites all the way to the borders.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ahi, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh has given me a big blessing and a big task to lead the many clans of Gad; or
  • Praise Yahweh for providing all the land we need to feed our herds and flocks, which Yahweh blessed us with.

Continue the drama.

The descendants of Gad wrote down the names of all the people the storyteller mentioned in a family record. The descendants did this during the reign of Jotham, king of Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam, king of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the descendants of Gad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are so glad that we have good family records and that we know who our ancestors were; or
  • We are grateful that our ancestors knew the importance of family records and that they wrote family lists down twice, during the reign of Jotham, king of Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam, king of Israel; or
  • We are glad our ancestors obeyed Yahweh. Yahweh told Moses to keep a family record so we know which tribe we are from.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:11–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The sons, or descendants, of Gad lived in Bashan, which was north of the tribe of Reuben.

Here, the word "sons" refers to the descendants of Gad. A descendant is any person from all future generations from the same family line. Use the same word or phrase for descendant as you used in previous passages. For more information on descendant, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller talks about some of Gad's descendants. These people are not Gad's sons, but they must have descended from Gad's sons. Joel was the leader of the main clan, and Shapham led the second clan. Here, the word leader means chief, or head of a group of men. Use the word or phrase for leader, or chief, as you used in previous passages.

The four men were the relatives of leaders of seven other fathers' households, or clans of the Reubenites: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jakan, Zia, and Eber. Use the same word or phrase for clans as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for households. "Fathers' households," or "houses," refers to everyone who lives in a family home. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about household. Pause this audio here.

Ahi was another descendant of Gad. Ahi was the son of Abdiel, and Abdiel was the son of Guni.

Here, son refers to a biological male child. Use the same word or phrase for son as you used in previous passages. For more information on son, refer to the Master Glossary.

The descendants of Gad lived in Bashan and its outlying villages, and in the pasturelands of Sharon. Pasturelands were open land where cattle and sheep could eat grass. Use the same word for village, or small town that probably did not have a wall around it, as you have used previously.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for pasturelands. Pause this audio here.

The descendants of Gad wrote down the names of all the people the storyteller mentioned in a genealogy, or family record. The descendants did this during the reign of Jotham, king of Judah, and during the reign of Jeroboam, king of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for genealogy as you used in previous passages. For more information on genealogy, refer to the Master Glossary.

Use the same word or phrase for king, Judah, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, Judah, and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 5:18–26
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 5:18–26 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:18–26 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:18–26 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we learned about the family history of the tribe of Gad, who some of Gad's descendants were, and where those people lived.

This story shows some of the history of the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes—Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh—who lived east of the Jordan River. The events in this story happened around 800 years before Christ was born. About 400 years before Jesus came, the storyteller shared this story with the Israelites who had recently come back to Israel after an enemy government held them captive for 70 years. Some Israelites return to their land, but the people of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh do not. Although these tribes are still in exile, the storyteller likely wants to give the people of Israel hope. The storyteller wants to remind the people that Yahweh's promises still matter. The storyteller shows that Yahweh blesses people who want to follow him and punishes those who disobey his commands.

The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh had a large army of 44,760 strong and brave men. The soldiers carried shields, swords, and bows. These men were skilled fighters and ready for war. The word for shield in the original language refers to a small, round shield that soldiers held in one hand at arm's length to protect their upper body and head. The storyteller uses a general word for a sword, which does not say whether the sword was long or short. The Israelites mostly used straight, double-edged iron swords about 45—60 centimetres long, or shorter dagger-like swords that people used as a backup weapon.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a typical Israelite sword, shield, and bow. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the army in your people group. Who are the soldiers, how old are they, are they men or women, and what skills do they have? Are these people professional fighters or trained civilians? What weapons do these people use when they go to war? Pause this audio here.

The people in the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh started a war against the Hagrites, and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. The Hagrites were a group of people who lived in tents in the deserts east of the Jordan River. The Hagrites were enemies of Israel, and you may remember that the tribe of Reuben fought against them during the reign of King Saul. This story takes place about 300 years after the reign of King Saul, when the two-and-a-half tribes of Israel attacked the Hagrites and the other desert tribes that were the allies of the Hagrites. We do not know anything else about these other tribes.

God helped the soldiers from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh fight against the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. The war was probably very serious because both sides were fighting for the land. The people in the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes mainly raised cattle, and the enemy tribes on their eastern border were also shepherds. People disagreed about the pastureland, so they began to fight and destroy each other. God made the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes defeat the Hagrites and their allies. The Israelites won because they cried out or prayed to God during the battle or war. God answered their prayers because the people trusted God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why does your people group have battles with other people groups? Talk about a situation where the soldiers from your people group fought against another people group and their allies and won. Talk about why you think your people's soldiers won the war or battle. Pause this audio here.

The soldiers of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh captured the possessions of the Hagrites and their allies. The people took 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, and 2,000 donkeys. The people also took 100,000 people captive, including men, women, and children. Many Hagrite soldiers died in the war because God was fighting against them. The people of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh took over the land of the Hagrites. The people lived there until the time of exile, or captivity, when the Assyrians took the Israelite people away as captives to the land of Assyria.

Stop here and look at pictures of camels, sheep, and donkeys. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate Israel, with the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, and Assyria. Pause this audio here.

There were very many people in the half-tribe of Manasseh. These people lived in the land of Bashan, which was a fertile region east of the Jordan River. Yahweh gave most of the region to the half-tribe of Manasseh, but the tribe of Gad also lived in part of it. Manasseh's land reached as far north as the mountain Baal Hermon, which people also call Senir or Mount Hermon.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate the land of the half-tribe of Manasseh. Pause this audio here.

The following men in the half-tribe of Manasseh were the leaders in their clans. These men's names were Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel. These men were all very good soldiers and well-known heads of their clans. We do not know anything more about these men.

However, the people of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh sinned against the God of their ancestors. These people did not worship the God of their ancestors. Instead, they worshipped the gods of the people who had lived in the land before, whom God had destroyed.

So, the God of Israel made King Pul of Assyria, also known as Tiglath-Pileser, invade the land of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. God caused King Pul to feel strong emotions or ideas that led him to attack Israel. King Pul took people from the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes as captives. This was part of God's plan to punish Israel because they had turned away from God. The Assyrians exiled the Israelites to places east and north of the Assyrian capital, Nineveh. The Assyrians took the people to the region of Halah town, the Habor River, which flowed into the large Euphrates River, the region of Hara, and the Gozan River. The people stayed there until "this day," which means that the people in the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh stayed in exile, unlike some of the rest of the Israelites who came back.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and find Assyria and the places where King Pul of Assyria took the Israelites as prisoners.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:18–26 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: We learn that the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh had a large army of 44,760 strong and brave men. The soldiers carried shields, swords, and bows.

In the second scene: The people in the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh started a war against the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. God helped the Israelite soldiers to defeat the enemy nations because they prayed to God and trusted him.

In the third scene: The soldiers of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh captured the possessions of the Hagrites and of their allies. The soldiers took 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, and 2,000 donkeys. They also took 100,000 people captive. The people in the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh took the land from the Hagrites and lived there. The people stayed in that land until the Assyrians came and took them away as captives to Assyria.

In the fourth scene: We learn that the people of the half-tribe of Manasseh lived in Bashan, and the storyteller tells us the names of some of their clan leaders who were all outstanding soldiers and well-known.

In the fifth scene: The people of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh sinned against the God of their ancestors. The people did not worship their ancestors' God. Instead, they worshipped the gods of the people who lived in the land and whom God had destroyed.

In the sixth scene: Because the two-and-a-half tribes were unfaithful to God, he made King Pul of Assyria, also called Tiglath-Pileser, invade their land. King Pul took them away as captives to places in Assyria. The people stayed there until the storyteller told this story.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The Reubenites
  • The Gadites
  • The half-tribe of Manasseh
  • Brave soldiers from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh
  • Enemy nations: the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab
  • God
  • 100,000 people from the enemy nations whom the Israelite soldiers took captive
  • Heads of clans in the half-tribe of Manasseh: Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel
  • And Pul King of Assyria, also known as Tiglath-Pileser

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

There are three storylines in this story:

  • First, the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh go to war against the Hagrites and their allies.
  • Second, we hear about some of the family history of the half-tribe of Manasseh.
  • Third, the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh are not faithful to God, and the Assyrian King Pul captures the tribes and takes them into exile.

The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh had a large army of 44,760 strong and brave men. The soldiers carried shields and swords, and drew bows, which means that they were shooting arrows. These soldiers were skilled fighters, ready for war.

The word for shield in the original language refers to a small, round shield that soldiers held in one hand at arm's length to protect their upper body and head. The soldiers held a sword or another weapon in their other hand. People made shields out of wood or metal, and sometimes people covered wooden shields with leather. The storyteller uses a general word for a sword that does not say whether the sword was long or short. The Israelites mostly used iron swords, which were either straight, double-edged swords about 45—60 cm long or shorter dagger-like swords that people used as a backup weapon. The Israelites also probably carried spears and slings as part of their weapons.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a typical Israelite sword, shield, and bow, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, talk about the words or phrases in your language for different types of shields, swords, and bows. Pause this audio here.

The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh started a war against the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. God helped the soldiers of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh to fight the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. God delivered the Hagrites and their allies into the hands of the Israelite tribes, or into their power. In the original language, the word "allies" means all who were with the Hagrites. The people from the two-and-a-half tribes won because they cried out or prayed to God during the war. God answered the people's prayers because they trusted God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about other people groups who are with you, or who are your allies, in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you say in your language to show that people cried out or called out to God for help? What do you say to show that God caused the Israelite tribes to win the battle or war? Pause this audio here.

The soldiers of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh captured the livestock of the Hagrites and of their allies. The word "livestock" in the original language refers generally to possessions and can include fields, animals, and prisoners of war. The Israelite soldiers took 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, and 2,000 donkeys. The Israelites also took 100,000 people captive, including men, women, and children. The people of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh took over the land of the Hagrites. The people lived there until the time of exile, when the Assyrians took the people away as captives to the land of Assyria.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What word will you use for "livestock" that shows it includes more than just animals, which the Israelite tribes took? Pause this audio here.

There were very many people in the half-tribe of Manasseh. The people lived in the land of Bashan, which was a fertile region east of the Jordan River. Manasseh's land reached as far north as the mountain Baal Hermon, which people also call Senir or Mount Hermon.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and locate the land of the half-tribe of Manasseh. Pause this audio here.

The following men of the half-tribe of Manasseh were the leaders in their clans. These men's names were Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel. These men were all outstanding soldiers and well-known heads of their clans.

The storyteller shows that the three tribes—Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh—stopped being faithful to God. The storyteller does not say it directly, but he means all three tribes, not just the half-tribe of Manasseh. Now, the storyteller focuses on how all of them turned away from the God of Israel. However, the people of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh sinned against the God of their ancestors. The word "sinned" here means to act treacherously or to be unfaithful. Whenever we hear the phrase "God of their ancestors," it refers to Yahweh, the God who chose Israel to be his special people. The two-and-a-half tribes did not worship Yahweh. Instead, they worshipped the gods of the people who had lived in the land before, whom God had destroyed. The original language uses special language to show that these tribes of Israel were like a prostitute in how they were unfaithful to Yahweh. A prostitute is a person who has sexual relations outside of marriage in order to gain money.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How are you going to say that the two-and-a-half tribes sinned against Yahweh? What words or phrases are you going to use to show that these tribes were unfaithful like a prostitute? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller now points out that what happens next is a result of the Israelites' sin against God. So, the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of King Pul of Assyria, also known as Tiglath-Pileser, to invade the land of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. The human spirit is an invisible part of a person that God speaks to in order to influence what a person does. When God stirred up the spirit of King Pul, God made King Pul want to act. King Pul took the people away as captives. The Assyrians exiled the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes to places east and north of the Assyrian capital, Nineveh. The Assyrians took the people to the region of Halah town, the Habor River, which flowed into the large Euphrates River, the region of Hara, and the Gozan River. The people stayed there until "this day," which means the time when the storyteller told the story.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map and find Assyria and the places where King Pul of Assyria took the Israelites as prisoners. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:18–26 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The Reubenites
  • The Gadites
  • The half-tribe of Manasseh
  • Brave soldiers from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh
  • Enemy nations: the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab
  • God
  • 100,000 people from the enemy nations whom the Israelite soldiers took captive
  • Heads of clans in the half-tribe of Manasseh: Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel
  • And Pul King of Assyria, also known as Tiglath-Pileser

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh had a large army of 44,760 strong and brave men. The soldiers carried shields, swords, and bows and were skilled fighters, ready for war.

The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh started a war against the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. God helped the soldiers from the Israelite tribes fight against the enemy nations, and God made the Israelite tribes defeat the Hagrites and their allies. The Israelites won because they prayed to God during the war. God answered their prayers because they trusted God.

The soldiers of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh captured the possessions of the Hagrites and their allies. They took 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, and 2,000 donkeys. They also took 100,000 people captive. Many Hagrite soldiers died in the war because God was fighting against them. The people of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh took over the land of the Hagrites. The people lived there until the time of exile, when the Assyrians took them away as captives to the land of Assyria.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing soldiers from the tribes of Reuben, Gad and the half-tribe, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel grateful! Praise be to Yahweh, for he heard our prayers! Yahweh went before us and helped us win the war against the Hagrites and the people of Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab; or
  • We feel so happy that we are now safe from these enemy nations! And we are happy with all their livestock we got! or
  • We feel pleased that we can now use their land to feed our many animals!

Continue the drama.

There were very many people in the half-tribe of Manasseh. The people lived in the land of Bashan, which was a fertile region east of the Jordan River. Manasseh's land reached as far north as the mountain Baal Hermon, which people also call Senir or Mount Hermon.

The following men from the half-tribe of Manasseh were the leaders in their clans. These men's names were Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel. They were all outstanding soldiers and well-known heads of their clans.

However, the people of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh sinned against the God of their ancestors. The people did not worship him. Instead, the people worshipped the gods of the people who lived in the land. These were the nations that God had destroyed.

So, the God of Israel made King Pul of Assyria, also known as Tiglath-Pileser, invade the land of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. King Pul took the people away as captives. The Assyrians took the people to the region of Halah town, the Habor River, which flowed into the large Euphrates River, the region of Hara, and the Gozan River. The people stayed there until the time when the storyteller told the story.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing people from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel free! Surely we do not have to obey only Yahweh. Surely it is fine to have wives from the land we conquered. Maybe they will want to follow Yahweh, but surely we can follow their gods too; or
  • We are scared! The Assyrians have invaded our land! Is Yahweh punishing us? or
  • We regret abandoning Yahweh. Yahweh blessed us when we followed his laws. Now it is too late, and the Assyrians have taken us into captivity! We must live in their land and obey their laws!

Ask the person playing God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I cry for my people, Israel. I love the Israelites so much, but they are unfaithful to me and are going after worthless idols; or
  • My heart is broken. I thought Israel would call me "Father" and not turn away from following me; or
  • I am so sad. I raised Israel as my children and brought them up, but they have rebelled against me.... I will discipline the Israelites and send them into exile, so they recognise their own wickedness and repent and turn to me.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 5:18–26 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The soldiers carried shields and swords, and drew bows.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a typical Israelite sword, shield, and bow, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for shield, sword, and bow. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

God helped the soldiers of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh to fight the Hagrites and the people in Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. God delivered the Hagrites and their allies into the hands of the Israelite tribes. This means that God gave the Israelites power over the Hagrites. Use the same phrase for "delivered into the hands of Israelites" as you used in previous passages.

The people from the two-and-a-half tribes won because they cried out or prayed to God during the war. God answered their urgent cry for help because they trusted God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cry for help to God, or pray to God. Look up pray in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated "cry for help" to mean prayer in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller lists the leaders, or chiefs of the households of these tribes, who were fighting men and famous. Use the same word or phrase for households, or father's houses, that you have used in previous passages. Household is in the Master Glossary.

However, the people of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh were unfaithful to the God of their ancestors. The people sinned—they did not worship him. Instead, they worshipped the gods of the people who had lived in the land before, whom God had destroyed. These gods were false gods, not the one true creator God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for gods. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

So, the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of King Pul of Assyria, also known as Tiglath-Pileser, to invade the land of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. The human spirit is the invisible part of a person that God speaks to in order to influence what that person does. When God stirred up the spirit of King Pul, God made King Pul want to act. Use the same word or phrase for God and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on God and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for spirit. Look up spirit in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

resource.label: Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold
Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold

title: 1 Chronicles 6:1–15
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 6:1–15 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:1–15 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, the storyteller told us some of the history of the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes—Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. People from these tribes won a battle against the Hagrites and their allies because the Israelites prayed to God for help. But later, the people from the two-and-a-half Israelite tribes sinned when they worshipped other gods instead of the true God. So God punished the people by sending the king of Assyria, who took them away as captives.

Next, the storyteller shares several stories from the family history of the tribe of Levi. The Levites lived all over Israel and helped the Israelites worship Yahweh wherever they were, no matter what happened in Israel's history. The family history of the tribe of Levi is the second longest in 1 Chronicles. The tribe of Levi is one of only two tribes that the storyteller follows right to the time of the exile. This shows how important the Levites were in the story of Israel. The storyteller puts the tribe of Levi in the centre of all the 12 tribes' family histories. You will remember from the book of Numbers that when the Israelites camped in the wilderness, the Levites stayed at the centre, close to the tabernacle or the special tent where Yahweh's presence was. All the other tribes camped around the Levites. This showed that worshiping Yahweh was the most important part of Israel's life.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you tell a story about different family clans or groups in a family history, how do you make one clan stand out above others because of the important things they do? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller is telling the story to the Israelites who returned to their land after 70 years in captivity in Babylon. Yahweh reminds the Israelites that the most important thing they can do is to worship Yahweh.

In this story, the storyteller tells us the family history of the family line of the high priests. The high priest served as Israel's most important spiritual leader and helped the people stay close to Yahweh. The high priest offered sacrifices to Yahweh for the people's sins, took care of the tabernacle or temple, led worship, and taught the people Yahweh's laws and how to obey those laws. The high priest also prayed for the Israelites and asked Yahweh to bless and protect them. The storyteller lists the high priests first to show how important worship was for Yahweh's people.

Levi was the third son of Jacob and the great-grandson of Abraham. Levi was the father of Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Now the family list focuses on the descendants of Kohath from whom the high priests came. Kohath was the father of Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Amram had two sons, Aaron and Moses, and a daughter, Miriam. While Aaron, Moses, and Miriam were from the family of Levi, only the direct descendants of Aaron served as high priests. You might remember that after the Israelites left Egypt, Yahweh gave Moses instructions for the priesthood on Mount Sinai. Yahweh chose Aaron to be the first high priest and Aaron's sons to be priests.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you tell a story about a family history, how do you decide which people to include? Tell a story about a particular time in your family's history. Explain why you have included some people and not others. Pause this audio here.

Aaron was the father of Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar. You will remember that Yahweh killed Aaron's oldest sons, Nadab and Abihu, because they disobeyed Yahweh. Therefore, after Aaron's death, Yahweh chose Aaron's third son, Eleazar, to be high priest. Eleazar helped Joshua to divide the Promised Land among the tribes of Israel. Eleazar was the father of Phinehas. You will remember that people knew Phinehas had zeal for Yahweh. Phinehas killed an Israelite man who sinned with a Midianite woman, and this action caused Yahweh to stop a plague that Yahweh had sent on Israel as judgment. Because of what Phinehas did, Yahweh promised Phinehas that his family would always serve as priests.

Phinehas was the father of Abishua, who became the high priest after Phinehas. Abishua was the father of Bukki, who passed the high priestly line to his son Uzzi. Uzzi then served as high priest, likely during the time when judges ruled the nation of Israel. Uzzi was the father of Zerahiah, who continued the high priestly line, and Zerahiah was the father of Meraioth. We do not know much about Zerahiah and Meraioth.

Meraioth was the father of Amariah, and Amariah was the father of Ahitub. Ahitub was the father of Zadok, who was the high priest during the reigns of King David and King Solomon. Zadok was the father of Ahimaaz, who helped King David during David's son Absalom's rebellion. Ahimaaz was the father of Azariah, and Azariah the father of Johanan. Johanan was the father of Azariah, who served as a high priest in the house or temple that Solomon built in Jerusalem. The temple is the place where the Israelites worshiped Yahweh. He is likely the same Azariah who opposed King Uzziah when King Uzziah wrongly tried to burn incense in the temple. Azariah was the father of Amariah, and Amariah was the father of Ahitub. Ahitub was the father of another Zadok, and Zadok was the father of Shallum. Shallum was the father of Hilkiah. Hilkiah found the scroll of Yahweh's law during the reign of Josiah, the king of Judah. Hilkiah helped King Josiah lead Israel to obey Yahweh's law again and restore worship. Hilkiah was the father of Azariah, and Azariah was the father of Seraiah, the last high priest before the Babylonian exile. Seraiah was the father of Jehozadak. At that time, Yahweh caused King Nebuchadnezzar to come and conquer the people of the kingdom of Judah and its capital city, Jerusalem. Nebuchadnezzar took the people away as captives to Babylon. Jehozadak, the last high priest in this story, was one of those captives.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the two kingdoms of Israel and Judah and the kingdom of Babylon. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Some Bible stories, like this one, are family lists with mostly names. As you hear some of the history about the people on this list, why do you think the storyteller chose these names for the list? What is the storyteller trying to help the audience understand? Pause this audio here.

This family history is the longest list of high priests in the Old Testament. This history covers over 800 years, from about 1,400 years before Jesus to 600 years before Jesus. The storyteller names 23 generations of high priests from Aaron's family through Aaron's third son, Eleazar. This shows that the high priestly family line continued unbroken. But the list is not complete and does not include every high priest in Israel's history.

Some of these high priests in the list also seem to have lived at a different time than when other Bible stories talk about them. We do not know exactly why this happened.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller follows the family line of Aaron, the first high priest, all the way back to Levi, who was Abraham's grandson. The storyteller lists the high priests of Israel from Aaron's time to the time when Israel was taking the land of Canaan.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists high priests of Israel from the time of the Judges to the time of King David and King Solomon.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists high priests of the Israelites from the time when Yahweh divided the kingdom of Israel into Israel and Judah until the time of the Babylonian exile.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Levi and his sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari
  • Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel
  • Amram's children: Aaron, Moses, and Miriam
  • Aaron's sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar
  • And Eleazar's descendants: Phinehas, Abishua, Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, Meraioth, Amariah, Ahitub, Zadok, Ahimaaz, Azariah, Johanan, Azariah, Amariah, another Ahitub, Zadok, Shallum, Hilkiah, Azariah, Seriah, and Jehozadak
  • Yahweh
  • And Nebuchadnezzar

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this story, the storyteller shares the family history of the high priests. The storyteller shows that the high priest's family line began with Levi, who was Abraham's great-grandson.

The storyteller lists the names of all the high priests in one long list, so it may help to divide the names into important time periods.

In scene one, the storyteller follows the family line of Aaron, the first high priest, all the way back to Levi, who was Abraham's great-grandson. The storyteller lists the high priests of Israel from Aaron's time to the time when Israel was taking the land of Canaan.

Levi had three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. The word sons in this part of the story means that these three men were Levi's biological sons.

Kohath had four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.

Amram had two sons, Aaron and Moses, and a daughter called Miriam.

Aaron was the first high priest. Aaron had four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

Eleazar became high priest after Aaron died. After this, the storyteller only names the son of each high priest who became high priest after his father died. When the storyteller says that Eleazar fathered Phinehas or became the father of Phinehas, it means that Eleazar's son Phinehas became high priest after Eleazar died.

Exodus 6:16–26 also lists some of Levi's descendants.

Stop here. If you have already translated Levi's family history in Exodus, listen to the story together as a team. Pay close attention to the names and how you translated them in the other story. Pause this audio here.

In scene two, the storyteller begins the second group of high priests in Israel with Abishua, the son of Phinehas, when the Judges ruled Israel. From this name on, the list follows the same structure and shows how each high priest was the son of the one before him. This group also includes Abishua's descendants: Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, Meraioth, Amariah, and Ahitub. The last high priest in this group is Ahitub's son Zadok, who served during the time of King David and King Solomon.

In scene three, the storyteller begins the third group of high priests in Israel with Zadok's son Ahimaaz, who lived when Yahweh divided Israel into two kingdoms, Israel and Judah. This group also includes Ahimaaz's descendants: Azariah, Johanan, Azariah, Amariah, Ahitub, Zadok, Shallum, Hilkiah, Azariah, and Seraiah. The group ends with Seraiah's son Jehozadak, when Yahweh allowed Nebuchadnezzar to take Jehozadak and the people of the kingdom of Judah, including those in its capital city Jerusalem, as captives to Babylon. The storyteller says that Yahweh sent the people into exile by the hand, or power, of Nebuchadnezzar.

Stop here and, as a group, look at the map. Locate the two kingdoms of Israel and Judah and the kingdom of Babylon. Pause this audio here.

You will notice that there are several high priests in the family list who had the same name, but were different people.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Levi and his sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari
  • Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel
  • Amram's children: Aaron, Moses, and Miriam
  • Aaron's sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar
  • And Eleazar's descendants: Phinehas, Abishua, Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, Meraioth, Amariah, Ahitub, Zadok, Ahimaaz, Azariah, Johanan, Azariah, Amariah, Ahitub, Zadok, Shallum, Hilkiah, Azariah, Seriah and Jehozadak.
  • Yahweh
  • And Nebuchadnezzar

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite listening to the passage.

First, the person playing the author should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If this section is too difficult to remember, you may listen to an audio recording again.

While you do this, think about the role of the high priests in the life of the Israelites. Think how the high priests' service to Yahweh affected the Israelites individually and as a nation over the years. High priests helped the Israelites by leading the people in worship and offering sacrifices to Yahweh. The high priests taught the people Yahweh's laws and prayed for them. As a nation, the Israelites depended on the high priests to keep the people close to Yahweh and guide them to follow Yahweh's commands.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should listen to this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Levi had three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Kohath had four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Amram had two sons, Aaron and Moses, and a daughter called Miriam. Aaron was the first high priest. Aaron had four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Eleazar was the father of Phinehas.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the storyteller of 1 Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed! Yahweh has had such a clear plan for how the people of Israel would know and worship him; or
  • Yahweh chose the descendants of Aaron, who were from the tribe of Levi, to be high priests and teach the people how to worship Yahweh; or
  • Yahweh loves the people of Israel so much that he forgave them their sins when the priests offered sacrifices.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller begins the second group of high priests in Israel with Abishua, the son of Phinehas. This group also includes Abishua's descendants: Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, Meraioth, Amariah, and Ahitub. The last high priest in this group is Ahitub's son Zadok.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We praise Yahweh for the high priests who serve him. We are grateful that the high priests do not have their own land, but live among us all throughout Israel; or
  • The high priests teach us how to worship Yahweh and how to be right with Yahweh. The high priests pray for our nation, too; or
  • The high priests help our kings to lead our nation. The high priests use the ephod to ask Yahweh what to do.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller begins the third group of high priests in Israel with Ahimaaz. This group also includes Ahimaaz's descendants: Azariah, Johanan, Azariah, Amariah, Ahitub, Zadok, Shallum, Hilkiah, Azariah, and Seraiah. The group ends with Jehozadak when Nebuchadnezzar took him and the people of the kingdom of Judah, including those in its capital Jerusalem, as captives to Babylon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh has used the high priests and prophets to tell us to repent of our sin for so many years; or
  • We are sorry that we did not obey Yahweh and that we worshipped other gods instead; or
  • We are so sad, because Yahweh punished us for disobeying him. Yahweh sent the Assyrians and then the Babylonians to conquer our kingdom and take us into captivity.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here. If you have already translated Levi's family history in Exodus 6:16–26, listen to the story together as a team. Pay close attention to the names and how you translated them in the other story. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Levi were Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Throughout this passage, the word sons refers to biological male children of the father. Use the same word or phrase for son as you used in previous passages. For more information on son, refer to the Master Glossary.

Azariah served as a high priest in the house or temple that Solomon built in Jerusalem. Priests were people who worked in the temple of God in Jerusalem. God had appointed priests to be in between God and the people. Priests made it possible for people to come before God and to have a good relationship with God. The priests did this by offering sacrifices on behalf of the people and by teaching people how to live.

The temple is the place where the Israelites worshiped God. Another name for the temple is the house of God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for priest and temple. Look up priest and temple in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh sent the people of Judah and Jerusalem into exile by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar. Use the same word for Yahweh and Judah as you have used previously, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information. Use the same phrase for "by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar" as you have used previously to show that someone did something using their strength.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 6:16–30
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 6:16–30 and put it in your hearts.
