Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:16–30 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:16–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller started to tell us the family history of the tribe of Levi. The storyteller began with the family line of the high priests, who all descended from Levi's great-grandson Aaron.

In this story, the storyteller continues with the family history of the tribe of Levi. We learn about three clans of Levi's descendants who were not high priests but carried out important duties at the tent of meeting, or tabernacle.

Levi was the third son of Jacob and the great-grandson of Abraham. Levi had three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Each of Levi's sons also had sons. Gershon was the father of two sons, Libni and Shimei. Kohath was the father of four sons, Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Merari was the father of two sons, Mahli and Mushi. The sons of Levi were the ancestors of the Levite clans or family groups. The storyteller introduces the Levite clans next.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: The storyteller begins by reminding us that the families in this story all came from Levi. Levi was one of the sons of Israel, or Jacob. Yahweh gave clans in Levi's large family different jobs. Who can you think of in your family who is really important or who started your family line? Tell a story of your family by starting with that person, just like the storyteller started with Levi. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller begins with the clan of Gershon, Levi's firstborn son. The storyteller lists a number of generations of the descendants of Gershon's firstborn son, Libni. Libni's son was Jehath. Jehath's son was Zimmah. Zimmah's son was Joah. Joah's son was Iddo, and Iddo's son was Zerah. Zerah was the father of Jeatherai.

You will remember that Yahweh gave each Levite clan an important job. As the Israelites travelled from place to place on their way to the Promised Land, the Levite clans took care of and carried parts of the tent of meeting, or tabernacle. The Israelites set up the tent of meeting in the centre of the Israelite camp, and this tent was very important for the Israelites because it was the place where people met with Yahweh. The Israelites worshipped Yahweh at the tent of meeting and brought their sacrifices there. Yahweh also spoke to Moses in the tent of meeting. And once a year, on the Day of Atonement, the high priest went into the tent of meeting to offer a special sacrifice to ask Yahweh to cover over the people's sins and to restore Yahweh's relationship with the people.

Yahweh gave the Gershonite clan, including the family line of Gershon's second son Shimei, the responsibility to care for and carry all the coverings of the tent, the curtains that were at the entrance to the tent and courtyard, and the curtains that formed a fence around the courtyard.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Yahweh gave each Levite clan its own special job to help with the spiritual life of Israel. Talk about different members of your family and the different responsibilities they have. Who is responsible for the spiritual life of your family? Who looks after elderly relatives? Who looks after family records? Who looks after family property? Who assigns these different tasks to various family members? What roles do members of your family have traditionally? What customs do you still follow today? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller now talks about the family history of the Kohathites, who were descendants of Levi's second son, Kohath. The storyteller lists many generations of Kohath's descendants. The storyteller begins the Kohathite family history with Kohath's son Amminadab, whose son was Korah. No other story in the Bible refers to Amminadab as Kohath's son, and we know from Exodus 6:21 that Korah was the son of Kohath's second son, Izhar. It is possible that Amminadab is another name for Izhar or that a scribe, or the person who wrote down a copy of this story, made an error. You will remember that Korah, along with other leaders of Israel, rebelled against Moses and Aaron. Yahweh punished these men by killing them and their families, but Korah's children did not die. Korah was the father of Assir, and Assir's son was Elkanah, the father of Ebiasaph. The storyteller in Exodus 6:24 lists Assir, Elkanah, and Ebiasath as three sons of Korah. This is a different family relationship from what the storyteller says here. Ebiasath was the father of another Assir. Assir was the father of Tahath. Tahath was the father of Uriel. Uriel was the father of Uzziah. Uzziah was the father of Shaul.

Elkanah, who was likely the son of Shaul, was the father of Amasai and Ahimoth. Ahimoth was the father of another Elkanah, who was the father of Zophai. Zophai was the father of Nahath, and Nahath was the father of Eliab. Eliab was the father of Jeroham, and Jeroham was the father of another Elkanah. You will remember from 1 Samuel chapter 1 that Elkanah and his ancestors, including Zophai, or Zuph, lived in the hill country of Ephraim. We know from 1 Samuel chapter 1 that Elkanah was the father of Samuel, who was a prophet and a judge over Israel, and who also carried out priestly duties. Samuel helped lead the people of Israel, taught them to follow God, and helped choose their first two kings, Saul and David. Samuel was honest, brave, and trusted Yahweh in everything. Samuel was the father of Joel and Abijah. When Samuel grew old, Samuel made his sons judges to help lead the people of Israel, just like Samuel had done. But Samuel's sons did not follow Samuel's example and were unfair and took bribes. Instead of helping people, Samuel's sons used their power the wrong way.

In the previous story, the storyteller told us about the Kohathite family line, who descended from Kohath's son Amram and who were high priests. The Kohathites in this story, who descended from Kohath's son Izhar, here called Amminadab, also had an important job. These Kohathites were responsible for caring for and carrying the most sacred or holy objects within the tent of meeting, or tabernacle. These items included the ark of the covenant, the showbread table, the golden lampstand, and the altars. The Kohathites also took care of and carried the utensils the priests used to serve in the tent of meeting and the curtain at the entrance to the Most Holy Place.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: As you have just heard, there are names like Elkanah that appear again and again in the family line. Talk about the different traditions people follow to give names to children in your culture. Discuss your own family histories and talk about names that repeat over time. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller talks about the family history of Merari, Levi's third son. Merari had two sons, but the storyteller only follows the family line of Merari's firstborn son, Mahli, and lists several generations of Mahli's descendants. Mahli was the father of Libni. Libni was the father of Shimei. Shimei was the father of Uzzah. Uzzah's son was Shimea. Shimea's son was Haggiah. Haggiah's son was Asaiah.

The Merarites had an important job to take care of and move the parts that held up the tent of meeting. The Merarites were in charge of the framework, the pillars, and the boards that made up the tent. The Merarites also took care of the courtyard. Whenever the Israelites set up camp or traveled, the Merarites made sure that people built the tent of meeting correctly and stayed strong during the journey. This was very important to keep the tent in good shape as the people moved through the wilderness.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:16–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the sons and grandsons of Levi.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists some of the descendants of Levi's firstborn son, Gershon.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists some of the descendants of Levi's second son, Kohath.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller lists some of the descendants of Levi's third son, Merari.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Levi
  • Levi's three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari
  • Gershon's sons: Libni and Shimei
  • Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel
  • Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi
  • Gershon's descendants: Libni, Jahath, Zimmah, Joah, Iddo, Zerah, and Jeatherai
  • Kohath's descendants: Amminadab, which is possibly another name for Kohath's second son Izhar, Korah, Assir, Elkanah, Ebiasaph, Assir, Tahath, Uriel, Uzziah, Shaul, Amasai, Ahimoth, Elkanah, Zophai, Nahath, Eliab, Jeroham, Elkanah, Samuel, Joel, and Abijah
  • And Merari's descendants: Mahli, Libni, Shimei, Uzzah, Shimea, Haggiah, and Asaiah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this story, the storyteller continues with the family history of the tribe of Levi. We learn about three clans of Levi's descendants who carried out important duties at the tent of meeting, or the tabernacle.

First, the storyteller shows that the clans started from Levi, Abraham's great-grandson.

Levi had three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Each of Levi's sons also had sons. Gershon was the father of two sons, Libni and Shimei. Kohath was the father of four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Merari was the father of two sons: Mahli and Mushi. The word son here means a biological male child.

Stop here and start to make a family tree of Levi's descendants. Gather lots of pebbles to represent people and sticks to make connections between people that the pebbles represent. Start by putting down on the ground the pebble for Levi. Then put down three pebbles below the "Levi" pebble to represent Levi's three sons: Gershon, Kohath and Merari. Join the "son" pebbles to the "Levi" pebble with three sticks. Then put down pebbles to represent Levi's grandsons and join them to the correct "father" pebbles. Continue to put down pebbles as you hear the names of the other descendants of Levi. Pause this audio here.

Exodus 6:16–26 also lists some of Levi's descendants.

Stop here. If you have already translated Levi's family history in Exodus, listen to the story together as a team. Pay close attention to the names. Make sure you use the same names for the same people. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller begins with the clan of Gershon, Levi's firstborn son. The storyteller lists a number of generations of the descendants of Gershon's firstborn son, Libni. From this point on, the list follows the same structure and shows how each man was the son of the one before him. Jahath was Libni's son, Zimmah was Jahath's son, and Joah was Zimmah's son. Iddo was Joah's son, Zerah was Iddo's son, and Jeatherai was Zerah's son.

The storyteller now lists many generations of Kohath's descendants. The storyteller introduces the list by saying, "The sons of Kohath." Please note that the word "son" has two meanings. Here, the word "sons" means descendants. But from this point on, the word "son" means biological male child. The storyteller begins the Kohathite family history with Kohath's son Amminadab, whose son was Korah. No other story in the Bible refers to Amminadab as Kohath's son, and we know from Exodus 6:21 that Korah was the son of Kohath's second son, Izhar. It is possible that Amminadab is another name for Izhar or that a scribe, or the person who wrote down a copy of this story, made an error. Korah was the father of Assir, and Assir's son was Elkanah, and Elkanah's son was Ebiasaph. The storyteller in Exodus 6:24 lists Assir, Elkanah, and Ebiasaph as three sons of Korah. This is a different family relationship from what the storyteller says here. Assir was Ebiasaph's son, Tahath was Assir's son, and Uriel was Tahath's son. Uzziah was Uriel's son, and Shaul was Uzziah's son.

Elkanah, who was likely the son of Shaul, had two sons: Amasai and Ahimoth. [There are several different Elkanahs in this passage, and here the storyteller tells us the descendants of Elkanah, who is likely the son of Shaul.]{.mark} Ahimoth was the father of another Elkanah, who was the father of Zophai. Zophai's son was Nahath, and Nahath's son was Eliab. Eliab's son was Jeroham, and Jeroham's son was another Elkanah. Even though this story does not say so, we know from 1 Samuel chapter 1 that this Elkanah was the father of Samuel. Samuel was the father of Joel and Abijah. The storyteller takes a little break in the structure to tell us which of Samuel's sons is firstborn.

Now the storyteller talks about the family history of Merari, Levi's third son. The storyteller tells us about several generations from Merari's first son, Mahli. The storyteller introduces the list by saying, "The sons of Merari." Here, the word "sons" means descendants. But from this point on, the word "son" means biological male child. Mahli was the father of Libni. Libni was the father of Shimei. Shimei's son was Uzzah. Uzzah's son was another Shimea. Shimea's son was Haggiah. Haggiah's son was Asaiah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: The storyteller is using the family lists of the Levite clans to tell a story about each clan. What story or important point is the storyteller telling in this passage through the different family lists of the Gershonites, the Kohathites, and the Merarites? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:16–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Levi
  • Levi's three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari
  • Gershon's sons: Libni and Shimei
  • Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel
  • Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi
  • Gershon's descendants: Libni, Jahath, Zimmah, Joah, Iddo, Zerah, and Jeatherai
  • Kohath's descendants: Amminadab, which is possibly another name for Kohath's second son Izhar, Korah, Assir, Elkanah, Ebiasaph, Assir, Tahath, Uriel, Uzziah, Shaul, Amasai, Ahimoth, Elkanah, Zophai, Nahath, Eliab, Jeroham, Elkanah, Samuel, Joel, and Abijah
  • And Merari's descendants: Mahli, Libni, Shimei, Uzzah, Shimea, Haggiah, and Asaiah

For this step, you will choose one person to play the part of the storyteller of Chronicles. The person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person playing the author should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If this section is too difficult to remember, you may listen to an audio recording again.

While you do this, think about the story that the storyteller is telling through each family line. Yahweh gave each Levite clan an important job. As the Israelites travelled from place to place on their way to the Promised Land, the Levite clans took care of and carried parts of the tent of meeting, or tabernacle. The tent of meeting was set up in the centre of the Israelite camp and was very important for the Israelites because it was the place where people met with Yahweh. The Israelites worshipped Yahweh at the tent of meeting and brought their sacrifices there. Yahweh also spoke to Moses in the tent of meeting. And once a year, on the Day of Atonement, the high priest went into the tent of meeting to offer a special sacrifice to ask Yahweh to forgive the people's sins.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage.

The group should listen to this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Levi had three sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Each of Levi's sons also had sons. Gershon was the father of two sons: Libni and Shimei. Kohath was the father of four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Merari was the father of two sons: Mahli and Mushi. The word son here means a biological male child.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the storyteller of 1 Chronicles, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am grateful that Yahweh had such a clear plan of who should help the people of Israel to stay close to him. Yahweh chose the Levites to do that job; or
  • I am in awe! Yahweh gave me very clear instructions about how to tell the story about each Levite family line. Yahweh had a reason why he wanted the Israelites to know who was in each clan; or
  • Each time I have told the story about the clans from the tribe of Levi, Yahweh has told me to make it clear that Levi was at the beginning of this important Israelite clan or tribe.

Continue the drama.

Gershon was the father of Libni. Libni's son was Jahath. Jahath's son was Zimmah. Zimmah's son was Joah. Joah's son was Iddo, and Iddo's son was Zerah. Zerah was the father of Jeatherai.

Kohath was the father of Amminadab, whose son was Korah. Korah was the father of Assir, and Assir's son was Elkanah, the father of Ebiasaph. Ebiasaph was the father of Assir. Assir was the father of Tahath. Tahath was the father of Uriel. Uriel was the father of Uzziah. Uzziah was the father of Shaul. Elkanah was the father of Amasai and Ahimoth. Ahimoth was the father of Elkanah, who was the father of Zophai. Zophai was the father of Nahath, and Nahath was the father of Eliab. Eliab was the father of Jeroham, and Jeroham was the father of Elkanah. According to 1 Samuel chapter 1, Elkanah was the father of Samuel, and Samuel was the father of Joel and Abijah.

Merari was the father of Mahli, and Mahli was the father of Libni. Libni was the father of Shimei, and Shimei was the father of Uzzah. Uzzah's son was another Shimea. Shimea's son was Haggiah, and Haggiah's son was Asaiah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are curious why Yahweh is reminding us about these clans of Levi. The storyteller mentions some names and not others; or
  • We are grateful to Yahweh for the Gershonites, the Kohathites, and the Merarites. Yahweh gave each Levite clan a specific job, and they cared for the tent of meeting before Solomon built the temple; or
  • We are grateful that the Levites helped us to stay close to Yahweh and worship him.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 6:16–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here. If you have already translated Levi's family history in Exodus 6:16–26, listen to the story together as a team. Pay close attention to the names. Make sure you use the same names for the same people. Pause this audio here.

When the storyteller lists the sons of Levi, he is referring to Levi's biological sons.

However, when the storyteller begins a list by saying "the sons of Kohath, the sons of Elkanah, or the sons of Merari," the storyteller is probably saying that he is about to list several of the descendants of Kohath, Elkanah, or Merari, and probably not just their biological sons.

Use the same word or phrase for son or descendant as you used in previous passages. For more information on sons and descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller says that these are the clans, or family groups, of the Levites according to their fathers. Use the same word or phrase for clan or family group as you used in previous passages. Levites are people who belong to the tribe of Levi, one of the 12 tribes of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Levites. Look up Levites in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The phrase "according to their fathers" means that the storyteller listed the Levite clans according to their fathers' households, or families. Use the same word or phrase for households as you have in previous passages. "Household" is in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 7:1–5
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This part of 1 Chronicles continues the family history of the 12 tribes of Israel. Now the history turns to the tribes that live in the northern part of the land of Israel. This list focuses on the family history of Issachar and the tribe of Issachar. You will remember that family histories do not usually tell all the names of the family members. Family histories include the names of famous members of the family. And most of the time, family histories record the names of the leaders or heads of the family. This way, the people of each tribe can easily trace their ancestors and their place in the family history.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In this family history, the narrator lists Issachar's four sons in the order that they were born. When you list people in your family history, how do you list them? Try listing the children of one of your ancestors. Notice what order you list those people. Pause this audio here.

Issachar is the fifth son of Jacob with Jacob's wife Leah. Remember that Yahweh gave Jacob the name Israel, which is how the people of Israel received their name. Later in the history of the people of Israel, the tribe of Issachar, along with the tribes of Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher, lived in the northern part of the land Yahweh gave to the tribes of Israel.

Stop here and look at a map of northern Israel. As a group, show where the other tribes of Israel settled in the past. Pause this audio here.

We will learn later in 1 Chronicles that while David ruled over Israel, David conducted a census of all the fighting men in Israel. The storyteller may have used the census as the source to write Issachar's family history. Perhaps David ordered his commanders to count David's troops. This might explain why Isaachar's family history is like a military list. This list gives the impression that the tribe of Issachar is a family of brave warriors.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The scene presents the sons of Issachar.

In the second scene: The scene presents the sons of Tola, the oldest son of Issachar. These people are the grandsons of Issachar.

In the third scene: The scene presents Izrahiah as the only son of Uzzi. Izrahiah is the great-grandson of Issachar.

In the fourth scene: The scene presents the sons of Izrahiah. These people are the great-great-grandsons of Issachar.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller finishes the family history of Issachar.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The sons of Issachar: Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron
  • The sons of Tola: Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and Samuel
  • The son of Uzzi: Izrahiah
  • The sons of Izrahiah: Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Isshiah
  • Wives and children of the sons of Izrahiah
  • All the fighting men of the tribe of Issachar

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller presents the names of Issachar's sons. The storyteller specifically mentions the number four to affirm that indeed Issachar has four sons. These sons were Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron. Even though the storyteller only mentioned the names and did not include details as to the birth order of the sons of Issachar, we can say that the order of the names probably starts from the eldest or firstborn son down to the youngest son.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how would you affirm in a natural way that Issachar did indeed have four sons? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller presents the names of Tola's sons. Tola is the firstborn son of Issachar. The sons of Tola were Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and Samuel. Again, the order starts from the eldest or the firstborn son down to the youngest. Although this time, the storyteller does not confirm the number of Tola's sons, the storyteller mentions other information about Tola's sons. The storyteller describes these men as the heads or leaders of their ancestral families. The storyteller also mentions that when David was the king of Israel, David listed a total of 22,600 descendants of Tola as mighty warriors.

The third scene is shorter than the previous scene. The storyteller only introduces Izrahiah as the son of Uzzi. In the next scene, the storyteller introduces the sons of Izrahiah. Perhaps the purpose of this short scene is to connect the next generation with the family history of Issachar.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller presents the names of the sons of Izrahiah. The sons of Izrahiah were Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Isshiah. Again, the storyteller arranges the names according to the order of their birth from the eldest to the youngest. These sons, together with Izrahiah, were the five chiefs or leaders of their clans or large family groups. Also, along with their families and clans, these chiefs had 36,000 mighty warriors under them. These chiefs had many wives and sons, which made them have large numbers of warriors.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller finishes the family history of Issachar. The storyteller mentions that Issachar's genealogy lists 87,000 mighty warriors from all of the descendants of Issachar. These warriors may have served the king of Israel during their time.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it. The group can draw a family tree of Issachar or use rocks and sticks to make a model of the family on the ground.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The sons of Issachar: Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron
  • The sons of Tola: Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and Samuel
  • The son of Uzzi: Izrahiah
  • The sons of Izrahiah: Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Isshiah
  • Wives and children of the sons of Izrahiah
  • All the fighting men of the tribe of Issachar

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces Issachar. Since Issachar was the head of the family, the person who plays the character of Issachar may have to appear older than the rest of the characters. Then the storyteller will call the names of the sons of Issachar from the eldest to the youngest. The storyteller will call Tola, Puah, Jashub, and then Shimron one by one. These people will stand in front of Issachar.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Issachar, "What are you feeling or thinking as the head of your family or clan?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored; or
  • I wonder if I will be able to lead my family according to the ways of Yahweh.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Issachar, "What are you feeling or thinking as the sons of Issachar?" The people who are playing the sons of Issachar might answer things like:

  • I think Yahweh wants us to honor our father by living in a way where we obey Yahweh's commands.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller introduces the sons of Tola. The storyteller will call the names of the sons of Tola from the eldest to the youngest. The storyteller will call Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and then Samuel. These men will stand in front of Tola. But this time, the sons of Tola will pose like mighty warriors. The storyteller will comment that during the time of David as king of Israel, there were 22,600 mighty warriors from the family of Tola alone.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Tola, "What are you feeling or thinking as the head of your clan, which serves as warriors for the king of Israel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud being a father of warriors who serve the king of Israel; or
  • I feel afraid for the safety of my sons who serve as warriors. My sons might get injured or lose their lives in times of war.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Tola, "What are you feeling or thinking as the sons of Tola?" The people who are playing the sons of Tola might answer things like:

  • I think Yahweh wants us to honor our father by living in a way where we obey Yahweh's commands; or
  • I feel committed to serve the king of Israel loyally and bravely.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller introduces Izrahiah, who is the son of Uzzi. The storyteller will call Izrahiah and let Izrahiah stand in front of Uzzi.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller introduces the sons of Izrahiah. The storyteller will call the names of the sons of Izrahiah again, from the eldest to the youngest. The storyteller will call Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and then Isshiah. These men will stand in front of Izrahiah but will move beside Izrahiah, because these men were considered chiefs or leaders of their clans along with Izrahiah. The storyteller will also call more people to act as wives and sons of these five chiefs. All the men from the family of Izrahiah, Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Isshiah will pose like mighty warriors. Because these five chiefs had many wives and children, there were 36,000 mighty warriors who came from the family of Izrahiah and the sons of Izrahiah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Izrahiah, "What are you feeling or thinking as the head of a large clan?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel thankful that Yahweh blessed me with a large family; or
  • I feel worried that I may not be able to lead these people in the ways of Yahweh.

Then ask the people who are playing Izrahiah and the sons of Izrahiah, "What are you feeling or thinking about having warriors as family members?" The people might answer things like:

  • We will feel proud that we have family members who are warriors; or
  • I feel afraid for the safety of my family members who serve as warriors. My family might get injured or lose their lives in times of war.

Continue the drama.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller highlights that there were 87,000 mighty warriors listed in the genealogy records of the family of Issachar.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Issachar, "What are you feeling or thinking after you heard your family history?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel impressed with my family history. We have so many mighty warriors!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The passage presents the family history of Issachar by immediately introducing the sons of Issachar in the first scene. The storyteller refers to these men as sons of Issachar. Although the original language uses a word that means sons, the word can also mean anyone who is descended from that person. In this case, the sons in this passage were most likely really sons of their fathers. Use the same word or phrase for sons that you used in the previous passages to mean sons. For more information on sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

The second scene of the passage presents the sons of Tola. The sons of Tola were Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and Samuel. These sons were the leaders or chiefs of their own families or "father's households." Remember that a chief leads a group of people. Use the same word or phrase for leaders and "father's households" that you used in the previous passages. "Households" is in the Master Glossary.

This scene also mentions that the descendants of Tola were mighty warriors, or fighting men, during the reign of David. There were 22,600 warriors from the descendants of Tola. Use the same word or phrase for warriors or fighting men as you used in previous passages.

The fourth scene presents the sons of Izrahiah. Izrahiah had four sons. The storyteller mentions that five of these sons were leaders, or chiefs. This includes Izrahiah as a chief, or head of a father's household, too. Use the same words or phrases for heads or chiefs of fathers' households as you used previously in this passage and in previous passages.

The final scene is the conclusion of the passage. But the emphasis of this scene is on the total number of warriors from the tribe of Issachar. The scene mentions that 87,000 warriors belonged to all of the large family groups, or clans, of the tribe of Issachar. Use the same word for clans, or large family groups, that you have used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:1–5 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 7:6–13
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 7:6–13 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:6–13 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:6–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This list continues the family history of the tribes of Israel. You will remember that family histories do not usually tell all the names of the family members. Family histories may include the names of famous members of the family. And most of the time, family histories record the names of the leaders or heads of the family. This way, the people in a tribe can easily trace their ancestors and their place in the family history.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If you have not done this before, remind each other of how you list important names in a family's history in your culture. Pause this audio here.

This list presents the family history of the tribe of Benjamin. Writers of family histories or genealogies often follow the birth order when they tell the names of the family members. But in this section, the writer did not follow the birth order when he told the family history. Benjamin is the youngest son of Jacob, whom people also called Israel, and of Jacob's wife Rachel. The storyteller of the book of Chronicles presents the family history of Issachar in the previous section. But the storyteller inserts the family history of Benjamin before the storyteller presents the family history of Naphtali in the next section.

At least two significant events in the history of Israel involved the tribe of Benjamin. The first king of Israel came from the tribe of Benjamin. Yahweh chose Saul to be the king of Israel when the people asked for a king. But surprisingly, the storyteller did not mention Saul in this list.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you tell the family history of your leaders? Pause this audio here.

The other event that involved the tribe of Benjamin happened when the united kingdom of Israel divided into two factions: the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah. The northern kingdom included the tribes Reuben, Simeon, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Issachar, Asher, Zebulun, Ephraim, and Manasseh. The tribe of Benjamin stayed with the southern kingdom of Judah.

Towards the end of this section, the storyteller includes the family history of Naphtali. The summary of Naphtali's family is the shortest of all the Israelite tribes. Naphtali is the son of Jacob, whom people also called Israel, with Jacob's concubine Bilhah. When Rachel, who was Jacob's wife, could not conceive a child, Rachel gave Bilhah to Jacob to have children with. Naphtali and Dan are the two sons of Jacob and Bilhah.

We will see a more complete family history of Benjamin in future passages of 1 Chronicles.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:6–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces the sons of Benjamin.

In the second scene: The storyteller introduces the descendants of Bela.

In the third scene: The storyteller introduces the descendants of Beker.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller introduces the descendants of Jediael.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller introduces the descendants of Naphtali.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller of Chronicles
  • Benjamin
  • Sons of Benjamin: Bela, Beker, Jediael
  • Sons of Bela: Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, Iri
  • 22,034 fighting men from the descendants of Bela
  • Sons of Beker: Zemirah, Joash, Eliezer, Elioenai, Omri, Jeremoth, Abijah, Anathoth, Alemeth
  • 20,200 fighting men from the descendants of Beker
  • Son of Jediael: Bilhan
  • Sons of Bilhan: Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Kenaanah, Zethan, Tarshish, Ahishahar
  • 17,200 fighting men from the descendants of Jediael

  • Ir
  • Sons of Ir: Shuppim, Huppim
  • Aher
  • Son of Aher: Hushim
  • Naphtali
  • Bilhah, the mother of Naphtali
  • Sons of Naphtali: Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, Shillem

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller suddenly lists the three sons of Benjamin without any other introduction. Benjamin was the youngest son of Jacob, or Israel. The sons of Benjamin are Bela, Beker, and Jediael. This list followed the birth order of the sons. Bela was the eldest, followed by Beker, and then Jediael was the youngest.

In the second scene, the storyteller introduces the sons of Bela. Bela was the eldest son of Benjamin. Bela had five sons. These sons were Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, and Iri. The storyteller mentioned that these sons were mighty warriors or soldiers. These sons were also the heads of their families. This means that these sons were the leaders of their clans. To be the leader of the clan means that these people are responsible for the welfare and protection of the members of the clan. The genealogy, or family history, recorded 22,034 warriors under their clans. The exact number of warriors may have come from the census during David's time as king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question. In your culture, what are the responsibilities of the heads or leaders of your clan? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller introduced the sons of Beker. Beker is the second son of Benjamin. The sons of Beker were Zemirah, Joash, Eliezer, Elioenai, Omri, Jeremoth, Abijah, Anathoth, and Alemeth. The storyteller emphasized that all of these names were indeed the sons of Beker. The storyteller did this by stating that they were the sons of Beker at the beginning of the list of names and stating it again at the end of the list of names.

Stop here and discuss this question: How will you make it clear that these sons were the sons of Beker? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also mentioned that the family record included 20,200 mighty warriors or soldiers under the heads of their clans. This gives the impression that the family of Beker is a family of warriors.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller introduced Jediael's son Bilhan. Then the storyteller immediately presented Bilhan's sons. The sons of Bilhan were Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Kenaanah, Zethan, Tarshish, and Ahishahar. Bilhan is the only son of Jediael listed here. Perhaps this is the reason why the storyteller considered the sons of Bilhan as the sons of Jediael, too. The narrator listed them as sons of Jediael, even though it includes not only the sons but also the grandsons of Jediael. The word for sons in this list can also mean descendants.

Stop here and discuss this question: How will you make it clear that these sons were the descendants of Jediael? Pause this audio here.

These sons were also listed as mighty warriors. There were 17,200 warriors listed under their clans who served in the army.

Towards the end of the fourth scene, the storyteller introduces Shuppim and Huppim as sons of Ir, and Hushim as the son of Aher. These people are probably descendants of Jediael. Shuppim, Huppim, and Hushim are the names of the sons, but some English translations use Shuppites, Huppites, and Hushites, which may refer to a group of people. We are not sure if these names refer to individual people or to groups of people. You may choose to follow the Bible translation of your area when you name these people.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller introduces the sons of Naphtali. You will remember that Naphtali is a son of Jacob and a woman named Bilhah. The sons of Naphtali were Jahziel, Guni, and Jesher. The storyteller finishes by reminding us that all these men were also the descendants of Bilhah.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:6–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller of Chronicles
  • Benjamin
  • Sons of Benjamin: Bela, Beker, Jediael
  • Sons of Bela: Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, Iri
  • 22,034 fighting men from the descendants of Bela
  • Sons of Beker: Zemirah, Joash, Eliezer, Elioenai, Omri, Jeremoth, Abijah, Anathoth, Alemeth
  • 20,200 fighting men from the descendants of Beker
  • Son of Jediael: Bilhan
  • Sons of Bilhan: Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Kenaanah, Zethan, Tarshish, Ahishahar
  • 17,200 fighting men from the descendants of Jediael

  • Ir
  • Sons of Ir: Shuppim, Huppim
  • Aher
  • Son of Aher: Hushim
  • Naphtali
  • Bilhah, the mother of Naphtali
  • Sons of Naphtali: Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, Shillem

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller calls Benjamin and lets him stand at the center. Then the storyteller calls the three sons of Benjamin from the eldest to the youngest. The storyteller will call Bela, Beker, and Jediael.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Benjamin, "What are you feeling or thinking as the head of your family or clan?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored and thankful. God had blessed me with a family; or
  • I feel anxious. I hope I will be able to lead my family and clan according to the ways of Yahweh.

Then ask the people who are playing Bela, Beker, and Jediael, "What are you feeling or thinking as the sons of Benjamin?" The people who are playing the sons of Benjamin might answer things like:

  • We think Yahweh wants us to honor our father by living in a way where we obey Yahweh's commands.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller calls Bela and lets him stand at the center. Then the storyteller calls the sons of Bela and lets them stand in front of Bela. The storyteller calls the sons of Bela in this order: Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, and Iri. The storyteller also mentions that these sons were mighty warriors who served the kingdom. These sons are also leaders of their clans.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Bela, "What are you feeling or thinking about as the head of your family or clan who serves as warriors or soldiers for the kingdom of Israel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud that I am a father of warriors who serve the kingdom of Israel; or
  • I feel afraid for the safety of my sons who serve as warriors. My sons might get injured or lose their lives in times of war.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Bela, "What are you feeling or thinking as you serve the kingdom of Israel as warriors or soldiers? And what are you feeling or thinking about your responsibility to lead your clans?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think Yahweh wants us to honor and serve as warriors for the sake of the kingdom of Israel, or
  • I feel that we are privileged to lead our clans.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller calls Beker and lets him stand at the center. Then the storyteller calls the sons of Beker and lets them stand in front of Beker. The storyteller calls the sons of Beker in this order: Zemirah, Joash, Eliezer, Elioenai, Omri, Jeremoth, Abijah, Anathoth, and Alemeth.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Beker, "What are you feeling or thinking about as the head of your family or clan who serves as warriors or soldiers for the kingdom of Israel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud that I am a father of warriors who serve the kingdom of Israel; or,
  • I feel afraid for the safety of my sons who serve as warriors. My sons might get injured or lose their lives in times of war.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Beker, "What are you feeling or thinking as you serve the kingdom of Israel as warriors or soldiers? And what are you feeling or thinking about your responsibility to lead your clans?" The people might answer things like:

  • I think Yahweh wants us to honor and serve as warriors for the sake of the kingdom of Israel, or
  • I feel that we are privileged to lead our clans. Not all will have the opportunity to serve in such a capacity.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller calls Jediael and lets him stand at the center. Then the storyteller calls Jediael's son, Bilhan and lets Bilhan stand at the center. Then the storyteller immediately calls Bilhan's sons and lets them stand in front of Bilhan. The storyteller calls the sons of Bilhan in this order: Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Kenaanah, Zethan, Tarshish, and Ahishahar. Then the storyteller says that all of these sons are sons of Jediael. The storyteller also mentions that the family of Jediael is a family of warriors or soldiers who serve the kingdom.

The storyteller then calls Ir together with Ir's sons Shuppim and Huppim. Then the storyteller calls Aher together with Aher's son Hushim. People believe that these men were descendants of Jediael.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jediael, "What are you feeling or thinking about having only one child?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel thankful to Yahweh for blessing me, even if it is only one child; or
  • I feel sad because I do not have many children.

Then ask the person who is playing Bilhan, "What are you feeling or thinking about being the only child?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think I have the responsibility to take care of my father and our family when my father grows old; or
  • I feel sad that I do not have other siblings.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Bilhan, "What are you feeling or thinking about being a family that serves the kingdom?" The people might answer things like:

  • We need to be responsible and live in the right way so that our family will have a good reputation; or
  • It is a privilege to serve the kingdom.

Then ask the people who are playing the other descendants of Jediael, "What are you feeling or thinking about being part of this family?" The people might answer things like:

  • We need to be responsible and live in the right way so that our family will have a good reputation; or
  • It is a privilege to serve the kingdom.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller calls Naphtali and lets him stand at the center. Then the storyteller calls the sons of Naphtali. The storyteller calls the sons of Naphtali in this order: Jahziel, Guni, and Jesher. The storyteller also calls Bilhah and introduces her as the ancestor of the tribe of Naphtali.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Naphtali, "What are you feeling or thinking about as one of the heads of the 12 tribes of Israel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel thankful to Yahweh for using me to fulfill Yahweh's promise to our ancestor Abraham; or
  • I feel I have the responsibility to live out the faith of our ancestor Abraham.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Naphtali, "What are you feeling or thinking about as the sons of Naphtali?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think we have the responsibility to take care of our family and live out our faith in Yahweh.

Then ask the person who is playing Bilhah, "What are you feeling or thinking about as a part of the family history of Israel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very honored to be part of the family.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:6–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The passage presents the family history of Benjamin by immediately introducing the sons of Benjamin in the first scene. The storyteller refers to them as sons of Benjamin. Although the original language uses a word that means sons, that word can also mean anyone who is descended from the person. In this case, the sons in this passage were most likely really sons of Benjamin. Later in this passage, the storyteller talks about the sons of Jediael to refer to Bilhan and Bilhan's sons. It is also possible that Shuppim, Huppim, and Hushim were actually groups of people rather than individuals. In these cases, the word for sons refers to descendants. Use the same word or phrase for sons and descendants that you have been using in the previous passages to mean sons. For more information on sons and descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

In the second scene, the passage presents the sons of Bela. The sons of Bela were Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, and Iri. These men were leaders, or chiefs, of their father's households. Use the same word or phrase for leaders, or chiefs, and fathers' households that you have been using in the previous passages. Use these same words throughout this passage as well. Household is in the Master Glossary.

The genealogy of the sons of Bela lists 22,034 warriors, or fighting men, from the descendants of Bela. Use the same words or phrases for warrior or fighting men, and for genealogy, as you used in previous passages, and throughout this passage as well. Genealogy is in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 7:14–19
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 7:14–19 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:14–19 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:14–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This list continues the family history of the tribes of Israel. You will remember that family histories do not usually tell all the names of the family members. Family histories may include the names of famous members of the family. And most of the time, family histories record the names of the leaders or heads of the family. This way, the people in a tribe can easily trace their ancestors and their place in the family history.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If you have not done this before, remind each other of how you list important names in a family's history in your culture. Pause this audio here.

In this section, the storyteller presents the family history of the tribe of Manasseh. The 12 sons of Jacob, who people also knew as Israel, became the heads of the 12 tribes of Israel. But people recognized Manasseh and Ephraim as part of the 12 tribes, even though they were the grandchildren of Jacob. Manasseh and Ephraim were the sons of Joseph. Joseph was the son of Jacob with Jacob's wife Rachel. Jacob honored the request of Joseph to Jacob to claim Manasseh and Ephraim as Jacob's own sons. Even though Joseph's brothers betrayed Joseph, Joseph saved Jacob and Jacob's family during a severe famine.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you usually honor or show appreciation to a person who did a good thing for you? Pause this audio here.

When the Israelites were about to cross the Jordan River to conquer the Promised Land, half of the tribe of Manasseh, along with the tribes of Reuben and Gad, asked Moses at that time to let them have the land on the east side of the Jordan River. This is because these tribes had a large number of farm animals, and the area across the Jordan had good pastureland that the people could use for farm animals.

Then the other half of the tribe of Manasseh settled on the west side of the Jordan River.

Stop here and look at a map as a group. Find the place where the tribe of Manasseh settled near the Jordan River after the Israelites conquered the Promised Land. Pause this audio here.

This time, the storyteller presented this family history in a different way from the family histories in the previous sections. The family history of Manasseh does not have details that describe military involvement or details about the heads of their clans. The storyteller presented the descendants of Manasseh in an interesting way. The storyteller emphasized certain characters from the family history of Manasseh.

For example, after the storyteller mentioned Manasseh, the storyteller introduced Asriel, who was Manasseh's son with Manasseh's concubine. This concubine was from the territory of Aram, or Syria.

Another example was when the storyteller gave more emphasis to the descendants of Makir. The storyteller repeated Makir's name four times in this family history. The storyteller also described Zelophehad as the person "who had daughters." Zelophehad, who was a descendant of Makir, did not have sons but only five daughters. You will remember the story about Zelophehad's daughters in the book of Numbers. After Zelophehad died, his five daughters asked Moses for Zelophehad's portion of land.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you usually pass properties or inheritance to the next generation? Pause this audio here.

Then, finally, the storyteller also emphasized the family history of Gilead. The storyteller repeats Gilead's name twice.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you show emphasis on a person when you are telling a family history?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:14–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller presents Manasseh and the descendants of Manasseh.

In the second scene: The storyteller presents Makir and the descendants of Makir.

In the third scene: The storyteller presents Hammoleketh and the children of Hammoleketh.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller presents Shemida and the sons of Shemida.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller, who presents the family history of Manasseh
  • Manasseh
  • Aramean concubine of Manasseh
  • Descendants of Manasseh: Asriel, Makir
  • Makir's wife
  • Maacah

  • Sons of Makir: Gilead, Peresh, Sheresh
  • Zelophehad
  • Sons of Sheresh: Ulam, Rakem
  • Son of Ulam: Bedan
  • Hammoleketh, the sister of Gilead
  • Sons of Hammoleketh: Ishhod, Abiezer, Mahlah
  • Shemida
  • And the sons of Shemida: Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, Aniam

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller presents the descendants of Manasseh. The storyteller introduces Asriel as the son of Manasseh with Manasseh's concubine, who came from Aram, which we know today as Syria. We do not know the name of that concubine. Then this concubine gave birth to Makir, who became the father of Gilead.

In the second scene, Makir probably took Maacah as his wife from the family of Huppim and Shuppim. Many scholars believe that Maacah is a sister of Huppim and Shuppim, rather than Makir's sister, but we are not sure. It seems that Zelophehad was another descendant of Makir, but we are not sure exactly how Zelophehad is related to Makir.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you say that you are not sure how a particular person is part of the family? Pause this audio here.

Zelophehad had no sons. Zelophedad only had five daughters.

Then the storyteller goes back to presenting the family of Makir. This time, the storyteller clearly mentions a woman named Maacah as the wife of Makir. Maacah gave birth to a son for Makir. Maacah named the son "Peresh." Peresh had a brother named Sheresh. The sons of Sheresh were Ulam and Rekem. And the son of Ulam was Bedan.

Finally, the storyteller concludes this scene when he emphasizes that these people were indeed the sons or descendants of Gilead, who is the son of Makir, who is the son of Manasseh. It is possible that the storyteller wants to emphasize that the people he has just listed are all related to Gilead.

In the third scene, the storyteller introduces Hammoleketh. Many scholars believe that Hammoleketh is the sister of Gilead. Then the storyteller presents the children of Hammoleketh. The children of Hammoleketh were Ishhod, Abiezer, and Mahlah.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller ends the presentation of the family history of Manasseh. The storyteller introduces Shemida and the sons of Shemida. The sons of Shemida were Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, and Aniam. Shemida may be a son of Gilead, but we are not sure how Shemida is connected to the family.

We are not sure about many of the relationships in this passage, so you may choose to follow the most recent and well-known Bible translation of your area when you make decisions about how family members are related to each other in this passage.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:14–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller, who presents the family history of Manasseh
  • Manasseh
  • Aramean concubine of Manasseh
  • Descendants of Manasseh: Asriel, Makir
  • Makir's wife
  • Maacah

  • Sons of Makir: Gilead, Peresh, Sheresh
  • Zelophehad
  • Sons of Sheresh: Ulam, Rakem
  • Son of Ulam: Bedan
  • Hammoleketh, the sister of Gilead
  • Sons of Hammoleketh: Ishhod, Abiezer, Mahlah
  • Shemida
  • And the sons of Shemida: Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, Aniam

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces and calls Manasseh to the front. Then the storyteller introduces Manasseh's concubine. The storyteller mentions that the concubine came from the land of Aram and that she was an Aramean. The storyteller lets the concubine stand beside Manasseh. Then the storyteller mentions that Manasseh's concubine gave birth to Manasseh's sons. The sons of Manasseh and Manasseh's concubine were Asriel and Makir. After that, the storyteller calls Asriel and Makir and lets them stand in front of Manasseh and Manasseh's concubine. Then the storyteller presents Makir as the father of Gilead. Gilead is the firstborn son of Makir. The storyteller will call Gilead and let him stand in front of Makir.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Manasseh, "What are you feeling or thinking about as part of the 12 tribes of Israel and being the head of your family or clan?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored to be recognized as part of the 12 tribes of Israel. I feel I have the responsibility to lead my family and clan to obey the laws of Yahweh; or
  • I feel worried about whether I will be able to fulfill my role as the father of my clan faithfully.

Then ask the person playing Manasseh's concubine, "What are you feeling or thinking about living with a person whose culture is different from yours?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think this will be hard for me, since I need to learn and live the new culture; or
  • I feel happy because these people accept me in the family even though my culture is different.

Then ask the people who are playing the sons of Manasseh, "What are you feeling or thinking about the fact that your father and mother came from different cultures?" The people might answer things like:

  • I think it is the will of Yahweh to love and accept people, even if the people have different cultures; or
  • I think it will promote peace and understanding.

Then ask the people who are playing Asriel and Makir, who were the sons of Manasseh, "What are you feeling or thinking about the fact that your father and mother came from different cultures?" The people might answer things like:

  • I think it is the will of Yahweh to love and accept people, even if the people have different cultures; or
  • I think it will promote peace and understanding.

Now ask the person playing Gilead, "What are you feeling or thinking about being included in the genealogy or family history of your family?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored to be included in our family history.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller talks more about Makir and Makir's family line. The storyteller calls Makir again and lets Makir stand in the center. Then the storyteller calls Maacah with her brothers Huppim and Shuppim. Maacah, Huppim, and Shuppim will stand at the left side of Makir. Then the storyteller calls Makir and Maacah to the center. The storyteller will tell that Makir took Maacah to be his wife.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Makir and Maacah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel very happy and honored because people have recognized us; or
  • I think I have the responsibility to lead my family to obey the laws of Yahweh and to keep the honor that people have given me.

Continue the drama.

Then the storyteller suddenly introduces Zelophehad. The storyteller mentions that Zelophehad is a descendant of Makir and that Zelophehad did not have sons, only five daughters.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Zelophehad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very thankful. Yahweh has blessed me with daughters; or
  • I feel sad. If only Yahweh would bless me with sons, too. How will my inheritance pass down to my family?

Continue the drama.

After that, the storyteller continues to present the family line of Makir. The storyteller says that Maacah, who was the wife of Makir, gave birth to a son. The storyteller calls Peresh, who was the son of Maacah and Makir. The storyteller lets Peresh stand in front of Maacah and Makir. Then the storyteller mentions that Peresh had a brother named Sheresh. The storyteller lets Sheresh stand beside Peresh. Then the storyteller presents the sons of Sheresh, who were Ulam and Rekem. The storyteller lets these sons stand in front of Sheresh. Then the storyteller calls Bedan and introduces Bedan as the son of Ulam. The storyteller lets Bedan stand in front of Ulam. The storyteller then finishes this scene when he says that these people were the descendants of Gilead. Gilead was the son of Makir, and Makir was the son of Manasseh.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Makir and Maacah, "What were you feeling or thinking when you had your children?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think we are to live our lives and lead our family according to the commands of Yahweh; or
  • We feel worried about whether we will be able to fulfill our roles as parents according to the commands of Yahweh.

Ask the people playing Peresh and Sheresh, "What are you feeling or thinking as the children of Makir and Maacah?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think we are to honor our parents by living according to the commands of Yahweh; or
  • We feel responsible to help our children live their lives in the right way.

Ask the people playing Ulam and Rekem, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think we have the responsibility to teach the laws of Yahweh to the next generation; or
  • Yahweh chose us as a people, and we feel very happy to be part of Yahweh's own people.

Ask the person playing Bedan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think I have the responsibility to pass the family's legacy to the next generation; or
  • I feel afraid because I do not know if I can fulfill my role to lead my family according to the commands of Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller introduces Hammoleketh and lets Hammoleketh stand in the center. Many scholars believe that Hammoleketh is the sister of Gilead. Then the storyteller will introduce the children of Hammoleketh. The children of Hammoleketh were Ishdod, Abiezer, and Mahlah. The storyteller lets the children of Hammoleketh stand in front of Hammoleketh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hammoleketh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and honored to be included in our family history; or
  • I feel worried about whether I can live up to the expectations of my family.

Ask the people playing Ishdod, Abiezer, and Mahlah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think we have the responsibility to teach the laws of Yahweh to the next generation; or
  • Yahweh chose us as a people, and we feel very happy.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller introduces Shemida and lets Shemida stand in front. Shemida was a descendant of Gilead. Then the storyteller will call the sons of Shemida. The sons of Shemida were Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, and Aniam. The storyteller lets the sons of Shemida stand in front of Shemida.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shemida, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel the responsibility to continue the traditions and practices of my family; or
  • I feel worried that I may fail my family.

Ask the people playing Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, and Aniam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think we have the responsibility to preserve our tradition and connect it to the next generation; or
  • We feel very happy that we have the opportunity to make things better as we lead the next generation.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:14–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The first scene introduced Manasseh along with his Aramean concubine and their sons. Aram was a place in the northeast outside of Israel. Today, Aram is called Syria. Use the same word for Aram here as you have used in previous passages.

Although the storyteller uses the word son in this passage, son can also mean a descendant, or the grandsons and great-grandsons of a father. In this passage, the word for sons means descendant when the storyteller lists the "sons, or descendants" of Manasseh. Asriel, Zelophehad, and the "sons of Gilead" are all also probably descendants rather than the direct son of someone. Use the same word or phrase for sons or descendants that you have been using in the previous passages. For more information on sons and descendants, refer to the Master Glossary.

Use the same word or phrase for concubine, or wife of lower status, as you used in previous passages.

Maacah was the sister of Huppim and Shuppim. Other translations understand these two names as groups of people, which they call Huppites and Shuppites. You can choose to use the names Huppim and Shuppim, or you can address them as people of Huppites and Shuppites.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 7:20–29
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 7:20–29 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:20–29 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 7:20–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.
