These leaders all lived in Jerusalem. Jerusalem is the most important city of the Jewish people. Translate Jerusalem the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 8:29–40
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This passage is a part of the introduction to the book of 1 Chronicles. Remember that the previous passages were the first two parts of a three-part story about descendants in the tribe of Benjamin. The storyteller in the previous passage listed all the leaders of the families who lived in Jerusalem. Now, in this passage, we learn about some other descendants of the tribe of Benjamin. These descendants of Benjamin all lived in Gibeon and Jerusalem. This passage also includes the ancestors and descendants of Saul, the first king of Israel.

In this passage, the storyteller lists 15 generations of the genealogy of Saul. This passage is very similar to another passage later in 1 Chronicles that also describes the genealogy of Saul. Some of the names in this passage are spelled differently than in other passages of the Old Testament, even though it is the same person here and in the other passages. The current passage also includes names that we do not find in any other passages of the Old Testament.

The storyteller begins with Jeiel, the father of the city of Gibeon. Some translations do not have the name Jeiel. Instead, these translations say "the father of Gibeon" but do not say the person's name. We know from a later passage in 1 Chronicles that Jeiel was the father of Gibeon. Gibeon is the name of a city, and not the name of a person. "Father of Gibeon" probably means that this person started the city of Gibeon, or that this person was the leader of Gibeon. Remember that Gibeon was an important city in the land of the tribe of Benjamin.

Stop here and look at a map that includes the city of Gibeon as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone in your community who started a town, village, or city. Describe how this person started the town, village, or city. Pause this audio here.

Jeiel lives in the city of Gibeon, and Jeiel's wife's name is Maacah. This passage says that Jeiel has eight sons, but we know from other passages that Jeiel has 10 sons, and the storyteller does not list two of the sons in this passage. Jeiel's firstborn or eldest son is Abdon. The other sons in this passage are Zur, Kish, Baal, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, and Zeker. From the later passage in 1 Chronicles, we know that Ner is also a son of Jeiel. Some translations include Ner in this current passage.

Jeiel's son Zeker is called Zechariah in the passage later in 1 Chronicles. Also, this current passage says that Mikloth is the father of Shimeah, and it is not clear here that Mikloth is also a son of Jeiel. However, the later passage in 1 Chronicles does include Mikloth as the 10th son of Jeiel. Shimeah is also called Shimeam in the later passage in 1 Chronicles.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Ask each person on your team the different ways they might say their names. Pause this audio here.

The next part of the passage is difficult to understand. It is difficult to know who lived near their relatives in Jerusalem, and who these relatives were. Some people think that these words in the passage mean that some of the people of the tribe of Benjamin moved from Gibeon to Jerusalem. Others think that this passage says that Jeiel and his wife lived in Gibeon, and all of Jeiel's sons lived in Jerusalem. Finally, other people think that maybe only Mikloth and Shimeah lived in Jerusalem. If it is Mikloth and Shimeah who live in Jerusalem, that means that, unlike their brothers, Mikloth and Shimeah lived in Jerusalem with other people from the tribe of Benjamin, and not with their own brothers. Remember that the other sons of Jeiel probably lived in Gibeon.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem as a group. Pause this audio here.

Most of the rest of this passage is about the descendants of Ner, one of the 10 sons of Jeiel. The storyteller does not talk about any descendants from the other sons of Jeiel, besides Mikloth's son, Shimeah.

Ner is the father of Kish, and Kish is the father of Saul, the first king of Israel. It is surprising that the storyteller says Ner is the father of Kish, because in another part of the Old Testament, the storyteller says that Abiel is the father of Kish. Some translations include here that Saul was the king.

Saul has four sons: Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal. In another part of the Old Testament, the storyteller says Saul only has three sons: Jonathan, Malki-Shua, and Esh-Baal. This passage is difficult to understand because we learn here that Saul has four sons and many descendants, but in the passage later in 1 Chronicles, we learn that all of Saul's family died with Saul. It is possible that not all of Saul's family died with Saul, because here we see that Saul had many descendants.

In other parts of the Old Testament, sometimes Saul's son Esh-Baal has the name Ish-Bosheth, and sometimes Jonathan's son Merib-Baal has the name Mephibosheth. It is possible that people in Israel changed these names because it was shameful to have a son with the same name as the Canaanite god, Baal. The new names end with Bosheth, which means "shame" in the original language.

Jonathan's son Merib-Baal is the father of Micah. Micah has four sons, Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz. Micah's son Ahaz is the father of Jehoaddah. Again, Jehoaddah is called Jadah in the later passage in 1 Chronicles. Jehoaddah and Jadah are the same person.

Jehoaddah has three sons named Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri is the father of Moza, and Moza is the father of Binea. Most people think that the correct translation of the next part of this passage is that Binea is the father of Raphah, Raphah is the father of Eleasah, and Eleasah is the father of Azel. But some people think that Binea, Raphah, Eleasah, and Azel are all sons of Moza because it says, "Raphah his son," and this could mean Moza is the father of Raphah.

Azel has six sons named Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. "Bokeru" is very similar to the word for "firstborn" in the original language, so some people think this means that Azrikam is Azel's firstborn son. If a translation says "Azrikam the firstborn," then there is one son's name missing from the six sons of Azel in this passage.

Eshek has three sons named Ulam, Jeush, and Eliphelet. It is most likely that Eshek is the brother of Azel here. However, it is also possible that Eshek is not the brother of Azel. Eshek might be another descendant of Benjamin who is related to Benjamin in a different way.

The first son of Eshek is Ulam. The second and third sons of Eshek are Jeush and Eliphelet. Ulam's sons were brave warriors who could handle the bow. A bow is a weapon that warriors use to shoot arrows at their enemies.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bow and arrows as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the brave warriors and mighty men in your community. Pause this audio here.

The sons of Ulam had 150 sons and grandsons. This part of the passage might be from a list of military men who fought for the tribe of Benjamin.

In the final part of this passage, the storyteller says that all these were the descendants of Benjamin. It is most likely that this is a final summary of the entire three-part story about the descendants of the tribe of Benjamin.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: Jeiel has 10 sons. One of Jeiel's sons is Ner. These people all live near their relatives in Jerusalem.

In the second scene: Saul's grandfather is Ner, and Saul's father is Kish. Saul has four sons.

In the third scene: Saul's son Jonathan has a son named Merib-Baal. Merib-Baal's son is Micah, and Micah has four sons. One of Micah's sons is Ahaz.

In the fourth scene: Ahaz's son is Jehoaddah. Jehoaddah has three sons, including one son named Zimri. Zimri is the father of Moza. Moza is the father of Binea, Binea is the father of Raphah, Raphah is the father of Eleasah, Eleasah is the father of Azel.

In the fifth scene: Azel has six sons. Eshek has three sons. One of Eshek's sons is Ulam. The sons of Ulam were brave warriors who used the bow.

In the sixth scene: All of these were descendants of the tribe of Benjamin.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeiel
  • Jeiel's wife, Maacah
  • Jeiel's 10 sons, Abdon, Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zeker, and Mikloth
  • Shimeah, the son of Mikloth
  • Kish, the son of Ner
  • Saul, the son of Kish
  • Saul's four sons, Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal
  • Merib-Baal, the son of Jonathan
  • Micah, the son of Merib-Baal
  • Micah's four sons, Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz
  • Jehoaddah, the son of Ahaz
  • Jehoaddah's three sons, Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri
  • Moza, the son of Zimri
  • Binea, the son of Moza
  • Raphah, the son of Binea
  • Eleasah, the son of Raphah
  • Azel, the son of Eleasah
  • Azel's six sons, Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan
  • Eshek, the brother of Azel
  • Eshek's three sons, Ulam, Jeush, and Eliphelet
  • And 150 sons and grandsons of Ulam

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that this is the third and final passage in a three-part story about the descendants of the tribe of Benjamin. In this passage, we see descendants of Benjamin who lived in Gibeon and Jerusalem. Gibeon and Jerusalem are both important cities for the genealogy or family history of Saul, the first king of Israel.

In the first scene, Jeiel is the father of Gibeon, and Jeiel lives in the city of Gibeon. Remember that some translations do not have the name Jeiel. Instead, those translations say "the father of Gibeon." Remember that Gibeon is a city, and not a person. Also, remember that "father of Gibeon" probably means that Jeiel started the city of Gibeon. Gibeon is a city about 10 kilometers northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of the city of Gibeon again as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the first part of this passage? Some examples include:

  • Jeiel was the founder of the city of Gibeon. Jeiel also lived in the city of Gibeon; or
  • The person who started the city of Gibeon also lived in the city of Gibeon.

Pause this audio here.

Jeiel also has a wife. Jeiel's wife's name is Maacah. It is likely that Maacah also lives in the city of Gibeon with her husband, Jeiel. Your team may choose to translate this part of the passage to say that Jeiel and his wife lived in Gibeon.

In the next part of this scene, the storyteller lists the names of Jeiel's sons. The firstborn son of Jeiel is Abdon. Remember that in this current passage, there are only eight names of Jeiel's sons. These eight sons are: Zur, Kish, Baal, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, and Zeker. However, we know from the later passage in 1 Chronicles that Jeiel has two other sons named Ner and Mikloth. Some translations choose to include the names Ner and Mikloth in this current passage. Your team can decide if you want to include Ner after Baal and before Nadab, and if you want to include Mikloth as the final son of Jeiel. Ner is in the next scene of the passage, so it might be helpful to include Ner here as well so that people remember that Ner is one of the sons of Jeiel. Mikloth is the father of Shimeah in this passage, but it is not clear here that Mikloth is a son of Jeiel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How many sons of Jeiel will you include in this passage? Will you include Ner and Mikloth as sons of Jeiel in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Also, in the later passage in 1 Chronicles, Zeker is called Zechariah and Shimeah is called Shimeam. Zeker is the same person as Zechariah, and Shimeah is the same person as Shimeam. It is a different way to say the same name. It would be best to say these names the same in both places of 1 Chronicles.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you say the names Zecher and Shimeah in this passage? Pause this audio here.

In the final part of this scene, we learn that "they lived near their relatives in Jerusalem." Remember that it is hard to know who lived near their relatives in Jerusalem, and who the relatives were. In the original language it says they lived both "with their relatives" and "opposite their relatives." Many people think "with their relatives" and "opposite their relatives" are two ways to describe the same idea. The word "opposite" can also mean "unlike." Here are some translations you can choose for this part of this passage:

  • Some of the people of the tribe of Benjamin moved from Gibeon to Jerusalem. These people who moved to Jerusalem lived near, or alongside, their relatives; or
  • Jeiel and his family lived in Gibeon, and all of Jeiel's sons lived in Jerusalem near their other relatives; or
  • Unlike their brothers, Mikloth and his son Shimeah lived in Jerusalem. The rest of the sons of Jeiel lived in Gibeon. Mikloth and Shimeah lived alongside other people from the tribe of Benjamin.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the final part of this scene about people who lived near their relatives in Jerusalem? Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem again as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Ner is the father of Kish. Kish is the father of Saul, the first king of Israel. This part about Saul and his ancestors and descendants is probably the most important part of this passage. Remember that Ner is one of Jeiel's sons. Some translations choose to say that Saul is the son of Kish and the grandson of Ner so that the focus is on Saul. Most people that heard 1 Chronicles when it was written already knew that Saul was the first king of Israel, but it might be helpful to add "King" Saul here to remind listeners that Saul was the first king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will your translation team talk about Saul in this passage? Some translations include:

  • King Saul was the son of Kish, and the grandson of Ner; or
  • Ner was the father of Kish. Kish was the father of King Saul; or
  • Ner was Kish's father, and Kish was Saul's father.

Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Saul has four sons. Saul's sons are Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal. Remember that in the later passage in 1 Chronicles, Saul has only three sons. Also, Esh-Baal is the same person as Ish-Bosheth from another part of the Old Testament. In the original language, "bosheth" means shame, and people who wrote 1 Chronicles probably changed the name from Baal to Bosheth because Baal was a Canaanite god.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the name Esh-Baal in this passage? You can choose to translate it as Ish-Bosheth instead, you could choose to include both names as ways to say this son's name, or you could choose to explain what the name means. Pause this audio here.

Saul's son Jonathan has a son named Merib-Baal. Merib-Baal is the same person as Mephibosheth. In other parts of the Old Testament, people call Jonathan's son Mephibosheth.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Will you translate Jonathan's son's name as Merib-Baal here, or as Mephibosheth? Pause this audio here.

Merib-Baal has a son named Micah. Micah has four sons named Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz. In the later passage of 1 Chronicles, Tarea is called Tahrea. Your team should decide how you want to say Tarea's name. It would be best to say the name the same way in both places in 1 Chronicles.

In the fourth scene, we learn about the descendants of Ahaz, who is the son of Micah. Ahaz is the father of Jehoaddah. In the later passage of 1 Chronicles, Jehoaddah is called Jadah. Your team should decide how you want to say Jehoaddah's name. It would be best to say the name the same way in both places in 1 Chronicles.

Jehoaddah has three sons. These sons' names are Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri has a son named Moza. Moza has a son named Binea. Binea is the father of Raphah, Raphah is the father of Eleasah, and Eleasah is the father of Azel. In the later passage of 1 Chronicles, Raphah is called Rephaiah. Your team should decide how you want to say Raphah's name. It would be best to say the name the same way in both places in 1 Chronicles.

In the fifth scene, Azel has six sons named Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. Remember that the word "Bokeru" in the original language normally means "firstborn" instead of the name of a person. It is possible that some people made Bokeru a name so that there would be six sons of Azel. The storyteller repeats again that all of these were the sons of Azel. Your translation team can decide if you want to repeat this again or not.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will your translation team translate the names of Azel's sons? Will you repeat that "all of these were the sons of Azel?" Pause this audio here.

Azel has a brother named Eshek. Translations say "his brother" and not "Azel's brother," so some people think that Eshek is not the brother of Azel, but someone else's brother. Eshek has three sons named Ulam, Jeush, and Eliphelet. Ulam is the first son, Jeush is the second son, and Eliphelet is the third son.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Ask someone from your translation team to tell you about their sons in the order that the sons were born. Pay attention to how you talk about the order of sons in your language. How will you translate the three sons of Azel in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Ulam's sons were brave warriors who could handle the bow, or who knew how to shoot arrows from a bow with great skill. Remember a bow is a weapon that people used to shoot arrows at their enemies. Some translations say that the sons of Ulam were brave warriors or mighty bowmen. The sons of Ulam had 150 sons and grandsons.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bow and arrows as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember the discussion from the previous script about brave warriors and mighty men in your community. How will you translate this part of the passage about Ulam's sons who were mighty bowmen? Pause this audio here.

In the final scene, we hear a summary for the entire three-part passage about the descendants of the tribe of Benjamin. All of these people were descendants of Benjamin.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeiel
  • Jeiel's wife, Maacah
  • Jeiel's 10 sons, Abdon, Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zeker, and Mikloth
  • Shimeah, the son of Mikloth
  • Kish, the son of Ner
  • Saul, the son of Kish
  • Saul's four sons, Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal
  • Merib-Baal, the son of Jonathan
  • Micah, the son of Merib-Baal
  • Micah's four sons, Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz
  • Jehoaddah, the son of Ahaz
  • Jehoaddah's three sons, Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri
  • Moza, the son of Zimri
  • Binea, the son of Moza
  • Raphah, the son of Binea
  • Eleasah, the son of Raphah
  • Azel, the son of Eleasah
  • Azel's six sons, Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan
  • Eshek, the brother of Azel
  • Eshek's three sons, Ulam, Jeush, and Eliphelet
  • And 150 sons and grandsons of Ulam

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Jeiel started the city of Gibeon. Jeiel lived in Gibeon, and Jeiel's wife was named Maacah. Jeiel's firstborn son was Abdon. Jeiel's other sons were Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zeker, and Mikloth. Mikloth was the father of Shimeah. Unlike their brothers, Mikloth and Shimeah lived in Jerusalem near their other relatives.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeiel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that I have so many sons. Yahweh blessed me with these children; or
  • I worked hard to start the city of Gibeon, and I am proud of the city that I built; or
  • I am glad that Gibeon is such an important city in Israel.

Continue the drama.

Ner was the father of Kish. Kish was the father of King Saul. Saul was the father of Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal. Jonathan was the father of Merib-Baal, and Merib-Baal was the father of Micah. Micah was the father of Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Saul, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud to be the first king of Israel; or
  • I named my youngest son Esh-Baal because my wife likes the Canaanite god, Baal; or
  • I am proud of my oldest son, Jonathan.

Continue the drama.

Ahaz was the father of Jehoaddah, and Jehoaddah was the father of Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri has a son named Moza. Moza has a son named Binea. Binea is the father of Raphah, Raphah is the father of Eleasah, and Eleasah is the father of Azel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ahaz, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I am a descendant of Benjamin; or
  • I am also glad that I am a descendant of Saul, the first king of Israel; or
  • I am proud that I have so many descendants.

Continue the drama.

Azel had six sons. Azrikam was Azel's firstborn son. Azel's other sons were Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. These were all the sons of Azel. Azel's brother, Eshek, had three sons. Eshek's first son was Ulam, his second son was Jeush, and his third son was Eliphelet. The sons of Ulam were mighty warriors who were good at using a bow. The sons of Ulam had 150 sons and grandsons. All of these were the descendants of Benjamin.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the sons and grandsons of Ulam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are proud to be mighty warriors to protect our people; or
  • We work hard to be excellent at shooting a bow; or
  • We are proud that we are descendants of Saul, the first king of Israel.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Jeiel founded, or started, the city of Gibeon. Gibeon was one of the cities where Levites could live in the land of the tribe of Benjamin.

One of Jeiel's 10 sons was Mikloth. Mikloth and his son, Shimeah, lived near their other relatives or family members in Jerusalem. Use the same word for relatives and for Jerusalem as you used in previous passages. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

Saul was the son of Kish and the grandson of Ner. Ner was one of Jeiel's sons. Saul was the first king of Israel. Use the same word for Saul as you used in previous passages.

All of these people were descendants of Benjamin. The tribe of Benjamin was one of the 12 tribes of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Benjamin as you used in previous passages.

The sons of Ulam were good at shooting arrows with their bows. Use the same words and phrases for bows and arrows as you used in previous passages.

In this passage, "sons" means descendants. Use the same word for son as you have in previous passages. For more information on sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 9:1–9
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 9:1–9 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:1–9 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller finished a three-part story about descendants in the tribe of Benjamin. The final part of the three-part story included descendants of Benjamin who lived in Gibeon and Jerusalem. The storyteller also included descendants of Saul, the first king of Israel. Now, the storyteller starts to list the Israelites who returned to live in Jerusalem after their time of captivity, or exile, in Babylon. People from the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh, as well as priests, Levites, and temple servants, all returned to Jerusalem. In this passage, the storyteller lists the leaders of the families from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin who return to Jerusalem. This passage is the first part of a four-part story about the first Israelites who return to Jerusalem.

Some translations include the first part of this passage with the previous passage because the two together form a summary of the genealogies, or family records, for all of the previous passages in the book of 1 Chronicles. The storyteller says that people listed or wrote down the history of the kings of Israel in a book. Those people also wrote down the official family records of all of the Israelites in the same book of the kings of Israel. When the storyteller talks about the official family records of "all of Israel," he is including both the northern and southern kingdoms of Israel. The storyteller might say "all Israel" because the storyteller hoped that Israel would be a united kingdom someday.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your family remember the history of your family? Pause this audio here.

In the next part of the passage, the storyteller talks about the time when the Babylonians captured the people of Judah and forced them to go to Babylon. Babylon was the capital city of the country of Babylonia, and the Babylonians were enemies of the Israelites. The people of Judah are the people in the southern kingdom of Israel that included the tribes of Judah and Benjamin. Yahweh allowed the Babylonians to capture the people of Judah because the people of Judah were unfaithful to Yahweh. To be unfaithful means to rebel or sin against Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss a time when your people group or another people group had to leave your home. Why did you leave? How did you come back home if you were ever able to return home? Pause this audio here.

The exiled Israelites were in captivity for 70 years. Now, the first exiled Israelites come back from Babylon to their villages in Israel again. The Israelite exiles live on the land that their ancestors owned in the past. The storyteller says that some Israelites, as well as some religious people, come back to Jerusalem. These religious people include priests, Levites, and temple servants. Priests, Levites, and temple servants were all people who worked in the temple of Yahweh. In the following passages, the storyteller will list the names of the priests, Levites, and temple servants who returned to Jerusalem.

The rest of this passage is about the other Israelites who come back to Jerusalem from their captivity in Babylon. These Israelites are not Levites, and they do not work in the temple of Yahweh. This passage is only about people who return to live in Jerusalem, and it is not about people who return to live outside of Jerusalem. This passage is similar to a list of names in the book of Nehemiah. Nehemiah also lists the people who return to Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem, Babylon, Israel, and Judah as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that some people from the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh return to live in Jerusalem. We know from the book of Nehemiah that only about one family out of every 10 families goes back to live in Jerusalem. The tribes of Judah and Benjamin formed the southern kingdom of Judah, and the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh were the two most important tribes in the northern kingdom of Israel.

In the rest of these lists about people who come back to Jerusalem after the exile to Babylon, we see no more information about families from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh. This might be because these lists only include the leaders of larger families who returned to Jerusalem. It is likely that only single families and individuals from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh returned to Jerusalem, and not any leaders of large families from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh. For the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh, Jerusalem was not the home of their ancestors, and that might be why not a lot of families from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh come back to Jerusalem.

The storyteller lists people from the tribe of Judah who return to Jerusalem. You will remember that Judah had three sons named Perez, Shelah, and Zerah. First, the storyteller talks about descendants of Perez, the son of Judah. Uthai is probably the leader of this group of people because the storyteller says Uthai's name first. Uthai is the son of Ammihud. Ammihud is the son of Omri. Omri is the son of Imri. Imri is the son of Bani. Bani is a son, or descendant, of Perez. These people are all descendants of Judah's son Perez.

Next, the storyteller talks about the descendants of Shelah, the son of Judah. Most people think that the Shilonites are the same as the Shelanites, or the descendants of Shelah. Asaiah is the firstborn, or eldest, son of Shelah. Asaiah is probably the leader of the Shilonites.

Finally, the storyteller lists the sons, or descendants, of Zerah, another son of Judah. Jeuel is probably the leader of the Zerahites. The total number of people from the tribe of Judah who move back to Jerusalem is 690. This is not the total number of families from the tribe of Judah who move back to Jerusalem from Babylon. This number is different from the number in the lists in Nehemiah, probably because Nehemiah did not count people at the same time as the storyteller here in 1 Chronicles counts people.

In the final part of this passage, the storyteller lists people from the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem. Remember that not all of the people of the tribe of Benjamin return to Jerusalem. The storyteller gives the names of four leaders of families in the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem. These four leaders of families are Sallu, Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam.

The storyteller lists some of the ancestors of these four important leaders of the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem. Sallu is the son of Meshullam. Meshullam is the son of Hodaviah. Hodaviah is the son of Hassenuah. Ibneiah is the son of Jeroham. Elah is the son of Uzzi. Uzzi is the son of Micri. Meshullam is the son of Shephatiah. Shephatiah is the son of Reuel. Reuel is the son of Ibnijah.

This list of descendants of the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem is similar to the list in the book of Nehemiah. Sallu and Ibneiah are both in the list in the book of Nehemiah, but Elah and Meshullam are not in the list in the book of Nehemiah.

The total number of people from the tribe of Benjamin who move back to Jerusalem from captivity in Babylon is 956. This is not the number of families, but the number of individual people. However, each of the men whom the storyteller lists by name for the tribe of Benjamin is a leader of the house of their father. A house of their father is all of the people who live in the same household together, and other closely related family members. The head of the household is the leader of the people in the household.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller says that someone had written down all of the names of the people of Israel in the genealogies of the history of the kings of Israel.

In the second scene: The people of Judah go to Babylon because the people of Judah are unfaithful to Yahweh.

In the third scene: The people of Judah return to Jerusalem to live in their own villages. The people who return include Israelites, priests, Levites, and temple servants. People from the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh return to Jerusalem.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller lists the descendants of the tribe of Judah who return to Jerusalem. The family leaders are descendants of Judah's three sons named Perez, Shelah, and Zerah.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller lists four important descendants of the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem: Sallu, Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam.

The characters in this passage are:

  • People of Israel
  • People of Judah
  • Some Israelites who return to Jerusalem
  • Priests
  • Levites
  • Temple servants
  • Three sons of Judah: Perez, Shelah, and Zerah
  • Uthai, a descendant of Perez
  • Asaiah and his sons, a descendant of Shelah
  • Jeuel, a descendant of Zerah
  • 690 people from the tribe of Judah
  • Sallu
  • Ibneiah
  • Elah
  • Meshullam
  • And 956 people from the tribe of Benjamin

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that this passage comes after a three-part story about the descendants of the tribe of Benjamin. In the previous passage, the storyteller listed descendants of Benjamin who lived in Gibeon and Jerusalem. Now, the storyteller begins a final summary of all of the genealogies, or family histories, in the book of 1 Chronicles so far. The storyteller also lists the Israelites who return to Israel after their captivity in Babylon. Remember that this passage is the first part in a four-part story about all the first Israelites, priests, Levites, and temple servants who returned to Jerusalem after their captivity in Babylon.

The first scene is the end of the previous passages in the book of 1 Chronicles about the family history of many different tribes of Israel. Remember that some translations include this first scene with the previous passage. "All Israel" does not mean that these genealogies included every person in Israel. "All Israel" probably means these genealogies included both the northern and southern kingdoms of Israel.

The "book of the kings of Israel" means the history of the kings of Israel. This history of the kings of Israel included the official family records for the tribes of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate "book of the kings of Israel"? How will you show that people also wrote, or listed, the names of the people of Israel in this book of the kings of Israel? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the Babylonians take the people of Judah to Babylon. Remember that Babylon is the capital city of the country of Babylonia, and the Babylonians are the enemies of the people of Judah. Babylon is in the present-day country of Iraq. It is important to remember that the Babylonians take the people of Judah to Babylon. However, the reason that the people of Judah go to Babylon is because the people of Judah were unfaithful to Yahweh. It might be better to include this reason first—that the people of Judah were unfaithful to Yahweh—so Yahweh allowed the Babylonians to take the people of Judah to Babylon.

Stop here and look at a map of Babylon and Israel as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that the Israelites went to Babylon because the Israelites were unfaithful to Yahweh? Pause this audio here.

The Israelites went to Babylonia a long time before the other scenes in this passage happen, but the storyteller has this scene here to describe what happens after the time of captivity in Babylon. Some people think that the first scene in this passage happens before the action in the second scene. If this is true, it would mean that scribes wrote down all the genealogies of the people of Israel before the people of Israel went to Babylon. However, it is not clear whether the first scene happened before the second scene. It is possible that the storyteller starts a new idea in the second scene when he talks about the exile to Babylon. Then, the storyteller lists the first Israelites who come back from Babylon to Jerusalem in the following scenes.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In the first scene of this passage, the storyteller makes a summary statement about how someone wrote down all of the genealogies of Israel in a book about the kings of Israel. We do not know when this happened. However, in the second scene, the people from the tribe of Judah were unfaithful to Yahweh, so the Babylonians captured the Israelites and took the Israelites to Babylon. How will you show that the second scene starts a new topic? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the Israelites return from their exile in Babylon to their homes in Israel. These homes are the villages, property, and land that the ancestors of the Israelites owned in the past. Remember that most of the Israelites who returned to Israel probably were not born in Israel, and they probably never lived in Israel before this time.

The storyteller says that some Israelites return to Israel, and some Israelites who are religious workers for the people of Israel also return. These religious people include priests, Levites, and temple servants. Remember that the storyteller lists the names of the priests, Levites, and temple servants who return to Israel in the following passages.

Israelites from the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh return to Jerusalem. Remember that these Israelites are not religious workers. Also, remember that not all of the people in the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh return to Jerusalem. Remember that we see no more information about people from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh in the lists of people who return to Jerusalem. It is possible that the storyteller wants to show that Israel should be unified as one kingdom, and that is why the storyteller includes tribes from both the northern and southern kingdoms of the divided kingdom of Israel.

Stop here and look again at a map of Jerusalem, Babylon, Israel, and Judah as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller lists the leaders from the tribe of Judah who return to Jerusalem. Remember that Judah's three sons are Perez, Shelah, and Zerah. The storyteller lists three important leaders—Uthai, Asaiah, and Jeuel—who are each descendants of one of Judah's three sons. At the end of the fourth scene, the storyteller says that 690 people from the tribe of Judah come back to live in Jerusalem. Some translations include this information at the beginning of this scene so that it is clearer that all of the people in this scene are from the tribe of Judah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate this scene so that it is clear that all of these people are from the tribe of Judah? Pause this audio here.

The first leader from the tribe of Judah is Uthai, who is a descendant of Perez. Uthai is probably the leader of his people. Uthai's father is Ammihud, Ammihud's father is Omri, Omri's father is Imri, Imri's father is Bani, and Bani's father is Perez.

The next important person from the tribe of Judah is Asaiah. The storyteller says that Asaiah is the firstborn, or eldest, son of Shelah. This probably just means that Asaiah is the leader in the clan of people who descended from Shelah. Asaiah is also probably the leader of his people. Remember that Shelah is one of the other sons of Judah. The Shilonites are probably a different way to say the Shelanites, who are the people descended from Shelah.

Finally, the third important person in the tribe of Judah is Jeuel. Jeuel is probably the leader of the Zerahites. Remember that Zerah is another son of Judah.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller lists four important leaders from the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem. These four leaders are Sallu, Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam. It might be helpful to clearly say at the start of the fifth scene that all of these people are from the tribe of Benjamin and that these people also returned to Jerusalem. The storyteller also lists some of the ancestors of each of these leaders of the tribe of Benjamin.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How can you show that the four main leaders in the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem are Sallu, Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam? Pause this audio here.

At the end of the fifth scene, the storyteller says that 956 people from the tribe of Benjamin move back to live in Jerusalem. The storyteller also says that each of the men whose names he said was a head, or leader, of the house of that man's father.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • People of Israel
  • People of Judah
  • Some Israelites who return to Jerusalem
  • Priests
  • Levites
  • Temple servants
  • Three sons of Judah: Perez, Shelah, and Zerah
  • Uthai, a descendant of Perez
  • Asaiah, a descendant of Shelah
  • Jeuel, a descendant of Zerah
  • 690 people from the tribe of Judah
  • Sallu
  • Ibneiah
  • Elah
  • Meshullam
  • And 956 people from the tribe of Benjamin

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

People listed the names of all the people of Israel in the official family records. People wrote that information in a book called the History of the Kings of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are honored that we are all in the official family records in the book of the kings of Israel; or
  • We are glad that we are a part of the people of Israel; or
  • We feel important because our names are in the genealogies in the book of the kings of Israel.

Continue the drama.

The enemies of the people of Judah captured the people of Judah. The enemies of the people of Judah took the people of Judah to Babylon. Yahweh allowed this to happen because the people of Judah were not faithful to Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We do not understand why Yahweh let our enemies capture us and take us to Babylon; or
  • We are very sorry that we were unfaithful to Yahweh and that we sinned against Yahweh; or
  • We hope that Yahweh rescues us and forgives us from our sin against Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

After a long time, the first people came back from their captivity in Babylon to live in Jerusalem on their own land and in their own villages. The first people who came back were some Israelites, as well as some priests, Levites, and temple servants. The Israelites who came back to live in Jerusalem were from the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Israelites, priests, Levites, and temple servants who are the first to return to Jerusalem, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We hope that we can live on our own land again and in our own villages again; or
  • We are happy to be back in our land now! or
  • We are very sorry that our ancestors sinned against Yahweh. We want to follow Yahweh so that we never need to leave our land again; or
  • We are honored that we are the people who get to come back to Israel first.

Continue the drama.

Some of the Israelites who came back were from the tribe of Judah. The total number of people who returned to Jerusalem from the tribe of Judah was 690 people. The people from the tribe of Judah were all descendants of Judah's three sons: Perez, Shelah, and Zerah. Perez's descendant Uthai returned to Jerusalem. Uthai was the son of Ammihud, Ammihud was the son of Omri, Omri was the son of Imri, Imri was the son of Bani, Bani was the son of Perez, and Perez was the son of Judah. Shelah's descendant Asaiah and Asaiah's sons also returned to Jerusalem. Asaiah was the oldest son of Shelah. Asaiah was one of the Shilonites. Zerah's descendant Jeuel also returned to Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Uthai, Asaiah, and Jeuel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are ready to be back in our land of Israel again; or
  • We are glad to be leaders of our family groups as we come back to our land; or
  • We are proud that we are descendants of Perez, Shelah, and Zerah, who are all sons of Judah; or
  • We are nervous that we are the leaders of this small group of people who came back to Israel. We hope that no one attacks us or tries to stop us from living in our land again.

Continue the drama.

Other Israelites who came back to Jerusalem were from the tribe of Benjamin. The total number of people from the tribe of Benjamin who came back was 956 people. Sallu, the son of Meshullam, was the first leader of the tribe of Benjamin. Meshullam was the son of Hodaviah, and Hodaviah was the son of Hassenuah. The next leader of the tribe of Benjamin was Ibneiah, the son of Jeroham. The third leader of the tribe of Benjamin was Elah, the son of Uzzi. Uzzi was the son of Micri. The final leader of the tribe of Benjamin was Meshullam, the son of Shephatiah. Shephatiah was the son of Reuel, and Reuel was the son of Ibnijah. All these men were leaders of the houses of their fathers.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Sallu, Ibneiah, Elah, and Meshullam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are honored to lead our people of the tribe of Benjamin as we come back to our land in Jerusalem; or
  • We hope that we can protect our people and live peacefully in our land now; or
  • We want to follow Yahweh and be faithful to Yahweh so that we do not have to go back to Babylon again.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

People wrote down the names of all Israel in the genealogies that they included in the history of Israel's kings. People wrote this history in the books of the kings of Israel. People wrote information, names, and histories on paper or stone tablets. During the time of Israel, people wrote on paper, and then they glued the paper together and wound it around two sticks. People called this a scroll, or a book. To read the scroll, people unrolled the scroll. People rolled the scroll around the two sticks when they were not reading the scroll.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scroll as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the idea of a book in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Israel means the people of Israel. Genealogies are lists of names of people who belong to the same ancestor. The kings of Israel are the kings who ruled in the land of Israel. Some of the kings ruled in the northern kingdom, which people also called Israel, and some of the kings of Israel ruled in the southern kingdom of Judah. Here, the history of the kings of Israel probably means both the northern and southern kingdoms. Translate Israel, genealogies, and kings the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Israel, genealogies, and kings, refer to the Master Glossary.

The enemies of the people of Judah take the people of Judah captive to Babylon. The people of Judah are the people in the southern kingdom of Israel. The tribe of Judah was the largest tribe in the southern kingdom. Babylon is the capital city of the country of Babylonia. Translate Judah the same as you have in previous passages. Translate Babylon or Babylonia in the same way as you have in other parts of the Old Testament. For more information on Judah, refer to Judah in the Master Glossary.

The people of Judah went to Babylon because the people of Judah were unfaithful to Yahweh. Unfaithfulness means the people of Judah are not faithful, that the people have sinned and rebelled against Yahweh. Translate unfaithfulness the same as you have in previous passages.

Some Israelites, priests, Levites, and temple servants were the first people to return to their own cities and land in Israel. Israelites are the people of Israel. Priests are from the tribe of Levi and the family of Aaron. Priests offer sacrifices in the temple, or house, of Yahweh. Levites are other people who work in the temple of Yahweh who are from the tribe of Levi, but they are not priests. Temple servants are other people who work in the temple of Yahweh. Translate Israelites, priests, cities, and Levites the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Israelites, priests, and Levites, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate temple servants? If you choose to call these people "temple servants," refer to temple and servant in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

People from the tribes of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh all return to Jerusalem.

Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh are four of the 12 tribes of Israel. Jerusalem is the most important city in Israel. Use the same words for Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, Manasseh, and Jerusalem as you used in previous passages. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller lists people from the tribe of Judah who return to Jerusalem. The storyteller says these people are sons of sons of Judah. Sons can sometimes mean descendants. So "sons of sons" is another way to talk about several generations of descendants. See sons, descendants, and generations in the Master Glossary to decide how you will translate sons in this passage.

The storyteller also lists people from the tribe of Benjamin who return to Jerusalem. All of these people were the leaders of their fathers' families, or households. A household includes all the people who live in a house, and the head is the leader of the household. But people could also use the word household, or house, to mean a large group of people who did not live in the same house, but who were part of a larger group. So we know that these men were leaders of the groups of their families. Other ways to translate this could be clan leaders, family leaders, heads of families, or leaders of the clans. Translate heads of families in the same way as you have in previous passages. For more information on household, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 9:10–16
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 9:10–16 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:10–16 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:10–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller started to list the first people who came back to Jerusalem after their time of captivity, or exile, in Babylon. The storyteller listed the names of people from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin who came back to live in Jerusalem. Now, the storyteller lists the priests and the Levites who returned to live in Jerusalem. This passage is the second part of a four-part story about the first Israelites who return to Jerusalem.

It is important that religious leaders are among the first people to come back to Jerusalem after the time of captivity in Babylon. Priests and Levites are both from the tribe of Levi. Priests are a certain kind of Levites who serve Yahweh in special ways. Priests are descendants of Aaron. Levites are not descendants of Aaron, but they serve Yahweh in other ways.

In the book of Nehemiah, we hear similar lists of priests and Levites who return to Jerusalem after they were captive in Babylon, but the lists do have some differences. We also know from the book of Nehemiah that these priests and Levites come back to serve in the temple, or house, of Yahweh, which the Israelites rebuild in Jerusalem. Remember that the enemies of the Israelites destroyed the first temple.

First, the storyteller lists the priests who return to the city of Jerusalem. Priests were people who worked in the temple of Yahweh. Priests also offered sacrifices to Yahweh. The six priests in this passage are Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jakin, Azariah, Adaiah, and Maasai. These are not all of the same priests that Nehemiah lists in the book of Nehemiah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe the religious leaders in your community. What do your religious leaders do? Pause this audio here.

The first three priests are Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jakin. We know from a later passage in 1 Chronicles that people divided the priests by lots. Jedaiah received the second lot, Jehoiarib received the first lot, and Jakin received the 21st lot. These lots told priests their assignments in the house of Yahweh.

Another priest who returns to Jerusalem is Azariah, the son of Hilkiah. Hilkiah was the son of Meshullam. Meshullam was the son of Zadok. Zadok was the son of Meraioth. Meraioth was the son of Ahitub. It is likely that the storyteller does not list all of Azariah's genealogy in this passage. In the book of Nehemiah, Azariah is called Seraiah.

It is difficult to know who the chief official, or high priest, was in charge of the house of Yahweh. Some translations say that Azariah was the high priest, but other translations say that Ahitub was the high priest. A high priest was the most important priest in the house of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the most important religious leader in your community. How is your most important religious leader different from the other religious leaders in your community? Pause this audio here.

The final two priests in this passage are Adaiah and Maasai. Adaiah was the son of Jeroham, Jeroham was the son of Pashhur, and Pashhur was the son of Malkijah. Maasai was the son of Adiel. Adiel was the son of Jahzerah. Jahzerah was the son of Meshullam. Mehsullam was the son of Meshillemith. Meshillemith was the son of Immer. Maasai is called Amashai in the book of Nehemiah, and Maasai's ancestors are also a little different in the book of Nehemiah.

There were 1,760 priests who returned to live in Jerusalem. These priests were all leaders of their families. This is a different number of priests than the number of priests in the book of Nehemiah. The book of Nehemiah lists the total number of priests as 1,192. It is possible that the storyteller of 1 Chronicles gives a more up-to-date list and includes more priests because now there are more priests than when Nehemiah wrote the book of Nehemiah.

The priests were all capable men. The priests were also skilled in their work in the house, or temple, of Yahweh. The men were qualified in their work. In the original language, capable men can also mean important men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the religious leaders in your community again. What skills do these religious leaders have? What do these leaders need to know how to do? Pause this audio here.

Next, the storyteller lists the Levites who return to the city of Jerusalem. Levites help the priests with work in the temple of Yahweh. The book of Nehemiah has a very similar list of Levites who return to Jerusalem. In the book of Nehemiah, Nehemiah says that 284 Levites return to Jerusalem. The storyteller in this passage does not say the total number of Levites who return to Jerusalem.

The storyteller gives us the names of seven of the Levites who return to Jerusalem. It is likely that many of these Levites were musicians in the temple of Yahweh. The Levites are Shemaiah, Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, Mattaniah, Obadiah, and Berekiah. The storyteller includes information about the ancestors of Shemaiah, Mattaniah, and Obadiah, who are from three important families in the tribe of Levi. Shemaiah was a descendant of Merari. Merari was one of the three sons of Levi. Mattaniah was a descendant of Asaph. Obadiah was a descendant of Jeduthun. The storyteller does not give any information about the families of Bakbakkar, Heresh, or Galal in this passage.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people become religious leaders in your community? Does the role of religious leader pass down from father to son, like in this passage, or does it happen in a different way? Pause this audio here.

The final Levite in this passage is Berekiah, the son of Asa. Asa was the son of Elkanah. Some people think that Obadiah and Berekiah live in the villages of Netophah. But, it is most likely that of these men, only Elkanah and his family lived in the villages of Netophah with the Netophathites. The Netophathites were descendants of Caleb, who was a descendant of Judah. Netophah was in the hill country of Judah near the village of Bethlehem. Remember that villages are smaller settlements without walls, and hills are raised areas of land.

Stop here and look at a map of the location of Netophah and a picture of hill country as a group.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:10–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists six priests who return to Jerusalem.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists seven Levites who return to Jerusalem.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The priests Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jakin
  • The high priest, Azariah
  • The priests Adaiah and Maasai
  • 1,760 priests who return to Jerusalem
  • The Levite Shemaiah from the clan of Merari
  • The Levites Bakbakkar, Heresh, and Galal
  • The Levite Mattaniah, a descendant of Asaph
  • The Levite Obadiah
  • And the Levite Berekiah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that in the previous passage, the storyteller said that some Israelites, priests, Levites, and temple servants were the first people to return to Jerusalem after their captivity in Babylon. The storyteller also listed the people from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin who returned to Jerusalem. Now, we learn the names of the priests and Levites who returned to Jerusalem. Remember that this passage is the second part in a four-part story about the first Israelites who return to Jerusalem. The final two parts of this story will be about the temple servants who return to Jerusalem.

As the first scene begins, it is important to remember that this passage continues to tell the story from the previous passage. Many translations begin this passage with "of the priests" or "from the priests," but it may be helpful to remind listeners that these priests are in the group of people who returned first to Jerusalem from their captivity in Babylon. The second scene begins in a similar way as the storyteller tells about the Levites who returned to Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to begin the first and second scenes in this passage so that listeners remember these priests and Levites are people who returned to live in Jerusalem? Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the storyteller lists six priests who return to Jerusalem. These priests are heads of families, and this list only includes the names of the heads of families. There were many more than six priests who returned to Jerusalem. Some translations include the names of all six of these priests at the beginning of the scene so that no one will be confused about who the priests are who return to Jerusalem and who are the ancestors of these priests. Some people also include here the general information about the priests that is at the end of the scene. Here is one way to translate the beginning of the first scene: "The priests Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jakin, Azariah, Adaiah, and Maasai all returned to live in Jerusalem. These priests were all leaders of their families. These priests were all capable men who were skilled in their work in the house of Yahweh."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate the beginning of this scene? Should you include all the names at the beginning of the scene? Should you also include the information about these men's skills and duties here, or at the end of this scene? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller does not give any additional information about the families of Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jakin. Azariah also returns. Remember that some people think that Azariah is the high priest, while other people think that Ahitub was the high priest because Ahitub is at the end of the list of names of the ancestors of Azariah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Would it be better to clearly state that Azariah was the high priest, or to make it possible for people to understand it was either Azariah or Ahitub? For example:

  • Azariah son of Hilkiah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Zadok, the son of Meraioth, the son of Ahitub. Azariah was the official in charge of the house of God; or
  • Azariah son of Hilkiah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Zadok, the son of Meraioth, the son of Ahitub, who was the official in charge of the house of God.

Pause this audio here.

The final two priests in this scene are Adaiah and Maasai. The storyteller tells us three generations of Adaiah's ancestors and five generations of Maasai's ancestors.

At the end of the first scene, the storyteller says that 1,760 priests returned to live in Jerusalem. These priests were all leaders of their families. Remember, the priests are all capable men who are skilled at the work they do in the temple of Yahweh. Some translation teams may want to include this information at the beginning of this scene instead of at the end of this scene.

In the second scene, the storyteller lists seven Levites who return to the city of Jerusalem. The Levites are Shemaiah, Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, Mattaniah, Obadiah, and Berekiah. Remember that three of the Levites in this passage are from important families in the tribe of Levi. Shemaiah is a descendant of Merari, Mattaniah is a descendant of Asaph, and Obadiah is a descendant of Jeduthun.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Would it be helpful here to include the information that a Merarite is a descendant of Merari, one of the three sons of Levi? Pause this audio here.

Berekiah, the son of Asa, also returns to Jerusalem. Asa was the son of Elkanah. Remember that it is most likely that Elkanah lived in the villages of Netophah. Remember, Berekiah lived in Jerusalem. The villages of Netophah were in the hill country of Judah, about eight kilometers southeast of Bethlehem.

Stop here and look at a map of the location of Netophah and a picture of hill country again, as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:10–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The priests Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, and Jakin
  • The high priest, Azariah
  • The priests Adaiah and Maasai
  • 1,760 priests who return to Jerusalem
  • The Levite Shemaiah from the clan of Merari
  • The Levites Bakbakkar, Heresh, and Galal
  • The Levite Mattaniah, a descendant of Asaph
  • The Levite Obadiah
  • And the Levite Berekiah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

These priests also returned to Jerusalem. The priests were Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jakin, Azariah, Adaiah, and Maasai. The priests were all leaders of their families. These priests were capable men who were skilled in their work in the temple of Yahweh. Azariah was the high priest in charge of the temple of Yahweh. Azariah was the son of Hilkiah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Zadok, the son of Meraioth, the son of Ahitub. Adaiah was the son of Jeroham, the son of Pashhur, the son of Malkijah. Maasai was the son of Adiel, the son of Jahzerah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Meshillemith, the son of Immer. There were 1,760 priests who returned to Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jakin, Azariah, Adaiah, and Maasai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are honored that we are priests in the temple of Yahweh; or
  • We are glad to be back in the land of Israel so we can worship Yahweh in this new temple; or
  • We are confident in our skills as we serve Yahweh in Yahweh's temple.

Continue the drama.

These Levites also returned to Jerusalem. The Levites who returned were Shemaiah, Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, Mattaniah, Obadiah, and Berekiah. Shemaiah was the son of Hasshub, the son of Azrikam, the son of Hashabiah, who was a descendant of Merari. Merari was one of the sons of Levi. Mattaniah was the son of Mica, the son of Zicri, the son of Asaph. Obadiah was the son of Shemaiah, the son of Galal, the son of Jeduthun. Berekiah was the son of Asa, the son of Elkanah, who lived in the villages of the Netophathites.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shemaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad to return to the land of Israel to serve in the temple of Yahweh; or
  • I am proud to be a descendant of Merari, one of the sons of Levi; or
  • I am ready to do anything I can to serve in the temple of Yahweh.

Also, ask the person playing Berekiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My ancestor was Elkanah, who lived in the villages of the Netophathites. I am proud that I am a part of the family of Elkanah; or
  • I am ready to serve Yahweh in Yahweh's temple; or
  • I am proud to be a Levite who serves Yahweh in Yahweh's temple.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:10–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The priests returned to Jerusalem. Priests were from the tribe of Levi and the family of Aaron. Priests offered sacrifices in the temple, or house, of Yahweh. Translate priests the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on priests, refer to the Master Glossary.

Azariah was probably the high priest in charge of the house of God. The high priest was the most important priest. The high priest offered the sacrifice for the people on the Day of Atonement, and the high priest was the only priest Yahweh allowed to go into the Most Holy Place of the temple. Although we know this means high priest, you should probably translate this part of the passage as "the leader of the house of God." The house of God means the temple of God. Translate "house of God" the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on high priest, house of God, and God, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller lists the ancestors of the priests who return to Jerusalem. For example, the storyteller says that Adaiah was the son of Jeroham, the son of Pashhur, the son of Malkijah. Sons can sometimes mean descendants. In this passage, son usually means a father-and-son relationship, although sometimes the storyteller does not list all of the generations in a family, and therefore "sons" may mean "descendants, or grandsons." Translate sons in the same way you have in previous passages. See sons and descendants in the Master Glossary.

The priests were the heads of their fathers' families, or households. This means the priests were leaders of the groups of their families. A household includes all the people who live in the house, and the head is the leader of the household. Other ways to translate this could be clan leaders, family leaders, heads of families, or leaders of the clans. Translate leaders of families, or fathers' households, in the same way as you have in previous passages. For more information on household, refer to the Master Glossary.

Levites were other people from the tribe of Levi who worked in the temple of Yahweh, but they were not priests. Levi was also the name of one of the 12 tribes of Israel. Remember that Levi had three sons: Kohath, Gershon, and Merari. One of Kohath's grandsons was Aaron, who became the first priest for the people of Israel. Male descendants of Aaron were responsible for serving Yahweh as priests for Israel. The descendants of Gershon and Merari did other work in the temple of Yahweh. Translate Levites in the same way as you have in previous passages. For more information on Levites, refer to the Master Glossary.

Berekiah was the son of Asa, who was the son of Elkanah. Elkanah lived in the villages of the Netophathites. Villages are small towns with no walls around them. Translate villages the same as you have in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.
