Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 9:17–27
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 9:17–27 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:17–27 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:17–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller continues the list of the people who return to Jerusalem after their exile to Babylon. The storyteller already listed people from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin, the priests, and the Levites who came back. Now, in this passage, the storyteller lists the gatekeepers who come back. The storyteller also tells what the gatekeepers do in the house of God. The gatekeepers protected the tent of Yahweh during the time of Moses, they protected the temple of Yahweh during the time of the kings of Israel, and now the gatekeepers protect the new temple in the time after the exile. This is the third part in a four-part story about the people who returned to Jerusalem.

In the past, the Israelites worshipped Yahweh in a tabernacle, or the Tent of Meeting. The Israelites could pick up this tent and move it from place to place. Later, King David's son Solomon built the temple of Yahweh. Enemies of the Israelites destroyed this first temple, and the enemies took the Israelites to Babylon. In this passage, some of the Israelites and some of the religious leaders have returned to Jerusalem, and the Israelites have rebuilt the temple. There is now a new, second temple. Both of these temples and the tabernacle shared the same basic structure, and there were gatekeepers for each of them.

We hear a similar passage in the book of Nehemiah about the gatekeepers who come back to Jerusalem after the exile. Some of the names of the gatekeepers are different in that passage. Some people think that the gatekeepers that the storyteller lists here are family names of the ancestors of the gatekeepers, and not the names of the actual gatekeepers who came back to Jerusalem.

There were four main gatekeepers. The gatekeepers were the temple guards at the gates, or entrances, of the temple. We know from later in this passage that the temple had one gate on each of the four sides of the temple. The gatekeepers defended the temple from any person or any animal who might try to come into the temple without permission. It was important to keep the temple spiritually clean according to the law of Moses. It was a big responsibility to protect the temple of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What places in your community have guards or gates? What do the guards or gates protect? Who are the guards? How do the guards protect the entrances to these places? Pause this audio here.

The names of the gatekeepers were Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their relatives. These gatekeepers were all Levites, which means that these men were all from the tribe of Levi. The chief gatekeeper, or the leader of the gatekeepers, was Shallum. Shallum guarded the King's Gate, which was the gate on the eastern side of the temple that the king usually used to enter the temple. Some people think that Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their relatives also guarded the King's Gate with Shallum.

A long time ago, King David appointed either Shallum or Shallum's ancestors to guard the gates of the Tent of Meeting. We know that the first gatekeepers were gatekeepers of the Tent of Meeting, which people also called the tabernacle. Later, when Solomon built the temple, the gatekeepers did the same jobs at the temple. The tabernacle and the temple are both the house of God. In some places in this passage, the storyteller calls the temple "the house of God." The Israelites sometimes continued to call the temple "the Tent of Meeting" or "the house of God." In this passage, the storyteller says that the descendants of Shallum still guard the gates of the temple.

It is difficult to know where the King's Gate and the camp of the Levites are, and whether the storyteller is talking about gatekeepers at the time when he wrote this passage, or gatekeepers during the time of Moses. Some people think that the gatekeepers in this passage guard the King's Gate, and the ancestors of those gatekeepers guarded the camp of the Levites during the time of Moses. Other people think that the gatekeepers in this passage guarded the King's Gate in the past, but now the same gatekeepers guard the camp of the Levites, which is where the Levites now live near the temple.

Shallum and his family guarded the thresholds, or entrances, to the house of God. This is probably the same Shallum who was the chief gatekeeper of the temple, but some people think this was a different person named Shallum. Shallum was the son of Kore, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah, and Shallum's relatives were from the Korahite family. Korah was from the tribe of Levi, just like Aaron and the high priests.

A threshold is usually a large stone block as wide as a door frame. A threshold is the bottom of a door frame. Shallum and his family stood in front of each threshold, or entrance, to the tent to protect and guard the tent. In this passage, tent means the temple. Shallum's ancestors also guarded the entrance to the dwelling, or house, of Yahweh. In the past, Yahweh's house was a tabernacle. During the time that the storyteller writes this passage, Yahweh's house is a temple.

Stop here and look at a picture of a threshold as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe the threshold or entrance into your home or another tent, building, or place that you know. How do you make sure that no one comes into the house, tent, building, or place without permission? Pause this audio here.

Another gatekeeper in the past was Phinehas, the son of Eleazar. Phinehas was the leader of the gatekeepers when the people of Israel went across the Jordan River into the land of Canaan. Phinehas' father, Eleazar, was the high priest at that time. Yahweh was with Phinehas. Some people translate this part of the passage as a prayer that Yahweh may be with Phinehas, but most people say that Yahweh was with Phinehas.

Another gatekeeper in the past was Zechariah, the son of Meshelemiah. Zechariah guarded the entrance to the tent of Yahweh's presence. Here, the tent of Yahweh's presence means the temple of Yahweh. We will see later in the book of 1 Chronicles that Zechariah was the firstborn, or oldest, son of Meshelemiah, and that Zechariah guarded the north side of the temple of Yahweh. It is not clear why the storyteller includes Zechariah here in this list of gatekeepers. Maybe Zechariah was a leader of the gatekeepers during Zechariah's life.

When the storyteller wrote this passage, there were 212 present-day guards at the entrances and gates of the temple. This included Shallum and all the temple guards in this passage so far. The number of gatekeepers is different here than in the book of Nehemiah. Some people think that the storyteller of the book of 1 Chronicles counted the gatekeepers at a different time or in a different way than the storyteller of the book of Nehemiah counted the gatekeepers.

The gatekeepers lived in the villages of their ancestors. The gatekeepers' ancestors lived in these villages since the time of Joshua. The people listed the gatekeepers in the genealogies, or family records, in the villages where the gatekeepers lived.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Where did your ancestors live? What are the villages, towns, or cities of your ancestors? How do you keep the records or the history of your families? Pause this audio here.

In the past, King David and the seer, or prophet, Samuel gave the gatekeepers from the past their jobs to guard the gates of the temple of Yahweh. A seer is a prophet, or a person who sees things from Yahweh that other people do not see. We know that Samuel already died before David was the king, but Samuel did prepare the way for David to be king. David organized the worship in the Tent of Meeting, or house of Yahweh, including choosing the guards of the house of Yahweh during David's time. It was important to choose faithful men to be the gatekeepers because this was a permanent official duty to guard and protect Yahweh's house.

The gatekeepers that David and Samuel chose, and the gatekeepers' sons, or descendants, were in charge of the house of Yahweh, which people called the tent. In this passage, the tent probably means the temple. It is also possible that the storyteller could mean both the tent and the temple since the gatekeepers guarded both the tent and the temple.

The gatekeepers were on all four sides of the temple because there were four gates of the temple. The gates were on the east, west, north, and south sides of the temple. The sun rises in the east, and the sun sets in the west. It is likely that the four gatekeepers at the beginning of this passage each guarded one of the four gates of the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about different directions in your language? Pause this audio here.

At certain times, relatives of the four main gatekeepers came from their villages to help guard the temple for seven days at a time. The chief gatekeepers probably lived in Jerusalem, and their relatives lived in villages outside of Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about something that you do regularly, or at certain times during the year. Make sure you choose something you do regularly and not something that you only do occasionally. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller reminds us that the four main gatekeepers were Levites, or from the tribe of Levi. These main gatekeepers were responsible for each of their four gates. These men were permanently on duty and were trustworthy. The men were also responsible to guard the storerooms and treasuries of the temple of Yahweh. The treasuries probably had supplies in them. Some people think that all of the gatekeepers or all of the Levites were responsible for the storerooms and treasuries of the temple, but it is probably best to say that the four main gatekeepers were responsible for the storerooms and the treasuries of the temple.

The main gatekeepers stayed near the temple at night. This might mean that the gatekeepers lived near the temple, or it might mean that the gatekeepers stayed up all night to protect the temple. The gatekeepers needed to be near the temple so that they could guard the temple and open the gates each morning. This might mean the gatekeepers unlocked the gates with a key or that the gatekeepers opened the gates. A key is a tool that people use to open a lock on a door or gate. People make keys out of wood or metal. The lock and key protect what is inside a door or a gate.

Stop here and look at a picture of a key as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the discussion from earlier in this step about places that people guard in your community. How long does one guard stay at the same place to guard the entrance to that place? How often do other people come to relieve this guard? If the guards sleep during their time on duty, where do the guards sleep?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:17–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the gatekeepers who returned for the temple of Yahweh. The storyteller also lists some of the gatekeepers who guarded the temple many years ago.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells the duties of the gatekeepers for the temple of Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Shallum, the chief gatekeeper and his family
  • Shallum's father, Kore, Kore's father, Ebiasaph, and Ebiasaph's father, Korah
  • Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman and their brothers
  • Phinehas, son of Eleazar
  • Yahweh
  • Zechariah, son of Meshelemiah
  • 212 gatekeepers
  • David
  • Samuel
  • Four chief gatekeepers
  • And relatives of the four chief gatekeepers

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that the storyteller continues to list the people who come back to Jerusalem after the exile. In the previous passage, the storyteller listed the priests and the Levites who came back. Now, the storyteller lists the gatekeepers. Remember that gatekeepers are also Levites, but gatekeepers take care of the temple in different ways than the other Levites. Remember also that the gatekeepers have taken care of the tent of Yahweh, or the temple of Yahweh, since the time of Moses. Remember that this is the third part in a four-part story.

In the first scene, it might be helpful to say the gatekeepers returned to live in Jerusalem after the exile. Remember that gatekeepers guard the entrances, or gates, of the temple. You could also translate gatekeepers as guards, temple guards, or security guards.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion in the previous step about guards in your community. How will you translate gatekeepers in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The chief gatekeeper was Shallum. The other gatekeepers were Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their relatives. It might be better to say that Shallum was the main gatekeeper before you list the other gatekeepers. Shallum guarded the King's Gate on the east side of the temple.

Remember that some translations say these gatekeepers not only guard the camp where the Levites currently live, but they also used to guard the King's Gate. Remember also that other people believe the storyteller of 1 Chronicles means these gatekeepers guarded the King's Gate at the time when the storyteller wrote this, but that different gatekeepers guarded the camp of the Levites in the time of Moses.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Use objects, like rocks, to show the temple with four gates and the camp of the Levites. Use other objects, like sticks, to show the gatekeepers. Show the different ways to translate this part of the passage. How will your translation team translate this part of the passage? Pause this audio here.

Shallum and his relatives guarded the thresholds, or entrances, to the temple. Remember that in this passage, the storyteller uses the words tent and temple to both mean "temple." Remember that we learn later in this passage that the temple had four entrances, one on each side of the temple. Remember that Shallum is probably the same person who is the chief gatekeeper earlier in this passage. Remember that Shallum is from the Korahite family, which is an important family in the Kohathite clan in the tribe of Levi. In the past, Shallum's ancestors also guarded the entrance to the house of Yahweh, which they called the tabernacle in the past.

Stop here and look at a picture of a threshold as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate threshold or entrance in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The rest of the first scene describes two other gatekeepers of the temple of Yahweh who were gatekeepers many years before. These gatekeepers are Phinehas, son of Eleazar, and Zechariah, the son of Meshelemiah. Phinehas was a leader of the gatekeepers during his time, and Yahweh was with Phinehas. Other ways to translate "Yahweh was with Phinehas" are "Yahweh's presence was with Phinehas" or "Yahweh helped Phinehas."

Zechariah, the son of Meshelemiah, also guarded the entrance to the Tent of Meeting. Remember that the storyteller sometimes calls the temple "the Tent of Meeting" in this passage.

In the second scene, the storyteller gives information about the duties of the gatekeepers of the temple of Yahweh. First, the storyteller says there were 212 gatekeepers when the storyteller wrote the book of 1 Chronicles. Remember, the numbers in 1 Chronicles are not exactly the same as the numbers of gatekeepers in the book of Nehemiah.

Next, the storyteller tells where the gatekeepers lived. The gatekeepers lived in the villages of their ancestors, and the people wrote down the genealogies, or family records, of the places where the gatekeepers lived.

The storyteller also says that David and the seer, or prophet, Samuel appointed these trustworthy gatekeepers to these important positions to guard the temple of Yahweh. Remember that Samuel was a prophet during the time of David, and Samuel received messages from Yahweh that other people did not hear.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that the gatekeepers had an important role that required David and Samuel to trust them? How will you translate "seer," or "prophet"? Some different ways to translate this part of the passage are:

  • David and the seer, Samuel, made the important jobs of gatekeepers. The job of gatekeeper was a position of trust; or
  • King David and the prophet Samuel wanted faithful men to be gatekeepers; or
  • King David and Samuel, the prophet of Yahweh, trusted these men. So, David and Samuel chose the first men to do the important work to guard the gates of the temple.

Pause this audio here.

The gatekeepers that David and Samuel chose had children. The sons, or descendants, of these gatekeepers became the new gatekeepers of the tent of Yahweh. Remember that the tent of Yahweh probably means the temple in this passage.

Next, the storyteller says the gatekeepers guarded all four sides of the temple. There was a gate on each of the four sides of the temple at the east, west, north, and south. Remember, the sun rises in the east, and the sun sets in the west. When you face the sun as it rises in the east, the north is on the left hand and the south on the right hand.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion in the previous step about different directions in your language. How will you translate the four sides of the temple? Pause this audio here.

The four chief gatekeepers guarded the gates, but sometimes relatives of the chief gatekeepers came to relieve the chief gatekeepers. The chief gatekeepers probably lived in Jerusalem so they could always be near the temple, and the relatives of the chief gatekeepers lived in villages outside of Jerusalem. The relatives came at certain times to help, and these relatives stayed to guard the gates of the temple for seven days at a time.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion from the previous step about something that you do regularly. How will you translate this part of the passage that the relatives of the main gatekeepers came "from time to time" to relieve the main gatekeepers in their duties to guard the temple? Pause this audio here.

The four chief gatekeepers were all Levites, and these men had a permanent responsibility to guard the temple. The gatekeepers also guarded the storerooms and treasuries of the temple. These gatekeepers stayed near the temple at night, which might mean they stood and guarded the gates all night long, or it could mean they slept in rooms near the temple so they could get up easily to unlock the gates in the morning.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion from the previous step about how long guards stay in the same place to guard that place, and when or if guards sleep during their time on guard duty. How will you say that the gatekeepers stayed around the temple all night long? Here are some translation options:

  • The gatekeepers stood as guards near the gates of the temple all night; or
  • The gatekeepers slept in rooms near the gates of the temple at night; or
  • The gatekeepers stayed near the gates at night.

Notice that the final translation option might mean that the gatekeepers guarded the gates at night, or it might mean that the gatekeepers slept somewhere near the gates at night. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:17–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Shallum, the chief gatekeeper
  • Shallum's father, Kore, Kore's father, Ebiasaph, and Ebiasaph's father, Korah
  • Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman and their brothers
  • Phinehas, son of Eleazar
  • Yahweh
  • Zechariah, son of Meshelemiah
  • 212 gatekeepers
  • David
  • Samuel
  • Four chief gatekeepers
  • And relatives of the four chief gatekeepers

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The gatekeepers returned to live in Jerusalem after the exile. The chief gatekeeper was Shallum. Shallum guarded the King's Gate on the east side of the temple until this day. Other gatekeepers were Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their brothers. These men were all Levites. Shallum was the son of Kore, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah, and Shallum was from the family of the Korahites. Shallum guarded the entrances to the temple in the same way that Shallum's ancestors also guarded Yahweh's Tent of Meeting in the past.

Pause the drama. Ask the person who plays Shallum, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is an honor to be the chief gatekeeper of the temple of Yahweh; or
  • I am glad that I can guard the temple of Yahweh, just as my ancestors guarded the tent of Yahweh during the time of Moses; or
  • I am proud that I am from the same clan of Kohathites as Aaron and the high priests.

Continue the drama.

Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, was the chief gatekeeper in the past. Yahweh's presence was with Phinehas. Zechariah, the son of Meshelemiah, was also a gatekeeper at the entrance of the tent of Yahweh in the past.

Pause the drama. Ask the person who plays Phinehas, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I was the chief gatekeeper when the people of Israel crossed over the Jordan River into the land of Canaan; or
  • I am encouraged that I did my work well because Yahweh was with me; or
  • I hope that future gatekeepers will also work hard to protect the tent of Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

There were 212 gatekeepers of the entrances to the temple. The people wrote down the names of the gatekeepers in the villages of the ancestors of the gatekeepers. In the past, King David and Samuel, the prophet of Yahweh, trusted these men. So, David and Samuel chose these men to do the important work to guard the gates of the temple. These gatekeepers and their sons, or descendants, continued to guard the gates of the house of Yahweh, the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the people who play David and the prophet Samuel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • It was important for us to choose trustworthy men to guard the temple of Yahweh. That is why we chose these trustworthy men to become the gatekeepers; or
  • The gatekeepers have an important responsibility to guard the temple, and I hope these men will do a good job with their important work; or
  • We know that the descendants of these gatekeepers will continue to guard the temple of Yahweh. We hope the future generations of gatekeepers will also do their work well.

Continue the drama.

The gatekeepers guarded all four sides of the temple on the east, west, north, and south. Relatives of the chief gatekeepers came from their villages to relieve the chief gatekeepers at certain times. These relatives guarded the gates for seven days at a time. The chief gatekeepers had a permanent responsibility to guard the gates. The chief gatekeepers also guarded the storerooms and the supply rooms of the temple of Yahweh. The chief gatekeepers stayed near the gates of the temple at night so they could guard the gates of the temple. Every morning, the chief gatekeepers opened the gates of the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the people who play the chief gatekeepers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • This is a big responsibility to guard the temple of Yahweh; or
  • We will not let anyone or anything into the temple of Yahweh that should not be there. We will also faithfully protect the storerooms and supply rooms of the temple of Yahweh; or
  • We are glad that our relatives sometimes come to give us relief from our work as guards. We need a break from this important work as we guard the temple; or
  • We hope that our relatives from the villages will also do a good job when they guard the temple.

Also, ask the people who play the relatives of the chief gatekeepers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are honored that we can also have a turn to guard the temple of Yahweh; or
  • We are glad that we do not need to stay here permanently, and that we can return to our homes in the villages after our seven days of guard duty; or
  • We respect our relatives, the main gatekeepers, for doing this work. This is a big responsibility! or
  • We are ready to protect the temple of Yahweh so that no person or animal enters the temple who should not be there.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:17–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The gatekeepers were Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their brothers. Gatekeepers guarded the gates to the temple of Yahweh. People also called the gates the entrances or the thresholds of the temple. Sometimes people also called the temple of Yahweh the house of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate gatekeepers in this passage? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, "tent," "Tent of Meeting," and "temple" all mean the house of Yahweh. Over the years, the Israelites sometimes exchanged the names of the temple and the Tent of Meeting. Translate temple and tent of Yahweh the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on temple, tabernacle, and house of God, refer to the Master Glossary.

Shallum was the chief, or main gatekeeper, during the time that the storyteller wrote this passage. Translate chief or leader in the same way as you have in previous passages. Shallum guarded the King's Gate on the east side of the temple. The King's Gate was the gate where the king entered the temple. A king is a person who rules over a city or a territory. Translate king the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Shallum and the other gatekeepers were Levites, or from the camp of the Levites. The Levites are descendants of the tribe of Levi, one of the 12 tribes of Israel. Translate sons of Levi the same as you have in previous passages. Sons of Levi means Levites. For more information on "sons of Levi," refer to Levites in the Master Glossary.

Phinehas was another chief gatekeeper. Yahweh was with Phinehas. Yahweh is God's personal name. Translate Yahweh the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Also, translate "house of God" the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people listed the gatekeepers in the genealogies of each of their villages. Genealogies are lists of names of people who belong to the same ancestor. Villages are smaller than cities and do not have walls around them. Translate genealogies and villages the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on genealogy, refer to the Master Glossary.

David and the prophet Samuel put these gatekeepers in their positions of trust. David was the second king of Israel. Samuel was the prophet who anointed David as the next king of Israel after King Saul. Prophets, or seers, receive messages from Yahweh. Translate David and Samuel the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on David and prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate seers in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The gatekeepers and their sons, or descendants, guarded the gates of the house of Yahweh on the east, west, north, and south sides. Remember that the east side of a building is the side from which the sun rises. Translate the directions for east, west, north, and south in the same way as you have previously.

In this passage, sons usually means descendants. Translate "sons" in the same way as you have in previous passages when it means descendants. See descendants in the Master Glossary for more information.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 9:28–34
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 9:28–34 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:28–34 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:28–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller continues the list of the people who return to Jerusalem after their exile to Babylon. The storyteller already listed people from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin, the priests, the Levites, and the gatekeepers who came back. Now, in this passage, the storyteller describes the duties of other Levites in the temple. This is the fourth part in a four-part story about the people who returned to Jerusalem.

Some people think that in this passage, the storyteller continues to talk about the Levites who were gatekeepers in the previous passage. This would mean that gatekeepers from the previous passage have other duties besides their duties as gatekeepers. Other people think that in this passage, the storyteller now describes the duties of other Levites who are not gatekeepers in the house of Yahweh.

These Levites had some important duties in the house of Yahweh. These people took care of the utensils of service, which were probably the valuable silver and gold dishes, cups, and tools that the priests used in the house of Yahweh. When the priests needed these articles, people brought the cups, tools, and dishes out of the treasure room in the temple so that the priests could use these articles in the worship service. These Levites in this passage carefully counted the articles every time people brought the articles out of the treasure room. Then, after the worship service was finished, people brought the articles back to the treasure room. Again, these Levites counted the articles when the people brought the articles back to the room. These Levites counted the articles before and after people used the articles to make sure that none of the valuable articles got lost.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you take care of valuable things in your home or your community? How do you make sure that no one loses these valuable things, or that no one steals these valuable things? Pause this audio here.

People put other Levites in charge of the equipment, or the furniture and other holy utensils for the sanctuary. In the original language, furniture is the same word as the utensils of service that the Levites took care of in the previous part of this passage. Here, furniture probably means other items that the Levites took care of. Holy utensils were things like buckets and shovels, which priests used in the temple. People kept this sacred or holy equipment in the inner rooms of the house of Yahweh.

These same Levites were also in charge of the flour, the wine, the oil, the incense, and the spices. To make flour, people ground the inner kernels of wheat into a powder. Then people used the flour to make grain offerings and other food offerings in the temple. Wine is grape juice that people have fermented. People used wine to make drink offerings. People crushed olives to make oil. People used olive oil to make food offerings. People mixed spices to make incense, which is a sweet-smelling smoke, or sometimes people mixed the spices together with oil to make anointing oil for priests. The spices included myrrh, cinnamon, cane, and cassia. Only priests could mix the spices.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: People used flour, wine, oil, and spices in the temple of Yahweh. What are some common foods that you use to help you worship your gods in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Mattithiah was the Levite who baked the bread for the temple. Mattithiah probably cooked this bread in a pan, and people used this bread to make offerings to Yahweh. Mattithiah was the firstborn son of Shallum the Korahite. This may be the same Shallum we heard about in a previous passage, who was the chief gatekeeper of the temple. It was Mattithiah's permanent responsibility to bake the offering bread. In the original language, this is the same word that described the permanent responsibility of the gatekeepers in a previous passage. Mattithiah was a reliable or faithful worker. People trusted Mattithiah with this role.

Some of the Kohathites also had an important role. The Kohathites were from the clan of Kohath, one of the sons of Levi. Some people think the Kohathites were responsible for preparing the bread and also putting 12 loaves of bread in two rows on the golden table in the Holy Place every Sabbath. In the original language, people call this bread "the bread of the row." We know from the book of Exodus that there were always 12 loaves of bread on the table in the Holy Place. The Holy Place is a part of the temple where only priests can go. The priests were also the only ones who could eat the bread on the table in the Holy Place.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of what the bread on the table might have looked like. Pause this audio here.

It is also possible that the Kohathites only made the bread, and someone else set the bread on the table in the Holy Place. Or maybe the Kohathites only set the bread on the table. Some people think the Kohathites both made the bread and set the bread on the table. The Sabbath is the seventh day of the week, which is Saturday for people today. The Sabbath is a day of rest for the people of Israel. The people of Israel also worshipped Yahweh on the Sabbath every week.

The musicians were heads of fathers' houses, or leaders of their families. These musicians were also all Levites. The musicians lived in rooms in the temple area. The musicians did not have other work to do in the temple because the musicians needed to be ready to perform at any time in a worship service to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a job in your community where a person is always on duty, and someone could call this person to come work at any time. Pause this audio here.

All of the Levite men in the past few passages were heads of fathers' houses, or leaders of their families. These men all had work to do in the temple of Yahweh. People listed these men's names in the records of their ancestors. The storyteller says that these people all lived in Jerusalem, but we know from previous passages that some of these people actually lived in the villages around Jerusalem.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:28–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Some of the Levites had the job to count the tools that people used in the temple. Other Levites were responsible for the furniture, other holy utensils of the sanctuary, flour, wine, oil, incense, and spices. Certain priests mixed the spices.

In the second scene: Mattithiah made the offering bread. Some Kohathites prepared the bread for the holy table every Sabbath.

In the third scene: Some of the Levites were musicians and lived in rooms in the temple area.

In the fourth scene: These men were all leaders of Levite families and lived in Jerusalem.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Levites who were in charge of the articles in the temple
  • Levites who were in charge of the furniture, other articles, flour, wine, oil, and spices
  • Priests who mixed the spices
  • Mattithiah
  • Some Kohathites who put bread on the table
  • Levites who were musicians
  • And all the Levites who were heads of their families

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that this is the fourth part in a four-part story about the people who returned to Jerusalem after their exile to Babylon. In the previous passage, the storyteller described the duties of the temple gatekeepers.

This passage is the final passage in a long section of the book of 1 Chronicles. This section started at the beginning of chapter one, where it described the ancestors of the Israelites from among all the nations from the very beginning. Now, this section ends in a similar way when it describes the Israelites who came back to Jerusalem from among the nations where the Israelites were in exile.

In the first scene, remember it is not clear whether the storyteller is describing additional duties of the Levite gatekeepers of the temple or if the storyteller is now describing the duties of a different group of Levites within the temple. In the original language, the storyteller says "some of them," which could mean some of the Levite gatekeepers or some of the other Levites who were not also gatekeepers.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "some of them" in this passage? If possible, choose to translate "some of them" so that people could understand "them" to refer to either the gatekeepers or to some of the other Levites. Pause this audio here.

The Levites in this passage had important work. First, the storyteller says that the people were in charge of the articles of the temple. Remember that these articles were probably the gold and silver dishes and other things that the priests used in the temple. Next, the storyteller gives more specific information about how these Levites took care of the articles of the temple. Remember that every time people took the articles out, these Levites counted the articles. And every time people put the articles back, these Levites counted the articles again. Remember that the Levites probably counted the articles to make sure no one stole or lost these things.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "articles" in this passage? Other ways to translate this could be utensils, tools, or dishes. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe a common job in your community. First, give an overall description of the job. Then, tell more specific details about the job. How will you translate this part of the first scene that tells the general work of these Levites to take care of the utensils of service, and then the specific details about how the Levites did this work? Pause this audio here.

In the next part of the first scene, the storyteller describes the role of other Levites who took care of the furniture, other holy utensils in the sanctuary, flour, wine, oil, incense, and spices. In the original language, the storyteller does not say who put these Levites in charge of these things. In your language, it may be better to translate this as, "Someone assigned these Levites" or "Someone gave these Levites the duty to take care of these things."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the passage about the Levites who were in charge of the furniture, other holy utensils in the sanctuary, flour, wine, oil, incense, and spices? Pause this audio here.

So far in this passage, Levites are in charge of all of the articles, tools, and food in the temple. But in the final part of the first scene, we see that only priests could mix the spices. Remember that not all Levites are priests. Levites are all descendants of Levi, but priests are a special group of Levites who are the sons, or descendants, of Aaron, who was also a descendant of Levi. In the original language, the storyteller says that "the sons of the priests" can mix the spices. "Sons of the priests" is another way to say "priests."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about two different kinds of jobs in your community, where one job is a job that many people could do, and another job is a job that only certain, special people can do. Pay attention to how you show a difference or contrast between the two jobs. How will you show the difference between the work of the Levites and the work of the priests who mix the spices? Pause this audio here.

The second scene is about the special offering bread in the temple. First, we learn the name of the man who made this bread. We also learn this man's tribe, his father's name, his clan, and his birth order. The man's name was Mattithiah, and he was a Levite. Mattithiah was the firstborn son of Shallum, of the Korahite clan. Then, we learn about Mattithiah's job. Mattithiah had the permanent job to bake the offering bread. Mattithiah was trustworthy and reliable, and that is why Mattithiah had this important job to bake the bread for the offerings. The original language does not say offerings, but we know that the priests used this bread for offerings to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe an important person in your community. Include information about the person's father, birth order, tribe, job, and anything else that is important to know. What is the best way to introduce Mattithiah in this scene? For example, would it be best to say Mattithiah's name, his father's name, his birth order, clan name, tribe name, or his important job first? What should come next? Also, how can you show that Mattithiah's job was his permanent responsibility? Pause this audio here.

Some of the Kohathites had the responsibility for the bread that people placed on the table every Sabbath and that stayed there throughout the week. Remember that Kohathites are from the clan of Kohath, one of the sons of Levi. Remember that we do not know if these Kohathites only baked this bread, or if they only set it out on the table, or if the Kohathites both baked the bread and set it out on the table. Remember, the bread is sometimes called "showbread" because people set this bread out in two rows on the table. Some other ways to translate this bread are the "bread of the Presence," "showbread," or "bread that people dedicate to Yahweh."

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of what the bread on the table might have looked like. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the bread that the Kohathites prepared every Sabbath? How will you show that the Kohathites were responsible for this special bread? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller describes the musicians in the temple. Some people think there should be a list of names here because it says "now these are" in the original language. However, there is no list of names here.

The musicians were all heads of fathers' houses. This means the musicians were all leaders of their families. The musicians were also all Levites. The musicians lived in rooms in the temple area because the musicians were on duty all the time. These rooms were probably along the outer walls of the temple. The musicians needed to be ready to play music at any time of the day or night.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about your discussion from the previous step about jobs where people are on duty for a certain period of time. What is the best way to translate the idea in this passage that the musicians needed to be available for their work at any time of the day or night? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller gives a summary of all of the men in the last few passages. All of these men were heads of fathers' houses, or leaders of their families. People listed these men's names in their family records. The storyteller says that all these people lived in Jerusalem, but we know that some of these people actually lived in the villages outside of Jerusalem.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:28–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Levites who were in charge of the articles in the temple
  • Levites who were in charge of the furniture, other articles, flour, wine, oil, and spices
  • Priests who mixed the spices
  • Mattithiah
  • Some Kohathites who put bread on the table
  • Levites who were musicians
  • And all the Levites who were heads of their families

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Some of the Levites took care of the utensils and articles that people used for worship in the temple. These Levites counted the articles when people took the articles out, and the Levites counted the articles again when people brought the articles back in.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Levites who were in charge of the articles of the temple, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are proud to have this important job to take care of the articles of the temple; or
  • We want to be very careful to count all of the articles correctly because we do not want anything to get lost in the temple of Yahweh; or
  • We always feel relieved when we finish counting the articles people bring back from the temple and see that nothing is missing.

Continue the drama.

Other people took care of the equipment and other articles in the temple. This included flour, wine, olive oil, incense, and spices. Some priests mixed the spices.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Levites who were in charge of the furniture, other articles, flour, wine, oil, and spices, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Our role is important because we prepare the equipment and food that people use to worship Yahweh; or
  • We want to do our work very carefully so that everything is prepared correctly for the worship service to Yahweh; or
  • We are glad that we are not responsible for mixing the spices. It is difficult to mix the spices correctly, and it is better that the priests mix the spices.

Continue the drama.

Mattithiah was a Levite. Mattithiah was the first son of Shallum, who was from the Korahite clan. Mattithiah was in charge of making the bread for the offerings in the temple. Some men from the clan of the Kohathites were in charge of preparing the bread that someone set out on the table in the Holy Place every Sabbath.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Mattithiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel that my job to prepare the bread for the offerings is very important; or
  • I do my job to prepare the bread as worship for Yahweh; or
  • I am proud that I am from the clan of the Korahites and the tribe of Levites.

Also, ask the people playing the Kohathites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Our job to prepare the bread of the Presence is a way for us to serve Yahweh; or
  • Every time we go into the Holy Place to arrange the bread of the Presence, we feel that we are in Yahweh's presence; or
  • We are thankful for this opportunity to serve Yahweh by preparing the bread every Sabbath to put on the table in the Holy Place.

Continue the drama.

The musicians were family leaders in the tribe of the Levites. The musicians stayed in rooms in the temple, and the musicians were ready to play music day and night. Because the musicians were always on duty, the musicians did not have other work to do in the temple besides their work as musicians.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Levites who were musicians, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are honored to serve as musicians in the temple of Yahweh; or
  • We are always ready to play music to help in the worship service to Yahweh; or
  • We are relieved that we do not have other work to do in the temple of Yahweh except to play music for the worship of Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

These were all of the family leaders of the Levites. People listed all of these Levites in their family records. These men lived in Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Levites who were heads of their families, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are proud to serve Yahweh in the temple; or
  • We hope that we will live a long time and continue to serve Yahweh for all our lives; or
  • We want our families to continue to serve Yahweh as we do now.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:28–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Some of the Levites were in charge of the articles of service in the temple. Other Levites were in charge of other articles of service, and over other special articles. You can translate the name of these articles as "holy articles," or "articles of the sanctuary," or holy place**.** Something that is holy is something that people set apart as special for Yahweh. The holy articles are the special tools and vessels that people used in the holy parts of the tabernacle. Sometimes people call the whole tabernacle the "sanctuary" or holy place, and sometimes people call the special rooms in the tabernacle where only the priests could go "the sanctuary." The tabernacle was a tent that people could move. The tabernacle was where the Israelites worshiped Yahweh. When the Israelites built the temple, it also had a holy place where only priests could enter.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate these special articles? Will you call them holy articles, or articles of the sanctuary? Refer to the Master Glossary for more information on sanctuary and holy. Pause this audio here.

The Levites in charge of the other articles of service were also in charge of the flour, wine, olive oil, incense, and spices. People used the flour to make offering bread. Wine was fermented grape juice. Oil is often a symbol of the Holy Spirit. People burned incense in the temple to make a good smell for Yahweh. Spices are a symbol of the prayers that the Israelites offered to Yahweh. Translate flour, wine, olive oil, incense, and spices the same as you have in other parts of the Old Testament. For more information on wine, refer to the Master Glossary.

Sons of the priests were responsible to mix the spices for the temple. In this passage, sons probably means the descendants of the priests who also served as priests. Priests served Yahweh in the temple, and priests were descendants of Aaron. Translate sons and priest the same as you have in previous passages. For information on priests, refer to the Master Glossary. For more information on sons when it refers to all descendants, refer to descendants in the Master Glossary.

Mattithiah was the person who made the offering bread for the temple. Mattithiah was a Levite. Levites were people from the tribe of Levi who worked in the temple but were not priests. Translate Levites in the same way as you have in previous passages. For more information on Levites, refer to the Master Glossary.

Mattithiah made the bread for the grain offerings. Even though the word "offering" is not in the original language, it may be helpful to refer to offering and grain offering in the Master Glossary to remember how and why the Israelites made offerings to Yahweh.

Some of the Kohathites prepared the bread that the people put on the table in the Holy Place in the temple every Sabbath. People call this special bread the bread of the Presence, because it is in the holy place of the tabernacle where Yahweh's presence lives. The Sabbath is the seventh day of the week for the Israelites, and it is a day of rest. Translate Sabbath the same as you have in other parts of the Old Testament. For information on Sabbath and bread of the Presence, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate Sabbath and bread of the Presence in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The musicians stayed in rooms at the temple. Use the same word for temple as you have used previously, and refer to temple in the Master Glossary for more information.

All of these people were the leaders of their fathers' families or households. A household includes all the people who live in a house. But people could also use the word household, or house, to mean a large group of people who did not live in the same house, but who were part of a larger group. So, we know that these men were leaders of the groups of their families. Other ways to translate this could be clan leaders, family leaders, heads of families, or leaders of the clans. Translate leaders of families in the same way as you have in previous passages. For more information on household, refer to the Master Glossary.

These men were leaders according to their generations. This means these leaders are in the list of names that belong to the same ancestor. People call this list a genealogy, so you may say that these men were leaders in their genealogy. A generation is a group of people who live at the same time and who belong to the same age group. Translate generation, or genealogy, in the same way as you have in previous passages. For more information about listing generations of people, refer to genealogy and generation in the Master Glossary.

All of these men lived in Jerusalem. Jerusalem is the most important city in Israel. Use the same word for Jerusalem as you have in previous passages. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 9:35–44
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 9:35–44 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:35–44 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:35–44 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller finished a four-part story that listed the people who returned to Jerusalem after their exile to Babylon. Now, in this passage, the storyteller repeats the genealogy of Saul, the first king of Israel. The storyteller repeats Saul's family here because the following story is about Saul's death.

In this passage, the storyteller lists the genealogy, or ancestors and descendants, of Saul. This passage is very similar to another passage earlier in 1 Chronicles, where the storyteller also listed the family of Saul. Here, some of the names seem different when people see them written, but they are the same names.

This passage begins with Jeiel, the father of Gibeon. Gibeon is the name of a city, and not the name of a person. "Father of Gibeon" probably means that Jeiel started the city of Gibeon, or that Jeiel was the leader of Gibeon. Remember that Gibeon was an important city in the land of the tribe of Benjamin.

Stop here and look at a map that includes the city of Gibeon as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk again about someone in your community who started a town, village, or city. Describe how this person started the town, village, or city. Pause this audio here.

Jeiel lives in the city of Gibeon, and Jeiel's wife's name is Maacah. Jeiel has 10 sons. Remember that in the previous passage in 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, the storyteller listed only eight of Jeiel's sons. But now we hear the names of all 10 of Jeiel's sons. These names include Ner and Mikloth, who are not in the previous passage. Jeiel's firstborn or eldest son is Abdon. Jeiel's other sons are Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth. The storyteller lists these sons in the order in which the sons were born. In the previous passage of 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, the storyteller called Zechariah "Zecher." Mikloth is the father of Shimeam, whom the storyteller called "Shimeah" in the previous passage about Saul's family.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember the conversation from the previous passage about Saul's family and how people on your translation team might say the names of your team members in different ways . Talk about other names that you know that people might say in different ways. Pause this audio here.

In this next part of the passage, the storyteller gives the same information we heard in the previous passage about Saul's family. However, this part of the passage is difficult to understand because we are not sure which people the storyteller is saying lived near their relatives in Jerusalem, or who those relatives were. Some people think that these words in the passage mean that some of the people of the tribe of Benjamin moved from Gibeon to Jerusalem. Other people think that this passage says that Jeiel and his wife lived in Gibeon, and all of Jeiel's sons lived in Jerusalem. Finally, other people think that maybe only Mikloth and Shimeam lived in Jerusalem. If it is Mikloth and Shimeam who live in Jerusalem, that means that, unlike their brothers, Mikloth and Shimeam lived in Jerusalem with other people from the tribe of Benjamin, and not with their own brothers. Remember that the other sons of Jeiel probably lived in the city of Gibeon.

Stop here and look at a map that includes Jerusalem and Gibeon as a group. Pause this audio here.

Most of the rest of this passage is about the descendants of Ner, one of the 10 sons of Jeiel. The storyteller does not talk about any descendants from the other sons of Jeiel, besides Mikloth's son, Shimeam.

Ner is the father of Kish, and Kish is the father of Saul, the first king of Israel. It is surprising that the storyteller says Ner is the father of Kish, because another passage in the Old Testament says that Abiel is the father of Kish.

Saul has four sons: Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal. In the following passage about Saul's death, the storyteller only lists three of Saul's sons: Jonathan, Malki-Shua, and Esh-Baal. This passage is difficult to understand because we learn here and in the previous passage that Saul has four sons and many descendants, but in the passage that follows this one, the storyteller says that all of Saul's family died with Saul. We are not sure why the storyteller says that all of Saul's family died with Saul, because we see here that Saul had many descendants.

In other parts of the Old Testament, sometimes Saul's son Esh-Baal has the name Ish-Bosheth, and sometimes Jonathan's son Merib-Baal has the name Mephibosheth. It is possible that people in Israel changed these names because it was shameful to have a son with the same name as the Canaanite god, Baal. The new names end with Bosheth, which means "shame" in the original language.

Jonathan's son Merib-Baal is the father of Micah. Micah's sons are Pithon, Melech, and Tahrea. In the previous passage in 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, the storyteller called Tahrea "Tarea," and here the storyteller says that Micah has a fourth son named Ahaz. In this current passage, the storyteller does not include the name Ahaz in the list of Micah's sons, but it is likely that Micah did have four sons because the next name in the list is Ahaz. Ahaz is the father of Jadah. The storyteller calls Jadah "Jehoaddah" in the earlier passage in 1 Chronicles. Jehoaddah and Jadah are the same person.

Jadah has three sons whose names are Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri is the father of Moza, and Moza is the father of Binea. Most people think that the correct translation of the next part of this passage is that Binea is the father of Rephaiah, Rephaiah is the father of Eleasah, and Eleasah is the father of Azel. But some people think that Binea, Rephaiah, Eleasah, and Azel are all sons of Moza because it says, "Rephaiah his son," and this could mean Moza is the father of Rephaiah. In the previous passage of 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, the storyteller calls Rephaiah "Raphah."

Azel has six sons whose names are Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. In the original language, "Bokeru" is very similar to the word for "firstborn", so some people think this means that Azrikam is Azel's firstborn son. If a translation says "Azrikam the firstborn," then there is one son's name missing from the six sons of Azel in this passage. The storyteller repeats the information again that "these were the sons of Azel."

In the earlier passage in 1 Chronicles, the storyteller also includes the names of Eshek's sons and grandsons. In this passage, we do not hear any information about Eshek or Eshek's family.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:35–44 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: Jeiel has 10 sons. One of Jeiel's sons is Ner. These people all live near their relatives in Jerusalem.

In the second scene: Saul's grandfather is Ner, and Saul's father is Kish. Saul has four sons.

In the third scene: Saul's son Jonathan has a son named Merib-Baal. Merib-Baal's son is Micah, and Micah has four sons. One of Micah's sons is Ahaz.

In the fourth scene: Ahaz's son is Jadah. Jadah has three sons, including one son named Zimri. Zimri is the father of Moza, Moza is the father of Binea, Binea is the father of Rephaiah, Rephaiah is the father of Eleasah, and Eleasah is the father of Azel.

In the fifth scene: Azel has six sons.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeiel
  • Jeiel's 10 sons, Abdon, Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth
  • Mikloth's son Shimeam
  • Kish, the son of Ner
  • Saul, the son of Kish
  • Saul's four sons, Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal
  • Merib-Baal, the son of Jonathan
  • Micah, the son of Merib-Baal
  • Micah's four sons, Pithon, Melech, Tahrea, and Ahaz
  • Jadah, the son of Ahaz
  • Jadah's three sons, Alemeth, Azmaveth and Zimri
  • Moza, the son of Zimri
  • Binea, the son of Moza
  • Rephaiah, the son of Binea
  • Eleasah, the son of Rephaiah
  • Azel, the son of Eleasah
  • And Azel's six sons, Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that in the previous four passages, the storyteller told about the exiles who returned to Jerusalem. Before those four passages, the storyteller listed the ancestors and descendants of Saul. Now, the storyteller repeats the list of the ancestors and descendants of Saul. The storyteller wants listeners to remember the family of Saul, the first king of Israel, before the storyteller talks about Saul's death in the next passage.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story that includes important information at the beginning about one of the main characters of the story. Then, include a few other important stories or ideas. Then, return to the first main character. How do you reintroduce the main character and that person's part in the story? Pause this audio here.

This passage is very similar to the previous passage in 1 Chronicles about Saul's family. To help you remember the way you translated parts of the previous passage about Saul's family, listen to the previous passage again.

Stop here and listen to your translation of 1 Chronicles 8:29–40 in your own language. Listen to this previous passage about Saul's family again while you work on this step to help you remember how you translated certain parts of the passage. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, Jeiel is the father of Gibeon, and Jeiel lives in the city of Gibeon. Remember that Gibeon is a city, and not a person. Also, remember that "father of Gibeon" probably means that Jeiel started the city of Gibeon. Be sure to translate "father of Gibeon" the same way as you did in the previous passage of 1 Chronicles about Saul's family. Gibeon is a city about 10 kilometers northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of the city of Gibeon again as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Jeiel also has a wife. Jeiel's wife's name is Maacah. It is likely that Maacah also lives in the city of Gibeon with her husband, Jeiel. Your team may choose to translate this part of the passage to say that Jeiel and his wife lived in Gibeon. Make sure to check how you translated this part of the passage in the previous passage about Saul's family, and translate it the same way here.

Remember that Jeiel has 10 sons. Remember that here, the storyteller lists both Ner and Mikloth as sons of Jeiel. Also, remember that in the previous passage, the storyteller calls Zechariah "Zecher" and Shimeam "Shimeah."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Decide if you want to translate the names Zechariah and Shimeam the same way as you did in the previous passage in 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, or if you want to translate the names as Zechariah and Shimeam here. How will you show that Zechariah is the same person as Zecher and Shimeam is the same person as Shimeah? Pause this audio here.

In the final part of this scene, we learn that "they lived near their relatives in Jerusalem." Remember that it is hard to know who lived near their relatives in Jerusalem, and who the relatives were. In the original language, the storyteller says they lived both "with their relatives" and "opposite their relatives." Many people think "with their relatives" and "opposite their relatives" are two ways to describe the same idea. The word "opposite" can also mean "unlike." Here are some translations you can choose for this part of this passage:

  • Some of the people of the tribe of Benjamin moved from Gibeon to Jerusalem. These people who moved to Jerusalem lived near, or alongside, their relatives; or
  • Jeiel and his family lived in Gibeon, and all of Jeiel's sons lived in Jerusalem near their other relatives; or
  • Unlike their brothers, Mikloth and his son Shimeam lived in Jerusalem. The rest of the sons of Jeiel lived in Gibeon. Mikloth and Shimeam lived alongside other people from the tribe of Benjamin.

Pay attention to how you translated this part of the passage in the previous passage in 1 Chronicles about Saul's family. Be sure to translate it in the same way in this current passage.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem again as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Ner is the father of Kish. Kish is the father of Saul, the first king of Israel. This part about Saul and his ancestors and descendants is probably the most important part of this passage. Remember that Ner is one of Jeiel's sons.

Saul has four sons. Saul's sons are Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal. Remember that in the passage after this current passage, the storyteller only lists three of Saul's sons. Also, Esh-Baal is the same person as Ish-Bosheth from another part of the Old Testament. In the original language, "bosheth" means shame, and people who wrote 1 Chronicles probably changed the name from Baal to Bosheth because Baal was a Canaanite god. Decide if you want to translate the name Esh-Baal the same way as you did in the previous passage about Saul's family, or if you want to translate Esh-Baal as Ish-Bosheth.

In the third scene, Saul's son Jonathan has a son whose name is Merib-Baal. Merib-Baal is the same person as Mephibosheth. Other parts of the Old Testament call Jonathan's son

"Mephibosheth." Again, decide as a group how you want to translate the name Merib-Baal in this passage.

Merib-Baal has a son named Micah. Micah has three sons whose names are Pithon, Melech, and Tahrea. In the earlier passage of 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, the storyteller calls Tahrea "Tarea." Decide how you want to translate Tahrea in this passage.

In the previous passage about Saul's family, the storyteller says that Micah has a fourth son whose name is Ahaz. Remember that the name Ahaz is not in the original language, but we know from the previous passage about Saul's family that Micah also had a son named Ahaz. It is probably best to include the name Ahaz in this current passage about the family of Saul.

In the fourth scene, we learn about the descendants of Ahaz. Ahaz is the father of Jadah. Remember that the storyteller calls Jadah "Jehoaddah" in the previous passage about Saul's family. Decide how you want to translate Jadah in this passage.

Jadah has three sons whose names are Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri has a son whose name is Moza. Moza has a son named Binea. Binea is the father of Rephaiah. Rephaiah is the father of Eleasah. And Eleasah is the father of Azel. Remember that in the earlier passage of 1 Chronicles about Saul's family, the storyteller calls Rephaiah "Raphah." Your team should decide how you want to translate Rephaiah's name in this passage.

In the fifth scene, Azel has six sons named Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. Remember that the word "Bokeru" in the original language normally means "firstborn" instead of the name of a person. Be sure to translate the names of Azel's sons the same as you did in the previous passage of 1 Chronicles about Saul's family.

The storyteller repeats again that all of these men were the sons of Azel.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:35–44 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeiel
  • Jeiel's 10 sons, Abdon, Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth
  • Mikloth's son Shimeam
  • Kish, the son of Ner
  • Saul, the son of Kish
  • Saul's four sons, Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal
  • Merib-Baal, the son of Jonathan
  • Micah, the son of Merib-Baal
  • Micah's four sons, Pithon, Melech, Tahrea, and Ahaz
  • Jadah, the son of Ahaz
  • Jadah's three sons, Alemeth, Azmaveth and Zimri
  • Moza, the son of Zimri
  • Binea, the son of Moza
  • Rephaiah, the son of Binea
  • Eleasah, the son of Rephaiah
  • Azel, the son of Eleasah
  • And Azel's six sons, Azrikam, Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Jeiel started the city of Gibeon. Jeiel lived in Gibeon, and Jeiel's wife's name was Maacah. Jeiel's firstborn son was Abdon. Jeiel's other sons were Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth. Mikloth was the father of Shimeam. Unlike their brothers, Mikloth and Shimeam lived in Jerusalem near their other relatives.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeiel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that I have so many sons. Yahweh blessed me with these children; or
  • I worked hard to start the city of Gibeon, and I am proud of the city that I built; or
  • I am glad that Gibeon is such an important city in Israel.

Continue the drama.

Ner was the father of Kish. Kish was the father of King Saul. Saul was the father of Jonathan, Malki-Shua, Abinadab, and Esh-Baal. Jonathan was the father of Merib-Baal, and Merib-Baal was the father of Micah. Micah was the father of Pithon, Melech, Tahrea, and Ahaz.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Saul, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud to be the first king of Israel; or
  • I named my youngest son Esh-Baal because my wife likes the Canaanite god, Baal; or
  • I am proud of my oldest son, Jonathan.

Also, ask the people playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We wonder why the storyteller wants to tell us about Saul again. We already heard all of Saul's ancestors and descendants in an earlier story; or
