  • We wonder why King Saul named his youngest son Esh-Baal, and why Jonathan named his son Merib-Baal. Those do not seem like good names for the son and grandson of the king of Israel because Baal is a false god; or
  • We know that Saul did not have a good end to his life. We wonder if the storyteller will describe Saul's death next.

Continue the drama.

Ahaz was the father of Jadah, and Jadah was the father of Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri has a son named Moza. Moza has a son named Binea. Binea is the father of Rephaiah, Rephaiah is the father of Eleasah, and Eleasah is the father of Azel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ahaz, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I am a descendant of Benjamin; or
  • I am also glad that I am a descendant of Saul, the first king of Israel; or
  • I am proud that I have so many descendants.

Continue the drama.

Azel had six sons. Azrikam was Azel's firstborn son. Azel's other sons were Bokeru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. These were all of Azel's sons.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Azel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I have six sons; or
  • I am proud that I am a descendant of Saul, the first king of Israel; or
  • I am happy that I am a descendant of Benjamin in the tribe of Benjamin.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 9:35–44 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Jeiel founded, or started, the city of Gibeon. Gibeon was one of the cities in the land of the tribe of Benjamin where Levites could also live.

One of Jeiel's 10 sons was Mikloth. Mikloth and his son, Shimeam, lived near their other relatives, or family members, in Jerusalem. "Relatives" is a general term for any type of family member, even people who are distant family members. Jerusalem is the most important city in Israel. Use the same words for relatives and for Jerusalem as you used in previous passages. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

In this passage, we learn the names of the ancestors and descendants of Saul. Sometimes, "sons" means descendants, but here it means the son of a father. Use the same word for son as you have in previous passages. For more information on sons, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 10:1–14
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 10:1–14 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 10:1–14 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 10:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, we heard King Saul's family history. The storyteller started with Saul's great-grandfather Jeiel and ended with his descendant Moza, who came from the family line of Saul's son Jonathan. Saul's family history connects Israel's past with Saul and the time when David began to rule over Israel.

Now, in this story, the storyteller begins a new section of 1 Chronicles. The storyteller describes the last battle between King Saul and the Philistines to show how Saul's rule over Israel ended. The storyteller bases the story on the battle that the storyteller in 1 Samuel 31 describes, but some details are different.

Stop here. If you have already translated 1 Samuel 31, listen to the recording now as a team. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines fight against the Israelites on Mount Gilboa in the Valley of Jezreel. The Philistines kill many Israelite soldiers, and the Israelite army starts to lose the battle. The Israelites must retreat. The Israelites, including King Saul and his sons, run away quickly to save their lives. But the Philistines catch up to Saul and his sons and kill three of Saul's sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malki-Shua. We know that Saul has another son, Ish-Bosheth, who survives, but we do not know if Ish-Bosheth was in the battle. The word "kill" here means to use a weapon, like a sword or spear, to strike someone and cause that person to die.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and locate Mount Gilboa, where the Israelites and Philistines fought this battle. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines fight fiercely against Saul. After they kill Saul's sons, the Philistines get close to where Saul is. The Philistines attack hard and fight the men who are still with Saul, including Saul's bodyguard. Then the arrows that the enemies' archers shoot from their bows hit Saul. The arrows wound Saul very badly. Saul's wounds are so bad that he is about to die, and Saul has no chance to escape.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of the bow and arrows. Pause this audio here.

Then Saul asks his armor-bearer to pull out his sword and kill Saul by stabbing him. An armor-bearer is a young man who helps an older soldier or leader by carrying extra weapons and equipment. Saul wants his armor-bearer to kill Saul because Saul is afraid the Philistines will kill Saul slowly and painfully. Saul thinks it would be more shameful and painful if the Philistines killed Saul. Saul also worries that the Philistines will mock Saul and hurt him for their own entertainment. Saul calls the Philistines "uncircumcised fellows," a negative term the Israelites used. The Israelites circumcised all Israelite male children as a sign of their special agreement with God, and the Israelites looked down on uncircumcised men. The armor-bearer refuses to kill Saul because he is afraid to harm the king of Israel, whom God appointed. Since Saul's armor-bearer will not kill Saul, Saul pulls out his own sword and kills himself by falling on the sword's point.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about times in your people's history when a leader ended their own life because an enemy hurt them or because their own army was losing a fight. Pause this audio here.

When Saul's armour-bearer sees that Saul is dead—either because he sees Saul kill himself or because he sees Saul's dead body—the armor-bearer throws himself on the point of his own sword and kills himself. The armour-bearer dies with Saul. This means the armor-bearer dies at about the same time, in the same place, and in the same way as Saul.

In that way, Saul and three of Saul's sons die. The men die on the same day along with "all of Saul's house." The phrase "all of Saul's house" means Saul's extended family and anyone who could become king after Saul. Saul and these people all die in the same battle on the same day.

The storyteller pauses the story about the battle between Israel and the Philistines to explain what happens after the Philistines win. When the Israelite people who live in the Jezreel Valley realise that the Israelite army ran away and that Saul and three of Saul's sons have died, the people run away from their cities quickly to live somewhere else. These people do not destroy their homes or buildings. The people just abandon the buildings. Then the Philistine soldiers and their families move into the empty cities, live there, and take control of the area.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and locate the area from where the Israelites ran away and where the Philistines took over. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller now goes back to the story of the battle. The day after the fight, the Philistine soldiers go back to Mount Gilboa to take valuable things from the dead Israelite soldiers. The Philistines "strip the slain." This means the Philistines take weapons and armour from the Israelite soldiers whom they killed with swords. While they do this, the Philistines find Saul and his three sons lying dead.

The Philistine soldiers take off Saul's armour and cut Saul's head off. Saul's "armour" also refers to Saul's weapons. So the Philistines remove Saul's military clothes and the weapons Saul had carried, like his sword, shield, and spear. After this, the Philistines send men all over the land of Philistia to announce the good news that Saul is dead and that the Philistines won the battle. These men possibly carry Saul's weapons and armour as proof of victory. The Philistine messengers announce the good news of the victory to their idols and their people. An idol is an object that people make to represent the god they worship. The Philistine men also tell the news to the Philistine people, possibly in public places like markets or smaller worship areas. People spread this news quickly from person to person and from town to town throughout the land of Philistia.

Stop here and look as a group at pictures of Saul's armour and weapons. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group how people in your community used to spread important news, whether good or bad, that affected the whole nation. Then, discuss how people spread important news now. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines put Saul's armour and weapons in a temple of one of their gods. A temple was a building where people showed statues or pictures of their god and prayed to those images. The Philistines hang Saul's head in the temple of Dagon, another one of their false gods. The Philistines probably stick Saul's head on a pole or attach it to a wall inside Dagon's temple. The Philistines want to honour Dagon as the god who helped them win against Saul and Israel. This is the Philistines' way of saying that their god is stronger than the God of Israel. When the Philistines show Saul's armour, weapons and head in their gods' temples, the Philistines embarrass Israel and insult the God of Israel. When the Philistines do not bury Saul's body, the Philistines shame Saul and the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What do people in your people group do to show gratitude to false gods after a war victory? Pause this audio here.

When the people in Jabesh Gilead hear that the Philistines insulted Saul's dead body, the brave warriors from Jabesh Gilead decide to act. The warriors leave their city and go to get the bodies of Saul and Saul's sons. Jabesh is in Gilead, on the east side of the Jordan River, about 34–40 kilometers from Mount Gilboa. The story does not say exactly where the Philistine temples are, but they are probably near Mount Gilboa, where people fought the battle. The people of Jabesh, who are Israelites, feel grateful to Saul because Saul saved them from the Ammonites in the past.

The brave warriors bring back Saul's dead body and the bodies of Saul's sons to Jabesh. The warriors bury the bones of Saul and Saul's three sons under an oak tree that many people probably knew well in Jabesh. The warriors do this to honour Saul and his sons. Then the people of Jabesh fast, or stop eating, for seven days to show their sadness and to honour Saul after his death.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and locate the city of Jabesh in Gilead. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of an oak tree. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are the burial customs in your people group? What do people do to honour a dead person? Talk about the burial of a special person in your people group who people honoured after their death. What did the person do to earn the honour? How did people honour that person? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller explains what happened in the story. Saul died because he was not faithful or obedient to Yahweh and did not follow Yahweh's commands. The storyteller might mean that Saul did not usually follow Yahweh's commands, or the storyteller might mean that Saul did not obey Yahweh when the prophet Samuel told Saul something Yahweh said to do. One time, Saul even asked a woman who talked to spirits of dead people for advice, even though Yahweh told the Israelites not to do that. Since Saul did not ask Yahweh for help, Yahweh punished Saul. Yahweh killed Saul, took the kingdom away from Saul, and gave the kingdom to David, the son of Jesse.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 10:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has eight scenes.

In the first scene: The Philistines fight the Israelites on Mount Gilboa and kill many Israelite soldiers. The Israelites, including King Saul and three of Saul's sons, run away quickly. The Philistines catch up to them and kill Saul's sons.

In the second scene: The Philistines fight fiercely against Saul and get close to where Saul is. Then the archers' arrows hit Saul and wound him very badly. Saul asks his armor-bearer to kill Saul, but the armor-bearer refuses because he is scared. Saul kills himself by falling on the point of his sword. When Saul's armor-bearer sees this, the armor-bearer throws himself on the point of his own sword and kills himself.

In the third scene: The storyteller summarises what happened that day. Saul and three of Saul's sons die. They die on the same day along with all of Saul's extended family.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller pauses the story about the battle between Israel and the Philistines to tell what happens after the Philistines won. The Israelite people who live in the Jezreel Valley realise that the Israelite army fled or ran away and that Saul and three of Saul's sons died. The Israelites flee or run away from their cities to live somewhere else. Then the Philistine soldiers and their families come into the empty cities and live there.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller returns to the story of the battle. The day after the fight, the Philistine soldiers go back to Mount Gilboa to take valuable things like weapons and armour from the dead Israelite soldiers. The Philistines find Saul and his three sons lying dead, and the Philistines remove Saul's military clothes and the weapons he carried and cut off his head. The Philistines send men all over the land of Philistia to announce the good news that Saul is dead and that the Philistines won the battle with their gods and their people.

In the sixth scene: The Philistines put Saul's armour and weapons in a temple of one of their gods. The Philistines fasten Saul's head in the temple of Dagon, another one of their false gods.

In the seventh scene: When the people in the city of Jabesh Gilead hear the news about how the Philistines insulted Saul's dead body, all the brave warriors from Jabesh Gilead leave their city and go to get the bodies of Saul and his sons. The warriors take Saul's dead body and the bodies of his sons and carry them back to Jabesh. Afterwards, the warriors bury the bones of Saul and his three sons under a large tree. The people of Jabesh fast, or stop eating, for seven days.

In the eighth scene: Now the storyteller explains what happened in the story. Saul died because he was not faithful or obedient to Yahweh and did not follow Yahweh's commands. Since Saul did not ask Yahweh for help, but asked for help from a woman who speaks to spirits of dead people, Yahweh punished Saul by killing him. Yahweh took the kingdom away from Saul and gave it to David, the son of Jesse.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Philistine army
  • Israelite army
  • King Saul
  • Three of Saul's sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malki-Shua
  • Philistine archers
  • Saul's armor-bearer
  • Saul's extended family, who died with Saul
  • Israelites, or Israelite people who lived on the other side of the Jezreel Valley and beyond the Jordan River, who ran away from their homes
  • Philistines, or Philistine people
  • Israelite people who lived in the city of Jabesh in the region of Gilead
  • And brave Israelite warriors from the city of Jabesh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller now uses language that introduces a new event. The Philistines fight the Israelites on Mount Gilboa in the Valley of Jezreel. The Philistines kill many Israelite soldiers. The Israelites, including King Saul and his sons, run away quickly to save their lives. But the Philistines catch up to Saul and his sons and kill three of Saul's sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malki-Shua. Here, the storyteller repeats the word "Philistines" several times to stress that it was the Philistines who killed the Israelites.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and locate Mount Gilboa, where the Israelites and Philistines fought this battle. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are the natural ways in your language to stress that it was the Philistines who killed the Israelites? Pause this audio here.

The Philistines fight fiercely against Saul. The Philistines get close to where Saul is. The Philistines attack hard and fight the men who are still with Saul, including Saul's armor-bearer. Then the archers shoot their arrows, and they hit Saul. The arrows wound Saul very badly. Saul is in severe pain from the arrows, and Saul cannot move easily.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of the bow and arrows. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and talk as a group about how you will say that the enemy archers shoot their arrows and that the arrows hit Saul. Pause this audio here.

Then Saul asks his armor-bearer to pull out his sword and kill Saul by stabbing him. Saul wants his armor-bearer to kill Saul because Saul is afraid the Philistines will kill Saul slowly and painfully. Saul also worries that the Philistines will mock Saul and hurt him for their own entertainment. Saul calls the Philistines "uncircumcised fellows," a negative term the Israelites used. The armor-bearer is very scared and refuses to kill Saul. Since Saul's armor-bearer will not kill him, Saul pulls out his own sword and kills himself by falling on the sword's point.

Stop here and talk as a group about negative words or nicknames that people in your community use to describe people from enemy nations or people groups. Pause this audio here.

When Saul's armour-bearer sees that Saul is dead, the armor-bearer throws himself on the point of his own sword and kills himself. This means that the armour-bearer pushes himself onto the point of his own sword to make it go into his body and end his life. The armour-bearer dies at about the same time, in the same place, and in the same way as Saul.

Stop here and, as a group, discuss how you will describe how Saul's armour-bearer throws himself or pushes himself onto the point of his own sword. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller summarises what happened that day. Saul, three of Saul's sons, and all of Saul's extended family die in battle on the same day.

The storyteller pauses the story about the battle between Israel and the Philistines to tell what happens after the Philistines win. When the Israelite people who live in the Jezreel Valley realise that the Israelite army ran away and that Saul and three of Saul's sons have died, the people run away from their cities quickly to live somewhere else. The storyteller says that the people "run away" twice in this story to show that the Israelite army runs away quickly, and the Israelite people also run away in a similar way. Then the Philistine soldiers and their families come into the empty cities, live there, and take control of the area.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and locate the area from where the Israelites ran away and where the Philistines took over. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller now goes back to the story of the battle. The day after the fight, the Philistine soldiers go back to Mount Gilboa to take valuable things like weapons and armour from the dead Israelite soldiers. While the Philistines do this, the Philistines find Saul and his three sons lying dead.

The Philistine soldiers take off Saul's armour and cut off Saul's head. Saul's "armour" also refers to Saul's weapons. So the Philistines remove Saul's military clothes and the weapons Saul carried, like his sword, shield, and spear. After this, the Philistines send men all over the land of Philistia to tell the good news that Saul is dead and that the Philistines won the battle. These men announce the good news in the temples of their idols and to the Philistine people, possibly in public places like markets or smaller worship areas.

Stop here and look, as a group, at pictures of Saul's armour and weapons. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words you have in your language for armour and weapons. What do soldiers in your people group wear for battle? Pause this audio here.

The Philistines put Saul's armour and weapons in a temple of one of their gods. The Philistines fasten Saul's head in the temple of Dagon, another one of their false gods. When the Philistines fasten Saul's head, they probably drive something through it, like a tent peg, and then drive that tent peg into a pole or wall inside Dagon's temple. The story does not mention the location of either temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group which word or phrase you will use to say that the Philistines fastened Saul's head in the temple. Talk about different words that you have in your language that mean to fasten or to attach. Pause this audio here.

But when the people in Jabesh Gilead hear that the Philistines insulted Saul's dead body, the brave warriors from Jabesh Gilead take action. The warriors leave their city and travel to get the bodies of Saul and his sons. Jabesh is in Gilead, on the east side of the Jordan River, about 34–40 kilometers from Mount Gilboa, near where the Philistine temples probably were. The warriors bring Saul's dead body and his sons' bodies back to Jabesh. Then the warriors bury the bones of Saul and Saul's three sons under an oak tree that was likely famous in Jabesh. After that, the people of Jabesh fast, or stop eating, for seven days to mourn the deaths of Saul and his sons.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: The "brave warriors" were the "most courageous warriors" in the army. What words do you have in your language to describe someone like that? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: The storyteller calls the city of Jabesh "Jabesh Gilead" in this story to show that it was Jabesh in the region of Gilead. If your country has cities or towns with the same name but in different regions, how do you tell them apart? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of an oak tree. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller makes it clear to us that he is going to explain what happened in the story. Saul died because he did not trust Yahweh and did not keep the word of Yahweh, which means that Saul did not obey Yahweh. The storyteller reminds us that Saul even asked a woman who talked to spirits of dead people for advice, even though Yahweh told the Israelites not to do that. Since Saul did not ask Yahweh for help, Yahweh punished Saul. Yahweh killed Saul, took the kingdom away from Saul, and gave the kingdom to David, the son of Jesse.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What will you say to show that now the storyteller is explaining what happened in the story? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 10:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has eight scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Philistine army
  • Israelite army
  • King Saul
  • Three of Saul's sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malki-Shua
  • Philistine archers
  • Saul's armor-bearer
  • Saul's extended family, who died with Saul
  • Israelites, or Israelite people who lived on the other side of the Jezreel Valley and beyond the Jordan River, who fled their homes
  • Philistines, or Philistine people
  • Israelite people who lived in the city of Jabesh in the region of Gilead
  • And brave Israelite warriors from the city of Jabesh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The Philistines fight the Israelites on Mount Gilboa in the Valley of Jezreel. The Philistines kill many Israelite soldiers. The Israelites, including King Saul and his sons, run away quickly to save their lives. But the Philistines catch up to Saul and his sons and kill three of Saul's sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malki-Shua.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Philistines, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are so happy that we are winning against the Israelites at last; or
  • We are grateful to our gods who are helping us; or
  • We are so happy that we killed Saul's three sons. Now we need to kill Saul.

Continue the drama.

The Philistines fight fiercely against Saul. The Philistines get close to where Saul is. The Philistines attack hard and fight the men who are still with Saul, including Saul's bodyguard. Then the archers' arrows find their target, Saul, and hit him. The arrows wound Saul very badly.

Then Saul asks his armor-bearer to pull out his sword and kill Saul by stabbing him. Saul wants his armor-bearer to kill Saul because Saul is afraid the Philistines will kill Saul slowly and painfully. The armor-bearer is very scared and refuses to kill Saul.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Saul, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am desperate. My sons are dead and now I am badly wounded; or
  • I am afraid that the Philistines will treat me badly; or
  • My only hope is to die quickly.

Continue the drama.

Since Saul's armor-bearer will not kill him, Saul pulls out his own sword and kills himself by falling on the sword's point. When Saul's armour-bearer sees that Saul is dead, the armor-bearer throws himself on the point of his own sword and kills himself. Saul, three of his sons, and all of Saul's extended family die in battle on the same day.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Saul's armor-bearer, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am scared. King Saul asked me to kill him, but I am here to save Saul's life; or
  • I failed at my job. King Saul killed himself; or
  • My only hope is to die quickly.

Continue the drama.

When the Israelite people who live in the Jezreel Valley realise that the Israelite army ran away and that Saul and three of Saul's sons have died, the people run away from their cities quickly to live somewhere else. Then the Philistine soldiers and their families come into the empty cities and live there.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites on the other side of Jezreel Valley and those who live beyond the Jordan River, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are scared because Saul's army has fled, and King Saul is dead; or
  • We are really worried! Who is going to protect us from the Philistines now? The Philistines will kill us; or
  • We must run away as soon as we can. Yahweh will provide another place for us to live.

Continue the drama.

The day after the fight, the Philistine soldiers go back to Mount Gilboa to take valuable things like weapons and armour from the dead Israelite soldiers. While the Philistines do this, the Philistines find Saul and his three sons lying dead.

The Philistine soldiers take off Saul's armour and cut off Saul's head. The Philistines remove Saul's military clothes and the weapons he carried. After this, the Philistines send men all over the land of Philistia to share the good news that Saul is dead and that the Philistines won the battle.

The Philistines put Saul's armour and weapons in a temple of one of their gods. The Philistines fasten Saul's head in the temple of Dagon, another one of their false gods.

When the people in the city of Jabesh Gilead hear the news about how the Philistines insulted Saul's dead body, all the brave warriors from Jabesh Gilead leave their city and go to get the bodies of Saul and his sons. The warriors take Saul's dead body and the bodies of his sons and carry them back to Jabesh. Afterwards, the warriors bury the bones of Saul and his three sons under a large tree. The people of Jabesh fast, or stop eating, for seven days.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of the city of Jabesh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are so sad and angry that the Philistines killed Saul and treated his dead body so disrespectfully; or
  • Saul was kind to us and saved us from the Ammonites. We will now get Saul's body and bring it back to our city and bury it in an honourable way; or
  • We are so happy that we were able to rescue the bodies of Saul and his sons. We will honour these men by burying their bones under the oak tree and we will fast for seven days.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller explains what happened in the story. Saul died because he did not trust and obey Yahweh and did not follow Yahweh's commands. Since Saul did not ask Yahweh for help, but instead asked a woman who speaks to spirits of dead people, Yahweh punished Saul by killing him. Yahweh took the kingdom away from Saul, and gave it to David, the son of Jesse.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 10:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The Philistines fight against Israel on Mount Gilboa, and the men of Israel run away from the Philistines. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

The Philistines fight fiercely against Saul. Then the archers' arrows find their

target, Saul, and hit him. Use the same word or phrase for archer as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look, as a group, at a picture of the bow and arrows. Pause this audio here.

Then Saul asks his armor-bearer to pull out his sword and kill Saul by stabbing him, so that the uncircumcised fellows do not kill Saul slowly and painfully. Use the same word or phrase for sword as you used in previous passages.

Circumcision is the Jewish custom where people cut off the loose skin at the end of a boy's penis. People would perform circumcision when a baby boy was eight days old. The circumcision was a sign that God included the person in the covenant that God had made with Abraham.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for circumcised and uncircumcised. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. For more information on circumcision, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Saul and his entire house died. This means that the members of Saul's household died. In Israel, a person's household included the children and servants and all the people who lived in the house. Use the same word for household that you have used in previous passages, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about household.

The Philistines send men all over the land of Philistia to tell the good news that Saul is dead and that the Philistines have won the battle. These men announce the good news to their idols and to the Philistine people. An idol is an object that people make in order to worship a god. The object reminds the people of some characteristics of this god. People make an object or an image of a god and worship that object as if it were that god. God forbade the Israelites in his commandments to ever make such an idol, or image, or to worship anything else besides God himself.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for idol and false god. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. For more information on idol and gods, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines put Saul's armour and weapons in a temple of one of their gods. The Philistines also fasten Saul's head in the house, or temple, of Dagon, another one of their false gods. A temple is a place where people worship their gods. Sometimes people call this a "house" for their god. This temple, or house, was not for Yahweh, but for false gods.

Saul died because he did not trust Yahweh and did not keep the word or commands of Yahweh.

This means that Saul did not obey Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "word of Yahweh." If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase that you have used there. For more information on Word and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 11:1–9
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 11:1–9 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:1–9 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller begins the story of David, King of Israel. In the previous story, the storyteller told us about Saul's death and that Saul was unfaithful to Yahweh. Yahweh allowed the Philistines to kill Saul and gave Israel to David. Now the storyteller wants to tell us about David, a king who was faithful to Yahweh. This passage tells the same story as in 2 Samuel 5:1–16, but with some differences.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If you have already translated 2 Samuel 5:1–16, listen to your translation of that passage. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller starts by telling us about the time when David became king of all Israel. The storyteller tells us that all Israel went to David at the city of Hebron. Some people from every tribe of Israel gathered together and went to David to make David king.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a time when your people all wanted the same thing and went to the leaders to make sure it happened. Pause this audio here.

The people who spoke to David reminded David that they were closely related to David by saying they were David's bone and flesh. The people had the same ancestors as David. The people reminded David of times in the past, while Saul was still king, when David led the men of Israel into battle and then back to their homes. The people reminded David of the words that Yahweh, David's God, said to David. David's God is Yahweh, the same God that the people from all Israel follow. At some time, before David became king, Yahweh told David that David would shepherd the people of Israel and be the ruler of the people of Israel. A shepherd protects and guides sheep or goats. Israelites kept sheep and goats for meat and wool. David would do the work of a shepherd to protect and guide the people of Israel, just like a shepherd protects and guides sheep or goats.

Stop here and look at a picture of a group of sheep with a shepherd. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also said that David would be a ruler or a prince one day. David would be the person that Yahweh chose or appointed to lead the people of Israel.

Then the storyteller tells us more about the meeting at Hebron. The people who came to David were the elders of the tribes of Israel, or the male leaders of clans or families in the tribes of Israel. When the elders gathered with King David in the city of Hebron, David made a covenant with the elders. A covenant is an agreement between two people or groups of people where both make strong promises to do or not do certain things. We do not know what David or the elders promised to each other in Hebron. David made the covenant before Yahweh. In other words, Yahweh knew the promises that David made to the elders. The ark of the covenant represented Yahweh's presence. Often, when the storyteller tells us that the people did something before Yahweh, it means that the ark was there. However, we do not know if the ark of the covenant was present at Hebron or somewhere else. Even so, Yahweh knew what promises David made to the elders. The elders of Israel anointed David king of Israel. The elders made David king and placed special oil on David's head. David was Yahweh's chosen king of Israel. The storyteller tells us that Yahweh fulfilled the promise that Yahweh had spoken through Samuel to make David king.

The storyteller tells us that David and all the Israelites went to Jerusalem to attack Jerusalem. This is different from the passage in 2 Samuel where another storyteller said that the king and his men went to Jerusalem. In this passage, the storyteller does not mean that everybody in Israel went to Jerusalem, but that all of Israel supported David as David and his men went to Jerusalem. The storyteller wants to show us that Israel is now united with David. People also knew Jerusalem as Jebus.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel that shows the cities of Hebron and Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

The Jebusites, the people who lived in Jebus, said to David that the Israelites would not get into the city to conquer it. The Jebusites did not talk directly to David but gave a message that someone else reported to David. The Jebusites were confident that the Israelites could not conquer their fortress, or stronghold, which people also called Zion. Even though the Jebusites were confident and the fortress of Zion was strong, David captured the fortress of Zion. The storyteller tells us that later people began to call it the City of David, which was the name of the place at the time of the storyteller.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a place that had one name but now has a different name. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us more about how David conquered the fortress. The storyteller gives us more information in this story than the storyteller in 2 Samuel did. In this story, David said that David would make the first person to attack the Jebusites the chief and commander of the Israelite army. A chief and a commander are the same thing. This is the most important person in a group of people, or the most important leader of the army. Joab, the son of Zeruiah, was the first to attack the Jebusites. Zeruiah was David's sister. It is unusual for a storyteller to identify a person in the Bible as the son of their mother rather than the son of their father. Because Joab attacked the Jebusites, David made Joab the most important leader of David's army.

The storyteller tells us some of the things that David did while David was king. Sometime after the battle for Jerusalem, David lived in the stronghold, the fortress in Jerusalem. Because David lived in the stronghold, people named it the City of David. The City of David was one part of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem at the time David became king. Pause this audio here.

David ordered people to build the city around the City of David. The people built the city from the Millo, where the land was filled in, probably like a terrace. The people built around and on all sides of the City of David inward towards the wall around the fortress.

Stop here and look at a picture of terraces on the side of a hill. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Get a bowl and place it upside down on the ground. Take a long strip of paper or a long, wide leaf and make it into a ring that fits around the bowl. Put the ring around the bowl with the fine edge touching the ground and a space between the ring and the bowl. Now, use earth to fill the space between the slope of the bowl and the ring of paper or leaf and press it down until you make a flat, level surface between the slope of the bowl and the straight edge of the ring. The filled area with a flat level surface is like the Millo or terraces at Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that Joab repaired the rest of the city.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a place where some people lived, but then those people left that place, and other people came and lived there instead. Talk about what people needed to do to be able to live in the place after the first people left the place empty. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Place a big stone on the flat base of your bowl to represent the City of David. Place small stones on the flat level surface between the slope of the bowl and the straight edge of the ring to represent the houses David built outside the fortress. Place more small stones outside the ring of paper or leaf to represent the buildings Joab repaired. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that David became greater or more powerful over the time of David's reign because Yahweh was with David. The storyteller gives Yahweh an additional name, "the God of hosts" or "God almighty." God of hosts means the ruler of all heavenly beings. God Almighty means the all-powerful ruler of the armies of Israel.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: People from all of Israel met with David at the city of Hebron. The people told David all the reasons why David should be king of Israel. The elders of Israel met with David, and David made a covenant with them. The elders anointed David king of Israel. This fulfilled the prophecy that Yahweh gave to Samuel.

In the second scene: David went with people from all the tribes of Israel to Jerusalem to attack the Jebusites, and the Jebusites said that David would never conquer the fortress.

In the third scene: David conquered the fortress of Zion, which people later called the City of David.

In the fourth scene: David told the Israelites that the person who attacked the Jebusites first would become the commander of the Israelite army. Joab was first to attack the Jebusites, and David made Joab commander of the army.

In the fifth scene: During David's reign, David moved into the stronghold, the City of David, and built more buildings in the city on the land inside the terraces up to the wall of the fortress. Joab repaired the rest of Jerusalem. David became more powerful because Yahweh, God Almighty, was with David.

The characters in this passage are:

  • People from all the tribes of Israel
  • David
  • Yahweh
  • Elders from the tribes of Israel
  • Fighting men from all the tribes of Israel
  • Jebusites
  • And Joab, son of Zeruiah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this passage, the storyteller tells us about two important events when David became king and began to reign over Israel. Then the storyteller gives an overview of David's work in Jerusalem. Some time after Saul and the rest of Saul's family died, people from all the tribes of Israel went to David at the city of Hebron. The storyteller does not mean everybody in Israel went to Hebron, but some people from every tribe of Israel gathered together and went to David to make David king. When the people went to see David, the people gave all their reasons why they now wanted David to be king over all of Israel. The people talked about events from the past, when David led the Israelite army for Saul and when Yahweh gave a prophecy to David. Both of these events happened many years before David and the people from Israel met in Hebron. In the prophecy from Yahweh, when David was younger, Yahweh said David would become a shepherd and ruler of the Israelite people. The storyteller reminds us later that Yahweh gave this prophecy to the Israelites through Samuel.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a time when a friend or someone in your family talked about what you would be when you were older or grown up. Pay attention to how you talk about a conversation from your past when people talked to each other or you about your future or who you are now. Pause this audio here.

When the people from all the tribes of Israel gathered in Hebron, it was the elders who spoke with David. David made a covenant with the elders, and Yahweh witnessed the covenant. Then the elders anointed David king over all the tribes of Israel. This fulfilled Yahweh's promise to David through Samuel. Yahweh gave the prophecy to Samuel many years earlier, when David was younger.

After the elders anointed David king of all Israel, David decided to go to Jerusalem, which people also knew as Jebus, to attack and conquer Jerusalem. The Jebusites felt confident that David could not conquer the stronghold of Zion. But David did conquer the stronghold. Now the storyteller gives us extra information. The storyteller says that Zion is what we call the City of David today. We know that at some point, people began to call Zion the City of David because David lived there. At the time the storyteller lived, people still called the fortress the City of David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you say to show that something happened even when you did not think it would happen, or you did something to stop it from happening? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us how Joab became the commander of the Israelite army. Before David and the Israelites conquered the fortress, David promised to give the job of the most important leader to the first person to attack the Jebusites. Joab was the first person to attack, and so he became the most important commander of the army.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call the person who is the most important leader of your army or fighting people? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller now tells us about what David did in Jerusalem while David was king. David moved his home from Hebron to the fortress, and people began to call the fortress the City of David. During David's reign in Jerusalem, David built up the city around the fortress. David did not build the buildings himself but directed other people to build what David wanted. David got people to build more buildings around the fortress from the edge of the Millo, the filled terrace area, to the wall of the fortress. Also, during David's reign in Jerusalem, Joab repaired other parts of the city of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem at the time David became king, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller uses a word that means to make alive. We do not know if Joab brought the rest of the city to life by looking after the people or by making the buildings better. The storyteller uses the same word that Nehemiah used to tell the story of how the Israelites rebuilt Jerusalem when the Israelites returned from Babylon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remind each other of the stories you told about how people came back to live in places that were empty and what they did to make the homes good to live in. How will you translate this idea of what Joab did to repair the city? Pause this audio here.

While David was king, David became greater and more powerful because Yahweh, God Almighty, was with David. David did not become larger or stronger in his body, but David had more political and military power. "God Almighty," or the "God of the Hosts," or of all the heavenly beings, was a special name the Israelites gave to Yahweh when the Israelites talked about how Yahweh has power over everything on earth and in heaven.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • People from all the tribes of Israel
  • David
  • Yahweh
  • Elders from the tribes of Israel
  • Fighting men from all the tribes of Israel
  • Jebusites
  • And Joab, son of Zeruiah.

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

People from all of Israel met with David at the city of Hebron. The people told David all the reasons why David should be king of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people from all the tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We want you to be our king; or
  • We feel confident that Yahweh wants you to be king of Israel; or
  • We want to do what Yahweh wants us to do.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are family. We should be one nation together; or
  • I did lead all the tribes when I was younger; or
  • This is what Yahweh said I would be. I feel honoured to look after my people.

Continue the drama.

The elders of Israel met with David, and David made a covenant with them. The elders anointed David king of Israel, and that fulfilled the prophecy that Yahweh gave to Samuel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the elders from all the tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David will look after us and make us a great nation; or
  • We will make David our king; or
  • We feel happy to have a king to rule all the tribes in Israel.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The tribes will follow me; or
  • This is what Samuel prophesied about when Yahweh's spirit was on Samuel; or
  • Now I am king, and I will be everything that Yahweh promised me.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Samuel said my promises to David a long time ago; or
  • I am happy when I hear the covenant David makes with his people, the Israelites; or
  • I said David would be king. Here he is!

Continue the drama.

David went with people from all the tribes of Israel to Jerusalem to attack the Jebusites, and the Jebusites said that David would never conquer the fortress.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I need a home in the middle of Israel; or
  • It is time the Jebusites gave up their fortress; or
  • I feel strong when all the tribes of Israel follow me.

Ask the person playing the fighting men from all the tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We will follow David to a great victory; or
  • The fortress is big and strong on the hill, but we will conquer it.

Ask the person playing the Jebusites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The Israelites will never break our fortress and make us leave; or
  • We feel safe in our fortress.

Continue the drama.

David conquered the fortress of Zion, which people later called the City of David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The Jebusites were wrong; or
  • We have conquered the fortress and will take it for us to live in; or
  • This fortress will be my home, and I will make it mine.

Continue the drama.

David tells the Israelites that the person who attacks the Jebusites first will become the commander of the Israelite army. Joab was first to attack the Jebusites, and David made Joab commander of the army.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want the person who leads my army to be brave and the best fighter; or
  • I wonder who will win? or
  • Joab is brave and strong. Joab will lead my army for me.

Ask the person playing the fighting men from all the tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David will make sure that our commander is brave and strong; or
  • We wonder who will be first to attack the Jebusites; or
  • Joab will lead us well.

Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to be the commander of the Israelite army; or
  • I am first! David will make me the most important leader of the army.

Continue the drama.

During David's reign, David moved into the fortress, the City of David, and built more buildings in the city on the land inside the terraces up to the wall of the fortress. Joab repaired the rest of Jerusalem. David became more powerful because Yahweh, God Almighty, was with David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will make the fortress my home, and I will call it the City of David; or
  • I will make the city bigger and have enough homes and buildings for my people around my fortress; or
  • Yahweh is blessing me and making me a strong and powerful king.

Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will look after the city of Jerusalem and build homes for our people; or
  • We will bring Jerusalem to life with lots of people in the city and places for the people to live in; or
  • Yahweh is making David into a strong and powerful king of Israel.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David is being a good king, and David is obedient to me; or
  • I am blessing David and making David a powerful king of Israel.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Some people, the leaders, from all the tribes of Israel, met with David at Hebron. For more information on Israel and Hebron, refer to the Master Glossary. The people said they were David's bone and flesh, which means that the people were closely related to David.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for bone and flesh. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people from all the tribes reminded David what Yahweh, David's God, had said to David. Yahweh is God's personal name. In this passage, God is called Yahweh. Be sure to translate Yahweh and God in the same way as you have in previous passages, and remember that Yahweh and God are in the Master Glossary.

David led Israel's soldiers out to battle and back home when Saul was king of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The leaders of the tribes reminded David that Yahweh said David would shepherd Yahweh's people. A shepherd is a person who takes care of sheep and goats, and so David would take care of Yahweh's people in the same way that a shepherd takes care of animals.

Stop here and look at a picture of a group of sheep with a shepherd if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for shepherd. Look up shepherd in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also said David would be ruler or prince over Israel. This kind of ruler is one that someone in authority appoints. Yahweh is appointing David to rule.

Stop here and review what word or phrase you used for this type of ruler in 1 Chronicles 5:2. Use the same word or phrase for this type of ruler that you used there. Pause this audio here.

The elders or leaders of the tribes of Israel went to King David at Hebron.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for elders. Look up elder in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King David made a covenant with the elders at Hebron. A covenant is a solemn promise between two people or groups of people that the people cannot break.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for covenant. Look up covenant in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David made the covenant with the elders before Yahweh. In other words, Yahweh witnessed the covenant agreement.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for before Yahweh. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The elders anointed David as king, or poured special oil on David and made David king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for anointed. Look up anoint in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David and all the Israelites went to Jerusalem. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary. The Jebusites said that David would not get into the fortress, or stronghold.

Even though the Jebusites thought David could not get into the fortress, David captured the fortress of Zion. There are two kinds of safe places like this in the Bible. One kind of fortress or stronghold is one that people made with bricks or stone and high walls. Another kind of stronghold is one that is a natural strong cave or safe place. The fortress, or stronghold, in this story is a safe place that people built. People usually built this kind of fortress with thick walls around it and often on top of a hill.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for fortress, or stronghold, and Zion. Look up stronghold and Zion in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David said that the person who led and was first to attack the Jebusites would be the chief and commander, the most important leader of the Israelite army. Use the same word or phrase for chief as you used in previous passages. A commander was a military leader who carried a staff to show he was a leader.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for chief commander. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David lived in the fortress and called it the City of David. David built the city around the fortress from the Millo or terraces towards the fortress. The city was a large settlement with a wall around it. Use the same word or phrase for city as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of terraces on the side of a hill, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Millo, or terraces. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David became more powerful because Yahweh Almighty, the Yahweh of "Hosts" or all heavenly beings, was with David. You can translate this name to mean almighty or all-powerful.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Yahweh Almighty, or Yahweh of Hosts. Look up "Yahweh of Hosts" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this title in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

resource.label: Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold
Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold

title: 1 Chronicles 11:10–19
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 11:10–19 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:10–19 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:10–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, the storyteller gave a summary of how David defeated the city of Jerusalem with the help of his soldier Joab. David then made Joab commander of the whole Israelite army. Now, the storyteller will tell us about David's mighty men.

The storyteller says he will name the chiefs of David's mighty men, or the most elite group of David's soldiers. David specially chose and trained the mighty men, and these men took orders directly from David. These famous warriors gave David strong support in the kingdom, which means these men were very loyal to David as their king. David became king, or ruler of Israel, through the word of Yahweh, or just as Yahweh promised.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Who are the elite or greatest fighters in your culture? Why are these people so well-known? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then begins to give an account of, or name, David's mighty men. The mighty men supported David from the time when Saul was king to David's own reign as king. Some of the mighty men began to follow David while he was on the run from Saul. After David was king, the men helped David to defeat his enemies and unify the tribes of Israel. David's mighty men consisted of two groups: the top military officers whom David called "The Three," and the next level of important soldiers whom people called "The Thirty." Jashobeam, who was a Hacmonite, probably from a place called Hacmoni, was chief, or the highest leader, of the group of three. Jashobeam was best known for the time when he defeated 300 men during one battle using only his spear. A spear is a type of weapon with a wooden staff and a sharp metallic end that soldiers used to stab people.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear as a group. Pause this audio here.

The second man the storyteller lists as part of the three mighty men is Eleazar. Eleazar was a descendant of Dodai the Ahohite, probably from the clan, or family, of Ahoah. Eleazar stood with David at Pas Dammim to fight against the Philistines. Pas Dammim was most likely an area located to the west of Bethlehem toward the Philistine coast. The storyteller tells us that Eleazar stood with David to defend a field of barley from a group of Philistine soldiers after the rest of the Israelite soldiers ran away. Eleazer stayed with David, defended the field, and won a great victory with the help of Yahweh. Barley is a grain that people use to make bread and porridge. Barley can grow in places with little water and poor soil where wheat cannot grow, so barley was a very important grain in Israel.

Stop here and look at pictures of barley and a barley field as a group. Pause this audio here.

Although we know from 2 Samuel 23 that Shammah was the other soldier who was a part of The Three, the storyteller does not mention him in this passage. Because of the similarities between the stories of how Shammah defended a field of lentils with David and this story of how Eleazar defended a field of barley with David, it is likely that the story of how Eleazar defended the field may originally have been the story about Shammah.

The storyteller now changes to tell a different story. Three of David's mighty men go to "the rock" to visit David at the cave of Adullam. It is unclear whether these are the same three leaders as before or different mighty men. The rock most likely refers to a large rock that provided protection near the cave. The cave itself was probably a limestone cave, about 20 kilometers or 12 miles southwest of Bethlehem, the city where David was born. This cave and rock together created a stronghold, or a place where the natural areas of the land can protect people from enemies or danger.

Stop here and look at a picture of a limestone cave as a group. Discuss what a stronghold would look like in your culture and how it is the same or different from where David hid. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines were camped in the Valley of Rephaim, which was a wide valley southwest of Jerusalem on the border between Judah and the northern tribes. While David was staying in his stronghold, or cave, which was well-protected, the Philistines had a garrison, or a group of soldiers, who were staying in and had control of Bethlehem.

Stop here and look at a map of Adullam, the Valley of Rephaim, and Bethlehem as a group. Pause this audio here.

While David was in the cave, David wished that he could have a drink of water from a well by the gate of the city of Bethlehem. A well is a hole in the ground where people can find water naturally. When the three men heard that David wanted a drink of water, the men traveled to Bethlehem, broke through the Philistine soldiers staying there, and brought water from the well back to David. The soldiers showed their complete loyalty to David by going to so much trouble to get the water.

Even though David really wanted to drink the water his soldiers brought him, David poured the water on the ground. When an Israelite person poured water on the ground, it was like they were giving a drink offering to worship and honor Yahweh. David emphasizes that only Yahweh is fit to receive such a costly offering since David's soldiers risked their lives for David.

And so, these were some of the great acts of David's mighty men.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:10–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us about the three chiefs of David's mighty men who were loyal and supported David as their king.

In the second scene: Jashobeam fights with his spear and defeats 300 men during one battle.

In the third scene: Eleazar and David defend a barley field from the Philistines in Pas Dammim. Even when all the other Israelite soldiers run away, Eleazar continues to fight until Yahweh gives him a great victory.

In the fourth scene: Three mighty men visit David in the cave of Adullam. David says he longs for a drink from a well in Bethlehem, even though the Philistines currently control the city of Bethlehem. The three men break through the Philistine soldiers and draw water from the Bethlehem well. The men bring the water back to David. David pours the water on the ground as an offering to Yahweh and says he cannot drink the water because the cost of the men's lives was too high.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • David
  • Jashobeam
  • The 300 enemies whom Jashobeam killed with a spear
  • Eleazar
  • The three mighty men who visited David
  • And Philistine soldiers whom David's men defeated

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller begins by telling us that he will introduce a group of soldiers who were very loyal to David and gave David great support as their king. The storyteller says that not just the soldiers, but all of Israel, or the nation of Yahweh's chosen people, supported David as their king.

The phrase "according to the word of Yahweh" means that David was king and had the support of the people, just as Yahweh promised.

Then the storyteller lists David's mighty men, beginning with a group of elite soldiers called "The Three."

The storyteller describes the next events as separate and as having happened at different times and locations. These are events that have already happened. The mighty men helped David defeat his enemies early in his reign as king after David brought the tribes of Israel together.

Jashobeam is the chief of the three mighty soldiers, and Jashobeam was most famous for killing 300 soldiers at one time with his spear. The phrase "at one time" does not mean at the same moment, but during the same battle. It is important to note that the number of soldiers is different from the similar story in 2 Samuel 23, which says there were 800 soldiers.

The next person the storyteller talks about is Eleazar. When the storyteller says, "and next to him was Eleazar," the storyteller does not mean that Eleazar was physically standing beside Jashobeam, but rather that Eleazar was the next person the storyteller was going to talk about.

The storyteller tells about a time when Eleazar stood with David in a place called Pas Dammim. After all of the Israelite soldiers ran away, Eleazar stayed with David and defended a field of barley from a group of Philistine soldiers until Yahweh gave him a great victory. It is important to remember that barley was a grain that people used to make bread and porridge, which made barley a valuable crop during this time.

Stop here and look at pictures of barley and a barley field as a group. Pause this audio here.

It is important to note that the storyteller does not name the third man, Shammah, who was a part of this elite group of three soldiers.

The storyteller then tells about when three of David's mighty men visited David at the rock near the cave of Adullam. "The rock" most likely refers to a large rock that provided protection near the cave. The storyteller does not tell us if these are the same men he named earlier in the passage.

The storyteller says David was in David's stronghold. A stronghold was a high place that was well-protected, so this most likely refers to the cave of Adullam.

Stop here and look at a picture of a natural stronghold as a group. Discuss with your group how you will describe a place like this in your language. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines had a garrison, or a group of soldiers, in control of Bethlehem. This means the Philistine soldiers stayed in and had control of the city of Bethlehem. The three men visit David and hear David say, "Oh, that someone would give me water to drink from the well of Bethlehem that is by the gate!" It is important to remember that David was not asking for someone to get water from the well, but David was saying that he wanted that water even though that was not possible at that time.

Stop here and discuss as a group: David spoke of something he desired, but David did not think it was possible for anyone to do. How will you translate this into your language? Pause this audio here.

The three soldiers decided to get the water for David. The men walked the 12 miles, or 19 kilometers, from the cave to Bethlehem, broke through the Philistine soldiers who were stationed at Bethlehem, drew water from the well, and brought it to David. The phrase "break through" means the mighty men were able to get past the camp of Philistine soldiers and reach the well. The mighty men may have fought the soldiers, but it seems most likely that the men snuck past the soldiers at night.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you translate the action of breaking through the soldiers in your language? Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that the well or cistern was a place where people could find water that collected naturally.

The soldiers arrived back at the cave with the water for David. When David received the water, even though he really wanted it, David poured the water out on the ground as an offering to Yahweh. David responded by saying, "God forbid that I should drink this." David said this to emphasize that he could never drink the water that the men brought to David.

When David asked, "Shall I drink the blood of the men who went at the risk of their lives?" David was not actually asking if he should drink the blood of his soldiers. Instead, David said this to show he felt unworthy that his soldiers should risk their lives to give David what he wanted. Only Yahweh was worthy of the men risking their lives.
