Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a time when someone did not accept what a person gave them because that person did not feel worthy. Pay attention to what words you use so that you can accurately translate how David responded to his loyal soldiers. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller clearly finishes this part of the story of David's mighty men by stating, "These are the kinds of things that the three mighty men did." The storyteller is not telling every story about what the mighty men did, but just giving some examples of what the men did.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:10–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • David
  • Jashobeam
  • The 300 enemies whom Jashobeam killed with a spear
  • Eleazar
  • The three mighty men who visited David
  • And Philistine soldiers whom David's men defeated

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how the storyteller tells us about David's three mighty men who were loyal and supported David as their king so that David's kingdom would be strong.

The audience would have known this group of soldiers well. One way to show this is by having the actors for the mighty men follow David wherever he goes.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh showed great favor to me when Yahweh gave me so many loyal soldiers; or
  • I feel honored to be the king of Yahweh's people; or
  • I am thankful that my soldiers have helped me defeat my enemies and that these men are loyal to me when I need them most.

Ask the person playing the three men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel loyal. I will follow David wherever he goes; or
  • It is clear that God is on David's side; or
  • I would gladly give my life in battle for David.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Jashobeam fights soldiers with his spear. Jashobeam defeats 300 men during one battle.

Next, act out how Eleazar defends a barley field from the Philistines in Pas Dammim. Even when all of the other Israelite soldiers run away, Eleazar continues to fight until Yahweh gives him a great victory.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelite soldiers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am impressed by Eleazar's skill and bravery! or
  • It is clear that Yahweh used Eleazar to give us victory; or
  • I am ashamed that I ran away. I should have trusted Yahweh and stayed with Eleazar.

Continue the drama.

Act out how three of the mighty men visit David in the cave of Adullam. David says he longs for a drink from a well in Bethlehem, even though the Philistines currently control the city. The three men walk to Bethlehem, break through the Philistine soldiers, and draw water from the well. The three mighty men bring the water back to David. David pours the water on the ground as an offering to Yahweh and says he cannot drink the water because the cost of the men's lives was too high.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the three mighty men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am impressed by David's great love for Yahweh; or
  • I am surprised. I was sure David would love to drink the water from his hometown; or
  • I am humbled and reminded that my greatest desire should be Yahweh.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am humbled that my men are so loyal to me; or
  • I am worried that my men could have died just because I wanted a drink of water! or
  • I hope my soldiers know to honor Yahweh more than me.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:10–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller begins this section by describing David's mighty men. These mighty men refer to a group of soldiers whom David had specially chosen and trained well. The mighty men were very strong. The men reported directly to the king and did special assignments for him.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for mighty men. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says the mighty men gave David strong support in his kingdom, with all of Israel, to make David king. Israel was the nation of Yahweh's chosen people, the descendants of Jacob, whom Yahweh named "Israel." David was the king, or ruler, of Israel. David's kingdom refers to the territory of Israel. The loyalty and support of David's mighty men helped David defeat his enemies to become king. Use the same words or phrases for king, kingdom, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, kingdom, or Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

David became king and had the support of his soldiers and people according to the word of Yahweh, or just as Yahweh promised. The "word of Yahweh" refers to everything Yahweh tells people. Yahweh specially chose David when David was a young man to become the next king of Israel. Yahweh completed that promise. Use the same word or phrase for "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. For more information on "word of Yahweh," refer to the Master Glossary.

Jashobeam, the chief of the mighty men, fights with his spear and defeats 300 men during one battle. Jashobeam was the chief, or leader, of the mighty men, and Jashobeam reported directly to the king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for chief, or leader. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

A spear refers to a weapon that has a wooden shaft and a metallic, sharp end that people used to stab other people. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for spear that you have used in previous passages.

Eleazar defended a field of barley from the Philistines who were attacking them, even after the rest of the Israelite soldiers ran away. Yahweh gave Eleazar a great victory over the Philistines. Yahweh is the personal name of the God of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Three of the mighty men visited David when he was staying in the stronghold, or, in this case, the cave of Adullam. Here, stronghold refers to a high-up and well-protected area, which was a cave near Adullam. This type of stronghold was natural, which means that people did not make this stronghold like they made the fortress or stronghold at the City of David.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a natural stronghold. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. For more information on stronghold, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

When David's soldiers brought him the water from Bethlehem, David asked, "Should I drink the blood of the men who went at the risk of their lives?" Here, blood does not refer to actual blood, but it is special language that refers to the soldiers' lives. Use the same word or phrase for blood as you used in previous passages. For more information on blood, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 11:20–25
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 11:20–25 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:20–25 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:20–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues to remember David's mighty men. As you remember, David especially chose and trained these soldiers, and they reported directly to the king. David's mighty men consisted of two groups: the top military officers whom David called "The Three," and the next level of important soldiers whom people called "The Thirty." Now, the storyteller tells some stories about men from "The Thirty."

First, the storyteller tells about Abishai, who was the brother of Joab. As you remember, both Joab and Abishai were David's nephews. Abishai and Joab were famous among the people as skilled fighters. We know from previous passages that Joab was the commander of the Israelite army, but here, besides being the brother of Abishai, the storyteller does not mention Joab as part of David's mighty men.

One reason people knew Abishai was that Abishai killed 300 men during one battle using just his spear. As you remember, a spear was a weapon with a wooden shaft and a sharp metallic end that people used to stab enemies.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says Abishai was the commander, or leader, of The Three. However, because the words for "The Three" and "The Thirty" are very similar in the original language, it is hard to know what Abishai's position was among "The Thirty." It is clear that people considered Abishai the most famous among the mighty men, and that the other men regarded Abishai as a leader. The people honored Abishai more than "The Three," though people did not consider him to be one of the three main leaders of the mighty men.

Next, the storyteller tells about Benaiah, the son of Jehoiada, who came from the city of Kabzeel. Kabzeel was in the southern part of the territory of Judah. Benaiah was a very brave man who did many brave things. One thing people remembered Benaiah for was that he killed two ariels, or champion fighters, in Moab. Scholars translate "ariel" as "lion of God," and think this means they were champion fighters in Moab. It may be easier to say he killed the two best fighters in the land of Moab.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a champion fighter who people celebrated in your culture. What kinds of brave actions make someone famous where you live? Pause this audio here.

Another one of Benaiah's brave acts was when he killed a lion in a pit or deep well. It was very cold, and snow, or cold white flakes of frozen water, had fallen from the sky like rain. Most likely, the snow made the lion go closer to the town in search of food. Since lions are large, dangerous hunters with sharp teeth and claws, it was very impressive that Benaiah could kill such an animal.

Stop here and look at a picture of some snow and a lion as a group. Pause this audio here.

Benaiah was also famous for killing a very large Egyptian man who was 5 cubits tall. This would have been around 7.5 feet or 2.5 meters tall.

Stop here and, as a group, stretch your arms as high as you can. Now imagine if you added another arm's length on top of that. That is how tall the Egyptian man was! Pause this audio here.

The Egyptian had a spear the size of a weaver's beam. When a person was weaving cloth, they would tie a set of threads to a large wooden stick while they wove another set of threads between them. People called this large stick a weaver's beam. The storyteller makes this comparison to say that the spear was thicker and longer than a normal spear.

Stop here and look at a picture of a weaver's beam as well as a wooden staff as a group. Pause this audio here.

While the Egyptian man used a spear, Benaiah fought him with only a club or a heavy wooden staff. While fighting the man, Benaiah managed to take the Egyptian's spear away from him and kill the Egyptian with the Egyptian's own weapon. Actions such as these won Benaiah a name. This means these acts made Benaiah famous, and people knew Benaiah just as well as they knew "The Three." The storyteller once again emphasizes that although people considered Benaiah to be famous among "The Thirty" mighty men, Benaiah was not a part of "The Three."

The storyteller says that David put Benaiah in charge of David's bodyguard. David's bodyguard was most likely a special group of soldiers who personally guarded King David.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:20–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Abishai fights against and kills 300 men in one battle using only his spear. People see Abishai as a leader amongst the 30 mighty men.

In the second scene: Benaiah does several amazing things in sequence. First, Benaiah fights and kills two great fighters from Moab. Then Benaiah finds a lion caught in a pit in the snow. Benaiah kills the lion. Finally, Benaiah fights against a strong, tall Egyptian man with a large spear. Benaiah fights the Egyptian with only a staff. During the fight, Benaiah takes the spear from the Egyptian man and kills him with his own weapon. These actions make Benaiah as famous as the three mighty men.

In the third scene: All of the 30 mighty men cheer for Benaiah, and David makes Benaiah the leader of David's bodyguard.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King David
  • Abishai
  • Benaiah
  • The Three
  • The 30 Mighty Men
  • 300 enemies
  • Two Moab fighters
  • Lion
  • The great Egyptian man

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that the storyteller is telling about events from the past. These are battles that have already happened.

The storyteller tells about Abishai. The original language makes it clear that this is a new part of the story about David's mighty men. Abishai was Joab's brother, which means Abishai was David's nephew.

The storyteller describes Abishai as chief of the 30 mighty men, but it is possible that Abishai was chief of "The Three." It is difficult to know which group the storyteller is referring to because the words for the two groups are so similar in the original language. It is hard to know exactly how to translate Abishai's rank. It is clear that people considered Abishai the most famous among the 30 mighty men and that the other men regarded Abishai as a leader. Abishai was most likely not a part of the separate group of the three mighty men.

Abishai killed 300 men at one time with his spear. It is important to remember that Abishai did not kill all 300 men at the same time but during the same battle.

Abishai wins or makes a name for himself because he defeats the men. When someone wins a name, that person becomes famous because of what they do. People respected Abishai even more than they respected the three chief mighty men, but Abishai was not one of "The Three" chief mighty men.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate the concept of winning a name for yourself. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then begins to tell about Benaiah and his different heroic and brave actions. Benaiah was the son of Jehoiada and was from Kabzeel, a city in the territory of the tribe of Judah.

The storyteller describes Benaiah as a valiant, or brave man. First, Benaiah fought against and killed the two ariels of Moab. Scholars translate "ariel" as "lion of God," and think this means they were champion fighters in Moab. It may be easier to say that Benaiah killed the two best fighters in the land of Moab. The Moabites were enemies of the Israelites.

Next, Benaiah finds a lion in a well near a village in the snow. Benaiah fights the lion and kills it.

Finally, Benaiah fights against an Egyptian man, or a man from Egypt, who is 5 cubits tall. A cubit is around 50 centimeters, so this means the man was 7.5 feet or 2.5 meters tall. The Egyptian had a spear as large as a weaver's beam, which was another way to say it was very large.

Stop here and look at a picture of a weaver's beam as a group. Compare how people weave thread in your culture to how the storyteller describes weaving here. Think about how you will translate the spear's comparison to a weaver's beam to mean that the spear was very long. Pause this audio here.

Benaiah only has a wooden staff, but he manages to take the spear from the Egyptian man and kill the Egyptian with the Egyptian's own weapon. It is important to remember that the spear was a better weapon, but Benaiah still managed to win the fight.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear and a picture of a wooden staff as a group. Pause this audio here.

The 30 mighty men congratulate, or honor, Benaiah, but Benaiah is not part of the separate group of three elite fighters. David sets Benaiah over, or puts him in charge of, David's bodyguard, or the group of men who protect David.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:20–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King David
  • Abishai
  • Benaiah
  • The Three
  • The 30 Mighty Men
  • 300 enemies
  • Two Moab fighters
  • Lion
  • The great Egyptian man

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how the storyteller tells about David's mighty men. The storyteller tells how Abishai fought against and killed 300 men in one battle using only his spear. The 30 mighty men see Abishai as their leader.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Abishai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy to defend my uncle. I would do anything for David; or
  • I am thankful to Yahweh for the victory that Yahweh gave me; or
  • I see how the men look at me. I want to lead the men well.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Benaiah does several amazing things. First, Benaiah fights and kills two great fighters from Moab. Then Benaiah finds a lion caught in a pit in the snow. Benaiah kills the lion.

Act out how Benaiah fights against an Egyptian man who is a lot taller than Benaiah. The Egyptian man has a very large spear, but Benaiah fights the Egyptian with only a staff.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Egyptian, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel amused. This man is small and only has a staff. This fight will be easy; or
  • I feel insulted. I cannot believe David thinks that one man can defeat me; or
  • I feel concerned. This man seems too confident. I have heard stories of David's men who fight with the power of their God.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Benaiah fights the Egyptian, takes the man's spear, and kills the Egyptian with the Egyptian's own weapon. Act out how all of the 30 mighty men honor Benaiah. David makes Benaiah the commander of David's bodyguard.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Benaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am honored to be in charge of keeping David safe; or
  • I am determined to do everything I can to keep David safe; or
  • I am eager to be in David's presence more often. I have much to learn from David.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:20–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Abishai was chief of "The Three." As you remember, David split his mighty men into two groups: the top military officers whom David called "The Three," and the next level of important soldiers whom David called "The Thirty." So Abishai was either chief or leader of the group of three elite soldiers or chief of the whole group of 30 mighty men. Be sure to use the same words or phrases for chief, "The Three," and "The Thirty" as you used in previous passages.

Abishai killed 300 men at one time with a spear. Be sure to use the same word for spear that you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear as a group. Pause this audio here.

Abishai also becomes the commander of the mighty men, which means Abishai was their military leader. Use the same word or phrase for military commander or leader as you have previously.

Benaiah killed a lion in a snowy pit. Snow is frozen water that looks like cold white flakes and falls from the sky like rain.

Stop here and look at a picture of snow as a group. Discuss what word you will use for snow. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

A lion is a large, dangerous hunter with sharp teeth and claws. Be sure to use the same word for lion that you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion as a group. Pause this audio here.

Benaiah also struck down, or killed, two great fighters from Moab and a large Egyptian man who was 5 cubits tall. A cubit is a way to measure how long something is. One cubit is about as long as a man's arm from his elbow to his fingertips, which is about 50 centimeters. This means the Egyptian man was 7.5 feet or 2.5 meters tall.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cubit. Look up cubit in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David put Benaiah in charge of David's bodyguard. A bodyguard refers to a group of soldiers who personally protected King David. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for bodyguard that you have used in previous books.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for bodyguard. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

acai.preferred_label: Bring Honor and Glory on Oneself

title: 1 Chronicles 11:26–47
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 11:26–47 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:26–47 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:26–47 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues to tell us about David's mighty men. As you remember, David's mighty men consisted of two groups: the top military officers whom David called "The Three," and the next level of important soldiers whom people called "The Thirty." Now, the storyteller names these mighty men.

It is important to note that the names of the mighty men who were part of "The Thirty" changed during the time that King David ruled the kingdom of Israel. The names changed because, as men died, David needed to replace those men. There were even mighty men, such as Asahel, who were a part of this group before David was king.

In the list, the storyteller names each man and then describes each man by his ancestors, where he is from, or both. These are the names of David's mighty men:

  • Asahel, the brother of Joab

As you remember, Abner killed Asahel after Asahel refused to stop chasing and attacking Abner.

  • Elhanan, son of Dodo from Bethlehem

You will remember that Bethlehem was a city located in the hill country of Judah. Bethlehem was also the hometown of David.

  • Shammoth, the Harorite

This means that Shammoth was most likely from Harod, which was a city in the Jezreel Valley.

  • Helez, the Pelonite

The Pelonites were the people group or clan that Helez belonged to.

  • Ira, the son of Ikkesh from Tekoa

Tekoa was a town 16 kilometers, or 10 miles, south of Jerusalem.

  • Abiezer, from Anathoth
  • Sibbecai the Hushathite

Sibbecai was famous because he killed the giant Saph. Sibbecai was a Hushathite, which means he was from the town of Hushah in Judah.

  • Ilai, the Ahohite, from the place called Ahoah
  • Maharai, the Netophathite
  • Heled, the son of Baanah the Netophathite

Both Maharai and Heled were Netophathites, which means they were from Netophah, a city close to Bethlehem.

  • Ithai, son of Ribai from Gibeah in Benjamin

Gibeah was a city in the territory of Benjamin near the border of Judah.

  • Benaiah, the Pirathonite

Pirathonite means a person from the city of Pirathon, which was located in the territory of Ephraim.

  • Hurai, from the brooks of Gaash

The brooks of Gaash refer to a body of water that flows continuously. These brooks were located at the base of Mount Gaash, southwest of Shechem.

  • Abiel, the Arbathite from the place called Arbah, or Arbah's clan
  • Azmaveth, the Barhumite

Azmaveth was from the city called Bahurim.

  • Eliahba, the Shaalbonite

The Shaalbonites were a people group who lived in the city of Shaalbim, which was located in the territory of Dan.

  • The sons of Hashem, the Gizonite from a place called Gizon
  • Jonathan, the son of Shagee the Hararite
  • Ahiam, the son of Sacar the Hararite

The Hararites were the people group that Jonathan and Ahiam belonged to, from the place called Harar.

  • Eliphal, the son of Ur
  • Hepher, the Mekerathite

The Mekerathites were the people group that Hepher belonged to, probably from Mekera.

  • Ahijah, the Pelonite

The Pelonites were the people group Ahijah belonged to, probably from a place called Pelon.

  • Hezro, the Carmelite

Carmelite refers to someone who is from the city of Carmel, which was located in the territory of Judah.

  • Naarai, the son of Ezbai
  • Joel, the brother of Nathan
  • Mibhar, the son of Hagri
  • Zelek, the Ammonite

Zelek was from Ammon, or a non-Israelite territory between the Jabbok River in the north and the Arnon River in the south, and from the Jordan River in the west to the eastern desert region.

  • Naharai, the Beerothite, the armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah

Beeroth was originally a Gibeonite city, or a foreign city, that Joshua assigned to the territory of Benjamin. Naharai was an armor-bearer, which means he carried extra weapons for Joab, who was the commander of the Israelite army. As you remember, Zeruiah was the sister of David.

  • Ira, the Ithrite
  • Gareb, the Ithrite

Ira and Gareb were Ithrites and from the city of Jattir. This was a city located in the territory of Judah.

  • Uriah, the Hittite

The Hittites were the people group that Uriah was from. As you remember, Uriah was the husband of Bathsheeba and a soldier of David. Uriah died in battle after David ordered Joab to put Uriah where the battle was worst.

It is important to notice that, unlike the similar list of mighty men in 2 Samuel 23, here the storyteller continues to list 16 extra heroes. These men's names were:

  • Zabad, the son of Ahlai
  • Adina, the son of Shiza the Reubenite, a chief of the Reubenites, and thirty with him.

This means Adina's father, Shiza, was the chief of the Reubenites, including thirty men who followed his command.

  • Hanan, the son of Maacah
  • Joshaphat, the Mithnite

Joshaphat was from the city of Mithna.

  • Uzzia, the Ashterathite

An Ashterathite was a person from the town of Ashtaroth in the region of Bashan.

  • Shama and Jeiel, the sons of Hotham the Aroerite

Hotham was from the town of Aroer.

  • Jediael, the son of Shimri
  • Joha, the brother of Jediael, who was a Tizite

Joha belonged to the Tizite people group.

  • Eliel the Mahavite

Eliel belonged to the Mahavite people group.

  • Jeribai and Joshaviah, the sons of Elnaam
  • Ithmah, the Moabite

Ithmah was from the region of Moab.

  • Eliel
  • Obed
  • Jaasiel the Mezobaite

Jaasiel was from the town of Zobah.

In this list, the storyteller names 32 different men who were a part of "The Thirty," since the sons of Hashen probably refers to at least two men. As you may have noticed, not all of David's mighty men were Israelites. David included many foreigners, whom David most likely added to the group when he was running away from Saul before David became king.

As we can see by the number of men on the list, the storyteller emphasizes how many good relationships David had throughout his lifetime and how loyal those soldiers were to David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people remember lists of names in your culture? When have you heard a list of names similar to this before? Who is in that list? What format is that list in? Why do people make these kinds of lists? What are ways you can remember this list of names? Try to remember a list of names of famous people and tell the list to each other.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:26–47 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has one scene.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us the names of David's mighty men. The storyteller lists 47 men in total.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller
  • And the 47 mighty men

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that David's mighty men consisted of two groups: the top military officers whom David called "The Three," and the next level of important soldiers whom people called "The Thirty."

It is important to remember that the group called "The Thirty" does not refer to an exact number because the number of men changed throughout David's lifetime.

This list of David's mighty men is different at times from the 2 Samuel list of mighty men. Many times, storytellers were from different areas, and they may have pronounced or said names differently. It is also possible that one person may have made a mistake in the way they wrote down the names. In any case, the translators should keep the same names and differences between the two lists. As in any list of names, there will always be differences between different people who write the names down.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: It is important to note the differences between this list of mighty men here and the list of David's mighty men in 2 Samuel. Discuss with your translation team how you will handle these differences. Pause this audio here.

We do not know what all the descriptions of the men mean. Sometimes, the description about a man may refer to the name of the man's family clan, or it may refer to the city or region from where they came. Sometimes it refers to both!

Stop here and, as a group, discuss how you will describe each man when you are not sure if they are from a certain place or a certain family group. Pause the audio here.

It is important to remember that in the 2 Samuel passage, we do not hear about the last 16 men the storyteller names in this story.

It is important to note that many of these men were from places outside of Israel. David most likely added to his group of mighty men when he was running away from Saul before David became king.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Many cultures use songs, poetry, or art to remember long lists of information. Other times, it can be helpful to group certain names together based on where those people were from or who they were related to. Talk to your group about how you will organize, sing, or draw the list of names to best remember them. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:26–47 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has one scene.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller
  • And the 47 mighty men

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how the storyteller stands and recites the names of David's mighty men.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing one of the mighty men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am honored to serve King David; or
  • I would do anything for David because he is a man who walks closely with Yahweh; or
  • I am proud to be a member of David's mighty men. These are all great men! or
  • I would protect David with my life.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 11:26–47 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller lists the names of David's mighty men. These were men whom the king specially chose, trained, and to whom he gave direct orders. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for mighty men as you used in previous passages.

Elhanan was the son of a man named Dodo, who was from the town of Bethlehem. Here, son does not just mean descendant, but it means Dodo was Elhanan's father. Be sure to use the same word for son as you used in previous passages. Son is in the Master Glossary.

The storyteller refers to Naharai as the armor-bearer of Joab. As you remember, Joab was the commander of the entire army of Israel. An armor-bearer was a servant who carried weapons and went into battle with their commander. People chose armor-bearers for their bravery, and these men were responsible for protecting the commanders they served. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for armor-bearer as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 12:1–7
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 12:1–7 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:1–7 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, the storyteller told us about the 30 mighty men who supported David and his kingdom, and about some of the brave and powerful things those men did in battle.

In this story, we will now learn specifically about the men who went with David to Ziklag. You will remember that David went to Ziklag before he went to Hebron and then finally to Jerusalem. This means that the information in this passage actually happened before the stories we just heard in the previous passages.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel. Find Hebron and Jerusalem on the map. Pause this audio here.

This story begins when it says that this is a list of the fighting men who came to David at Ziklag. We know from 1 Samuel 27:7 that David went to Ziklag because King Saul, the son of Kish, chased David away. You will remember that Ziklag was a Philistine city, and that Achish, the king of Gath, gave Ziklag to David as a place of safety from Saul.

Ziklag was about 17.5 kilometers southeast of Gaza. David lived in Ziklag for one year and four months before he went to Hebron after Saul died. These men who came to David at Ziklag were brave soldiers who helped David fight in battle.

Stop here as a group and find the city of Ziklag on the map of Israel. Pause this audio here.

These soldiers could shoot arrows very well from a bow. The bow was an important weapon because soldiers could use it to shoot an arrow at an enemy from a long distance away.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a bow and arrow. Pause this audio here.

A sling was a very deadly weapon that helped soldiers to throw stones very hard and fast at an enemy. You will remember that David used a sling to kill Goliath with a stone when David was a very young man.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a sling. Pause this audio here.

These men were Benjamites. This means the men were from the tribe of Benjamin, which is the same tribe that King Saul came from. It is very surprising that men from Saul's own tribe went to Ziklag to support David instead of supporting King Saul!

Where the storyteller says these men were Benjamites, we are not sure if this means only the men in the list in this passage, or if this means all of the men in the other stories in this chapter. Most likely, the storyteller is talking only about the list of men in this passage.

Most Israelites at this time were right-handed, which means they used their right hand to do most tasks rather than their left hand. Many people knew that there were many left-handed people in the tribe of Benjamin, which was very unusual. However, the storyteller says that these particular Benjamite soldiers could shoot using either their left or their right hand, which would have been extremely unusual even for a Benjamite.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, which tasks do people usually do with their right hand, and which tasks do they do with their left hand? Which hand is easier for you to use for difficult tasks? Who do you know who can use either hand equally well for any task? Pause this audio.

Now the storyteller begins to list each man's name. The first man is Ahiezer, who is the chief leader of the soldiers. Ahiezer and the next man on the list, Joash, are the sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite. This means these men were brothers. It is not clear whether Joash was also a leader, or if only Ahiezer was a leader. A Gibeathite was a way to talk about a person from Gibeah. You will remember that the city of Gibeah was just a short distance north of Jerusalem.

Stop here as a group and find the town of Gibeah on the map of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a list of people in your community and include where each person comes from. Pay attention to how you describe each person. Do you call the person by the name of the place they come from? Or do you give the person's name first and then tell the name of the place they come from? Pause this audio here.

Jeziel and Pelet appear next on the list. These men were the sons of Azmaveth.

Next are Beracah, Jehu the Anathothite, and Ishmaiah the Gibeonite. Today we know that Anathoth was located about 5 kilometers northeast of Jerusalem, and Gibeon was about 8.5 kilometers north of Jerusalem.

Stop here as a group and find Anathoth and Gibeon on the map of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Ishmaiah was a mighty warrior among David's 30 mighty men. The storyteller says Ishmaiah was a leader of "The Thirty," which may mean he led the 30 mighty men for a period of time.

Next are Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, Jozabad the Gederathite, Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite. The storyteller does not give any details about these men except to mention the hometowns of Jozabad and Shephatiah. We know that a Gederathite was a person from a town in southern Judah, and Haruphite may refer to someone from the Calebite clan of Hareph. We are not sure why these men came from cities outside of the land of Benjamin, since most scholars agree that this is a list of Benjamite leaders. It may be that these were Benjamites, or descendants of Benjamin, who just lived in other cities outside of their family lands.

Stop here as a group and find the region of southern Judah on the map of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Next are Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam. These men were all Korahites, which means men from the clan of Korah. However, since this is a list of leaders of the tribe of Benjamin, this is not the clan of Korah from the tribe of Levi—it is a different clan from the tribe of Benjamin.

The last men to appear in this list are Joelah and Zebadiah, the sons of Jeroham from a city called Gedor. Gedor was a city in Judah.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: We learn that this is a list of men who came to David when David was staying at Ziklag because Saul, the son of Kish, chased David away. These men were warriors who helped David fight in battle.

In the second scene: We learn more details about these men. These men could shoot arrows with a bow very well. The men could also shoot arrows or sling stones with either their right or their left hand. These men were from the tribe of Benjamin.

In the third scene: The storyteller gives the name of each man. The storyteller also tells the names of some of the towns where these men came from.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Saul, the son of Kish
  • Ahiezer and Joash, the sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite
  • Jeziel and Pelet, the sons of Azmaveth
  • Beracah, Jehu the Anathothite, and Ishmaiah the Gibeonite
  • Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanon, Jozabad the Gederathite, Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite
  • The men from the clan of Korah, the Korahites: Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam
  • And Joelah and Zebadiah, the sons of Jeroham from Gedor

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller says that these are the men who came to David at Ziklag when David could not move around in the area much because Saul, the son of Kish, chased David away. In the original language, the storyteller emphasizes Saul's actions and how that made it impossible for David to travel around freely during this time.

In the second scene, before the storyteller tells us the names of the men, the storyteller gives some important details about all of these men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story where you first tell your audience what the story will be about, but then you give some background information before you actually begin to tell your story. Pay attention to the words you use to help people understand that you are giving background information first, before you tell the story. Pause this audio here.

First, all of these men had bows as weapons, and the men could shoot arrows from their bows with great skill. You will remember that a bow is a long, narrow piece of wood. People tie a strong string tightly to either end of this piece of wood so that the wood bends into a curved shape. Arrows are long, thin sticks with a pointed tip on one end. People often tie bird feathers onto the other end to help the arrow fly straight through the air. A person can shoot an arrow from the bow by pulling back the string and then letting go of the string quickly so that it pushes the arrow forward and makes it fly through the air very fast and far to hit an enemy.

Stop here as a group and look again at a picture of a bow and arrow, if needed. Pause this audio here.

These men could use either their right hand or their left hand to shoot arrows or to sling stones. You will remember that people could throw stones very hard and fast when they used a sling. People attached leather strings or straps to both sides of a wider leather band or pocket to make a sling. A soldier would take a stone and put it into the leather pocket. Then the soldier would hold both straps tightly and swing the stone in the pocket above his head very quickly. When the soldier let go of one strap, the stone would fly out of the sling at speeds of up to 160 kilometers per hour.

Stop here as a group and look again at a picture of a sling, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Most people are either right-handed or left-handed. This means that the person either prefers to use their right hand or their left hand for tasks that require extra skill or accuracy, like to throw a ball, work with a tool, or write a letter. Although it is extremely unusual, sometimes a person can do even the most difficult tasks equally well with either hand. So the storyteller is saying that all of these men were like this—able to shoot arrows from their bows or throw rocks with their slings using either hand.

All of these men were Benjamites. This probably means that just the men that the storyteller lists in this passage are Benjamites. You will remember that King Saul also came from the tribe of Benjamin.

In the third scene, the storyteller now begins to list each man's name. We do not find the names of these men anywhere else in the Bible.

Next, the storyteller begins to say the names of each of the men. The storyteller does this in a special way. First, the storyteller says the names of some men, and then he includes certain details about those men before he says the names of more men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: As you listen to the list of names in this passage and how the storyteller includes extra information about the men, what is the most natural way for you to list these names and information in your translation? Pause this audio here.

First in this list is Ahiezer, who was the chief, or leader, of these men. Joash was one of these men, too. Ahiezer and Joash were the sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite. A Gibeathite was a person from Gibeah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your community, when you want to talk about how a person came from one town or another, how do you say it? Now, talk about how you will show which towns these men came from in your translation. Will it make more sense to call the men by the name of the town, or will it be more natural to say their name and then say where they are from? For example, would it be better to say, "Shemaah the Gibeathite," or "Shemaah who was from the town of Gibeah"? Pause this audio here.

The second group of men in the list is Jeziel and Pelet, the sons of Azmaveth.

The third group of men in the list is Beracah, Jehu the Anathothite, and Ishmaiah the Gibeonite. An Anathothite was a person from the town of Anathoth, and a Gibeonite was a person from the town of Gibeon. The storyteller also mentions that Ishmaiah was a strong warrior who was among "The Thirty" and who was a leader of "The Thirty." This probably means Ishmaiah led David's 30 mighty men for a period of time. We are not sure why the list of the 30 men in the last passage did not include Ishmaiah, but perhaps Ishmaiah had already died by the time the storyteller put together the previous list.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about an important and powerful person, like a military or political leader. Tell how that person led for a period of time, but then how someone else became the new leader after that person died. Pay attention to how you talk differently about a current leader than you do about someone who was the leader in the past. Pause this audio here.

The fourth group of men in the list is Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, Jozabad the Gederathite, Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite. A Gederathite was a person from the town of Gederah, and a Haruphite may refer to someone from Hareph, although we do not know if this was the name of a town.

The fifth group of men in the list is Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam, who were all Korahites. However, we know this is not the Levite clan of Korah but a Benjamite clan of Korah, because all of the men in this list are from the tribe of Benjamin.

The final group of men in this list is Joelah and Zebadiah. These men are the sons of Jeroham from a town called Gedor.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Saul, the son of Kish
  • Ahiezer and Joash, the sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite
  • Jeziel and Pelet, the sons of Azmaveth
  • Beracah, Jehu the Anathothite, and Ishmaiah the Gibeonite
  • Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanon, Jozabad the Gederathite, Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite
  • The men from the clan of Korah, the Korahites: Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam
  • And Joelah and Zebadiah, the sons of Jeroham from Gedor

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller says something like, "When David went to the town called Ziklag, he could not move around the area much because of Saul, the son of Kish. Many men came to David there. These men were among the brave soldiers who helped David fight in battle."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I felt very sad and discouraged when King Saul chased me away, but I was very grateful that these men came to support me! or
  • These men were some of Israel's very best warriors. I hope people will understand how strong and brave these men were.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller says something like, "These men could shoot arrows very well using their bows. These men could shoot arrows and use slings to throw stones with either their right or their left hand. These men were from the same tribe as Saul, the Benjamin tribe."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the warriors, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Most people do not understand how difficult it is to learn how to shoot arrows and sling stones accurately. It takes a lot of skill and practice! or
  • Loyalty is very important in our culture. People expected us to support King Saul and our own tribe, but we chose to support David instead, because we believed in him!

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller lists each of the men, saying something like:

  • "Their leader Ahiezer and his brother Joash, the sons of Shemaah from Gibeah.
  • "Jeziel and Pelet, the sons of Azmaveth.
  • "Beracah, Jehu from Anathoth town, and Ishmaiah from Gibeon town. Ishmaiah belonged to the special group of 30 brave soldiers, and he was a leader of that group.
  • "Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, and Jozabad. Jozabad was from Gederah.
  • "Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah, who was from Hariph.
  • "Elkanah, Isaiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam, who all descended from a man named Korah.
  • "And, Joelah and Zebadiah, who were the sons of Jeroham from the town called Gedor."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the men in the list, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel honored to have our names and our tribe remembered like this; or
  • People may not remember much about our individual families, but they remember how we fought together for David and Yahweh, and that is what is important; or
  • The story of Yahweh and the powerful things he does are what matter most. We feel honored to be a part of that story!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The men in this passage came to David at Ziklag when David was shut up or restricted because of Saul, the son of Kish. To be shut up or restricted means that David could not travel around freely, but had to stay near Ziklag during this time.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for shut up or restricted. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people had armed these men, or equipped them with bows, which they used to shoot arrows, and with slings, which they used to throw stones. When someone "arms" someone, they give that person weapons to fight with. Use the same word for bow as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use to describe that someone arms the men with slings. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

These men were Saul's kinsmen, family members, or male relatives. Use the same word for relatives or family members as you have used in previous passages.

Ahiezer was the chief or leader of this group of men. To be chief means to be the top leader or head person in charge of a group. Use the same word or phrase for chief or leader as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller describes some of these men as sons of other men. In this passage, a son refers to the male child of his parents. Use the same word for sons when it means male children as you have used previously. Sons is in the Master Glossary.

Ishmaiah was a mighty man among The Thirty. A mighty man means a strong warrior, and here, "The Thirty" refers to David's 30 mighty warriors that the storyteller listed in the last passage. Use the same words or phrases for "mighty men" and "The Thirty" as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 12:8–15
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 12:8–15 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:8–15 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:8–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we learned about the mighty warriors from the Israelite tribe of Benjamin who lived with David in Ziklag and fought alongside David while Saul was still king of Israel. In this story, we will now learn about the warriors from the Israelite tribe of Gad who came to support David even earlier, while David was hiding from Saul in the wilderness, before David went to Ziklag.

This story begins when the storyteller says that some men from the tribe of Gad went over to David at David's stronghold, or protected place, in the wilderness. These were probably some of King Saul's soldiers who became frustrated with Saul and chose to support David instead.

This stronghold was probably somewhere in the wilderness of Judah, or perhaps on the east side of the Jordan River in Moab, but we are not sure where. We know from other stories in the Bible that David had strongholds in several different places in the wilderness.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the regions of Judah and Moab. Pause this audio here.

The wilderness was a dry, rocky place where people usually did not live because there was no water or good soil to grow food. David's stronghold was probably a place high on top of a mountain, among large rocks—a place where the men would be protected from enemies who might want to attack them.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a place on a high mountain in the wilderness that people may have used for a stronghold. Pause this audio here.

Just like the men of Benjamin in the last story, these men from Gad were also mighty warriors with a lot of experience fighting in battles. These men knew how to fight very well using shields and spears.

Stop here and look at a picture of a shield and a picture of a spear as a group. Pause this audio here.

The men's faces were like the faces of lions. A lion was the strongest and most dangerous of all wild animals in Israel, so this is special language that probably means the men's faces looked fierce and that they were very strong warriors.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion as a group. Then discuss this question: Where you live, what kind of animals are the most powerful and dangerous? How do people talk about these animals? Also, how do you talk about people who show strength or fierceness like these animals? Pause this audio here.

The men were also as swift, or able to move fast, as gazelles. Gazelles are a type of antelope that can run fast over the rocks, and these warriors could run fast too.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gazelle as a group. Pause this audio here.

There were 11 of these warriors from Gad. The storyteller now lists these men in order of their level of responsibility: Ezer was first as their chief, or leader. Obadiah was second. Eliab was third. Mishmannah was fourth. Jeremiah was fifth. Attai was sixth. Eliel was seventh. Johanan was eighth. Elzabad was ninth. Another Jeremiah was tenth. And Macbannai was eleventh.

All of these men from the tribe of Gad were officers, or leaders, of the military men. Even the weakest of these men could fight against 100, and the strongest of these men could fight against 1,000.

It was these men who once bravely crossed over the Jordan River during the first month of the year, which is the time when the river overflows its banks, or is very deep and wide because of all the water. It would have been very dangerous to cross the river during this time. Then these men fought the people in the valleys there, and those people had to run away. Those people ran to the east and to the west.

Stop here and look at a picture of a river when it is very full of water and overflows its banks.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:8–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: We learn about the mighty men from Gad who joined David at his stronghold in the wilderness before David went to Ziklag. These fierce warriors were experts at fighting with shields and spears, and each man could run very fast. The storyteller gives the name of each of these warriors.

In the second scene: We learn that even the weakest of these warriors could fight 100 enemy soldiers, and the strongest could fight 1,000. Then the storyteller tells about a time when these men crossed over the Jordan River when the water was very high. These men attacked the people who lived in the valley there so that the people ran away to the east and to the west to escape.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Gadite warriors named Ezer, Obadiah, Eliab, Mishmannah, Jeremiah, Attai, Eliel, Johanan, Elzabad, another Jeremiah, and Macbannai
  • And the people in the valley on the other side of the Jordan River who ran away from these Gadite warriors

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, we learn about the Gadites, or men from the tribe of Gad, who went to David at David's stronghold in the wilderness. In the original language, "wilderness stronghold" means the top of a mountain where there were caves and large rocks that would protect people from enemies. This would be a place where it would be very difficult for David's enemies to come up and attack David and his men. It is also likely that David and his men could find protection behind rocks if they did have to fight.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Where you live, what do soldiers use for strongholds, or places to protect them from enemies? What natural places do soldiers use for protection, like rocks or mountains, or what types of structures do they build for protection? How do you talk about these places and the way they help to protect the soldiers? Pause this audio here.

These Gadite men were experienced warriors who could fight very well with shields and spears. A shield is a large, flat object that soldiers would carry to protect themselves from their enemies. People usually made shields with a wooden frame and stretched leather over the frame. A soldier would hold this shield in front of him as he went into battle to help protect against arrows, stones, spears, and other enemy attacks.

Stop here and look at a picture of a shield as a group. Pause this audio here.

A spear is a weapon that people made by attaching a sharp blade to the end of a long stick. Soldiers could use a spear to fight up close by thrusting the spear at their enemy. Or soldiers could also throw a spear at an enemy farther away.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says the faces of these men were like the faces of lions. This is special language that probably means the men could make their faces look very fierce, and that the men were very strong and dangerous fighters, just like a lion is dangerous to its enemies. In ancient Israel, people feared lions more than any other wild animal because lions were such powerful and dangerous animals.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion again as a group, if needed. Then discuss this question: How will you talk about these men in your translation so that people will understand how the men were fierce-looking and strong like lions? If needed, think about your discussion in step two about people who are fierce and strong like strong animals. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also says the men were as swift as gazelles on the mountains. This means the men could run extremely fast, even over very rocky ground. Gazelles are a type of antelope that are about a meter long and 60 centimeters tall. These animals can run extremely fast over the rocks in the mountains of the wilderness, so the storyteller says these warriors could do the same thing.

Stop here and again look at a picture of a gazelle as a group, if needed. Then discuss this question: In your translation, how will you talk about these men to show how they could run very fast over the rocky mountain tops like gazelles can run? Pause this audio here.

Next, the storyteller lists the names of each one of these warriors. The list includes 11 Gadite men altogether. The Bible does not talk about these particular men anywhere else in the Old Testament, although some of these men have the same name as some other people in the Bible.

Ezer was the first warrior. Ezer was their chief, or the head man who led the rest of these men. Then the storyteller lists the men in order of their rank. Obadiah was second in command. Eliab was third. Mishmannah was fourth. Jeremiah was fifth. Attai was sixth. Eliel was seventh. Johanan was eighth. Elzabad was ninth. Another Jeremiah was tenth. And Macbannai was eleventh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is a good way to list these names in your translation? Think about the way the storyteller says not just the men's names, but also their order of responsibility. Here are some examples of how you might do this:

  • Ezer was their leader. Obadiah was second. Eliab was third....; or
  • First was Ezer, and he was their leader. Next was Obadiah, and he was second in command. Then came Eliab, who was third....; or
  • Ezer the first, Obadiah the second, Eliab the third....

Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that all of these Gadite men were officers, or leaders, of the soldiers. We are not certain what the original language means when the storyteller says, "the lesser over a hundred and the greater over a thousand." Some people think this means that the most important of these men led 1,000 Israelite soldiers, while the least important led 100 soldiers. However, most scholars think this means that the most powerful of these men could fight against 1,000 enemies, while even the weakest of these men could fight against 100. In other words, all of these men were very strong! So it is best to follow this interpretation in your translation too.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part to show that the strongest men in this group can fight 1,000 enemies, and even the weakest of these men can fight 100? Pause this audio here.

Finally, we hear a short story about a particular battle that these men fought in the past. The storyteller says the men crossed the Jordan River in the first month. The Israelites counted the first month of the year as the time we call March or April. This was the time of year when snow that had fallen on Mount Herman during the winter months began to melt, and also when it rained more often. You will remember that a river is a body of water that flows downhill, usually from a mountain or lake, or perhaps a sea. Usually, over time, the water cuts a deep path into the ground, which we call a "river bed," so that it leaves the higher ground on either side of the river dry. This higher ground on each side of the river we call a "river bank." However, sometimes when a large amount of extra water suddenly flows into the riverbed, like when it rains heavily, or when a lot of snow melts in the mountains very quickly, all of the extra water cannot fit down into the riverbed at once, so the water spills out onto the ground on either side. This makes the river much wider and deeper. This is what the storyteller means when he says that the Jordan River overflowed its banks. And so people usually could not cross the Jordan River at this time of year, because it was too dangerous. However, the storyteller says the men did cross the Jordan. Then the men attacked the people who lived in the valleys, or the lowlands between the hills near the riverbanks.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What happens when there is a lot of water where you live, either from rain or from snow that melts? Where does all the water go, and how does this affect where people can travel? How do you talk about this time of year? Pause this audio here.

We are not certain what the original language means when the storyteller says that the men "put to flight all those in the valleys, to the east and to the west." Most scholars think this means that the Gadite warriors attacked the people who lived in the valleys, and so these people ran away in different directions on both sides of the Jordan River. However, some people instead believe this means that the men attacked at the same time that the river flooded these valleys, and so the floodwaters from the river forced the people to run away to the east and to the west at the same time these men attacked. It is probably best in your translation to follow what most scholars think, so that the men attacked the people who lived in the valleys, and so that the people ran away on both sides of the Jordan River. You will remember that "east" is the direction that the sun rises, and "west" is the direction that the sun sets. The Jordan River flows north to south, so either side of the river is east and west.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:8–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Gadite warriors named Ezer, Obadiah, Eliab, Mishmannah, Jeremiah, Attai, Eliel, Johanan, Elzabad, another Jeremiah, and Macbannai
  • And the people in the valley on the other side of the Jordan River who ran away from these Gadite warriors

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller says something like, "Some soldiers from Gad's tribe went to join David's army at David's strong place in the desert. These men were brave warriors who knew how to fight very well using shields and spears. These men could fight like strong, fierce lions. These men could also run very fast like a deer that runs across the mountains. The first man was Ezer, their leader. The second in command was Obadiah. Eliab was third. Mishmannah was fourth. Jeremiah was fifth. Attai was sixth. Eliel was seventh. Johanan was eighth. Elzabad was ninth. Another Jeremiah was tenth. And Macbannai was eleventh."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel so grateful that these strong warriors came to support me when Saul turned against me; or
