  • These men are some of the toughest warriors I know. I had to fight lions when I was a shepherd, so I know that these men truly are as fierce and strong as those lions! or
  • These men would have been more comfortable living with King Saul in the city. I am very grateful that these men were willing to give that up to come fight with me.

Ask the men playing the warriors from the tribe of Gad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We did not want to dishonor King Saul, but we wanted to support David because we believed that Yahweh was with David; or
  • It was difficult to live in the wilderness, but we were strong warriors and knew how to survive and fight very well there; or
  • David's stronghold in the rocks was not comfortable, but it was very strong and easy to defend from our enemies. David chose a wise place to stay.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller says something like, "These men from the tribe of Gad were leaders in the army. Even the weakest of these men was as powerful as 100 normal soldiers, and the strongest of the men could fight against 1,000 men. It was these men who went across the Jordan River at the first of the year when the river filled so full of water that it overflowed. And these men fought the people who lived in the valleys so that the people ran away to both sides of the river."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the warriors from Gad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We were some of the very best warriors in Israel. We were not afraid of anything! or
  • Most people could not cross the river when it was flooded. But that is why it was such a good time for us to attack, because no one expected it!

Ask the people who are playing the people in the valleys, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We were startled and terrified! We had no idea where this army of warriors had come from so suddenly; or
  • Normally, we did not cross the river at this time of year, because we were afraid we might drown. But we were so afraid of these warriors that some of us did not hesitate but ran and crossed the river to get away.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:8–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this passage, we learn about the Gadites who went to David at David's stronghold in the wilderness. You will remember that Gadites are the tribe of people who descended from Gad, one of Jacob's 12 sons. Use the same word or phrase for Gadites as you used in previous passages.

A stronghold is a fortified, secure place that soldiers use for refuge or as a center of defense during battle. A stronghold might be something people build, like a high, strong wall. But here, David's stronghold was probably a natural place in the rocks, on a high mountain, where people could not easily crawl up, but where the men could fight back from behind the protection of the large rocks. Use the same word or phrase for a natural stronghold as you used in previous passages. For more information on stronghold, refer to the Master Glossary.

Wilderness is a dry, barren place without water and usually with very few bushes or trees. The wilderness around Israel is very rocky and mountainous in some places, and this was the kind of place where David had his stronghold. Use the same word or phrase for wilderness as you used in previous passages. For more information on wilderness, refer to the Master Glossary.

The men could handle a shield and a spear. Use the same words or phrases for shield and spear as you have used previously.

The storyteller says that these men's faces were like the faces of lions. This is special language that probably means these men looked very fierce and were very strong, so that other people would be afraid to fight these men. A lion is a very large, wild cat. Lions were one of the strongest animals in ancient Israel, and people were more afraid of lions than any other animal. Lions would often attack and eat other large animals, like sheep. Most people were afraid of lions. Use the same word or phrase for lion as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller also says that these men were as swift as gazelles upon the mountains. To be swift means to move very quickly or to run very, very fast. A gazelle is a type of antelope that lived in ancient Israel and could run and jump across the rocks on the mountains very quickly without losing its footing. People who can run fast on flat ground usually cannot move fast over the rocks in these mountains, but the storyteller says that these men could, just like the gazelles that live in this place.

Stop here as a group and again look at a picture of a gazelle, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for gazelle. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that Ezer was the chief of these 11 men. To be chief means to be the top leader or head person in charge of a group. However, the storyteller uses the same word to say that all of these Gadite men were chiefs, or leaders, of the army. So this means that Ezer was in charge of the other 10 men in this list, but all 11 of the men were also in charge of other soldiers. Use the same words or phrases for chief or military leader that you used in previous passages.

It was this group of men who crossed over the Jordan River when the river was overflowing its banks. Remember that a river that overflows its banks becomes much wider and deeper than normal.

Stop here as a group and again look at a picture of a river overflowing its banks, if needed. Then discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "overflowing its banks." If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

These Gadite warriors put to flight all the people in the valleys. To put people to flight means to chase those people or to make them run away. Use the same word or phrase for "put to flight" or chase as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 12:16–18
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 12:16–18 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:16–18 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:16–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we learned about the warriors from the tribe of Gad who joined David at his stronghold in the wilderness. Now, in this story, we will learn about men from the tribes of Benjamin and Judah who also came to David during this time. We do not know if this story happened before or after the last story about the men of Gad. But we do know that it happened when David was still at his wilderness stronghold, before David went to Ziklag. You will remember from the previous passage that this stronghold was a place in the mountains with many large rocks where David and his men could protect themselves from their enemies. You will also remember that the wilderness is a very dry place without water and with very few plants or trees. Because of this, people usually do not live in the wilderness.

Stop here as a group and look again at a picture of a place on a high mountain in the wilderness that people may have used for a stronghold. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller of Chronicles would have known that the tribes of Judah and Benjamin stayed together even when the nation of Israel later split into two kingdoms. That may be why the storyteller talks about these two tribes together throughout the book of Chronicles. Most Israelites of the time thought of the tribes of Judah and Benjamin together, so it would have made sense that these men came to David together at the same time.

Stop here as a group and look at a map that shows where the tribes of Judah and Benjamin lived. Pause this audio here.

When these men arrived at David's stronghold, David came out of the stronghold to meet the men and speak to them. David told the men that if they had come as friends to help David, then David welcomed the men to stay. But if the men had come to help David's enemies kill David, even though David had done nothing wrong, then David prayed that God would punish these men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Act out a story about a time when someone came from far away to visit your community. Show how, at first, you were not sure if you could trust this person as a friend, or if the person might be an enemy. Act out how you talked to this person. How did you greet this person in a polite but cautious way? Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller mentions that Amasai, "chief of The Thirty," spoke to David. We do not hear the name Amasai anywhere else in the Bible, so we are not certain who Amasai was. Some scholars believe this is a different name for Abigail's son, whom Absalom made captain in Joab's place earlier in the book of 1 Chronicles. Others think Amasai might be another name for the son of Zeruiah, who we also heard about earlier in 1 Chronicles, but this seems unlikely. Whoever Amasai was, the storyteller shows that Amasai later became an important leader of David's 30 mighty men. You will remember from a previous story how we learned about the 30 men who fought for David. In that story, the storyteller said that a different man named Abishai was chief of those 30 men. So, while Amasai was not the leader of these men at the time of this story, it seems Amasai became their leader at a later time.

The storyteller says that God's Spirit came upon Amasai, or took control of Amasai, and gave him power and words from God to speak. Amasai said that he and the men would serve David, Jesse's son, because they had come to help David. Then Amasai asked God to bless David, because Amasai knew that God was with David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time in your community when God or a spirit took control of a person. What did the person do or say? How did other people respond? Pause this audio here.

Then David accepted these men as his friends and made them officers in David's army.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:16–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Some men from the tribes of Benjamin and Judah come to David's stronghold in the wilderness. David goes out to meet the men. David tells the men that if they have come as friends, the men are welcome to stay. But if the men have come to help David's enemies, even though David has done nothing wrong, then David prays that God will punish these men.

In the second scene: God's spirit takes control of Amasai, who later became the leader of David's 30 warriors. Amasai says that he and the other men will serve David, the son of Jesse, because they have come to help David. Amasai also asks God to bless David, because Amasai and these men know that God is with David.

In the third scene: David accepts these men as his friends, and David makes the men leaders in his army of soldiers.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Men from the tribes of Benjamin and Judah
  • David
  • God
  • God's Spirit
  • And Amasai

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller starts with a word in the original language that some people translate as "one time." We do not know exactly when this time was, but we do know that it was while David was still staying at his stronghold in the wilderness.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a real thing that happened in the past without saying exactly when it happened. As you tell the story, pay attention to the words you use to talk about time in a general way rather than using specific times. Pause this audio here.

As the story begins, some descendants of Benjamin and Judah come to David's stronghold. You will remember that King Saul was from the tribe of Benjamin. You will also remember that Saul wanted to kill David. So it is very surprising that these men would come to help David. However, the storyteller does not mention that the men were disloyal to King Saul.

You will also remember from a previous story how warriors from the tribe of Benjamin came to David when David was at Ziklag. Although those men were also Benjamites, that story happened after the time this story happened. We know this because this story happens when David is still in the wilderness, before David goes to Ziklag.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of two different stories that are connected in some way. For example, maybe these two stories are about the same person, or maybe they happened in the same place. Now tell the two stories, but tell them in the opposite order of how they happened. As you tell the stories, pay attention to how you talk about when the stories happened. For example, when you tell the second story, how do you show that it happened before the first story happened? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that David now goes out to face these men. This means that David comes close to the men and stands so that when the men look right in front of them, they see David, who stands there and looks at their faces. David says, "If you have come in peace to help me, I am ready for you to join me." To come in peace means to be friends with a person and not harm that person.

David continues, "But if you come to betray me to my enemies, when my hands are free from violence, then may the God of our ancestors see it and judge you." The phrase "my hands are free from violence" is special language that means that David has not hurt anyone physically. You will remember in previous stories how David chose not to kill King Saul, even when David could have done so very easily. So David is reminding these men of this.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Act out a story about a group of people who get angry and accuse someone of doing a bad thing even though that person has not done that thing. Have the person talk to the angry group and explain how they are not guilty. Pay attention to what words the person uses to describe how they are innocent. Pause this audio here.

When David talks about "the God of our ancestors," David means the one true God, Yahweh, whom all of the Israelites follow. So David is reminding the men that if the men pretend to be friends, but then they hurt David, the one true God will see not only that David is innocent, but also that these men have harmed David, and God will punish the men for it. In other words, David wants the men to be afraid to harm David because they fear God, even if they are not afraid of David himself.

As David stops speaking, God's spirit suddenly "clothes" Amasai, or comes on Amasai. This is special language that means that God literally took control of Amasai's whole body and made Amasai speak. Since this is the first time the storyteller has mentioned Amasai's name, he says here that this is the Amasai who later became the chief leader of The Thirty, or the special group of warriors who led all of David's soldiers.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your translation, how will you talk about how God took control of Amasai and made Amasai speak these words? If needed, look back at the conversation you had in step two to see how you talked about this. Pause this audio here.

Amasai says, "We are yours, David! We are with you, son of Jesse!" Where Amasai says "we," Amasai means all of the men of Benjamin and Judah who had come to David. You will remember that David's father is Jesse. In ancient Israel, people used the father's name to show which family a person came from, much like many people use surnames today.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you name people so that you know what family or tribe they belong to? What will be the best way to translate "David, son of Jesse" in your language so that people will understand what it means? Pause this audio here.

Amasai continues, "Peace, peace to you, and peace to your helpers!" In the original language, the word peace means more than just the absence of war. Peace means that people will live well and be prosperous. So Amasai asks God to make David and David's men successful.

Finally, Amasai says, "For your God helps you." In the original language, the words Amasai uses here show that Amasai knows that God has helped David in the past and also that God continues to help David.

Finally, David receives these men as his friends, and David makes the men officers, or leaders, of his fighting men.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:16–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Men from the tribes of Benjamin and Judah
  • David
  • God
  • God's Spirit
  • And Amasai

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller says something like, "One time, other men from the tribes of Benjamin and Judah also came to David at the stronghold. David went out to meet these men, and David said, 'If you have come here to help me, we can be friends. But if you have come to tell my enemies where I am hiding, even though I have not done anything wrong, I pray that God will punish you.'"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope these men want to be my friends, because I need strong warriors to fight with me; or
  • I am not sure if I can trust these men. I thought Saul was my friend, but then Saul tried to kill me even though I fought to protect Saul and his kingdom. So maybe these men want to kill me too; or
  • I know that these men could lie to me, but God will know if they are lying. So I will ask God to protect me.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller says something like, "Amasai, who later became the leader of the 30 warriors, was one of these men who came to David. God's Spirit took control of Amasai, and he said, 'We will serve you, David, Jesse's son. We have come to help you! May God bless you and give you success, and may God bless those who help you!'"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Amasai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • That was amazing! I did not know what I was going to say to David, but suddenly I felt this power come over me, and it gave me just the right words to say! or
  • I was afraid David might think I was lying if I said we were his friends. But I think David could tell that God was speaking through me and that God sent us to help David.

Ask the people playing the men from Benjamin and Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We were not sure what to say to David, but what Amasai said was perfect! or
  • We came to David because we believed that God was with David. And now that we have seen how God gave Amasai these words to say, we feel even more confident that God is blessing David.

Continue the drama.

Finally, David accepts these men as his friends, and David makes the men leaders in his army of soldiers.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Amasai and the rest of the men from Benjamin and Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel so happy that David decided to trust us and to let us stay and fight in his army; or
  • We feel honored that David chose us to be leaders over his men. We will be brave and strong leaders, and because God is on David's side, we know we will be successful.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:16–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this passage, we learn about men, or the "sons" of Benjamin and of Judah, who come to David's stronghold. You will remember that Benjamin and Judah were two of Jacob's 12 sons. Sons of Benjamin means descendants of Jacob's son Benjamin, and sons of Judah means descendants of Jacob's son Judah. Use the same word or phrase for "sons" when it means "descendants" that you used in previous passages. For more information, look up descendants in the Master Glossary.

A stronghold is a fortified, secure place that soldiers use for refuge or as a center of defense during battle. In this case, the stronghold is natural, probably in caves and rocks in the hills. Use the same word or phrase for natural stronghold as you used in previous passages. For more information on stronghold, refer to the Master Glossary.

David says that if the men came peacefully or to be friends with David, then David's heart will be united with the men. In the Bible, the heart is special language for the place inside a person where that person's thoughts, feelings, and decisions happen. To unite means to join or to come together as one. Remember that the idea of peace is more than just the absence of war. When someone has peace with someone else, they live in harmony, as friends, and are content with each other, with God, and with things around them.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for peace and "heart would be united." Look up heart and peace in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David also talks about what will happen if the men betray David to his adversaries. To betray someone means to be disloyal to them or act unfaithfully. For example, you might betray someone if you tell that person's secret to other people or you act in a way that harms or deceives that person. In the original language, the word for adversaries here means the kind of enemies who oppress David and cause him great emotional stress or anguish.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for betray and adversaries. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David says that if these men try to hurt David, then David asks the God of their fathers to rebuke the men. Here, fathers refers not to their literal fathers, but to the ancestors of David and these men who came many generations before them. Use the same word or phrase for fathers, when fathers means ancestors, as you used in previous passages.

In the original language, the idea of rebuke means both to decide and to punish or condemn, much like a judge must first decide if a person is guilty of a crime, and then the judge must decide what punishment to give the person for their crime.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for rebuke. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says the Spirit clothed Amasai, the chief or leader of The Thirty. Translate "chief or leader," and "The Thirty," the same way you have in previous passages. "The Thirty" refers to the 30 mighty men who fought for David. Spirit means the spirit of God. The Bible often used special language to describe God's Spirit as like a wind or breath of air that blows over a person. The word clothed is also special language. It means to dress or to cover like clothes cover a person's body. The way the storyteller uses these two words together is similar to other passages in the Bible that describe how God's Spirit took control of a person so that the person prophesied, or spoke words from God. The New Testament describes God's Spirit as the Holy Spirit, which God gives to all believers in Jesus for all time. But in the Old Testament, God's Spirit only came on a few people, and only for a certain period of time. People do not translate "Spirit" in the Old Testament the same way they translate "Holy Spirit" in the New Testament. However, you can refer to Holy Spirit in the Master Glossary to learn more about the work of God's Spirit in the Old Testament.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for "the Spirit clothed Amasai." If you have already translated this idea in another book of the Old Testament, use the same phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The passage ends when the storyteller says that David received the men and made them leaders, or officers of David's raiding bands. The word for leaders here is the same word as that for chiefs previously, but these kinds of chiefs are leaders in charge of other fighting men. Use the same word or phrase for officers or leaders as you used in previous passages. Raiding bands are fighting men who attack to steal what they want, like food and weapons, and to create fear and chaos among the people they attack.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for raiding bands. If you have already translated this idea in another book of the Old Testament, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 12:19–22
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 12:19–22 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:19–22 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:19–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we learned about men from the tribes of Benjamin and Judah who came to David at his stronghold in the wilderness. There, David and Amasai spoke to each other. Amasai blessed David and said that Amasai and the men with Amasai would serve David. The story ended when the storyteller said that David made these men leaders over David's raiding bands.

In this story, we will now hear how men from the tribe of Manasseh also joined David when David lived at Ziklag. David went to live in Ziklag after the events in the last story.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel. Find the city of Ziklag again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

This story begins when some men from the tribe of Manasseh left Saul's army and went over to join David's army instead. This happened at the time when David went with the Philistines to the battle against Saul. We know from 1 Samuel 29, which also tells this story, that David went to this battle with King Achish, a king of the Philistines. You will remember that David and his men first hid from Saul at a stronghold in the wilderness, but then later David found protection with King Achish, who gave David the city of Ziklag to live in. Because of this, King Achish probably expected David to help him fight in this battle.

However, David and his men did not help the Philistines fight this battle after all. When David and his men arrived at the battlefield, the other leaders of the Philistines said to each other that if they allowed David to fight alongside them, David might switch loyalties and begin to fight for Saul against the Philistines as a way to make Saul happy with David again. If David did this, then Saul and David together would be able to win the battle and kill the Philistines. So King Achish sent David and David's men back home to Ziklag.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Tell a story about a game where two teams of people compete, like soccer or another physical sport. At first, both teams have the same number of skilled players, so neither team is able to easily win. Then tell how some players from one team suddenly switch to the other team so that the bigger and stronger team quickly wins the game. Pause this audio here.

It was while David and his men returned to Ziklag that these men from Manasseh left Saul's army and came to join David and his men. The names of the leaders of these men from the tribe of Manasseh were Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, another Jozabad, Elihu, and Zillethai. Each one of these men was the leader of 1,000 soldiers.

In the original language, we are not sure what the next part of the story means. The storyteller may say that these soldiers of Manasseh helped fight a group of raiders. This would most likely refer to the story in 1 Samuel 30, which tells how the Amalekites burned Ziklag while David and his men were away. Or the storyteller may say that these soldiers from Manasseh helped David when they became leaders over David's own raiders, or troops. Either way, the storyteller says that these men were brave warriors and that eventually they became commanders in David's army.

Finally, the storyteller says that day after day, more and more men came to help David until David had a very great army, like the army of God. This does not mean that David's army had more men than Saul's. Instead, it means that David's army was very powerful because God was with them.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:19–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: We learn about men from the tribe of Manasseh who left Saul and joined David's army. This happened when David went with the Philistines to fight in the battle against Saul. But David and his men did not fight in this battle. Instead, when David arrived, the Philistine leaders said, "David may turn against us and instead fight for Saul. And if he does, they will kill us all!" So they sent David back home to Ziklag.

In the second scene: We learn the names of the men of Manasseh who left Saul and joined David while David returned to Ziklag. These men helped David and his soldiers. These men were brave warriors and became leaders in David's army.

In the third scene: The storyteller says that each day after this story happened, again and again, more men came to help David. So David had a large and powerful army that God made.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Men from the tribe of Manasseh
  • David
  • The Philistines
  • Saul
  • The leaders of the Philistines
  • The leaders of the men from Manasseh named Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, another Jozabad, Elihu, and Zillethai
  • And God

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller says that some men from the tribe of Manasseh defected to David. In the original language, this literally means that the men "fell to" David. Although the storyteller does not specifically mention Saul's name here, we know that this means the men deserted Saul, or stopped being loyal to Saul, and instead left and became loyal to David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about someone who leaves the group of people they are a part of and instead becomes loyal to an enemy group? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says these men defected to David at the time when David went with the Philistines to fight against Saul in battle. That story, which we hear about in 1 Samuel chapters 27 through 29, happened at an earlier time when David and his men lived at Ziklag. "But," the storyteller adds, "David and his men did not help the Philistines." This is because the rulers of the Philistines talked together and decided it was too dangerous to allow David to fight with them. In the original language, the Philistine rulers literally said to one another, "For our heads, he will fall away to his master Saul." This means that to try to make Saul happy with David, during the battle, David might make his men turn and fight against the Philistines instead of against Saul. Then Saul might easily win the battle and kill the Philistines. People often cut off the heads of their enemies to show they had won a battle, just like David once cut off Goliath's head. This is probably why the Philistine rulers said David might do this "for our heads."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about something that might happen, but you are not sure if it will? How do you talk about the consequences if that thing does happen? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, as David and his men return to Ziklag, the men who defect and come to join David are Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, another man with the same name Jozabad, Elihu, and Zillethai. We do not hear these men's names anywhere else in the Bible, so we do not know anything more about these men except that they each command 1,000 soldiers from the tribe of Manasseh. Since there are two men with the same name, Jozabad, in this story, you may wish to say that in your translation.

The storyteller says these men from the tribe of Manasseh helped David. We are not sure if the original language says the men helped David "against" the raiding bands, or "over" the raiding bands. If the word means "against," this may refer to how these men helped David and his men fight the Amalekites who raided Ziklag, which we hear about in a story in 1 Samuel 30. But if it means "over," this may refer to how these men helped David by later serving as leaders over David's soldiers, who often raided other towns themselves.

In the third scene, the storyteller has finished his story about the men of Manasseh. But the storyteller wants to say one more important thing about all of the stories he has told in this chapter about the men who have come to David. The storyteller says that day after day, men continued to come to help David. This does not mean that the same men came again and again, but rather that new men continued to come each day so that David's army grew bigger and bigger.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you say this in your translation so it will be clear that different men came each day and not the same men over and over again? Pause this audio here.

Finally, the storyteller says that David's army became very great, "like an army of God." In the original language, these words show not only that David had many soldiers, but more importantly, that God was the one who brought these men together and that the men were strong because God gave them God's power.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the story to make it clear that it was God who made David's army strong? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:19–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Men from the tribe of Manasseh
  • David
  • The Philistines
  • Saul
  • The leaders of the Philistines
  • The leaders of the men from Manasseh named Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, another Jozabad, Elihu, and Zillethai
  • And God

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller says, "Some men from Manasseh's tribe also joined David's army. These men came to David when David went with the Philistines to fight against Saul. But David and his men actually did not help the Philistines fight Saul. Instead, the Philistine leaders discussed it and decided to send David away. The leaders did this because they were afraid David might betray them during the battle and help Saul fight and kill the Philistines instead."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not want to fight against Saul, but I feel obligated to go with King Achish to the battle because he was kind to me and gave me Ziklag to live in; or
  • I was always loyal to Saul, but Saul has put me in a difficult situation here! or
  • I know God does not want me to kill King Saul. Maybe God will make a way so that my men and I do not have to fight in the battle.

Ask the people playing the Philistine leaders, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • David seemed very loyal to Saul before Saul chased David away. How can we be sure David does not still want to help Saul? or
  • David's army is not very big, but they are very strong. We are afraid of what David's army could do if they turned against us; or
  • We still remember when David's God helped David kill Goliath. We are not sure we can trust David and his God.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller says, "So David returned to Ziklag, and these men from Manasseh went with David. The men were Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, another man whose name was also Jozabad, Elihu, and Zillethai. Each of these men was the leader of 1,000 soldiers, and all of those soldiers belonged to the tribe called Manasseh. They helped David fight against bad men who went around the country stealing things from people. All of these men were brave soldiers, and they became officers in David's army."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the men from the tribe of Manasseh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We do not want to be disloyal to Saul, but we see that God is with David, and we want to be on God's side; or
  • David follows God's laws, but Saul is trying to murder David. If we must fight, we must fight for what is right.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am grateful to God for sending these brave and strong men to help me! or
  • I can see that these men are very experienced in battle. Maybe one day I will make these men leaders over the rest of my soldiers.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller says, "Every day, more men came to help David until his army became large and powerful, like an army of God."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • For a time, I felt like God had abandoned me. But now I can see that God is taking care of everything; or
  • We may not have as many men as Saul, but God sent me some of the best and bravest soldiers in Israel.

Ask the person playing Saul, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I tried to kill David because I felt threatened by David's power. But now my men are leaving me and going to David, so things are even worse than before!

Ask the person playing God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased with David for trying to do the right thing even when Saul tried to murder David; or
  • I will send more men to support David so that David will know I am with him; or
  • I will give David's soldiers my power and protection, and I will help them succeed in their battles, because I am pleased with David.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:19–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The story begins when the storyteller says that some men from the tribe of Manasseh defected, or deserted, to David. In the original language, this word literally means the men "fell to" David. Although the storyteller does not specifically mention Saul's name here, the idea is that these men quit their loyalty to Saul in order to go help David and his army instead.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for defected, or deserted. Pause this audio here.

The Philistine rulers took counsel or consulted together about David. You will remember that a ruler means a lord, or someone who has authority over other people. Use the same word or phrase for ruler as you used in previous passages. In the Bible, when people took counsel, it meant that they came close together and discussed or debated something before they made a final decision about it.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for took counsel. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The Philistine rulers were afraid that David would fight for his lord, or master, Saul. A lord is a person who is a master over someone else and has authority over them. People often call their kings or gods "lord."

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for lord. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about lord. Pause this audio here.

The men from the tribe of Manasseh helped David fight against a raiding band. You will remember that a raiding band is a group of people who attack without good reason in order to steal things or capture people. These people often attack suddenly and violently when people are not expecting an enemy, and then they move on very quickly before people can organize and fight back. Use the same word or phrase for "raiding band" as you used in previous passages.

These men fought very well because they were mighty men of valor. This means they were very strong and brave. Use the same word or phrase for "strong and brave men" as you used in previous passages.

David eventually made these men commanders in his army. Commanders are the same thing as chiefs, or leaders over groups of soldiers. Use the same word or phrase for military commanders or leaders as you used in previous passages.

Day after day, men continued to come help David until there was a great army, like an army of God. Here, "army of God" means it was not only a large army, but also that God made this army and gave the men their strength.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 12:23–40
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 12:23–40 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:23–40 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:23–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we learned about men from the tribe of Manasseh who left Saul and joined David's army. That story happened while David and his men were away from Ziklag during the battle between Saul and the Philistines. The storyteller also completed the last several stories by saying that day after day, new people continued to come to help David until David had a very great army.

In this story, we now hear a complete list of the armies that came to David at Hebron. You will remember that David went to Hebron after he lived in Ziklag, so this story happened after the last story. The storyteller begins when he says that these are the numbers of the soldiers who came to join David's army at Hebron. These soldiers came prepared to fight because they wanted to make David the new king after Saul. The storyteller reminds us that Yahweh had promised these things would happen.

Now we hear the list of soldiers, in groups, of each tribe of Israel. The storyteller gives these in order of where the tribes live, going from the south of Israel to the north.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the area where each tribe lived. Also find the city of Hebron. Pause this audio here.

From the tribe of Judah, 6,800 soldiers came to David at Hebron. These soldiers carried shields and spears.

From the tribe of Simeon, 7,100 men came to Hebron. The storyteller calls these men brave warriors.

From the tribe of Levi, 4,600 men came to Hebron. This group of men included Jehoiada, a prince or leader of Aaron's clan who led 3,700 men. The group also included Zadok, a brave young warrior who came with 22 military commanders from his family group.

From the tribe of Benjamin, 3,000 men came to Hebron. Here, the storyteller reminds us that the tribe of Benjamin was the tribe Saul came from, and most of this tribe had stayed loyal to Saul until this time.

From the tribe of Ephraim, 20,800 men came to Hebron. The storyteller calls these men brave warriors and says that people from these men's clans respected these men very much.

From the half-tribe of Manasseh, 18,000 men came to Hebron. The storyteller mentions that the clans that these men came from specifically chose these men to go and help make David king. Also, you will remember that the tribe of Manasseh was divided into two parts, or "half-tribes." This half of Manasseh was the part that lived on the west side of the Jordan River.

Stop here as a group and again look at a map of Israel that shows the areas where each tribe lived. Find the west side of the Jordan River where this half of the tribe of Manasseh lived. Pause this audio here.

From the tribe of Issachar, 200 chiefs, or military leaders, came with all of their men. The storyteller does not give the entire number of soldiers in this group. However, the storyteller does say that these were wise men who understood the times and knew what Israel should do. We are not sure exactly what the storyteller means by this, but he probably means that these 200 men were wise political leaders and that other people listened to these men and followed what they said.

From the tribe of Zebulun, 50,000 men came to Hebron. These men had a lot of experience fighting in battle, and they came armed with all sorts of weapons. These men came to help David and to obey him completely.

From the tribe of Naphtali, 1,000 military commanders came with 37,000 men who carried shields and spears.

From the tribe of Dan, 28,600 men to Hebron.

From the tribe of Asher, 40,000 men came to Hebron. The storyteller calls these men experienced soldiers prepared for battle.

Finally, the storyteller tells us that 120,000 soldiers came from the east side of the Jordan River to Hebron. These men were from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the other half-tribe of Manasseh. These men came armed with every type of weapon. This shows that the men were a very powerful army.

Stop here as a group and find where the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and this half of the tribe of Manasseh lived on the east side of the Jordan River. Pause this audio here.

All of the men in this list were soldiers ready to fight in battle. All of these men came to Hebron because they wanted to make David king over all Israel. And all of the people of Israel agreed that David should become king.

All of the men spent three days there in Hebron with David, where they ate and drank during a celebration feast. The storyteller says that these men's brothers prepared all of the food for them. Their "brothers" were probably the people from the tribe of Judah who lived in Hebron. But it was people from all of the tribes of Israel who brought all of this food. People even came from as far as away as the tribes of Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali. The storyteller probably mentions these specific tribes because they lived in the far north of Israel and had to come the farthest. This shows just how much people wanted David to be king.

Stop here and look again at a map of Israel as a group. Find where Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali lived and how far away that was from Hebron. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about an event or celebration in your community where people came from other places to attend. Now tell a story about an event that was so unusual and special that people from very far away came to this event even though they did not come to other events because of the great distance. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says how all of these people brought all of the food on donkeys, camels, mules, and oxen, which are all large, four-legged animals that people used to carry heavy burdens. People brought all kinds of food that included flour, cakes of figs, clusters of raisins, wine and oil, and oxen and sheep for meat. Again, the storyteller shows us just how much people wanted to support David and his men.

Finally, the storyteller says that the people did all of these things because the people in Israel felt very joyful.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:23–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: We learn about the groups of soldiers from each of the tribes of Israel who came to Hebron to help David fight and to make him king of Israel.

In the second scene: We learn more about how much these men and all the people of Israel wanted David to become king. We also learn how all of these men celebrated a feast with David for three days, and how people from all of the tribes of Israel brought food for these men to eat.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Saul
  • Yahweh
  • Soldiers who came to Hebron from all the tribes of Israel, including Judah, Simeon, Levi, Benjamin, Ephraim, the half-tribe of Manasseh from west of the Jordan River, Issachar, Zebulun, Naphtali, Dan, Asher, Reuben, Gad, and the other half-tribe of Manasseh from the east side of the Jordan River
  • Jehoiada, the leader of the family of Aaron, which was part of the tribe of Levi
  • Zadok and the 22 military commanders who were also from the tribe of Levi
  • The 200 chiefs of Issachar
  • And all of the people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller begins when he says that these are the numbers of the troops, or men armed for battle, who came to David at Hebron. The reason these men came to Hebron was to turn Saul's kingdom over to David. This means that the men wanted to take control of the kingdom away from Saul's family and make David king instead. Although the storyteller does not say it here, we know that Saul had died in battle. However, a king's son or another close family member of the king would often become the new king. So the men in this story came ready to go to battle, if needed, to make David king instead. The storyteller says that this was according to the word of Yahweh, which means this is what Yahweh had already said would happen.

The storyteller will now begin to list each of the tribes of Israel and some details about each group.

Stop here and discuss: How will you structure the information about each tribe in this story? For example, will you say the name of the tribe or the number of people first? Will you use longer sentences like a story, or will you use shorter sentences like a list? Here are some examples of ways you might say it:

  • Six thousand eight hundred men from the tribe of Judah came armed for battle. These men carried shields and spears. Seven thousand one hundred men came from the tribe of Simeon. These men were brave soldiers ready for war...; or
  • Judah: 6,800 men armed with shields and spears. Simeon: 7,100 well-trained men....

Pause this audio here.

From Judah's tribe, 6,800 men came to David at Hebron. These men carried shields and spears. These men knew how to fight very well. You will remember that a shield is a large, flat object that soldiers would carry to protect themselves from their enemies, and a spear is a weapon that people made when they attached a sharp blade to the end of a long stick.

Stop here as a group and look again at a picture of a shield and a spear, if needed. Pause this audio here.

From Simeon's tribe, 7,100 men came. The storyteller calls these men "mighty men of valor," which means these men were very brave and knew how to fight very well.

From Levi's tribe, 4,600 men came. Jehoiada, who was a prince or leader of Aaron's clan, came with 3,700 men, and Zadok, who was a brave young soldier, came with 22 military commanders. We know that these men were part of the Levite tribe, but it is not clear in the original language if the 3,700 men and 22 officers were part of the 4,600 Levites or if they were in addition to those men. However, most scholars agree that these men were part of the 4,600.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate this part of the story so people will understand that the men with Jehoiada and Zadok are part of the 4,600 Levites. For example, one way you might say this is, "From Levi's tribe, 4,600 men came. Jehoiada was one of these men. He was a prince or leader of Aaron's clan, and he commanded 3,700 soldiers. Zadok was also in that group of Levites. Zadok was a brave young soldier, and he came with 22 military commanders from his clan." Pause this audio here.

From Benjamin's tribe, 3,000 men came. The storyteller calls these men the relatives, or brothers, of Saul. This is because these men all descended from the same ancestor, Benjamin. The storyteller says that until this story happened, the majority had served Saul faithfully. In the original language, it is not clear if this means the majority of all the people from the tribe of Benjamin, or if it just means the majority of these 3,000 soldiers.

From Ephraim's tribe, 20,800 men came. These men were brave soldiers, and the people in their clans respected these men very much.

From the half-tribe of Manasseh, 18,000 men came. The leaders of the clans these men came from chose these men by name to come and help David become king. To call "by name" means that they chose each man carefully and individually out of all of the men in this tribe. You will remember that this half of the tribe of Manasseh lived on the west side of the Jordan River.

Stop here and discuss: Bible translations talk about the two half-tribes of Manasseh in different ways. For example, one translation says "West Manasseh" and "East Manasseh." Another translation says, "from the half-tribe of Manasseh west of the Jordan." As a group, stop now and discuss how you will talk about this in your translation so that it will be easy for everyone to understand. Pause this audio here.

From the tribe of Issachar came 200 chiefs and all of the men from their tribe who obeyed the chiefs' commands. The storyteller says that these officers "understood the times to know what Israel ought to do." Some people think this means the men were astrologers, or people who watch where the stars move in the night sky as a sign for what people should do. But most scholars agree that this probably just means that these were very wise men who understood what Israel needed to do at this time.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about people who are very wise and can help people know what they should do in different situations? Pause this audio here.

From the tribe of Zebulun, 50,000 men came. These men were brave warriors who came ready to fight in battle with different types of weapons. These men were completely faithful to David.

From Naphtali, 1,000 military commanders came with 37,000 men who carried shields and spears.

From Dan, 28,600 men came. All of these men came ready to fight in battle.

From Asher, 40,000 men came. These men were all trained soldiers who were also ready to fight in battle.

Finally, from the east side of the Jordan River, 120,000 men came. These soldiers belonged to the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the other half of the tribe of Manasseh. These men came armed with many different kinds of weapons.

The storyteller says that all of these men came "arrayed in battle order" to Hebron. To be arrayed in battle order means to come together in a battle line. Soldiers would often stand together in a long line, side by side, before marching forward together toward the enemy in battle, so this probably means these men marched to Hebron in a similar way.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your translation, how will you describe the way the soldiers came together in a battle line? Pause this audio here.

All of these men were soldiers who were ready for battle. And these men came to Hebron with a "whole heart" to make David king. The storyteller also says that the rest of the people in Israel were of a "single mind." The Bible often talks about the heart as the center of a person where that person's thoughts, feelings, and decisions live. But in a similar way, the Bible also talks about the mind as the place where people think and make decisions. So here, these two phrases have a very similar meaning. "Whole heart" means that these men had the very same desire or purpose when they came to David, and "single mind" means that everyone had the same idea and agreed that they too wanted David to be king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When people agree about something they want to happen, and those people come together to make it happen, how do you talk about this? Pause this audio here.

All of these men spent three days there with David, eating and drinking, because their brothers prepared it for them. This means the men had a feast or celebration together, and their brothers cooked or prepared all of this food. "Their brothers" probably means the people from the tribe of Judah who lived in Hebron. And also their neighbors from as far away as Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali came and brought food on donkeys, camels, mules, and oxen. You will remember that all four of these are large, four-legged animals that people used to carry people and heavy burdens on a journey.

Stop here and look at pictures of a donkey, a camel, a mule, and oxen as a group. Pause this audio here.

The people brought abundant provisions, which means a very large amount of food, to feed the men. These provisions included flour to make bread, cakes of figs, clusters of raisins, wine, oil, and oxen and sheep for meat.

Stop here as a group and look at pictures of flour and bread, cakes of figs, clusters of raisins, wine, oil, oxen, and sheep. Pause his audio here.

Finally, the storyteller says that the people brought all of this food because there was joy in Israel. In the Bible, people often use the word joy to mean a special sort of happiness that people feel because of what God does. People often held special festivals or celebrations to show the joy they felt and to worship God. So here, the storyteller is saying that the people celebrated because they were very happy to make David king.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:23–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Saul
  • Yahweh
  • Soldiers who came to Hebron from all the tribes of Israel, including Judah, Simeon, Levi, Benjamin, Ephraim, the half-tribe of Manasseh from west of the Jordan River, Issachar, Zebulun, Naphtali, Dan, Asher, Reuben, Gad, and the other half-tribe of Manasseh from the east side of the Jordan River
  • Jehoiada, the leader of the family of Aaron, which was part of the tribe of Levi
  • Zadok and the 22 military commanders who were also from the tribe of Levi
  • The 200 chiefs of Issachar
  • And all of the people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller says something like, "These are the numbers of the soldiers who came to join David's army at Hebron. These men wanted to make sure David would become the new king after Saul. These men were ready to fight to make this happen, just as Yahweh said would happen.

"From Judah's tribe, 6,800 men came who carried shields and spears. These men knew how to fight very well.

"From Simeon's tribe, 7,100 strong men came who knew how to fight well.

"From Levi's tribe, 4,600 men came. One of these men was Jehoiada, a leader of Aaron's clan. Jehoiada commanded 3,700 men. Zadok was also in that group. Zadok was a brave young soldier. Zadok came with 22 military commanders from his clan.

"From Benjamin's tribe, the tribe that Saul belonged to, 3,000 men came. Most of those men served Saul faithfully until that time.

"From Ephraim's tribe, 20,800 men came. The people respected these men very much.

"From the half of the tribe of Manasseh that lived on the west side of the Jordan River, 18,000 soldiers came. The clans of these men chose the men by name to come and help David become king.

"The tribe of Issachar sent 200 wise men who understood the right thing for Israel to do at the right time. These men came with their brothers, or the other men in their tribe, who obeyed what these men commanded them to do.

"From the tribe of Zebulun, 50,000 brave warriors came ready to fight. These men knew how to use many different kinds of weapons. These men were completely faithful to David.

"From Naphtali's tribe, 1,000 military commanders came. These men had 37,000 soldiers with them who carried shields and spears.

"From Dan's tribe, 28,600 men came ready to fight.

"From Asher's tribe, 40,000 trained soldiers came ready to fight.

"And from the east side of the Jordan River, 120,000 men came from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the other half of the tribe of Manasseh. These soldiers came with all kinds of weapons, ready for battle."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing all of the soldiers who came from each of these tribes, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • David is a brave soldier and a very good leader. David won many battles for Saul. In fact, we think David is a much better leader than Saul was! or
  • David was the only one who had the courage to fight Goliath, and David has bravely led many other battles. David is the kind of person we need as our next king; or
  • Yahweh has protected David many times, so we know Yahweh is with David. We want a king whom Yahweh will make successful.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I was very discouraged when Saul tried to kill me, and I had to run away. But now I feel hopeful because Yahweh has sent all these men to support me; or
  • I knew that I could not kill Saul, even to defend myself, because Yahweh had made Saul king. But now that Saul is dead, it seems like Yahweh does want me to be the next king.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller says something like, "All these men were trained soldiers, and they came to Hebron ready for war. These men wanted to make David king of Israel, and they were willing to fight for David. All of the other people of Israel agreed with these men.

"The men stayed there with David for three days. During this time, the men ate and drank because their relatives had prepared food for them. Other Israelites also came from as far away as where the tribes of Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali lived. These people brought food on donkeys, camels, mules, and oxen. So they had plenty of flour to make bread, and lots of figs, raisins, wine, olive oil, cows, and sheep. All the Israelites had a very happy party!"

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing all of the soldiers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We came ready to fight to make David king, if needed, but now we will celebrate with David! or
  • We feel confident that Yahweh is with David, and we are very happy that everyone in Israel seems to agree with us that David should be king.

Ask the people playing the people of Hebron who prepared the food, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We love David, so we are very grateful that all of these men have come to fight for David. We want to help all of these men celebrate David as the new king; or
  • We prepared a lot of food, but we did not expect this many men to come. We need more food!

Ask the people playing all the Israelites who bring food from different places in Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • It is a long journey to come here to Hebron, but we want to show David and all of his men our support; or
  • Wow, this is a large group of men! We are glad we brought a lot of food, but we hope it is enough!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 12:23–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller says that these are the numbers of the divisions of the armed troops. A division is a group of soldiers who fight together under the same leader. "Armed troops" means soldiers who have their weapons with them and are ready to fight.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for divisions and armed troops. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

These men came to turn Saul's kingdom over to David according to the word of Yahweh. A kingdom is the territory or the people that a king rules, and "word of Yahweh" means the things Yahweh tells people. Use the same word or phrase for "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. Look up "Yahweh," "Word of God," and "kingdom" in the Master Glossary for more information.

The storyteller calls the Simeonites, Zadok, and the Ephraimites men of valor. Valor means brave or strong. Use the same word or phrase for valor as you used in previous passages.

Jehoiada is the prince of the house, or clan, of Aaron. Here, the word prince means a high or most important leader. Use the same word or phrase for prince or "most important leader" as you used in previous passages.

Zadok came with 22 military commanders from his father's house. Use the same word or phrase for "military commanders" as you have in previous passages. In this story, the storyteller uses the term "house" or "father's house" throughout this passage to mean a clan, or large family group. This means that these men were Zadok's relatives, or that they were all part of the same large family. However, it probably did not mean they lived in the same house. You can use any phrases that mean a larger "household," "relatives," or "clan." For more information, refer to household in the Master Glossary.

The Benjamites were the brothers, or relatives, of Saul. In this case, "brothers" refers to relatives, and not necessarily to brothers from the same mother. Instead, it means people who all come from the same ancestor. If this ancestor lived a long time ago, this group of related people could be very large. Use the same word or phrase for brothers, when it means relatives, as you used in previous passages.

The half tribe of Manasseh came to make David king. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory. For more information, look up king in the Master Glossary.

Two hundred chiefs, or clan leaders, came from the tribe of Issachar. Use the same word for chiefs, or leaders, as you have used in previous passages.

Like Zadok, Naphtali came with 1,000 military commanders. Use the same word for military commander here as you did for the 22 military commanders that Zadok brought.

All of these men came to Hebron with a whole heart to make David king. And all of the rest of the people in Israel were of a single mind. Remember your conversation about this in step three. You may also look up heart and mind in the Master Glossary for more information, if needed. Use the same words or phrases for heart and mind as you used in previous passages.

People from all over Israel brought food to Hebron on donkeys, camels, mules, and oxen. A donkey is a large mammal with hooves. A donkey is related to the horse, but with longer ears, a short mane, and a smaller but strong body. A camel is also a large mammal with hooves. Camels tend to be quite large, with longer legs and neck. Camels also have one or two large humps on their backs. Mules are another type of large mammal. Mules come from breeding a male donkey with a female horse. The law of Moses did not allow the Israelites to breed two different animals together, so we are not sure where these mules came from, but the Israelites might have taken them from their enemies when they won a battle. Finally, oxen are a type of large, strong cattle. The Israelites often used oxen to pull a plow in their fields or to pull carts that carried heavy items. Use the same words or phrases for donkeys and camels as you used in previous passages. For more information on donkey, see the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for mules and oxen. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people brought lots of food, including flour, cakes of figs, clusters of raisins, wine and oil, and oxen and sheep. People made bread by grinding grain into flour and then mixing it with water and cooking it over a fire. Figs are a type of fruit, and people would press multiple figs together to make larger fig "cakes." People make raisins from grapes, which are a type of fruit that grows on a vine. People set grapes out in the sunlight until they dry and shrivel up to become raisins. Wine is an alcoholic drink that people make from grapes. Oil is a thick liquid that can come from many things, but in Israel, people usually made oil from olives, which are a type of fruit that grows on olive trees. Finally, oxen and sheep are both large four-legged animals that the Israelites could kill and cook to eat meat. Use the same words or phrases for wine and oil as you used in previous passages. For more information on wine, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for flour, cakes of figs, clusters of raisins, and sheep. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Finally, the storyteller says people did all these things because there was joy in Israel. This means that people felt very happy and celebrated what God had done.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "there was joy in Israel." If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.
