One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 13:1–14
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 13:1–14 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 13:1–14 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 13:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller described the many soldiers from different tribes who started to follow David as the new king of the Israelites. These men spent three days celebrating and feasting with David in the city of Hebron.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and find Hebron as a group. Pause this audio here.

This true story happens sometime after many Israelites feasted with David in Hebron. We do not know for sure where this story happens, but most likely it is Jerusalem. In this story, David talks and plans with David's most important military officers. David says, "If you all agree, and if Yahweh our God wants this to happen, then let us send a message to the rest of our people in the other Israelite regions." David is suggesting that the men choose a few people who will travel to these other regions and deliver the message for them.

David says the messengers should share the message with the priests and Levites who live in those regions with the other Israelites. In those days, priests and Levites were the ones who did important work to take care of the tabernacle and help Israel worship Yahweh.

David wants to bring the ark of "our God," or the ark of Yahweh, back to the people of Israel.

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of God as a group. Discuss this question as a group: Tell of something or someone in your community that is a sign of God's presence, or perhaps the presence of spirits or gods. How do you think this is the same or different from the ark of God? Pause this audio here.

David talks about bringing the ark of Yahweh to where David and the Israelites are. Although the storyteller does not say the name of the city, we know that David is talking about moving the ark of Yahweh to the city of Jerusalem. Jerusalem is where David lives as the king of Israel at the time of this story.

Stop here and look at a map that includes Jerusalem as a group. Pause this audio here.

When David says, "We did not seek it during Saul's time," David is saying that the Israelites did not ask Yahweh what to do about the ark of Yahweh while Saul was king. Then all the Israelites agree to do what David suggests because the Israelites think this idea sounds good.

So, David assembles all the Israelites in Kiriath Jearim. Kiriath Jearim is on the border between the territories of Judah and Benjamin, about a half-day's walk northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map that includes Kiriath Jearim as a group. Pause this audio here.

So, David and all the Israelites with David go up to Baalah, which means to Kiriath Jearim. The storyteller then says that Kiriath Jearim is in the region of Judah. David and the Israelites go there to get the ark of God, which people knew by the name, "Yahweh who sits above the cherubim." The Israelites had carved cherubim on top of the ark. Cherubim are angels, or creatures with wings. The Israelites believed that Yahweh literally lived in the space directly above the ark. So, people called the ark by Yahweh's name because the ark symbolized Yahweh's presence.

Then the Israelites put the ark of Yahweh on a new cart to move it from Abinadab's house. The storyteller does not tell us who Abinadab is, but we know from 1 Samuel 7 that Abinadab had taken care of the ark of Yahweh at his house for a long time. "A new cart" refers to a cart that people have never used before. Two men, Uzzah and Ahio, are guiding the cart. The storyteller does not mention this, but we know from another story that these men are walking and steering the cart while oxen pull the cart. Yahweh had given a command many years before that people must carry the ark on poles. So when people carry the ark on a cart, the people are disobeying Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of oxen pulling a cart as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe a holy object in your culture. What rules do you have for how people can interact with the object? What would happen if someone did not follow these rules? Pause this audio here.

While the men guide the cart, David and all the Israelites with David celebrate near the ark. Because the ark represents Yahweh's presence, the Israelites would say that they are celebrating "before Yahweh." The Israelites dance joyfully and play music to celebrate.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when many people in your culture celebrated something together. What kinds of things did people do to celebrate? What types of dances did people dance? What types of songs or music did people play? What types of musical instruments did people use? Pause this audio here.

The Israelites play lyres, harps, timbrels, cymbals, and trumpets as they walk along with the ark.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kind of musical instruments do people play to celebrate something good? Pause this audio here.

The first type of instrument the Israelites play is a lyre. Lyres are stringed instruments similar to a very small harp.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lyre as a group. Pause this audio here.

The second type of instrument is a harp. Harps are stringed instruments, similar to lyres but larger and with more strings.

Stop here and look at a picture of what this kind of harp may have looked like as a group. Pause this audio here.

The third type of instrument is timbrels. Timbrels are small, handheld drums similar to a tambourine but without rattles.

Stop here and look at a picture of what a timbrel may have looked like as a group. Pause this audio here.

The fourth type of instrument is cymbals. Musicians hold two cymbals and strike them together to make a high-pitched sound.

Stop here and look at a picture of cymbals as a group. Pause this audio here.

The fifth type of instrument is a trumpet. In those days, people made a trumpet by hammering metal, either silver or bronze, into a tube. The tube had a narrow opening on one end and a wide opening on the other end. A person blew into the narrow part of the trumpet and made their lips vibrate to force air through the other end of the tube. This made a loud, clear sound that people could hear from far away. The trumpet player could only play low notes, medium notes, and high notes. The trumpet player could make short sounds by blowing the trumpet quickly with just a little bit of breath. The trumpet player could make long sounds by blowing the trumpet slowly with a long breath.

Stop here and look at a picture of a metal trumpet. Pause this audio here.

David and the Israelites continue to move the ark and celebrate. The group arrives at a threshing floor, a type of flat farm area where farmers process grain. People called this specific threshing floor "the threshing floor of Kidon." We do not know for sure what "Kidon" means. "Kidon" could be the name of the place where the threshing floor was or, more likely, the name of the man who owns the threshing floor.

At this threshing floor, Uzzah reaches out his hand to hold the ark because, when the oxen stumble, the box starts to fall off the cart.

Right away, Yahweh feels very angry at Uzzah and kills Uzzah because Uzzah touched the ark. We know that Uzzah dies there before God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kind of consequences do people experience if they do not obey God, gods, or spirits? Pause this audio here.

David feels distressed because Yahweh has killed Uzzah. So, David names the place where Uzzah died, "Perez Uzzah," which means that Yahweh has punished Uzzah in this place. The storyteller mentions that the Israelites continue to call the place Perez Uzzah even at the time when the storyteller writes down this story.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story about a time when someone was angry at God, gods, or the spirits. Pause this audio here.

David feels afraid of God because God has killed Uzzah. So, David says, "How can I bring the ark of God to me?" David does not think that David should continue to take the ark with David to Jerusalem, so David does not take the ark with him. The storyteller mentions the "City of David," which refers to Jerusalem, since this is where David lives at this time. Instead, David takes the ark to the house of Obed-Edom, a Gittite man. Obed-Edom keeps the ark in his house for three months, and Yahweh blesses Obed-Edom and all the people in Obed-Edom's household.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kind of good things happen to people when God, gods, or spirits bless them?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 13:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: David discusses with David's officers David's desire to gather all the other Israelites and bring the ark of Yahweh to Jerusalem. David then shares this with all the other Israelites there. These Israelites agree with David.

In the second scene: David assembles the Israelites from all regions to the city, Kiriath Jearim. David and the Israelites start to move the ark of Yahweh on a new cart, which oxen pull. David and the Israelites celebrate and play musical instruments. Uzzah and Ahio steer the cart.

In scene three: At a threshing floor, the oxen stumble, and Uzzah reaches out to hold the ark. Yahweh feels very angry and kills Uzzah right there. David feels angry at God. David decides not to bring the ark to David's city. Instead, David takes the ark to Obed-Edom's house.

In scene four: Obed-Edom keeps the ark at his house for three months. During that time, Yahweh blesses Obed-Edom and everyone in Obed-Edom's house.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • David's officers
  • All the Israelites who live near David
  • All the Israelites in other regions
  • Uzzah and Ahio
  • The oxen
  • Yahweh
  • And Obed-Edom, along with the people who live in Obed-Edom's house

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller introduces the story with a word that means that this story happens sometime after the last story.

In scene one, David consults with David's officers, the "princes of thousands and of hundreds, with every leader." These "princes" are David's most important military officers who lead different sizes of military groups. Some officers are the military commanders of 1,000 men. Other officers are the military commanders of 100 men.

The storyteller then uses a word to show that David did something next. The storyteller says that "David says to all the assembly of Israel...." "All the assembly of Israel" may refer to all the military leaders that the storyteller mentioned earlier. More likely, this refers to all the Israelites who live near David at this time.

Then David suggests an idea of something for the Israelites to do. David also makes it clear that David only makes this suggestion if the Israelites and Yahweh agree with David's idea.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what are some ways you can say that one thing will happen only if everyone agrees to it? Pause this audio here.

David suggests that David and the Israelites with David send a message to ask all the other Israelites, including priests and Levites, who live in different regions to come to where David is. David says these other people are "our brothers who remain in all the land of Israel." "Our brothers" refers to all the other Israelites who live in different regions other than Jerusalem.

David also mentions the priests and Levites who live in the areas that surround the Israelites' cities. Levites came from the family of Jacob's son, Levi. Levites did important work to help people worship Yahweh. Priests came from the tribe of Levi. The storyteller mentions priests separately because they did the most important work for worshipping Yahweh. In previous stories, we know that God had instructed Moses to give the Levites pasturelands outside the Israelite cities. In these pasturelands, the Levites could raise grazing farm animals. The Levites continued to do this during the time of David. In this story, David asks these other Israelites to come to the place where David and the Israelites who are with David are now.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about a bigger city or village that you know about. Describe the area around the city or village. How do you think this is similar or different from where the priests and Levites raised grazing animals in pasturelands outside of the Israelite cities? Pause this audio here.

David explains that he wants to send this message so that all of these Israelites can "bring the ark of our God to us." David is saying that David wants the Israelites to go get the ark and bring it to Jerusalem. "The ark of our God" refers to the ark of Yahweh. David continues to explain that the Israelites did not "seek" or "inquire of" the ark of God when Saul was king. The ark of God represents Yahweh. So, when David says that the Israelites did not "seek" or "inquire of" the ark, it means that the Israelites did not ask Yahweh what Yahweh wanted to do.

All the assembly, which means all the Israelites who have gathered or assembled to listen to David, say that they will do what David said because this is "right in their eyes." In those days, if someone said something was right in someone's eyes, it meant that that person agreed and thought this was a good idea. The Israelites agree that David's idea is a good idea.

In scene two, the storyteller uses a word that shows that the Israelites agree to follow David's suggestion. David gathers "all Israel," which means all the Israelites who live from the southern border of Israel, which is Shihor of Egypt, to the northern border of Israel, which is Lebo Hamath. All the Israelites prepare to get the ark of God from Kiriath Jearim. We do not know the exact meaning of "Lebo Hamath." It may refer to the entrance to the city of Hamath, a mountain pass to the south of Hamath, or "Lebo Hamath," a city located in the modern country of Syria. The name "Kiriath Jearim" means "city of woods." Kiriath Jearim is in the territory of the tribe of Judah, on the border between the territories of Judah and Benjamin, about a half-day's walk northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel. Find Shihor, Lebo Hamath, and Kiriath Jearim as a group. Pause this audio here.

David and all Israel go to Baalah in the territory of the tribe of Judah. "Baalah" is the old name for Kiriath Jearim. The Israelites are going to "bring up" or get from there the ark of Yahweh. Then the storyteller gives a title for the ark. Scholars are not certain what the original language means, but it probably means, "Yahweh enthroned upon the cherubim which is called a Name," because the Israelites believed that Yahweh literally lived above the ark. "Cherubim" are winged creatures, like angels, that someone carved on top of the ark. So, people called the ark by Yahweh's name because the ark symbolized Yahweh's presence.

Stop and discuss: How might you describe "Yahweh enthroned upon the cherubim which is called a Name?" Stop the recording here.

The Israelites start to transport the ark of Yahweh on a new cart that people have never used before. The Israelites start moving the ark on the cart at Abinadab's house. The storyteller does not mention this yet, but we know that some oxen pulled the cart. Uzzah and Ahio are guiding the cart. This means that Uzzah and Ahio are helping to direct the oxen that are pulling the cart.

And David and all Israel, meaning the Israelites with David, celebrate "before Yahweh" with all their strength. "Before Yahweh" means that the Israelites are doing this in front of the ark of Yahweh, since the ark represents Yahweh's presence. "All their strength" means that the Israelites are celebrating with full devotion, enthusiasm, and energy. The Israelites are also playing music on lyres, harps, timbrels, cymbals, and trumpets.

Stop and discuss: How might you describe how the Israelites celebrate before Yahweh with all their strength? Pause the audio here.

In scene three, the storyteller uses a word to show that something else is happening at the same time as David and the other Israelites celebrate. The group arrives on a threshing floor, or a place where people thresh grain. In those days, threshing floors were usually a high, hard, and flat area. Farmers used this area to hit and separate the part of the grain plant that people can eat from the part of the grain plant that people use for animal beds. In this story, people call this specific threshing floor "the threshing floor of Kidon." We do not know for sure what "Kidon" refers to. "Kidon" could be the name of a place or the name of the man who owns the threshing floor.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of someone threshing grain. Then discuss what word or phrase you will use to describe this place where people thresh grain. Pause this audio here.

It is here at the threshing floor that Uzzah reaches out to hold the ark. Uzzah does this because the oxen stumble. The phrase "the oxen stumbled" implies that when the oxen stumbled, the ark of Yahweh must have started to fall off the cart. In other words, Uzzah reaches out his hand to keep the ark of God from falling off the cart when the oxen stumble. The storyteller uses a special word to show that Yahweh immediately feels very angry that Uzzah touched the ark.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are some different ways in your culture to say that someone is extremely angry? What are some ways to say that God, or local spirits or gods, are angry at someone? Practice using these phrases to say that Yahweh felt extremely angry at Uzzah. Discuss which phrases you think are most like how Yahweh felt. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then uses a word to show that what happens next is a direct result of Uzzah's mistake. Yahweh instantly kills Uzzah because Uzzah put his hand on the ark. The storyteller adds that Uzzah died right there, facing God. By "facing God," the storyteller is saying that Uzzah died in front of the ark, which represents Yahweh. The passage does not describe how Yahweh kills Uzzah. The Israelites could not see Yahweh, but the Israelites could see Uzzah's dead body next to the ark of God.

Stop here as a group and talk about a time when someone died suddenly because of their own bad choice. Discuss what words or phrases you will use to describe how Uzzah died. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then uses a word to show that David feels emotionally troubled because Yahweh killed Uzzah. David likely feels several kinds of negative emotions at this time. David names the place where Uzzah died "Perez Uzzah" or "the breach of Uzzah." A breach is like when people break through an enemy's city wall in battle. This place name means that Yahweh was so angry with Uzzah in this place that Yahweh killed Uzzah as punishment. The storyteller mentions that the Israelites continue to call the place Perez Uzzah even at the time when the storyteller writes this story.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are some ways to describe how David felt here? Discuss which ones you think are the most like how David felt. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that David is afraid of Yahweh that day, which means the day when Uzzah died. David says, "How can I bring the ark of Yahweh home to me?" David did not expect anyone to answer this question. David was saying that David could not and did not want to take the ark to Jerusalem after what had happened.

So, David does not take the ark into David's city, Jerusalem. Instead, David takes the ark to the house of Obed-Edom, a Gittite. A Gittite is someone from a city that people called "Gath." We do not know for sure what people group Obed-Edom comes from.

In scene four, the storyteller says that Obed-Edom keeps the ark of Yahweh in Obed-Edom's house for three months. Yahweh blesses Obed-Edom's household and everything that belongs to Obed-Edom, including family members and, possibly, servants. We do not know how Yahweh blesses Obed-Edom, but Yahweh does good things for the household.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 13:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • David's officers
  • All the Israelites who live near David
  • All the Israelites in other regions
  • Uzzah and Ahio
  • The oxen
  • Yahweh
  • And Obed-Edom, along with the people who live in Obed-Edom's house

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

David discusses with David's military officers and then talks with all the Israelites. David suggests that, if the Israelites and Yahweh agree, David will send messengers to gather all the Israelites from other regions. This includes the priests and Levites in the areas that surround those other regions. All the Israelites together can then go get the ark of "our" God. David also explains that the Israelites did not seek the ark during Saul's time as king. All of the Israelites who are there with David agree to David's suggestion because they think it is a good idea.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites with David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is a great idea! We need to have Yahweh's presence with us again! or
  • We are so excited to get the ark back again after so long; or
  • David is wise to suggest this idea; or
  • We like that we have a king who involves us when he makes decisions.

Continue the drama.

So David gathers all the Israelites from the Shihor River area in Egypt to Lebo Hamath. The Israelites all go to the region of Judah to a town that people originally called Baalah. People later called this town Kiriath Jearim. This town is where the ark of God is. People call the ark, "Yahweh, who sits above the cherubim, which is called by the Name." The Israelites move the ark from the house of a man named Abinadab. The way the Israelites move the ark is by placing the ark on a new cart. Uzzah and Ahio steer the cart, and the oxen pull the cart.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I clearly told the Israelites that only Israelite priests could carry the ark on poles. David and the other Israelites are disobeying me when they put the ark on a cart; or
  • The Israelites are dishonoring me.

Continue the drama.

David and all the Israelites celebrate with all their strength before God. The Israelites play songs with lyres, harps, timbrels, cymbals, and metal trumpets.

Pause the drama. Ask the person or people playing David and the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I, David, am starting my time as king. I can do a great thing by getting the ark back for the Israelites; or
  • We are so excited and happy that we have the ark of God after we lost access to it for three generations; or
  • We want to celebrate because we are finally in Yahweh's presence again; or
  • We want to praise Yahweh for the return of the ark of God to the Israelites.

Continue the drama.

The Israelites continue to celebrate and come to the threshing floor of Kidon. Here, at the threshing floor, Uzzah reaches out to stop the ark from falling when the oxen stumble.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Uzzah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The ark of God is special and should not touch the ground; or
  • I know that I am not supposed to touch the ark, but I do not want the ark to fall.

Continue the drama.

Yahweh feels very angry at Uzzah for Uzzah's error of touching the ark. So, Yahweh takes Uzzah's life right away. Uzzah dies right next to the ark.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The Israelites already disobeyed me when they put the ark on a cart instead of the Israelite priests carrying it. Now, Uzzah has done a terrible thing when he touches the ark; or
  • No person is supposed to directly touch the ark because they are not completely good like I am; or
  • Uzzah's consequence for his own bad choice is death.

Continue the drama.

David feels distressed because Yahweh has killed Uzzah. Because of this, David thinks that David cannot continue to take the ark of Yahweh to David's city. Instead, David takes the ark of Yahweh to Obed-Edom's house.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am not good like Yahweh. I do not deserve to bring the ark to my city; or
  • I am terrified of Yahweh because of Yahweh's power; or
  • I took part in the disobedience to Yahweh that led to Uzzah's death; or
  • I do not want to die or be responsible for anyone else dying because of the ark of Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

Yahweh blesses everyone in Obed-Edom's house the whole three months that Obed-Edom keeps the ark there.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Obed-Edom is taking good care of my ark. I want to bless Obed-Edom; or
  • When people spend time in my presence, I will bless those people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 13:1–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

David talked with his most important military officers, who were military commanders in David's army. Use the same words or phrases for "most important military officers" and "military commanders" as you used in previous passages.

At the beginning of the story, David talks with "all the assembly of Israel." Israel refers to the Israelites or people who are descendants of Jacob. These people lived in the country called Israel. To assemble means to come together for a special purpose, so "all the assembly" means all the Israelites who live near David and have come to hear David speak. Later in the story, David gathers "all of Israel," which means the Israelites from every region. This means that many Israelites traveled from far away. For more information on Israel and Israelites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word that you used for Israel, or Israelites, in previous passages.

David also calls together the priests and Levites. These men come from the cities and pasturelands across Israel. For more information on priests and Levites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for priests and Levites as you used in previous passages. Use the same word or phrase for cities and pasturelands as you used in previous passages.

This story tells us how King David and the Israelite army moved the ark of God to Jerusalem. The ark was a special rectangular box covered in gold. Yahweh had told Moses to put sacred objects in the ark. This included the tablets that Yahweh had written Yahweh's law on. The Israelites believed that Yahweh literally lived in the space directly above the ark, so the ark represented Yahweh's presence. Yahweh used the ark to remind the Israelites that Yahweh had promised to be with the Israelites.

Stop here and, if needed, look at a drawing of the ark of God as a group again. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the general term for God, and for the ark of God. Look up God and the ark of the covenant in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

People called the ark of God by the name of "Yahweh who lives above the cherubim, which people call by the Name." Yahweh is God's personal name. The Israelites said the ark belonged to Yahweh, and the Israelites also worshipped or prayed to Yahweh at the ark. A cherub is an intelligent, powerful angelic being. Cherubim is the word that means more than one cherub. Cherubim are guardians of sacred places. Cherubim have a body like a lion, wings like a bird, and a face like a human. The ark of God has two cherubim on the top of it. Yahweh's presence is on top of the ark, like how a king sits on a throne. Since the cherubim are on the top of the ark, the Israelites say that Yahweh lives above the cherubim. The Israelites call the ark by Yahweh's Name.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cherubim. Look up cherubim in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss what words or phrases you will use for "Yahweh who lives above the cherubim, which people call by the Name." If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase that you have used there. Look up Yahweh in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

As the Israelites moved the ark of God, they played several instruments: lyres, harps, timbrels, cymbals, and trumpets. Here is a little more detail about these instruments.

The first kind of instrument the Israelites played was the lyre. Lyres are stringed instruments similar to a very small harp. People in those days often played lyres and sang at the same time. People played the lyre strings with one or both hands by plucking or strumming. In those days, people made lyres with 3 to 10 strings. People sometimes call this a "kinnor harp." The Israelites likely made the strings for lyres out of the dried large intestines of sheep. People in those days likely made lyres by carving wood into curved shapes and then stretching strings across the wooden frame from bottom to top. Lyres have a larger base at the bottom of the instrument and a smaller bar at the top of the instrument. The lyre usually has two arms, or bars, on the sides so the musician can hold the instrument. Lyre strings usually have different thicknesses and lengths. The person who plays the lyre plucks or strums the strings so the strings vibrate and make a sound. When a musician plucks a longer and thicker string, the lyre makes a lower sound. When a musician plucks a shorter and thinner string, the lyre makes a higher sound. The person who plays the lyre would often pluck or strum with one of their hands and use their other hand to stop one or more of the strings from vibrating to block the sound from those strings.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you will use for lyre. If necessary, look at a photo of a lyre. Pause this audio here.

The second kind of instrument the Israelites play is the harp, which is like a large lyre with more strings. In those days, the kind of harps that people played looked and sounded different from harps today. This type of harp probably had around 12 strings. The harps in those days had a wider and thicker bottom end of the instrument to help make the sound louder. This made the instrument look similar to a wineskin container that has a larger bottom. So, people called this kind of harp by the same name as a wineskin container, a "nabal" harp.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you will use for harp. If necessary, look at a photo of a harp. Pause this audio here.

The third kind of instrument the Israelites play is the timbrel. A musician held this small, handheld drum in one hand and hit the drum with their other hand. To make these instruments, people would first make a round, shallow frame. Then the people would dry an animal skin and stretch it across the frame so that it was very tight. The shallow frame meant that the drum did not make a very loud sound. People may also have attached many tiny pieces of thin metal to the frame so that it would rattle when the musician hit or shook the instrument. People call this kind of drum a "tambourine" when it also has metal rattles on it.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you will use for timbrel. If necessary, look at a photo of a timbrel. Pause this audio here.

The fourth kind of instrument the Israelites play is the cymbal, which people make like a thin, round metal plate. A set of cymbals has two identical pieces that musicians hit together to make a higher-pitched, crashing or ringing sound. Musicians either hold one cymbal in each hand or tie two cymbals with some kind of string to different fingers on the same hand to hit together. Smaller cymbals make quieter sounds, and larger cymbals make louder sounds.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you will use for cymbals. If necessary, look at a photo of cymbals. Pause this audio here.

The fifth kind of instrument the Israelites play is the trumpet. We know from previous stories that Yahweh had told the Israelites to play on metal trumpets during days of celebration. Skilled Israelite workers made these trumpets by beating bronze or silver into a long tube. The tube had a small opening on one end where the musician could use their mouth to blow air into the instrument. The other end of the tube had a wider opening. A musician would play a trumpet by closing their lips tightly against the small opening of the trumpet. Then the musicians would blow the air in a way that made their lips vibrate. This vibrating sound increased as the air moved through the widened part of the tube. A trumpet player could only play low notes, medium notes, and high notes. The trumpet player could make short sounds by blowing the trumpet quickly with just a little bit of breath. The trumpet player could make long sounds by blowing the trumpet slowly with a long breath. People could hear the loud, clear sounds of trumpets from far away.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word you will use for metal trumpets. If necessary, look at a photo of metal trumpets. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh blessed all of Obed-Edom's household because Obed-Edom kept the ark of Yahweh. Here, household refers to all the people who lived with Obed-Edom in his house. For more information on bless and household, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word that you used for bless and household in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 14:1–7
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 14:1–7 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:1–7 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The story in this passage does not follow on from the story about the ark in the previous passage. The storyteller stops the main story about the ark's journey to tell us about Yahweh's blessings to David because David was obedient and faithful. The storyteller uses this passage to talk about Yahweh's blessings to David during David's reign over Israel.

The storyteller reminds us of stories from 2 Samuel 5 to show how Yahweh blesses David.

Stop here and listen to your translation of the passage 2 Samuel 5:11–16, if possible. Pause this audio here.

The passages in 2 Samuel and 1 Chronicles are very similar.

When David was king of all Israel, Hiram, the king of Tyre, a city in Phoenicia, sent special messengers to David to talk about trade and to show friendship to Israel. Tyre was a rich and powerful city.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel that shows the city of Jerusalem in Israel and the city of Tyre in Phoenicia. Pause this audio here.

Hiram sent gifts to David to show that Hiram wanted the nations of Tyre and Israel to be friends. Hiram also sent logs of cedar wood, which were good for building houses. Cedars were large trees with straight trunks that people used for building houses. A log is a section of the tree trunk or a main branch of the tree from which a person removes the side branches.

Stop here and look at a picture of a cedar tree. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of cedar wood logs. Pause this audio here.

Hiram also sent stonemasons, people who shaped stone with tools and built with stone, and carpenters, people who used wood to build houses and furniture. The stonemasons and carpenters helped the Israelites to build David's palace, his special house in the city of David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your community or country, how is your king's or most important leader's house different from other people's houses? Did people make the house for your king or leader, or did people make it for someone else and then give it to your king or leader? Pause this audio here.

Hiram, king of Tyre, treated David as a rich and powerful king, someone equal in power to Hiram. This helped David to know that Yahweh made David more powerful and influential during the time David was king. The storyteller talks about David's success but makes it clear to us that Yahweh was responsible for Israel's success and David's success as king. Yahweh established David's reign, which means that he made it secure over time. Yahweh highly exalted David's kingdom, or made David's kingdom very successful, so that this time was very good for the Israelites, Yahweh's people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a good and happy time for your people or nation. What made it a good time for your people? Pause this audio here.

When David was in Jerusalem, Yahweh showed that he blessed David by giving David more children. David married more women and brought these women into David's household. A wife was a woman who had the full rank and benefits of a married woman in the man's home. In this passage, this storyteller does not mention that David added concubines to his household. David fathered more children when he was in Jerusalem, in addition to the children that David already had when David was in Hebron. The storyteller tells us the names of David's sons who were born while David lived in Jerusalem and was King of Israel. The Israelites thought sons were more important than daughters. These sons of David were called Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon, Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet, Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia, Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet. David based some of the names of his sons on the northern or Canaanite name for God, which was Elohim or Baal. This was different from the names of David's earlier sons, which he based on the name of Yahweh. In 2 Samuel, the storyteller gives the name Eliada to Beeliada. The name Beeliada is based on Baal. After David was king, the Israelites thought it was wrong to use the Canaanite name for God, which was Baal, in names. Sometimes the storytellers changed people's names with Baal in them in stories to something different. Even though the storyteller tells this story many years later than the story in 2 Samuel, the storyteller in 1 Chronicles uses Beeliada's original name.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your community, how have names changed over time? What names can people not use because the names are offensive to your people? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller names two more sons than the storyteller in 2 Samuel. The two additional sons were called Elpelet and Nogah. We do not know why there is a difference between the two stories. The storyteller does not give the names of David's daughters.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: During David's reign, Hiram, king of Tyre, sent messengers, wood to build things, and men to build with stone and wood to help build a palace for David. These events helped David know that Yahweh wanted David to be king and wanted to give David a successful kingdom.

In the second scene: When David was king in Jerusalem, David married more wives and had more children. The storyteller tells us the names of 13 of David's sons born in Jerusalem.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Hiram, king of Tyre
  • Messengers, stonemasons, and carpenters from Tyre
  • David
  • Yahweh
  • David's wives
  • And David's sons who were born in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon, Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet, Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia, Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The story in this passage does not follow on from the previous passage about how David moved the ark of the covenant. The storyteller starts a new part of his story to show that, because David acted in a faithful way, good things happened to David while David was king of Israel. The events in this passage probably happened later in David's reign. Hiram became king of Tyre many years after David became king of Israel. When Hiram and David were both kings, Hiram sent messengers and gifts so that there would be a good relationship between Tyre and Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call people who travel to other people groups or nations to talk about how they can trade or work together as two nations? Pause this audio here.

Hiram's gifts helped the Israelites to build a special house, a palace for David. You may notice that in 2 Samuel, the storyteller says carpenters and then stonemasons, but in 1 Chronicles, the storyteller says stonemasons and then carpenters.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call a special building that people make for the leader of your people to live in? Pause this audio here.

After being king for many years, David knew that Yahweh had made David an established king of Israel, a secure and successful king. David knew that Yahweh made David's kingdom very successful in order to bless Yahweh's people, the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think Yahweh made David's kingdom successful? How will you make it clear that Yahweh made David's kingdom successful in order to bless Yahweh's people? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then tells us another way that Yahweh blessed David for being faithful. During David's reign in Jerusalem, David married more women as wives and had more children. The storyteller tells us the names of David's sons born in Jerusalem while David was king in Israel. David's sons were called Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon, Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet, Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia, Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Hiram, king of Tyre
  • Messengers, stonemasons, and carpenters from Tyre
  • David
  • Yahweh
  • David's wives
  • And David's sons who were born in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon, Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet, Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia, Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

During David's reign, Hiram, king of Tyre, sent messengers, wood to build things, and men to build with stone and wood to help build a palace for David. These events helped David know that Yahweh wanted David to be king and wanted to give David a successful kingdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hiram, king of Tyre, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Israel is more powerful now that David is king; or
  • I want to work and trade with Israel; or
  • Israel is a good country for Tyre to work and trade with.

Ask the person playing the messengers, stonemasons, and carpenters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honoured to represent my king; or
  • I must do a good job for my king, Hiram; or
  • Israel is so big and powerful. We want King David to be our friend, not our enemy.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Tyre sees that Israel is powerful and strong; or
  • I am pleased that Hiram sent wood and people to help build my palace; or
  • Yahweh is blessing me and Israel.

Continue the drama.

When David was king in Jerusalem, David married more wives and had more children. The storyteller tells us the names of 13 of David's sons born in Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Now that I am king of all Israel, I need to have more wives from Jerusalem and the other tribes; or
  • More wives will give me more sons to help lead Israel and be king after me; or
  • I can have as many wives as I want now that I am king of Israel.

Ask the person playing David's wives, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are honoured to be part of David's household; or
  • We hope that we give David more sons; or
  • There are many of us now, and David has lots of sons.

Ask the person playing David's sons born in Jerusalem, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We hope that David will like us as much as his children born before us; or
  • There are lots of us, and we all want David to like us the most; or
  • Which one of us will be king next?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Hiram, king of Tyre, sent messengers to David to give David a message from King Hiram. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information, refer to king in the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for messengers. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Hiram sent logs of cedar wood. Logs are sections of a tree trunk or the main branch of a tree. Cedar trees are big trees with straight trunks, which are good for building.

Stop here and look at a picture of cedar wood logs, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for logs. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cedar. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Hiram sent stonemasons, people who made things from stone, to Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for stonemasons. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Hiram sent carpenters, people who made things from wood, to Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for carpenters. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The stonemasons and carpenters built a special house, a palace, for David. In the original language, the word is "house," but the people built a special house for a king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a special house or palace. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David knew that Yahweh had made David king of Israel and had established David's kingdom. Yahweh is God's personal name. In this passage, the storyteller calls God "Yahweh." Be sure to translate Yahweh, kingdom, and Israel in the same way as you have in previous passages, and remember that Yahweh, kingdom, and Israel are in the Master Glossary.

David married more wives in Jerusalem and had more children.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 14:8–17
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 14:8–17 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:8–17 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:8–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller continues to tell us about Yahweh's blessings to David because David was obedient and faithful. In this passage, the storyteller gives two examples of David's power over David's enemies when David did what Yahweh told David to do. We can also find these stories in 2 Samuel 5:17–25, but with some differences.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If you have already translated 2 Samuel 5:17–25, listen to your translation of that passage. Pause this audio here.

People brought news from Judah to Philistia to tell the Philistines that David was king over all of Israel. You will remember that the Philistines are the people who live in the country of Philistia to the west of Israel. The Philistines probably heard quite quickly from people that the Israelites had anointed David as king. The storyteller says that David is king of all of Israel because the storyteller wants to remind us that David was a great king who united Israel. When David became king of Israel, the Philistines knew that David was no longer a friend of the Philistines. Israel and Philistia were enemies. Therefore, David became an enemy of the Philistines. Israel was more dangerous to the Philistines because all of Israel was now united under King David.

The Philistines went to search for David after the Israelites anointed David as king. We do not know if the Philistines looked for David before or after David conquered Jerusalem. The Philistines sent all their fighting men, their full force of men, to look for David. David was somewhere in Israel. The Philistines wanted to capture David to reduce the power of the Israelites. When David heard from his people that the Philistines were looking for him, David left where he was to get ready to fight the Philistines.

The Philistines went into Israel and raided through the Rephaim Valley. The Philistines moved across the valley to different places to attack and steal from the people in the valley. The Rephaim Valley was a wide and long valley about 5 kilometers from Jerusalem. The Israelites who lived in the valley grew food for the people of Israel.

Stop here and look at a map of southern Israel and eastern Philistia and find the Rephaim Valley, Jerusalem, and the possible location of Baal Perazim as a group. Pause this audio here.

David asked God what David should do. In this passage, the storyteller sometimes uses the name God, the creator God, instead of Yahweh. In this passage, God and Yahweh are the same person. David probably did not talk directly to God but probably used a Levite priest, possibly Abiathar, to ask questions of God. David asked if he, David, should go up against or attack the Philistines. David also asked if God would give the Philistines into David's hand. In other words, David asked God if God will give David the victory over the Philistines. Yahweh told David to go up against, to attack, the Philistines. Yahweh told David that Yahweh would give the Philistines into David's hand.

David and his men went to attack the Philistines because Yahweh told them to go. David and his men went up to Baal Perazim, in the hills near the Rephaim Valley. David defeated the Philistines there. David said that God broke out or burst through his enemies using David and his men. David gave the place a name that described what Yahweh did to defeat the Philistines. You will remember that the Canaanite people used the word "Baal" for God, or lord. In David's language, Baal Perazim means that God bursts or breaks through. David and his men, with the help of Yahweh, quickly moved through the Philistines and defeated them. The Israelites moved through the Philistines quickly, like water bursts through something and makes a flood.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when someone won a battle quickly. Pause this audio here.

The Philistines left their gods behind when David and his men defeated them. The gods were special images or objects that represented their gods. The people also called these objects idols. The Philistines believed that the idols would bring help from the Philistine gods and give the Philistines the victory. David told his men to burn and destroy the Philistines' gods with fire. David did this to be obedient to Yahweh's instructions to Moses in Deuteronomy 7:5 and 25, which said to destroy foreign idols.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Listen to Deuteronomy 7:5 and verse 25, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then tells us about another time that the Philistines attacked the Israelites. The Philistines probably attacked the Rephaim Valley again. We do not know how much time passed between the first attack and the second attack. The second attack may have happened before or after David conquered Jerusalem. The storyteller tells us that David went to God again to ask what to do. This time, God told David not to attack the Philistines from the front but to move around the Philistines and attack from the back.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What different battle strategies have you seen, used, or heard of? Pause this audio here.

God told David to move to a place behind the Philistines, opposite a group of trees. Different translations of the passage name the trees as poplar, balsam, or mulberry trees. We do not know what type of trees they were, but in the original language, the storyteller calls them baka trees.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What trees, if any, do you notice more than others because the leaves make more noise when the wind blows through them? Talk about the noise the leaves make. Pause this audio here.

God told David to attack quickly after God made the tops of the trees make a noise like the noise when people march. Tops of trees move with the wind, and leaves of trees make a noise when the wind moves through the leaves. God made the tops of the trees sound like when people march with their feet, or walk with a strong, regular rhythm. It is common for fighting people to march or walk together so that each person takes a step at the same time. The rhythmic sound was different from the normal sound of wind when it moves through the trees.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do marching feet sound like? How might marching feet sound when leaves at the tops of trees make the sound? Pause this audio here.

David attacked the Philistines in the way that God told David to do. David did not go on his own but went with his men to fight the Philistines. God helped David and David's fighting men to defeat the Philistines. David and his men fought and chased the Philistines from Gibeon in Israel to Gezer, close to Philistia. It was about 19 kilometers from Gibeon to Gezer.

Stop here and look at the map and find Gibeon and Gezer as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller ends the story by telling us that these victories over the Philistines helped to make David's name famous in every country.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Who is famous in your country? What stories have you heard about them, and what do these stories tell you about the person or people? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh made all the countries be afraid of David and respect David.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:8–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has nine scenes.

In the first scene: The Philistines heard from people that the Israelites had anointed David king of all Israel, and the Philistines went to Israel to find David.

In the second scene: David heard from people that the Philistines were looking for David and moved to get ready to fight the Philistines.** **

In the third scene: The Philistine fighters raided the Rephaim Valley.

In the fourth scene: David asked God what David should do. Yahweh replied that David should go to attack the Philistines, as Yahweh would make sure David won the battle.** **

In the fifth scene: David attacked the Philistines at Baal Perazim. The Philistines left behind their statues of their gods, and David and his men burned the statues.** **

In the sixth scene: The Philistines attacked Israel in the valley.

In the seventh scene: David asked God what David should do. God replied that David should attack the back of the Philistines in front of the trees. David should attack when David heard a marching sound in the tops of the trees. Then David would know that God had gone in front of David's army to attack the Philistines.

In the eighth scene: David followed God's instructions, and David and his men attacked and chased the Philistines from Gibeon to Gezer.** **

In the ninth scene: The storyteller tells us that everyone in the nations nearby knew David's name, and Yahweh made all the nations be afraid of David.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Philistines
  • David, king of Israel
  • Philistine fighters
  • God, or Yahweh
  • And David's fighters

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller re-tells the stories in 2 Samuel 5:17–25 to show that David was faithful and obedient to Yahweh and a great king of a united Israel.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If you have already translated 2 Samuel 5:17–25, listen to your translation of that passage. Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller tells us about two separate times that the Philistines invaded Israel and moved across Israel. We do not know exactly when the attacks happened, but they were after the people anointed David as king of Israel. The two attacks might have happened at a similar time, or they might have happened many years apart. This passage does not follow on directly after the previous passage.

In the first attack, the Philistines went "up" to search for David after the Philistines heard from people that the Israelites had anointed David king of Israel. The Philistines went from the coast inland to the land of Israel, so the storyteller says that they went "up." The Philistines sent many fighting people to find David. When David heard that the Philistines were looking for him, David moved out from where he was to get ready to fight the Philistines. Later, David moves "up" to meet the Philistines.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about times you have travelled around your country, particularly from low to high or high to low areas. How will you translate how people go up and go down in David's country? Pause this audio here.

At the time David moved to fight the Philistines, the Philistines raided the Rephaim Valley. The Philistines moved across the Rephaim Valley to steal people's belongings and attack the Israelites in the valley.

David asked God what God wanted David to do. David wanted to know if God wanted David to go to attack the Philistines and if God would give the Philistines into the hands of the Israelites. You will remember from earlier passages that David uses the word "hand" to symbolise power. David asks Yahweh if Yahweh will give David power over the Philistines. The storyteller uses both names for Yahweh—"Yahweh" and "God"—in this story.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What different names do you use for Yahweh? When do you use the different names, and do those names have different meanings? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh replied to David and told David to go. Yahweh wanted David to attack the Philistines. Yahweh promised to give David victory over the Philistines.

David went up to Baal Perazim near the Rephaim Valley and defeated the Philistines. David knew Yahweh helped the Israelites win the battle by giving the Israelites the power to break through the Philistines. David saw Yahweh's power break through the Philistines just as water can break or burst through a barrier.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have two or more people stand close together. Have another person or a few people quickly push through the people who stand together, from one side of the group to the other. How would you describe water that bursts or breaks through a barrier like these people broke through the group of people? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time you saw water quickly start to flow out of somewhere or overflow something. What happened to everything that the water went over? Pause this audio here.

David named the place Baal Perazim to remind the people that it was the place where Yahweh used David to break through the Philistines. When the Israelites defeated the Philistines, the Philistines abandoned their statues, or idols, of their gods. When a person abandons something, the person leaves that thing behind, even though it was important and the person should care for that thing.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Baal Perazim means that the lord, or master, breaks through. How will you translate the name Baal Perazim? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words or phrases do you use to describe something that is precious but that people left behind because they could not carry it or take it with them? Pause this audio here.

After the Philistines left, David ordered his fighting men to burn the statues of their gods.

Sometime after the first attack, the Philistines raided the valley again. David responded to the attack by going to ask God again what to do. God replied to David with a different way to defeat the Philistines. God wanted David to go and fight the Philistines, but God did not want David to attack the Philistines from in front of the Philistines. God told David to move his fighting men around the Philistines until the Israelites were behind the Philistines.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one or two people stand together to represent the Philistine fighters. Now have one or more people stand to one side to represent the Israelite fighters. Move the Israelite fighters around the Philistine fighters so that they are behind the Philistine fighters, and then touch the Philistine fighters on their backs. Pause this audio here.

God wanted David to attack the Philistines in front of some trees at a special time. God told David that God would signal the Israelites to move by making the trees make an unusual noise, the sound of people marching. The sound told David that God was in front of the Israelites, attacking the Philistines. David followed God's instructions and struck down the Philistines. In other words, David hit, killed, and injured the Philistines all the way from Gibeon, north of Jerusalem and the Rephaim Valley, to Gezer, near Philistia. David and his men chased the Philistines out of Israel.

Stop here and look at a map of southern Israel and eastern Philistia, and find the Rephaim Valley and Gezer as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller adds his own comment to the end of the two stories. The storyteller tells us that David's success at defeating the Philistines made David famous everywhere. Also, Yahweh made every nation or country be afraid of David so that the people would respect David. This was a feeling of awe or fear because David was more powerful than they were.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:8–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has nine scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Philistines
  • David, King of Israel
  • Philistine fighters
  • God, or Yahweh
  • And David's fighters

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The Philistines heard from people that the Israelites had anointed David king of all Israel, and the Philistines went to Israel to find David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Philistines, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are angry! This man is supposed to serve us, not be the king of Israel; or
  • David is a powerful fighter and he will be a powerful king; or
  • We need to capture David so that the Israelites lose their power.

Continue the drama.

David heard from people that the Philistines were looking for David, and David moved to get ready to fight the Philistines.** **

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know the Philistines are angry at me; or
  • I am worried about my people; or
  • What should I do? I need Yahweh to help me.

Continue the drama.

The Philistine fighters raided the Rephaim Valley.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Philistines fighters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We will take everything we can from these farmers; or
  • Where is David? Will he come to defend his people? or
  • We are happy to hurt our enemies.

Continue the drama.

David asked God what David should do. Yahweh replied that David should go to attack the Philistines, as Yahweh would make sure David won the battle.** **

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must do what Yahweh wants me to do; or
  • I want to attack the Philistines. The Philistines are hurting my people; or
  • Will we win against the Philistines?

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that David is asking me what to do; or
  • I want David to help rescue my people and defeat the Philistines; or
  • I will make sure that David wins the battle.

Continue the drama.

David attacked the Philistines at Baal Perazim. The Philistines left behind their statues of their gods, and David and his men burned the statues.** **

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh is with us! We are breaking through the Philistine army! or
  • I am so happy! I will name this place to remind our people of Yahweh's help; or
  • We must burn the Philistines' gods to be obedient to Yahweh's instructions to us.

Ask the person playing Philistine fighters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are defeated! The Israelites are too strong for us; or
  • Our gods are not strong enough. The Israelites' God is stronger than our gods; or
  • We need to run away before the Israelites kill us all.

Ask the person playing the David's fighters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We won! Yahweh helped us to break through all the Philistine fighters; or
  • We are so happy to see the Philistines run away; or
  • I am so afraid of these statues! We will burn all the statues of their gods.

Continue the drama.

The Philistines attacked Israel in the valley.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Philistine fighters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We can take the food from our Israelite enemies; or
  • We hate the Israelites; or
  • Maybe this time we will win.

Continue the drama.

David asked God what David should do. God replied that David should attack the back of the Philistines in front of the trees. David should attack when David heard a marching sound in the tops of the trees and God had gone in front of David's army to attack the Philistines.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We must follow God's instructions for us; or
  • God knows the best way to attack the Philistines; or
  • I am happy that God will be in front of us at the battle.

Ask the person playing God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know the best way to defeat the Philistines; or
  • I am happy that David trusts me; or
  • We will defeat the Philistines together.

Continue the drama.

David followed God's instructions and David and his men attacked and chased the Philistines from Gibeon to Gezer.** **

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I can hear the marching noise in the trees! Let's attack the Philistines; or
  • We are winning because God is with us; or
  • Let's chase and fight the Philistines all the way back to their land.

Ask the person playing David's fighters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The Philistines are running away; or
  • We are strong with God with us.

Ask the person playing the Philistine fighters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are surprised! The Israelites are attacking from behind us! or
  • We cannot win because the Israelites are too strong; or
  • We need to get back to Philistia, but the Israelites are still attacking us.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller tells us that everyone everywhere knew David's name, and Yahweh made all the nations be afraid of David and respect David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Everyone has heard the stories of how David defeated the Philistines; or
  • I want everyone to know how strong and powerful David is.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 14:8–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The Philistines heard that the Israelites had anointed David king of all of Israel. Use the same words or phrases for Philistines, anointed, king, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information about anoint, king, and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

David went out against the Philistines. David left where he was and went to take action against the Philistines.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for went out. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.
