Yahweh performs miracles to teach people something, to show the people that Yahweh is powerful and that they can trust him.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for wonders, miracles, and judgments. What different words do you have in your language to talk about the different kinds of amazing things God does? Look up miracle in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites are Yahweh's servants. A servant is a person who does work for someone else. A servant is often a low position. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages.

The Israelites are the offspring or descendants of Jacob. Some translations also say "seed," which is another way to talk about a person's descendants. A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. The Israelites are descendants of Jacob, Abraham's grandson. Jacob's name is also Israel. Sometimes people refer to the Israelites as Israel. For more information on descendant or seed, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for descendant as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 16:14–22
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 16:14–22 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:14–22 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:14–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This song is a Hebrew poem. We recommend that your team complete the "Introduction to Poetry Module 1" before you continue with "Step 2: Setting the Stage."

Stop here, if needed, and review the translation decisions your team has made regarding the translation of poetry. Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, the Levites started to sing to celebrate how the ark was now in Jerusalem. The singer sang that the Israelites should remember the wonderful things Yahweh has done. This passage is the second part of the song. The singer reminds the Israelites of how Yahweh made a promise to the Israelites' ancestors and then protected their ancestors.

At the beginning of this passage, the singer reminds the Israelites to whom they sing: Yahweh, the Israelites' God. The Israelites know who Yahweh is. Yahweh has been their people's God since the time of Jacob, many, many years ago! And their God is important. Yahweh's judgments or commands are for people all over the world because Yahweh rules the whole world. This is the God the Israelites serve and should worship!

Then the singer sings that the Israelites should always remember and think about Yahweh's covenant. This covenant or special promise is the one that Yahweh made with Abraham, the Israelites' ancestor. Some translations say that Yahweh remembers this covenant forever, which is true. But here, the singer is probably urging the Israelites to remember the covenant.

The singer reminds the Israelites how Yahweh kept this promise with each of Abraham's descendants. Yahweh swore an oath or solemnly promised this covenant again to Isaac, Abraham's son. Yahweh confirmed the covenant to Jacob, Isaac's son. Yahweh confirmed the covenant as a statute or promise, which is an announcement that a ruler makes. Yahweh gave this everlasting covenant, or promise that lasts forever, to Israel. While Israel is another name for Jacob, people also used this name to refer to Jacob's descendants, the Israelites. So here, the singer could be referring to both Jacob and the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When a leader makes a solemn promise with a group of people, how does the leader show that they will keep the promise? When a new leader begins to lead, how does the new leader show that they will still keep this special promise? Pause this audio here.

Then the singer sings about what Yahweh promised. Yahweh said he would give the land of Canaan to Abraham and Abraham's descendants. The land is a portion, or an area that Yahweh has measured out for the Israelites to inherit. Normally, a person inherits something after someone else dies. That person now has the right to own that thing. In this case, Yahweh does not have to die in order for the Israelites to inherit the land of Canaan. As the ruler over all the earth, Yahweh has the authority to give the land of Canaan to the Israelites.

Yahweh protected Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and their families while they were waiting for Yahweh to fulfil his promise and give them the land of Canaan. Throughout their lives, Abraham and his sons did not live in one place but wandered from place to place. Each man and his family were sojourners or temporary residents. They lived for short times in the nations or kingdoms of other people groups, so Abraham and his family were like strangers in the land. While Abraham's family did grow, they were not a huge people group like the Israelites are in this passage.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are some people groups in your area or in your history who do not live in one place? When those people groups are in other nations' lands, how safe are those people groups? How do other governments or people groups treat the people groups who wander? Pause this audio here.

Though other people groups and rulers could have hurt Abraham and his family as they traveled, Yahweh protected them. Yahweh did not allow anyone to oppress Abraham and his family. Yahweh even rebuked or strongly criticized foreign kings in order to defend Abraham's family. Yahweh told those kings not to touch or harm Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, whom Yahweh calls his anointed ones and prophets. "Anointed ones" are people that Yahweh has chosen for a special purpose. Here, the word prophets means, in a general way, people to whom Yahweh speaks. Some people think "my anointed ones" and "my prophets" also refer to the people of Israel, because the Israelites speak God's words to the world. The singer is reminding the Israelites of how Yahweh protected Abraham and his descendants in the past to remind the Israelites that their God protects his people.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:14–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

Stop here and review as a group: We recommend that your team complete the "Introduction to Poetry Modules 2 & 3" before you continue with "Step 3: Defining the Scenes." Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The singer reminds the Israelites that Yahweh is their God, who rules over the whole Earth. So the Israelites should remember the covenant that Yahweh made with the Israelites' ancestors. Yahweh promised to give Abraham and his descendants the land where the Israelites live now.

In the second scene: The singer sings about how Yahweh protected Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and their families when they wandered through other nations.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The singer, who may be a Levite like Asaph, along with a group of Levites
  • The Israelites who are listening
  • The Israelites' ancestors: Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and their families
  • Yahweh
  • And kings

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember, while it is not clear exactly who wrote or sang this song, the singer is probably a Levite like Asaph, who is perhaps singing with a group of Levites. This part of the song has two sections, and each section has a different subject. In the first section, the singer sings about who Yahweh is and the promise Yahweh made to the Israelites' ancestors. Then in the second section, the singer sings about how Yahweh protected the Israelites' ancestors. The singer also continues to sing using companion phrases, where he says something in one line, then in the next line or couple of lines, he develops or adds to the idea in some way.

In the first section and scene, the singer sings about who Yahweh is. Yahweh is the Israelites' God. Yahweh's judgements are for the whole earth, so he has authority over Israel and every other nation. Remember that in the last section, the singer urged the Israelites to remember the wonderful things Yahweh does. So next, the singer sings about one of the wonderful things that the Israelites should remember. Yahweh made a special promise to Abraham and his descendants. In the first line of a companion phrase, the singer sings that the Israelites should remember this promise forever. The singer then sings in the next line that the Israelites should remember for a thousand generations. A generation refers to a group of people who are living at the same time. A thousand generations would be a thousand times longer than a person lives! This is another way to say "forever."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what are some ways that you emphasize how someone should do something forever? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh keeps this same promise through each new generation of Abraham's descendants. The singer sings about three ancestors that the Israelites respected: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, whose name is also "Israel." In two pairs of companion phrases, the singer emphasizes the way that Yahweh kept this covenant as he repeats that Yahweh confirmed it to each man's son. Yahweh confirms it with an oath to Isaac and as a statute or promise to Jacob. The singer then sings that Yahweh made this promise forever with Israel. Remember in the last section that the singer called the Israelites Jacob's descendants, so this promise is for them too. So here, the singer is probably referring both to the man named Israel as well as the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people swear oaths or make solemn promises? What do people say or do to show that this is a very serious promise? Pause this audio here.

In the last companion phrase of this section, the singer reveals part of the covenant that Yahweh made. Yahweh promised that he would give the land of Canaan to Abraham and his descendants. The singer explains in the second line that this is Abraham and his descendants' portion of an inheritance, or something that legally and rightfully belongs to them. The singer is showing how faithful Yahweh is because Canaan now belongs to the Israelites.

In the second scene and section, the singer sings about a small group that wanders from nation to nation. The singer has just sung about Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, so we know that he is singing about those men and their families. The singer is reminding the Israelites of how Yahweh protected the Israelites' ancestors. The singer does this to comfort and remind the Israelites that Yahweh will continue to protect them. Yahweh has done this several times, such as when the Israelites traveled through the wilderness. Sometimes, when leaders remind the Israelites about what God has done for their ancestors, the leaders will say, "This is what God has done for you," instead of "This is what God has done for your ancestors." So some translations say "when you were a small group" instead of "when they were a small group." The leaders are reminding the Israelites that God has treated the Israelites who live at that time, and their ancestors who lived long ago, in the same way.

Stop here and discuss how you want to translate the way the singer talks about the Israelites' ancestors. As an activity, pretend you are giving a speech to your people group. Talk about the good things that have happened to your people group throughout your history. How do you show that the people you are talking to are connected to the other members of the group, even the people who lived a long time ago? Pause this audio here.

In a companion phrase, the singer repeats that the Israelites' ancestors were a small group. The singer sings "Yes, they really were few people!" The singer calls them strangers or temporary residents in Canaan. In the next companion phrase, the singer says twice that the small group was wandering in different people's lands. The singer says in the second line that they were wandering through kingdoms, or places where kings ruled. The singer is emphasizing the danger the small group of Israelite ancestors were in. Because they lived in other nations' lands, Abraham and his family did not have any rights or a king who would protect them. Large people groups or nations could have hurt the small group!

The singer contrasts their dangerous situation with the way Yahweh protected them in the next two companion phrases. Yahweh did not allow anyone to oppress the Israelites. In fact, as the singer sings in the next phrase, Yahweh even rebuked kings! Then the singer sings Yahweh's words. These may have been exact words that Yahweh spoke or the kind of things Yahweh would say to the kings. Both statements mean the same thing, to emphasize how serious Yahweh's command to these kings was. Those kings should not harm the Israelites or even touch them! Yahweh calls Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob two names: "my anointed ones" and "prophets." Yahweh is showing how special Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are when he calls them these names.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what are some ways that you rebuke or warn someone not to hurt someone else? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what do you call people whom God or gods speak to? When people choose a certain person for a special task, what do you call the person they choose? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:14–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The singer, who may be a Levite like Asaph, along with a group of Levites
  • The Israelites who are listening
  • The Israelites' ancestors: Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and their families
  • Yahweh
  • And kings

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The Levites continue to sing:

"He, Yahweh, is our God.

His judgments are for all the world.

Remember Yahweh's covenant forever

Remember the promise he made that will last forever.

Yahweh made that agreement with Abraham.

It is the oath that he swore to Isaac.

God renewed this covenant with Jacob as a law.

God made a covenant with Israel that would last forever.

God said, "I will give the land of Canaan to you

as the part you will inherit."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know people become worried or scared when they are waiting for something. I will confirm this promise many times to reassure Abraham's descendants; or
  • I also know the Israelites will face many difficult things, even in the new land I have given them. I want the Israelites to remember how I keep my promises so that they can trust that I will continue to be their God and protect them; or
  • I rule over the whole earth, and I give land to all peoples, not just the Israelites.

Ask the person playing the singer, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • When I serve in Yahweh's presence, there are many things to remind me of what Yahweh is like. However, some of the Israelites live far away and do not get to see what I see every day. I want these Israelites to tell each other stories about their people's history to remind each other of what Yahweh is like; or
  • When I remember what Yahweh is like and what he has done, it is easier for me to trust Yahweh to take care of us. I want the Israelites to feel this too; or
  • I am amazed at how Yahweh has cared for our ancestors and our people for hundreds of years!

Continue the drama.

"When our ancestors were only a few people,

When they were just a small group of nomads

Our ancestors moved around from one country to another

They went from one kingdom to another

But Yahweh did not let anyone mistreat them

He warned kings in order to defend our ancestors

God said, 'Do not harm these people I have chosen.

Do not harm my prophets.'"

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We have been in lands where the kings do not respect or like our people. Sometimes, I feel worried about my family and if they will be safe. But every time, Yahweh has protected us; or
  • Sometimes God told us to move, or sometimes we would move to find better feeding places for our animals. It was difficult sometimes, not knowing where we would live next; or
  • Sometimes our families were in danger because we made mistakes. But Yahweh was still kind and saved us and our families. I am so thankful for what Yahweh has done for us.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:14–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Yahweh is the Israelites' God. The name Yahweh was a very personal name for God that showed the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. The word for God is a general word for God. For more information on Yahweh and God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh and God as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh's judgments are for all the people of the world. Judgement can refer to the way Yahweh rules and the decisions Yahweh makes. Sometimes, Yahweh performed a miracle to punish people, and people would often call these miracles "judgments." A miracle, which people sometimes call a wonder, is something that happens that is very unusual and surprising and seems impossible. For more information on "judgments," refer to miracle in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for judgment as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh made a covenant with Abraham. When people make a covenant, they make a solemn promise to each other that they cannot break. Yahweh promised to give Abraham a land and many descendants. Later, Yahweh confirmed this covenant with Abraham's descendants several times.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for covenant. For more information on covenant, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for covenant as you used in previous passages. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also remembers his word, or everything that he says. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh's word as you have used in previous passages, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about "word."

The Israelites should remember this covenant for a thousand generations. A generation refers to a group of people who live at the same time and belong to about the same age group. For more information on generation, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for generation as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh promised the covenant to Isaac with an oath. When people make a promise to each other, they can swear an oath to make the promise more serious. When someone swears an oath, they say, "I will absolutely keep my promise. If I do not, God may punish me." When God made a promise to people, God often made an oath as well. There is no one higher than God who can punish God if he breaks an oath, so God made these oaths in his own name, which means that God would punish himself if he broke his promise.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for oath. Look up oath in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also confirmed the covenant to Jacob as a statute. The songwriter actually says that this covenant is like a decision, or formal announcement to Jacob, that God will always be with Jacob.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for statute. Look up statute in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The land of Canaan is the portion that the Israelites inherit. When you inherit, you receive a thing or a right from someone else. So here, this means that the Israelites receive the land as their own.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for inherit. Refer to heir in the Master Glossary for more information about inherit. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The land of Canaan is an area of land between the Jordan River in the east and the Mediterranean Sea in the west. People called this land Canaan because the descendants of the man named Canaan lived there. This is the land God promised to give to the Israelites. For more information on Canaan, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Canaan as you used in previous passages.

The Israelites' ancestors were like strangers or foreigners in Canaan. A person is a foreigner in a country when they are in a country that is not where they were born. Strangers or sojourners are people who are foreigners in a country and temporarily live there, even though it is not where those people were born.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for foreigner. Look up foreigner in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites' ancestors wandered from kingdom to kingdom and from nation to nation. A kingdom is the territory or the people that a king rules. A nation is a group of people who belong together, live in the same country, and have the same ruler. For more information on kingdom and nation, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for kingdom and nation as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh tells kings not to harm the Israelites. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a region or a country. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh calls the Israelite ancestors his anointed ones or anointed people. To anoint someone means to pour sweet-smelling oil over someone to show that God has chosen that person for a special role. People used the word anointed, or anointed one, for people whom God had chosen, even if no one had done a ritual where they poured oil on that person's head.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for anointed ones. Look up anoint in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also calls the Israelite ancestors his prophets. A prophet is a person whom God speaks to, so that the person can give messages from God to the people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for prophet. Look up prophet in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 16:23–33
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 16:23–33 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:23–33 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:23–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This song is a Hebrew poem. We recommend that your team complete the "Introduction to Poetry Module 1" before you continue with "Step 2: Setting the Stage."

Stop here, if needed, and review the translation decisions your team has made regarding the translation of poetry. Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, the Levites urged the Israelites to remember the way Yahweh protected the Israelites' ancestors. Because of what Yahweh has done, the singer now urges all people to sing to Yahweh, who is the only real God. Everything and everyone in the world should rejoice and worship Yahweh. Yahweh created everything in the past, rules over everything now, and will come to rule in the future.

To begin this part of the song, the singer sings that all the earth or all the people in the world should sing to Yahweh to respond to the amazing things Yahweh does. People should proclaim or publicly announce Yahweh's salvation. Yahweh's salvation is the way Yahweh saves people. The Israelites should declare Yahweh's glory, or how beautiful, powerful, and majestic Yahweh is. The Israelites should tell all people groups about the wonderful things Yahweh has done!

Everyone should know and praise Yahweh because Yahweh is great. Yahweh is worthy of or deserves everyone's praise. Everyone should fear Yahweh, or treat him with deep respect and honor. Everyone should fear Yahweh above or more than all other gods. These gods are spirit beings that other nations worship as if those gods have power. Sometimes people use wood or metal to make idols, which are statues or pillars to represent their gods. These other gods are just idols, nothing more powerful than a statue or pillar. In fact, in the original language, the word for idols means worthless or nothing. These gods are worthless!

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, or in the cultures around you, what do people make to represent their gods? How would you talk about gods that you do not worship or believe to be real gods? Pause this audio here.

Other people groups claim that their gods have power over the heavens. Sometimes, the heavens can mean the sky or the place where God lives. But Yahweh is the one who actually created both the sky and the place where God lives! Unlike those worthless idols, Yahweh is powerful and created everything. And unlike those idols, Yahweh is great, honorable, and majestic, because he is a king. Anyone who is before Yahweh or near him can see this. In the place where Yahweh lives, are strength and joy, because Yahweh is strong and causes people to feel joy. The singer may be singing about the place where Yahweh lives, in heaven, but the singer may also be talking about the place where Yahweh's presence is. This is the tent with the ark of God, which is where Yahweh meets with the Israelites. Yahweh told the Israelites to put special things, like the ark, in this place to remind them what Yahweh is like. When people see these majestic and beautiful things, people should remember to worship their powerful and majestic God!

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What should a king or great leader be like? What do people know about the leader when they are near the leader? How do people feel when they are near a really good leader? Pause this audio here.

Again, the singer sings that everyone should praise Yahweh! The singer sings to the families of all the people on the earth. All people should ascribe glory and strength to Yahweh. To ascribe means to give or declare that something is true. Yahweh is strong and is glorious, and everyone should humbly declare that! People should give Yahweh the glory that Yahweh's name deserves. Remember, someone's name is their reputation or their character, so Yahweh's name represents Yahweh. When people give glory to Yahweh, they declare how beautiful, powerful, and majestic Yahweh is. Because Yahweh rules over all people, not just the Israelites, Yahweh deserves for all people to praise him!

The singer also urges people to bring an offering to Yahweh and come before him. An offering is a gift or sacrifice that people give to God. People do this in God's presence, which is wherever God has chosen to meet with his people. At this time, this is at the tent with the ark of God. When people praise and worship Yahweh, it is as if people are giving Yahweh a gift or offering. So this offering can mean either a physical gift like an animal, or this offering can also simply be the way that people praise Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kinds of gifts or offerings do people bring to gods or kings? Why do people do this? Pause this audio here.

People should bow down and worship Yahweh, to show that they respect and honor Yahweh. People should worship Yahweh in holy splendor. This could mean that people should come in special, beautiful clothes to worship Yahweh. This could also mean that they should worship Yahweh because his holiness is beautiful. In other words, Yahweh is so special in good ways that everything about Yahweh is beautiful. The beautiful things in Yahweh's holy place of worship, like the ark of God, remind people of how holy and set apart Yahweh is.

The singer tells the earth or the people of the earth to tremble in Yahweh's presence. This describes the way that people humble themselves in a fearful or respectful way before a powerful ruler or before God. People should be humble and respectful because Yahweh created the world and put it firmly in its place. If the world is so strong that nothing can move it, then the God who made the world must be even stronger and more powerful! People should tremble and bow down as they recognize how holy, beautiful, and powerful Yahweh is.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how should people act when they are in front of a very powerful king or leader? What do people do to show their deep respect and fear? Pause this audio here.

Then, the singer sings that the world and everything in it should praise Yahweh! The singer talks about the world like it were a person that can sing and celebrate. Yahweh created everything on the earth. The beautiful, powerful, amazing things on earth all show us what a good creator Yahweh is. It is like the world is praising Yahweh or saying out loud, "Look how well God made us! God really is an amazing creator!"

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of a friend who makes beautiful and amazing things, such as very beautiful clothing or drawings. When you see one of their creations, how do you know who has made it? What do you learn or know about your friend when you look at their creations? How would you describe the way that the creation makes you think about the person who made it? Pause this audio here.

The singer sings that everything Yahweh has created should celebrate Yahweh. The heavens and the earth should rejoice and be glad like a person does. Again, the heavens could be both the sky and the place where God lives. The Israelites should say to the people of other nations that Yahweh is king. The sea and everything in it should resound or shout praise to Yahweh. The fields and everything in them should exult or show their joy. Each tree in a forest should sing for joy in front of Yahweh. Everything on earth should rejoice because Yahweh comes to judge or rule over the earth. Yahweh is a good king who cares for every person and every thing. So everyone and everything should look forward to the way Yahweh rules.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:23–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

Stop here and review as a group: We recommend that your team complete the "Introduction to Poetry Modules 2 & 3" before you continue with "Step 3: Defining the Scenes." Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Everyone should sing to Yahweh and tell others about the wonderful things Yahweh has done! Yahweh is real and powerful, unlike the gods that other nations worship.

In the second scene: The singer sings that all people should give praise and worship Yahweh because Yahweh created the world.

In the third scene: The whole world, even things like the water and the trees, should celebrate that Yahweh is coming to rule over the earth.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The singer, who may be a Levite like Asaph, along with a group of Levites
  • Yahweh
  • All the people of the world
  • And the Israelites

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This passage is part of the same song as last time. This part of the song is also in Psalm 96. Remember, while it is not clear exactly who wrote or sang this song, the singer is probably a Levite like Asaph, who is perhaps singing with a group of Levites. As the singer praises Yahweh, the singer is showing the Israelites how they can worship God and announce to all people that God rules over everything and everyone. This part of the song is in three sections. Throughout this passage, the singer uses a lot of words that mean similar things to emphasize certain instructions and help the Israelites remember the important ideas he is singing about.

Remember, the singer is singing in front of a group of Israelites. In this song, the singer also sings to all the people of the earth. Though all the other people groups are not in front of the singer right now, the Levites and the Israelites are supposed to repeat this song and tell everyone about Yahweh. When people hear about Yahweh, they should praise Yahweh, too! The singer also sings about things like the sky and the seas as if they are people too. The singer does not actually expect the ground or the sky to listen to him and sing as a person does. The singer is reminding the Israelites of the way that everything shows the amazing way Yahweh has created and cares for them. In the same way, the people Yahweh has created should show and tell about the amazing things Yahweh has done to create and care for them.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you are singing songs, when are some times when you would sing to both people who are with you and people who are not with you right now? Pause this audio here.

In the first scene and section, the singer sings that all people should praise Yahweh because of these wonderful things Yahweh has done. And the people should not just quietly appreciate or think about Yahweh. Instead, the singer uses lots of similar words in companion phrases to show that people should talk loudly about Yahweh! People should sing, proclaim, and declare out loud in front of people and nations! These people and nations are all the people in the world who are not Israelites. Yahweh's salvation, his glory, and his marvelous works are all the wonderful ways that Yahweh helps, saves, and cares for people. Yahweh is so wonderful and does such wonderful things that people should proclaim what Yahweh does day to day or every day!

In a companion phrase, the singer explains why all people, not just the Israelites, should praise Yahweh. Yahweh is great and deserves for everyone to praise and fear him because Yahweh is real and powerful. Everyone should fear Yahweh more than all other gods. This does not mean that people should fear those gods as well as Yahweh. Yahweh is above or greater than those gods, so people should fear Yahweh instead. The singer compares Yahweh to the other gods that people worship. While those gods are worthless idols, Yahweh created the heavens! Yahweh created everything, including the sky, so everything belongs to him. Yahweh deserves to rule, and he does. Anyone who is before Yahweh or in Yahweh's presence can see how great and like a king he is. Yahweh is strong and causes joy, and anyone in Yahweh's dwelling place can see this. This dwelling place may be the place where Yahweh lives or the place where people go to worship Yahweh. Yahweh has shown that he is God, so everyone should praise and fear him.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about the way that kings are impressive and great? As an activity, think of a great king or leader in your history. Pretend you were in the grand and beautiful place where that king meets with people, and the king is in front of you. Describe what you would see and feel about the king when you see him in this beautiful place. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the singer repeats three times that all people should ascribe or give praise to Yahweh. In this time, people would often repeat something three times to show that it is important. Notice that in each companion phrase of this section, the singer gradually gives more information as he repeats the instruction to give praise to Yahweh. First, the singer sings about who should praise Yahweh: all people of the world. Then he sings how the people should do this: they should tell Yahweh how glorious and strong Yahweh is. Then the singer sings why the people should do this: because Yahweh deserves it. The singer completes the whole idea in the final phrase and tells the Israelites what they should do. The Israelites should bring an offering and come before Yahweh.

The singer explains where the people should do this: in Yahweh's presence, or at the place where Yahweh has chosen to meet with people. This is the tent that David has just set up for the ark of God. When people come to this tent, they should bring an offering or a gift, and the best offering is their praise! And when people do come and see how holy and beautiful Yahweh is, they should act in a deeply respectful way. The singer uses companion phrases to talk about the respectful ways to worship Yahweh. The word for worship can mean to bow down. People should worship, bow down, or tremble, which are all very respectful and appropriate ways to treat someone who rules and has power. The people worship Yahweh in holy splendor, which can refer to special clothes people wear to honor Yahweh, or Yahweh's holy majesty—Yahweh's greatness. Yahweh's actions always show Yahweh's holy splendor. Yahweh always deserves praise for what Yahweh does, so here the singer is probably singing about Yahweh's holy splendor or greatness.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss how you want to translate when the singer sings to "Worship Yahweh in holy splendor." Some translations state this line so that it can mean either option, while others pick one option to make it clear. Both are acceptable translations. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh has shown that he is powerful and deserves to rule the world because he created or established the world. Nothing else can move the world or change what Yahweh has done. The singer emphasizes this when he says, "Yes! Yahweh has really established the earth!"

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you emphasize that something really happened? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene and section, the singer sings that everything on earth should celebrate Yahweh. This includes the Israelites, who can and should say out loud that Yahweh rules over everything. The singer mentions the heavens, the sea, the fields, and the forest, as well as everything in them, to refer to everything Yahweh has created. The singer also says that everything Yahweh has created should be glad, rejoice, shout, and exult. The singer is saying the same thing with different words. The singer is saying that everything Yahweh has created should celebrate, just like the Israelites do when they announce that their good God, Yahweh, rules. The Israelites should celebrate how Yahweh rules now and how everything looks forward to how Yahweh will continue to rule in the future.

Stop here and discuss whether in your language you would ever talk about the sky or the animals like they were a person. As an activity, describe the way that an animal you take care of greets you and listens to you. Describe the way that an animal would respond to someone who hurts or scares them. Even though this animal is not a person, how does the animal show how good or wonderful you are when they greet you? Discuss how you want to translate the way that the singer says that the earth and sky are glad. You can compare the earth and sky to a person and say, "Earth and sky be glad like people are glad." Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:23–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The singer, who may be a Levite like Asaph, along with a group of Levites
  • Yahweh
  • All the people of the world
  • And the Israelites

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The Levites continued to sing,

"All you people of the world, sing to Yahweh!

Every day, announce the good news that Yahweh saves

Declare among the nations how great God's deeds are

Proclaim his miraculous deeds among all peoples

For Yahweh is great and deserves our greatest praise

People should fear Yahweh more than all the false gods

For all the false gods that other nations worship are only worthless idols

But Yahweh is not worthless. He created the heavens!

Yahweh is glorious and majestic like a king is

Strength and joy are in the place where Yahweh is."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel encouraged when I hear this song. Even though our people have faced many difficult things, our God has always protected us because he is greater than any other people or their gods; or
  • I was surprised when I heard that all people should sing to Yahweh. Usually, people worship the god of their people. I guess Yahweh is the God of all people; or
  • I feel excited when I remember the wonderful things Yahweh has done and when we celebrate and sing together like this. I want to tell people about how I feel and what I remember about Yahweh. I even want to tell people I do not know!

Continue the drama.

"All you peoples on earth, give praise to Yahweh.

Praise him, tell him how great and powerful he is.

Give the honor to Yahweh that he deserves

Bring an offering and come into his presence.

Bow down to worship Yahweh because he is very holy.

All you people who live on earth, humble yourselves in fear in Yahweh's presence!

Indeed, Yahweh has put the world firmly in its place, and nothing can move it."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the singer, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Right now, as we are all gathered here in front of the tent with the ark of God, it is easy to get excited and want to celebrate Yahweh. However, the Israelites should remember to worship Yahweh even on normal days. I hope this song reminds the Israelites of the way they should continue to act; or
  • Yahweh is so great that all people should worship him! I wonder what it would be like if all people worshiped Yahweh as they should. It would be an amazing celebration; or
  • I am amazed at how powerful Yahweh is. When we see the amazing things Yahweh does, we should also treat Yahweh with great respect and fear.

Continue the drama.

"May the heavens rejoice, may the earth be glad

Let people say among nations, 'Yahweh rules as king!'

May the sea and all the creatures that are in the sea shout with praise to Yahweh

May the fields and everything that is in them celebrate

May the trees in the forest shout for joy in Yahweh's presence

because he comes to rule over the whole world."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I remember how the land looks when the plants are growing and the sky is beautiful. It is almost like the world has put on beautiful clothes to celebrate something. Now I see that the world is celebrating Yahweh. Everything Yahweh creates shows how amazing Yahweh is; or
  • I suppose if the world is celebrating Yahweh even though it cannot speak, I should celebrate Yahweh too. And I have lots of ways to sing and say how amazing Yahweh is!

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I carefully made all things to be beautiful and good, and I care about all of it. I am happy when I see the things and people I made living as they should; or
  • I am a just and fair ruler. I want to help and care for everything I have created; or
  • I am glad when my people celebrate me. I love the beautiful songs they sing to me. I love them and want to help them.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:23–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The singer urges people to sing to Yahweh. The name Yahweh was a very personal name for God that showed the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

People should proclaim Yahweh's salvation. Salvation is the way that someone rescues another person from a difficult or dangerous situation.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for salvation or the way that Yahweh saves people. Look up salvation in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

People should also declare Yahweh's glory or how glorious Yahweh is. Glory is the fame and admiration that you gain by doing something impressive. When Yahweh shows his glory, people can see how beautiful, powerful, and majestic Yahweh is. For more information on glory, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for glory as you used in previous passages.

The people should declare Yahweh's glory among the nations. A nation is a group of people who belong together, live in the same country, and have the same ruler. For more information on nations, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for nations as you used in the previous passage.

People should declare the wonders or wonderful things Yahweh has done. Use the same word for wonder, or "wonderful thing that Yahweh has done," as you have used earlier in this song.

Yahweh is worthy of praise, or deserves for everyone to praise him. When you praise Yahweh, you speak out loud good things about Yahweh and to Yahweh because you recognize that Yahweh is good and awesome and that Yahweh deserves great honor. For more information on praise, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for praise as you used in previous passages.

People should fear Yahweh more than they fear any god. To fear Yahweh means to have reverence, honor, and respect for Yahweh. People show this fear by obeying Yahweh because of the respect they have for Yahweh, not because they are afraid of cruelty from Yahweh. For more information on fearing Yahweh, refer to "fear of God" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for "fear of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

The other gods are false gods. For more information on gods, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for gods as you used in previous passages.

These gods are just idols. An idol is an object that people make in order to worship a god.

In the original language, the word for idols means worthless or nothing. For more information on idols, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for idol as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh made the heavens. The word heaven sometimes means the visible sky: the place where birds fly, where we can see clouds, and where we can see stars at night. Heaven also refers to the place where God lives. Sometimes it is not clear if, in a certain passage, the word "heaven" means the visible sky or the place where God lives. Sometimes, both meanings can be true at the same time. Since Yahweh created everything, Yahweh certainly made both the skies and the place where Yahweh lives!

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for heavens. Look up heavens in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Splendor and majesty are in Yahweh's presence, and strength and joy are in his place or dwelling. These are words that describe a powerful and good king. Anyone who is near Yahweh or his presence can see how great, majestic, and strong Yahweh is, and they feel joy because of that.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about the good things people can see and feel about Yahweh. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh's place or dwelling can mean either heaven, where God lives, or the place on earth where God lives among his people. Although God does not need to live in a house, God told the Israelites that he would live among them and meet with them in the special portable place the Israelites prepared for him. At the time when the singer sang this song, this was the tent that David set up for the ark of God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Yahweh's place or dwelling. Look up "house of God" and "heaven" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

People should ascribe glory and strength to Yahweh. To ascribe means to give or say that something belongs to someone. To ascribe glory to Yahweh means to see or understand the glory that Yahweh already has and declare how beautiful, powerful, and majestic Yahweh is.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for ascribe or give. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

People should bring an offering to Yahweh. An offering is a gift or sacrifice that people offer in a ritual to God. An offering can be an object, like an animal, or it can be the way that people worship and praise Yahweh. For more information on offering, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for offering as you used in previous passages.

The people should bow down or worship Yahweh. When people worship Yahweh, they give honor to Yahweh. People in the Bible often showed their worship to God by bowing down to him. In this time, people worshiped God together at special places of worship, like the tent David set up for the ark of God. People worshiped God through their songs, prayers, and sacrifices.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for worship or bow down. Look up worship in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people worship because Yahweh is very holy. This means Yahweh is completely good, completely pure, powerful, and majestic. It means that Yahweh is whole, complete, and unchangeable. But most of all, it means Yahweh is God! For more information on holy, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for holy as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh comes to judge the earth. When a person judges, they decide if what another person does is right or wrong, and therefore if the other person is guilty or innocent of breaking a law. A judge will punish the person if the judge decides that the person is guilty of doing wrong. For more information on judge, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for judge as you used in the previous passage.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 16:34–36
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 16:34–36 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:34–36 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:34–36 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

Remember that in the last passage, the singer sang that everything and everyone in the world should rejoice and worship Yahweh. So now, in this final part of the song, the singer urges the Israelites to give thanks to Yahweh because of the way Yahweh loves. The singer then ends this song of praise with a prayer for God to save his people so that God's people can praise and thank God.

The singer sings that people should thank Yahweh because Yahweh is good and faithfully loves forever. Yahweh shows this love as he keeps his special promise to the Israelites to be their God always and to protect and take care of them.

The singer then teaches the Israelites how to pray to Yahweh when they need help. The singer reminds the people that God saves them, so the Israelites can ask God to save them. The Israelites can pray this when the Israelites are lost, in danger, or when other people have captured the Israelites and taken them to other lands. The Israelites should trust and ask Yahweh to gather and save them from other nations. Then the Israelites can thank Yahweh's holy name, or Yahweh who is holy. The Israelites will "glory" or will be proud to sing praises to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Pretend someone who has never prayed before asks you how to pray. How would you teach that person to pray? Think of some things that you often say to God, or certain ways that you often pray to God. Why do you often say these same things? Who taught you to pray in this way? Pause this audio here.

But the Israelites do not need to wait until the next time Yahweh saves them to praise Yahweh! The singer urges the Israelites to praise Yahweh now! Some translations say to bless Yahweh, which means a similar thing. When people praise or bless Yahweh, they worship Yahweh and thank Yahweh for his goodness and his blessings. Yahweh is the Israelites' God, and Yahweh deserves for the Israelites to praise him from everlasting to everlasting, or forever! The singer is reminding the Israelites how the Israelites should respond to Yahweh and what Yahweh does. The Israelites should praise and thank Yahweh!

When the singer finishes singing, the Israelites respond to the song. The Israelites say "Yes, we agree!" and then they praise Yahweh as they should.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:34–36 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The singer urges the Israelites to thank Yahweh because of the way Yahweh loves.

In the second scene: The singer teaches the people how to pray to Yahweh and reminds them to praise Yahweh, both now and when Yahweh saves them.

In the third scene: The Israelites respond to the song and praise Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The singer, who may be a Levite like Asaph, along with a group of Levites
  • Yahweh
  • And the Israelites

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This part of the song is also in Psalm 106. In the last section, the singer urged everything and everyone to celebrate how Yahweh rules now and look forward to how Yahweh will continue to rule in the future. Because of this, the singer sings in the first section that people should thank Yahweh, because when Yahweh rules, he continues to love in the faithful way he has already shown to the people. The way the singer urges the people to thank Yahweh because of his love is a common reminder that people often use in the Old Testament. The singer is reminding the Israelites how Yahweh described himself to Moses and the Israelite people.

Remember that as the singer praises Yahweh, the singer is showing the Israelites how they can worship God and announce to all people that God rules over everything and everyone. Now, the singer shows the people how to pray to God for help. In the second section of this part of the song, the singer tells the people to cry out to Yahweh or to pray these words to Yahweh. The Israelites should pray that God will save, gather, and deliver them. In response, the Israelites should promise and remind themselves to thank, praise, and rejoice in Yahweh. This is the end of the prayer that the singer teaches to the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Pretend you are teaching the other people in a group how to speak respectfully to an important leader. In the middle of your teaching, tell your listeners something specific that they should say. Pay attention to the way that you show that you have finished telling them what to say and are now going to talk about something else they should do. Discuss how you want to translate the way the singer teaches the prayer in order to show where the prayer ends. Pause this audio here.

Then the singer finishes his song by urging the Israelites one more time to praise Yahweh. Since Yahweh has done great things for the Israelites, the Israelites should praise Yahweh right now! The Israelites should praise Yahweh from everlasting to everlasting, which means they should never give up praising Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how would you tell someone that they should continue to do something forever, such as continue to eat meals or sleep? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller describes what the Israelites do after they hear this song. The Israelites say "Amen," which is a word people say to strongly approve and agree with something they have just heard. Because the Israelites agree, they do what the singer instructed them to do and praise Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what do people say when they strongly agree with something? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:34–36 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The singer, who may be a Levite like Asaph, along with a group of Levites
  • Yahweh
  • And the Israelites

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The Levites sing,

"Give thanks to Yahweh, for he is good. He faithfully loves us forever.

Say to Yahweh, 'O God our Savior, save us!

Gather us together and rescue us from other nations

so that we may thank you who are holy

and we will be proud to sing your praises.'

Praise Yahweh, the God of Israel

Praise him forever and ever."

Then all the people said loudly, "May it be so." And the people praised Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want my people to pray to me for help because I want to help my people. I am powerful and stronger than any problem my people face; or

  • I love the Israelites, even when they are not faithful to me. I punish and correct the Israelites because I love them, just like a father does. I also love to give the Israelites good things and a good relationship with me.

Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored when I remember that Yahweh will never stop loving us, even though we do not always love Yahweh well. I want to thank Yahweh for how faithful he is; or

  • It is hard to praise Yahweh when I am in trouble. All I can think about are the dangerous or bad things around me. I want to pray like the singer has taught us, since I do want Yahweh to help and save me when I am trouble; or
  • Everything the singer has sung is very good and right! We should praise Yahweh, and we will start right now; or
  • People are singing beautifully and making beautiful music on their instruments. I love to gather with people to celebrate Yahweh!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:34–36 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The Israelites should give thanks to Yahweh. The name Yahweh was a very personal name for God that showed the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh shows steadfast love forever. This is the loyal love of God. Some translations call this unfailing or faithful love.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the way Yahweh loves. Look up steadfast love in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people should pray to God, their savior. This word for God is a general word for God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

God is the Israelites' "Savior" or salvation. A savior is someone who rescues people from a difficult or dangerous situation. Some translations say God is the Israelites' salvation, which is the way that someone rescues someone else.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Savior. Look up savior and salvation in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites should pray that Yahweh would save them from the other nations. A nation is a group of people who belong together, live in the same country, and have the same ruler. For more information on nations, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for nations as you used in previous passages.

When God saves them, the Israelites should give thanks to Yahweh, who is holy. This means Yahweh is completely good, completely pure, powerful, and majestic. It means that Yahweh is whole, complete, and unchangeable. But most of all, it means Yahweh is God! For more information on holy, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for holy as you used in previous passages.

The Israelites will also boast or glory in singing praise to God. This means to proudly sing praise to Yahweh. For more information, refer to glory in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for boast or glory as you have used in previous passages.

The singer urges anyone who listens to his song to bless or praise Yahweh. To bless Yahweh means to worship Yahweh and thank him for his goodness and his blessings. Many translations say to praise Yahweh, since it means a similar thing. When you praise Yahweh, you speak out loud good things about Yahweh and to Yahweh, because you recognize that Yahweh is good and awesome and that Yahweh deserves great honor. For more information on praise or bless, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for praise or bless as you used in previous passages.

When the singer finishes his song, the Israelites say, "Amen!" Amen can mean something like, "I accept this," "May it be true," "Indeed," or "Truly." Here, the Israelites are agreeing to what the singer has sung and agreeing to praise Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for amen. Look up amen in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 16:37–43
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 16:37–43 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:37–43 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 16:37–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, the Israelites praised Yahweh as they celebrated that the ark of God was now in Jerusalem. This passage is about the men David chose to serve Yahweh at the places of worship. The Israelites now have two places of worship, one in Gibeon and one in Jerusalem.

Before the song in the last passage, David chose men to serve Yahweh. To begin this passage, the storyteller reminds us that David chose men to serve Yahweh at the place of worship in Jerusalem. David chooses Asaph and some other Levites to serve at the tent with the ark of the covenant of Yahweh, which is another name for the ark of God. The ark of God reminded the Israelites of Yahweh's covenant or special promise that the Israelites would be Yahweh's people. Asaph and the other Levites would regularly serve at the tent with the ark, doing what needed to be done each day. This probably means that Asaph and the Levites would sing the songs that were appropriate for each day and for various festivals. These men may also take care of the tent and help with any sacrifices or offerings.

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of Yahweh's covenant as a group as needed. Pause this audio here.

David also instructs Obed-Edom and 68 relatives or other Levites to serve along with Asaph. Obed-Edom is the son of Jeduthun, and Obed-Edom and Hosah work together as gatekeepers, the men who guard the gate or entrance to the tent area. Men would guard the entrance to keep any unclean or improper people or animals away from the special area. Yahweh commanded the Israelites to keep any unclean people or animals from entering Yahweh's places of worship to show that these places were holy or different from normal areas.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are some holy or special places in your area? How do people show that these are holy or special places? Who can enter these places? What do people have to do to enter these places? Pause this audio here.

David tells Zadok the priest and the other priests to serve at the tabernacle. The tabernacle is a special tent where the Israelites would worship God. The ark of God used to be in the tabernacle. The ark was then in a town called Kiriath Jearim for a while before David and the Levites brought the ark to the new tent in Jerusalem. The tabernacle is at the high place, or place of worship, in Gibeon, which is about 10 kilometers northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a picture of the tabernacle tent as a group. Look at a map of Israel and find the cities of Gibeon and Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

The priests present the burnt offerings at the tabernacle. People would give burnt offerings to Yahweh to show how dedicated those people were to Yahweh. For a burnt offering, the priests would cut up an entire animal and burn it on a special altar, which is the altar for burnt offerings. The priests did this every morning and evening, according to the Law of Yahweh, which Yahweh had commanded the Israelites to do.

Stop here and look at a picture of the altar of burnt offerings as a group. Pause this audio here.

Heman and Jeduthan also serve at the tabernacle with Zadok and the priests. Heman and Jeduthan, along with the other men David has chosen, give thanks to Yahweh because Yahweh faithfully loves forever. Heman and Jeduthan are responsible for, or are in charge of, the trumpets and cymbals. Cymbals are a musical instrument with two metal discs that people strike together in order to make a shrill, clashing sound. Trumpets are long, straight instruments that people make from hammered metal, either silver or bronze. People blow air into trumpets to make a small range of notes.

Stop here and look at a picture of cymbals and a trumpet as a group. What kind of similar instruments do you have in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Heman and Jeduthun are also in charge of the other men who play the other musical instruments for the sacred songs or the songs of praise to God. Jeduthun's sons guard the gate or the entrance to the tabernacle. In this time, people sometimes say the word "sons" to mean relatives. So here, the sons of Jeduthun can mean both Jeduthun's actual sons as well as some members of Jeduthun's clan. This Jeduthan and his sons and relatives are probably different than Obed-Edom's father, Jeduthan.

Then all the people who had gathered to celebrate that the ark of God was in Jerusalem return to their homes. David also returns home to bless his household, which is everyone who lives in his house. Someone's household includes any wives, children, unmarried daughters, and any married sons and their wives and children.
