Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your translation, how would you introduce the actions of the Syrians if their actions are different from what the listener expects? What word or phrase would you use to introduce the actions of the Syrians? Pause this audio here.

"Beyond the Euphrates River" refers to the east side of the Euphrates River from the point of view of a person located in Israel.

In scene three, when someone tells David what the Syrians are doing, David gathers all the fighting troops of Israel, and they cross the Jordan. David comes to the Syrians and arranges his soldiers in battle positions against the Syrians. Then the Syrians fight against David. Even though the storyteller mentions only David's name here and in the rest of the story many times, we know that both David and David's army fought against the Syrians.

But the Syrians run away from the army of Israel, and David kills 7,000 of their chariots and 40,000 of their foot soldiers. Here, the storyteller is saying that even though the Syrians attacked first, the Syrians ran away from David's army.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your translation, what word or phrase would you use to describe the fact that even though the Syrians attacked first, the Syrians ran away from David's army? Pause this audio here.

We do not know the meaning of "7,000 chariots" for sure. It may refer to "7,000 chariot drivers" or "7,000 men from chariot teams." Both interpretations are possible.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What translation do you want to use for "7,000 chariots"? Pause this audio here.

David also kills Shophach, the commander of the Syrian army.

In scene four, when the servants or supporters of Hadadezer realize that the Israelites have defeated the Syrians, they make peace with David and become David's servants. So the Syrians are not willing to help the Ammonites again. "Make peace" means that the servants or supporters of Hadadezer accepted the terms for peace that David's army demanded.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture or history, what did weak nations do in order to be able to live in peace with a strong or a victorious nation that just won a great battle? How would you describe what the weak nations did? Pause this audio here.

"Servants of Hadadezer" are of high status, and they are not like household servants. You can also call these people "followers" or "supporters" of Hadadezer.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when a group of people became servants of another group. What word or phrase did you use to describe these servants? Pause this audio here.

At the end of scene four, the storyteller says that the Syrians are not willing to help the Ammonites again.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your translation, how will you describe the storyteller's final statement about how the Syrians are unwilling to help the Ammonites again? What word or phrase would you use to describe the transition from the actions of Hadadezer's servants to the storyteller's final statement? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 19:10–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Joab
  • Troops who fight with Joab
  • Abishai
  • Troops who fight with Abishai
  • The Syrian army
  • The Ammonite army
  • Someone who tells David what the Syrians are doing
  • David
  • Syrian messengers who call Hadadezer's army to fight
  • Shophach, the commander of Hadadezer's army
  • The Syrian army under Shophach
  • And servants of Hadadezer

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In scene one, Joab realizes that he would have to fight in the front and behind at the same time. So, Joab selects the best fighters of the army of Israel and arranges them in battle positions to fight against Aram, the Syrians. Joab puts the rest of the men under the command of Abishai, his brother, and Joab arranges them in battle positions to fight against the Ammonites. Joab tells Abishai that if the Arameans or Syrians are too strong for Joab, then Abishai shall help Joab, but if the Ammonites are too strong for Abishai, then Joab shall help Abishai. Joab tells Abishai to be strong and courageous for the Israelites and the cities of their God. Joab also says, "May Yahweh do what is good in Yahweh's sight."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am concerned for my soldiers; or
  • I am very nervous as unexpected things can happen during a battle; or
  • I will fight with the best of my ability to be a good example for my soldiers.

Ask the person playing Abishai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We cannot lose. We are the only ones who can stop the Syrian army from destroying the nation of Israel; or
  • I will trust Yahweh to make us win this battle; or
  • I am very nervous as the enemies are on both sides of the army of Israel.

Continue the drama.

Then, Joab and the troops with Joab move forward to fight the Syrians, and the Syrians flee before Joab. When the Ammonites see that the Syrians are running away from Joab and Joab's soldiers, the Ammonites also run away from Joab's brother, Abishai, and go back inside the city. Then Joab comes back to Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad for the soldiers who died during the battle, but I am happy that we won the battle; or
  • I am thankful to Yahweh that he let me go home safely to my family; or
  • I am very proud of the army I lead.

Ask the person playing Abishai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very proud to fight with my brother, Joab; or
  • I am very happy to go home; or
  • I thank Yahweh a lot for this victory.

Continue the drama.

In scene two, when the Syrians realize that the Israelites have defeated them, the Syrians send messengers to get other Syrians from beyond the Euphrates River, with Shophach, the commander of Hadadezer's army, as their leader.

In scene three, when someone tells David what the Syrians are doing, David gathers all the fighting troops of Israel and crosses the Jordan. David comes to the Syrians and arranges his soldiers in battle positions against the Syrians.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am determined to defend the nation of Israel; or
  • I feel stressed as I am responsible to lead the army of Israel; or
  • I will need to figure out ways to defeat the Syrians together with my military leaders.

Continue the drama.

When David arranges his soldiers in battle positions against the Syrians, the Syrians fight against David. But the Syrians run away before the army of Israel, and David kills 7,000 of their chariot drivers and 40,000 of their foot soldiers. David also kills Shophach, the commander of the Syrian army.

In scene four, when the servants of Hadadezer realize that the Israelites have defeated the Syrians, they make peace with David and become David's servants. So the Syrians are not willing to help the Ammonites again.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing one of the servants of Hadadezer, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will do everything in my power to protect my family and country; or
  • I am afraid to fight against David and David's army; or
  • I want to have King David's protection.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 19:10–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Arameans are people from Syria who people also call Syrians. Use the same word or phrase for "Arameans" or "Syrians" as you used in previous passages.

Use the same word or phrase for God and Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on God and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

A city is a large town with a wall around it. Use the same word for city as you have used in previous passages.

Chariots in Syria at that time were light vehicles with two wheels and six or eight spokes on each wheel. Use the same word or phrase for chariot as you used in previous passages.

Shophach is the military commander of Hadadezer's army. Use the same word for a military commander as you have used in previous passages.

In this story, the servants of Hadadezer were more like supporters of the king Hadadezer. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary. Because these people were not household servants, you may need to call these people Hadadezer's followers or supporters.

In this story, "make peace" means that the servants or supporters of Hadadezer surrendered and stopped fighting David's army. These people became David's servants. For more information on peace, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 20:1–3
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 20:1–3 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:1–3 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:1–3 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, King David's army defeated the Aramean, or Syrian, army, so that the Syrian army could no longer help the Ammonites. In this story, Joab leads the Israelite army to attack the Ammonites. Joab besieges Rabbah and defeats the city. King David comes and takes the crown of Ammon and makes the Ammonite people become David's slaves. Then David returns to Jerusalem with his army.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Ammon as a group. Pay close attention to the location of Jerusalem and Rabbah. Pause this audio here.

Joab was David's top military commander. In a previous story, David sent Joab to lead the army in a battle against the Ammonites. In this story, Joab is in charge of the same task again while David stays in Jerusalem. This happens in the spring of the year, a time when kings normally go to war. In Israel, spring is the time of year after the rainy season has ended. During the rainy season, the ground would become muddy, and the soldiers would have a difficult time traveling, so most kings would wait until the rains were over before they would send an army out to fight.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: Name some activities that, in your country, you only do during a specific season of the year. What are the reasons why you only do those activities during that time of year? Name some other activities that most people would never do during a particular season of the year. What are the reasons why you would never do those activities during that time of year? Pause this audio here.

Joab leads a siege of Rabbah, the capital city of Ammon. This means that the Israelite army surrounded the city of Rabbah and prevented the people inside from coming out. The people inside Rabbah could not get outside the city to get food, and once their food supplies ran out, they would starve to death. Joab only needed to wait outside the city to attack any Ammonite soldiers who tried to get out, or for the people inside the city to give up.

Stop here and tell a story about a military battle. If you know a story about an army that is surrounded or trapped in a place where they cannot escape and cannot get food, that would be the best story to tell. Pause this audio here.

After some time, Joab was able to get his army inside the walls of Rabbah and overthrow the city. Then King David left Jerusalem and came to Rabbah. David took the crown of the king of the Ammonites. In most societies with a king, the king wears special clothes and special jewelry to show that he is the king. A crown is a special piece of jewelry. The king wore a crown on top of his head, like a hat. Only the king can wear the crown. People used expensive materials like gold and precious stones to make crowns. The size and value of the crown showed that the person who wore the crown was a wealthy and powerful person. A crown is also a symbol of authority. King Saul had a crown, and a messenger brought this crown to David after Saul died.

Stop here and look at a picture of a crown. Pause this audio here.

The Ammonites had made their king's crown with gold and precious stones. This crown weighed 34 kilograms, which means it was unusually large. The storyteller tells us that David put this crown on his head, which symbolized that David now had power over the Ammonites and over their land.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your culture, how do the leaders show that they are leaders? How do those in power show that they have power? If one person takes power away from another person, does that person do anything to show they now have power? Pause this audio here.

David brings two things out of the city of Rabbah: plunder and people. Plunder refers to material goods that once belonged to the people who lived in Rabbah. These things probably included food, clothing, animals, and materials like wood or stone. King David took these things in order to make his own people richer and also to make the Ammonites too weak to fight back.

David also brought out the Ammonite people from inside the city of Rabbah and made them do hard physical work. It was a common practice at that time for people who won a battle to make the people they had conquered become their slaves. David probably made the Ammonites do work like sawing wood, digging in the ground, and cutting stone. The storyteller says that David did this to all of the Ammonite people, not just the people who lived in Rabbah. Bible scholars believe that after the war with Ammon, David needed laborers to rebuild the damaged and destroyed structures throughout the country. David probably used the Ammonite people to do this rebuilding. Finally, David and his army returned to Jerusalem.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:1–3 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: Joab leads the Israelite army and attacks the Ammonites while David stays in Jerusalem. Joab fights against the Ammonites and attacks their capital city, Rabbah.

In the second scene: David comes to Rabbah and takes the crown of the Ammonites. David takes plunder, or material goods, out of Rabbah and makes the Ammonite people into slaves. Then David returns to Jerusalem with his army.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Joab
  • Israelite soldiers
  • Ammonite soldiers
  • And the Ammonite people

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Joab and the Israelite army march out to fight against the Ammonites. This part of the story is also in the beginning of 2 Samuel chapter 11. If you have already translated 2 Samuel, it would be helpful to go back and listen to the very beginning of chapter 11 to remind yourselves how you translated it.

Stop here and listen to the first part of the story in 2 Samuel chapter 11. Listen for any ways that the story in 2 Samuel is a little different from the story in 1 Chronicles. Pause this audio here.

Joab leads the Israelite army to Ammon in "the spring of the year," which refers to March or April according to the modern calendar. This time of year is also shortly after the new year or the first month of the year, according to the Hebrew calendar. This time of year comes after the cold season, when temperatures begin to warm again. The storyteller says that "the spring of the year" is the time that kings normally go off to war. In the Middle East, most of the rain falls from November to February. During this time, the land becomes muddy, and it is difficult to travel. This is not a good time for the army to go out and fight, so a king or military leader usually waited until after the rains ended to send the army into battle.

Stop here and discuss how your team will translate this phrase for this time of year. Pause the audio here.

But this time, when it is time to go fight, Joab leads the Israelite army to fight the Ammonites, while King David stays in Jerusalem. Joab and the army ravage the country, which means they cause a great deal of damage and destruction. Joab and the army probably cause severe damage or even completely destroy villages, farms, roadways, bridges, waterways, or other structures that you might find in the land.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will describe what Joab did to the country of the Ammonites. Pause this audio here.

It is only after this that Joab and the army attack the capital city, Rabbah. When the Israelite army besieges Rabbah, it means that after the Israelite army fights against the Ammonites, the Ammonite survivors go inside the walls of Rabbah and close all the entrances to prevent the Israelite army from entering the city. Then the Israelite army surrounds Rabbah and waits for the people inside to begin to starve. The Israelite army hopes that the Ammonites will give up, or that the defenders of the city will become so weak from lack of food that the Israelites can defeat the Ammonites easily.

Stop here and discuss how you will describe the siege of Rabbah. Pause the audio here.

Joab succeeds and conquers Rabbah. The king of the Ammonites is probably inside Rabbah, so when the Israelites conquer the city, it means the Israelites conquer all the Ammonite people.

In the second scene, King David leaves Jerusalem and comes to Rabbah. You can listen to this part of the story at the end of 2 Samuel chapter 12. If you have already translated 2 Samuel, it would be helpful to go back and listen to the last part of chapter 12 to remind you of how you translated it.

Stop here and listen to the end of 2 Samuel chapter 12. Listen for any ways that the story in 2 Samuel is a little different from the story in 1 Chronicles. Pause this audio here.

When King David comes to Rabbah, David takes a crown from the Ammonites. Bible scholars disagree as to whether this crown belonged to the King of Ammon or if it was a decoration on the head of an idol that represented the Ammonite god Molech. The reason for this disagreement is that the name "Molech" and the word "king" are very similar in the Hebrew language. Although the king is usually the one who wears the crown, this crown weighed 34 kilograms. This crown was probably too heavy for a person to wear on his head. It would make more sense if the crown were on the head of an idol.

Stop here and find an object that weighs around 34 kilograms. See if someone can lift that object and transfer its weight to the head of a person. Be careful not to hurt that person! After you complete this activity, do you think David was able to put the 34-kilogram crown on his head? Pause this audio here.

Regardless of who the crown originally belonged to, the symbolism was the same. When David puts the crown on his head, it shows that David has defeated the Ammonites.

Stop here and discuss whether you will translate this phrase to mean that David took the crown from the King of Ammon or that David took the crown from Molech. Pause the audio here.

It is also not clear whether David puts the entire Ammonite crown on his head, or whether David takes a jewel out of the crown and puts the jewel into David's own crown. Since the entire crown weighs 34 kilograms, it would be difficult, or maybe even dangerous, for David to put the crown on his head. Many Bible scholars think that it makes more sense for David to take the jewel out of the Ammonite crown and put it in David's own crown.

Stop here and discuss whether you will translate this sentence to mean that David put the entire Ammonite crown on his head, or that David took a jewel out of the Ammonite crown and put it in his own crown. Pause this audio here.

David takes plunder out of Rabbah. This means that after David conquers the city, David and the Israelite army take many valuable goods with them. This was common practice after a military leader conquered a city or settlement. As the soldiers went through the city, they would take any items they considered valuable out of homes or shops. This does not necessarily mean that David personally goes through the city and takes all the valuable goods for himself. It is more likely that David tells his military commanders to go through the city and bring valuable goods back to Israel.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: In your language, how do you talk about a situation where a leader gives an order and then his followers do what he says? Who gets the credit or blame for that situation? Pause this audio here.

David not only makes the people of Rabbah slaves, but David also made slaves of all the people who live in all the other Ammonite towns. David makes the people do hard physical labor using different kinds of tools. The tools the storyteller mentions here are probably different kinds of tools that people use for cutting wood, digging in the ground, or shaping stone for use in buildings, walls, and other structures. The storyteller names three kinds of tools that the Ammonite people used during their hard labor.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: Talk about the tools that you use to cut wood, to chisel stone, and to dig. Think of as many different words as you can in your language for these kinds of tools. Pause this audio here.

Finally, David goes back to Jerusalem. When it says that "all the people" went with David, this does not mean that every single Israelite and every single Ammonite went with him. David probably took most of his army, but left a few soldiers behind to oversee the slaves that worked in Ammon. It is possible that David might have taken some Ammonite people with him back to Israel, but David also needed those Ammonite slaves to stay in Ammon and to do physical labor and produce goods for David. So when the storyteller says that "all the people" went with David, this is just a quick way to say that the war was over and most of the Israelite army left Ammon.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:1–3 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Joab
  • Israelite soldiers
  • Ammonite soldiers
  • And the Ammonite people

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Joab led the Israelite army to Ammon and attacked the Ammonites, while King David remained in Jerusalem. Joab destroyed the country and besieged Rabbah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I cannot wait to finally defeat the Ammonites; or
  • I hope that this time we will finally be successful; or
  • I really want to show King David that he can count on me to win battles for him.

Ask the people playing the Israelite soldiers "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • I hope we can end this war quickly; or
  • I am angry at the Ammonites and want to attack them; or
  • I hope that I do not die in this war!

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I trust Joab and know he will get the job done; or
  • I am so mad at the King of Ammon for how he treated my messengers! or
  • It will be very good for our country to get the resources that the Ammonites have.

Ask the people playing the Ammonite soldiers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I do not know if we can win this battle by ourselves; or
  • I am afraid because we are probably going to lose this war; or
  • I am angry that we have to fight against David's army because our king was disrespectful to David.

Continue the drama.

Joab defeated Rabbah, and King David came to the city. David took the crown of the Ammonites and wore it himself. David took valuable goods out of the city of Rabbah and made the Ammonite people do hard physical labor. Then David and the army went back to Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very pleased that we had such a successful campaign! or
  • I am glad the war is over, and that I can go back home; or
  • I am excited that we got so many valuable things from Rabbah.

Ask the people playing the Israelite soldiers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am so happy we won! or
  • I am excited to take all this plunder back to Israel; or
  • I am glad that our king now controls the territory of the Ammonites; or
  • I am relieved that I can finally go home and see my family.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased that Joab and our army did such a good job; or
  • I can relax now that I have avenged the wrong that the King of Ammon did to me; or
  • I am happy that we got so much good plunder from Rabbah; or
  • Now we have to figure out how to keep this territory under control.

Ask the people playing the Ammonite people, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am depressed because many of my people are dead, and now I am a slave; or
  • I am angry that the Israelites have taken over my country; or
  • I am afraid of what may happen to my children and my country. Will things ever go back to the way they were?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:1–3 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Before you begin this step, remember that you can listen to part of this story at the beginning of 2 Samuel chapter 11. If you have already translated 2 Samuel, it would be helpful to go back and listen to the very beginning of chapter 11 to remind you of how you translated it.

Stop here and listen to the first few sentences of 2 Samuel chapter 11. Pay attention to how you translated any key terms. Pause this audio here.

This story begins in the spring, a time when kings normally go out to battle. Use the same word for king as you have used in previous passages, and remember that king is in the Master Glossary.

When Joab came to the capital city, Rabbah, he besieged it. A siege happens when an army surrounds a city and traps all the people inside so that they cannot come out and get food, water, or other supplies.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the word "siege." See the word siege in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have used this word before in other books of the Bible, be sure to translate it in the same way that you did there. Pause this audio here.

Joab overthrew Rabbah, and then King David came to the city. You can listen to this part of the story in 2 Samuel at the end of chapter 12. If you have already translated 2 Samuel, it would be helpful to go back and listen again to this part of the story in chapter 12.

Stop here and listen to the end of 2 Samuel chapter 12. Pay attention to how you translated any key terms. Pause this audio here.

David symbolized his victory over the Ammonites by taking their crown and wearing it himself. A crown is a valuable piece of jewelry that the king wears on his head. People made crowns of gold and valuable stones, and a crown showed that the person who wore it was rich and powerful.

Stop here and look at a picture of a crown. Decide how you will translate the word crown. If you have translated this word in another book of the Bible, be sure to translate it the same way here. Pause this audio here.

David brought out a large amount of plunder from the city of Rabbah. Plunder is valuable goods that an army that conquers takes from the people it has conquered. This might include food, clothes, livestock, or materials like wood or stone.

Stop here and discuss what word you will use for plunder. If you have translated this word in another book of the Bible, make sure to translate it the same way here as you did there. Pause this audio here.

David gathered the Ammonites who survived and made them work as slaves. David made the people work with three different kinds of tools: saws, iron picks, and iron axes. People use saws to cut wood. People made saws out of metal. Saws have sharp points on them to help cut through the wood. One person could operate a smaller saw, but a larger saw might require two people to use it. The tool mentioned here could also be used to cut stone.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate the word "saw." Pause this audio here.

"Iron picks" means an iron tool that was sharp and could cut other things. We do not know if this was a large tool or a small tool. People could use a tool like an iron pick to work with stone. "Iron axes" refers to an iron tool that could cut wood or other things. Bible scholars are not sure what the difference is between an "iron pick" and an "iron axe." The most important details are that people made these tools out of iron and used them to cut other things.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate "iron picks" and "iron axes."

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 20:4–8
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 20:4–8 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:4–8 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:4–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, King David's army defeated and captured the people of the town of Rabbah. This story is about several battles that King David's army fights against the Philistines. Three of David's men defeat strong, tall Philistines, and the Israelite army defeats the Philistine army.

Some time after the previous story, the Philistines and Israelites fight a battle at the town of Gezer. Gezer is a Philistine town about 32 kilometers away from the Israelites' capital city of Jerusalem. The city of Gezer is in an area of low hills where the Israelites and Philistines would often fight. During this battle, one of David's strong warriors fights a strong Philistine. This warrior who fights for David is Sibbecai. Sibbecai is a Hushathite. Descendants of a man named Hushah live in a town called Hushah in Judah. So this could mean that Sibbecai is from the Hushathite clan or from the Hushathite town "Hushah." Sibbecai is one of the mighty men of David's army.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Philistia as a group. Find the cities of Jerusalem and Gezer. Pause this audio here.

Sibbecai kills Sippai, a Philistine. Sippai is a descendant of the Rephaites. The Rephaites were descendants of Rapha. The Rephaites were very tall and strong, so some people called them giants. Sometimes, people would call any tall person a Rephaite or a giant. When the Israelites first came to the land of Canaan, the Israelites killed most of the Rephaites. The only Rephaites who remained lived in the Philistine cities of Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are some common features that people groups in your area have? Perhaps the people from one group are often tall, or people from another group are very good farmers. If you heard someone was from one of those people groups, what would you assume about that person? Pause this audio here.

The Israelites defeat the Philistines in this battle. Some translations say that the Israelites make the Philistines serve the Israelites as slaves.

Later, during another battle with the Philistines, another of David's strong warriors kills another giant or tall Philistine. This warrior who fights for David is Elhanan, Jair's son. Elhanan kills a Philistine named Lahmi. Lahmi is the brother of Goliath. Goliath was the giant that David killed before David became king. Both Lahmi and Goliath are Gittites, which means these men lived in the Philistine city of Gath.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Philistia as a group. Find the city of Gath. Pause this audio here.

Lahmi had a very impressive spear. A spear is a long, straight rod with a sharp end. Soldiers would thrust this weapon at an enemy. Normally, the rod would be large enough for a man to hold comfortably in his grip. The storyteller says the rod of this spear was like a weaver's rod.

A weaver is a person who makes cloth on a loom. In this time, a loom had two long posts in the ground with a beam or rod across them. The storyteller could mean that the spear was very thick, like this beam, because Lahmi's hands were so large and Lahmi would need a larger rod to hold comfortably in his hand. But this could also mean that there was a loop of cord at the end of the spear to help Lahmi throw the spear. This looked like the rod with a loop of cord that weavers would use to lift threads on a loom. Either way, this is an unusual and impressive weapon.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear as a group. What weapons do you have in your culture that look like this spear? What would be a very impressive weapon in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Later, the Israelites fight another battle with the Philistines near the Philistine city of Gath.

Another tall Philistine challenges and insults the Israelites. The storyteller does not include what the Philistine says, but the Philistine probably says that the God of Israel could not protect the Israelites. This Philistine was an unusual man who had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot. This Philistine is also a descendant of Rapha. This time, David's nephew Jonathan kills this Philistine. Jonathan is the son of David's brother Shimea.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what do people think about a person whose body looks different than other people's bodies, such as someone with an extra finger, or someone who is much taller than most people? Pause this audio here.

David and his army defeat and kill these three Philistines from Gath. Remember that a few passages ago, the storyteller said that Yahweh caused David to win wherever David went. The storyteller does not specifically mention God, but these stories show how God helped David and David's people win.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:4–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The Israelites fight against the Philistines. During a battle, the Israelite warrior Sibbecai kills the Philistine Sippai. The Israelites defeat the Philistines.

In the second scene: During another battle with the Philistines, the Israelite warrior Elhanan kills the Philistine Lahmi.

In the third scene: During another battle with the Philistines, the Israelite warrior Jonathan kills the Philistine man with six fingers on each hand.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller summarizes the story when he says that David and the Israelites defeat these giant men from Gath.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The Philistine army
  • The Israelite army
  • Sibbecai the Hushathite
  • Sippai the Philistine
  • The Rephaites or giant people
  • Elhanan, son of Jair
  • Lahmi the Gittite, whose brother is Goliath
  • The Philistine with six fingers on each hand
  • Jonathan, son of David's brother Shimea
  • And David

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This section gives brief summaries of battles the Israelites fight with the Philistines. This part of the story is also at the end of 2 Samuel 21:18–22. If you have already translated 2 Samuel, you can go back and listen to the end of chapter 21 to remember how you translated it. Some of the names here sound a little different than in the 2 Samuel story.

Stop here and listen to the last part of the story in 2 Samuel 21:18–22. Listen for any ways that the story in 2 Samuel is a little different from the story in 1 Chronicles. Pause this audio here.

This story has three battles. In each battle, a different Israelite warrior fights and kills a Philistine warrior. The story is about the warriors, so the storyteller does not include a lot of other information about the battles. The storyteller does not even mention who wins two of the battles. Instead, the storyteller focuses on how Israelite warriors defeat three Philistines who were descendants of Rephaites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about something that happened three times in your people's history. Briefly tell the others in the group about these three events. Discuss how you chose to focus on the event that happened three times. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the Israelite army fights a battle against the Philistine army. The two armies fight at the town of Gezer. During this battle, one of David's warriors, Sibbecai, kills the tall and strong Sippai. The Israelites win this battle against the Philistines. The Israelite army may return home after this battle.

The second scene happens some time after the first battle. Again, the Israelites fight a battle against the Philistines. It is not clear where this battle happened. During this battle, one of the Israelites, Elhanan, kills the tall Philistine Lahmi. Lahmi is Goliath's brother. Lahmi has a big spear. Though the storyteller does not mention it, the Israelites probably win this battle too.

The third scene again happens some time after the second battle. Again, the Israelites fight a battle against the Philistines, this time near the city of Gath. During this battle, one of the Philistines taunts or insults the Israelites. This Philistine has six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot. The storyteller does not tell us this Philistine's name. David's nephew, Jonathan, kills this tall and strong Philistine. Again, though the storyteller does not mention it, the Israelites probably win this battle.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you talk about warriors and how they defeated their enemies? What information do you include about the warriors? Pause this audio here.

The last scene is a summary of how David's warriors killed the Philistines. All three of these Philistine warriors are tall and strong men who are descendants of the Rephaites. These three Philistines are all from the Philistine city of Gath. The storyteller says the Philistines fall at the hands of David and David's men. This means that David and his men kill these Philistines.

We are not sure if David actually led his warriors into these battles. The storyteller probably says that "David and his men" defeat the Philistines because David is the king and is responsible for what his army does.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: List some things that your leaders or leaders in your area have done or decisions those leaders have made. Discuss whether the leader was able to do those things by themselves or if they had other people help them. How do you talk about things that the leader and his people do while the leader rules, even if the leader does not directly do the action himself? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:4–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The Philistine army
  • The Israelite army
  • Sibbecai the Hushathite
  • Sippai the Philistine
  • The Rephaites or giant people
  • Elhanan, son of Jair
  • Lahmi the Gittite, whose brother is Goliath
  • The Philistine with six fingers on each hand
  • Jonathan, son of David's brother Shimea
  • And David

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Some time later, the Israelites fought a battle against the Philistines at Gezer. In this battle, Sibbecai the Hushathite killed a giant named Sippai. Sippai was a descendant of a giant called Rapha. The Israelites won against the Philistines.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed that we defeated the Philistines even though the Philistines had such a tall and strong warrior with them! or
  • I am so glad that we defeated the Philistines! We can keep our people safe! or
  • I saw how Sibbecai killed that huge Philistine. I am glad that Sibbecai is in our army!

Continue the drama.

During another battle with the Philistines, Jair's son Elhanan killed Lahmi. Lahmi was the brother of Goliath from Gath. Lahmi had a spear that was very thick and heavy. The spear's handle was as large as the piece of wood that people use to make cloth.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elhanan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I could see that Lahmi was a strong warrior with a dangerous weapon. I knew it would be a hard fight, but I also knew that Yahweh would help me win; or
  • This man I killed was the brother of Goliath. I am proud that I killed a giant like our king David did; or
  • I want to protect my people, so I will fight even dangerous enemies like Lahmi.

Continue the drama.

In yet another battle at Gath, the Israelites fought against a very large Philistine. This Philistine had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, so he had 12 fingers and 12 toes. He, too, was a descendant of Rapha. This Philistine shouted insults at the Israelites. So Jonathan killed this Philistine. Jonathan was the son of David's brother Shimea.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Philistine with six fingers on each hand, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so big and strong that no one can defeat me; or
  • I do not care about the Israelites or their god. I am better than any Israelite warrior; or
  • I will taunt the Israelites and make them nervous before we fight. Perhaps that will help us win.

Ask the person playing Jonathan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I felt a little nervous when I saw how large this man was, but I would not let him continue to say these insults about us; or
  • I was angry when I heard what this Philistine said; or
  • I was not worried about the way this Philistine spoke. I knew with Yahweh's help we would be able to defeat this man and his fellow Philistines.

Continue the drama.

These Philistine men were sons of the giants from the town of Gath. David and his warriors killed these Philistines.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Philistines, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed that the Israelites continue to defeat us, even though we have such tall and strong warriors! or
  • Surely the Israelites' God helps them win these battles; or
  • I feel discouraged that we have lost so many battles.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so thankful that Yahweh has caused the Israelites to win so many battles! I know we could not have done this without Yahweh's help; or
  • I am honored that such strong warriors chose to defend me and the other Israelites; or
  • Even if the Philistines chose to attack again, I am not worried. Yahweh will help us win.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 20:4–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

These Philistine warriors are descendants of the Rephaites. A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. The Rephaites were tall people, or giants. For more information on descendants, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for descendants as you used in previous passages.

Lahmi the Philistine has a very impressive spear. A spear is a long, straight rod with a sharp end. People normally made spears out of wood with a metal tip. A spear was usually as long as a man was tall, and a man could wrap his hand around the rod of a spear. Usually, people thrust a spear at an enemy, but occasionally they would throw a spear. Use the same word or phrase for spear as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a spear again as a group as needed. Pause this audio here.

This spear was like a weaver's rod. A weaver is someone who crosses threads over and under each other on a loom to make cloth. The weaver's rod could be a thick beam on their loom or a piece of wood with a loop of cord at the end.

Stop here and look at a picture of a weaver's rod as a group. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for weaver's rod. Use the same word or phrase you used for weaver's rod in 1 Chronicles 11:23. Pause this audio here.

The Philistine with six fingers challenged and insulted Israel, which means that he mocked the people of Israel. Use the same word for Israel as you have used previously, and see the Master Glossary for more information about Israel.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 21:1–6
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 21:1–6 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:1–6 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, David was at war with the Philistines. The storyteller listed the different victories that David's soldiers won over very strong enemies. Now, the storyteller begins to tell about a new event that happened during David's reign as king. This is a similar story to 2 Samuel 24:1–9, but this story has some differences.

Stop here and listen to your story in 2 Samuel 24:1–9, if you have already translated it. Notice the differences between the way the storyteller tells the story in 2 Samuel and the way he tells it here. Pause this audio here.

In this story, the storyteller says it was Yahweh who caused David to take the census. The storyteller says that Satan rose up against, or brought trouble to, David and caused him to take a census of the people. The name Satan means adversary, and it is the name for the devil, the leader of all evil spirits. Satan tempts people to disobey Yahweh. It is likely that Yahweh permitted Satan to influence David to act in a way that displeased Yahweh. As a result, Yahweh is angry with his people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, when someone does something wrong, who or what do they usually blame? Talk about a time, in your culture, when someone blamed a spiritual being for influencing them to do something bad. Pause this audio here.

David orders Joab, the commander of Israel's army, and the other army commanders who were under Joab's authority, to take a census of the people of Israel. We know from 2 Samuel that this was a military census. Therefore, they only counted the number of fighting men in Israel. It is unclear why it was wrong in Yahweh's eyes to count the number of fighting men in the kingdom. Many scholars have suggested that the real sin was that David wanted recognition for being a successful king, and David did not trust that Yahweh would provide enough people.

David orders the men to go from Beersheba to Dan and count all of the fighting men in Israel. This means Joab would only count men who were physically able to serve in the military. From Beersheba to Dan was another way to say "go through all of Israel from one end to the other," since Dan was in the far north and Beersheba was in the far south of Israel. Once the commanders have counted the fighting men in Israel, David wants Joab to return and report the number to David.

Stop here and look as a group at a map of Dan and Beersheba in the territory of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Joab replies respectfully to King David, though he does not agree with David. Joab tells David that he, too, desires Yahweh, David's God, to increase David's army a hundred times over, or to largely increase the number of men. But Joab respectfully asks David, "Are the people not your servants already?" to imply that the people were already loyal and served David as their king. Joab does not approve of David's decision. Joab implies that to take the census will make Israel guilty in Yahweh's eyes because David is showing that he does not trust Yahweh.

But King David's word overruled Joab, which means that David did not change his mind and continued to firmly command that Joab take the census. So, Joab and his commanders left the king and followed David's order to begin to count the fighting men of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do government authorities respond when people question their decisions? Pause this audio here.

Joab traveled throughout all the land of Israel and counted the fighting men in each place that he visited. We do not know the exact route Joab took to count the Israelites. But at the end of his journey, Joab returned to Jerusalem to report the census numbers to David.

In total, Joab counted 1,100,000 fighting men, or men who drew the sword, in all of Israel, and 470,000 fighting men in Judah. We do not know if Judah is included in the total for all of Israel or if the 470,000 men are in addition to the 1,100,000 fighting men. As you remember, the nation of Israel had two main territories. The southern part was called Judah, while the northern part was called Israel and included the territories of all the other tribes.

Stop here and look as a group at the map of Israel that shows Judah within the nation of Israel. Pause this audio here.

It is unclear why these numbers do not match the numbers the storyteller gave in 2 Samuel, but it is best to translate the numbers as the storyteller gives them in each story.

The storyteller says Joab did not count men from the tribes of Levi or Benjamin. Joab did not include the Levites because Yahweh told the Levites they did not have to fight in the army since they were devoted to caring for the tabernacle. Most likely, Joab did not count the men in Benjamin because at this time, the tabernacle was located in the territory of Benjamin at Gibeon. Joab did not count these tribes because Joab was deeply offended that David asked him to take the census. Joab knew that Yahweh would not be happy with the census.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Joab did not count the tribes of Levi and Benjamin because Joab was deeply offended that David had asked him to do something that Joab considered wrong. In your culture, what things would cause people to feel deeply offended if someone asked them to do those things?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Satan causes David to take a census of the people of Israel.

In the second scene: David calls in Joab and his military commanders. David orders these men to take a census, or count all the fighting men in Israel, from Beersheba to Dan. Joab respectfully disagrees with David. Joab says this thing will cause Israel to be guilty.

In the third scene: David insists on the census. Joab and the military commanders leave David and go to take the census. Joab travels throughout the land of Israel and Judah, but he does not count the fighting men in the tribes of Levi or Benjamin because Joab is deeply offended by the census.

In the fourth scene: Joab returns to Jerusalem to report the numbers of the census to David: there are 1,100,000 fighting men in Israel and 470,000 fighting men in Judah.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Satan
  • David
  • Joab
  • The military commanders
  • And the people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Satan rose up against David. This does not mean Satan was sitting down and then stood up, but rather it is another way to say that Satan caused trouble for David. Satan does this by causing David to take a census of the people of Israel.

David calls for Joab and his army commanders and orders them to count all of Israel, "from Beersheba to Dan." It is important to note that "from Beersheba to Dan" was another way to say "all of the kingdom." This is because Dan was located in the far north, and Beersheba was located in the far south.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you translate "from Beersheba to Dan" in a way that means Joab counted the fighting men in the whole land? Be sure to use the same word or phrase you used in 2 Samuel 24:2 for the idea of the entire land of Israel from Beersheba to Dan. Pause this audio here.

Because Joab was the commander of Israel's army, we know that this was a military census, so Joab was only to count the number of fighting men in Israel. Once Joab finishes counting, David wants Joab to return and report the numbers to David.

Joab respectfully disagrees with King David. Joab calls David "my lord, the king" as a way to show respect to David as a higher authority.

Joab says he hopes Yahweh will multiply David's army a hundred times over. Joab asks, "Are all the people not my lord's servants?" as a way to imply that all of the people were already loyal to David as their king and served David. Joab politely disagrees with David's decision by asking David why David wants to do this thing. Joab implies that David is doing wrong when Joab says the census will bring guilt to Israel. Joab most likely means the people of Israel would be guilty of not trusting Yahweh, but rather trusting that their army is strong.

The king's word, or decision, overruled Joab, which means that David did not change his mind. Joab leaves to take the census of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a time when someone in authority insisted on doing something in his way. Pay attention to what phrases and words you use to describe how the person in authority insists on doing things his way. Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that, as Joab travels throughout the land, Joab does not count the fighting men in the tribes of Levi or Benjamin.

After a long time traveling, Joab returns to Jerusalem and reports the numbers of the census to David: 1,100,000 fighting men in Israel and 470,000 fighting men in Judah. We are not sure if the number 1,100,000 includes all of the fighting men in Israel and Judah, or if the 470,000 men from Judah were in addition to that number. Remember, these men were "able to handle a sword," which means the men were physically able to fight.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you say very large numbers like one million, one hundred thousand people? How will you talk about these numbers in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that Joab found David's command to take the census to be repulsive. This means that Joab had very strong emotions of disapproval and even disgust.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think about something that you find repulsive and describe how it makes you feel. How will you describe this strong feeling of disapproval and disgust in your language? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Satan
  • David
  • Joab
  • The commanders of the fighting men
  • And the people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how Satan persuades David to take a census of the people of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We have had many victories lately. I must have thousands of soldiers by now. I want to know exactly how many people are at my command; or
  • Things have changed since I was a shepherd boy. I am proud of the king I have become; or
  • I want to know how many soldiers I have to know how powerful I have truly become.

Continue the drama.

Act out how David orders Joab and the other army commanders to go through all of Israel, from Beersheba to Dan, to count the fighting men.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This does not sound right. Is this something Yahweh wants for Israel, or is it just what David wants? or
  • How do I speak against David without offending him? or
  • I must say something to get David to change his mind.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Joab respectfully disagrees with David. Joab says he hopes Yahweh, his God, will multiply David's army, but asks David why he would want to do such a thing. Joab asks if the people are not already David's servants. Act out how David refuses to change his mind. David insists that the men go out and take the census.

Act out how Joab and the commanders leave David and, after some time, leave Jerusalem to take the census. Joab travels throughout the land but he does not count the fighting men in the tribes of Levi and Benjamin because Joab is deeply offended by the census.

Act out how Joab reports the census numbers to David. Joab tells David there are 1,100,000 fighting men in Israel and 470,000 fighting men in Judah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel upset. It is wrong for us to count the fighting men. Yahweh is the one who gives us victory! or
  • I cannot count Levi or Benjamin in the census. I do not want to involve the tribe dedicated to the tabernacle or the tribe where the tabernacle is located in such a sin; or
  • I feel angry. David is going to make Yahweh angry with us all.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud. Israel has really grown during my reign as king; or
  • I feel secure. With this number of people, we can defeat anyone! or
  • I feel satisfied. I knew we had strength in numbers.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Satan rises up against Israel and causes David to take a census. The name Satan means the adversary, and it is the name for the devil, the leader of all evil spirits. Satan tempts people to disobey Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Satan. Look up Satan in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Here, Israel refers to God's chosen people, the Israelites, or the descendants of Abraham. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel and Israelites, refer to the Master Glossary.

David wants to take a census, or to count, the people of Israel. In this case, David wants to count the number of men who are able to go to war.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for census. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Joab is with the military commanders of the fighting men, or army, when David commands Joab to take the census. The commanders were leaders of the Israelite army, and they were under the authority of Joab. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for military commanders as you have used in previous passages.

Joab says, "May Yahweh add to his people a hundred times as many as they are!" Yahweh is the personal name of the one true God of the Israelites. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Joab refers to David as my Lord, the king. The title "my Lord" refers to someone who is a master and has full authority over other people. "King" refers to someone who rules over a country. Joab uses these two titles to show great respect to David, even though Joab disagreed with David's decision. Use the same word or phrase for Lord and king as you used in previous passages. For more information on Lord or king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Joab asks David, "Are they not all of them my lord's servants?" Joab asks this to imply that the people are already David's servants. A servant is someone who is under the authority of their master. A servant does not have the freedom to leave their master when they want to. In this case, Joab is saying the people already obey David as their king. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Joab asks David why he would want to bring guilt on all of Israel by taking the census. Guilt is when someone is responsible for doing something wrong. Joab is implying that Yahweh will see all of Israel as responsible for the sin instead of only seeing David as responsible for the sin.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for guilt. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Joab reported the number of men who could handle a sword, or who could fight well. Use the same word for sword as you have used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 21:7–17
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 21:7–17 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:7–17 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:7–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Yahweh allowed Satan to tempt King David to take a census in Israel and Judah. David sent his army commander, Joab, throughout the land to count all of the fighting men. After Joab counted all of the tribes except for Levi and Benjamin, Joab returned and reported the census numbers to David.

The storyteller tells us that not only was Joab displeased because he had to take the census, but Yahweh was also displeased and saw the census as evil, or the opposite of good. So Yahweh punishes the people for David's evil action of ordering the census.

After counting the people, David prays to Yahweh. David feels strong guilt and is sad because he ordered Joab to count the people. David tells Yahweh that David has sinned greatly, or done something very wrong, by counting the people. David calls himself Yahweh's servant and asks Yahweh to take away his guilt, or to forgive David for the wrong he has done. David admits that he has acted very foolishly.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a time when a leader or someone in authority felt deep sadness over a decision they made that affected the whole community. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh responds to David's prayer and speaks to Gad. Gad was David's seer, or the prophet who stayed in David's palace. Yahweh used prophets to speak on Yahweh's behalf to his people. Yahweh tells Gad to go to David and to give David a message about how Yahweh will punish David. We only hear a part of Yahweh's message when Yahweh speaks to Gad. We will hear the rest of the message when Gad gives the full message to David later.

Gad finds David and repeats Yahweh's message. Gad tells David he must choose from three different forms of punishment. One possible punishment is three years of famine, or lack of food in the land. Another possible punishment is that David's enemies will chase David for three months. The final option Yahweh gives David is three days of horrible disease in the land that an angel with a sword makes happen. Gad tells David to think about these options and then make a decision so that Gad can give David's answer to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Discuss the different punishments that Yahweh offered to David. Which punishment would you choose and why? Pause this audio here.

David tells Gad he is deeply distressed. In other words, David was in a very difficult position, and it was hard for David to decide. David tells Gad he wants Yahweh to choose what will happen, because David knows Yahweh is full of mercy. When Yahweh is merciful, Yahweh pities people, helps them, and forgives them. David does not want to fall into the hands of people or to let other people have power over David, because David knows people are not as merciful as Yahweh is.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a time when someone showed mercy to another person or group of people. Pause this audio here.

So Yahweh chooses to send the plague, or the horrible disease, on the people of Israel. During this time, 70,000 people died in Israel.

Yahweh uses the angel to destroy the people with a plague. Angels are supernatural beings who are messengers of Yahweh and carry out Yahweh's instructions. Yahweh orders the angel to destroy Jerusalem. But when the angel is about to destroy the city, Yahweh decides to stop punishing the people. Yahweh commands the angel to "withdraw his hand" or to stop using his power to harm the people because enough people had already died.

When the angel stops his destruction, the angel is by the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite, just outside the city of Jerusalem. As you remember, a threshing floor was a hard, level surface located outside a town, where blowing winds could separate the grain seeds from the husks. Jebusites were a group of non-Israelite people who lived in Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a picture of people working on a threshing floor. Pause this audio here.

When David looks up and sees the angel in the sky with his sword pointed toward Jerusalem and ready to destroy the people, David bows to the ground and prays to Yahweh. David was with the elders, or other leaders of Israel, and all these men were dressed in sackcloth to show that they were very sad. Sackcloth is a rough cloth that people made out of goats' hair. Because sackcloth was so uncomfortable, people wore it to show they were really upset.

Stop here and look as a group at an image of a person wearing sackcloth. What do people in your culture wear to show they are very sad? Pause this audio here.

David tells Yahweh that it was David's idea to count the fighting men. David wants to emphasize that it is David himself whom Yahweh should punish because David did wrong. David says that the punishment is a result of David's sin. David says the Israelites are as innocent as sheep. Since sheep follow the shepherd, David is saying that the Israelites only followed David like sheep follow a shepherd.

Stop here and look at a picture of sheep with a shepherd. As a leader, David compared himself to a shepherd. Discuss how your leaders describe themselves to the people they lead. Pause this audio here.

David asks, "What have they done?" to emphasize that the people are not responsible for the wrongdoing. David does not think it is fair that the Israelites have to experience punishment for David's actions. So David asks Yahweh to only show Yahweh's anger toward David and David's family. David asks Yahweh to take the plague away from the people.

In the next passage, we will see Yahweh's response to David.

Defining the Scenes
