Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:7–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: David feels very sad that David ordered the census. David prays to Yahweh and says, "I have sinned greatly in what I have done.... Now I beg of you, take away the guilt of your servant. I have done a very foolish thing."

In the second scene: Yahweh gives a message to Gad for David. Yahweh tells Gad that Yahweh is giving David the choice between three punishments.

In the third scene: Gad goes to David and gives David Yahweh's message. Gad says David must choose between three different forms of punishment. Gad asks if David would rather have three years of famine in the land, three months of David's enemies chasing David, or three days of plague in the land. David is deeply distressed. David asks Yahweh to choose the punishment because David knows Yahweh is full of mercy. David says he does not want to fall into human hands.

In the fourth scene: Yahweh chooses to punish David with a horrible disease. Yahweh sends an angel with a sword to use his power to kill the people of Israel with the disease. Seventy thousand people die from the disease throughout the land. Yahweh then sends the angel to destroy Jerusalem.

In the fifth scene: When the angel is at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite, Yahweh tells the angel to stop the destruction because enough people have already died. At the same time, David and the elders are all wearing sackcloth clothes. When David sees the angel above Jerusalem, David and the elders bow to the ground. David prays to Yahweh to stop the destruction because the people are as innocent as sheep. David asks Yahweh to only punish David and his household.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Gad
  • David
  • The elders
  • The angel
  • The people of Israel
  • And Araunah the Jebusite

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story is also in 2 Samuel 24:10–17. If you have already translated this passage in 2 Samuel, stop here and listen to your translation. This story has some differences. Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that David has just ordered a census and received a report from Joab of all the fighting men in Israel. Joab did not approve of the census, but David ordered it anyway.

The storyteller tells us that David's command to take the census was evil in Yahweh's sight. Evil is the opposite of good, so Yahweh sees that David has disobeyed, or sinned. The consequence of David's disobedience is that Yahweh will punish the people of Israel.

David feels great sadness and prays to Yahweh. David says he has sinned greatly and acted foolishly. When a person is foolish, that person does not use good judgment but makes decisions and acts in a way that is unwise.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a leader who acted foolishly. What did that person do? Pay attention to the words you use to describe this leader. Pause this audio here.

David begs Yahweh to "take away the guilt of your servant." Here, David is referring to himself as a servant of Yahweh since Yahweh is David's master. David wants Yahweh to take away his guilt, or his punishment, for counting the people.

Yahweh responds to David's prayer by giving a message to Gad, David's seer. Gad was a prophet who lived in David's palace. Yahweh wants Gad to give his message to David.

Gad goes to David and tells David Yahweh's message. Gad says David must choose between three different punishments from Yahweh. Gad asks if David would rather have three years of famine or lack of food in the land, three months of being swept away by David's enemies with their swords, or three days when the sword of Yahweh spreads plague in the land.

When people sweep away their enemies, they attack their enemies, and their enemies run away from them. In this case, Yahweh is suggesting that enemies come to attack the Israelites and make the Israelites run away for three months.

Yahweh compares "the sword of Yahweh" with a destroying angel that Yahweh would send. This angel will hold a sword and will cause a horrible disease to spread.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you translate Yahweh using "the sword of Yahweh" to refer to the destroying angel that Yahweh will send? Pause this audio here.

Gad says David must think about his answer and let Gad know what David has chosen so that Gad can tell "the one" who sent Gad. Gad is referring to Yahweh as the one who sent Gad.

David is deeply distressed. In other words, David has a very hard decision to make and is worried about what to choose. David is worried because he cannot decide which punishment is worse and will bring greater shame.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of someone who was deeply distressed over a decision they had to make. Discuss how you will describe this feeling in your language. Pause this audio here.

David asks Gad to let David and Israel fall into the hands of Yahweh and not into the hands of man. It is important to remember that "into the hands" of someone is special language. "Into the hands of" is another way to say that David wants Yahweh to choose what will happen, because David knows Yahweh is full of mercy. If someone or something is in the hands of someone else, it means that the other person has power or control over the person or situation.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of someone or something who had power or control over someone else. Discuss how you will describe "into the hands of" in your language. Pause this audio here.

David says he wants to fall into the hands of Yahweh, or the power of Yahweh, because David knows Yahweh is full of mercy. This is another way to say that people know Yahweh is compassionate and forgiving.

As a result of David and Gad's conversation, Yahweh chooses to send a plague or horrible disease on the people. We know from the story in 2 Samuel 24 that Yahweh does this by sending an angel with a sword to Israel to spread the disease and kill people. An angel is a supernatural being who is a messenger of Yahweh. Angels live in heaven with Yahweh, so this angel would have come from heaven to Israel.

The disease begins to spread the same morning Gad talks to David. During this time, 70,000 people die throughout Israel.

Yahweh sends the angel to destroy Jerusalem. But as the angel begins to destroy Jerusalem, Yahweh decides to stop the punishment because Yahweh is sad when Yahweh sees the people suffer.

Yahweh tells the angel, "Enough! Withdraw your hand," which is special language that means that Yahweh wants the angel to stop using his power to destroy the people. This happens when the angel is at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. This threshing floor was located just outside the city of Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss with your group: Imagine the angel is just about to kill more people in Jerusalem. The angel starts to stretch out his hand to kill them, but Yahweh stops the angel. How will you describe this scene? Be sure to use the same language as you used in 2 Samuel 24. Pause this audio here.

David sees the angel with a sword in his hand between heaven and earth, or in the sky. When the angel stretches the sword toward Jerusalem to destroy the people there, David and the elders with him all fall to their faces. These men do not actually fall down, but bow down with their faces to the ground to show respect to Yahweh. David and his elders, or the wise men who help David lead, have already been dressed in sackcloth before this moment to show their deep mourning. The men wear this uncomfortable fabric to outwardly show how sad and upset the men are.

David prays to Yahweh. David asks, "Was it not I who ordered the fighting men to be counted?" David wants to emphasize that David alone is responsible for this wrongdoing and not the people. David says he is the one who has sinned and done wrong. "Sinned" and "done wrong" mean the same thing, but David uses repetition to emphasize that he is the only person to blame.

David compares the people to sheep and says they are innocent and not responsible for the punishment. This is another way to say that David is like a shepherd, and that David led the people into wrongdoing. David asks, "What have they done?" not because David wants an answer, but to emphasize that the people are blameless. David asks for all of Yahweh's anger to fall on David and his household, or immediate family, and no one else. David asks Yahweh to take the plague away from the people.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of someone who was very angry with someone else and how they showed their anger. In this story, the storyteller says that David wants Yahweh's anger to fall on David. How do you talk about someone who shows their anger in this way in your language? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:7–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Gad
  • David
  • The elders
  • The angel
  • The people of Israel
  • And Araunah the Jebusite

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how David feels very sad because he ordered the census. David prays to Yahweh and says, "I have sinned greatly in what I have done.... Now I beg of you, take away the guilt of your servant. I have done a very foolish thing."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel deep sadness. I should have listened to Joab's advice; or
  • I am worried about how my sin will affect the people around me; or
  • I am hopeful that Yahweh will forgive me for what I have done.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Yahweh gives a message to Gad for David. Yahweh tells Gad that Yahweh is giving David a choice between three forms of punishment.

Act out how Gad goes to David and gives him Yahweh's message. Gad says David must choose between three different forms of punishment. Gad asks if David would rather have three years of famine in the land, three months of David's enemies chasing David, or three days of plague in the land. David is deeply distressed. David asks Yahweh to choose David's punishment because David knows Yahweh is full of mercy. David says he does not want to fall into human hands.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Gad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad David has brought Yahweh's punishment on the people; or
  • I respect David for letting Yahweh choose his punishment; or
  • I can see that David truly does care for the people, and David is worried about how the people will suffer.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am overwhelmed! This decision is too hard for me; or
  • I am worried. I do not want to be shamed by my enemies defeating me; or
  • I feel sad. I can only hope that Yahweh will show me mercy.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Yahweh chooses to punish David with a horrible disease. Yahweh sends an angel with a sword to use his power to kill the people of Israel with the disease. Seventy thousand people die from the disease throughout the land. Yahweh sends the angel to destroy Jerusalem. When the angel is at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite, Yahweh tells the angel to stop destroying people because enough people have already died.

At the same time, act out how David and the elders wear sackcloth together. When David sees the angel above Jerusalem, David and the elders bow to the ground. David prays to Yahweh to stop destroying people because the people are as innocent as sheep. David asks Yahweh to only punish David and his household.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David has turned back to me and is truly sorry for what he has done. I will end David's punishment; or
  • I am sad because of the consequences David has brought on my people; or
  • I know my people are like sheep. I will send their true shepherd soon.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:7–17 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Yahweh found David's command to take the census to be evil. Evil is the opposite of good. When people stop obeying God, people start to do evil things. God hates evil. When God establishes his kingdom forever, God will destroy evil forever. Until that time, God still allows evil to happen.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for evil. Look up evil in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The census was evil in the sight of God. Here, God refers to the most powerful spiritual being, the God who created everything. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary.

As a result of David's disobedience, Yahweh decided to punish the people of Israel. Israel refers to the territory where the Israelites lived. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

David feels sorry for what he has done and prays to Yahweh. David says he has sinned greatly. When someone sins, they disobey God or act in rebellion toward God. David also asks Yahweh to take away his guilt. Use the same words or phrases for sin and guilt as you used in previous passages. For more information on sin or guilt, refer to the Master Glossary.

David calls himself Yahweh's servant. David shows that Yahweh has authority over David and that David serves Yahweh as his master. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh responds to David by giving a message to Gad for David. Yahweh is the personal name for God that shows the close relationship between God and Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Gad was David's seer or the prophet who lived in David's palace. A prophet is someone who gives messages from Yahweh to the people. Use the same word or phrase for prophet or seer as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh gives David three options for punishment. One of the options for David's punishment is famine in the land for three years. Famine refers to a severe lack of food in the land, usually due to no rain. Use the same word for famine that you used in previous passages

Another option for David's punishment is that the sword of Yahweh, or a destroying angel, will cause three days of plague in the land. In this context, a plague is a horrible disease.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for plague. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

An angel is a supernatural being who gives messages and performs tasks on behalf of Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for angel as you used in previous passages. For more information on angel, refer to the Master Glossary.

When David hears the options for Yahweh's punishment, David does not choose. Instead, David says, "Let us fall into the hands of Yahweh and not into the hands of man." If someone or something is in the hands of someone else, then it means the other person has power or control over that person or circumstance. Use the same word or phrase for "into the hands of" as you used in previous passages.

David describes Yahweh as being full of mercy. When someone shows mercy, they show goodness, kindness, and compassion. When Yahweh is merciful, he shows pity, helps, and forgives.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for mercy. Use the same word or phrase for mercy as you used in previous passages. For more information on mercy, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The angel comes to the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. Jebusites are the people group that used to live around Jerusalem before David conquered the city. Threshing floors were outdoor workplaces located just outside of cities where people worked to gather grain. These were flat, hard areas where people used the blowing winds to separate wheat grains from the husk. Be sure to use the same words or phrases for threshing floor and Jebusite that you have used in previous passages.

David sees the angel standing between heaven and earth. Here, heaven refers to the sky, so David had to look up to see the angel above Jerusalem. Use the same word or phrase for heaven, meaning sky, as you used in previous passages. For more information on heaven, refer to the Master Glossary.

David is with the elders of Israel, and these men are wearing sackcloth to show that they are in mourning. An elder is a person with authority in the community. Use the same word or phrase for elder as you used in previous passages. For more information on elder, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, Yahweh sent an angel to bring a plague, or a horrible disease, to Israel. This disease killed 70,000 people. Yahweh used the disease to punish David for counting all of the fighting men in Israel. Yahweh told the angel to stop the punishment just as the angel reached the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

The angel of Yahweh, who speaks to people on Yahweh's behalf, orders Gad to speak to David. As you remember, Gad was the prophet who lived in David's palace. Gad tells David that Yahweh wants David to build an altar to Yahweh on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. An altar was a place where people made sacrifices to Yahweh. An altar was like a table or a platform that people made using earth or large stones. People placed gifts such as animals, food items, or sweet-smelling spices on the table, then burned them as an offering to Yahweh.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of an altar. Pause this audio here.

The threshing floor was the same place where Yahweh had compassion on the people and ordered the angel to stop the punishment. Gad spoke in the name of Yahweh, or on Yahweh's behalf. When David followed Gad's instructions, David was obeying Yahweh.

As you remember, the angel was in the sky above the threshing floor when Yahweh ordered the angel to stop destroying the people. Araunah and his four sons are threshing wheat in the field when Araunah sees the angel. When Araunah's sons see the angel, they hide, most likely because they are afraid.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Imagine you are working in a field when you see an angel suddenly appear over you. How would you react and why? Pause this audio here.

At that moment, Araunah sees David coming up the hill toward the threshing floor. Araunah recognizes David and immediately goes out from the threshing floor to show David respect by bowing down to David. David asks Araunah to sell his threshing floor for the full price so that David can build an altar to honor Yahweh and stop the plague or horrible disease.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story of a time when someone was willing to pay any amount of money to resolve a problem. Pause this audio here.

Araunah tells David that David can have the threshing floor and make whatever offerings David wants to Yahweh. David does not have to pay for it. Araunah even offers to provide oxen for the burnt offering, threshing boards for wood, and wheat for grain offerings. At this time, people used oxen, or male cows, to thresh wheat, or separate the husks from the grain. The oxen pulled heavy wooden boards, called threshing sledges, over the grain that people had spread out.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of oxen pulling threshing sledges. Pause this audio here.

Araunah offers the oxen to David as animals that David can burn on the altar as a burnt sacrifice to Yahweh. Araunah also offers the threshing sledges as wood for the fire. Araunah offers wheat for grain offerings. People offered grain offerings to Yahweh when they mixed grain with sweet-smelling spices and olive oil. According to the law of Moses, the Israelites had to offer a grain offering with every burnt offering.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of wheat grains. Pause this audio here.

Araunah speaks to David and calls David "My Lord, the King" to show great respect. Araunah says he will give all of these things to David. But David says he will not accept Araunah's offer. Instead, David wants to pay Araunah for everything. David does not want to offer a burnt offering to Yahweh that costs David nothing. David wants to pay Araunah for the threshing floor and everything that came with it. In David's culture, David had to pay for the land, even though Araunah offered it to him. Otherwise, David would have then been in debt to Araunah, and it would not have been a true sacrifice for David.

So David buys the threshing floor and oxen for 600 shekels of gold. Since a shekel is a unit of measurement that weighs around 11.4 grams, this means David paid 6,840 grams of gold for the threshing floor. This was a very large amount of money at this time.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss why you think David would pay such a high price for something that someone offered to David for free. Tell a story of someone who paid more for something than its price because they knew the true value of the item. Pause this audio here.

David then builds an altar to honor Yahweh. David offers burnt offerings, or animals that David completely burns on the altar, as a gift to Yahweh. David also offers peace offerings on the altar. A peace offering is when someone kills an animal as an offering to Yahweh, then eats the meat as part of a meal that celebrates a good relationship with Yahweh. The storyteller tells us that David prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh answers David by sending fire from heaven to consume the burnt offerings on the altar. You may remember that Yahweh confirmed that he approved of the tabernacle when the Israelites first built it by sending fire from heaven to consume their offerings. Yahweh once again uses fire from heaven to show that he approves of David's sacrifices on the threshing floor. It is most likely that David took this sign of approval from Yahweh to mean that it should be the new building site for the future temple.

After David makes his offerings on the altar, Yahweh tells the angel to put his sword away in its sheath. A sheath was a case about the size and shape of a sword that people used to cover and carry the sword. By putting its sword away, the angel shows that it will no longer cause the horrible disease to spread among the people.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of a sword and its sheath. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller then gives a summary of what David did and why he did it. According to the law of Moses, the Israelites made their sacrifices to Yahweh on the altar of burnt offerings at the tabernacle, or the portable tent where Yahweh's presence lived. At that time, the tabernacle was located on a high place in Gibeon. But David could not travel that far to make his sacrifices.

Stop here and look as a group at a map of where Jerusalem is located in relation to Gibeon, as well as a picture of the tabernacle. Pause this audio here.

Instead, the presence of the angel and his sword causes David to be afraid. David decides to offer sacrifices at the place that Yahweh chose for the future temple. David says they will build the "house of Yahweh," or the temple, on the threshing floor. David says they will also build the altar where the people of Israel would offer burnt offerings to Yahweh on the threshing floor.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The angel of Yahweh gives Gad a message for David. Gad comes to David and says Yahweh wants David to build an altar on the threshing floor of Araunah. David obeys.

In the second scene: Araunah is working on his threshing floor with his four sons when he sees the angel. Araunah's sons hide when they see the angel. At that moment, Araunah sees David approaching. Araunah goes out to meet David and bows down to David. David says he wants to buy the threshing floor to build an altar to Yahweh. David wants the horrible disease in the land to stop. David wants to buy the threshing floor at the full price.

In the third scene: Araunah offers to give David the threshing floor, his oxen, the threshing sledges, and the wheat so that David can make an offering to Yahweh. But David refuses. David says he does not want to give Yahweh an offering that David did not pay for. David pays Araunah 600 shekels of gold.

In the fourth scene: David builds the altar on the threshing floor and makes burnt offerings and peace offerings. David prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh answers by sending fire from heaven to consume the burnt offerings on the altar. Yahweh tells the angel to put his sword away. David says they will build the temple and the altar of burnt offerings for all of Israel on the threshing floor.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Gad
  • The angel of Yahweh
  • King David
  • Araunah
  • Araunah's four sons
  • And oxen

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that David has just asked Yahweh to forgive David and to stop punishing the people for David's disobedience. David asks Yahweh to only punish David.

It is important to remember that the angel of Yahweh was still in the sky above the threshing floor of Araunah when Yahweh commanded the angel to stop spreading the plague or horrible disease.

The angel of Yahweh orders Gad to give a message to David. The angel speaks to Gad in the name of Yahweh, or on Yahweh's behalf. As you remember, Gad was a prophet who specifically served as Yahweh's messenger to King David.

Gad goes to David and gives him the message from Yahweh. Yahweh tells David to raise, or build, an altar on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. It is important to remember that an altar is a rock table where people make sacrifices or offerings to Yahweh. As you remember, the threshing floor of Araunah is where Yahweh stopped the angel from destroying Jerusalem.

David obeys Gad's instructions and goes to the threshing floor according to the word that Gad spoke in the name of Yahweh. To speak in the name of Yahweh means that Gad represented Yahweh before David. David obeyed Yahweh's command by following Gad's instructions.

The threshing floor is located just outside the city of Jerusalem, and the angel is still in the sky above the threshing floor. Araunah and his four sons are most likely using oxen to thresh wheat when they see the angel. Araunah's sons immediately stop working and hide when they see the angel, but Araunah stays on the threshing floor.

At the same time, David walks uphill toward the threshing floor. When Araunah sees David coming, Araunah leaves the threshing floor to meet David and bows at David's feet. By bowing, Araunah shows respect for David as an authority. It is important to remember that Araunah was not an Israelite, but he still recognized who King David was.

David says he has come to buy the threshing floor and to build an altar so that the horrible disease in the land will stop. David refers to Yahweh as the one who will bring an end to the disease. David says he wants to pay the full price for the threshing floor.

Araunah tells David to take whatever David wants and to do whatever seems good. Araunah refers to David as "my Lord, the king" to emphasize his respect for and submission to David.

Here, Araunah uses special language and says David should do what is "good in his eyes" or what is pleasing to David. Again, Araunah is showing respect to David as Araunah's authority.

Stop here and discuss with your group: How might someone in your culture talk to a person who has power and authority? What phrases does that person use to show respect? How will you talk about David doing something that is "good in his eyes"? Pause this audio here.

Araunah says David can use the oxen as a burnt offering and the wood from the threshing sledges for the fire. Araunah also says David can use the wheat as grain offerings.

But King David does not accept the gifts. Instead, David says he will buy Araunah's threshing floor and oxen for the full price. David says he will not offer to Yahweh something that belongs to Araunah. David did not want to offer a sacrifice to Yahweh that did not cost David anything. So David pays Araunah 600 shekels of gold for the threshing floor and oxen. You may remember that in the similar story in 2 Samuel 24, David only pays 50 silver shekels for the threshing floor. It is unclear why there is a difference. In any case, David paid a lot of money.

David builds an altar on the threshing floor and then kills the oxen as burnt offerings. David also makes a peace offering, or fellowship offering, which would have included a peace offering meal. Yahweh shows his acceptance by sending fire from heaven to consume the burnt offerings on the altar.

Stop here and discuss with your group: The storyteller says the fire comes down from heaven and "eats up" the burnt offerings like people quickly eat food. How will you translate the way the fire acted in your language? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh orders the angel to put his sword away in its sheath, and the angel obeys.

The storyteller then shifts to give us a summary of what David did and why he did it. David could not offer sacrifices at the tabernacle in Gibeon because David was afraid of the angel. As you remember, the angel was holding a sword and had the power to cause people to die from a horrible disease. Instead, David obeyed Yahweh's earlier command and offered sacrifices on the threshing floor, the place Yahweh chose for the future temple.

David says his people will build the "house of Yahweh" with the altar where the people of Israel would offer burnt offerings on the threshing floor. It is important to note that David is talking about the future temple, which will be the more permanent house of God. David is not saying the Israelites will build another tent like the tabernacle.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Gad
  • The angel of Yahweh
  • King David
  • Araunah
  • Araunah's four sons
  • And oxen

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how the angel of Yahweh gives Gad a message for David. Gad comes to David and says Yahweh wants David to build an altar on the threshing floor of Araunah. David obeys.

Act out how Araunah works on his threshing floor with his four sons. When they see the angel, Araunah's sons hide. At that moment, Araunah sees David approaching. Araunah goes out to meet David and bows down to David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Araunah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I understand why my sons ran! I have never seen anything as frightening as that angel; or
  • I feel confused. Why is the angel just staying there with his sword? What is the angel going to do to us? or
  • The Israelite king is here! I will give the king whatever he wants to see if he can get the angel to leave.

Continue the drama.

Act out how David says he wants to buy the threshing floor and build an altar to Yahweh to stop the horrible disease in the land. David says he wants to buy the threshing floor for the full price.

Act out how Araunah offers to give David the threshing floor, his oxen, the threshing sledges, and the wheat so that David can make an offering to Yahweh. David refuses. David says he does not want to give Yahweh an offering he did not pay for. David pays Araunah 600 shekels of gold for the threshing floor.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am determined. This is the place Yahweh chose. There is no price too high to pay; or
  • I will not make a sacrifice to Yahweh that costs me nothing. Yahweh deserves everything I can offer Yahweh, and more; or
  • I feel fearful. I hope Yahweh accepts my offerings.

Continue the drama.

Act out how David builds the altar on the threshing floor and uses the oxen to make burnt offerings and peace offerings. David prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh answers by sending fire from heaven to eat up the burnt offerings on the altar.

Act out how Yahweh tells the angel to put his sword away. David says he will build the house of Yahweh and the altar of burnt offerings for all of Israel on the threshing floor.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel relief. Yahweh forgave me and accepted my offerings; or
  • I feel joyful. All of our people will remember this place as the place where Yahweh showed us mercy; or
  • I will make sure my son builds the house of Yahweh here.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 21:18–22:1 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The angel of Yahweh gives Gad a message for David. An angel is a supernatural being who gives messages and performs tasks on behalf of Yahweh. An angel of Yahweh is a special angel who speaks with so much authority that it is as if God himself is speaking. Yahweh is the personal name of God that shows the close relationship between God and Israel. Use the same word or phrase for "angel of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. For more information on "angel of Yahweh" and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Gad tells David that Yahweh wants David to build an altar on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. An altar is a place where people make sacrifices to Yahweh. It is like a table or a platform that people make of earth or large stones. People would place gifts such as animals, food items, or sweet-smelling spices on the table and then burn those things as an offering to Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for altar as you used in previous passages. For more information on altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

David must build the altar on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. Threshing floors were flat, hard areas where people used the blowing wind to separate the wheat grains from the husk. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for "threshing floor" that you have used in previous passages.

Jebusite means that Araunah was not an Israelite but someone who lived in the Jerusalem area before the Israelites conquered it. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for Jebusite that you have used in previous passages.

David went up to the threshing floor because he obeyed Yahweh's word that Gad had spoken in Yahweh's name. Use the same word or phrase for word, and "in someone's name" as you have used in previous passages. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about "word."

After seeing the angel, Araunah looks down and sees David coming. David wants to buy the threshing floor and make an offering to bring an end to the plague, or horrible disease. Use the same word or phrase for plague as you used in previous passages.

Araunah bows down to David. Araunah calls David "my lord, the king."

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "bow down." Refer to bow in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

The title "my Lord" refers to someone who is a master and has full authority over other people. A king is someone who rules over a country. In this case, David ruled over Israel. Araunah recognized that David had authority over Araunah. Use the same words or phrases for Lord and king as you used in previous passages. For more information on Lord and king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Araunah says David can have his threshing floor and oxen as burnt offerings. When a person brings God a burnt offering, he kills an animal and burns this animal completely on an altar, which is a special place meant for bringing gifts to God. Use the same word or phrase for burnt offering as you used in previous passages. For more information on burnt offering, refer to the Master Glossary.

David also offers a grain offering on the altar. People offered grain offerings to Yahweh when they mixed grain with sweet-smelling spices and olive oil. The Israelites had to offer a grain offering with every burnt offering.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for grain offering. Look up grain offering in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David pays 600 shekels of gold for the threshing floor. A shekel is a unit of measurement that weighs around 11.4 grams.

Stop here and decide as a group what word or phrase you will use for shekel. Use the same word or phrase for shekel as you used in previous passages. For more information on shekel, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

After paying for the threshing floor, David builds an altar and offers burnt offerings and peace or fellowship offerings on the altar. A peace or fellowship offering is when someone kills an animal as an offering to Yahweh and then eats the meat as part of a meal that celebrates a good relationship with Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for peace, or fellowship, offering as you used in previous passages. For more information on "peace or fellowship offering," refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller tells us Yahweh responded to David by sending fire from heaven to eat the burnt offerings on the altar. Here, heaven refers to the sky. Use the same word or phrase for heaven, meaning sky, as you used in previous passages. For more information on heaven, refer to the Master Glossary.

David offered sacrifices on the threshing floor because David was so afraid of the angel that he could not travel to the tabernacle in Gibeon. The tabernacle was a portable temple that the Israelites carried with them. This was the place where the Israelites kept the ark of the covenant and where they worshiped Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for tabernacle as you used in previous passages. For more information on tabernacle, refer to the Master Glossary.

The Israelites built the tabernacle with Moses in the desert or wilderness. A wilderness is a dry, rocky place without water where few people live. Use the same word or phrase for wilderness as you used in previous passages. For more information on wilderness, refer to the Master Glossary.

David says that he will build the house of Yahweh God on the threshing floor. The house of God is a way to talk about the place on earth where Yahweh lived among his people. Although Yahweh does not need to live in a house, Yahweh told the Israelites that he would live among them and meet with them in the special portable tent they prepared for Yahweh. The Israelites called this tent the tabernacle. But here, David is referring to the temple, which is the more permanent house that the Israelites would build for Yahweh in the future. People sometimes called both the tabernacle and the temple the "house of God." David calls this the house of Yahweh God. Yahweh God is a title that the Israelites sometimes used for Yahweh, their God. For more information on the house of God, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 22:2–19
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 22:2–19 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 22:2–19 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 22:2–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember that in the previous story, David identified the place where people would build the temple. David had displeased Yahweh when he counted the people of Israel, and Yahweh sent a plague on the Israelites. But then David saw an angel with a sword at the threshing floor of Ornan. David built an altar there and offered sacrifices. Yahweh ended the plague, and then David declared this spot would be the house of Yahweh and the place for Israel's altar.

In this story, the storyteller talks about how David prepares for the building of the temple. David cannot build the temple himself, but David wants to help his son Solomon succeed in this important task. This part of Chronicles is a story about a special moment in Israel's history. We do not hear about how David passes on the work to his son Solomon in any other parts of the Bible.

The storyteller calls the temple the "house of Yahweh" in this story. David orders workers to cut and prepare stones for building the temple. These workers are foreigners who live in Israel. These foreigners may have been descendants of the people who lived in the land before the Israelites. Many stone quarries existed near Jerusalem where workers could get limestone.

David provides lots of iron that people can use to make nails and hinges for the gate doors. David also gives more bronze than anyone can weigh for the other decorative work in the temple. In early times, iron was very valuable. David got these metals from the enemies David defeated in battles. The people of Tyre and Sidon bring many cedar logs to David. Cedar from Lebanon was famous, and people used cedar in many countries to build important buildings like temples and palaces.

Stop here and look at pictures of cedar trees and wood as a group. What materials do people in your community use to build important buildings? Pause this audio here.

The temple needs to be a beautiful building that brings fame and glory to Yahweh. David decides to make preparations before he dies because David's son Solomon, who will build the temple, is still young. From this moment, David links himself and Solomon together as a team to build the sanctuary. David prepares almost everything for the temple ahead of time. David knows that a young person, even an intelligent one, will need help with such a big job.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do older people in your community help younger people learn important skills? Pause this audio here.

David tells Solomon that David wants to build a temple for Yahweh. But Yahweh had told David before that David could not build the temple because David had fought in many wars and killed many people. The blood from battles made David unclean for this holy task. The person who built Yahweh's house needed to be a man of peace. Yahweh does not condemn David because David fought. The wars David fought actually helped create the peace that Solomon would enjoy. David tells Solomon that Yahweh had told David that Solomon would be born, and Solomon's name means peace. During Solomon's time as king, Yahweh will give Israel peace and quiet. Yahweh will keep Israel safe from their enemies.

Solomon will build a temple for Yahweh's name. Solomon will be like a son to Yahweh, and Yahweh will be like a father to Solomon. Yahweh will make Solomon's kingdom strong forever. This is part of Yahweh's special promise to David's family.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do leaders in your community decide who will do important jobs? Pause this audio here.

David prays that Yahweh will give Solomon wisdom and understanding. The gift of wisdom will help Solomon build the temple. David wants Solomon to obey Yahweh's law and follow Yahweh's instructions for how to build the temple. If Solomon obeys Yahweh's commands, Solomon will succeed. This prayer is like the instruction that Moses gave to Joshua when he told Joshua to obey Yahweh's commands.

Stop here and tell a story of a time when an older person encouraged a younger person who was about to do a difficult job. How did the younger person feel? How did the older person help the younger person? Pause this audio here.

David has worked hard to prepare materials for the temple. David has collected huge amounts of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone. The amounts are so large that they show how important the temple is. David says he collected 100,000 talents of gold. A talent weighs about 34 kilograms, so this equals about 3,750 tons of gold. David also collected 1,000,000 talents of silver, about 37,500 tons. Solomon can add more to what David has prepared. David also mentions the many skilled workers who work with these materials. These workers include stonecutters, carpenters, and all kinds of craftsmen who work with gold, silver, bronze, and iron.

Stop here and tell a story about a time when people in your community worked together on a big project, perhaps to build a large building. What kind of skilled workers helped? Pause this audio here.

David also commands all the leaders of Israel to help Solomon. David reminds the leaders that Yahweh has caused them to have peace, so no enemies attack. David knows that if the leaders are faithful to Yahweh, they will be faithful to Solomon. The land is now under the control of Yahweh and his people. David tells the leaders to seek Yahweh their God. In Chronicles, when someone seeks Yahweh, they do more than just ask Yahweh to guide them. A person who seeks Yahweh obeys Yahweh. The leaders can seek Yahweh by helping build the temple. The leaders should build the sanctuary so they can bring the ark of the covenant and the sacred vessels into the temple they will build to honor Yahweh's name. These sacred vessels are all the items that people dedicated to Yahweh so the priests and Levites could use them in worship.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What sacred objects do people in your community have? How do people treat these objects? Pause this audio here.

For the people who first heard Chronicles, this story reminded them how important the temple and the worship that people did in the temple were for their community. The temple was at the center of their relationship with Yahweh.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 22:2–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: David tells the foreigners who live in Israel to come together. David gives the people work to do. David gathers materials for people to build the temple. These include stones, iron, bronze, and cedar logs. David collects so many supplies that no one can weigh or count them because the temple must look amazing. David prepares because Solomon is young and has never built anything like this before.

In the second scene: David talks privately with his son Solomon. David tells Solomon that David wanted to build a temple for Yahweh. But Yahweh told David he could not build this temple because David had fought too many wars and killed too many people. Yahweh picked Solomon to build the temple instead. David tells Solomon how to build the temple. David reminds Solomon to obey Yahweh's laws. David shows Solomon all the building supplies he has gathered. David also tells Solomon about the workers who will help with the building.

In the third scene: David orders all the leaders of Israel to help Solomon. David reminds them that Yahweh has stopped their enemies from attacking them. The land now belongs to Yahweh and his people. David tells the leaders to follow Yahweh with their whole heart. David asks the leaders to help build the holy place. This place will hold the ark of the covenant and other holy items.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Solomon
  • The foreigners who live in Israel
  • The leaders of Israel
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story happens near the end of David's life. Yahweh has already told David where to build the temple. David has been a successful warrior king who defeated many enemies. Now Israel has peace. David knows he will die soon, so David makes preparations for the temple that Solomon will build.

David has gathered a huge amount of building materials. David mentions gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone. The amount is so large that people cannot count or weigh some of the materials. Later, David talks about the millions of talents of gold and silver that David has gathered. David may be exaggerating to emphasize how magnificent the temple will be. This kind of exaggeration was common in ancient times. The round numbers suggest that David expected people to understand that David was not talking about exact amounts.

David has organized many kinds of skilled workers. There are stonecutters and masons who cut and carve stone. There are carpenters who cut wood, and skilled craftsmen who work with gold, silver, bronze, and iron. The craftsmen will use the iron to make nails for the doors in the gateways as well as for the clamps. We are not sure what the clamps are exactly, but clamps may be another part of the door, such as the hinges or strips of metal that strengthen the door.

The people from Sidon and Tyre had brought cedar logs to David, so David has a lot of cedar. Sidon and Tyre were cities on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea, north of Israel. The leader of these cities had peace with the Israelites and traded with the Israelites.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and the Mediterranean Sea as a group. Find the cities of Sidon and Tyre. Pause this audio here.

David speaks directly to Solomon and calls him "my son." Solomon is young and inexperienced. David speaks to Solomon with authority and gives Solomon commands and advice. David had it in his heart to build the temple. People would talk about someone's heart when they wanted to talk about the place where a person feels and thinks. David means that David wanted to build the temple. David tells Solomon that Yahweh has promised that Solomon will have a peaceful reign. Solomon's name is related to the Hebrew word for peace. Yahweh has promised to establish Solomon's kingdom forever if Solomon is faithful.

David also speaks to the leaders of Israel with authority. David reminds the leaders that Yahweh has given them victory over their enemies. The leaders have a responsibility to help Solomon and to seek Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people in your culture show respect when they speak to Yahweh? How will you translate the way David speaks of Solomon as the one Yahweh chose? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, David emphasizes that the temple will be for "the name of Yahweh." This phrase appears several times. Remember that a person's name represents their character, so this temple will bring glory to Yahweh. And the temple is not just a building. The temple is a holy place where Yahweh's presence will dwell among his people. David calls it a "sanctuary," which is a holy place. The temple will contain the ark of the covenant and holy articles that the priests will use to worship Yahweh.

Yahweh will establish the throne of Solomon's kingdom over Israel forever. Yahweh means that Yahweh will cause Solomon's descendants to continue to rule as king over Israel. Kings would sit on the throne, or a special seat for a king, so sometimes people used a king's throne to represent the way that a king rules.

David speaks of "seeking Yahweh" as an important duty. David tells both Solomon and the leaders to devote themselves to seeking Yahweh. This involves obeying Yahweh's laws and being committed to serving Yahweh.

David asks Yahweh to give Solomon discretion and understanding. A person who has discretion is wise and knows the right way to act. A person who has understanding can understand the world because that person pays attention and listens. David encourages Solomon with phrases like "be strong and courageous" and "do not be afraid or discouraged." These are similar to words that Moses spoke to Joshua before Joshua led Israel into the Promised Land. David also assures Solomon that "Yahweh will be with you."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people in your culture encourage someone who has a difficult task ahead? How will you translate these phrases of encouragement in a way that sounds natural? Pause this audio here.

David says that a word from Yahweh came to David. This means that Yahweh gave a message to David. When David speaks about how Yahweh prevented David from building the temple, David accepts Yahweh's decision without complaint. David focuses instead on what he can do to help Solomon succeed in the task. Even though David cannot build the temple himself, David takes pains, or works very hard, to prepare everything Solomon will need.

When David talks to the leaders, David asks them two questions that David does not expect the leaders to answer. David asks if Yahweh is really with the Israelites and if Yahweh has given the Israelites rest on every side. David does not wonder if Yahweh has done these things. David wants to emphasize that Yahweh really is with the Israelites. David also wants the leaders to remember that Yahweh has given the Israelites rest, or has stopped enemies from attacking the Israelites from any direction. David also says that Yahweh has given the people who live in the land "into David's hands," which means that Yahweh has caused David to have power over these people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you emphasize that something is really true? Discuss how you will translate the way that David asks the two questions. Pause this audio here.

David tells the leaders to devote their heart and soul to seek Yahweh. A soul is the invisible part of a person that may live on after a person dies. When someone talks about doing something with their heart and soul, they mean to do it earnestly, with all of their attention and focus. David wants the leaders to devote their whole selves to seek, or to serve and worship, Yahweh.

Stop here and tell a story about a person who was completely focused on doing one thing, like raising their children, so that they constantly thought about, worked for, and cared for that thing. How would you describe the way the person completely devoted themselves to that thing? Discuss how you want to translate the way that David tells the leaders to devote their heart and soul to seek Yahweh. Pause this audio here.

At the end of the passage, David speaks of the temple as a place "built for the name of Yahweh." The temple will bring honor to Yahweh. The temple's purpose is to provide a permanent place for the ark of the covenant and the sacred articles.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people prepare for important projects that will continue after they die? How will you translate the idea that David is preparing for something he will not live to see completed? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it. As you do this, you might want to use drawings, objects, or even people to represent David gathering materials, foreign workers cutting stones, and the large piles of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone. Consider showing how David talks with Solomon and passes on Yahweh's message that David cannot build the temple, but Solomon will. You could also show how David speaks to the leaders of Israel and how everyone works together to prepare for the temple that will honor Yahweh and hold the ark of the covenant.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 22:2–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three parts.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Solomon
  • The foreigners who live in Israel
  • The leaders of Israel
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

David tells the foreigners who live in Israel to come together. David gives these people work to do. David selects stonecutters to prepare stones to build God's temple. David provides large amounts of iron to make nails for the doors and fittings. David provides more bronze than anyone can weigh. David also provides many cedar logs, which the Sidonians and Tyrians brought to David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to make sure Solomon has everything he needs to build the temple; or
  • I am excited to prepare for God's house even though I cannot build it myself; or
  • I feel responsible to gather the best materials from everywhere for God's temple.

Ask the person playing one of the foreign workers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am working hard to shape these stones perfectly for God's house; or
  • I wonder why King David is collecting so many materials.

Continue the drama.

David says, "My son Solomon is young. Solomon has never done anything like this before. But Yahweh's house must be so great and beautiful that people throughout the world know about it! I will gather supplies for this building." That is why David gathers many supplies before he dies.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want the temple to honor God and show his greatness to all nations; or
  • I worry that Solomon is too young for such a big project; or
  • I need to use my last days to prepare as much as possible for the temple.

Continue the drama.

David calls for Solomon and tells him to build a house for Yahweh, the God of Israel. David says to Solomon, "My son, I wanted to build a house to worship Yahweh more than anything. But then Yahweh spoke to me. Yahweh said, 'You have killed many people and fought in many battles. You cannot build a house to worship me because I have seen all the blood of the people you have killed. But you will have a son, and he will rule peacefully. His name will be Solomon. I will make sure that none of Solomon's enemies attack Solomon. While Solomon rules as king, the people of Israel will be able to live peacefully. Solomon will build a house for me. Solomon will become my son, and I will become his father. I will make Solomon's kingdom strong, and will make sure that someone from Solomon's family will rule Israel forever!'"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I accept that God chose Solomon instead of me to build the temple; or
  • I am thankful that God promises to bless my son; or
  • I feel both disappointed and honored by God's decision.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel overwhelmed by this responsibility to build God's temple; or
  • I am honored that God chose me for this special task; or
  • I wonder if I can live up to my father's expectations.

Continue the drama.

David says, "Now, my son, I pray that Yahweh will help you. I pray that you will be successful when you build the temple of Yahweh, your God, just as Yahweh said you would. I pray that Yahweh will cause you to be wise, so that you will understand things. You will need to be wise when Yahweh makes you king of Israel, so that you may obey the law of Yahweh, your God. You will be successful if you carefully obey Yahweh's commands and rules, which Yahweh gave to Moses for Israel. Be strong and brave. Do not be afraid or discouraged."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want Solomon to know his success depends on following God's laws; or
  • I hope my encouragement will help Solomon be brave in this big task; or
  • I pray that God will give my son the wisdom he needs.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I need God's help to complete this task; or
  • I want to make my father proud and follow God's commands; or
  • I feel strengthened by my father's confidence in me.

Continue the drama.

David says, "I have worked hard to provide for Yahweh's temple. I have provided 100,000 talents of gold, 1,000,000 talents of silver, and more bronze and iron than anyone can weigh. I have also provided wood and stone. You may add to them. You have many workers with special skills. Some workers know how to cut stone. Other workers know how to build with stones or with wood. Some workers can use gold, silver, bronze, and iron to make things. There are more workers than you can count. So begin the work! I pray Yahweh will help you."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope Solomon realizes how much preparation I have done; or
  • I want to assure Solomon he has everything he needs to succeed; or
  • I feel satisfied that I have done everything possible to help with the temple.

Continue the drama.

David orders all the leaders of Israel to help his son Solomon. David says to the leaders, "Yahweh, your God, is certainly with you! Yahweh keeps you safe from all your enemies. Yahweh has caused me to rule over the people who live in this land. Now this land belongs to Yahweh and his people. So you must obey and serve Yahweh completely. Begin to build the holy place for Yahweh, your God. Then you can place the ark of the covenant of Yahweh as well as the other holy things in the temple. The building will bring honor to Yahweh."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to make sure everyone supports Solomon in this important work; or
  • I remind the leaders that God has blessed us, so we should honor God; or
  • I hope the leaders understand their responsibility to help build God's house.

Ask the person playing one of the leaders of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I see that David wants all of us to support his son; or
  • I recognize how important this temple project is to David and to God; or
  • I want to help Solomon succeed in building the temple.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 22:2–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

David orders foreigners to help prepare for the temple. Foreigners were non-Israelite people who lived in Israel. These people had fewer rights than the Israelites, but were protected by laws. David assigned these foreigners to cut stones and prepare building materials. For more information on foreigners, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for foreigners as you used in previous passages.

David prepares many materials to build the house of Yahweh. Another name for the house of Yahweh is the "house of God" or the temple. The temple is the place where the Israelites worshiped Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for "house of Yahweh" as you have used in previous passages. Look up "house of God" in the Master Glossary for more information.

David gathers together stonecutters, who are people who cut large blocks of stone, which people use to build buildings. Later, David says he has gathered masons and carpenters. Masons build things with stones, and carpenters build things with wood.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for stonecutter, mason, and carpenter. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David has gathered a lot of bronze. Bronze is a metal that people make out of copper and tin. For more information on bronze, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for bronze as you used in previous passages.

This house will be for Yahweh. Yahweh is the personal name of God in the Bible. This name appears many times throughout this passage. David often refers to Yahweh as the God of Israel when David explains why his son will build the temple instead of David. For more information on Yahweh and God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh and God as you used in previous passages.

David had it in his heart to build a house for Yahweh. People use the word "heart" to represent the place where people think, feel, and make decisions. David means that he really wanted to build a house for Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the phrase "had it in his heart." Look up heart in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

The word of Yahweh came to David. This means that Yahweh gave a message to David. For more information on the "word of Yahweh," refer to "word" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh told David that David had shed much blood, or killed many people. For more information on blood, refer to blood in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for blood as you used in previous passages.

David calls Solomon a "man of peace." This means someone who brings or experiences peace rather than war. David contrasts his own life of war with Solomon's future peaceful reign. This peaceful condition was needed before people could build the temple.

Peace refers to a state of safety and calm without war or conflict. David explains that Solomon's name is connected to peace. God promises to give Israel peace during Solomon's reign. This peace is necessary for building the temple. For more information on peace, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for peace as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for man of peace. What words in your language describe someone who brings or lives in peace? Pause this audio here.

David mentions the kingdom of Israel that Solomon will rule. Kingdom refers to the land and people under the authority of a king. God promises that Solomon's kingdom will last forever. For more information on kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for kingdom as you used in previous passages.

Solomon will have success if he keeps Yahweh's laws, statutes, and ordinances. When people in the Bible talk about Yahweh's laws, statutes, and ordinances all together, they mean the set of instructions that God has given the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for laws, statutes, and ordinances. Look up "law," "statute," and "ordinance" in the Master Glossary for more information. If necessary, you can simply say that Solomon will have success if he keeps the set of instructions that God has given the people of Israel. Pause this audio here.

David says that he has provided 100,000 talents of gold and 1,000,000 talents of silver. A talent is a unit of measurement that weighs about 34 kilograms. Use the same word or phrase for talent as you used in previous passages. Talent is in the Master Glossary.

David speaks to the officials or leaders of the people of Israel. Some translations call these men "princes." Use the same word or phrase for Israel and leaders as you used in previous passages. Israel is in the Master Glossary.

David tells the leaders to devote their hearts and souls to seek Yahweh. A person's soul is the total invisible, inner part of a person. This part of a person may live on even after their body dies. David means that the leaders should devote their whole selves to serve and obey Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate the phrase "devote your hearts and souls." Look up "heart" and "soul" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David mentions the rest that God gives to Israel. Rest means safety from enemies and peace in the land. God gave Israel rest by defeating their enemies. This rest created the right conditions for people to build the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for rest. What words in your language describe a time of safety and peace after conflict? Pause this audio here.

David calls the temple a sanctuary. A sanctuary is a holy place set apart for the worship of God. The sanctuary will house the ark of the covenant and other sacred objects that people used in worship. For more information on sanctuary, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sanctuary as you used in previous passages.

David describes the holy articles that people will place in the temple. These articles are holy or set apart for God. These were special objects that people used in worship and that belonged to God. For more information on holy, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for holy as you used in previous passages.

The ark of the covenant was a special box that represented God's presence with Israel. The ark contained the stone tablets with the Ten Commandments. People would place the ark in the temple that Solomon would build. For more information on the ark of the covenant, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase "ark of the covenant" as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 23:1–6
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 23:1–6 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:1–6 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?
