Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, David prepared materials for people to use to build the temple. David told Solomon and the leaders of Israel to help with this important task. Now the story continues as David makes plans for those who will serve in the temple.

When David became very old, David made his son Solomon the king of Israel. This marks an important transition of power. David helped Solomon establish his rule even before David died. This arrangement helped create stability in the kingdom.

David gathered all the leaders of Israel, along with the priests and Levites. David wanted to organize these people before he died because David knew the work the Levites would do at the temple was very important. This gathering was an informal meeting. The leaders, priests, and Levites were mostly there to witness what David planned to do.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do leaders in your community prepare for the future? Pause this audio here.

David counted the Levites who were 30 years old or older. There were 38,000 Levite men in total. Yahweh instructed Moses long ago that the Levites who are 30 to 50 years old will serve Yahweh. In later stories, we will learn that David lowered the age to 20. David made this change because the duties of the Levites were changing. The Levites no longer needed to be as physically strong since they would not have to carry the ark from place to place.

David assigned different roles to these Levites. David put 24,000 Levites in charge of the work in Yahweh's temple. David appointed 6,000 Levites to be officials and judges. These officials kept records and helped govern the people. The judges settled arguments among the people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people in your community divide up important work? Pause this audio here.

David assigned 4,000 Levites to be gatekeepers. Gatekeepers guarded the entrances to the temple. Gatekeepers made sure that only the right people entered the sacred spaces.

David also appointed 4,000 Levites to praise Yahweh with musical instruments. David had people specially make these instruments for worship. Music became one of the main duties of the Levites after people built the temple. These Levites played instruments like lyres, harps, and cymbals.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you use music in your community's worship? Pause this audio here.

David organized the Levites into working groups based on the three sons of Levi. These sons were Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Each son's family formed a division with specific responsibilities. This organization helped prepare for the temple that Solomon would build. David could not build the temple himself, but David did everything he could to help his son succeed.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In what ways do older people in your community help prepare younger people for important responsibilities?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: David is very old. David makes his son Solomon king over Israel. This marks the beginning of the transfer of power from David to Solomon. David is preparing to hand over both the kingdom and the responsibility for building the temple to his son.

In the second scene: David gathers all the leaders of Israel along with the priests and Levites. David counts the Levites who are 30 years old or older. There are 38,000 Levites in total. David begins to organize the large group of Levites for their future service in the temple.

In the third scene: David assigns specific duties to the Levites. David puts 24,000 in charge of the work in Yahweh's temple. David makes 6,000 officials and judges. David assigns 4,000 to be gatekeepers. David designates 4,000 to praise Yahweh with musical instruments that David provided for worship. David then organizes the Levites into working groups based on the three sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Solomon
  • The leaders of Israel
  • The priests
  • The Levites
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story happens near the end of David's life. David is "old and full of days," which means that David has lived a long and complete life. David knows he will die soon, so David is making preparations for the future of the kingdom of Israel and for the temple that Solomon will build.

David makes Solomon king over Israel. This happens while David is still alive. This means both David and Solomon rule together for a time. David prepares Solomon to take over as king.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What words in your language describe the son of a king who becomes king while his father is still alive? Pause this audio here.

David's preparations include organizing the people who will serve in the temple. First, David focuses on the Levites, who are from the tribe of Levi. The Levites have special responsibilities in worship. David counts the Levites and assigns them specific duties. Some will supervise the work in the temple. Others will serve as officials and judges. Some will be gatekeepers. And some will lead worship with musical instruments.

David speaks with authority to the leaders, priests, and Levites. As king, David has the power to organize the people and assign their duties. David provides musical instruments for the Levites to use to praise Yahweh. David shows that he is very committed to establish proper worship practices.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people in your culture prepare for important leadership transitions? How will you translate the idea that David is preparing for the future when he will no longer be king? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, David divides the Levites into groups based on the Levite clan they come from. The three sons of Levi were Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Each Levite belongs to one of these family lines. To organize people by family groups was important in Israelite society. People's roles and responsibilities often depended on which family they belonged to.

The temple is a central focus in this passage. Even though people have not built the temple yet, David is already organizing the people who will serve there. The temple will be Yahweh's house, a place where God's presence lives among his people. David assigns such a large number of Levites to serve in the temple because he wants to make sure that the Israelites properly worship Yahweh.

David assigns 4,000 Levites to praise Yahweh with musical instruments. Music is an important part of worship. The instruments that people mention elsewhere in the Bible include lyres, harps, and cymbals. The Levites who lead music have the special task of helping the people show their praise to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember what you discussed about music and musical instruments in worship in the last step. How will you translate the idea that Levites praise Yahweh with instruments? Pause this audio here.

At this time in Israel's history, the people are no longer wandering in the wilderness. The people have settled in the Promised Land. The permanent temple in Jerusalem will replace the portable tabernacle. This change affects the duties of the Levites. In the past, the Levites carried the tabernacle from place to place. Now the Levites will have new responsibilities in the temple.

David shows wisdom when he prepares for this transition before his death. David does not wait until the last minute to organize the temple service. Instead, David carefully plans for the worship that will continue after he is gone. David wants to make sure that proper worship of Yahweh will continue into the next generation.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how important is it to prepare for things that will happen after you die? How will you translate the idea that David is organizing worship that he will not live to see fully established? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three parts.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Solomon
  • The leaders of Israel
  • The priests
  • The Levites
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

David is very old and full of days. David makes his son Solomon king over Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know my time is coming to an end, and I must prepare for the future of Israel; or
  • I am proud to see my son take the throne while I am still alive; or
  • I feel relief knowing the kingdom will continue under Solomon's leadership.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel the weight of responsibility that my father places on my shoulders; or
  • I am honored that my father trusts me to lead Israel; or
  • I hope I can be as good a king as my father has been.

Continue the drama.

David gathers all the leaders of Israel together with the priests and Levites. David orders people to count the Levites who are 30 years old or older. There are 38,000 Levites in total.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed at how many Levites God has provided for service; or
  • I need to organize these Levites properly before I die; or
  • I want to make sure the temple worship is well-established for generations to come.

Ask the person playing one of the Levites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder what task people will give me to do in the service of Yahweh; or
  • I feel honored to be counted among those who will serve in the temple; or
  • I am curious why King David is counting all of us at this time.

Continue the drama.

David assigns duties to the Levites. David puts 24,000 in charge of the work in Yahweh's temple. David assigns 6,000 to be officials and judges. David designates 4,000 to be gatekeepers. David appoints 4,000 to praise Yahweh with musical instruments that David provided. David organizes the Levites into divisions based on the three sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to make sure everything is in order for proper worship when the temple is built; or
  • I am fulfilling my responsibility to organize God's people for service; or
  • I feel satisfaction when I complete this important task before I die.

Ask the person playing a Levite who David assigned to temple work, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am honored that David chose me to work in Yahweh's temple; or
  • I will do my best to serve faithfully in this important role; or
  • I am glad to be part of such a large group that serves Yahweh together.

Ask the person playing a Levite assigned to be a musician, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am excited to praise Yahweh with the instruments King David provided; or
  • I will use my musical talents to honor God with all my heart; or
  • I feel blessed to be able to lead others in worship through music.

Ask the person playing a leader of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I see how important proper worship is to King David; or
  • I am impressed by David's careful organization of the Levites; or
  • I will support these arrangements for temple service under Solomon's reign.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:1–6 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

David is the king of Israel, and David is now old. David makes his son Solomon the new king. For more information on David, king, and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for David, king, and Israel as you used in previous passages.

David gathers together the political or military leaders, priests and the Levites. Both the priests and the Levites are descendants of Levi. Priests are the descendants of Aaron who serve in the temple. Other Levites who were not descendants of Aaron helped the priests in their duties. For more information on priests and Levites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for political or military leaders, priests, and Levites as you used in previous passages.

David assigns 24,000 Levites to supervise the work in the temple. The temple is the house of God, which people will build in Jerusalem. For more information on temple, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for temple as you used in previous passages.

The Levites will serve in Yahweh's temple. Yahweh is the personal name of God in the Bible. Some Levites will offer praises to Yahweh with instruments. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

David assigns 6,000 Levites to be officials and judges. Officials are Levites who serve as leaders in the community. These officials help govern the people. In the time of Moses, officials helped lead the people. Officials worked with judges to apply God's laws. Officials helped organize the tribes and maintain order in the community.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for officials. What words in your language describe people who help govern and lead a community? Pause this audio here.

Judges are Levites who make decisions about disputes among the people. David assigns these Levites to work with officials. For more information on judges, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for judges as you used in previous passages.

David assigns 4,000 Levites to be gatekeepers. Gatekeepers are Levites who guard the entrances to the temple. Gatekeepers had an important role in the tabernacle before people built the temple. Gatekeepers protected the sacred areas and treasures. In the time of Moses, the Levites camped around the tabernacle to guard it. Later, gatekeepers became more organized under King David and Solomon. Use the same word or phrase for gatekeepers as you have used in previous passages.

Four thousand Levites will praise Yahweh with musical instruments. For more information on the word for praise, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for praise as you used in previous passages.

David organized the Levites into their family groups that were the descendants of the three sons of Levi. In this case, sons refers to the male children of Levi. For more information on the word for sons, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sons as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 23:7–11
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 23:7–11 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:7–11 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:7–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, David organized the Levites into divisions, or working groups, based on the three sons of Levi. These sons were Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Now we learn more about the families that came from Gershon.

Gershon was the oldest son of Levi. In the time of Moses, the Gershonites had very important duties. The Gershonites took care of the curtains, coverings, and hangings of the tabernacle. The Gershonites carried these items when the Israelites moved from place to place in the wilderness. Yahweh assigned the Gershonites to camp on the west side of the tabernacle. The Gershonites received two carts and four oxen to help with their work.

Gershon had two sons named Ladan and Shimei. These two sons started the main family lines of the Gershonites. In other parts of the Bible, people call Ladan by the name Libni.

Ladan had three sons. The oldest son was Jehiel. The other sons were Zetham and Joel. These three men led all the clans of Ladan.

Shimei also had sons. These sons were Shelomoth, Haziel, and Haran. These three sons led important families.

It is unclear, but Shimei probably had four more sons. These sons were Jahath, Ziza, Jeush, and Beriah. Jahath was the oldest, and Ziza was second. Jeush and Beriah did not have many descendants. Because their families were small, David counted Jeush and Beriah's families as one clan and assigned them one job together.

David helped prepare for the temple that Solomon would build by organizing the workers. Each family line had specific responsibilities. David was making sure everything was ready for worship in the temple.

In the early days, the Gershonites needed to be strong. The Gershonites had to carry the tabernacle materials when Israel traveled in the wilderness. Now that the ark would have a permanent home in the temple, the Gershonites would have different duties. The Gershonites would no longer need to carry heavy items from place to place. We learn later in this story that this is why David changed the starting age for service from 30 to 20.

Later in Israel's history, some Gershonites became musicians in the temple. Some of the Gershonites joined a group called the Asaphites. They helped with music during worship. Others served as officials and judges for the people.

The family organization also created a sense of connection across generations. Sons would serve in the same roles as their fathers. This helped people pass down knowledge and traditions from one generation to the next.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you divide families to do certain tasks in your community?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:7–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces the two sons of Gershon: Ladan and Shimei. Gershon was one of the three sons of Levi that the storyteller mentioned at the end of the previous passage. The passage begins by showing how David organized the Levites by their family groups.

In the second scene: The storyteller names the sons of Ladan. These sons were Jehiel the first, then Zetham, and then Joel. There were three sons in total. The storyteller also mentions three sons of Shimei: Shelomoth, Haziel, and Haran.

In the third scene: The storyteller describes four more sons of Shimei: Jahath, Ziza, Jeush, and Beriah. Jahath was the oldest, and Ziza was the second. Jeush and Beriah did not have many sons. Because they had few descendants, David counted Jeush and Beriah's families as one family group and gave them one assignment.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Gershon
  • Ladan
  • Shimei
  • Jehiel
  • Zetham
  • Joel
  • Shelomoth
  • Haziel
  • Haran
  • Jahath
  • Ziza
  • Jeush
  • And Beriah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This passage continues from the previous passage. David is still organizing the Levites for their future service in the temple. Now David is organizing the Levites according to their family groups. This passage focuses on the descendants of Gershon, who was one of the three sons of Levi.

This passage shows the importance of family relationships in Israelite society. People's roles and duties often depended on which family they belonged to. David organizes the Levites by their family lines, starting with the descendants of Gershon.

In this passage, David continues to organize the Levites for temple service. This organization is very detailed. David is making sure that every Levite knows his place and role in the temple service. David's careful organization shows how important proper worship was to him. David wanted to make sure that the temple service would continue smoothly after his death. Each person needed to know their duties.

The passage records not just the main family heads but also smaller details like which son was first or second, and which families had fewer members. David will say, "This is the oldest son," and then, after David says all the sons' names, David says how many sons there were in all.

Stop here and, as an activity, tell the others in the group about a family that has many children. Tell the group all the children's names. Pay attention to when you choose to say how many children there are and how you talk about who the oldest is. Then discuss how you want to translate the way that David talks about who the oldest sons are and how many sons are in the family. Pause this audio here.

David talks about the families of Gershon's two sons, Ladan and Shimei. Later, David talks about the sons of Shimei, then says that these sons are the leaders of the family clan of Ladan. Then David talks about more sons of Shimei. This part of the passage is not clear. It is possible that there is more than one Shimei in the Gershonite tribe. It is possible that Ladan has a descendant whose name is Shimei, and then David talks about the sons of Levi's son Shimei. Or David could talk about only one man whose name was Shimei in this passage. We are not sure of the exact family lines in this passage, but you should translate this part the way it is in the text.

David mentions that two of Shimei's sons, Jeush and Beriah, did not have many descendants. Because of this, David combines these sons' families and counts them as one group for temple service. This shows that the organization of the family groups was practical. When a family line had few members, this family line could join with another small family for their duties.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do family connections affect roles and responsibilities in your culture? How will you translate the idea that David combined small families for practical purposes? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the concept that David organizes people by family groups so they can do their work duties? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it. As you do this, you might want to use drawings, objects, or even people to represent the family tree of Gershon. Show Gershon with his two sons, Ladan and Shimei. Then show Ladan with his three sons: Jehiel, Zetham, and Joel. Show Shimei with his first three sons: Shelomoth, Haziel, and Haran. Also show Shimei's four other sons: Jahath, Ziza, Jeush, and Beriah. You could show Jeush and Beriah standing together because they had few sons. This will help you understand how David organized the Levites by their family groups.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:7–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Gershon
  • Ladan
  • Shimei
  • Jehiel
  • Zetham
  • Joel
  • Shelomoth
  • Haziel
  • Haran
  • Jahath
  • Ziza
  • Jeush
  • And Beriah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

David organizes the Levites by their family groups. Gershon is one of the three sons of Levi. Gershon has two sons: Ladan and Shimei.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to make sure every Levite family knows their assignment for the temple; or
  • It is important to keep good records of all the Levite families; or
  • I need to organize the Levites carefully so that worship in the temple will run smoothly when Solomon builds it.

Ask the person playing Gershon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud that my family will serve Yahweh in the temple; or
  • I hope my sons and grandsons will do their duties well; or
  • I feel honored to be one of the three main Levite families.

Continue the drama.

Ladan has three sons: Jehiel the first, Zetham, and Joel. Shimei also has three sons: Shelomoth, Haziel, and Haran. These were the leaders of the families of Ladan.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ladan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am thankful for my three sons who will continue our family line; or
  • I hope Jehiel leads his brothers well, as my oldest son; or
  • I want my family to serve Yahweh faithfully in the temple.

Ask the person playing Shimei, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am thankful that Yahweh caused me to have several sons; or
  • I want my sons to be good leaders for the families of Ladan; or
  • I feel a great responsibility to prepare my sons for leadership.

Ask the person playing Shelomoth, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will do my best to lead the family group I am responsible for; or
  • I want to honor my father Shimei by being a good leader; or
  • I must work closely with my cousins to make sure the temple service runs smoothly.

Continue the drama.

Shimei has four more sons: Jahath, Ziza, Jeush, and Beriah. Jahath is the oldest, and Ziza is the second. Jeush and Beriah do not have many sons. Because they have few descendants, David counts Jeush and Beriah as one family group and gives them one assignment.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jahath, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • As the oldest of these four brothers, I feel responsible for them; or
  • I want to make sure my family serves well in the temple; or
  • I am thankful that my father has so many sons to serve Yahweh.

Ask the person playing Jeush or Beriah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I understand why King David counts our families as one, since we have fewer sons; or
  • Even though our family is smaller, our service to Yahweh is still important; or
  • I am glad we still have a place in the organization, even with fewer descendants.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Every Levite family needs a clear assignment, even the smaller ones; or
  • I want to be fair and practical when I organize the Levites; or
  • This organization will help the temple worship continue for generations.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:7–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Gershon is one of the three sons of Levi. The other sons are Kohath and Merari. Gershon's family line is called the Gershonites. The Gershonites are part of the Levites who serve in the temple.

Ladan is one of the sons of Gershon. Some people call Ladan "Libni" in other parts of the Bible. Ladan's family becomes an important group within the Gershonites. Some of Ladan's descendants become heads of their father's households or large family groups. For more information on household, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the leader or head of their father's household as you used in previous passages.

Son is a male child of a parent. In the Bible, sons are very important. Sons carry on the family name. Sons inherit family property. Sons continue family traditions. In this passage, we learn about the sons of Gershon, who was a son of Levi. For more information on son when the storyteller uses the word to refer to a male child of a parent, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for son as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 23:12–20
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 23:12–20 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:12–20 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember that in the previous passage, David organized the Levites into working groups based on the three sons of Levi. These sons were Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Now the storyteller gives details about the descendants of Kohath.

Kohath had four sons. These sons' names were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Each of these sons became the head of a family group within the Kohath family group.

Amram was the father of Aaron and Moses. God chose Aaron and his descendants for a special purpose. God set these people apart to prepare the holy things for the temple service. The descendants of Aaron offered sacrifices, or burned incense, to Yahweh. They served Yahweh in the temple. They also blessed the people in Yahweh's name. This was the duty that Aaron's descendants always had.

Stop here and tell a story about a particular family group that has a special task that only they can do. Pause this audio here.

People called Moses "the man of God." Moses and his sons were a part of the tribe of Levi. Unlike Aaron's descendants, Moses' descendants did not have a special status. These descendants were ordinary Levites.

Moses had two sons named Gershom and Eliezer. Gershom's first son was Shubael. Eliezer had only one son named Rehabiah. Rehabiah was the family leader. Rehabiah had many descendants.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about family groups in your culture who have more or better status than others. Why is this? Pause this audio here.

Kohath's second son, Izhar, had a son named Shelomith. Shelomith was the head of his clan.

Hebron, who was Kohath's third son, had four sons. Jeriah was the first son and the leader. Amariah was the second son. Jahaziel was the third son. Jekameam was the fourth son.

Uzziel, who was Kohath's youngest son, had two sons. Micah was the first son and the leader. Isshiah was the second son.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why is it important to know your family relationships? Pause this audio here.

This detailed list shows how David organized the Levites. Each person had a specific place in the temple service. This organization helped prepare for worship in the temple that Solomon would build.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does good organization help people get important work done in your community?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces the four sons of Kohath. Kohath was one of the three sons of Levi that the storyteller mentioned at the end of a previous passage. The storyteller continues to show how David organized the Levites by their family groups. Kohath's sons were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.

In the second scene: The storyteller then tells about the sons of Amram, who were Aaron and Moses. Yahweh set Aaron and his descendants apart for special service. Yahweh chose these people to prepare the holy things in the temple. Yahweh also chose them to offer sacrifices to Yahweh and to proclaim blessings in his name forever.

In the third scene: The storyteller names the sons and descendants of Moses, and the other sons of Kohath. Moses had two sons named Gershom and Eliezer. Gershom's son was Shubael, who was the family leader. Eliezer had only one son named Rehabiah, but Rehabiah had many descendants. Then the storyteller lists the descendants of Kohath's other sons. Izhar's son was Shelomith. Hebron had four sons: Jeriah was first, Amariah was second, Jahaziel was third, and Jekameam was fourth. Uzziel had two sons: Micah was first, and Isshiah was second.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Kohath
  • Amram
  • Izhar
  • Hebron
  • Uzziel
  • Aaron
  • Moses
  • Gershom
  • Eliezer
  • Shubael
  • Rehabiah
  • Shelomith
  • Jeriah
  • Amariah
  • Jahaziel
  • Jekameam
  • Micah
  • And Isshiah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This passage continues from the previous passage. David is still organizing the Levites for their future service in the temple. Now David is organizing the Levites according to their family groups. The storyteller now focuses on the descendants of Kohath, who was one of the three sons of Levi.

In this passage, the storyteller shows the special place of Aaron's family in Israelite worship. Aaron and his descendants were set apart to be priests. These people had special duties that other Levites did not have. These people prepared the holy things, burned incense, and blessed the people in Yahweh's name.

The passage also shows that even though Moses was an important leader, Moses' family did not receive special status. Moses' sons were regular Levites. This shows that people received roles in temple service by family line, not by personal achievement or status.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people assign important roles in your culture? How will you translate the idea that Aaron's family had special duties that others did not have? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, David continues to organize the Levites in a very detailed way. David makes sure that every Levite knows his place and role in the temple service. The passage records not just the main family heads but also details about which son was first, second, third, or fourth. This shows that each person had their place in the system.

The storyteller also notes when a family had few descendants, like Jeush and Beriah in the previous passage, or many descendants, like Rehabiah in this passage. This shows that David paid attention to things like family size.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the idea that some families had many descendants, and some families had just a few descendants? Pause this audio here.

The organization of the Levites by family groups helped ensure that temple worship would continue properly. Each family group had specific responsibilities. This system would continue from generation to generation.

The family organization also created continuity over time. Sons would serve in similar roles as their fathers. This helped traditions continue and made sure that people passed down important knowledge. The storyteller also says, "this is the first son" or "second son," which shows how important the birth order was in families.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the way that the storyteller tells us which son was born first, second, third, or fourth? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it. As you do this, you might want to use drawings, objects, or even people to represent the family tree of Kohath. Show Kohath with his four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Then show Amram with his two sons: Aaron and Moses. Show that God set Aaron's family apart for priestly duties. Show Moses with his two sons: Gershom and Eliezer. Then show the sons of each of Kohath's other sons. This will help you understand how the storyteller tells us the names of the Levites by their family groups.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 3 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • Kohath
  • Amram
  • Izhar
  • Hebron
  • Uzziel
  • Aaron
  • Moses
  • Gershom
  • Eliezer
  • Shubael
  • Rehabiah
  • Shelomith
  • Jeriah
  • Amariah
  • Jahaziel
  • Jekameam
  • Micah
  • And Isshiah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

David is organizing the Levites by their family groups. Kohath is one of the three sons of Levi. Kohath has four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I need to organize all the Levite families carefully for the temple service; or
  • I want to make sure the worship of Yahweh continues properly after I am gone; or
  • It is important to keep good records of all the Levite families and their duties.

Ask the person playing Kohath, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud that my family will have important duties in Yahweh's temple; or
  • I hope my four sons will lead their families well in service to Yahweh; or
  • I feel honored to be one of the three main Levite families.

Continue the drama.

Amram has two sons: Aaron and Moses. Aaron and his descendants are set apart for special service. These people prepare the holy things, offer sacrifices to Yahweh, and pronounce blessings in Yahweh's name. Yahweh calls Moses "the man of God," but counts his sons as regular Levites.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Amram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am blessed to have two such important sons as Aaron and Moses; or
  • I am proud that Aaron's family will serve as priests in Yahweh's temple; or
  • I am thankful that both my sons have important roles in service to Yahweh and his people.

Ask the person playing Aaron, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is a serious responsibility to take care of the holy things in Yahweh's temple. Yahweh has honored me with this job and I know I must pay close attention to what I do; or
  • I want to make sure my sons understand the importance of their priestly duties; or
  • I am honored that Yahweh chose my family to serve him as priests forever.

Ask the person playing Moses, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am content that my sons will serve as regular Levites in the temple; or
  • Even though I led Israel, my sons do not have special status; or
  • I am called "the man of God" because I followed Yahweh faithfully, not because of my family position.

Continue the drama.

Moses has two sons: Gershom and Eliezer. Gershom's son is Shubael, who is the family leader. Eliezer has only one son, Rehabiah, but Rehabiah has many descendants. Izhar's son is Shelomith. Hebron has four sons: Jeriah, Amariah, Jahaziel, and Jekameam. Uzziel has two sons: Micah and Isshiah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Gershom, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am proud that my son Shubael is a family leader among the Levites; or
  • Even though we are not priests, our service to Yahweh is still important; or
  • I want my family to serve faithfully in the duties we are given.

Ask the person playing Eliezer, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am thankful that my one son, Rehabiah, has many descendants; or
  • Our family line will continue strongly through Rehabiah's many children; or
  • I am grateful that our family will continue to serve in Yahweh's temple.

Ask the person playing Hebron, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am blessed to have four sons who will lead their families well; or
  • I hope Jeriah will guide his brothers wisely as the oldest; or
  • I want all my sons to work together to serve Yahweh properly.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This detailed organization will help the temple service run smoothly; or
  • Every Levite family has their place and purpose in Yahweh's service; or
  • I am making sure that people will continue to worship Yahweh properly in future generations.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller talks about the sons of Levi. Sometimes in this passage, the word "son" means the male child of his parents and sometimes son means "descendant." A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. Aaron's descendants are the priests. Moses' descendants are counted as Levites. For more information on Levi, son, and descendant, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for Levi, son, and descendant as you used in previous passages.

Aaron and his sons consecrate the holy things. To consecrate means to make something or someone holy for a special purpose. Holy means set apart for God. The things in the temple are holy. For more information on consecrate and holy, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for consecrate and holy as you used in previous passages.

Aaron and his sons minister before Yahweh. To minister means to serve God in the temple. This is part of the priests' special job.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for minister. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Aaron and his sons ask God to bless the people. When you bless people, you ask God to give good things to people. For more information on bless, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for bless as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 23:21–32
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 23:21–32 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:21–32 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:21–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues the list of Levi's descendants in this passage. Now the storyteller focuses on the family of Merari, who was one of Levi's sons along with Gershon and Kohath. All together, these families formed the tribe of Levi.

Merari had two sons named Mahli and Mushi. Mahli's sons were Eleazar and Kish. Eleazar died without having any sons. Eleazar only had daughters. These daughters married their cousins, who were the sons of Kish. This marriage helped to keep property within the tribe, as the Law of Moses required. Mushi had three sons named Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth.

This passage shows how David organized the Levites for temple service. David counts the Levites who are 20 years old or older. In earlier times, Levites started their work for the temple at the age of 30. David counted the Levites just before the end of his life. This was one of David's last official acts as king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do leaders in your community prepare for the future before the next leader begins to lead? Pause this audio here.

David said that Yahweh had given peace to his people, Israel. David also said that Yahweh had chosen to make Jerusalem the permanent place for Yahweh's presence among his people. Yahweh's special presence would be in the temple in Jerusalem. Because of this, the Levites no longer needed to carry the tabernacle or its equipment from place to place. The tabernacle was the portable tent with the ark of the covenant, where people worshiped before people built the temple. The ark represented Yahweh's presence among his people. Because the men no longer needed to be strong enough to carry the equipment, David could start to count the men from the age of 20 years old.

Stop here and tell a story about a time when a leader changed the rules about something. Why did that leader change the rules? How did that leader say that they had changed the rules? Pause this audio here.

Just like the Levites served at the tabernacle, now the Levites would soon serve at the temple. While the place of worship changed, the Levites still focused on honoring Yahweh. The Levites' job was still to help Aaron's descendants, the priests, in the temple service. Now, instead of carrying the tabernacle tent, the Levites took care of the temple courtyards and rooms. The Levites made sure all the holy things that the priests used in worship stayed pure, or ritually clean. The Levites helped with many other tasks in Yahweh's temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How have worship practices changed in your community over time? Pause this audio here.

The Levites were responsible for preparing the "bread of the presence" that sat on the table in the holy place. The Levites arranged this bread in two rows of six loaves. The Levites kept the flour for grain offerings. The Levites made the flat bread, which they made without yeast. The Levites mixed and baked the bread for offerings. The Levites also made sure all measurements were correct.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What jobs do people in your community do to help with worship? Pause this audio here.

Every morning and evening, the Levites stood in their places to thank and praise Yahweh. The Levites also helped when the priests presented burnt offerings to Yahweh. These offerings happened on Sabbath days, at New Moon festivals, and on other special days or festivals. Sabbaths were weekly days to rest and worship Yahweh. New Moon festivals happened on the first day of the month, when people could only see the thin edge of the moon. During the New Moon festivals, people would sacrifice or offer gifts to Yahweh. The correct number of Levites had to be present at these times. The Levites followed the rules for worship.

The Levites watched over the Tent of Meeting and the sanctuary, or the Holy Place. The "Tent of Meeting" was another name for the tabernacle. The Holy Place could refer to the inside of the tabernacle or to the temple. The Levites helped their relatives, Aaron's descendants, with serving and working in Yahweh's temple. The storyteller wanted to show that the priests and the Levites cooperated very well together.

Stop here and tell a story about a time when different groups worked together but had different specific roles. Why is it important for people to have specific roles when they work together? Pause this audio here.

For the people who first heard this story, these details reminded them how important it was for people to worship in the temple so that they could have a good relationship with Yahweh. The temple was the special place where Yahweh chose to make his presence known among his people. The temple was the center of the Israelites' community life. The way the Levites faithfully performed their duties set an example for the community.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:21–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: We learn about the descendants of Merari. Merari has two sons named Mahli and Mushi. Mahli's sons are Eleazar and Kish. Eleazar dies without having any sons. Eleazar only has daughters. These daughters marry their cousins, the sons of Kish. Mushi has three sons named Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth.

In the second scene: David explains why the Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle. Yahweh has given peace to his people. Yahweh now lives in Jerusalem forever. The Levites will not need to carry the tabernacle or any of its equipment anymore. Now, David counts all the Levites who are 20 years old or older. These Levites will serve in the temple.

In the third scene: David describes the new duties of the Levites. The Levites help Aaron's descendants with temple service. The Levites take care of the courtyards and rooms. The Levites make sure the holy things stay pure. The Levites prepare the sacred bread, flour for offerings, and special cakes. The Levites stand every morning and evening to thank and praise Yahweh. The Levites help with sacrifices on Sabbaths, New Moon festivals, and other special days. The Levites follow all the proper procedures for serving Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Merari
  • Mahli
  • Mushi
  • Eleazar
  • Kish
  • The daughters of Eleazar
  • The sons of Kish
  • Mahli
  • Eder
  • Jerimoth
  • David
  • Aaron's descendants
  • The other Levites
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story happens at the end of David's life. David has organized the Levites into groups to help the priests in the temple that Solomon will build. The storyteller shows how the role of the Levites changes when the temple replaces the tabernacle as the center of worship. The Levites will no longer need to transport the ark and sacred items from place to place. Instead, the Levites will serve in the permanent temple in Jerusalem.

David explains that Yahweh has given his people peace. The land now belongs to Yahweh and his people. The Levites' new duties focus on helping the priests by maintaining the temple and its surroundings. The Levites take care of the courtyards and rooms. The Levites prepare the holy bread and offerings. The Levites lead morning and evening praises to Yahweh. The Levites assist with ceremonies on holy days.

The Levites work alongside the priests but have specific tasks that are different from priestly duties. Only descendants of Aaron can serve as priests. The other Levites support the priests in their work. The Levites now serve from age 20 instead of 30. This change helps provide enough workers for all the temple duties.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people in your culture prepare buildings for worship? What words or phrases would you use to translate the duties of the Levites to maintain the temple? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, David speaks of specific tasks the Levites must do. The Levites must keep the temple courtyards and rooms clean. The courtyards are the open areas that surround the temple. The Levites must make sure all holy objects remain pure. The Levites prepare different kinds of bread and cakes for offerings. The Levites weigh and measure ingredients to ensure everything follows Yahweh's instructions. The Levites stand each morning and evening to thank and praise Yahweh through music and singing.

David talks about the special bread for the temple table. This bread sits on a gold table in the Holy Place. The priests place 12 fresh loaves there each Sabbath day, and they call it the "bread of the presence," which means the bread that sits in the presence of Yahweh, or near where Yahweh lives. The Levites also prepare fine flour for grain offerings. The Levites make thin wafers without yeast. The Levites prepare cakes that they cook with oil. The Levites make sure all these things follow the exact amounts that Yahweh's law requires.

The storyteller mentions special times when the Levites assist with sacrifices. These times include Sabbath days, New Moon festivals, and celebrations that happen each year. The Levites follow exact procedures during these special times. The Levites make sure the right number of offerings occurs at the right times.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people prepare special food for religious ceremonies? What words or phrases would you use to translate the different kinds of bread and offerings that the storyteller mentions in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also describes how the Levites help maintain proper worship. The Levites work under the direction of the priests. The Levites guard and take care of the holy places and objects. The Levites' work ensures that worship continues properly. Even though their duties change, the Levites remain important to Israel's worship of Yahweh.

David organizes the Levites because he knows he will not live to see when people finish the temple. David prepares everything Solomon will need to build and maintain the temple. This shows David's commitment to proper worship of Yahweh, even if David cannot participate in it himself.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it. As you do this, you might want to use drawings, objects, or even people to represent the family tree of Merari's descendants. Consider showing how David counts the Levites who are 20 years old or older. You could also show how the Levites clean the temple courtyards, prepare bread offerings, stand to sing praises to Yahweh, and help the priests during special festivals.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:21–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three parts.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Merari
  • Mahli
  • Mushi
  • Eleazar
  • Kish
  • The daughters of Eleazar
  • The sons of Kish
  • Mahli
  • Eder
  • Jerimoth
  • David
  • Aaron's descendants
  • The other Levites
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The passage begins with a list of Merari's descendants. Merari has two sons, Mahli and Mushi. Mahli's sons are Eleazar and Kish. Eleazar dies without having any sons. Eleazar only has daughters. These daughters marry their cousins, the sons of Kish. Mushi has three sons named Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Eleazar's daughter, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am concerned about continuing our family line since my father has no sons; or
  • I am glad that I can marry my cousin and keep our inheritance within our family; or
  • I feel responsible to preserve my father's place in the tribe of Levi.

Ask the person playing one of Kish's sons, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am honored to marry my cousin and help continue Eleazar's family line; or
  • I understand the importance of keeping our inheritance within our family.

Continue the drama.

David explains that the Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle. David says, "Yahweh, the God of Israel, has given his people rest. Yahweh will now live in Jerusalem forever. The Levites will not need to carry the tabernacle or any of its equipment anymore." David counts all the Levites who are 20 years old or older. These Levites will serve in the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased that Yahweh has given us peace and a permanent place to worship him; or
  • I want to make sure the Levites have clear duties now that they will not have to carry the tabernacle; or
  • I am planning ahead for the temple worship even though I will not see it completed.

Ask the person playing one of the Levites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder what my duties will be now that we will not have to carry the tabernacle; or
  • I am excited to serve in the permanent temple instead of moving from place to place; or
  • I feel honored to be counted among those who will serve in the temple.

Continue the drama.

David describes the duties of the Levites. David says, "The Levites will help Aaron's descendants with temple service. The Levites will take care of the courtyards and rooms. The Levites will make sure the holy things stay pure. The Levites will prepare the sacred bread, flour for offerings, and special cakes. The Levites will stand every morning and evening to thank and praise Yahweh. The Levites will help with sacrifices on Sabbaths, New Moon festivals, and other special days. The Levites will follow all the proper procedures for serving Yahweh."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to ensure proper worship continues after I am gone; or
  • I am establishing clear duties so no one will be confused about the Levites' responsibilities; or
  • I want the Levites to know how important their service is to Yahweh and to all Israel.

Ask the person playing one of Aaron's descendants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I appreciate that the Levites will help us with our duties in the temple; or
  • I understand that the priests and Levites must work together for proper worship; or
  • I am thankful that David has organized the temple service so carefully.

Ask the person playing one of the Levites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud that my duties include preparing offerings and helping with temple worship; or
  • I am honored to stand and praise Yahweh every morning and evening; or
  • I want to learn all the proper procedures so I can serve Yahweh correctly.

Continue the drama.

The passage concludes with a summary of the Levites' responsibilities. The Levites watch over the Tent of Meeting and the Holy Place. The Levites help their relatives, the descendants of Aaron, with the service of Yahweh's temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing one of the Levites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I understand that my role is to support the priests in their service to Yahweh; or
  • I feel privileged to care for the holy places where we worship Yahweh; or
  • I am committed to serve faithfully according to the duties David has assigned to us.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 23:21–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller talks about the Levites by their family groups. The Levites are people from the tribe of Levi. The Levites have special duties in the worship of Yahweh. David organized the Levites into three main family lines: the Gershonites, the Kohathites, and the Merarites. For more information on Levites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Levites as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller talks about the sons of Levi. At the beginning of this passage, the storyteller lists the sons of Merari and their sons. In this case, the word "son" means the male child of his parents. Then the storyteller talks about the "descendants" of Levi by their family, and the descendants of Aaron. A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. For more information, refer to son and descendant in the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for sons and descendants as you used in previous passages.

Use the same word or phrase for heads or leaders of families that you have used in previous passages.

David says Yahweh will dwell in Jerusalem forever. Jerusalem is the city where David ruled and where Solomon would build the temple. Yahweh chose Jerusalem as the place where people would honor Yahweh's name, or worship Yahweh. Yahweh is God's personal name. For more information on Jerusalem and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for Jerusalem and Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

David mentions that Yahweh, the God of Israel, has given rest to Israel. Israel refers to the people that Yahweh has chosen. Rest refers to safety from enemies and peace in the land. For more information on God and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for God, Israel, and rest as you used in previous passages.

David says the Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle because God will now live permanently in Jerusalem. The tabernacle**,** or Tent of Meeting, was the portable worship place where people worshiped Yahweh before people built the temple. For more information on tabernacle, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for tabernacle as you used in previous passages.

The Levites will serve in the house of Yahweh instead of carrying the tabernacle. This kind of serving is actual physical work that the Levites will do in the temple. The "house of Yahweh" is another name for the temple. The temple is the permanent building for worship that Solomon will build in Jerusalem. For more information on the temple and "house of Yahweh," refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for temple or house of Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

The Levites purify the holy things in the temple. When you purify something, you make that thing ritually clean for worship. For more information on holy and on purify, or purification, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for holy and purify or purification as you used in previous passages.

The Levites take care of the Holy Place. The Holy Place, or sanctuary, is the sacred area inside the temple. For more information on Holy Place, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Holy Place as you used in previous passages.

The courtyards are the areas around the tabernacle or temple, where the people gather together.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for courtyards. Pause this audio here.

The Levites make the bread of the Presence. The "bread of the Presence" is the special bread that the priests put on a table in the Holy Place of the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for bread of the Presence. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about "bread of the Presence." Pause this audio here.

The Levites also praise Yahweh in the temple. Use the same word or phrase for praise as you have used previously. Praise is in the Master Glossary.

The Levites help the priests present burnt offerings to Yahweh on special days. Burnt offerings are animal sacrifices that the priests completely burn on the altar. For more information on burnt offerings, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for burnt offerings as you used in previous passages.

The Sabbath was one of those special days. The Sabbath is the seventh day of the week for the Israelites. The Sabbath is a day for people to rest and worship God. For more information on Sabbath, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Sabbath as you used in previous passages.

The New Moon Festival was another one of those special days. In Old Testament times, the people of Israel celebrated this festival at the beginning of each month, when the people could see the new moon rise in the sky. During this festival, the Israelites stopped their work, offered special sacrifices, and blew trumpets to celebrate.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for the New Moon festival. Look up "Festival of the New Moon" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

These Levites helped their brothers, or relatives, who were the descendants of Aaron and who were priests in the temple. Use the same word or phrase for brothers when it means relatives as you have used previously.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 24:1–19
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 24:1–19 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 24:1–19 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 24:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passages, when King David grew old, David made his son Solomon the new king of Israel. Then David counted all of the men of the tribe of Levi and organized the men so that the men would be ready to serve in the new temple that Solomon would build for Yahweh to replace the tabernacle.

In this passage, we will now learn how David and the leaders counted the men who descended from the first high priest, Aaron, who was also a Levite. We will also learn how David and the leaders organized these men into groups to serve in the new temple. You will remember that it was the Levites who descended from Aaron who served as the priests.

The storyteller begins when he says that he will now tell us how they divided the descendants of Aaron into groups. But first, the storyteller will provide some short background information about the descendants of Aaron.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story in your language. As you begin the story, first tell some background information that happened long before the time of the story, but that people need to know to understand what happens in the story. Then tell the rest of your story. Pause this audio here.

Aaron had four sons, whose names were Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. But Nadab and Abihu died while their father, Aaron, was still alive. The storyteller does not retell this sinful part of the story here because his goal is to focus on how God was with David in the kingdom. But we know from Leviticus chapter 10 and also Numbers chapter three that Nadab and Abihu disobeyed Yahweh and burned the wrong kind of fire as an offering. So Yahweh punished Nadab and Abihu when Yahweh put them to death with fire.

The storyteller reminds us that Nadab and Abihu did not have any sons to take their place as priests, and so Nadab and Abihu's younger brothers, Eleazar and Ithamar, became the next priests. However, by the time of this story, Eleazar and Ithamar had grown old and died. So instead, Zadok, one of Eleazar's descendants, and Ahimelech, one of Ithamar's descendants, helped David count all of Aaron's descendants who would serve as priests in the temple. This may mean that David told Eleazar and Ahimelech what to do, and then Eleazar and Ahimelech helped to organize the men into the right groups according to the kind of work each man would do in the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when people in your culture organized a group of people to do an important task. Pause this audio here.

Eleazar's descendants had more men who knew how to lead than Ithamar's descendants did. So David, Zadok, and Ahimelech organized the priests so that Eleazar's descendants had 16 groups with their leaders, and Ithamar's descendants had eight groups with their leaders. This means they had a total of 24 groups of men.

The storyteller explains that Eleazar's clan and Ithamar's clan included both consecrated officers and officers of God. So to make it fair for everyone, David, Zadok, and Ahimelech used lots to decide how to divide the groups of men. We are not sure how the men did this, but you will remember that to cast lots was a way people asked Yahweh's will. To do this, the men probably used a number of objects, like stones or sticks, which they may have marked in some way to represent a particular group or family. Then the men would have thrown or "cast" these lots onto the ground. The way each stone or stick landed showed how Yahweh wanted them to organize the men by the jobs each priest would do in the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you make decisions in a fair way when you organize groups of people? For example, maybe you are about to play a team sport, and you need to pick which people will play on which team. Or maybe you will work together to build a house, and you need to give each person a different part of the job to do. What do you use to help make these decisions? Pause this audio here.

One of the Levite men was named Shemaiah. Shemaiah was the son of Nethanel. Shemaiah was a scribe. A scribe is a person whose job is to write down and keep important histories and other information.
