First, organize your group so that the oldest or most experienced people in the group will do the tasks people enjoy most, and the youngest or least experienced people will do the tasks people enjoy least. After you have organized yourselves in this way, have each person in the group describe how they feel about this way of doing things. Next, find a way to randomly choose who gets to do each task. For example, you could write each person's name on a piece of paper, fold the papers up so no one can see the names, put all of the papers in a bowl or cup, and then pull one piece of paper out at a time from first to last to decide who gets to do which task. Now, once again, have each person say how they feel about this new way to organize things. Pause this audio here.

Another thing we notice about this list of sons is that the storyteller wrote many of the names differently here than in the previous story. For example, the son whom the storyteller called Zeri in the last story appears as Izri in this list, and the son Asarelah is now Jesharelah. Scholars are not sure why many of these sons' names appear differently in this list, but we know that these are the same men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about some people in your community who people call by more than one name. For example, maybe a person has the name their parents gave to them at birth, but people also call that person by another name to describe the way the person looks or something they did that people remember. Or maybe someone has their own name, but people also call the person by their family name or their father's name.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 25:8–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller says that the people cast lots to choose the order of the men's responsibilities. Whether a man was young or old, and whether that man had a lot of experience as a musician or was just learning, people treated every man in the same way.

In the second scene: The storyteller gives us a list of each man's name and the number of their family members who served with them. The storyteller lists 24 sons from the families of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun. And each one of these sons and their relatives with them formed a group of 12 men.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun, although the storyteller only mentions Asaph's name in this story
  • Joseph, son of Asaph
  • Gedaliah, son of Jeduthun
  • Zaccur, son of Asaph
  • Izri, son of Jeduthun
  • Nethaniah, son of Asaph
  • Bukkiah, son of Heman
  • Jesarelah, son of Asaph
  • Jeshaiah, son of Jeduthun
  • Mattaniah, son of Heman
  • Shimei, son of Jeduthun
  • Azarel, son of Heman
  • Hashabiah, son of Jeduthun
  • Shubael, son of Heman
  • Mattithiah, son of Jeduthun
  • Jerimoth, son of Heman
  • Hananiah, son of Heman
  • Joshbekashah, son of Heman
  • Hanani, son of Heman
  • Mallothi, son of Heman
  • Eliathah, son of Heman
  • Hothir, son of Heman
  • Giddalti, son of Heman
  • Mahazioth, son of Heman
  • Romamti-Ezer, son of Heman
  • And all of the relatives they chose to serve with each of these sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

You will remember that in the last story, the storyteller told how King David and the leaders chose Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun and their sons to serve as musicians in the temple. The storyteller listed each son's name and told how all of these men, along with their relatives who were also trained as musicians, totalled 288 people. Now, the storyteller will continue with his story and give more information about these men and how they divided up their responsibilities.

In the first scene, the storyteller says that these men cast lots for their duties. The storyteller may mean that the men cast the lots to decide which group would perform which task. But most scholars agree that it is more likely that this just means that they cast the lots to decide which order the men would serve in the temple, just like the other Levites did in a previous story.

The storyteller also emphasizes that all of the men alike cast lots for their duties, whether the men were young or old, a teacher or a student. In Israelite culture, people treated older people with more respect and authority, and also gave more authority to a person who was more skilled or experienced and could teach others. However, the storyteller seems to say that in this situation, they used the lots to decide who would go first, so that in some cases, younger or less-experienced people might have gone before older and more experienced people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you talk about people who are older or more important and also people who are younger or less important in the community? Also, how do you talk about people who are more skilled or experienced at something and people who have little experience or are just starting to learn that thing? What would be some good ways to talk about these ideas in your translation? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller tells the order in which the lots chose the men. The storyteller also tells how many sons and relatives served with that man.

Stop here as a group and listen again to 1 Chronicles 25:8–31. When you hear the storyteller give the list of these men, try to identify the pattern the storyteller uses to give that information. Pause this audio here.

You will notice that the storyteller talks about the first son on the list, Joseph, a little differently than the other sons. First, this is the only son where the storyteller mentions the father by name—Asaph. We are not sure why the storyteller does this, but you should do the same in your translation. Second, we notice that the first son, Joseph, is the only one in the list whom the storyteller does not mention other sons and relatives. However, we know that Joseph did have relatives with him to form a group of 12 men. We know this because in the last story, the storyteller told us there are a total of 288 men. There are 24 sons, and each of the other sons' relatives with him forms a group of 12 men. So each of the 24 groups of men would need to be 12 men to add up to 288 men altogether. Therefore, even though it is not in the original language, some translations add the phrase after Joseph's name to say that his sons and relatives, together with him, formed a group of 12 men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you talk about Joseph? Will you add the information after his name to say that he and his relatives formed a group of 12 men? Or will you not say this information about Joseph and trust that people will understand this is true for Joseph just as it is true for all of the other sons in the list? Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you structure the information about each son's family members and the size of their group? In the original language, the storyteller repeats the same information about the family group after each man's name. However, if you prefer, you could instead choose to give that information one time at the beginning and then just say each son's name in order after that. For example, "The 288 men divided according to their families into 24 groups, with a total of 12 men in each group and one man in charge of each group. These are the names of the leaders of each group and the order in which the groups did their work: First was Joseph of the family of Asaph, second was Gedaliah, third was Zaccur...." Pause this audio here.

You will also notice that in this list, the storyteller says some of the names of the sons a little differently than in the previous story. The son Izri in this story is the same son as Zeri in the last story, the son Jesharelah is the same son as Asarelah, and the son Azarel is the same as Uzziel. Although the storyteller says or writes these names a little differently in each story, we know that each of these sons is the same person. So if you think it would make your translation clearer and easier to understand, you may wish to use the exact same name for each of these sons in both this story and the last story.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Will you translate these names differently in each story, as the storyteller did, or will you make the names the same in both stories? If you decide to use the same names, which ones will you use? If you already talked about this and made a decision when you translated the last story, make sure to follow the same decision here. Pause this audio here.

Stop here as a group and practice telling this list of sons and their family groups. As you do this, you may wish to sit in a circle as a group so that each person in your group tells about the next son on the list. Help each other remember all of the names on the list. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 25:8–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun, although the storyteller only mentions Asaph's name in this story
  • Joseph, son of Asaph
  • Gedaliah, son of Jeduthun
  • Zaccur, son of Asaph
  • Izri, son of Jeduthun
  • Nethaniah, son of Asaph
  • Bukkiah, son of Heman
  • Jesarelah, son of Asaph
  • Jeshaiah, son of Jeduthun
  • Mattaniah, son of Heman
  • Shimei, son of Jeduthun
  • Azarel, son of Heman
  • Hashabiah, son of Jeduthun
  • Shubael, son of Heman
  • Mattithiah, son of Jeduthun
  • Jerimoth, son of Heman
  • Hananiah, son of Heman
  • Joshbekashah, son of Heman
  • Hanani, son of Heman
  • Mallothi, son of Heman
  • Eliathah, son of Heman
  • Hothir, son of Heman
  • Giddalti, son of Heman
  • Mahazioth, son of Heman
  • Romamti-Ezer, son of Heman
  • And all of the relatives they chose to serve with each of these sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

For this step, choose one person to play the part of the storyteller. This person will read or recite this passage out loud to the rest of the group in your language. Everyone else in the group will play the role of an ancient Israelite who listens to the passage.

First, the person who plays the storyteller should read or recite the passage out loud without stopping. Pay attention to the flow and the focus of the passage. Help the reader if some of the names are difficult. If the passage is too hard to remember, you can also play the audio recording out loud.

While you do this, imagine you are an ancient Israelite who listens to this list of men and their families who served in the temple as musicians. Remember how important it was for the Israelites that Yahweh was their God, and that they had the Levites to help the rest of the Israelite people obey Yahweh. Not just anyone could serve before Yahweh, so it was very important for people to know which families these men came from.

Pause this audio here and recite the passage.

The group should listen to this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person who plays the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person who plays the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and read or recite the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The storyteller says, "The men cast lots to assign their work. All of them drew lots, whether they were young or old, experts or beginners."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the men who cast the lots, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Those of us who are older and more experienced could probably go first if we wanted to, but we do not want people to complain that we were unfair; or
  • We will give everyone an equal chance by casting lots to let Yahweh choose. That way, if someone does not like the order, they can blame Yahweh and not us!

Continue the drama.

The storyteller continues: "The men divided themselves by family into groups, with a total of 12 men in each group and one man in charge of each group. These are the names of the men in charge of each group and the order in which the groups did their work: First, Joseph of the family of Asaph. Second, Gedaliah. Third, Zaccur. Fourth, Izri. Fifth, Nethaniah. Sixth, Bukkiah. Seventh, Jesarelah. Eighth, Jeshaiah. Ninth, Mattaniah. Tenth, Shimei. Eleventh, Azarel. Twelfth, Hashabiah. Thirteenth, Shubael. Fourteenth, Mattithiah. Fifteenth, Jerimoth. Sixteenth, Hananiah. Seventeenth, Joshbekashah. Eighteenth, Hanani. Nineteenth, Mallothi. Twentieth, Eliathah. Twenty-first, Hothir. Twenty-second, Giddalti. Twenty-third, Mahazioth. Twenty-fourth, Romamti-Ezer."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the men chosen first, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are surprised that Yahweh would choose us first, since some of us are younger and have little experience; or
  • We did not expect to be first, but we feel very honored that Yahweh chose us; or
  • It is a big responsibility to go first. This is the first time anyone will serve in the new temple, and we want to do it well!

Ask the people playing the men chosen last, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel surprised and a little offended that Yahweh chose us last. Some of us have more experience than anyone else; or
  • We are relieved that we do not have to go first. This way, we can watch how the other groups perform their music and learn from them before it is our turn to perform.

Ask the people who play the Israelites when they listen to the men play music in the temple, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We can tell which men seem to know and love Yahweh the best, because we see it in how they talk and act towards other people; or
  • The men who help us worship Yahweh the best are not always the most skilled at singing and playing instruments. We love to hear the beautiful sound of the music, but it is the words of the music and the way the men live their lives that teach us how to follow Yahweh.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 25:8–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In the first scene, the storyteller says that the people cast lots for their duties. You will remember that we do not know exactly how people cast lots, but they may have taken some objects like stones or sticks and written one man's name on each object. Then the people may have thrown the objects on the ground so that the way the items landed showed the order Yahweh gave to each man and that man's relatives to serve in the temple. Use the same word or phrase for cast lots or throw lots as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller says that the lots determined the men's service or duties. In the original language, this word means a service someone must do very carefully to fulfill a covenant or law. All of the Levites who served in the temple, including these musicians, helped the Israelites to keep the covenant with Yahweh by following Yahweh's commands. In the original language, this special word can also mean to heed or give attention to. You will remember from the last story that the musicians did not just sing and play music for Yahweh to hear. Even more important, the words of the music helped the people to learn about Yahweh and remember to obey Yahweh's commands.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the men's duties. Pause this audio here.

All of the men cast lots in the same way for their duties, whether they were small or great, teacher or student. In the original language, "small" means young or not important, and "great" means older or more important. Also, "teacher" means a person who is very experienced and can teach other people with less experience, and a student is someone who is learning from other people with more experience.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for teacher. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Each one of the 24 men, with their sons and brothers, formed a group of 12 men. In this passage, the sons are the literal sons of their fathers. Also in this passage, the word "brothers" could either mean men who came from the same literal father, or it could mean other family members who came from a common ancestor. Use the same words or phrases for sons and for brothers, or relatives, as you have used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 26:1–19
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 26:1–19 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:1–19 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

King David is getting everything ready for the temple that his son Solomon will build. Even though people had not built the temple yet, David wanted the people to be well-organized and prepared. Only Levites, the descendants of Jacob's son Levi, could work in the temple. The Levites had three main jobs: some were priests, some were musicians, and some were gatekeepers or temple guards. In the previous passage, we heard about how King David organized the musicians. Now, we learn more about the gatekeepers.

The gatekeepers had a very important job. The gatekeepers had to guard and take care of the temple. The gatekeepers' main duty was to guard the gates or doors to make sure everything stayed in order and that only the right people came in. The gatekeepers also opened and closed the temple each day, protected it at night, took care of supplies, and helped to get everything ready for worship. Basically, the gatekeepers made sure the temple stayed clean, safe, and ready for worship every day.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, who watches over special or important places? Is it religious leaders? Guards? Family members? Pause this audio here.

The temple had gates on all four sides: north, south, west, and east.

Stop here and look at a picture of a temple gate as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look also at a picture or pictures of the temple as a group. See the four sides of the temple with gates or entrances on each side. Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller describes how the Israelites divided the gatekeepers into groups. There were three main families of gatekeepers: the first was Meshelemiah and his sons, the second was Obed-Edom and his sons, and the third was Hosiah and his family members. Meshelemiah and Obed-Edom were part of the Korah family, also called the Korahites. Korah was from Kohath, one of the sons of Levi.

The storyteller gives us a list of leaders of the gatekeepers from each family.

First, the storyteller talks about Meshelemiah's family. Meshelemiah was the son of Kore, and Kore came from a man named Asaph.

Now, this is a little confusing. In a previous passage, we heard that Asaph was a temple musician, and from a different family line, the family of Gershon, not Kohath. The Asaph here is not the same person as the temple musician.

Meshelemiah had seven sons. In order of their age, oldest first, the sons are Zechariah, Jediael, Zebadiah, Jathniel, Elam, Jehohanan, and Eliehoenai.

Next, the storyteller talks about Obed-Edom's family. You might remember Obed-Edom from an earlier story. Obed-Edom looked after the ark of the covenant, the special box that showed God's presence, for three months. And during that time, God blessed or showed favour to Obed-Edom's family. Obed-Edom had eight sons. The oldest son was Shemaiah, then there were Jehozabad, Joah, Sacar, Nethanel, Ammiel, Issachar, and Peullethai. The storyteller says that God "richly" or greatly blessed or favoured Obed-Edom with many sons. In those days, if someone had many sons, people saw this as a sign of Yahweh's blessing.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what is a sign that God has blessed a person or some people? What do people think about a family that has many sons? What are other signs of blessing in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Now, the storyteller mentions Shemaiah, the oldest son of Obed-Edom. Shemaiah had sons, too. Shemaiah's sons were strong and capable men, and they were leaders in their "father's house." In other words, the sons were well-respected leaders within their families.

These sons' names were Othni, Rephael, Obed, and Elzabad. The storyteller also names two other people, Elihu and Semakiah, who were especially strong and capable men. We are not sure if these were Shemaiah's sons or maybe his cousins.

Altogether, Obed-Edom had 62 family members who served as gatekeepers. All the "sons" or descendants of Obed-Edom were very capable men and had the strength and power to do the work. The storyteller may have meant that these men were physically strong enough to work as guards of the temple, or the storyteller may have meant that these men were wise and able leaders.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What are the signs in your culture that someone is strong, not just physically, but in character or spirit? Tell stories about a man who was an able and well-respected leader within your community. What did that man do that made him so great? Pause this audio here.

There were 18 men from Meshelemiah's family altogether. These men were also very strong and capable men.

Then we hear about Hosah, who was from a different family, the family of Merari, another son of Levi. Hosah had four sons: Shimri, Hilkiah, Tabaliah, and Zechariah. Usually, the oldest son became the leader. But here, Hosah chose Shimri, even though Shimri was not the oldest. The storyteller does not tell us why. Maybe the oldest had died or was not a good leader. Hosah's whole group had 13 people who served as gatekeepers. So while Hosah only had four sons who served as gatekeepers, there were another nine of Hosah's relatives who served as gatekeepers.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what do different sons in the family do? What responsibilities does the oldest son usually have? Pause this audio here.

All the gatekeepers were Levites, and they all had duties in the temple, just as their "brothers," or other Levites, also served in the "house of Yahweh," or the temple, in other ways.

So how did the people decide who guarded which gate? The Levites used a method of casting sacred lots. This was a common way to let Yahweh make the decision. We do not know exactly what the Levites used, but the "lots" could be stones, sticks, or pieces of wood with names that people had written on them. The people might put the items in a container, and then pick one. The idea was simple: Yahweh would guide the result. This was a fair way to organise the work among the families of Levite gatekeepers. Everyone had an equal chance, "small and great alike," so things like their age, status, or experience did not matter.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your community decide who does what job? Is it by age, wisdom, family line, or does someone make the decision? How do you make these decisions fairly? Pause this audio here.

Now, let's look at which jobs the Levites had.

The Levites cast lots to decide which gate each group would guard. The lot for the temple's east gate "fell to" Meshelemiah and his group. In other words, the result of the lot showed that Yahweh had chosen Meshelemiah and his group to guard the east gate. The east gate was the most important gate because it was the main way into the temple. Here, the storyteller calls Meshelemiah "Shelemiah," a short form of Meshelemiah.

The Levites also cast lots for Zechariah, the son of Meshelemiah, who was a wise counselor or advisor. The lot showed that Zechariah's group was the one Yahweh chose to guard the north gate. So that became their job.

The lot showed that Yahweh had chosen Obed-Edom to guard the south gate. Obed-Edom's sons got the lot to guard the storerooms or storehouses. We do not know exactly what these buildings were or what the Israelites used them for, but they were probably part of the temple treasury. So they were places where the Levites kept money, other valuable things and supplies.

Then we hear about Hosah and someone named Shuppim who had to guard the west gate. But there is a problem here. We have not heard of Shuppim before! Some Bible experts think this might be a mistake. The name "Shuppim" looks and sounds a lot like the word for Praetorium"storehouses" in the original language. So it is possible that the storyteller meant to say that Hosah was supposed to guard the west gate and take care of the storehouses.

There is something else a bit tricky here. The storyteller says that the lot showed that Yahweh chose Hosah and Shuppim to guard "the gate of Shalleketh on the road that goes up." It is not clear exactly what this means. The gate of Shalleketh could be another name for the west gate, or it could be a second gate on the west side.

The "road that goes up" was a steep road that people had to walk on to reach a place that was higher on the land. This road led "up" to some important place, maybe the king's palace or the temple.

Stop here and look at the picture of the temple as a group again. Look at the four sides of the temple and remind yourselves who was the leader of each of the sides. Pause this audio here.

Next, the storyteller talks about how the guards worked together. We are not sure exactly how the gatekeepers organized themselves. They may have worked next to each other, or the groups may have taken turns one after another to guard the gates. So one group would finish their time, and the next group would begin. The important point in this part of the story is that King David carefully organized the gatekeepers. King David wanted everyone to do everything for the temple with order, care, and respect, even the way the guards worked together.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time people had to take turns to guard something important, like animals, crops, or someone's house. How did the people do this in a careful way? Pause this audio here.

Here is how King David arranged the guard duty. Each day, six Levites stood at the east gate, four Levites watched the north gate, and four more Levites stood at the southern gate. There were also two pairs of Levite guards who watched over the storehouses, where all the valuable things were. On the west side of the temple, six Levites guarded the area. Four of those Levites on the west side were on the road that led up to the temple, and two stood near a special area near the west side of the temple, which may have been a big porch with pillars. A porch is an area with a roof at the front or side of a building, not inside and not fully outside. Sometimes a porch has strong poles to hold up the roof. What is clear is that David cared about the porch. David made sure that the porch had guards.

Stop here and look at a picture of a porch with pillars as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller ends this passage with a summary that these Levite families, descendants of Korah and Merari, were the gatekeepers of the temple.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces the Levite families who served as gatekeepers. The storyteller names the Levite leaders and sons and tells us that all of these leaders were strong and capable men.

In the second scene: The leaders assign or give each group of gatekeepers their jobs. The leaders divide the men and place the men at different gates of the temple: north, south, east, and west. The gatekeepers also guard the storehouses and some other important places.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Meshelemiah and his family. Meshelemiah's sons were:
  1. Zechariah
  2. Jediael
  3. Zebadiah
  4. Jathniel
  5. Elam
  6. Jehohanan
  7. Eliehoenai
  • Obed-Edom and his family. Obed-Edom's sons were:
  1. Shemaiah. Shemaiah's sons were:
  • Othni
  • Rephael
  • Obed
  • Elzabad

Obed-Edom's next sons were:

  1. Jehozabad
  2. Joah
  3. Sacar
  4. Nethanel
  5. ^ ^Ammiel
  6. Issachar
  7. Peullethai

Obed-Edom also had two other family members:

  • Elihu
  • Semakiah

  • Hosah and his family
  1. Shimri, who was the leader among his brothers, although Shimri was not the oldest
  2. Hilkiah
  3. Tebaliah
  4. Zechariah
  • Some translations include Shuppim
  • Other Levites who would have helped cast lots and assigned the gates
  • And Yahweh. The storyteller does not mention Yahweh, but Yahweh is present because Yahweh chooses the outcome of the lots.

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

As background to the first scene, King David is preparing for the work in the temple. The action likely takes place in the area where people will build the temple, in Jerusalem. While the storyteller does not mention David in this part, we know that David specially chooses certain families to guard the temple gates or entrances as gatekeepers.

Stop here and discuss what words or phrases you will use to translate "gatekeepers." Pause this audio here.

These men are Levites or descendants of Levi. David has chosen these men because they are strong and capable. These men are well-respected and trustworthy men. We hear a list of the sons of the three main families—that is, Meshelemiah's family, Obed-Edom's family, and Hosah's family. Meshelemiah and Obed-Edom both descended from Levi's son Kohath, through Kore, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah. Hosah descended from Levi's son Merari.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Listen to the passage again and then use items like small pebbles and sticks to represent and understand this family tree. Use pebbles to represent each person and sticks to show the family line or connection. Consider using a larger pebble for Levi, and then smaller pebbles for Levi's sons. Do the same for the other descendants until you get to the characters in this passage. Pause this audio here.

Meshelemiah has 18 men in his family who will work as gatekeepers and are all strong and capable.

Obed-Edom has 62 men altogether. Yahweh has blessed or favoured Obed-Edom with a large family. Obed-Edom has eight sons. Obed-Edom's oldest son, Shemiah, has four sons, who are all very able. They are also well-respected leaders within their father's "house," that is, within their family. The storyteller also mentions two other strong and capable men from Obed-Edom's family. All the men from Obed-Edom's family are very strong and capable. They are well-suited for this work.

Hosah has 13 men in his family who will work as gatekeepers.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember how, in the last script, you discussed and told stories about a man who was a strong leader in your community. What words or phrases did you use to describe that man? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Collect a bag of pebbles, beans, or other small objects. Place larger pebbles as the three men who are the leaders of their families: Meshelemiah, Obed-Edom, and Hosah. Place smaller pebbles beneath each of the three larger pebbles to represent the family members within each of the three families. Notice how much longer Obed-Edom's family is than the others. Pause this audio here.

The second scene also likely takes place in the area where people will build the temple. Now, the Levites are "casting lots" to decide in a fair way which family will guard which gate. Both the old and the young, the more experienced and the less experienced, all have the same possibility to guard each gate.

It is important to remember that this method does not use magic, luck, or chance. It is Yahweh who decides the outcome.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Choose an object to represent each of the different tasks that gatekeepers and their helpers would have done, like to guard each of the gates, let the right people in, keep others out, and so on. The objects should be similar, like different colored rocks or pieces of paper. Put all the objects into a container. Ask each person to take turns "casting lots" and take an object from the container. Then ask each person which task their object represents, and talk about what that job was and why it was important. Notice how everyone, the youngest and the oldest, all got a fair chance to have each task. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about the way that someone chooses between people in a fair way, no matter how different the people are? Discuss how you want to translate the way that the storyteller says that the Levites cast lots in a fair way. Pause this audio here.

The result of this method shows which family will guard which gate. Through casting lots, Yahweh decides that Meshelemiah will guard the most important east gate. In the same way, Yahweh decides that Meshelemiah's oldest son Zechariah will guard the north gate. Yahweh decides that Obed-Edom will guard the south gate, and Obed-Edom's sons will guard the storehouses.

Yahweh decides that Hosah and someone called Shuppim will guard the west side of the temple and a special place called the "gate of Shalleketh," which may have been a place where people threw out rubbish. This gate is near a road or path that goes "up" towards a place higher on the land, maybe to the king's palace or up to the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss how you are going to translate the "gate of Shalleketh." In your culture, do you have a way to talk about an area that people use for waste or rubbish? You can use this to help your discussion. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate "the road that goes up." Also, discuss how you will talk about the east, west, north, and south sides of the temple. Remind each other of what you discussed earlier in the book when you talked about the four sides of the temple. Pause this audio here.

The guards work in teams in an organized way. The groups may take turns to guard their area, one after the other.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Lay out pebbles in a line to represent the guards. Use a stick to represent the gatekeeping duty. Show how one pebble, or guard, passes the stick to the next. This is how they worked, one group after the other, taking care of the temple gates. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think of your discussion on the previous script about a time people had to take turns to guard something. What words or phrases did you use to talk about this? Pause this audio here.

King David arranges the structure of the work. Every day, there will be six Levites standing at the east gate, four at the north gate, and four at the south gate. There will also be two pairs of Levite guards to watch over the storehouses. On the west side of the temple, there will be six Levite guards. Four of the guards will be on the road that leads up to the temple, and two will stand near the special porch area.

Stop here and, as a group, look again at the picture of a porch with pillars. Do buildings have something similar to this in your culture? How will you translate "porch?" Pause this audio here.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Draw a simple square on the ground or on paper to represent the temple. Use objects such as stones or sticks to mark out the four different directions: north, south, east, and west. You can use two sticks with a leaf to represent the special porch area on the west side, and you can mark out a road or path that goes "up" to a higher point of land. Let volunteers stand as the leaders of the gatekeepers who guard the four sides of the temple. Pause this audio here.

Finally, the storyteller summarises that the families of Korah and Merari divide their gatekeepers in this way.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Meshelemiah and his family. Meshelemiah's sons were:
  1. Zechariah
  2. Jediael
  3. Zebadiah
  4. Jathniel
  5. Elam
  6. Jehohanan
  7. Eliehoenai
  • Obed-Edom and his family. Obed-Edom's sons were:
  1. Shemaiah. Shemaiah's sons were:
  • Othni
  • Rephael
  • Obed
  • Elzabad

Obed-Edom's next sons were:

  1. Jehozabad
  2. Joah
  3. Sacar
  4. Nethanel
  5. ^ ^Ammiel
  6. Issachar
  7. Peullethai

Obed-Edom also had 2 other family members:

  • Elihu
  • Semakiah

  • Hosah and his family
  1. Shimri, who was the leader among his brothers, although Shimri was not the oldest
  2. Hilkiah
  3. Tebaliah
  4. Zechariah
  • Some translations include Shuppim
  • Other Levites who would have helped cast lots and assigned the gates
  • And Yahweh. The storyteller does not mention Yahweh, but Yahweh is present because Yahweh chooses the outcome of the lots.

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The storyteller introduces the three main families whom King David has chosen to be gatekeepers. First, we hear of Meshelemiah and his sons. Meshelemiah had seven sons. Next, we hear about Obed-Edom and his eight sons. The storyteller tells us that Yahweh had greatly blessed or favoured Obed-Edom with many sons. We now hear that Obed-Edom's oldest son Shemiah himself had four sons who were strong and capable men and who were well-respected leaders within their families. There were also two other men within Obed-Edom's family who were very capable men. In total, Obed-Edom had 62 descendants, and all of them were very capable and well-suited for their work.

The storyteller adds that Meshelemiah had 18 sons and relatives, and that they also were very strong and capable men.

Both Meshelemiah and Obed-Edom descended from Levi's son Korah. Meshelemiah's father was Kore, and his grandfather was Ebiasaph.

Now, we hear about Hosah, who descended from another of Levi's sons, Merari. Hosah had 13 sons. The leader among Hosah's sons is called Shimri. Usually the oldest son is the leader, but in this case, Hosah had chosen Shimri as the leader, although Shimri was not the oldest.

As the other Levites served at the temple in other ways, these Levites had to serve as the gatekeepers or guards of the gates or entrances to the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must make sure that people protect the temple. I must choose strong, trustworthy men to guard it carefully; Or
  • I must organize everything here properly before I die so that my son Solomon can take over and future generations can follow; or
  • I feel hopeful that the temple will be a place where Yahweh's people can meet with Yahweh for generations to come; Or
  • I feel pleased that Yahweh has brought me such excellent and trustworthy men to serve in Yahweh's temple.

Ask the person playing the gatekeepers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am greatly honoured that King David has chosen me and my family to be gatekeepers. It is such an important role, and I feel grateful and humble that the king has chosen me; or
  • At the same time, this is a great responsibility! It is very important that we guard the gates of the temple well. I must be sure that my sons and grandsons do the job properly; or
  • I feel proud of my family, of my strength, and my good reputation.

Continue the drama.

The gatekeepers "cast lots" to decide which group would guard which gate. Everyone had an equal chance. In this way, Yahweh showed that Meshelemiah would guard the east gate, Meshelemiah's wise son Zechariah would guard the north gate, Obed-Edom would guard the south gate, and Hosah would guard the west gate.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We must be fair. When they cast lots for the gates, Yahweh must choose, not me*.* I want to be just; or
  • I feel that I trust Yahweh completely. This decision is for Yahweh alone to make and I have complete trust in Yahweh to make the right choices.

Ask the person or people playing the gatekeepers "What are you thinking or feeling?" The person might answer things like,

  • This is a huge honour! King David is giving me a duty to guard the temple! I am feeling proud and excited about what part I will get; or
  • I feel humbled. I realise that Yahweh did not choose me because of my skills, but by Yahweh's will. It is not about being the strongest or oldest, but about being willing; or
  • I feel eager to serve Yahweh. I am ready to take on whatever responsibility the lot decides.

Continue the drama.

The groups of guards take turns in an organised way to guard the gates. King David planned that every day, six Levites would stand at the east gate, four at the north gate, and four at the south gate. There would also be two pairs of Levite guards to watch over the storehouses. On the west side of the temple, there would be six Levite guards. Four of the guards would be on the road that leads up to the temple, and two would stand near the special porch area.

This is the way King David organised the temple gatekeepers, all of them descendants of Korah and Merari.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased and joyful. I gave David the vision for the temple. It pleases me to see David and my people obediently preparing for it and honouring my instructions, which brings me joy; or
  • I feel proud when I see these strong, capable, trustworthy men guarding my special temple; or
  • I deeply care for my people. I want to protect the temple, but I also want to protect my people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Gatekeepers will guard the temple, or house of Yahweh. For more information on the temple or "house of Yahweh," refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases you have used for gatekeeper and temple, or "house of Yahweh," in previous passages and use the same word or phrase throughout this passage.

The storyteller lists the names of the gatekeepers and their sons. In this passage, the storyteller usually uses this word to talk about a real son of someone. However, when the storyteller makes a summary statement about all the men from a family, he uses "sons" to mean a man's grandsons, descendants, or other relatives. The storyteller also talks about a man's "brothers," which can sometimes mean relatives. For more information on descendants and sons, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for sons or descendants, as well as brothers or relatives, as you used in previous passages.

Two of the leaders or chiefs of the gatekeepers were descended from Korah, Meshelemiah, and Obed-Edom. Meshelemiah has seven sons, and Obed Edom has eight sons. The storyteller says that God has blessed Obed-Edom greatly. For more information on God and blessed, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for leaders, God, and blessed as you used in previous passages.

Shemaiah's sons are leaders within their father's house, which is another way to say family. For more information on "house," refer to household in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for house as you used in previous passages.

This passage uses the personal name for God, Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

The men in this passage are Levites, or descendants of Levi. For more information on Levites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Levites as you used in previous passages.

People chose the different duties of the leaders of the gatekeepers by casting lots. Use the same word or phrase for casting lots as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 26:20–28
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 26:20–28 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:20–28 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The previous story was about the gatekeepers who would guard the temple. Remember that David chooses these Levites to prepare for the temple, which David's son will one day build. Now this story is about the Levites who take care of the temple storerooms.

The Levites who are in charge of the storerooms in this story are relatives of the Levites from the last story. The storerooms or treasuries are rooms where people store useful and valuable things for God's house. The house of God can mean either the tabernacle or the temple, which are both places where people worship God. At this time, these Levites probably work in the tabernacle's storerooms, and one day will work in the temple's storerooms. The storerooms of the house of God probably have the vessels and utensils that priests use. These rooms probably also have food items such as flour, oil, and wine. The storerooms for dedicated things probably have gold, silver, and other valuable items that people dedicate or set aside as a special gift to God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kinds of gifts do people give or dedicate to God or to other gods? Why do people do this? Who takes care of these gifts? Where do people keep these gifts? Pause this audio here.

Two groups of Levites take care of the storerooms: one group from the family line of Gershon and the other group from the family line of Kohath. Both Gershon and Kohath were Levi's sons. Gershon had a descendant named Ladan, and leaders of Ladan's descendants are in charge of the storerooms of Yahweh. Jehieli is one of these leaders. Jehieli is the father of Zetham and Joel, who are also leaders. These descendants of Ladan are in charge of the storerooms in the house of Yahweh.

Then the storyteller introduces the Kohathite clans: the Amramites, Izharites, Hebronites, and Uzzielites. Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel were sons of Kohath. Members of the Amramite clan also work in the storerooms.

Shubael is from the Amram clan. Shubael is a descendant of Gershom, Moses's son. Shubael is also an official in charge of the storerooms. Shubael probably works with Ladan's descendants, as well as a man named Shelomith and Shelomith's relatives. Shelomith is a descendant of Eliezer, who is also from the Amram clan. Eliezer was the brother of Gershom, Shubael's ancestor. Eliezer was the father of Rehabiah, who was the father of Jeshaiah, who was the father of Joram. Joram was the father of Zikri, who was the father of Shelomith. So Shelomith and his relatives, Shubael, and leaders of Ladan's descendants take care of the storerooms.

In the storerooms are all the special gifts that people dedicate to God. King David and leaders of the Israelite families have dedicated gifts to God. These family leaders are also military leaders who are in charge of groups of soldiers, from 100 men to 1,000 men. Some family leaders are other kinds of military leaders.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, who leads family groups or clans? What are the armies or military groups like? Who is in charge of the soldiers? Pause this audio here.

Some of the gifts are plunder or things that people took from their enemies in war. People dedicate these things for the Levites to eventually use to take care of the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people maintain or take care of places of worship? Who provides the resources or money to do this? Pause this audio here.

Shelomith and his relatives are also in charge of the gifts which the prophet Samuel, King Saul, Abner, and Joab brought. You will remember that Samuel was Yahweh's prophet and that Saul was king before David. Abner was Saul's army commander, and Joab is David's army commander. Samuel, Saul, Abner, and probably Joab all die before people build the temple. These men may have given these gifts for the tabernacle.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller explains which Levites are in charge of the storerooms. First, the storyteller talks about the descendants of Gershon.

In the second scene: The storyteller then talks about the descendants of Kohath who work in the storerooms.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Gershon's descendant Ladan
  • Ladan's descendant Jehieli
  • Zetham and Joel
  • The Kohathite clans of Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel
  • The men from the Amram clan, which includes Moses's sons Gershom and Eliezer; Gershom's descendant Shubael; Eliezer's descendants: Rehabiah, Jeshaiah, Joram, Zikri and Shelomith; and Shelomith's relatives
  • King David
  • Leaders of the Israelite families, who are also military leaders
  • The prophet Samuel
  • King Saul, Kish's son
  • Abner, Ner's son
  • And Joab, Zeruiah's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the original language, we are not sure what the storyteller says at the beginning of the first scene. There are two main translations. Some translations begin this story with "Their Levite relatives are in charge of the storerooms." Other translations say that "Ahijah the Levite is in charge of the storerooms." We are not sure which of these the storyteller actually said because the word for brother or relative sounds very similar to the name Ahijah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What version of this passage have you heard? What version would people in your area be familiar with or understand better? Discuss how you want to translate this portion. We suggest that you follow the accepted translation in your area. Pause this audio here.

These storerooms are for the house of God or "Yahweh's house," which is another name for the temple. Remember that people have not built the temple yet, but people have already begun to give and store things for the temple. The Levites may already use some of these things in the tabernacle, which is the place where people worship God.

Some of Gershon's descendants and some of Kohath's descendants will take care of the storerooms. First, the storyteller talks about Gershon's descendants. The wording in this part of the story is a bit difficult to understand. You may find that translations say it in different ways. Gershon had a descendant named Ladan. You may remember that in 1 Chronicles 6:17, some translations call this man "Libni." To make your translation easier to understand, you may decide to use the same name in both places.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Which version of this name have you heard? Discuss if you will use the same name for Ladan in both passages. If so, which name will you choose? Pause this audio here.

Some of Ladan's descendants are leaders of family groups. Jehieli is one of these leaders. You may remember that in 1 Chronicles 23:8, some translations call this man "Jehili." To make your translation easier to understand, you may decide to use the same name in both places.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Which version of this name have you heard? Discuss if you will use the same name for Jehieli in both passages. If so, which name will you choose? Pause this audio here.

Zetham and Joel are also leaders. Here, the storyteller calls Zetham and Joel "Jehieli's sons." Some translations call Zetham and Joel "Jehieli's brothers." These translations say this because in 1 Chronicles 23:8, the storyteller refers not only to Jehieli, but also to Zetham and Joel, as "sons" of Ladan. However, "sons" is another word for descendants, so Zetham and Joel are probably Jehieli's sons. Jehieli, Zetham, and Joel are some of the men in charge of the storerooms.

Then the storyteller talks about the men from the Kohathite clans who work with Jehieli, Zetham, and Joel. The storyteller lists four clans: the Amramites, Izharites, Hebronites, and Uzzielites. We know from other passages that these are Kohathite clans. The storyteller then begins to talk about Shubael. Though the storyteller does not say it here, we know from an earlier passage that Shubael is from the Kohathite clan of Amram. The storyteller talks about the clan of Amram in this passage and will talk about the other clans in later stories.

Shubael is a descendant of Gershom, Moses's son. Shubael works with Shelomith, a descendant of Eliezer, Moses's other son. So Shelomith is Shubael's relative through Eliezer. The storyteller includes Shelomith's family line, which is Shelomith's ancestors in order from father to son. First Eliezer, then Rehabiah, then Jeshaiah, then Joram, and then Zicri, who is Shelomith's father.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: As an activity, tell the others in the group the names of your ancestors from several generations. Start with one man, like your great-great-grandfather, and then tell this man's descendants until you reach your own name. Pay attention to the way you show how one man is the next man's father. Then discuss how you want to translate Shelomith's family line. Pause this audio here.

You may notice that in this passage and others, the storyteller sometimes calls Eliezer's descendant "Shelomoth" and sometimes "Shelomith." To make your translation easier to understand, you may decide to use the same name in both places.

Stop here as a group and discuss this question as a group: Which version of this name have you heard? Discuss if you will use the same name for Shelomith in both passages. If so, which name will you choose? Pause this audio here.

Shubael, Shelomith, and Shelomith's relatives are in charge of the storerooms, too. There may have been different rooms that each man was in charge of, or perhaps the men all worked together as a team to lead other Levites. Many people gave or dedicated gifts to Yahweh. The Levites keep these gifts in the storerooms until the Levites use the gifts to serve Yahweh. King David and leaders of clans or family groups gave some gifts. These family group leaders are also military leaders. Some of the gifts that the king and the leaders gave were plunder or things they took from the enemies they defeated.

Shelomith and his relatives are also in charge of the gifts that Samuel, Saul, Abner, and Joab gave. Samuel was a seer. A seer is a prophet of God. God would often tell Samuel about things that would happen in the future.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call a person who has dreams or visions from God about the future? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller includes Saul's father's name, Kish, to remind us that this Saul is the man who was king before David. The storyteller also includes Abner's father's name, Ner, and Joab's mother's name, Zeruiah.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Gershon's descendant Ladan
  • Ladan's descendant Jehieli
  • Zetham and Joel
  • The Kohathite clans of Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel
  • The men from the Amram clan, which includes Moses's sons Gershom and Eliezer; Gershom's descendant Shubael; Eliezer's descendants: Rehabiah, Jeshaiah, Joram, Zikri, and Shelomith; and Shelomith's relatives
  • King David
  • Leaders of the Israelite families, who are also military leaders
  • The prophet Samuel
  • King Saul, Kish's son
  • Abner, Ner's son
  • And Joab, Zeruiah's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Other men from the tribe of Levi were in charge of the temple storerooms. These men also supervised the storerooms for the valuable things that people gave as gifts to Yahweh.

Some of these Levites were heads of different families who all belonged to the one big family of Ladan. Ladan was from the clan of Gershon. Jehieli was the leader of one of Ladan's family groups. Jehieli was the father of Zetham and Joel, who were also leaders. These descendants of Ladan were in charge of the storerooms in the temple of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jehieli, Zetham, and Joel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel honored that we help look after the storerooms in the house of Yahweh; or
  • This is a big responsibility. We are glad that other men help us with this job; or
  • We like that we can work with our relatives!

Continue the drama.

Members of the Kohathite clans of Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel had responsibilities, too.

Shubael, a descendant of Moses' son Gershom, was the official in charge of the storehouses. A descendant of Moses' son Eliezer also worked in the storehouses. This man's name was Shelomoth. Eliezer's son was Rehabiah. Rehabiah's son was Jeshaiah. Jeshaiah's son was Joram. Joram's son was Zikri. Zikri's son was Shelomith. Shelomith and his relatives were in charge of all the storerooms for the special gifts.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shubael and Shelomith, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to honor Yahweh with the way I work! or
  • I am amazed at the way that people honor Yahweh with all these beautiful gifts; or
  • I am thankful that I have a good and important job.

Continue the drama.

King David had dedicated gifts to Yahweh. The leaders of families also dedicated gifts. These leaders were commanders of troops of thousands or hundreds of fighting men, or were other army commanders. These leaders took things from their enemies in war and dedicated some of those things for people to use to repair the house of Yahweh.

Shelomith and his relatives were in charge of everything that people dedicated to Yahweh. This included the gifts which the prophet Samuel, Kish's son King Saul, Ner's son Abner, and Zeruiah's son Joab brought.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing all the people who dedicated gifts to Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am thankful for all the things that Yahweh has done for me! I want to show everyone that I am thankful to Yahweh; or
  • I love Yahweh and want to serve Yahweh. If I dedicate gifts, the priests can lead the people to worship Yahweh in the right way; or
  • Yahweh is my God and is powerful. I want to honor Yahweh with these gifts.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased that these people have dedicated gifts to me; or
  • I love that the Levites work in a way that honors me; or
  • I am happy that David and the Levites are organized and take care of the good supplies and gifts.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Some Levites take care of the storerooms. For more information on Levites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Levites as you used in previous passages.

The storerooms or treasuries for the tabernacle, and later for the temple, are buildings or rooms where people store useful and valuable things for the tabernacle or the temple. Use the same word or phrase for storerooms or treasuries as you used in previous passages.

These are the storerooms of the house of God or the house of Yahweh. Remember that God is a more general name for God, while Yahweh is the personal name for God. The house of God is a way to talk about the place on earth where God lived among his people. People sometimes called both the tabernacle and the temple the "house of God." These storerooms were probably the tabernacle's storerooms at the time of the story. When people build the temple, these same Levites will work in the storerooms for the temple. So some translations say the "temple of Yahweh" instead of the house of Yahweh. For more information on house of God, refer to "house of God" and temple in the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for house of God or Yahweh, and for temple, as you used in previous passages.

In the storerooms are gifts that people dedicated or consecrated. People could consecrate an object to do a special task for God. These objects were now holy and belonged to God. People could not use these objects for ordinary use. For more information on consecrate and holy, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for consecrated as you used in previous passages.

Some of Ladan's descendants are in charge of the storerooms. A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. For more information on descendants, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for descendants as you used in previous passages.

Some of Ladan's descendants are leaders of family groups or clans. Use the same words or phrases for clans and leaders of family groups as you used in previous passages.

King David dedicated gifts for the temple. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

The leaders of the family groups are also military commanders. Some of these leaders are in charge of groups of hundreds or thousands of men. Some of the leaders are other kinds of military leaders. Use the same words or phrases for these kinds of military leaders as you used in previous passages.

King David and the military leaders dedicate some of their plunder. Plunder is anything that a winning army takes from the army they defeated after the battle. Use the same word or phrase for plunder as you used in previous passages.

Samuel was a seer. A seer is a prophet. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people. For more information on seer, refer to prophet in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for seer as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 26:29–32
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 26:29–32 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:29–32 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:29–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The previous passage was about the Levites who worked in the storerooms. Those Levites were descendants of Gershon and of Kohath's son Amram. This passage is about the descendants of Kohath's other sons, Izhar and Hebron.

This passage begins with Kenaniah and his sons, who are from the Izhar clan. Kenaniah and his sons do not work at the temple or "house of God." Instead, Kenaniah and his sons work throughout Israel as officials and judges. It is not clear exactly what these men's duties are, but they probably help with legal problems.

Then there are two groups from the clan of Hebron who also work throughout Israel. Hashabiah and 1,700 Hebronites are in the first group. These Hebronites are skilled workers and are in charge of religious and government business throughout Israel on the west side of the Jordan River. The Hebronites might make sure that people follow the king's laws and God's rules. The Hebronites might also collect taxes for both the king and the temple.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the part of Israel that is on the west side of the Jordan River. Pause this audio here.

Jeriah leads the other group from the clan of Hebron. During David's 40th year as king, someone looked in the genealogical records, or the records of the Israelites' families. This person found that many of Hebron's descendants were strong and good leaders. There are 2,700 of these leaders who live in the town of Jazer in the region of Gilead. Gilead is on the east side of the Jordan River. Jeriah leads these 2,700 men. King David chooses Jeriah and these 2,700 other Hebronites to supervise the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and East Manasseh. The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and East Manasseh live on the east side of the Jordan River. So Jeriah and his fellow Hebronites are in charge of religious and government business throughout the part of Israel that is on the east side of the Jordan River. Jeriah and his fellow Hebronites do the same work that Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites do.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the territories of Ruben, Gad, and East Manasseh on the east side of the Jordan River. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What people are in charge of government business in different regions in your area? What people are in charge of religious business in different regions in your area? Who chooses these people?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:29–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller explains what a group of Levite men from the Izhar clan does.

In the second scene: The storyteller explains what one group of Levite men from the Hebron clan does. These men work throughout Israel on the west side of the Jordan River.

In the third scene: The storyteller explains what a second group of Levite men from the Hebron clan does. These men work throughout Israel on the east side of the Jordan River.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Kenaniah and his sons
  • Hashabiah and the 1,700 skilled workers
  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • Jeriah and the 2,700 leaders from Jazer
  • And the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and East Manasseh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that in the last section, the storyteller introduced the four clans whose ancestors are the four sons of Kohath. Then the storyteller talked about men from the first clan, the Amramites. In this passage, the storyteller talks about men from the Izharite clan and the Hebron clan. The storyteller does not talk about the men from the last clan, the Uzzielite clan, here.

In the first scene, the storyteller talks about Kenaniah and his sons, who are from the Izharite clan. King David assigns Kenaniah and his sons to work outside or away from the temple. Kenaniah and his sons work as officers and judges. Here, officers can mean either military officers or government officers. And here, judges are people who make decisions on legal problems. Kenaniah and his sons work all over Israel.

In the second scene, the storyteller talks about a group of men from the Hebronite clan. Hashabiah and 1,700 other Hebronites are strong men. This phrase in the original language can mean men who are physically strong or are skilled and able to do many things. Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites work in the portion of Israel that is west of the Jordan River. Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites work for the king and Yahweh. This probably means that Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites are in charge of both religious and government business.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how would you talk about the way that people take care of both religious and government matters? Discuss how you want to translate the way that Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites work for the king and for Yahweh. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller talks about a second group of men from the Hebronite clan. Jeriah leads this group of men. It is possible that Jeriah leads all the Hebronites. The storyteller pauses his story to explain how King David and other people knew that Jeriah was the leader and why King David chose Jeriah and his men. During David's 40th year as king, someone searched through the genealogical records for the Israelite families. This person found that there are many capable Hebronites at the town of Jazer in Gilead. Jeriah and these 2,700 men are leaders of family groups and also able men. So King David puts Jeriah and these 2,700 men in charge of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and East Manasseh, who live on the east side of the Jordan River. Like Hashabiah, Jeriah and his fellow Hebronties do the work for the king and Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: As an activity, tell a story about a time when you chose a group of people to work on something together. As you tell the story, explain how you learned that this group of people was the right group to do this kind of work. Pay attention to the way that you explain what you learned about the people and when you choose to tell it. Discuss how you want to translate the way the storyteller explains why David chose Jeriah and the other Hebronites. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:29–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Kenaniah and his sons
  • Hashabiah and the 1,700 skilled workers
  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • Jeriah and the 2,700 leaders from Jazer
  • And the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and East Manasseh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.
