The Izharites were another Kohathite clan. Kenaniah and his sons were from the Iszhar clan. Kenaniah and his sons did not work in the temple. These men worked across Israel as officers and judges over the people of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Kenaniah and his sons, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy to have this good job. I like to help the Israelites when they have problems; or
  • I understand that this is an important job and I will try to do it well; or
  • I hope Yahweh helps me make wise decisions.

Continue the drama.

The Hebronties were another Kohathite clan. Hashabiah and his relatives were from the clan of Hebron. There were 1,700 Hebronites who were skilled workers. These Hebronties were in charge of all religious and government business throughout Israel on the west side of the Jordan River. These men served both Yahweh and the king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is a big portion of Israel to look after! I am glad there is a large group of Hebronites who work with me; or
  • I hope the Israelites listen to us! or
  • I want to serve my king and Yahweh well!

Continue the drama.

Jeriah was another of Hebron's descendants. Jeriah was the leader of the Hebron clan. When King David had ruled for 40 years, people looked in the ancestral records of Israel. These people found many descendants of Hebron who were strong and capable leaders. These Hebronites lived in the town of Jazer in the region of Gilead. Jeriah had 2,700 men who were strong and experienced leaders in their clans. King David chose these Hebronite leaders to supervise the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and East Manasseh. These Hebronites served both Yahweh and the king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I was glad to see that there were so many skilled Hebronites! or
  • Since these Hebronites already live on the east side of the Jordan River, I will assign these Hebronites to work there; or
  • I am thankful that we keep good records of our people, so we can know where the leaders of families live.

Ask the person playing Jeriah and his fellow Hebronites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I do not have to move across the river to do my job; or
  • I am thankful that I get to work with people I know; or
  • I hope that I can lead the other Israelites well.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 26:29–32 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Kenaniah and his sons work "outside," or away from the house of God. The storyteller just says that the men work outside, but we know that he means that the men work away from the tabernacle, which later becomes the temple. Some translations just say that they worked away from the temple, or house of God. Use the same word for temple or house of God in this passage as you used in previous passages. For more information on "temple" or "house of God," refer to the Master Glossary.

Kenaniah and his sons work as officials and judges. For more information on "judge," refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for officials and judges as you used in previous passages.

Kenaniah and his sons work all over Israel. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites work throughout the portion of Israel that is west of the Jordan River. The Jordan River is a long river that runs through Israel from north to south. For more information on the Jordan River, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the Jordan River as you used in previous passages.

Hashabiah and his fellow Hebronites serve both the king and Yahweh. For more information on king and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for king and Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

People check the genealogical records or the places where people keep track of people's genealogies. A genealogy is a list of names of people who belong to the same ancestor. A genealogy can begin with one person and then give a list of this person's descendants, or it can begin with one person and give a list of this person's ancestors. For more information on genealogy, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for genealogy as you used in previous passages.

The second group of Hebronites serve both God and the king. This is a more general term for God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 27:1–15
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 27:1–15 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:1–15 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

At the beginning of this passage, the storyteller says that he is writing a list of all the people who have served in King David's army. It is important to remember that everyone in the army was an Israelite, which means they were descendants of Israel. The storyteller includes both commanders and officials, because these are likely two different positions. The storyteller uses "commanders" to refer to the leaders of the army divisions or groups. The storyteller also uses the word "officials" to refer to the men who kept the records for the army and who assisted the commanders. The storyteller has listed each of these commanders in previous passages as part of lists of David's "Thirty" brave and special warriors.

The storyteller organizes the list in a specific way to show the many people who are commanders or leaders and the hundreds and thousands of people who are soldiers in the army. The commanders and soldiers take turns each month. King David may have chosen this system so that commanders and soldiers could rest. King David had a strong army each month because both the commanders and soldiers were ready for battle.

Throughout the list, you can see that the storyteller repeats a consistent pattern. In this pattern, each commander begins to lead at the start of each new month. Each commander has a group of 24,000 soldiers to take the place of tired or killed soldiers. The storyteller also shows that there are leaders for both "hundreds" and "thousands" of soldiers. When the storyteller lists a commander's name, it is likely that this person is in charge of the entire group. These commanders then lead several other leaders who lead smaller groups of soldiers within the 24,000 men.

As the storyteller lists the monthly groups, it is important to remember that the first month does not necessarily mean "January" or whatever month your culture sees first in a calendar or new year. It is likely that the first month is actually March or April. It is helpful to know this because it will help you visualize the months that the storyteller intended for this passage.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you talk about months? Do you have different names for months of the year, or do you tend to use weather or numbers to show how time passes? Pause this audio here.

This passage is a different version of the list that you heard in an earlier passage from 1 Chronicles. This passage is also very similar to a list in 2 Samuel. The storyteller probably includes this list to show the strength of King David's army. This list repeats the names of David's mighty men whom King David trusted. This passage is the beginning of a longer list where the storyteller shows how important King David is and how brave David's appointed workers are.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of as many people as possible that you would trust to keep you safe. Discuss why you would trust these people the most. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller includes the following commanders and the months they served in the list:

  1. Jashobeam, son of Zabdiel, served in the first month.
  2. Dodai the Ahohite served in the second month.
  3. Benaiah, son of Jehoiada the priest, served in the third month. The storyteller reminds us that Benaiah was one of the leaders of David's special fighting force of 30 brave warriors.
  4. Asahel, brother of Joab, served in the fourth month.
  5. Shamhuth the Izrahite served in the fifth month.
  6. Ira, son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, served in the sixth month.
  7. Helez the Pelonite, of the sons of Ephraim, served in the seventh month.
  8. Sibbecai the Hushathite, of the Zerahites, served in the eighth month.
  9. Abiezer of Anathoth, a Benjaminite, served in the ninth month.
  10. Maharai of Netophah, of the Zerahites, served in the tenth month.
  11. Benaiah of Pirathon, of the sons of Ephraim, served in the eleventh month.
  12. Heldai the Netophathite, of Othniel, served in the twelfth month.

For each month, the storyteller changes the commander's name and where he is from. But, it is important to pay attention to the ways the storyteller keeps each month's description the same. Each month, the storyteller says that the commander is in charge of an army division of 24,000 soldiers. This means that King David had 288,000 men in his army in this list.

It is likely that this list represents the army structure during peacetime. This structure allowed soldiers to serve for a month and then return to their homes and jobs until they were needed again. This structure also likely saved a lot of money and resources because the commanders only needed to feed 24,000 soldiers at a time.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you support your family if you had to be away for a month? What responsibilities would you need to consider before you leave? Pause this audio here.

There are a few important people to note in this list. The storyteller lists Jashobeam as the first commander. This may mean that Jashobeam is the chief or top commander of all other commanders and the commander of King David's mighty men. This could also mean that Jashobeam is simply the first commander for the first month.

The storyteller lists two different times when a son takes the place of a father after the father dies. Ammizabad takes the commander position after his father, Benaiah, dies. Similarly, Zebadiah replaces Asahel after Asahel dies. These moments seem to show how important it is to the Israelites to honor each generation.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What expectations or traditions do you have in your culture that require you to continue doing a family member's job or responsibilities after the family member dies? Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember why the storyteller chooses to repeat this list of people. The storyteller likely wants to show how powerful and strong King David's army is and how prepared the army is for future battles. By keeping the army in rotation, each division commander trains and continues the military preparations for future battles. This also means that when war breaks out, each commander already has a group of soldiers to lead.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces a list of King David's commanders for his peacetime military. Each commander served one month at a time.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists the 12 commanders, two replacements, and one additional leader, who take turns each month, for 12 months. Each commander leads 24,000 different men.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller of 1 Chronicles
  • Jashobeam, son of Zabdiel
  • Dodai the Ahohite
  • Mikloth, a leader alongside Dodai
  • Benaiah, son of Jehoiada
  • Ammizabad, son of Benaiah
  • Asahel, brother of Joab
  • Zebadiah, son of Asahel
  • Shamhuth the Izrahite. Shamhuth is either from the city or the family of Izrah.
  • Ira, son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, from the city of Tekoa
  • Helez the Pelonite, from the tribe of Ephraim, from the city or the family of Pelon
  • Sibbecai from the city of Hushah, from the family of Zerah
  • Abiezer from the city of Anathoth, from the tribe of Benjamin
  • Maharai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Zerah
  • Benaiah from the city of Pirathon, from the tribe of Ephraim
  • Heldai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Othniel
  • 288,000 men—24,000 in each of the 12 months

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This passage is the beginning of a longer list of King David's military structure, commanders, workers, administrators, and most trusted leaders.

In the first scene, the storyteller begins this list with an introduction to the list of commanders, the fathers or families of the commanders, the month each commander is in charge, and the number of people the commander is assigned to lead. Each commander leads 24,000 men, and each commander has a specific month when that commander and his 24,000 men serve.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think about a situation in your culture where certain groups of people are responsible for certain activities at certain times of the year. Describe these situations. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to describe the activities, the number of people involved in the activities, and the times of year they do them. Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the word "commander" most likely means the person in charge of the military men. Likewise, the word "officer" most likely means the person who keeps a record of the men and is the person the commander communicates with if King David calls the full military to serve. The "heads of fathers' houses" most likely means the leaders of the clans of the different tribes of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In your culture, what words do you use to show the difference in the levels or types of leadership? Discuss how you can explain King David's levels of leadership clearly. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that each month, 24,000 men are on military duty. Then, for each month, the storyteller repeats this information when the storyteller lists information about each commander. Some translations only give the information about the number of men once in the introduction and then list the commanders by month. 288,000 total men serve each 12-month period, because 24,000 men serve each month.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Discuss how you will keep the passage simple when you list the people and their responsibilities. In your culture and language, is it more helpful to restate information for each month, or is it more helpful to state the information once at the beginning of the passage? Discuss the most natural ways to tell this list as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller lists the 12 commanders in order of the months the commanders served. If you have decided to include the number 24,000 for each commander for each month, it is important to make this clear as you form the list.

Each commander serves one month, and each commander has a new group of 24,000 men to lead. The storyteller lists the following leaders and the months the leaders serve:

First month: Jashobeam, son of Zabdiel.

Second month: Dodai the Ahohite. In other words, Dodai was from the family of Ahoah. Mekloth seems to have been Dodai's chief officer.

Third month: Benaiah, son of Jehoiada the priest. Ammizabad, the son of Benaiah, may have replaced or helped Benaiah.

Fourth month: Asahel, brother of Joab. Zebadiah, the son of Asahel, replaced Asahel.

Fifth month: Shamhuth the Izrahite. Shamhuth is either from the city or the family of Izrah.

Sixth month: Ira, son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, from the city of Tekoa.

Seventh month: Helez the Pelonite, from the tribe of Ephraim, from the city or the family of Pelon.

Eighth month: Sibbecai from the city of Hushah, from the family of Zerah.

Ninth month: Abiezer from the city of Anathoth, from the tribe of Benjamin.

Tenth month: Maharai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Zerah.

Eleventh month: Benaiah from the city of Pirathon, from the tribe of Ephraim.

Twelfth month: Heldai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Othniel.

Stop here and try an activity as a group: Find 12 objects that are the same type of object but that have small differences. For instance, you can use 12 crayons that are different colors. Or 12 rocks that are different colors or shapes. Also, collect three items that each share a color or similar shape with one of the 12 objects.

Each of the 12 objects will represent a month. The three extra items will represent the two men who took over for their fathers, and Mikloth, who helped lead. Make sure these three objects match the three that they are replacing or helping.

Use these objects to help represent the 12 commanders and the months that the commanders served. This will help you start to picture the division of groups. Take turns talking through the order and the details for each month. Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that this list is in other passages, so you may have chosen specific names already for each person. You should take your time as you go through the list and refer to 2 Samuel 23:8–38 to make sure you use the same names consistently.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it. Use the suggestion above and find other ways to help you understand the passage.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 2 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller of 1 Chronicles
  • Jashobeam, son of Zabdiel
  • Dodai the Ahohite
  • Mikloth, a leader alongside Dodai
  • Benaiah, son of Jehoiada
  • Ammizabad, son of Benaiah
  • Asahel, brother of Joab
  • Zebadiah, son of Asahel
  • Shamhuth the Izrahite. Shamhuth is either from the city or the family of Izrah.
  • Ira, son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, from the city of Tekoa
  • Helez the Pelonite, from the tribe of Ephraim, from the city or the family of Pelon
  • Sibbecai from the city of Hushah, from the family of Zerah
  • Abiezer from the city of Anathoth, from the tribe of Benjamin
  • Maharai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Zerah
  • Benaiah from the city of Pirathon, from the tribe of Ephraim
  • Heldai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Othniel
  • 288,000 men—24,000 in each of the 12 months

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The following list shows a record of the number of people in Israel. The list shows how many men serve in King David's military, the commanders for the army, the leaders of Israel's tribes, and the number of people that each person leads.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope I do not forget to include anything! or
  • This is a big responsibility! or
  • I hope King David approves!

Continue the drama.

King David appointed 12 commanders to help lead his military during peacetime. King David assigned one commander per month, and 24,000 new men rotated into King David's service each month. 288,000 total men served in a 12-month period. Twelve original commanders, plus two commanders who took over for their fathers, took turns serving for one month as well.

  • In the first month, Jashobeam, son of Zabdiel, led the men. Jashobeam also served as the head of all the commanders.
  • In the second month, Dodai the Ahohite led the men. Mikloth also helped lead.
  • In the third month, Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, led the men. When Benaiah died, his son, Ammizabad, took his place and led the men.
  • In the fourth month, Asahel, brother of Joab, led the men. When Asahel died, his son, Zebadiah, took his place and led the men.
  • In the fifth month, Shamhuth the Izrahite led the men.
  • In the sixth month, Ira, son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, led the men.
  • In the seventh month, Helez the Pelonite, of the sons of Ephraim, led.
  • In the eighth month, Sibbecai the Hushathite, of the Zerahites, led.
  • In the ninth month, Abiezer of Anathoth, a Benjaminite, led.
  • In the tenth month, Maharai of Netophah, of the Zerahites, led.
  • In the eleventh month, Benaiah of Pirathon, of the sons of Ephraim, led.
  • In the twelfth month, Heldai the Netophathite, of Othniel, led.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ammizabad or Zebadiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad that my dad died; or
  • I must make the men respect me! or
  • I wonder if King David will approve of me! or
  • This is a big responsibility!

Ask the person playing Jashobeam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am honored to serve King David! or
  • I had better get this right! People are looking up to me for help! or
  • I hope I do not disappoint King David!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The storyteller talks about leaders of families, or clans. Each army commander also has officers who help him. Use the same words for leaders of fathers' houses, or clans, and officers, as you have used previously. For more information about fathers' houses, refer to household in the Master Glossary.

The storyteller presents a list of King David's army structure and describes how the army operates. King David divides the army into 12 separate divisions or groups. Each division has 24,000 soldiers. Each division also has a commander who is in charge of all other leaders and soldiers. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for division, army, soldiers, and commander. If you have already translated these words in previous passages, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller lists 12 commanders and two replacement commanders. The two additional commanders took over for their fathers after their fathers died. The 12 original commanders are some of the men whom King David included in his list of "mighty men," also known as "The 30," who fought well for King David's kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for King David's "Mighty Men" or "The 30" as you used in previous passages, especially in 1 Chronicles 11:10–31.

It is important for you to review and discuss as a group the names to make sure you use the same names as you previously chose. Here is the list again to help you compare:

  1. Jashobeam, son of Zabdiel.
  2. Dodai the Ahohite.
  3. Mikloth, a leader alongside Dodai.
  4. Benaiah, son of Jehoiada.
  5. Ammizabad, son of Benaiah.
  6. Asahel, brother of Joab.
  7. Zebadiah, son of Asahel.
  8. Shamhuth the Izrahite. Shamhuth is either from the city or the family of Izrah.
  9. Ira, son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, from the city of Tekoa.
  10. Helez the Pelonite, from the tribe of Ephraim, from the city or the family of Pelon.
  11. Sibbecai from the city of Hushah, from the family of Zerah.
  12. Abiezer from the city of Anathoth, from the tribe of Benjamin.
  13. Maharai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Zerah.
  14. Benaiah from the city of Pirathon, from the tribe of Ephraim.
  15. Heldai from the city of Netophah, from the family of Othniel.

Use the same names for the list of King David's commanders as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 27:16–24
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 27:16–24 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:16–24 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the storyteller explained the system that King David used for his military during peacetime. The people of Israel needed army structures in place because the structures helped keep the military active and ready for future battles.

In this passage, the storyteller shows us that King David records each tribal leader. You will remember that when Moses counted the number of Israelites in the story in Numbers chapter 1, he appointed a tribal leader from each tribe to help him. In a similar way, King David appoints tribal leaders so that the leaders can help King David count the people in each leader's tribe. These tribal leaders may also have communicated with the king and with each military commander serving in each month of the year. The word for leader also meant a prince or ruler for the tribe.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you appoint leaders who help govern people on a regional level? How do these leaders share information with each other so that they can protect and govern the country well? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller lists the following chief officers and their tribe assignments:

  • Eliezer, son of Zicri, came from the tribe of Reuben.
  • Shephatiah, son of Maacah, came from the tribe of Simeon.
  • Hashabiah, son of Kemuel, came from the tribe of Levi
  • and Zadok came from the "tribe of Aaron" in the Levites.
  • Elihu, King David's brother, came from the tribe of Judah.
  • Omri, son of Michael, came from the tribe of Issachar.
  • Ishmaiah, son of Obadiah, came from the tribe of Zebulun.
  • Jerimoth, son of Azriel, came from the tribe of Naphtali.
  • Hoshea, son of Azaziah, came from the tribe of Ephraim.
  • Joel, son of Pedaiah, came from the half-tribe of Manasseh, who lived west of the Jordan River.
  • Iddo, son of Zechariah, came from the half-tribe of Manasseh who lived east of the Jordan River.
  • Jaasiel, son of Abner, came from the tribe of Benjamin.
  • Azarel, son of Jeroham, came from the tribe of Dan.

Stop here and look at a map of the different locations of the tribes as a group. Make sure to pay attention to all of the tribes that the storyteller mentions. Pause this audio here.

The first thing you may notice about this list is that the storyteller does not list two tribes that are usually included. The storyteller leaves out the tribes of Gad and Asher, and there is no real explanation for why the storyteller does this. The storyteller also includes a few new parts to the list of tribes.

The storyteller includes Aaron in the list as a tribe name, even though no other storyteller does this anywhere else in the Old Testament. It is likely that the storyteller includes Aaron as a tribe to represent the priests, who are a smaller part of the Levi tribe, also known as the Levites. Yahweh does not allow the Levites to fight in the military, so the storyteller probably includes them in this list to show that King David also has non-military tasks he wants leaders to do. The storyteller also lists Zadok as the chief officer of "Aaron's tribe," and this makes sense because Zadok is the high priest. It is probably best for you to note that the storyteller likely does not mean to include "Aaron" as a tribe, but the storyteller probably wants to show that the priests form a smaller group in the tribe of Levi and that Zadok is in charge of the group.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think about your culture and the rules you have for certain people. Describe anyone in your culture who leaders do not require to do tasks that most other people have to do. Discuss why this is the case and how the community responds to these rules. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also does something surprising when he divides the tribe of Manasseh into two half tribes. The storyteller chooses to divide the tribe by showing a geographical divide of how the tribe of Manasseh lives on both sides of the Jordan River. King David assigns the part of the tribe of Manasseh that is west of the Jordan River to Joel. King David then assigns the part of the tribe of Manasseh that is east of the Jordan River to Iddo. Again, the storyteller does not give a clear reason for this, but it is important to remember this difference from previous lists.

Stop here and look at a map of the Jordan River and the territory of the two half-tribes of Manasseh as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller seems to present this tribal list without explaining the reason for it. However, at the end of this passage, the storyteller gives us a possible reason for this list. King David asks Joab, son of Zeruiah, to count the Israelite men. It is important to notice that Zeruiah is Joab's mother, because storytellers normally only mention fathers' names. Zeruiah is King David's sister, so it is possible that the storyteller wanted to show the strength of King David's family line, because Zeruiah's sons served as well in King David's military. A person counts the number of people in a specific place by completing a census. A census shows how many people live in a certain region.

Stop and discuss as a group: How do you keep a count of the people in your community, tribe, or town? Discuss the different ways, both formal and informal, that you use in order to know how many people are in your community. Pause this audio here.

Joab does not count anyone under the age of 20 years old. It is likely that King David is trying to find out how many people are available to help defend Israel. King David only wants to count the people who are old enough to serve in the military. In previous passages, the storyteller shows that King David's motives for counting the men are not good. Many years before, Yahweh had promised to make the Israelite people as many people as there were stars in the sky, and although King David only counts the men over 20 years old, he seems to forget this promise. King David likely wants to know the number of Israelite men so that David can feel he has control and is prepared.

Stop and discuss as a group: Perhaps David thinks he is not disobeying Yahweh when he only counts men over 20 years old. Think of a time when you tried to get what you wanted by only partly breaking the rules in your home or community. Share with the group what happened. Pause this audio here.

However, Joab does not want to count the people, and Joab does not finish counting. Joab does not count the tribes of Levi or Benjamin, because Joab does not agree with King David's request. Even though Joab does not complete the census, Yahweh is angry at King David's decision. The storyteller never includes the full number of fighting men in King David's official records, or the book of the history of King David.

Stop and discuss as a group: Think of a time when someone forced you to do something that you knew was wrong. It may be that your leader made you do this, or it could be that your friends pressured you into doing something. Describe what happened to the group and how you felt about it. Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that the storyteller includes this list as part of a bigger list. Instead of only showing leaders in a military context, this list shows tribal leaders who have other responsibilities outside of the army. The storyteller presents this list to show that King David's government was large, organized, and strong.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the tribes of Israel and lists the officers that King David appointed over each tribe.

In the second scene: King David commands Joab, son of Zeruiah, to complete a census or to count the people of Israel. Yahweh shows his anger because King David tries to take control instead of trusting Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller
  • Eliezer, son of Zicri: Tribe of Reuben
  • Shephatiah, son of Maacah: Tribe of Simeon
  • Hashabiah, son of Kemuel: Tribe of Levi
  • Zadok: "tribe of Aaron" in the Levites
  • Elihu, King David's brother: Tribe of Judah
  • Omri, son of Michael: Tribe of Issachar
  • Ishmaiah, son of Obadiah: Tribe of Zebulun
  • Jerimoth, son of Azriel: Tribe of Naphtali
  • Hoshea, son of Azaziah: Tribe of Ephraim
  • Joel, son of Pedaiah: Half-tribe of Manasseh—west of the Jordan River
  • Iddo, son of Zechariah: Half-tribe of Manasseh—east of the Jordan River
  • Jaasiel, son of Abner: Tribe of Benjamin
  • Azarel, son of Jeroham: Tribe of Dan
  • Joab, son of Zeruiah
  • King David
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this passage, the storyteller continues to list the people King David has appointed to key roles in his kingdom. In the previous passage, the storyteller lists the structure of the military during peacetime, each commander in charge, and the month of the year each commander serves. The storyteller continues the list with the tribes and the men in charge who look after the tribes. King David most likely appoints all his leaders to help take care of his kingdom, but King David also likely uses this system to help his leaders communicate with other important leaders.

In the first scene, the storyteller lists tribes that make up the people groups of Israel. Each tribe that the storyteller lists has a leader or officer who is in charge of the tribe. This likely means that the officer keeps account of the people in the tribe and also communicates with army commanders who serve each month of the year. The officers are probably a link between the commanders and the people in Israel's tribes.

Stop and discuss as a group: How can you tell the difference between different types of officers, government workers, and leaders in your culture? Think about how these people dress, their titles, where they live, and other differences. How will you show the differences between the army commanders from the previous passage and the tribal leaders in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller includes several differences in this list compared to previous lists of Israel's tribes. The storyteller does not include the tribes of Gad and Asher in this list. The storyteller does not give a reason.

Another difference is that the storyteller notes a smaller group of people in the tribe of Levi. The storyteller seems to call one of the tribes "Aaron," and this is the only time a storyteller mentions Aaron in this way in the Old Testament. It is highly likely that the storyteller makes this point because the priests are a smaller group of the larger tribe of Levi. The storyteller names Zadok as the person in charge, and the storyteller likely wants to show that tribes sometimes have smaller groups in charge of certain tasks in Israel and the tribes.

The storyteller highlights another difference when he divides the tribe of Manasseh into two half-tribes. The storyteller uses the Jordan River as a clear dividing line for the tribe. Half of the tribe of Manasseh lives to the east of the Jordan River. The other half of the tribe of Manasseh lives to the west of the Jordan River. Again, the storyteller does not give a reason for this in this passage, but it is good to be aware of this difference.

Stop and discuss as a group: In your culture, what words or phrases would you use to show smaller divisions of a group that still shares the same name? In the passage, the tribe of Manasseh has half-tribes, but the half-tribes are still a part of the larger tribe. Think about how you can describe this. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller lists the tribes like this:

  • Over the tribe of Reuben: Eliezer, son of Zicri.
  • Over the tribe of Simeon: Shephatiah, son of Maacah.
  • Over the tribe of Levi: Hashabiah, son of Kemuel.
  • Over the tribe of Aaron: Zadok, who is in the Levites.
  • Over the tribe of Judah: Elihu, King David's brother.
  • Over the tribe of Issachar: Omri, son of Michael.
  • Over the tribe of Zebulun: Ishmaiah, son of Obadiah.
  • Over the tribe of Naphtali: Jerimoth, son of Azriel.
  • Over the tribe of Ephraim: Hoshea, son of Azaziah.
  • Over the half-tribe of Manasseh that is west of the Jordan River: Joel, son of Pedaiah.
  • Over the half-tribe of Manasseh that is east of the Jordan River: Iddo, son of Zechariah.
  • Over the Tribe of Benjamin: Jaasiel, son of Abner.
  • Over the Tribe of Dan: Azarel, son of Jeroham.

Stop and discuss as a group: In your language, how would you list people, and give their titles and family names? In what order would you give the information? How will you most naturally give the information about each Israelite tribal leader? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller talks about a census or a way to count the people of Israel. In the story, King David ordered Joab, son of Zeruiah, to count every male who was 20 years old or older. King David did not count anyone who could not serve in the military.

Stop and discuss as a group: How can you describe a census or a way to count people? How does your culture describe this action? Pause this audio here.

This story shows how David wants to control the success of his military and his kingdom. Joab does not want to count all of the people, because Joab remembers that Yahweh made a promise to make Israel so large that there would be no way for anyone to count all of the people. Yahweh told Abraham many years before that someday there would be more Israelites than there are stars in the sky. Yahweh wants King David to trust Yahweh, and King David is not doing this. Joab chooses not to finish the counting because Joab knows it is not the right thing to do.

Stop and discuss as a group: In your language, how will you translate the phrase "more than the stars in the sky"? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues and says that, even though Joab does not finish, King David still tries to do the wrong thing. King David's decision angers Yahweh, and Yahweh shows his strong anger, or wrath, to Israel. The storyteller finishes this passage by saying that the book of the history of King David never includes the final number of Israel's people.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller
  • Eliezer, son of Zicri: Tribe of Reuben
  • Shephatiah, son of Maacah: Tribe of Simeon
  • Hashabiah, son of Kemuel: Tribe of Levi
  • Zadok: "tribe of Aaron" in the Levites
  • Elihu, King David's brother: Tribe of Judah
  • Omri, son of Michael: Tribe of Issachar
  • Ishmaiah, son of Obadiah: Tribe of Zebulun
  • Jerimoth, son of Azriel: Tribe of Naphtali
  • Hoshea, son of Azaziah: Tribe of Ephraim
  • Joel, son of Pedaiah: Half-Tribe of Manasseh—west of the Jordan River
  • Iddo, son of Zechariah: Half-Tribe of Manasseh—east of the Jordan River
  • Jaasiel, son of Abner: Tribe of Benjamin
  • Azarel, son of Jeroham: Tribe of Dan
  • Joab, son of Zeruiah
  • King David
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The storyteller should recite the drama, and the team should act it out.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses:

In this story, King David appoints officers to lead the different tribes of Israel. Each officer takes charge of each tribe.

Here are the tribes the storyteller listed and the officers who helped to lead the tribes:

Eliezer, the son of Zicri, led the Tribe of Reuben. Shephatiah, the son of Maacah, led the tribe of Simeon. Hashabiah, the son of Kemuel, led the tribe of Levi, and Zadok the high priest led the "tribe of Aaron" in the Levites. Elihu, King David's brother, led the tribe of Judah. Omri, the son of Michael, led the tribe of Issachar. Ishmaiah, the son of Obadiah, led the tribe of Zebulun. Jerimoth, the son of Azriel, led the tribe of Naphtali. Hoshea, the son of Azaziah, led the tribe of Ephraim. Joel, the son of Pedaiah, led the half-tribe of Manasseh located west of the Jordan River. Iddo, the son of Zechariah, led the half-tribe of Manasseh located east of the Jordan River. Jaasiel, the son of Abner, led the tribe of Benjamin. Azarel, the son of Jeroham, led the tribe of Dan.

As part of the tribe of Levi, Zadok is in charge of the priests who begin with Aaron.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joel or Iddo, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad I do not have to lead an entire tribe alone! or
  • Am I more in charge of the tribe, or is he?

Ask the person playing Hashabiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope Zadok and I can work together; or
  • I wonder what kind of work I will do because I am not allowed to fight, since I am a Levite!

Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you thinking or feeling?" The person might answer things like:

  • Why did Yahweh choose not to include the tribes of Gad and Asher? or
  • I want this list to make King David look strong and powerful!
  • I hope I remembered every detail correctly!

Continue the drama.

King David commands Joab, son of Zeruiah, to count all of the men in Israel who are 20 years of age or older so that King David can know how many people are in King David's military. Joab does not want to do this because Joab believes that Yahweh does not want Joab to. Yahweh promised that Yahweh would make Israel's population so large that no one would be able to count it. King David tries to take control and wants to know how strong his kingdom is. Joab does not finish counting all of the people, because Joab knows that Yahweh will not approve. Even though Joab stops counting, Yahweh is angry and shows his anger to all of Israel. No one records a final number of Israelites in any records.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • What is King David thinking?! or
  • Yahweh is going to be so angry with me! or
  • Maybe I can convince King David not to do this!

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must know how many people there are who can defend me! or
  • If I do not include women or children, surely this will not offend Yahweh?! or
  • What have I done?!

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so sad that King David has disobeyed me and does not trust me! or
  • I must punish Israel for King David's disobedience!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this passage, the storyteller lists people whom King David appointed to lead tribes within Israel. This list is a part of a larger passage that shows all of the people who work for King David and who help lead. Use the same words or phrases for tribes and chiefs, or leaders of tribes, as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller lists the following people and tribes:

  • Eliezer, son of Zicri: Tribe of Reuben
  • Shephatiah, son of Maacah: Tribe of Simeon
  • Hashabiah, son of Kemuel: Tribe of Levi; and Zadok: "tribe of Aaron" in the Levites
  • Elihu, King David's brother: Tribe of Judah
  • Omri, son of Michael: Tribe of Issachar
  • Ishmaiah, son of Obadiah: Tribe of Zebulun
  • Jerimoth, son of Azriel: Tribe of Naphtali
  • Hoshea, son of Azaziah: Tribe of Ephraim
  • Joel, son of Pedaiah: Half-tribe of Manasseh—west of the Jordan River
  • Iddo, son of Zechariah: Half-tribe of Manasseh—east of the Jordan River
  • Jaasiel, son of Abner: Tribe of Benjamin
  • Azarel, son of Jeroham: Tribe of Dan

It is important to pay attention to each name and make sure you use the same names and spellings as you used in previous passages. Use the same word or phrase for tribes and Levites as you used in previous passages. Look up Levites in the Master Glossary for more information.

After the storyteller finishes the list, the storyteller talks about the census or counting of Israel. King David commands Joab, son of Zeruiah, to count all of the men who are 20 years old or older. Use the same word for Israel or Israelites as you used in previous passages, and see the Master Glossary for Israel and Israelites if needed.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for census or counting a population. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Joab does not want to do this, so Joab does not finish counting the men. Yahweh does not want King David or his people to keep count of the Israelites, because Yahweh promised to make Israel so big that no one could count the people. When King David and Joab try to count the men, Yahweh shows his wrath. Look up king and Yahweh in the Master Glossary for more information if needed.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for wrath. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Look up wrath in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that no one writes down the number of the Israelites in the special book about the Acts of King David. In those days, people wrote books on long pieces of animal skins or paper that they made from papyrus.

Stop here and look at a photo of a scroll. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate scroll or book. Use the same word for book or scroll as you have used previously.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 27:25–31
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 27:25–31 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:25–31 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller makes a transition to listing the people in charge of King David's property instead of people concerned with the kingdom as a whole. As the storyteller lists each person and their role, it is important to pay attention to locations as well. The storyteller likely includes the location names to show how much land and resources King David ruled over during this time.

The storyteller lists the 12 men David appointed in order as follows:

  • Azmaveth, son of Adiel
  • Jonathan, son of Uzziah
  • Ezri, son of Kelub
  • Shimei, the Ramathite, from the city of Ramah
  • Zabdi, the Shiphmite, from the city of Shepham
  • Baal-Hanan, the Gederite, from the city of Geder
  • Joash
  • Shitrai, the Sharonite, from the city of Sharon
  • Shaphat, son of Adlai
  • Obil, the Ishmaelite, the descendant of Ishmael
  • Jehdeiah, the Meronothite, from the city of Meronoth
  • Jaziz, from the Hagrite people group

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In the list above, the storyteller mentions family names and where people are from. In your culture, what special ways do people use to introduce themselves, their family line, or where they are from? Discuss how you introduce yourself and others when you first meet each other. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller includes each man on this list for a purpose. Each person has a specific task to do for King David's kingdom. Storytellers never mention many of the men in this list anywhere else in the Old Testament. But King David gives these men honor by appointing them to each role.

Azmaveth, son of Adiel, oversees King David's storehouse and livestock. Some translators use the word "treasury," but the word for treasury can also mean storehouses and cellars. It is possible that Azmaveth acts as the royal treasurer over financial tasks as well as the storehouses and livestock. Storehouses usually refer to places where people keep grain and other crops. In the temple, priests put the tithes, or 10 percent of income, in storehouses. Azamaveth looks after King David's property in Jerusalem and makes sure that he protects it.

Stop here and look at a picture of a storehouse that holds grain and other crops. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at different types of livestock like cows, sheep, and goats. Pause this audio here.

Jonathan, son of Uzziah, also looks after storehouses and livestock, but Jonathan oversees all of the storehouses in the country, cities, villages, and towers where people go to guard fields. This is a large responsibility!

Stop here and look at a tower that people might use to guard an area. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues the list by naming Ezri, son of Kelub, the person in charge of all farmers and field workers who work the land and grow crops. This is also a large responsibility, because crops provide food for King David's home and for everyone in the kingdom.

The storyteller lists Shimei, the Ramathite, and Zabdi, the Shiphmite, as overseers of the vineyards. It is likely that Shimei is from a city called Ramah and is specifically in charge of the vineyard workers. Vineyards are large fields where people grow grapes on vines for food and to make wine. Shimei is in charge of making sure the workers plant and tend to the vineyard crops. Zabdi is likely from a city called Shepham and is in charge of the grapes once they are ripe and ready for harvest. Zabdi likely oversees the workers who harvest the grapes. Zabdi also oversees the people who make the wine as well as the process of storing the wine.

Stop here and look at a vineyard with grapes on the vines. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a person squeezing grapes. Also, look at people making wine from grapes. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a map that shows where Shimei and Zabdi are from. Look at Jerusalem, where King David is, and see where Ramah and Shepham are in relation to Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues with Baal-Hanan, from a village called Geder, who oversees the olive trees and sycamore fig trees throughout the land. Olive trees grow slowly, but olive trees live for a long time. Olive trees grow olives, and olives make olive oil. Sycamore fig trees are trees that have leaves like a mulberry tree and fruit like a fig tree. Sycamore trees grow all year long, and a sycamore tree's wood is soft and durable. Baal-Hanan is in charge of everything that keeps these trees alive and thriving, including the workers. The trees grow in a place called "Shephelah," which is located in the foothills of Judah, west of the mountains and east of the Mediterranean Sea. Joash likely worked closely with Baal-Hanan. The storyteller lists Joash as the person who oversees the olive oil. This is likely a big task, as people use olive oil to cook and in the temple.

Stop here and look at a sycamore fig tree with leaves and figs on it. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at an olive tree, olives, and olive oil. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a map that shows Jerusalem, where King David is, and the locations where Baal-Hanan and Joash are from, specifically Geder and Shephelah. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues to talk about the land when he lists both Shitrai, the Sharonite, and Shaphat, son of Adlai, as the people who oversee the herds of cattle. In this instance, the storyteller defines "cattle" as cows, bulls, calves, and heifers. Shitrai is from the plain of Sharon, a fertile land by the Mediterranean coast, and cares for the cattle in his homeland. Shaphat is in charge of taking care of the cattle in all the valley regions of Israel except the plain of Sharon. Shitrai and Shaphat likely oversee the workers who care for the cattle.

Stop here and look at a map that includes the plain of Sharon and all other valleys of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at photos of cows, bulls, calves, and heifers. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller ends the list with three workers who care for various animals throughout the land. The first worker is Obil, the Ishmaelite. Obil oversees the camels and all workers who care for the camels. The name Obil means "camel driver," so it is likely that Obil grew up around camels and knew how to tend them. Obil is one of the few people the storyteller listed who do not belong to the tribes of Israel. Obil is a descendant of Ishmael, Abraham and Hagar's son. King David is not against including workers who have joined the people of Israel in worshipping Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of a camel. Pause this audio here.

The second person the storyteller lists is Jehdeiah, the Meronothite. Jehdeiah most likely comes from a town called Meronoth. Jehdeiah oversees the donkeys. The donkeys are specifically female donkeys, and the people would likely use the donkeys to breed more donkeys. The final person the storyteller lists is Jaziz, the Hagrite. Jaziz tends the flocks of sheep and goats. Like Obil, Jaziz is also not an Israelite. Jaziz is a part of the Hagrites, a pastoral tribe that lives in the region east of Gilead. Again, both Jehdeiah and Jaziz likely oversee the other workers who tend the cattle and flocks.

Stop here and look at a map that shows where Jehdeiah and Jaziz are originally from. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a photo of a donkey, a sheep, and a goat. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a map that shows King David's property, including Jerusalem and all of the places the storyteller lists in this passage. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller ends this passage by saying that each worker he listed is a steward or caretaker of King David's property. It is important to remember that property can mean possessions that people can move, or buildings or objects that people cannot move. It is highly likely that King David trusted the men listed above very much, because these men managed everything King David owned. These men likely felt honored to be a part of King David's workers.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller lists the two men that David appointed to oversee the treasury, storehouses, and livestock for Jerusalem and beyond.

In the second scene: The storyteller then lists the person responsible for overseeing the land, the farming, and all of the workers and farmers.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists the two men who are in charge of the vineyards.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller continues by listing the two men who are responsible for the olive and sycamore fig trees, and also for the olive oil production.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller lists the two men who tend the cattle.

In the sixth scene: The storyteller finishes the list by listing three men who tend to various types of animals.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller or Chronicler
  • Azmaveth, son of Adiel
  • Jonathan, son of Uzziah
  • Ezri, son of Kelub
  • Shimei, the Ramathite, from the city of Ramah
  • Zabdi, the Shiphmite, from the city of Shepham
  • Baal-Hanan, the Gederite, from the city of Geder
  • Joash
  • Shitrai, the Sharonite, from the city of Sharon
  • Shaphat, son of Adlai
  • Obil, the Ishmaelite, the descendant of Ishmael
  • Jehdeiah, the Meronothite, from the city of Meronoth
  • Jaziz, from the Hagrite people group

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Throughout this passage, the storyteller describes each person in the same way. The storyteller lists the person's name, where the person is from, and what the person does for King David.

In the first scene, the storyteller lists two people who oversee the storehouses, treasury, and livestock. Azmaveth, son of Adiel, oversees King David's storehouses and treasury in Jerusalem, and Jonathan, son of Uzziah, oversees the rest of the storehouses and treasury in King David's kingdom. Both men likely look after the financial treasury and the storehouses that hold grain, crops, and other important items for the kingdom, like livestock. The storyteller uses the word "country" to refer to fields in a countryside instead of official areas of land with borders. The storyteller continues by using the word "cities" to refer to areas protected by walls or structures. The storyteller also uses the word "villages" to refer to small towns without walls of protection. Lastly, Jonathan looks after "towers," which means places where people might store equipment, or towers where people can watch over fields or land to protect them against invaders or other dangers.

Stop here and look at a picture of a storehouse that holds grain and other crops. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at different types of livestock like cows, sheep, and goats. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a map that shows King David's property, including Jerusalem and all of the places listed in this passage. You may want to keep this map available so that you can refer back to it. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller lists the appointed man who oversees the workers who farm the land and plant the crops. Ezri, son of Kelub, ensures the land is ready for planting and produces good crops for food and storage.

In the third scene, the storyteller lists the two men in charge of tending the vineyards. Shimei, the Ramathite, is from Ramah and oversees the vineyards until harvest as well as the vineyard workers. Zabdi, the Shiphmite, is from Shepham and oversees the people who harvest the grapes and the people who make the wine. Shepham also looks after the workers.

Stop here and look at a vineyard with grapes on the vines. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at people squeezing grapes. Also, look at people making wine with grapes. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a map that shows where Shimei and Zabdi are from. Look at Jerusalem, where King David is, and see where Ramah and Shepham are in relation to Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller lists the two men who make sure people produce the olives well, from growth to harvest. Baal-Hanan, the Gederite, from the city of Geder, grows sycamore fig trees and olive trees. Joash makes sure people produce the olive oil well. Both men likely oversee the workers as well.

Stop here and look at a sycamore fig tree with leaves and figs on it. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at an olive tree, olives, and olive oil. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at the map that shows Jerusalem, where King David is, and the locations where Baal-Hanan and Joash are from, specifically Geder and Shephelah. Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller lists the two men responsible for the cattle, such as cows, bulls, calves, and heifers, and the workers who tend the cattle. Shitrai, the Sharonite, from the city of Sharon, tends the cattle in the plain of Sharon. And Shaphat, the son of Adlai, tends the cattle in all other valleys of Israel apart from the plain of Sharon.

Stop here and look at a map of the plain of Sharon and all the other valleys of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at photos of cows, bulls, calves, and heifers. Pause this audio here.

In the sixth scene, the storyteller finishes the list with three men who tend different types of animals. Obil, the Ishmaelite, is a non-Israelite who tends the camels. Jehdeiah, the Meronothite from Meronoth, tends the female donkeys. Jaziz, the Hagrite, a non-Israelite from the region east of Gilead, tends the flocks of sheep and goats.

Stop here and look at a picture of a camel. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at the map that shows where Jehdeiah the Meronothite and Jaziz the Hagrite are originally from. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a photo of a donkey, a sheep, and a goat. Pause this audio here.

In this session, have the group storyboard or draw in order to visualize the story and the action in it. It may be helpful to find an object to represent each person listed. For example, you could use oil to represent "Joash" or grapes to represent "Zabdi." This may help you remember each person and the role each person plays.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller or Chronicler
  • Azmaveth, son of Adiel
  • Jonathan, son of Uzziah
  • Ezri, son of Kelub
  • Shimei, the Ramathite, from the city of Ramah
  • Zabdi, the Shiphmite, from the city of Shepham
  • Baal-Hanan, the Gederite, from the city of Geder
  • Joash
  • Shitrai, the Sharonite, from the city of Sharon
  • Shaphat, son of Adlai
  • Obil, the Ishmaelite, the descendant of Ishmael
  • Jehdeiah, the Meronothite, from the city of Meronoth
  • Jaziz, from the Hagrite people group

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The person playing the storyteller or Chronicler should read the script. It may be helpful to find an object to represent each person listed. For example, you could use oil to represent "Joash" or grapes to represent "Zabdi." This may help you remember each person and the role each person plays.

This is a story about a list of people who helped care for King David's property and possessions.

Azmaveth, son of Adiel, is the first person in the list and is the person who oversees King David's treasury, storehouses, and livestock in Jerusalem. Jonathan, son of Uzziah, oversees the rest of the storehouses, treasury, and livestock in the countryside, cities, villages, and towers outside of Jerusalem. Both men hold important jobs as they look after the money and the stored crops and important items in King David's kingdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jonathan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel so important when I do this job! or
  • I am anxious because I have so many things to look after!

Continue the drama.

The next person in the list is a man named Ezri, son of Kelub. Ezri ensures the land is ready for planting, that the food grows, and that people harvest the food. Ezri likely looks after the workers who dig up the ground, plant the crops, and harvest the crops.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ezri, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope we get enough rain so the crops grow! or
  • Should I help dig up the ground, or am I only supposed to oversee the workers? or
  • I am afraid that the crops will not grow.

Continue the drama.

The next two people on the list are two men responsible for tending the vineyards. First is Shimei, the Ramathite, a man from Ramah. Shimei ensures the vineyards produce grapes until harvest. Second is Zabdi, the Shiphmite, from Shepham. Zabdi manages the harvest of grapes. Zabdi makes sure that the workers make the wine correctly. Both men likely look after the workers who help them carry out their duties.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shimei, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must get this right so that there will be harvest and wine! or
  • I am worried the grapes will not grow! or
  • Can I really depend on my workers?

Ask the person playing Zabdi, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope no animals eat the grapes! or
  • The wine must be the best for King David! or
  • I am worried that diseases will kill the grapes and that I will get in trouble!

Continue the drama.

Two men oversee the olive production. Baal-Hanan, the Gederite, is a person from a village called Geder who grows the sycamore fig trees and olive trees. Baal-Hanan plants and grows the trees, ensuring the olives and figs develop properly. Joash is a man who oversees the workers who make the olive oil. Both men likely oversee the workers who help grow, harvest, and process the olives and figs.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Baal-Hanan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • These trees take a long time to grow! or
  • I hope the weather is good and that the trees stay safe!

Ask the person playing Joash, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope the olives that Baal-Hanan grows are perfect for making olive oil! or
  • I must hire the best workers so we can make the best olive oil for everyone!

Continue the drama.

The list continues with two men who oversee the cattle, such as cows, bulls, calves, and heifers, and the workers who tend the cattle. Shitrai, the Sharonite, from the city of Sharon, tends the cattle in the fertile plain of Sharon, and Shaphat, the son of Adlai, tends the cattle in all other valleys of Israel apart from the plain of Sharon.

Ask the person playing Shitrai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so lucky to work in the same place where I have always lived! or
  • I know the best places to take the cattle for feeding!

Ask the person playing Shaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope the valleys where I am are as good for the cattle as the plain of Sharon! or
  • I hope I can trust my workers to tend the cattle well.

Continue the drama.

The list ends with three men who take care of different types of animals. Obil, the Ishmaelite, is a non-Israelite who tends the camels. Jehdeiah, the Meronothite, is the person from Meronoth who tends the female donkeys. Jaziz, the Hagrite, is a non-Israelite from the region east of Gilead who tends the flocks of sheep and goats.

Ask the people playing Obil and Jaziz, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • It feels strange to work in the land of the Israelites! or
  • I wonder if people will accept me since I am not an Israelite!

Ask the person playing Jehdeiah, "What are you thinking or feeling?" The people might answer things like:

  • I hope these female donkeys are able to birth more donkeys! or
  • How do I make sure we get more donkeys?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller of this passage lists men who are the sons of other men. These are probably the literal sons of their fathers. Use the same word for sons as you have used in previous passages, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about sons.

The storyteller in this passage lists key people who work for King David and his kingdom. The storyteller begins by introducing Azmaveth, son of Adiel, and Jonathan, son of Uzziah, as keepers of the treasury. The treasury may refer to both money and storehouses. Storehouses are places where people store crops and resources. Translate king in the same way you have previously, and for more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary if needed.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for storehouses or treasury. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Jonathan was in charge of the treasuries in the districts, cities, villages, and towers. Use the same words or phrases for cities and villages as you have previously. Remember that cities are settlements with walls around them, and villages are smaller groupings of houses near cities. These towers were special places where people could store weapons. People could also use towers to watch over fields and areas to see when the enemy was attacking. Towers are well-protected, like a fortress.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for tower. A tower is like a fortress, but it is also a place to store weapons. A tower was usually a tall place from where people watched for dangerous activity. Pause this audio here.

The next people the storyteller lists prepare the land for crops, as well as growing and harvesting the crops. Ezri and his workers dig up and prepare the ground for the crops. Both Shimei and Zabdi manage different parts of the vineyards. Shimei helps to make sure the vineyards grow the grapes. Zabdi harvests the grapes from the vineyards to make and store wine for King David. Use the same word for wine that you have used previously. For more information on wine, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for vineyards. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. For more information on vineyards, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller continues with more people who oversee the land. Baal-Hanan is responsible for ensuring the sycamore fig trees and the olive trees grow and produce fruit. Joash is responsible for harvesting the olives and making olive oil. Use the same word or phrase for olive trees, olives, and olive oil as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a sycamore fig tree. This is different from a regular sycamore tree. Pause this audio here.

The next people the storyteller lists are Shitrai and Shaphat. Shitrai tends the cattle in the plain of Sharon. A plain is usually a flat and fertile section of land with natural resources like water and good soil. Shaphat tends the cattle in all other valleys apart from the plain of Sharon. Valleys are usually lowlands between mountains that have a stream or river and grassland. Use the same word or phrase for valleys as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a plain. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The last people the storyteller mentions are men responsible for various types of animals. Obil takes care of the camels. Jehdeiah tends the female donkeys. Jaziz cares for the sheep and goats.
