For more information on camels and donkeys, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for camels, donkeys, sheep, and goats as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller ends the passage by saying that everyone he lists is a person who takes care of King David's property. When a person owns property, the property can be something that people can move, like furniture or a bicycle. Property can also be something that stays still, like a house. It is important to make sure you use the correct words to show this difference.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a property. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 27:32–34
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 27:32–34 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:32–34 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:32–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause the audio here.

In previous passages, the storyteller or Chronicler listed Israel's military leaders and tribe leaders. The storyteller also listed people who were in charge of taking care of King David's property and possessions. In this passage, the storyteller finishes the list of King David's trusted workers by telling us King David's closest and most important people.

Stop here and think about the closest person or people that you trust to help you. Share with each other who you have chosen and why you trust your closest people. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller begins by listing Jonathan first. The storyteller says that Jonathan is King David's "uncle," but we do not know for sure who Jonathan is. Even though it is possible that Jonathan is either King David's uncle or nephew, it is true that Jonathan was close enough to King David to be a person whom King David trusts. The storyteller describes Jonathan as King David's counselor and scribe. The word "scribe" likely means that Jonathan gained a lot of knowledge through education and learning. Jonathan may have been someone who advised King David.

The storyteller also lists a man named Jehiel with Jonathan. It is likely that Jonathan and Jehiel were in charge of educating King David's sons. Both Jonathan and Jehiel were educated men. The storyteller says that Jehiel is Hacmoni's son. Even though it is not fully clear, it is probably true that Jehiel was a descendant of the Hacmoni family line. The name Hacmoni means "wise" or "skilled," so the Hacmoni line likely shared this skill in education.

Stop here and think about skills or jobs that your family does. What jobs, skills, or talents do you have in common with other members of your family? Think about your parents, grandparents, uncles, aunts, etc. Discuss why you think families often have these similarities. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller lists a man named Ahithophel as a counselor to the king. This means that King David likely had more than one counselor or advisor at any given time. In a different part of the story, King David's son, Absalom, wanted to become king instead of King David, and Ahithophel supported this and advised Absalom to attack King David. Absalom's desire to have power caused the kingdom to divide. However, Absalom did not listen to Ahithophel's advice and delayed attacking King David. When Ahithophel realised Absalom did not listen to him, Ahithophel killed himself by hanging himself.

Stop here and think about a time when a person gave you bad advice. Discuss with the group whether or not you listened to the advice and what happened. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also lists a man named Hushai the Arkite as King David's friend, or someone David could trust and tell anything to without worry. It is not clear what or who "the Arkite" refers to, but it is possible that "Arkite" refers to the people group who lived in the land of Benjamin's tribe. Hushai proved his loyalty to King David when he pretended to help Absalom. Hushai became a spy and gave information to King David about Absalom's plans to try to become king instead of King David.

After Ahithophel died, two advisors took Ahithophel's place in King David's group of people whom King David trusted the most. Jehoiada, who was Benaiah's son, acted as an advisor for King David. Abiathar was a priest who also advised King David. Abiathar was the only priest who escaped from King Saul's reign, and King David later appointed Abiathar as the high priest. The storyteller lists Joab last. Joab was David's nephew and the commander of King David's army. Both Abiathar and Joab were loyal to David for many years until they both supported King David's son, Adonijah, in Adonijah's attempt to become king instead.

King David's closest advisors were people he trusted. The storyteller makes this list of people seem perfect. When the storyteller leaves out certain information, it can seem like King David had the perfect people surrounding him. In this list, as well as the lists before this one, the storyteller tries to make King David's reign look perfect by only talking about the good things in King David's reign.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you learn lists of names like the storyteller has listed in this passage? What are helpful visual aids or ways to remember, especially with names that are difficult to pronounce?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:32–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The Chronicler or storyteller begins to list the closest people to King David. The storyteller lists Jonathan and Jehiel first and describes them as the people who most likely educated King David's sons.

In the second scene: The storyteller continues the list with Ahithophel and Hushai as King David's counselors.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists Jehoiada and Abiathar as the two men who took Athiophel's place after Athiophel died.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller finishes by listing Joab as the commander in King David's army. Joab is the last name in the list of King David's inner group of people he can trust.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Chronicler or storyteller
  • Jonathan
  • Jehiel
  • David's sons
  • Ahithophel
  • Absalom
  • Hushai
  • Jehoiada
  • Abiathar
  • And Joab

In this session, have the group storyboard or draw in order to visualize the story and the action in it. It may be helpful to find an object to represent each person the storyteller lists. For example, you could use a book to represent Jonathan or Jehiel since they both helped educate David's sons. This may help you remember each person and the role each person plays.

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces a man named Jonathan as both a counselor to King David and a scribe or educated person. The storyteller describes Jonathan as an "uncle," but Jonathan is most likely a relative of some sort on King David's side of the family. The storyteller says that Jonathan is a man of understanding, which means that Jonathan can understand the situation and the best way to handle the situation. The storyteller also introduces a man named Jehiel and says that both Jonathan and Jehiel educated King David's sons.

Stop here and discuss: In your culture, what word do you use for a person who is educated and teaches other people? Also, discuss how you would describe a "man of understanding" in your culture. Think about a person you know who fits the descriptions of an educated person and a person of understanding. Discuss the options for words that you could use. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller continues the list with a man named Ahithophel and a man named Hushai. Ahithophel was King David's counselor. Hushai was King David's closest and most trusted friend. The storyteller uses the words "counselor" and "friend" to show King David is close to both men. Ahithophel gives King David advice and helps King David work through difficult decisions, but the storyteller describes Hushai as King David's closest person. A friend is someone who is trustworthy and who will keep secrets and not share important information with other people.

Stop here and discuss: In your culture and language, how can you show the difference between a counselor and a close friend? Which words would you use to show the difference? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Jehoiada and Abiathar took Ahithophel's place after he died. Jehoiada was the son of Benaiah, and Abiathar served as a priest and then as the high priest.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller finishes the list of people by introducing Joab. Joab served as the commander of King David's army.

As before, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:32–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Chronicler or storyteller
  • Jonathan
  • Jehiel
  • David's sons
  • Ahithophel
  • Absalom
  • Hushai
  • Jehoiada
  • Abiathar
  • And Joab

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The person playing the storyteller or Chronicler should tell the story.

This is a story about King David's most important people, including David's closest advisors and trusted people. The list begins with Jonathan, who is likely David's uncle. Jonathan is King David's counselor and scribe. With Jonathan, a man named Jehiel is responsible for helping to educate King David's sons.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jonathan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so glad I get to help my family; or
  • I feel really important because I am helping the king! or
  • I hope I do not make a mistake!

Continue the drama.

Ahithophel is King David's counselor, and Hushai the Arkite is David's closest friend and most trusted person.

After Ahithophel dies, Jehoiada and Abiathar become King David's counselors.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hushai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • I feel honored to be King David's closest friend; or
  • I hope King David knows he can trust me.

Ask the person playing Abiathar, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • I must make sure everyone keeps God's laws! or
  • Since I am a priest, I hope I am appointed high priest one day.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller finishes the list with Joab as the commander of King David's army. Joab is the last name in King David's list of trusted people.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • I must keep King David and his people safe! or
  • This is a lot of pressure!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 27:32–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller of this passage continues on from the previous lists and introduces King David's most important people. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. Refer to king in the Master Glossary for more information.

The storyteller lists several roles throughout this passage. The storyteller describes both Jonathan and Ahithophel as King David's counselors. The storyteller says that Jonathan was a man of understanding, which means that Jonathan could understand a situation and the best way to handle the situation. This means that Ahithophel and Jonathan likely listened to King David and gave him ideas and answers to questions. The storyteller also calls Hushai the Arkite the king's friend. A friend is a person whom you can trust more than anyone else and who keeps your secrets without telling other people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for counselor and friend. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Both Jonathan and Jehiel educated King David's sons, but the storyteller specifically lists Jonathan as a scribe. A scribe would usually mean someone who is educated and teaches others. Use the same word or phrase for scribe as you used in previous passages. For more information on scribe, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller lists Joab last as the commander of King David's army. Use the same words for army and commander as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 28:1–8
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 28:1–8 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:1–8 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember in 1 Chronicles 23 that King David had gathered all the leaders of Israel, including the priests and Levites, to Jerusalem. Then, in the most recent stories, the storyteller had interrupted the story to tell us the names of the leaders and those who were in charge of King David's property. The storyteller tells us that the other leaders were the leaders of the 12 tribes of Israel, the leaders of the army troops that served the king, the commanders or generals of the military units made of 1,000 soldiers and of 100 soldiers each, the leaders who took care of the property and animals that belonged to the king and his sons, the leaders of those who served in the king's palace, and the powerful men and all the brave warriors.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss a kingdom or country that has one ruler. Talk about how that man organises the society of that kingdom or country. Name the groups of people that help the ruler to rule, and discuss the different levels of authority and power that the different groups of people have in the kingdom. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller returns to the gathering of leaders from 1 Chronicles 23. When all of these people had assembled together, probably in the palace, King David stood up and spoke to them. King David was an old man at this time, so it is likely that David had been sitting on his throne. David stood up to speak to the leaders because what he wanted to say was very important. King David told the people that he had had it in his heart, or greatly desired, to build a permanent house of rest for the ark of the covenant of Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of the covenant of Yahweh as a group. Pause this audio here.

From the day that Moses made the ark in the wilderness, the ark had moved from the wilderness to Gilgal, then to Shiloh, then to the cities of the Philistines, then to Beth Shemesh, then to Kiriath Jearim, and finally to Jerusalem. David is saying that he really wanted to build the temple where the people could permanently keep the ark of the covenant, just as Yahweh had instructed Moses. The Israelites believed that Yahweh's throne was the holy place between the cherubim above the ark of the covenant. Yahweh met and spoke to Moses from above the wings of the cherubim that were on the ark of the covenant. Therefore, the people believed that the ark or its cover was the place where Yahweh rested his feet. The place where people rest their feet is called a footstool. In that culture, someone would sit on a chair and place their feet on a small stool to relax.

King David told the elders that because of his great desire, David had started preparing to build the temple. King David probably means that he had begun to gather the material and skilled builders that the people would need.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the places of worship in your community and neighbouring communities. Talk about the day that the people made each place of worship. Talk about all the preparation that you needed to do. Mention the materials you needed, the people who did the building, and the cost and time it took. Discuss also people whom you did not allow to be involved in this holy work, and the reason why you did not allow them to help. Pause this audio here.

King David told the leaders that he really wanted to build the temple and had made all the preparations, but that Yahweh told David that David could not be the one to build the temple. Yahweh had chosen King David to fight battles and to bring peace to Israel. David would have had a hard time building the temple before he had defeated all the enemies. David was a warrior who had killed many people, so David was unclean, or unfit to touch holy things or go into holy places. The temple would be very holy. Yahweh told the prophet Nathan to tell David that David's son would build the temple, and Yahweh promised to build David a kingdom instead. Yahweh promised David that his son Solomon would have rest from battle and be the king of peace. Solomon's name means "peaceful."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of a king or chief who ruled in difficult times and fought many battles, and another king who ruled in peaceful times. Compare the kind of things they were both able to do in their times. Pause this audio here.

King David now reminds the leaders of the promises that Yahweh had made to David and his descendants. This way, King David shows the leaders his right to be king of Israel. King David tells the leaders that even though Yahweh did not allow David to build the temple, nevertheless, Yahweh, the God of Israel, had chosen David and his descendants out of all his father's sons to be kings of Israel forever. David explained to them that this was because Yahweh had chosen the tribe of Judah to be the leader of all the tribes of Israel. And from the tribe of Judah, Yahweh had chosen David's father Jesse's house. And among the sons of Jesse, David's brothers, Yahweh had chosen David and made him king. Then David says that from his many sons, Yahweh had chosen Solomon to sit on the throne, or to be king of the kingdom of Yahweh. Yahweh was the real ruler of Israel, and Yahweh chose the man who would represent Yahweh as king. Different nations had different ways of deciding which man would be the ruler. Some chose kings from one family, others chose the strongest or richest family, but in Israel, it was Yahweh who chose the king because Yahweh is the true king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your community choose its rulers? Discuss the part that God, the spirit, or the ancestors play in choosing your rulers. If this does not happen in your community, discuss how it happens in another community. Pause this audio here.

David told the leaders that Yahweh chose Solomon to be the next king after David died. Yahweh also said that it would be Solomon who would build Yahweh's house or temple and Yahweh's courtyards. The courtyards of the temple were important places where the people of Israel held their festivals and celebrated before Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of the temple and its courtyards as a group. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh said that he had chosen Solomon to be his son, and Yahweh promised to be Solomon's father. In these days, if a great king gave someone a country in his kingdom to rule for him, the king made a covenant or agreement with that person and adopted him as his son and promised to be his father. This is how Yahweh promised to protect the kingdom of Israel for Solomon. Yahweh promised to establish Solomon's kingdom forever if Solomon continued to obey Yahweh's commands and ordinances, as he was already doing. It is also possible that Yahweh meant that David was the one obeying Yahweh's commands and ordinances.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does the ruler of your community relate with God, spirits, or the ancestors? Talk about the things that your ruler is supposed to do or not do to keep God, the spirits, or ancestors happy with him. Pause this audio here.

Then King David publicly encouraged and urged the leaders before all Israel, and in the presence of God, to be careful to do everything that Yahweh had commanded. David told the leaders that if they took care to study and obey the commands of Yahweh, they would continue to possess the good, fertile land that Yahweh had given them and also pass it down to their descendants. David himself had obeyed Yahweh and was now passing down his inheritance to his son Solomon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories about when a ruler was nearing the end of his rule and spoke to children and his people about what they should do after he dies. What kind of things did the ruler talk about, and why do you think he spoke of those things?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: King David summoned or called together all the leaders of the people of Israel to gather in Jerusalem.

In the second scene: King David stood up and told the leaders about the plans that he had for building the temple. David told the leaders that although he had begun preparing to build the temple, Yahweh had stopped David and told him that Yahweh had chosen Solomon to build it.

In the third scene: King David therefore urged the leaders of the people of Israel to study and obey the commands and ordinances that Yahweh had given the people of Israel. David said that if they did, they would continue to possess the good land that Yahweh had given them and also pass it down to their children.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • The leaders of the people of Israel. They were the leaders of the tribes, the leaders of the divisions of soldiers that served the king, the commanders of the military units of thousands of soldiers and of the military units of hundreds of soldiers, the leaders of those who looked after the king's property and the livestock of the king and of his sons, the leaders of those who served in the palace, the powerful men, and all the brave warriors.
  • Solomon
  • The other sons of David, Solomon's brothers
  • All the people of Israel and their descendants
  • And David's father's family, David's brothers

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the action of the story returns to when David assembled or gathered all the leaders of Israel in Jerusalem. The storyteller had interrupted the flow of the story that he started in 1 Chronicles 23 to list the names of Levites and the other officials according to how King David organised them. Now the storyteller tells us what happened after David gathered the leaders in Jerusalem. It is likely that the leaders were all standing before King David in the palace or somewhere public in the city of Jerusalem. David gathered the chiefs of the tribes or tribal leaders. Every one of the 12 tribes usually had one wise man whom they had chosen to be their leader. The officers of the divisions or leaders of the military units that served the king were also there. King David had divided them into 12 divisions of 24,000 officers or soldiers, and they took turns month after month to serve the king. King David further divided these divisions of 24,000 into units of 1,000 or 100, each with its captain. These captains of the 1,000 and 100 soldiers each were also there. The managers of the property and livestock of the king and of his sons were also there. It is very likely that King David's sons were there because this meeting is about who would become king after David. The palace officials or the king's advisors and government administrators were also there. All the mighty or powerful men, together with all the brave warriors, were also there. The storyteller uses one word for leader and then specifies what leader he is referring to by mentioning the leader's role.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of when there has been such a gathering in your community. What was the reason people gathered, and who called the gathering? When you discuss this question, try to use different and specific words for the different leaders. If you do not have specific words, use phrases like "the great chief" or name them according to their specific roles. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one person list out to the rest of the group all the leaders that were in the previous discussion. The person should try different ways of listing the leaders until they find the best way. One way the person can do this is in groups. For example, they might say the leaders of the men and then list out each individual leader that falls into the category of men, such as the leader of the old men, the leader of the warriors, and the leader of the unmarried men. The group should listen and make suggestions. You should repeat this activity until you find a way of listing the leaders that you all like. Pause audio here.

In the second scene, King David stood up and spoke to the leaders who were gathered before him. At this time, King David had grown old and would have likely been sitting down on his throne, but David stood up to address the leaders of Israel. It is important to note that although the king was old, the storyteller does not show him as a weak old man. The storyteller records the words that King David spoke to the elders directly. King David spoke to the leaders of Israel very pleasantly. David called them his brothers and his people. They were David's people because he was their king, and they were David's brothers because they were all from the same ancestors.

Stop here and discuss how a king or a great man addresses or talks to people who are lower or less important than himself. How would he speak to the people to show them that he is one of them and that he loves them? Pause audio here.

King David told the people that he had had it in his heart, or greatly desired, to build a house of rest for the ark of the covenant of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words, phrases, or special language do you use in your community to express a very strong desire? Pause this audio here.

The ark was a box which could not feel tired, but David speaks of building it a house of rest as if the ark was a living thing.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Give examples of ways in which you speak of non-living things as if they were alive in your language. Pause this audio here.

David calls the ark of the covenant the footstool or place of rest for the feet of the God of Israel. The ark was the place where Yahweh met with the people of Israel. Yahweh spoke from above the lid of the ark. Therefore, the people of Israel called the ark "Yahweh's footstool," or the place where Yahweh rested his feet.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the kind of special language that you use for a sacred place where God meets you. Explain what each phrase means. Suggest what special language you would use for a place where heaven and earth touch or where the presence of God from heaven touches the earth. Remember that the people of Israel believed that Yahweh both met them and rested his feet on the ark. Pause this audio here.

The temple is also called "a house for Yahweh's name." The Israelites would worship and honor Yahweh in the temple. Therefore, it is a house where people honor Yahweh's name.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about the temple as a house for Yahweh's name. Pause this audio here.

David told the leaders of Israel that although he had wanted to build the temple of Yahweh and was ready to begin, Yahweh had forbidden or stopped David. Yahweh told David that Yahweh would not allow David to build the house of Yahweh because David was a warrior and had shed much blood, which means that David had killed many people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a person who was a great warrior and had killed many people in battle. What kind of special language do you use to describe someone like this? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh did not reject the plan for the building of the temple. Yahweh rejected David as the man to do it. It is important to show the strong difference between the strong desire that David had to build the temple and how strongly Yahweh rejected David. The temple was to be the holy place where Yahweh's people would worship and honor Yahweh. Yahweh said he rejected David because David had shed the blood of many people in battle. This would have made David's hands unclean or unworthy to do the holy work of building the temple. It is important to note that this does not mean that David was evil. David told the leaders of Israel that, in spite of Yahweh rejecting David to build the temple, Yahweh had nevertheless chosen David and his descendants from all the sons of his father Jesse to be king over Israel. King David explained to the leaders that, formerly, Yahweh had chosen the tribe of Judah from the 12 tribes of Israel to be the leader. And from the tribe of Judah, Yahweh had chosen Jesse, David's father's house. And then from the sons of Jesse, David's brothers, Yahweh chose David to be king over Israel. Then David told the leaders that, of all his many sons, Yahweh had chosen Solomon to sit on the throne of Israel. A throne is the seat of a king. A throne symbolises the king's power and authority.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the symbol of a king's power and authority in your culture? How will you translate the word throne in this passage? The way you translate this word needs to show that Solomon has the power and authority in Israel. Pause this audio here.

Israel was the Kingdom of Yahweh, and it was Yahweh who chose the person who would rule as his representative.

King David told the leaders of Israel that Yahweh had told David that it would be Solomon who would build the temple or house of Yahweh and its courtyards. The temple area included two large open spaces called courtyards. One courtyard was for the priests to do their work. The other courtyard is where the rest of the people of Israel celebrated the feast of Yahweh. It was a place of joy and celebration to be in the presence of Yahweh. Yahweh said that Yahweh had chosen Solomon to be his son and that Yahweh would be Solomon's father. This is the language people used to make strong covenants or agreements. Yahweh was saying that his relationship with Solomon would last forever. But Yahweh said that he would only secure Solomon's kingdom as long as Solomon continued to obey Yahweh's commands and ordinances.

In the third scene, King David finishes his speech to the leaders of Israel by encouraging them to study and obey all the commands of Yahweh, the God of Israel. David says that he has spoken to the leaders in the sight or in the presence of all Israel, which is the assembly of Yahweh. An assembly is a formal gathering of people. It is another way of saying all Israel. It does not mean that every Israelite person was there, but that those who were there represented all of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when your people have had a gathering like that. Talk about why leaders called the assembly together and what happened. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to refer to the group of people who had gathered together. Pause this audio here.

It is unlikely that all of the people of Israel were there—it was just their leaders. David also said that Yahweh had witnessed everything that David said to his people. David told the people that if they obeyed Yahweh, they would continue to own the good and fertile land that Yahweh had given them and also pass the land down to their descendants. David knew that he was dying when he spoke these last words to the people. Therefore, David charged the people of Israel to continue obeying and trusting Yahweh.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 3 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • The leaders of the people of Israel. They were the leaders of the tribes, the leaders of the divisions of soldiers that served the king, the commanders of the military units of thousands of soldiers and of the military units of hundreds of soldiers, the leaders of those who looked after the king's property and the livestock of the king and of his sons, the leaders of those who served in the palace, the powerful men, and all the brave warriors.
  • Solomon
  • The other sons of David, Solomon's brothers
  • All the people of Israel and their descendants
  • And David's father's family, David's brothers

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

King David summoned or called together all the leaders of the people of Israel to gather in Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing king David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is an important day. I make my son Solomon king today. I am very happy; or
  • All of Israel will know that Yahweh has chosen Solomon to be king after me; or
  • I praise Yahweh who has been with me and pray that Yahweh will be with my son also. I want to make sure that the leaders of the people of Israel will obey Yahweh and support Solomon.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is an amazing day. I am very happy; or
  • Yahweh has loved me and blessed me; or
  • My father promised my mother that he would make me king after him and now he is fulfilling his promise.

Ask the person playing the leaders of the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Today is a very important day for our nation. Our king will tell us who will be the next king; or
  • We have all come to Jerusalem. All the leaders are here. There is great excitement in the city; or
  • The king is very wise. The king does not want the kingdom to be divided after he dies.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, King David stood up and told the leaders of Israel that he had greatly desired to build the temple of Yahweh, but that Yahweh had refused David and chosen his son Solomon instead. Yahweh had chosen Judah from the 12 tribes, and Jesse from the clans of Judah, and David from the sons of Jesse. And now, Yahweh had chosen Solomon from the many sons of David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing king David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • With all my heart I wanted to be the one who built the temple for our God. Yahweh made my house great, and I love him and wanted to honor him by making a place where the ark of the covenant, Yahweh's footstool, would rest; or
  • I even made the preparations needed to start this great work, but Yahweh did not allow me to be the one who had the honor of building his house. Yahweh chose my son Solomon instead. Yahweh is king over Israel, and he chooses whoever he desires. I praise Yahweh's name. I am very excited for my son; or
  • I was sad at first but I am happy because although Yahweh rejected me, he also blessed me by honoring my son. And I want to make sure that Solomon has the support of the whole nation because this is a great task. I am sad that I will not be here to see the house of Yahweh.

Ask the person playing the leaders of the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel excited. I feel great joy and happiness. We have long desired to complete this work that Yahweh gave us; or
  • Oh what a happy reign Solomon will have. Solomon will build the house of Yahweh. May Yahweh bless him; or
  • Israel will love Solomon just as we have loved his father, David. David bravely fought our enemies and brought us peace. Now Solomon will bring the glory to Israel.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is my will. David has obeyed me and I am happy; or
  • I will give Solomon rest from the wars his father had to fight. Solomon will rule in peace and build my house without enemies disturbing him; or
  • I am happy that the whole nation will know my will. All these leaders will take the message to them. I want them to support Solomon. Solomon is my chosen king.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very small. This work is very important and I am not experienced; or
  • I am happy that my father has made preparations for me; or
  • I will need the support of these men who have served my father wisely and loyally.

Continue the drama.

King David told the leaders that Yahweh said that he would be a father to Solomon and Solomon would be a son to Yahweh. Yahweh promised to secure Solomon's kingdom if Solomon obeyed the commands of Yahweh. Then King David charged the leaders of the people of Israel to obey Yahweh's commands so that Yahweh would keep them in the good land he gave them.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • If Solomon keeps my commands and ordinances as I have instructed then Solomon will have a long and happy rule; or
  • The leaders of the people of Israel must also obey my commands and teach the people to do the same and I will bless them; or
  • But if they do not and go after other gods and practice things that I have not commanded then I will throw them out of my land as I told Moses.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Obedience is very important. Yahweh will not bless disobedience; or
  • Yahweh has disciplined me when I have disobeyed him, and I know that Yahweh will remain faithful to himself. Yahweh cannot change. Yahweh treats disobedience seriously; or
  • I am sad that I will be leaving my people soon, but I hope that these leaders of the people of Israel will be obedient and make sure that they help Solomon to rule as Yahweh has commanded.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very happy. Yahweh has made great promises to me; or
  • I must be careful to obey Yahweh. I want my kingdom to be secured; or
  • My heart rejoices in Yahweh.

Ask the person playing the leaders of the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We have a great responsibility; or
  • Yahweh's blessings are great and many and we want them. We love this land and want to pass it down to our children, but we must obey; or
  • We will be loyal to Solomon, and we pray that he is obedient and pleasing to Yahweh.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King David called together to Jerusalem all the officials of Israel. Use the same words or phrases for king, Jerusalem, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, Jerusalem, or Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. The officials of Israel is a general phrase for the leaders of specific areas. The storyteller uses the general word for leader and then specifies the specific role that each leader played.

These were the officials in their specific roles: the officers over the tribes or tribal leaders were responsible for their specific tribes, and the commanders of the divisions were in charge of one unit of 24,000 soldiers. The commanders of thousands worked under the commander of the division and were in charge of 1,000 soldiers, whilst the commander of hundreds was in charge of 100 soldiers. You will remember that "divisions" refers to the groups of soldiers or workers that David had divided into groups. Some officers were in charge of all the property and livestock, which is the cattle, sheep, and goats, that belonged to the king and his sons. Use the same words or phrases for divisions, livestock, and officers or leaders as you used in previous passages.

King David also called all the palace officials who specifically worked in the palace, the mighty men, and all the brave fighting men. The "mighty men" normally refers to great warriors, but here it likely refers to powerful, influential men in general, who did not occupy any special public or court office.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: What will you call these leaders? Pause this audio here.

King David stood up and said that he had had it in his heart to build a "house of rest" or temple for the ark of the covenant of Yahweh and for a footstool of God. Look up heart and ark of the covenant in the Master Glossary for more information, and translate heart and ark of the covenant in the same way you have in previous passages. "House of rest" is another way to say the temple. It is also called the "house of God." It is the place where the people worshipped Yahweh. The temple is also the place where the people of Israel kept the ark of the covenant, which symbolized the presence of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "house of rest." Look up temple in the Master Glossary for more information to help you decide how to translate house of rest. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites believed that Yahweh's throne was the holy place between the cherubim above the ark of the covenant. Therefore, the ark was called his footstool or the place where Yahweh rested his feet.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for footstool. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller sometimes refers to the God of Israel by his proper name, Yahweh, and sometimes by the more general term God. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh and God as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh or God, refer to the Master Glossary.

Although Yahweh had refused for David to build the temple, Yahweh had nevertheless chosen David and his descendants from the house of Judah to be king over Israel and Solomon, out of David's many sons, to be king over the kingdom of Yahweh. The house of Judah refers to the family or tribe of Judah, or Judah's descendants. Use the same words or phrases for house or family, Judah, king, and kingdom as you used in previous passages. For more information on Judah, king, and kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh told David that it would be Solomon who would build Yahweh's house and its courts. The courts or courtyard of the temple were a very special place for the people of Israel. This is where those who were not from the tribe of priests celebrated the feasts of Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for courts as you did in 1 Chronicles 23:28.

Yahweh said that Solomon would be Yahweh's son and Yahweh would be Solomon's father. This is special language to say that Yahweh had made a covenant or agreement with Solomon. Yahweh said that he would establish the kingdom for Solomon if Solomon would keep Yahweh's commands and ordinances. Use the same words or phrases for commandments and ordinances as you used in previous passages. For more information on commandments and ordinances, refer to the Master Glossary.

David charged the leaders of Israel in the sight of all Israel or in the presence of the nation. David used this special language to call the people of Israel as witnesses. David also called all Israel, the assembly of Yahweh. The assembly of Yahweh means the gathering of Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for assembly as you used in 1 Chronicles 13:2.

David also said that he spoke to the leaders of Israel these instructions in the "hearing of God." David was saying that Yahweh was also a witness, and Yahweh heard everything they were saying.

David told the people that they should be careful to obey Yahweh so that they can keep owning the land and give it to their children as an inheritance. Use the same word or phrase for inheritance as you used in previous passages

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 28:9–21
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 28:9–21 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:9–21 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King David gathered all the leaders of Israel in Jerusalem and told them Yahweh had chosen Solomon to build the temple. David strongly urged the leaders to obey Yahweh.

In this story, David turned to Solomon in front of the leaders and gave Solomon the same strong commands. David told Solomon to be committed to Yahweh, David's God. David told Solomon to know Yahweh personally and to serve Yahweh with all his heart and mind. When someone is fully committed to something, the people of Israel say that the person has given his whole heart. If a person is not fully committed, they say that person is distracted or has a divided heart.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when you were fully committed to do something. Pause the audio here.

David emphasized that Yahweh knows everyone's heart and intentions. David was telling Solomon that Solomon must not lie or hide his motives from Yahweh. If Solomon obeyed, Yahweh would accept Solomon, but if Solomon abandoned Yahweh, Yahweh would reject Solomon forever. Yahweh had made a covenant or strong promise that he would secure Solomon's kingdom if Solomon obeyed Yahweh's commandments. If Solomon did not build the temple, Yahweh would reject Solomon. David warned Solomon to take this seriously and get to work on building the temple, as Yahweh had instructed.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of strong promises that you personally and also your community together have made with God, spirits, or your ancestors. What are the ways in which you personally or your community could have broken those promises or failed to honor the agreement, and what would have happened to you or your community? Pause the audio here.

David then gave Solomon the plans for the temple. We do not know what the plans looked like. It is probable that these plans were instructions that someone wrote down for the skilled builders to follow.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What does the place of worship in your community look like? Describe it in as much detail as you can. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us all the instructions that were in the plans that David gave to Solomon. The plans included details for various sections of the temple, starting from the entrance of the main temple building to the Most Holy Place. The plans had instructions about how to build the portico or entrance of the temple. This was probably a long hallway without a roof that you had to pass through to enter the temple itself. The plan also had instructions about the temple buildings. This probably refers to buildings that surrounded the temple, but it could also refer to the two main rooms of the temple. The plans had instructions for the temple's storage rooms and the rooms on its roof, as well as instructions for the rooms inside the temple and the room for the mercy seat or place of atonement. This was the way that David referred to the ark of the covenant. People believed that the cover of the ark was a place where Yahweh lived. This is where the high priest asked Yahweh to forgive the sins of all the Israelites each year.

Stop here and look at a drawing of Solomon's temple as a group. See if you can find the different parts of the temple on the drawing. Pause the audio here.

David gave Solomon the plans that Yahweh's Spirit put in David's mind for the courts of Yahweh's temple. The temple had two courts, one for priests to sacrifice animals and another for people to gather and celebrate feasts.

Stop here and look at the drawing of Solomon's temple as a group again. Point out the two courts. Pause the audio here.

When the people of Israel left Egypt, Yahweh gave Moses detailed plans of how to build a tabernacle for Yahweh's honor. Moses instructed the Israelites to make the tabernacle of tents and move it from place to place. After David had defeated many enemies and brought peace to Israel, David desired to build a more permanent tabernacle or sanctuary to honor Yahweh. The plan for this more permanent sanctuary, also called a temple, was the same as those that Yahweh gave Moses for the tabernacle. However, there were also other things that David added to the temple. David says that the Spirit of Yahweh guided him when he was making the plans.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when someone has created something or come up with an idea with the help of God or the divine spirits in the community. Pause the audio here.

David also gave Solomon plans for the surrounding rooms, which were storerooms for temple items and treasures. They were a row of rooms, built one above the other on three floors or levels. It is likely that these rooms stored various vessels or utensils that people used in the temple service, as well as the food items such as flour, oil, and wine. The rooms were also the treasuries or storerooms of the things of gold, silver, and other valuable items that people had dedicated to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the most special gifts someone can give to God, the spirits, or the ancestors in your community. Pause the audio here.

David instructed Solomon on how to organize the priests and Levites for their work in the temple. David also detailed the amount of gold and silver that Solomon would need to make various temple utensils. David specified the amounts for the gold and silver lampstands with their lamps. The amount of gold or silver for the lampstands with their lamps depended on how people used them.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lampstand as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the instructions, David also told Solomon the weight of gold that he would require to make each golden table on which the priests would place the loaves of bread they offered to Yahweh. People made this table of acacia wood and then covered it with gold. David also gave Solomon the weight of silver for making the tables that people covered with silver.

Stop here and look at a picture of the golden tables as a group. Pause this audio here.

David also gave Solomon instructions on how much pure or true gold he should use to make the forks or meat hooks that the priests would use to handle the meat people gave as a sacrifice.

Stop here and look at a picture of a fork as a group. Pause this audio here.

David also told Solomon how much pure gold he would need to make the basins that the priests would use to hold the blood of the sacrificed animal, which they would sprinkle on the altar.

Stop here and look at a picture of a basin as a group. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon the amount of pure gold he needed to make the jugs that the priest would use in Yahweh's drink or wine offerings.

Stop here and look at a picture of the jugs as a group. Pause this audio here.

David also told Solomon the amount of gold for each gold and silver small bowl. People also used these bowls for the drink offerings.

Stop here and look at a picture of the gold and silver small bowls as a group. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon the amount of pure gold that he would need to make the altar that the priests used to offer sweet-smelling incense to Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of the altar of incense as a group. Pause this audio here,

David also gave Solomon the plan for making the chariot, which is the golden cherubim that spread their wings and cover the ark of the covenant of Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of the cherubim with their wings spread over the ark of the covenant as a group. Pause this audio here.

King David told Solomon that he had written all those plans because Yahweh had guided him and made him understand them. Yahweh wanted to make sure that Solomon built the temple according to Yahweh's plans.

Finally, David encouraged Solomon to be strong and brave, and he assured Solomon that Yahweh would be with him and ensure the temple's completion. David mentioned that priests, Levites, and craftsmen were ready to help with the work.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the biggest building project that you have done in your community. List all the people you needed for the project, and mention each person's role and how they felt about being involved in the great project.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: David turns from talking to the leaders of Israel and begins to talk to Solomon publicly. David urges Solomon to know Yahweh and to be completely committed to serving Yahweh. David tells Solomon to obey Yahweh and to build the temple. David warns Solomon that if Solomon rejects Yahweh then Yahweh will also reject Solomon.

In the second scene: David gives Solomon the plans he had prepared to build the temple and its buildings. David also tells Solomon the weight in gold and silver that he would need for making various utensils that the priests will use in their work. David tells Solomon how to organise the priests and Levites for their work.

In the third scene: David urges Solomon to be brave and to do the work that Yahweh has given him. David tells Solomon that the priests and Levites are ready to begin their work in the temple and that the skilled craftsmen are ready and willing to help Solomon do the work of building the temple. David also tells Solomon that the leaders of the people of Israel will obey everything that he tells them.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • David
  • Solomon
  • Priests and Levites
  • The leaders of Israel
  • All the skilled craftsmen
  • And all the people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The action of this story continues from the last story. David had been speaking to the elders of Israel and had just commanded them to obey Yahweh. In the first scene of this story David turns to his son Solomon, in the presence of the elders, and tells Solomon to obey Yahweh. The storyteller opens the scene by showing that David focused his words on Solomon even though there were many people there.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Imagine a great man is dying. Have the man speak his last words to all his children to encourage them about the future and remind them of the important things that he taught them. Then let the man choose one of his sons and give instructions about how to lead, support, and look after the rest of the family. Let the great man speak his words directly to the chosen son in front of the rest. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to show that the great man has switched the focus of his words from all his sons to the chosen son. Pause this audio here.

David urges and encourages Solomon to know Yahweh, David's own God and the God of his ancestors. This does not mean that Solomon had a different god. David is telling Solomon to learn to know Yahweh personally.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story of how you became friends with another person. Talk of how you met that person and how you got to know them better and better. Pay attention to the words or phrases you use to talk about how your relationship with that person grew or developed as you learnt more about them. Pause this audio here.

David tells Solomon to serve or to be Yahweh's slave with his whole heart and a willing mind. David is telling Solomon to be fully committed to Yahweh and not to be distracted. The Israelites talk of being fully committed as being whole-hearted or having a heart that is not divided.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Which organs of the body do you think of as representing being completely committed to something? Consider how you will translate this special language about Solomon's commitment to Yahweh. Pause this audio here.

David explains why Solomon should be devoted to Yahweh by telling Solomon that Yahweh searches or examines everyone's heart and knows or understands everyone's plans and thoughts. This special language means that Yahweh knows the reasons for everyone's thoughts.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell of a time when someone did something to help another person but with bad reasons or intentions and wrong thoughts. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to talk about the person's thoughts and motives. Pause this audio here.

David tells Solomon that if Solomon seeks or obeys Yahweh, Yahweh will accept Solomon. But if Solomon abandons or disobeys Yahweh, then Yahweh would reject Solomon forever. David tells Solomon that he must realize and never forget that Yahweh has chosen him to build a house or temple that would be Yahweh's sanctuary. A sanctuary is any holy place that people have set apart for the worship of Yahweh. People sometimes used sanctuary as another word for the temple of Yahweh. In that case, sanctuary can refer specifically to the Holy of Holies, the most important place in the temple. People use the word temple for the building as well as for the courtyards surrounding it, but people only use the word sanctuary for the building.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a very holy place or thing. Discuss who can enter the place or touch the thing and why. Tell of the reasons that make it holy. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to describe its holiness. Pause this audio here.

David charged Solomon to therefore be strong or firm and do the work or build the temple.

In the second scene, after telling Solomon to do the work of building the house of Yahweh, David gives Solomon the building plans for the temple. It is not clear what these plans looked like. Usually, building plans are drawings, but it is more likely that David's plans were written instructions. The storyteller interrupts the flow of the action to tell us the instructions that were in the plans. The plans had instructions for various parts of the temple. The storyteller lists these parts beginning from the outside of the temple and going to the inside.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Imagine talking to someone who has never been to your village. Describe to him in detail what your village's place of worship looks like. Begin your description from the outside of the place of worship, going in. Describe it just as someone would see it when they approach it and then go inside. Pause this audio here.

The plans David gave to Solomon had instructions on how Solomon should build the entrance of the temple. David also gave Solomon instructions for how to build the temple houses. The plans also had instructions for how to build the temple's storage rooms. The plans had instructions for the rooms that they would build on the roof of the temple. The plans also described the inner rooms of the temple and the room for the mercy seat. The room for the mercy seat or atonement cover for the ark of the covenant was the most important room in the temple. This room was called the Holy of Holies because this is where the people kept the ark of the covenant. The priests asked Yahweh to forgive the people's sins in the Holy of Holies.

Stop here and look at the drawing of Solomon's temple as a group. Point to the room called the Holy of Holies. Pause the audio here.

King David gave Solomon all the plans that had come to his mind through the Spirit of Yahweh. The Spirit of Yahweh uses people to do what Yahweh wants. The Spirit of Yahweh can speak to people directly, but he can also direct them through their own thoughts and plans. It is likely that David means that the Spirit directed David's mind when he was making plans for the temple. The Spirit directed David on how to build the courts of Yahweh's temple and the rooms around the courts. The temple had two courts or large open spaces. One was for the priest to make sacrifices, and the other court was for the people of Israel. They built rooms on the wall of the temple that surrounded the courts. The Spirit also directed David on how to build the treasuries or storerooms of Yahweh's house. The priests probably used these rooms to store the vessels or utensils that they used in their work, as well as the food items such as flour, oil, and wine. The Spirit also directed David on how to build the treasuries for the dedicated things, such as gold, silver, and other valuable items that people had offered to Yahweh. King David gave Solomon instructions about how to divide the priests and the other descendants of Levi into groups. David told Solomon to organise the priests or descendants of Aaron and their helpers, the Levites, into groups so that they could do their work in the temple in shifts.

King David also gave Solomon instructions about the vessels or utensils that the priests and Levites were to use when they did their work in the temple. The priests would use many different utensils of gold or silver in their temple work. David told Solomon how much gold or silver he should use to make each thing. The priest would use gold and silver lamps in their work. David told Solomon the weight of gold for each golden lampstand with its lamps and the weight of silver for the silver lampstands and their lamps also.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gold and silver lampstand as a group. Pause this audio here.

The weight of each golden or silver lampstand with its lamps depended on how the priests would use each lampstand. David also told Solomon the amount of gold for each table on which they would place the bread of the Presence, and also for the silver tables.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gold table as a group. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh had instructed the priest to have 12 loaves of bread, one for each tribe, before him on a table that people made of acacia wood and covered with pure gold. These loaves were called the bread of the Presence of Yahweh. We do not know how the priests used the silver tables. David also told Solomon the amount of pure or true gold that he would need to make the forks or meat hooks that the priests used to handle the meat of sacrifice.

Stop here and look at a picture of the forks as a group. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon the amount of gold and silver he would need to make the basins that the priests would use to hold the blood of the animals that they had killed for sacrifices. The priests were to sprinkle the blood of the sacrifice on the altar. David told Solomon the amount of gold that he would need to use to make the jugs and small bowls that the priests would use to pour out liquid or drink offerings.

Stop here and look at a picture of the basins, jugs, and small bowls as a group. Pause this audio here.

David gave Solomon the weight of pure gold and instructions for how to make the altar of incense. Yahweh told the priests to burn sweet-smelling incense to Yahweh every day. David also gave Solomon instructions about the golden chariot, which is the golden cherubim that spread their wings to cover the covenant box of Yahweh. The people of Israel considered the cherubim to be a chariot or vehicle on which Yahweh rode.

Stop here and look at a picture of the cherubim on the ark as a group. Pause the audio here.

King David told Solomon that he had written all the instructions that Yahweh's hand had given David. This means that Yahweh had guided David and helped David to understand all the details of how to build the house of Yahweh. It is important to remember here that Yahweh had also given Moses exact instructions for how to build the tabernacle.

In the third scene, after listing the instructions that were in the plans, the storyteller continues the flow of the action of the story. David urged Solomon to be strong and brave, and to do the work that Yahweh had given him. David told Solomon not to be afraid or discouraged because Yahweh would help Solomon. David spoke to Solomon directly and said his God, Yahweh, would help Solomon. David assured Solomon that Yahweh would not fail him or forsake him, and that Yahweh would make sure that all the work related to the temple for Yahweh was finished correctly. David had warned Solomon that if Solomon did not obey, or if he abandoned Yahweh, Yahweh would reject Solomon forever. Then David told Solomon to look and see that all the priests and Levites in their groups were ready to start their work in the temple. It is likely that David was actually pointing to the priests and Levites or their leaders, who were probably there with the rest of the leaders of Israel. David also told Solomon that many craftsmen had volunteered to help Solomon in the work to build the temple. He also told Solomon that the leaders of the people of Israel were ready to obey everything that Solomon commanded them. David had strongly commanded these leaders in the last story.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • David
  • Solomon
  • Priests and Levites
  • The leaders of Israel
  • All the skilled craftsmen
  • And all the people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, David urges Solomon to know Yahweh and to be completely committed to serving Yahweh. David tells Solomon to obey Yahweh and to build the temple. David warns Solomon that if Solomon rejects Yahweh then Yahweh will also reject Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very serious. This is very important. I pray that my son listens and obeys my words. The future of the nation of Israel depends on it; or
  • I love my son. I feel very proud of him. I think Solomon will be a good king, but only if he loves Yahweh and obeys. Yahweh is the real king of Israel. Solomon must know and understand this; or
  • O Yahweh, my God, please help my son and bless the people of Israel.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am nervous and also excited; or
  • I am happy to do the work that Yahweh has given me, but I am worried that I might make mistakes. My father has strongly warned me to be very careful to obey Yahweh; or
  • I must be committed to Yahweh as my father was committed to Yahweh. I want Yahweh to be happy with me just as he has been happy with my father. I pray that when Yahweh looks at my heart and mind, Yahweh will be happy.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, David gives Solomon the plans he had prepared to build the temple and its buildings. David also tells Solomon the weight in gold and silver that he would need to make various utensils that the priests will use in their work, and how to organise the priests and Levites for their work.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel great joy. My heart praises Yahweh. He has shown me the plans for building the temple and has allowed me to deliver them into the hands of the man who will build it. I pray that Yahweh will give Solomon the strength to do it; or
  • I have given Solomon all the plans that Yahweh gave me in front of all the leaders of Israel. Solomon must follow these plans carefully. This is a glorious day; or
  • I feel very happy and proud that my son will build Yahweh a house. I only wish that I could have seen it with my eyes and worshipped Yahweh in his temple.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Wow! These plans are amazing. Look at the detail. My father has thought about everything; or
  • Yahweh has given my father every direction for everything that will be needed. I am happy that Yahweh has done this; or
  • This will be a magnificent temple. I will need many skilled men who can follow these plans.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy. I am pleased with David. David has listened to me and done everything I told him; or
  • I will help David's son Solomon to finish the work of building the temple; or
  • Solomon is listening well, and I can see that he is serious about building the temple.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, David urges Solomon to be brave and do the work that Yahweh has given him. David tells Solomon that the priest and Levites are ready to begin their work in the temple and that the skilled craftsmen are ready and willing to help him do the work to build the temple. David also tells Solomon that the leaders of the people of Israel will obey everything that he tells them.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Everything has been prepared for Solomon to start and finish the work I have given him. Solomon has no excuse to fail; or
  • The priest and Levites, the skilled men, and the leaders of Israel will follow Solomon if he leads them; or
  • And I will be with Solomon, just as I was with Moses when he built the tabernacle.

Ask the person playing priests and Levites, the skilled craftsmen, and the leaders of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are ready to do all that Yahweh has commanded; or
  • Solomon is the new king and we will support him and obey him; or
  • We cannot wait to begin the work.

Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:
