  • I have delivered the kingdom and the responsibility of building the temple to Solomon in front of all Israel; or
  • This is a day to rejoice; or
  • I can now die in peace.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have a lot of responsibility; or
  • I am very young and inexperienced. I pray that I do not fail; or
  • O Yahweh, help me lead these people that you have given me to do the work. Give me wisdom and strength, O Yahweh.

Ask the person playing the skilled craftsmen, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and excited to begin work; or
  • I feel joy.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 28:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Listen to your translations of Exodus 25 and Exodus 27. Pay attention to the way you translated lampstands, table of the bread of Presence, forks, basins, cups, and golden bowls in those passages and translate the same in this passage. Pause this audio here

David tells Solomon to know the God of his father or David's God. David tells Solomon to be committed to the God of his father because Yahweh searches all hearts and understands every plan and thought. Use the same words or phrases for heart, Yahweh, and God as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh and God, refer to the Master Glossary.

David reminded Solomon that Yahweh had chosen Solomon to build Yahweh a house for the sanctuary and urged him to not give up, but to do it. Use the same words or phrases for house and sanctuary as you used in previous passages. For more information on "house of God," temple, or sanctuary refer to the Master Glossary.

David said that Yahweh's Spirit had given David all the plans for the temple. David just calls Yahweh's spirit "the Spirit" here, but we know that this is God's Spirit. For more information about God's Spirit, see Holy Spirit in the Master Glossary, and use the same word for Spirit, when it refers to God's Spirit, as you have in previous passages in 1 Chronicles.

Then David gave Solomon the plan or pattern for the entrance of the temple and of its houses, its treasuries, its upper rooms, its inner chambers, and the room with the mercy seat or atonement cover. The atonement cover or the mercy seat was where Yahweh removed the sins of the people of Israel and restored his relationship with his people, because this is where the high priest sprinkled the blood of the sacrifice.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for mercy seat, or atonement cover. Look up "ark of the covenant" and atonement in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David gave Solomon the plans for dividing the priests and Levites into the groups that they would do their temple work in. Use the same words or phrases for priests and Levites as you used in previous passages. For more information on priests and Levites, refer to the Master Glossary.

David told Solomon the amount of gold and silver that he would need for all the objects that the priests would use in the temple service, including the lampstands and their lamps, and the tables where they placed the bread of the Presence. The priests offered bread of the Presence to Yahweh as a symbol that Yahweh was present in the Tent of Meeting. The priests placed 12 loaves on the golden table that was in the room called the Holy Place. The priests replaced it with fresh bread each Sabbath. You should translate lamps, lampstands, and bread of the Presence as you did in previous passages, and refer to the Master Glossary for "bread of the Presence."

David told Solomon the amount of pure gold and silver that he would need to make the forks, basins, jugs, and the gold and silver for the bowls. David also told Solomon the amount of pure gold he would need for the altar of incense and for the golden chariot, the cherubim, with their wings spread over the ark of the covenant. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to God. The gift could be an animal, food items, or sweet-smelling spices. The altar of incense was a specific altar where people offered sweet-smelling spices to Yahweh. Use the same words or phrases for "ark of the covenant" and "altar of incense" as you have in previous passages, and refer to "altar" in the Master Glossary for more information.

A cherub is a type of angel, and people call more than one cherub "cherubim." Cherubim seem to especially guard sacred, or holy, places. Gold cherubim spread their wings over the top of the ark of the covenant and protected the throne where Yahweh met with his people from the top of the ark of the covenant. In the book of Ezekiel, Ezekiel sees a vision in which cherubim are carrying the throne of God. It is likely that the golden chariot is another way to refer to the cherubim that were on the ark.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for golden chariot and cherubim. Look up cherubim in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there.

David reminded Solomon that Yahweh's hand, or power, had given David all the plans he needed for the temple. Translate the idea of Yahweh's hand, or power, in the same way you have in previous passages.

David said that Yahweh would be with Solomon until the work for the service of the temple was finished. The service of the temple is the work that the Levites and priests do to keep the work of the temple going. Skilled craftsmen would help Solomon complete the work. A craftsman in this context is someone who has wisdom in how to conduct worship to Yahweh well.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for skilled craftsmen.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 29:1–9
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 29:1–9 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:1–9 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Yahweh guided King David to make plans and instructions for how the Israelites should build the temple, and King David gave these plans and instructions to Solomon. King David told Solomon in the presence of all the leaders of Israel to obey and serve Yahweh.

In this story, after King David commanded Solomon to do the work to build the temple, King David turned and spoke to all the assembly, or all the Israelites gathered there. King David had called all the leaders of the people of Israel to Jerusalem. King David told the leaders that his son Solomon was the only one that God had chosen to build Yahweh's temple. King David had many sons who could have become king after their father, David. Yahweh, the true King of Israel, had chosen Solomon to rule Israel as Yahweh's representative. King David told the people of Israel that Yahweh had given Solomon, who was young and inexperienced, the great work to build a house for Yahweh. King David is saying that Solomon had not yet learned everything that he needed to know about life and about being a king. It is not clear how old Solomon was when he became king, but it is likely that Solomon was not fully grown up. Solomon may have been around 20 years old.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the stages of life that a boy goes through in your community before people consider him a fully grown man. What are the things that he has to learn in the various stages, and at which stage would he be ready to be a leader in the community? Pause this audio here.

King David said that the palace that Solomon would build was not simply a great house for a king, but it was a house to worship Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe the kind of house that a king or the most important man in your community lives in. Then describe the house that your community builds for people to worship God. Talk about the differences between a great house for a great man to live in and a great house for people to worship God in. Pause this audio here.

Then King David told the people that because of the great work that Solomon had to do, and because Solomon was young and inexperienced, King David had done his best to help Solomon. King David said that he had provided Solomon with materials to build the temple. King David said that he had prepared gold, silver, bronze, iron, timber, precious stones and gems, stones for mosaics, and quantities of marble. The builders would need a lot of each of these materials.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell of a time when your leader successfully built a great building, especially a place of worship. Talk about the kind of building materials that they needed to complete the building and how much material they used. Discuss how the leader managed to get the people of the community involved in the work and the various ways that the people helped. For example, people could give money, land, building materials, and they could do the work. Pause this audio here.

King David also told the people that, in addition to preparing all these things for Solomon, King David had also given gold and silver from his own private wealth. King David said that he gave his own gold and silver because he was completely dedicated to the work of building the holy temple of Yahweh. King David gave 3,000 talents of gold from a place called Ophir. We are not sure where Ophir was, but it was famous for having the highest quality of pure gold. A talent of gold could weigh around 34 kilograms. So David likely gave about 100,000 kilograms of gold. David also gave 7,000 talents of refined or pure silver, which was around 230,000 kilograms. Refined silver is silver that people have made pure by heating it until they have removed all the impurities. King David said that he gave his gold and silver so that people could use it to decorate the walls of the temple houses and to make all the other objects that the skilled workers were to make. The people of Israel saw gold as a symbol of wealth, holiness, and Yahweh's glory.

King David then asked the leaders of the people of Israel which of them would follow his example and devote themselves to Yahweh by contributing to the work of the temple. When someone gave their possessions to Yahweh, they showed that they were dedicated to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about times you have given some of your possessions as a way to dedicate yourself spiritually. How did you feel when you gave? Talk about times when it was hard to give your things away, and you did not want to give your things away. Pause this audio here.

The leaders of the families of Israel, the leaders of the 12 tribes and the commanders of the units of 1,000 and 100 soldiers, together with the officials in charge of King David's business, all gave gold, silver, bronze, iron, and gems willingly. All together, they contributed to the work to build the temple, gold weighing 5,000 talents or 170,000 kilograms and 10,000 daric coins of gold. A daric was a coin that the kingdom of Persia used many years after the days of David and Solomon. It is unlikely that the people gave actual daric coins because the Persian Empire did not exist for another 420 years. It is likely that the storyteller uses the daric because he knows that the people who lived in the days that he wrote would understand the value of a daric.

Stop here and look at a picture of a daric coin as a group. Pause this audio here.

The leaders also gave 10,000 talents or 340,000 kilograms of silver, 18,000 talents or 612,000 kilograms of bronze, and 100,000 talents or 3,400,000 kilograms of iron. And those leaders who had gems or precious stones donated them to the treasury of the house of Yahweh. Those leaders gave the gems to a man called Jehiel, a Levite from the clan of Gershon. The descendants of Gershon were responsible for transporting the tabernacle's fabrics and covering. It is likely that David made Jehiel, their leader, responsible for the contributions that people gave for the temple.

The people of Israel rejoiced and celebrated because their leaders had contributed to the temple willingly. And King David was also very joyful because they had offered their valuable things to Yahweh willingly and with their whole heart.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of a time when the leaders of your community contributed to a project willingly, and people responded with great joy.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: King David told the leaders of Israel that Yahweh had chosen his young and inexperienced son Solomon. King David told the leaders about the materials that he had prepared for Solomon to build the temple.

In the second scene: Because of how committed and dedicated King David was to the work to build the temple, David contributed more material from his own private wealth. King David gave his own gold and silver to cover the walls of the temple and to make the other objects that the skilled workers would need to make.

In the third scene: King David asked the leaders if they would follow his example and also make contributions to the work of the temple. The leaders of Israel followed King David's example and also contributed willingly from their own private wealth. The leaders gave gold, money, silver, bronze, iron, and precious stones to Jehiel, the man in charge of the contributions for the temple.

In the fourth scene: The people of Israel rejoiced because their leaders gave willingly to the work of the temple of Yahweh. King David also rejoiced greatly.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • Solomon
  • All the leaders of the people of Israel: these were the leaders of the families, the leaders of the tribes, the military commanders of thousands of fighting men and hundreds of fighting men, and all the officials who were in charge of King David's business
  • The skilled workers who will build the temple and make the things that go in it
  • Jehiel the Gershonite
  • And the assembly, or the people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the previous story, King David had been speaking to Solomon in front of the leaders. David gave Solomon the plans for the temple and encouraged Solomon to do the work. When King David finishes instructing Solomon, King David turns to the whole assembly or the gathered leaders and addresses them. The storyteller begins this scene with a word that connects the last story with this one. King David speaks to the leaders directly and tells them that his son, Solomon, is still soft and tender or young and inexperienced. This does not mean that Solomon is physically weak or emotionally childlike, but that Solomon has not yet learned all the things he needs to know.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a person who received a great responsibility at an early age. How do you talk about how hard it is to have a great responsibility at a young age? Pause audio here.

King David said that although Solomon was young and inexperienced, God had chosen Solomon. In this story, King David refers to Yahweh by his personal name and also by the more general name, "God." It is important to refer to David as King David because he is speaking as a king to the people he rules. King David told the leaders that the work that Yahweh had given Solomon was great. It is likely that King David means that the work is important as well as large and difficult.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to describe the kind of work that Yahweh gave to Solomon in your language? Remember that the work was great because it was large and difficult as well as spiritually important. Pause this audio here.

King David said that the work was great because the palace that Solomon was going to build is not a palace for a man to live in, but for Yahweh, the God of Israel, to live in. King David calls the temple a palace, castle, or fortress. This usually refers to an important building from which a ruler rules a town or area. This kind of building was usually very secure against attack and was also the home of the king or ruler. It is likely that King David talks about the temple as if it were a king's palace because Yahweh is the real King of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Suggest various words that you use in your language to refer to a palace or a great house where a great man or ruler lives. This house would be protected against enemy attack. Pause this audio here.

King David told the assembly what he had done in order to help his young and inexperienced son Solomon. King David said that he did all that he was able to do to prepare materials for the building of the house of his God. King David did this to help his son Solomon, but the main reason he prepared the materials is that he loves his God, Yahweh. King David said he prepared gold, silver, bronze, iron, and wood for building. King Solomon would need all of these things to make various things for the temple. King David said that he had also provided precious stones for the work of decoration. King David provided onyx, a beautiful stone that people can find in many colors. David also provided blue-green stones called turquoise and other precious stones of different colors, as well as a large amount of marble. Marble is a beautiful type of stone that people used to make the beautiful floor of the temple.

Stop here and look at a picture of onyx, turquoise, and marble as a group. Name and describe the different types of stones that you have in your community. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, King David continued to talk to the elders. King David said that, in addition to the materials that he had already prepared, King David would now, or at that moment, contribute more from his own private wealth. King David said that he would do this because he was devoted to the house or temple of his God. David called the temple a holy temple or sanctuary. Any building becomes holy or a sanctuary when people dedicate it to honor Yahweh. A king has wealth from his position as king and also his own private wealth as a man. A king can do whatever he likes with his own private wealth, but the kingdom's wealth goes to the person who becomes king after him. King David contributed 3,000 talents of gold from Ophir. We are not sure where Ophir was, but people knew that it produced the highest quality of pure gold. A talent is a weight measurement. One talent is equal to around 34 kilograms. So King David gave around 100,000 kilograms of high-quality gold from Ophir. King David also gave refined or pure silver that weighed 7,000 talents or 230,000 kilograms. King David said that he gave these so that people could use them to cover the walls of the houses of the temple and for any other work that the skilled workers would need gold and silver to do. Yahweh had instructed them to cover the inside of the entrance room of the temple, the main room, and the walls of the Most Holy Place with gold.

In the third scene, King David asked the leaders which of them would follow his example. King David asked them if they would show their devotion to Yahweh by contributing to the work of the temple freely. Yahweh never demands free will offerings, but Yahweh receives gifts that people give joyfully. The leaders of the families of Israel, the leaders of the 12 tribes of Israel, the commanders of the military units of 1,000 and 100 soldiers each, and the officials who were in charge of King David's business, responded to King David. These people contributed freely to the work of the temple, without anyone forcing them. The leaders gave gold that weighed 5,000 talents or 165,000 kilograms, 10,000 coins of gold called darics, which is about 84 kilograms of gold, silver that weighed 10,000 talents or 330,000 kilograms, bronze that weighed 18,000 talents or 600,000 kilograms, and iron that weighed 100,000 talents or 3,300,000 kilograms. It is likely that these are the amounts of all their gifts added together. Also, those of them who had precious stones contributed them to the storehouse of the temple of Yahweh. Jehiel the Levite from the clan of Gershon was the one who was in charge of the storehouse of the temple of Yahweh.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Discuss the weight of the gifts that the leaders gave. Remember that a talent is around 34 kilograms, and a daric is a coin that people made of gold. How would you speak of these gifts in your own language using weights that you are familiar with? Pause audio here.

In the fourth scene, the people of Israel rejoiced because of the gifts that their leaders had given with their whole hearts to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about someone who gives generously and freely, as the leaders of Israel did? Take turns describing the leaders of your community who gave willingly to the project that you discussed in the last step. Pause this audio here.

And King David also rejoiced with great joy.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • Solomon
  • All the leaders of the people of Israel: these were the leaders of the families, the leaders of the tribes, the military commanders of thousands of fighting men and hundreds of fighting men, and all the officials who were in charge of King David's business
  • The skilled workers who will build the temple and make the things that go in it
  • Jehiel the Gershonite
  • And the assembly, or the people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

King David tells the leaders of Israel that Yahweh has chosen Solomon, who is young and inexperienced. King David tells the leaders about all the materials that he has prepared for Solomon to build the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel passionate. I am very committed to the work of the temple. I want everything to go well and for it to be a success; or
  • I feel worried because my son has no experience. I am really concerned that Solomon might fail; or
  • I have done everything to help Solomon. I have collected and provided all these materials that people will need. I am speaking to the leaders because I want them to be committed and help Solomon.

Ask the person playing the leaders of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are very excited by King David's message and his strong spirit; or
  • King David has done such a mighty work in providing all these materials. We love King David and will do everything that he tells us; or
  • Solomon will need our help. Yahweh has chosen Solomon. We will support Solomon.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel amazed. My father is very passionate and determined. My father's words give my heart courage; or
  • I am very nervous about the work. It is a big responsibility, and I am afraid of failing; or
  • I feel happy that my father has provided so much material for me to begin the work. I love my father, King David.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, King David tells the leaders of Israel about how committed and dedicated he is to the work of building the temple. King David therefore contributes from his own private wealth, gold, and silver to cover the walls of the temple and to make the other objects that the skilled workers will need to make.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very happy and excited; or
  • I love Yahweh, and I am eager for Yahweh's house to be built; or
  • I am ready to give generously from my wealth, which Yahweh has given me, to the work of the temple.

Ask the person playing the skilled workers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Praise Yahweh. This is a very great contribution from King David; or
  • This is the best gold and silver in the world. We are very excited to begin the work; or
  • What an amazing and beautiful temple it will be.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, King David asks the leaders if they will follow his example and also make contributions to the work of the temple. The leaders of Israel follow King David's example and also contribute willingly from their own private wealth. The leaders gave gold, money, silver, bronze, iron, and precious stones to Jehiel, the man in charge of the contributions for the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very happy. King David loves me, and the leaders of Israel also love me. They have all given generously and willingly. I know that they are devoted to me; or
  • I love the unity these men have all shown, and David, my servant, for leading them well; or
  • I am pleased with these men's hearts and minds.

Ask the person playing the leaders of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and blessed. What a day this is; or
  • We will gladly give with much joy in our hearts. We are eager to build the temple; or
  • We love King David and his son Solomon, and we love Yahweh our God and King.

Ask the person playing Jehiel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very happy. Everyone gives generously, and I, too, give what I have; or
  • People have given 165,000 kilos of gold, 330,000 kilos of silver, 600,000 kilos of bronze, 3,300,000 kilos of iron, and many gems and precious stones. I must keep very good records and store them well; or
  • I am honored to be involved in the work of the temple. What a great blessing.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, the people of Israel rejoiced because of their leaders' gifts that they, the leaders, had given freely to Yahweh. And King David also rejoiced with great joy.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is amazing. We are very happy. Our hearts are full; or
  • What a great day in Israel. What great leaders we have. They have proved their love for Yahweh and Israel; or
  • We love our leaders and praise Yahweh.

Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is the happiest day. I trust that the people will complete the temple; or
  • The leaders are devoted. The leaders will help Solomon and build Yahweh's house; or
  • Let's eat and drink and be merry. There is great joy in Israel today.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel hopeful. I am very encouraged by the devotion of the leaders; or
  • All the people of Israel are happy and excited about the temple. I must not fail; or
  • My father has been very loyal to Yahweh.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King David told all the assembly of Israel that his son Solomon, the same one that God had chosen, was young and inexperienced. An assembly of people is a special gathering of people who meet for a purpose. King David told the assembly that the task is great because the palace is not for man, but for Yahweh. Use the same words or phrases for assembly, King, God, and Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, God, and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

A palace is a large and beautiful home for a ruler or king. A palace is also where the king rules his kingdom from. King David likely calls the temple of Yahweh a palace because Yahweh is the true king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for palace. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King David said that the palace is for Yahweh God. David uses these two titles for the God of Israel to show that Yahweh is the God of Israel. There is no other god for Israel. You should use both titles—Yahweh and God—here in your translation.

King David told the leaders that he had done all that he could to provide gold, silver, bronze, iron, timber, precious stones, and gems for decorations, and a large amount of marble for the work of the temple, or the house of God. Use the same word or phrase for temple or "house of God" as you used in previous passages. For more information on the temple and "house of God," refer to the Master Glossary.

King David said that because he was dedicated to the work of the holy temple or holy house of God, he also gave gold and silver from his private wealth. We call a place that is dedicated to God "holy." The temple is holy because it is set apart for Yahweh's honor. Translate holy in the same way you have previously, and refer to holy in the Master Glossary for more information.

King David said that he was providing materials for the skilled craftsmen to use when they make the things that go inside the temple. These skilled craftsmen are men who make the things that go inside the temple, like the gold and silver bowls, jugs, utensils, tables, altars, and basins.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the skilled craftsmen who will make the objects that go inside the temple. Pause this audio here.

King David contributed gold and silver for people to decorate the walls of the temple and asked the leaders if they too would consecrate themselves by following his example and contributing freely to the work. In this story, consecrate means to dedicate yourself to Yahweh, or make yourself holy. The leaders did this by giving their wealth freely to the work of the temple. Use the same word or phrase for consecrate as you used in previous passages. Look up consecrate in the Master Glossary for more information.

The leaders, chiefs, or heads of the families or clans, and of the tribes of Israel, the commanders of thousands and of hundreds of soldiers, and the officials in charge of the king's work, gave willingly. Use the same words or phrases for the leaders, the chiefs of families or fathers' houses, the leaders of tribes, and the military commanders as you used in previous passages.

These people gave many talents of gold, silver, bronze, and iron. A talent is a measurement of weight. One talent is around 34 kilograms. You can calculate the weights into kilograms and use kilograms instead of talents if the word talent would cause confusion. Use the same word or phrase for talent as you have used previously, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about talent. The people also gave 10,000 darics. You may say 10,000 gold coins if the word daric would cause confusion.

Stop here and discuss again how you will translate the weight of the gold, silver, bronze, and iron that David and the leaders gave. Pause this audio here.

The leaders who had precious stones gave them to Jehiel the Gershonite. Jehiel was in charge of the treasury of the house of Yahweh. A treasury is a storeroom for valuable things. Use the same word or phrase for treasury as you used in previous passages in 1 Chronicles.

The people of Israel rejoiced because of the gifts that their leaders had given with their "whole heart" to Yahweh.

Stop here and decide as a group how you will translate that the leaders gave with their whole heart to Yahweh. If you use the word heart in your translation, use the same word or phrase as you have used previously, and refer to heart in the Master Glossary for more information.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 29:10–20
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 29:10–20 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:10–20 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:10–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King David and the leaders of the people of Israel rejoiced greatly because they had given generously to the work of the temple.

In this story, King David prayed. In his prayer, David praised and gave thanks to Yahweh because the leaders of the people of Israel had given willingly. King David felt that Yahweh had caused the leaders to give generously to the work of the temple. King David spoke to Yahweh directly in the presence of all the leaders of Israel. King David blessed or gave thanks and praise to Yahweh. Usually, it is Yahweh who blesses or does good things to people because Yahweh is greater than people. When people bless Yahweh, they are praising or worshipping Yahweh. David called Yahweh the God of Israel, or the God of Jacob. Jacob, whom Yahweh renamed Israel, was the ancestor of the people of Israel. King David said that all the praise, from eternity to eternity, belongs to Yahweh. King David said "eternity to eternity" because Yahweh has no beginning or end. Yahweh was there before creation and will be there when creation ends.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of a time when a leader of your community has spoken to God to say thank you or to worship and praise God. How did the leader speak, and what was the reason that led him to do this? Pause this audio here.

King David praised Yahweh by saying that all the greatness and power belonged to Yahweh. David was saying that there is no one who is greater or more powerful than Yahweh. King David also said that Yahweh is glorious or beautiful, victorious and majestic. King David was speaking about how powerful Yahweh is. People honor Yahweh as king, and Yahweh has the highest status. King David said that everything in heaven and on earth belongs to Yahweh because Yahweh rules over everything. Yahweh is the ruler of everything because Yahweh created everything in heaven and on earth. The kingdom of Israel belongs to Yahweh. Yahweh created it and was its true King. The Israelites worship one God, Yahweh, who is in charge of everything.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the greatest God, spirit, or important person in the history of your culture. Describe what makes them better than all the rest and how no one can compare them to any other thing. Pause this audio here.

King David thanked Yahweh, the ruler of all things, because Yahweh gives wealth and honor. King David is likely thinking about the wealth that the people have given for the temple and the honor of being the people that Yahweh chose to build Yahweh a house on earth. David also said that Yahweh has strength and power and is able to give people a higher status and to give people strength. It was Yahweh who had lifted the people of Israel from a few men to a large nation and from being slaves to being free. Yahweh had also lifted David from being a shepherd boy to become the King of Israel. King David said that they, the people of Israel, were thankful to Yahweh their God, and that they praised Yahweh's glorious or beautiful name. In Israel, people's names reflected their character. So to praise Yahweh's glorious name is the same as to praise Yahweh for who Yahweh is.

King David praised Yahweh for being great and then compared that greatness to how small David and Israel were. King David asked who he and his people, Israel, were that they were able to give generously to the work of the temple. Yahweh owns everything and does not need anything from anyone. King David and the people had brought their gifts and had given generously. But David and the people had only given back what Yahweh had given them.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of how you or a person you know gave to someone else in need, and later on, the one who was in need came back and helped the very person who had helped him or her. Pause this audio here.

King David said that they were sojourners and strangers in the sight or in the presence of Yahweh, just as their ancestors had been. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the ancestors of Israel, lived in the land and moved from place to place while they lived in tents. These people lived as if they did not own anything and had no security in the land. The land of Israel, as well as the world, belongs to Yahweh. Yahweh had given the children of Israel the right to live in the land of Israel and to enjoy its benefits, but the land really belonged to Yahweh. Yahweh told these people to live their lives as pilgrims or people on a journey. King David said that the days of the people of Israel are like a shadow, without hope. David was saying that people's days on the earth end quickly, just like the amount of time a traveller spends in a single place.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a place that you visited for a short time. Describe some of the things that you enjoyed at that place, such as the food or the weather. Talk about how long you were able to enjoy them and how you felt when you had to leave. Pause this audio here.

King David praised Yahweh for all the things that they had been able to give in large quantities because it all belonged to Yahweh.

King David said that he knew that Yahweh tests or sees every person's heart and that Yahweh is happy when people do what is right with a sincere heart. King David told Yahweh that he had given all his offerings willingly and with an honest heart or purpose. David said that he had also joyfully watched as the leaders of Israel contributed willingly. King David asked Yahweh, the God of the ancestors of Israel, to keep the people of Israel's hearts honest and sincere. King David is likely asking that Yahweh would help the people of Israel to serve Yahweh with sincerity or to obey Yahweh as they build the temple and also after they finish building the temple. Yahweh is interested in people's hearts. If the people build the temple but disobey Yahweh's commandments, Yahweh would not be pleased. King David also asked Yahweh to give Solomon a heart that is dedicated to obeying Yahweh's rules and instructions. King David asked Yahweh to enable Solomon to build the palace for Yahweh, or temple, that King David had prepared materials for.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss when a great leader has come to the end of their life or term before they have completed a great work. Who did that leader leave the work to and how did he tell others what he wanted them to do to continue the work that he started? Pause this audio here.

Then King David told all the people gathered there to praise Yahweh their God. So all the people gathered there praised or worshiped Yahweh by bowing their heads in respect and laying down with their faces to the floor to honor Yahweh and King David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the actions you do to show deep respect, admiration, and worship in your culture.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:10–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: First King David publicly praised Yahweh for who Yahweh is.

In the second scene: King David praised and gave thanks to Yahweh because Yahweh helped the people to give generously. King David also told Yahweh how small and unimportant King David was compared to Yahweh. Then King David asked Yahweh to help the people and Solomon to be obedient and to build the temple.

In the third scene: King David told all the people gathered there to praise Yahweh their God. So all the people gathered there praised or worshiped Yahweh by bowing their heads in respect and lying down with their faces to the floor to honor Yahweh and King David.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • Solomon
  • The assembly, or the people of Israel
  • And, the fathers of the people of Israel: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the previous story, the leaders of the people of Israel had given generously to the work of the temple. In this story, King David responds to their generous giving by giving praise to Yahweh. The first scene begins with a word that shows that David blessed Yahweh as a result of the leader's willingness to give. By blessing Yahweh, David is declaring that Yahweh is the source of all the generous gifts that David and the leaders of Israel have given. David blessed Yahweh in the eyes of all the assembly. This is special language to say that David prayed publicly or in front of all the people gathered there.

David's prayer is special, formal language that he uses when he prays publicly in celebrations. David praises Yahweh and talks about Yahweh's greatness and power.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about how your religious leaders or other leaders pray publicly. How is their prayer different from just praying at home alone or in a small group? What kinds of special language do you use in your culture when you pray publicly on formal occasions? Pause this audio here.

David spoke directly to Yahweh and said, "We praise you, Yahweh, God of our father Israel, from forever to forever." Israel is another name for Jacob, the ancestor of the Israelites. David told Yahweh that everything belonged to Yahweh. David says that Yahweh is the greatest and most powerful being. No one can match Yahweh's glory, majesty, and beauty. David also says Yahweh is a strong and victorious king with everlasting honor.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Choose a person to speak publicly as if he were addressing Yahweh. Let the person speak the words of David out loud while the rest of the group listens. The person may address Yahweh in direct speech. For example, he might say, "You, Yahweh, are powerful." Or, he might use indirect speech. For example, he might say, "To Yahweh belongs power." The rest of the people in the group should listen and decide the best way to communicate David's prayer. If they have other ways to communicate what David is saying, they should suggest those ways. Try out all suggestions until you have agreed on the best one. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, David praises Yahweh for helping the people to give generously. King David begins by asking Yahweh a question that he does not expect anyone to answer. David asks Yahweh who David and the people of Israel are that they were able to give so generously. Because everything in heaven and on earth belongs to Yahweh, David is saying that he and the people of Israel are not worthy to give to Yahweh. David said that the kingdom also belongs to Yahweh and that Yahweh is ruler over everything. It is likely that the kingdom that David is talking about is the kingdom of Israel. If so, David means that Yahweh is the true king and ruler of Israel. David continued praying and said that Yahweh gives riches and honor or respect to anyone whom Yahweh chooses because Yahweh is the ruler of everything. Yahweh had lifted David from being a shepherd boy to being King of Israel and had given David and the leaders of Israel their wealth. David says Yahweh is strong and powerful. David talks of strength and power as being in Yahweh's hand because it is Yahweh who lifts people up and makes them strong. David has been saying that the praise belongs to Yahweh because Yahweh is unique and no one compares to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss one or two great things that have happened to your community in its history. Who did the people think caused the great things to happen? How did the people credit the thing that had happened to them? Focus on events where the people felt that a spiritual being like God caused the events, and explain how people recognised that the spiritual being was responsible. Pause this audio here.

David then gives thanks to Yahweh and says that all the people there thank Yahweh and praise Yahweh's glorious or awesome name. David speaks for all the people of Israel. David gives the reason to thank Yahweh. David says that they, David and the people of Israel, are small and not special. David says that they, David and the leaders, were only able to give so generously because Yahweh, who owns everything, had first given them their wealth. David says that all the people of Israel are sojourners and strangers in Yahweh's sight or presence. Sojourners are people who are foreigners in a country and temporarily live there, even though it is not where they were born. A person is a stranger in a country when they are in a country that is not where they were born. David is saying that the people of Israel are just like their ancestors, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Yahweh owned the land, and the people lived in it and enjoyed it because Yahweh allowed them to do so.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about other communities that pass through your territory and stay only for a short time, such as communities that follow their animals around the country. Talk about those communities' relationship to your territory. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to refer to these communities' way of life. Pause this audio here.

David says that the days of the people of Israel on the earth are like a shadow. David means that their time on earth is short and passes away quickly, so that the people have no real hope of continuing in the land.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Get a torch or light and shine it above an object to make a shadow. Draw a small area on the ground below the object to represent the land of Israel. Then have the person who holds the torch move it as if it were the sun. Watch how the shadow passes through the land. Discuss the length of a person's life. Pay attention to the words, phrases, and special language you use to refer to how short it is. Pause this audio here.

For this reason, David tells Yahweh that all the wealth that they, David and the leaders, have provided to build the temple, came from Yahweh and belongs to Yahweh. David says that the temple is for Yahweh's holy name. The fact that Yahweh's name is holy refers to Yahweh's character. Yahweh is completely good, pure, powerful, and majestic. Yahweh is whole, complete, and unchangeable. But most of all, it means that Yahweh is Yahweh.

Then David prays for the people of Israel and for Solomon. David tells Yahweh that he knows that Yahweh tests or examines people's hearts and that Yahweh is happy when people are honest. Yahweh cares about what we do and why we do it. Yahweh knows everything, even our thoughts and feelings. David tells Yahweh that he has given to the temple with an honest heart and is happy to see the people of Israel giving willingly too. David asks Yahweh to keep this desire in the Israelites' hearts forever. David wants the Israelites to obey Yahweh in everything, not just in building the temple. David prays for the people of Israel to stay loyal to Yahweh. David calls Yahweh the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the Israelites' ancestors. David also prays for his son Solomon and asks Yahweh to give Solomon a committed heart to follow Yahweh's rules. David wants Yahweh to help Solomon build the beautiful temple. David prays for the hearts of the people and Solomon because the temple would mean nothing if they did not honor Yahweh with honesty and sincerity. The people of Israel believed that the heart was the place where people's intentions come from. Doing something with a pure heart, therefore, meant doing it with pure intentions.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of people who have done great things for your community, especially things that cost them greatly. Talk about the intentions of the people's hearts and minds and the reasons why they were willing to pay a high price for your community. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to describe their desires and willingness. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, David turned to all the people gathered there and told them to praise Yahweh their God. So the people did so. The people praised Yahweh, the God of their ancestors. The people bowed low and lay down with their faces on the ground to honor Yahweh and David, the king.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have a person play a great king. The rest of the people should honor that person by bowing low to him and lying down with their faces on the ground before him. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:10–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • Solomon
  • The assembly, or the people of Israel
  • And, the fathers of the people of Israel: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, King David praised Yahweh publicly because of who Yahweh is.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy. My heart is very glad. I rejoice because Yahweh has caused all of this great generosity; or
  • I want to sing and dance and praise our great God, Yahweh. I love Yahweh; or
  • No one compares to Yahweh. Yahweh is awesome, full of power and glory. Yahweh has made this day happen, and we are all happier than we have ever been.

Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are very joyful. Our hearts are full of happiness. Yahweh has blessed us greatly; or
  • Our king is happy, and we are happy with him. Truly, David is right. Yahweh owns everything; or
  • We feel very honored to be the people Yahweh has chosen to build the temple.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Pleased with my people. My people honor me; or
  • The people are right to praise me. Their praise is a pleasing offering to me; or
  • I alone am God, and my people recognise this.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, King David praised Yahweh by giving thanks to Yahweh for helping the people to give generously.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very thankful. I want the whole world to hear me thanking Yahweh, my God; or
  • It is Yahweh who has given us all these gifts that we have given to the work of the temple. There is great joy in Israel today; or
  • What a blessed people we are. Yahweh loves us. My heart is full of praise for Yahweh.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel respected and honored; or
  • David pleases me because he recognises that without me, he is nothing; or
  • My name is glorified, and I am happy.

Continue the drama.

King David prayed for the people and Solomon to be obedient and to build the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so happy. What a wonderful and glorious day this is; or
  • I feel humbled by the work ahead of me and by the greatness of my father, David; or
  • I feel like David loves me very much. David prays good things for me, and I pray that Yahweh will help me and give me a heart like David's so that I may serve Yahweh as David has.

Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Amen! Amen! O Yahweh, hear our king's prayer; or
  • We love Yahweh and want to love Yahweh forever; or
  • May Yahweh bless Solomon so that we may live in happy days like today.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, King David told all the people gathered there to praise Yahweh their God. So all the people gathered there praised or worshiped Yahweh by bowing their heads in respect and lying down with their faces to the floor to honor Yahweh and King David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Our hearts are full of songs of honor to Yahweh. We feel very blessed and favored to be here today; or
  • We are dancing and rejoicing because of all that has taken place. We thank Yahweh; or
  • We are very excited about the future. We worship and praise Yahweh. We also respect David for being a great king and leader.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel great joy and happiness; or
  • I want to serve Yahweh forever and do all that Yahweh asks; or
  • I love Yahweh, and I love my father David and all the people of Israel.

Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I have seen this day. My heart is at rest because I know that I have done all I could to prepare my son and the people of Israel to build the temple and to serve Yahweh; or
  • I feel peace and joy. I am full of hope for Solomon's reign; or
  • Yahweh has loved me and blessed me.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel loved and honoured; or
  • The people worship me, and they raise their voices in adoration to me. I am happy; or
  • I will bless the people and help them because I love them.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:10–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

David blessed Yahweh in the presence of the whole assembly, or the people gathered there**.** Usually, it is Yahweh who blesses people. When people bless Yahweh, it means they are worshiping Yahweh and thanking Yahweh for his goodness and his blessings. To bless is another way of saying to praise. Use the same word or phrase for bless, Yahweh, and assembly as you used in previous passages. For more information on bless and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

David called Yahweh the "God of our father Israel." Yahweh changed the name of the father of the 12 tribes from Jacob to Israel. Use the same word or phrase for God and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on God or Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

David said that the glory belonged to Yahweh because everything in heaven and earth belonged to Yahweh. David said that the kingdom belonged to Yahweh and Yahweh was head or ruler of all. Use the same words or phrases for glory, heaven, and kingdom as you used in previous passages. For more information on glory, heaven, or kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary.

David gives God thanks, and David praises God. Use the same word or phrase for praise as you have in previous passages, and remember that praise is in the Master Glossary.

David thanked Yahweh and said that all the abundant gifts that David had given for building a temple, or house, for Yahweh's holy name came from Yahweh. David uses the word for "house" and the word for "palace" when he talks about the temple in this passage. You may just use the word for temple throughout if you would like, or the word for palace that you used in the previous passage. If you use the word for temple, use the same words or phrases for temple and holy as you used in previous passages. For more information on temple or holy, refer to the Master Glossary.

David said that the people of Israel were sojourners and strangers before Yahweh. These two words have a similar meaning. A person is a sojourner in a country when they temporarily live in a country where they were not born, and perhaps move from place to place. Strangers are people who are foreigners in a country where they were not born.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sojourners and strangers. Look up sojourner in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David said that Yahweh is pleased with the integrity or uprightness of people's hearts. Integrity refers to how a person behaves and their motives or intentions. A person with integrity behaves in a way that honours Yahweh. That person obeys Yahweh's commandments. That person is complete or the same person with his actions, his thoughts, and his emotions. That person is sincere, strong, and honest. Integrity is also called "uprightness of heart."

Stop here and discuss what word or phrase you will use for integrity. If you use the word for heart in your description of integrity, then use the same word for heart as you have previously, and remember that heart is in the Master Glossary. If you have already translated integrity, or heart, in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David prayed that Yahweh would help Solomon to be committed to keep Yahweh's commands, testimony, and statutes wholeheartedly**.** Use the same words or phrases for wholeheartedly, commandments, and statutes as you used in previous passages. For more information on commandments and statutes, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh gave Moses the 10 commandments that he wrote on stone tablets. The people called these stone tablets the testimony. Therefore, "testimony of Yahweh" is another way to say the law of Yahweh. This law proved that God and the people of Israel had a covenant, or a special relationship.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for testimony. Look up testimony in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David told the people of Israel to praise Yahweh their God, and they bowed and lay on the ground to honor Yahweh and the king. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. When a person bows down before someone else, they lower their head or the whole top of their body forward. The person does this to show respect and humility. A person can also bow down by kneeling in front of a person and putting their head to the ground. Sometimes people even stretched themselves out flat on the ground in front of a person. This is called making yourself fall at someone's feet. Use the same word or phrase for bow down as you have used in previous passages, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about bow.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 29:21–25
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 29:21–25 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:21–25 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:21–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King David prayed and praised Yahweh for the generous gifts that the leaders of Israel had willingly given to the work of the temple.

This story takes place the day after that. The next day, the people of Israel offered sacrifices to Yahweh. The people offered burnt sacrifices and peace or fellowship sacrifices. In a burnt sacrifice, they killed, cut up, and then burned the whole animal completely on the altar. A person would offer a burnt offering to dedicate himself to Yahweh completely. In a fellowship or peace offering, the priests burned some parts of the animal on the altar and took some for themselves to eat. The priest also gave the person who offered the sacrifice the rest of the meat. The peace or fellowship offering represented sharing a meal with Yahweh, and so it showed that the person who offered the offering was in a good relationship with Yahweh. The people of Israel altogether offered 1,000 bulls or male cows, 1,000 rams or male sheep, and 1,000 male lambs. A lamb is a young sheep, less than one year old, whereas a ram is a sheep above one year old. The Israelites offered these animals with their specific drink offerings. Yahweh had instructed the Israelites in the days of Moses that they had to offer specific amounts of wine together with their animal offerings. The amount of wine the people should offer was related to the size of the animal they offered as well as to the importance of the type of offering. The drink offering showed that the person who offered it was completely surrendering and committing himself to serve Yahweh, just like the priest completely poured out to Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bull, a ram, and a lamb as a group. Discuss this question as a group: What kind of animal and drink sacrifices do your people make or used to make, and what does each sacrifice represent? Describe the amount or quality of the sacrifice for different types of sacrifice. Make sure you identify the relationship between the sacrifice and the life of the person who offered it. Pause this audio here.

Although the storyteller does not tell us, it is likely that the priests are the ones who actually burnt the sacrifices on the altar. The number of peace sacrifices that they offered was enough to feed all the people of Israel who were there. On that day, the people of Israel ate and drank in the presence of Yahweh with great joy. It is likely that the people made the offerings on Mount Moriah, on the land David had bought and where David had already built an altar to Yahweh. This is where Solomon would later build the temple. This great feast in the presence of Yahweh showed the Israelites' commitment, togetherness, and fellowship with Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What sacrifices or rituals do your people celebrate together in the presence of God, spirits, or ancestors? Talk about a time you shared a meal with God, the spirits or the ancestors. Explain why and how you did this. Pause audio here.

You will remember that David had already made Solomon king privately. The people of Israel confirmed or proclaimed David's son, Solomon, king a second time on that day. The people of Israel publicly anointed or poured oil on Solomon's head to show that he was their king. The people anointed Solomon as king in the presence of Yahweh or before Yahweh. The people also anointed Zadok as Yahweh's priest. Before this, Zadok and Abiathar had been important priests together. Zadok was from the family line of Phinehas, and Abiathar was from the family line of Eli. Yahweh had previously promised to choose Phinehas' descendants as high priest and to reject Eli's descendants. Therefore, it is likely that the Israelites anointed Zadok as the high priest or the most important priest. When people anointed a person, they showed that Yahweh's spirit was coming on the person because Yahweh had chosen the person for a special task. People anointed other people, objects, and buildings that were set apart for Yahweh with oil.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss how you dedicate people to serve God or spirits in your community. Tell a story of a time when your community anointed, recognised, proclaimed, or crowned a great leader. Pause this audio here.

So Solomon became King in his father David's place. David probably saw Solomon become king before David died. The storyteller says that Solomon sat on the throne of Yahweh. The throne of the king of Israel is called the throne of Yahweh because Yahweh was the true king of Israel. Solomon ruled as Yahweh's representative. The people of Israel obeyed Solomon, their king, and Solomon became successful. All the leaders of Israel and the mighty men, as well as the rest of David's sons, who were all of Solomon's brothers, promised to obey Solomon. The storyteller says that the leaders gave their hand or power to Solomon. It is likely that the storyteller is telling us that the leaders of Israel served Solomon, and no one challenged Solomon for the right to be king. Solomon's brothers would be the people most likely to challenge Solomon to become king of Israel, so it was very important that they all promised to obey Solomon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell two stories from your community's history. Tell one story of a great, successful leader that everybody obeyed. Tell also of another leader who some people challenged for the right to be their leader. Suggest the reasons why this happened to them. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh exalted or made Solomon great in the eyes of all Israel. The people of Israel all saw how Yahweh blessed Solomon and made him a great king. Yahweh gave Solomon great wealth, honor, or reputation and power, more than any other king of Israel had before Solomon.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:21–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The day after King David praised Yahweh and prayed for Solomon, the people of Israel offered sacrifices to Yahweh. The people offered burnt offerings and other offerings together with all their drink offerings. The people ate and drank with great joy in the presence of Yahweh.

In the second scene: The people of Israel anointed Solomon as king a second time. The people also anointed Zadok as high priest. All the mighty men and leaders, as well as King David's other sons, promised to be loyal to King Solomon.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells us that Yahweh made Solomon great before all the people of Israel. Yahweh gave Solomon more wealth, honor or reputation, and power than Yahweh had given any other king of Israel before Solomon.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David, Solomon's father
  • Solomon, King David's son
  • Zadok
  • The officers of the king
  • The mighty men
  • King David's other sons
  • The people of Israel
  • And, although the storyteller does not mention them, the priests who offered the sacrifices that the people of Israel brought were probably there as well

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the previous story, the people of Israel praised and worshipped Yahweh after King David had praised Yahweh and prayed for them and for Solomon.

This story takes place the following day. The storyteller says that "they," or the same people who had praised Yahweh in the previous story, offered sacrifices to Yahweh. These are the people of Israel who had gathered in Jerusalem. In the previous story, the people of Israel had worshipped Yahweh with their voices. In this story, the people continue to worship Yahweh by offering animal sacrifices. The people of Israel had several different kinds of sacrifices that they usually offered to Yahweh. In this story, the people brought more than one type of sacrifice. This great event in the history of Israel, when the people anointed King Solomon, took place over two days. This event would have involved a lot of work and preparation.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell of a great spiritual event that people celebrated to honor your community's spiritual being or beings. Discuss how long the event was, including the planning and the people involved. Pause this audio here.

Although King David was very old and was going to die soon, it is very likely that David was present at this event for Solomon as well.

The people of Israel offered or brought to Yahweh 1,000 bulls or male cows, 1,000 rams or male sheep, and 1,000 lambs or young male sheep as burnt offerings. Although the storyteller does not mention the priests, it is likely that the people brought the sacrifices to the priest because the priests were the only ones who Yahweh allowed to do the work of offering sacrifices to Yahweh. The people of Israel brought all their animal offerings with their specific drink or wine offerings. You will remember that Yahweh had commanded the people of Israel to bring a specific drink offering with each animal sacrifice. The people offered burnt offerings and drink offerings to show that they were dedicated to Yahweh completely. The people did not eat any of the meat of the burnt offering. It is likely that the people were dedicating themselves to the work of building the temple.

Stop here and look again at the pictures of the bull, the ram and the lamb as a group. Pause this audio here.

The people of Israel also brought a great number of other sacrifices. It is likely that these other sacrifices were fellowship or peace offerings because the priests did not completely burn these other offerings on the altar. The priests gave some of the meat to the people to eat. Unlike with the burnt offerings, we do not know the number of peace offerings that the people brought. We are only told that the number of these sacrifices was enough to feed all the people of Israel who were there. The people of Israel ate and drank with great joy in the presence of Yahweh that day. The people of Israel celebrated the anointing of their new king, Solomon, by eating and drinking together with Yahweh. Yahweh was not there in person. The storyteller likely means that they ate and drank near Yahweh's altar or that, although they could not see Yahweh, Yahweh was fellowshipping with the people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell of a time when your people have fellowshipped with God or spirits. Explain how people did this. Pay attention to the words you use to indicate that God or the spirits were present. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the people of Israel anointed Solomon as king. The people of Israel also anointed Zadok as priest. People poured sweet-smelling oil over someone's head to anoint them. This ritual showed that Yahweh had chosen this person for a special purpose and gave that person special authority.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk of a great celebration in the history of your people when the people acknowledged a leader before the spiritual being of your community. Explain the ritual that the people used to do this. Pay attention to the words that you use to describe the ritual that declared the person a leader. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that the people anointed Solomon king a second time. David had declared Solomon king before this day but that was probably in private. People had anointed both King Saul and King David twice, once in private and another time in public.
