It is not clear why the people anointed Zadok together with Solomon, because Zadok had already been serving as a priest. It is likely that the people anointed Zadok to serve as high priest on this day. Yahweh had given Solomon a holy task: to build the temple. It might be that Zadok, the chief priest, is included in the anointing to remind us of the holy nature of Solomon's kingship. Solomon would build the temple where Zadok would lead the people of Israel in worshipping Yahweh. The people anointed Solomon and Zadok before Yahweh. This means that Yahweh was a witness.

Therefore, Solomon ruled on the throne of Yahweh after his father, David, died. Yahweh was the true king of Israel, so King Solomon ruled as Yahweh's representative. King Solomon was successful, and all the people of Israel obeyed Solomon. The storyteller is likely summarising the entire reign of Solomon here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Return to the previous discussion. Have someone retell the events of the day that the people acknowledged the leader publicly. The person should then summarise the successes of that leader's life and rule after they tell of the events of the day that the people chose that leader. Pause this audio here.

All the leaders of Israel and the mighty men, as well as the rest of King David's sons, who were Solomon's brothers, promised that they would be loyal to Solomon. The mighty men might have been military leaders, or just important and influential men or leading citizens. The storyteller says that the men "put their hand under" Solomon. This is special language to tell us that the men accepted the authority of Solomon. The men may also have literally put their hands under Solomon, but the important thing is that they promised not to challenge Solomon's rule.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have a person swear his loyalty to another person from the group. If possible, the person should act out the ritual or ceremony that you would do in your culture on such occasions. Whilst this is happening, another person should narrate out loud what is happening. The narrator should use the special language that you have in your language for this ritual or ceremony. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller summarises what Yahweh did for King Solomon in all the years that he ruled. Yahweh exalted or made Solomon great in the eyes of all the people of Israel. This means that all the people of Israel saw how Yahweh made King Solomon great. Yahweh gave Solomon more kingly glory than all the kings that had ruled Israel before Solomon. It is likely that this mainly refers to Solomon's wealth and honor.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the leader from your community to whom God gave the most blessings of any other leader. What did this leader have that people could see and know that he was the leader whom God had most blessed? Pay attention to the words and phrases to describe how God blessed the leader. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:21–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David, Solomon's father
  • Solomon, King David's son
  • Zadok
  • The officers of the king
  • The mighty men
  • King David's other sons
  • The people of Israel
  • And, although the storyteller does not mention them, the priests who offered the sacrifices that the people of Israel brought were probably there as well

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The people of Israel made sacrifices to Yahweh. The people offered burnt sacrifices and other sacrifices, together with all their drink offerings, and ate and drank with great joy in the presence of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel great joy and happiness. This is a wonderful day of celebration and dedication; or
  • All of Israel sings and dances as we praise Yahweh for giving us a new king; or
  • We feel generous. Solomon will build the temple. We bring to Yahweh all the best of our animals to honor and praise Yahweh. The people of Israel rejoice and fellowship with Yahweh.

Ask the person playing the priests, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel joy and wonder at the goodness of Yahweh. We love Yahweh and King Solomon; or
  • We are blessed to offer these sacrifices to Yahweh for the people; or
  • We feel tired but happy. We are excited for people to build the temple.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and well pleased; or
  • I am satisfied and pleased with the sacrifices of my people; or
  • I love being in my people's presence.

Continue the drama.

The people of Israel anointed Solomon as king a second time. The people also anointed Zadok as high priest. All the mighty men and leaders, as well as King David's other sons, promised to be loyal to King Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very happy, honoured, and blessed by Yahweh; or
  • People feel great joy in Israel, and people have huge expectations of me. I am nervous; or
  • I am now king of Israel. The responsibility is heavy on me.

Ask the person playing Zadok, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Praise be to Yahweh for this day; or
  • I have the job of standing before Yahweh on behalf of the people in the temple; or
  • I will support Solomon in his task of building the temple. I am happy that Yahweh will have a house in the midst of Yahweh's people

Ask the person playing the officers, mighty men, and King David's other sons, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy, honored, and full of excitement. Solomon is king. We will obey Solomon; or
  • Yahweh has chosen Solomon, and we will listen to Solomon and support him in every way. Solomon is the king of Israel; or
  • The people have made our brother Solomon king publicly. We must show our support and promise to obey Solomon. But some of us are not happy. Several of us would be much better kings than Solomon. Why has our father chosen Solomon?

Continue the drama.

Yahweh made Solomon great before all the people of Israel. Yahweh gave Solomon more wealth, honor or reputation, and power than Yahweh had given any other king of Israel before Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel amazed. Solomon is the wealthiest man who has ever lived; or
  • Solomon is the most glorious king we have ever seen. Yahweh has blessed Solomon even more than David. Yahweh must love Solomon very much, and we love him too; or
  • We feel very happy to live under such a successful king as King Solomon.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and generous; or
  • I love Solomon. Solomon loves me and obeys me. Solomon will build my house as I asked; or
  • I will also rejoice to bless Solomon and make him very great.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:21–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The next day, the people of Israel made sacrifices to Yahweh. The people brought many male bulls, rams, and lambs as burnt offerings together with their drink offerings. Use the same words or phrases for the people of Israel, sacrifices, Yahweh, burnt offerings, and drink offerings as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, sacrifices, Yahweh, burnt offerings, and drink offerings, refer to the Master Glossary.

Use the same words or phrases for bulls, rams, and male lambs as you used in previous passages.

The people anointed Solomon as king and Zadok as priest in the presence of Yahweh that day. Use the same words or phrases for anointed, king, and priest as you used in previous passages. For more information on anoint, king, and priest, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon sat as king on the throne of Yahweh after his father, David, died. All the officers or leaders and mighty men or important men, as well as all of King David's sons, promised to obey King Solomon. Use the same words or phrases for throne, officers and mighty men as you used in previous passages. Remember that a throne symbolizes the authority and rule of a king.

Yahweh made Solomon great in the presence of all Israel and gave Solomon more glory than any other king of Israel before him. Use the same word or phrase for glory as you used in previous passages. For more information on glory, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Chronicles 29:26–30
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Chronicles 29:26–30 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:26–30 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:26–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the storyteller finishes telling the story of David. In the previous passage, the storyteller told us about the end of David's reign and how Solomon became king after David. Now, the storyteller gives us a summary of David's reign. The storyteller uses earlier stories told by other storytellers in Deuteronomy and Kings to help form this story.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If possible, listen to your translation of 1 Kings 2:10–12, where the storyteller of 1 Kings gives a summary of David's reign. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller reminds us that David was the son of Jesse. Jesse, David's father, was from the tribe of Judah, but Jesse was not a king. David was the king of all of Israel. The storyteller highlights that the kingdom of Israel was united when David reigned over Israel. The storyteller tells us that David reigned for 40 years in total. David reigned for seven years in Hebron. You may remember that in 2 Samuel, the storyteller of that book tells us that David reigned in Hebron for seven years and six months. We do not know why this storyteller in 1 Chronicles tells us seven years. This storyteller does not say that David's reign in Hebron was only over Judah because the storyteller does not want to remind people that Israel was divided into two kingdoms at the beginning of David's reign. David reigned over all of Israel for 33 years. When David was king over all of Israel, David lived in Jerusalem. David reigned over the kingdom of Israel from Jerusalem.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have somebody tell a story of a well-known leader of your country or people. Include how long that person was a leader for and where they lived while they were the leader. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller of 1 Chronicles probably used the book of 2 Samuel to help write the book of 1 Chronicles. The storyteller of this passage probably took the length of David's reign over Israel from the book of 2 Samuel. You may remember that at the time of the storyteller of 2 Samuel, the Israelites thought that 40 years represented the time it took for a complete generation of people to happen. We do not know if the storyteller of 2 Samuel gave us the actual time that David ruled Judah and Israel, or if the storyteller used the ideal numbers to make David seem better to the people who heard the story.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What things do you say about great leaders? When you describe your leaders, what kinds of details about the leaders' lives do you tell people? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that David died at a good old age. David lived a long life and was old when he died. David died after enjoying his long life, wealth, and honor, or the respect that people gave to David. All of these good things came from Yahweh and were Yahweh's blessing on David because David was faithful to Yahweh and kept the covenant that David swore to the people of Israel. The storyteller tells us that David's son Solomon succeeded David as king. Solomon was the next king of Israel after David. David was still alive when Solomon became king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time in your country when the most important leader in your country stopped leading, even though he was still alive, and a new person became the most important leader. Why did the most important leader stop leading your people? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller ends the passage by telling us that other people wrote in other books all the events or things that happened to David during King David's reign. The storyteller points us to the records, chronicles, or stories of Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer. All of these men were alive during David's life. Samuel heard from Yahweh and told the people of Israel that David should be the king of Israel. Nathan and Gad prophesied to David during David's reign. You will remember from earlier passages that a seer and a prophet are very similar to each other. A seer and a prophet both receive a message from God and see something about God that other people cannot see. A prophet speaks on behalf of God. Storytellers completed the records of Samuel, Nathan, and Gad many years before this storyteller told this story in 1 Chronicles. We do not know if the stories from Samuel, Nathan, and Gad are all part of the Bible. It is possible that the stories are the books of 1 and 2 Samuel and 1 and 2 Kings, or they might be stories that people lost after the storyteller told this story.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What records or stories of the history of your nation do you have? What do you call these records or stories, and what are they named after? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that other information about David is in the stories by Samuel, Nathan, and Gad. The stories from these men contain details about David's reign over Israel and David's power or strength as a ruler. The stories from Samuel, Nathan, and Gad also tell people about events that happened to David and to Israel and all the other kingdoms. The storyteller does not mean every kingdom in the whole world. The storyteller means the nations in the region around the country of Israel. These nations were Egypt, Syria, Philistia, Moab, Edom, Tyre, and Amon. You will remember that David defeated some of these nations. The kings of all of these nations respected David as king.

Stop here and look at a map that shows Israel and the surrounding nations. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What other countries or groups of people surround your country or people group? Include all the countries or groups of people that are next to or near your country or people.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:26–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller reminds us that David was the king of all Israel. David ruled for a long time, and Yahweh blessed David. Solomon became king after David was king.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells us that the records of Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer all include the events and details of David's reign and everything that happened to David and the surrounding kingdoms.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller
  • David
  • Solomon
  • And Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller ends the story of 1 Chronicles by giving a summary of David's reign over Israel. The storyteller reminds us that David was king over all of Israel. The storyteller tells us the total number of years that David was king and then tells us how many years David was a king while David lived in Hebron, and then how many years David was a king while David lived in Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Have someone tell a story that is based on another story. What parts of the story are the same? What parts of the story are different? Did the person leave out any parts of the original story? Pause this audio here.

David was very old when he died. During David's long life, David enjoyed many blessings from Yahweh. Yahweh blessed David with a long life or a life full of days.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you describe someone who was old when they died and had a good life? Pause this audio here.

David enjoyed many riches or wealth. David had lots of belongings, money, and valuable items. During David's life, David enjoyed honor. People respected David, and David had a good reputation among his people.

Solomon was king of all Israel after David. David was still alive when Solomon became king.

The storyteller then uses a word to introduce something new into the story and to draw our attention to it.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What word or phrase do you use to draw someone's attention to a new part of a story or get someone's attention? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that all the important events about David's reign over Israel are in three different records or stories. People named these records after three people who heard from God and shared messages from God with David and the people of Israel. The three people were Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer. Samuel, Nathan, and Gad were alive at the same time as David. Storytellers wrote the records of Samuel, Nathan, and Gad many years before the storyteller told the story of 1 Chronicles. The records contained details, or facts and information, about David's reign over Israel, David's power or strength as a king, and David's activities in Israel and with the surrounding kingdoms.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:26–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The storyteller
  • David
  • Solomon
  • And Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The storyteller reminds us that David was the king of all Israel. David ruled for a long time, and Yahweh blessed David. Solomon became king after David was king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David was a really good king. David united Israel; or
  • Yahweh blessed David because David was faithful to his covenant with Israel; or
  • Israel was such a good place at the time of David.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I had a good life; or
  • Yahweh blessed me while I was king; or
  • I kept my promises to my people and to Yahweh.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David anointed me king of Israel; or
  • I am honored to be king of Israel after my father, David.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller tells us that all the events and details of David's reign and everything that happened to David and the surrounding kingdoms are in Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer's records.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • There are so many ways for people to hear about King David; or
  • It is important for my people to see that Israel was strong when Israel was united and David was king; or
  • The other countries near Israel respected and feared David.

Ask the person playing Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We wrote down events from David's reign over Israel; or
  • We told the stories of King David because we wanted everyone to remember those stories.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Chronicles 29:26–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

David, son of Jesse, was king of all Israel. Use the same words or phrases for son, king, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on son, king, and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

David ruled, or was king of, Israel for 40 years. Use the same word or phrase for ruled as you used in previous passages.

David died an old man after David lived a long time and enjoyed wealth and honor, or a good reputation among people. Use the same words and phrases for wealth and honor as you used in previous passages.

David's son Solomon followed David as king. Solomon was the next person to be king after David.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about Solomon following David as the next king. Pause this audio here.

Someone wrote the events of King David's reign in the records or history of Samuel the seer, Nathan the prophet, and Gad the seer. People probably wrote these events on long scrolls or rolled-up pieces of parchment. The word in the original language for records is literally "the words of Samuel the seer."

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for records, or a history that someone writes down. Pause this audio here.

Use the same words for seer and prophet as you have used in previous passages. Remember that seer and prophet mean almost the same thing, and prophet is in the Master Glossary.

The storytellers wrote down the events in Israel and the surrounding kingdoms. In this case, kingdoms are territories that kings rule over. Use the same word or phrase for kingdom as you used in previous passages, and remember that the storyteller is talking about several kingdoms in this story. Kingdom is in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.
